topics.title: Deterministic Approach and Probabilistic Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Approach and Probabilistic Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Deterministic Approach

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
The **Deterministic Approach** to functional safety is a risk and reliability assessment methodology in which safety is guaranteed by using **worst-case (pessimistic) assumptions** about component behavior, operating conditions, and environmental stresses. The output is a single, fixed numerical value of safety (e.g., a Safety Factor, a Safe Failure Fraction, or a SIL rating) that does **not** depend on the statistical likelihood of events. As long as the design rules and safety factors are met, the system is deemed safe — there is no probability component in the final verdict.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Examiner Favourite):**
> "The deterministic approach assumes the worst possible combination of failures and environmental conditions. If the system survives this worst case, it is *deemed safe* by construction, irrespective of how unlikely that worst case actually is."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Parachute Rule"
Imagine an aircraft manufacturer designing a parachute. The **deterministic approach** says:
> *"We don't care how *likely* a failure of the cord is. We will design the cord to withstand 10× the maximum expected load, tested at −50 °C, +70 °C, soaked in saltwater, and after 20 years of storage. If it holds — ship it."*

Notice that no probability of failure is mentioned. Safety is **engineered in** by conservative over-design.

### Key Characteristics of the Deterministic Approach
- **Worst-case analysis** (no statistical averaging).
- **Fixed Safety Factors** (e.g., derating, redundancy rules, separation distances).
- **Qualitative Pass/Fail** verdict.
- Used in legacy safety standards (e.g., pre-IEC 61508 standards, ASME BPVC Section III, classical relay-logic safety systems).
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot distinguish between a system that is 10 % over-engineered and one that is 10 000 % over-engineered — both just "pass."

---

## 1.2 The Probabilistic Approach

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
The **Probabilistic Approach** (also called the *stochastic* or *risk-based* approach) quantifies safety using **statistical measures of failure likelihood** such as failure rates, probabilities of failure on demand, and risk reduction factors. Safety is expressed as a **probability number** (e.g., PFD = 10⁻³ per demand) rather than as a binary "safe/unsafe" judgement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Examiner Favourite):**
> "The probabilistic approach quantifies the likelihood of a hazardous event using failure rate data, reliability block diagrams, fault tree analysis, and event tree analysis. The system is deemed safe if its calculated probability of failure lies within the tolerable risk envelope (e.g., a defined SIL)."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Insurance Premium"
Now think of the same parachute manufacturer under the **probabilistic approach**:
> *"We have tested 1 million cords. On average, 2 cords fail per 100 000 openings. So the probability of a cord failing in your specific jump is 2 × 10⁻⁵. We can quantify this, charge an insurance premium, and decide if the risk is acceptable to the jumper."*

The verdict is **not** "safe" or "unsafe" — it is a **number that is compared to a tolerable threshold**.

### Key Characteristics of the Probabilistic Approach
- **Quantitative** — produces numerical probabilities.
- **Based on failure rate databases** (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500, IEC TR 62380).
- **Enables risk-informed decision making** and cost-benefit optimisation.
- Foundation of modern standards: **IEC 61508, IEC 61511, IEC 62061, ISO 26262**.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires good quality failure data; the answer is only as reliable as the input data.

---

## 1.3 Side-by-Side Comparison (Syllabus Snapshot)

| Aspect | Deterministic | Probabilistic |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single safe value / factor | Numerical probability |
| Basis | Worst-case assumptions | Statistical failure data |
| Redundancy | Fixed (e.g., 1oo2, 2oo3) | Calculated & optimised |
| Standards | Legacy / pre-1990 | IEC 61508 family |
| Data needed | Minimal | Extensive failure-rate data |
| Verdict | Pass / Fail | Acceptable / Unacceptable risk |
| Cost efficiency | Often over-engineered | Risk-informs cost trade-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern functional safety standards (IEC 61508) require a **probabilistic proof of safety** but still retain **deterministic design rules** (e.g., separation, diagnostic coverage). The two approaches are therefore **complementary, not mutually exclusive**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Functional Safety

The choice of approach directly determines:
- How SILs (Safety Integrity Levels 1–4) are assigned and verified.
- Whether the safety instrumented function (SIF) achieves its target **PFDavg** (IEC 61511) or **PFH** (IEC 62061).
- The design of diagnostics, proof-test intervals, and redundancy architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Probabilistic Approach

### 2.1.1 The Reliability Function R(t)
For a non-repairable component with **constant failure rate λ** (failures per hour), the probability that it will survive (function correctly) until time *t* is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **unreliability** (probability of failure by time *t*) is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **constant failure rate assumption** is the basis of the *useful-life* (flat) region of the classic "bathtub curve." It is the standard assumption in IEC 61508 reliability calculations.

### 2.1.2 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)
For an exponential distribution:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

### 2.1.3 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) — Repairable Systems

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} \approx \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad (\text{when MTTR} \ll \text{MTTF})$$

where **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair.

### 2.1.4 Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)
For a low-demand mode SIF (IEC 61511):

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \quad \text{(simplified 1oo1 dangerous undetected)}$$

where:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{\text{proof}}$ = proof-test interval (hours)

### 2.1.5 Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)
For a high-demand or continuous mode SIF (IEC 62061):

$$\text{PFH} = \lambda_D \quad \text{(simplified 1oo1)}$$

### 2.1.6 Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$$

### 2.1.7 SIL Targets (IEC 61508 / 61511)
| SIL | PFDavg (low demand) | PFH (high demand, per hour) | RRF |
|---|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | ≥ 10⁻² to < 10⁻¹ | ≥ 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | ≥ 10⁻³ to < 10⁻² | ≥ 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ | 100 to 1 000 |
| SIL 3 | ≥ 10⁻⁴ to < 10⁻³ | ≥ 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ | 1 000 to 10 000 |
| SIL 4 | ≥ 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁴ | ≥ 10⁻⁹ to < 10⁻⁸ | 10 000 to 100 000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Favourite:** The RRF column is computed simply as 1/PFD. Memorise the SIL-to-PFD mapping — at least one question every KTU cycle uses it.

### 2.1.8 Series and Parallel Reliability
For *n* **independent** components in **series** (all must work):

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

For *n* **independent** identical components in **parallel** (at least one must work):

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - R_i(t)\right)$$

For small $\lambda t$, the parallel (1oo2 — one out of two) PFD simplifies to:

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ÷ 3 and not ÷ 2?** The triangular integration of two parallel failure-rate curves over the proof-test interval. This is a frequently tested derivation — see Section 3.

### 2.1.9 Risk Definition
The foundation of probabilistic safety:

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Frequency} \times \text{Consequence}$$

Functional safety reduces risk by reducing the **frequency** of hazardous events through the safety instrumented function.

---

## 2.2 Deterministic Design Rules (Retained in Modern Standards)

Even when the *proof* of safety is probabilistic, modern standards retain **deterministic design constraints**:

| Deterministic Rule | Source Standard |
|---|---|
| Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) ≥ 60 % for SIL 1, ≥ 90 % for SIL 2, ≥ 99 % for SIL 3 | IEC 61508-2 |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) rules | IEC 61508-2 Table 2 |
| Type A / Type B subsystem restrictions | IEC 61508-2 |
| Minimum separation between safety and non-safety circuits | IEC 61511-1 |
| Single-fault criterion (no single failure causes loss of safety) | IEC 61508 |

> [!NOTE]
> **SFF Formula (Deterministic Quantity):**
> $$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$$
> where $\lambda_S$ = safe failures, $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous detected, $\lambda_D$ = total dangerous failures. Note that SFF is a **fraction (dimensionless)**, not a probability.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Each Approach is Used

- **Deterministic:** Pressure-vessel design (ASME), civil structural loads, traditional relay-based Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), nuclear Class 1E redundancy (2 out of 3 voting), aerospace single-fault tolerance.
- **Probabilistic:** Process-industry SIF verification (IEC 61511), machinery safety (IEC 62061), automotive functional safety (ISO 26262 — ASIL), nuclear PSA (Probabilistic Safety Assessment), aviation reliability centred maintenance (MSG-3).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks "Why has the industry moved from deterministic to probabilistic?", the answer is **three-fold**: (1) better data, (2) cost optimisation (no more massive over-design), (3) ability to compare different architectures (1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo3) on a common numerical basis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability Function from the Failure-Rate Definition

The probability of a component surviving a small interval $\Delta t$ when the failure rate is $\lambda$ (constant) is:

$$\Pr[\text{survive from }t\text{ to } t+\Delta t] = 1 - \lambda \cdot \Delta t$$

For survival from $0$ to $t$, split the interval into $n$ sub-intervals of length $\Delta t = t/n$. The probability of surviving *all* of them is the product of each individual survival probability (independence):

$$R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \lambda \cdot \frac{t}{n}\right)^{n}$$

Using the standard limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 - x/n)^{n} = e^{-x}$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The exponential form arises because failure events in continuous time with a constant rate form a **Poisson process** — the waiting time between events follows an **exponential distribution**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF from the Reliability Function

The mean (expected) time to failure is the first moment of the failure-density function $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

Using integration by parts (let $u=t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}dt$, so $du=dt$, $v=-e^{-\lambda t}$):

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

The boundary term vanishes at both limits (exponential decays faster than linear growth). The remaining integral is:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[\frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PFD of a 1oo1 System Over One Proof-Test Interval

A 1oo1 system has one channel. The dangerous failure rate is $\lambda_D$. Over a proof-test interval $T$, the probability that a failure has occurred *and not yet been revealed* at any instant $t$ is:

$$\text{PFD}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t \quad (\lambda_D t \ll 1)$$

The *average* PFD over the interval is the time-average:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T} \cdot \left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T} \cdot \frac{T^2}{2} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$

Hence the famous **"λ·T/2"** approximation:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PFD of a 1oo2 System (Identical Channels)

A 1oo2 system fails only when **both** channels have failed dangerously. For two identical channels with independent failures:

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2}(t) \approx (\lambda_D t)^2$$

(Using the binomial leading term — both fail in interval $t$, very small probability.)

Averaging over the proof-test interval $T$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt$$

Evaluating:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \frac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T} \cdot \left[\frac{t^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{\lambda_D^{\,2} \cdot T^3}{3T} = \frac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$$

Hence the famous **"λ²T²/3"** approximation:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic — Why this matters for 14-mark questions:** The 1oo2 system is dramatically safer (PFD is squared) but at the cost of doubled hardware. The probabilistic approach makes this **trade-off explicit and quantifiable** — something the deterministic approach cannot do.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation | Formula to Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Component reliability after time *t* | $R(t)=e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate regime |
| Average life of a non-repairable item | MTTF = 1/λ | First moment of exponential |
| 1oo1 SIF in low-demand mode | PFD = λT/2 | Worst case single channel |
| 1oo2 SIF in low-demand mode | PFD = (λT)²/3 | Both channels must fail |
| 2oo3 SIF (one of three can fail) | PFD ≈ 3(λT)²/3 | Two must fail for system failure |
| Risk tolerable threshold check | RRF = 1/PFD | Inverse of demand failure |
| Hardware fault tolerance check | SFF formula | Deterministic constraint |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Two Approaches in Functional Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Functional Safety Assessment] --> B{Approach Used}
    B -->|Deterministic| C[Worst-Case Assumption Set]
    B -->|Probabilistic| D[Failure Rate Database]

    C --> E[Apply Safety Factor / HFT Rule]
    D --> F[Compute R(t), PFD, PFH]

    E --> G[Pass / Fail Verdict]
    F --> H[Compare to SIL Target]

    H -->|PFD in range| I[SIL Achieved]
    H -->|PFD out of range| J[Design Iteration]

    G -->|Pass| K[System Declared Safe]
    G -->|Fail| J
    I --> K
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — 1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1["1oo1 Architecture"]
        A1n1[Channel A] --> A1out[Safety Function Output]
    end
    subgraph A2["1oo2 Architecture"]
        A2n1[Channel A] --> A2out[Safety Function Output]
        A2n2[Channel B] --> A2out
    end
    subgraph A3["2oo3 Architecture"]
        A3n1[Channel A] --> A3v[Voter Logic]
        A3n2[Channel B] --> A3v
        A3n3[Channel C] --> A3v
        A3v --> A3out[Safety Function Output]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Risk Reduction Through a SIF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Hazard] --> P2[Unmitigated Risk Level]
    P2 --> P3{Compare to Tolerable Risk}
    P3 -->|Exceeds| P4[Apply Safety Instrumented Function]
    P4 --> P5[Probability of Failure PFD]
    P5 --> P6[Risk Reduction Factor RRF = 1 over PFD]
    P6 --> P7[Residual Risk = Unmitigated divided by RRF]
    P7 --> P3
    P3 -->|Within Tolerance| P8[Acceptable Risk Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Approach to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[New Safety-Critical System Design] --> Q2{Is failure rate data available?}
    Q2 -->|No| D1[Use Deterministic Worst-Case]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Cost optimisation required?}
    Q3 -->|No| D1
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Standard requirement}
    Q4 -->|IEC 61508 or 61511| P1[Use Probabilistic with SIL Target]
    Q4 -->|Legacy Standard| D1
    P1 --> P2[Apply both deterministic constraints and probabilistic proof]
    D1 --> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Notice how Section 4.4 explicitly shows that **both approaches are usually applied together** in modern functional safety work — the probabilistic proof sits *on top of* a deterministic design envelope.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Differentiate between the deterministic approach and the probabilistic approach in functional safety assessment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Deterministic approach** (1 mark): Uses worst-case assumptions and fixed safety factors. Produces a pass/fail verdict. Does not consider the statistical likelihood of failure.
- **Probabilistic approach** (1 mark): Uses failure rate data to compute numerical probabilities such as PFD or PFH, then compares to a SIL target. Produces a quantifiable safety margin.
- **Key distinction** (1 mark): Deterministic is qualitative (safe/unsafe); probabilistic is quantitative (PFD value) — and the modern IEC 61508 family standards mandate the probabilistic approach with deterministic design rules as constraints.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Suggested Question]
**Define Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg). Write the simplified formula for a 1oo1 system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition** (1.5 marks): PFDavg is the average probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) will fail to perform its safety action when a demand occurs, averaged over the proof-test interval. It is the key metric for low-demand mode SIFs as per IEC 61511.
- **Formula** (1.5 marks):
$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$$

where $\lambda_D$ is the dangerous failure rate (per hour) and $T_{\text{proof}}$ is the proof-test interval (hours).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Probabilistic Approach Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of neat derivations, explain how the reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is obtained from the constant failure-rate assumption. Define MTTF and derive its value for an exponentially distributed failure model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo1 SIF is used in a low-demand mode application. The dangerous failure rate of the sensor is $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and the proof-test interval is 1 year (= 8760 hours). Compute the PFDavg, the RRF, and identify the SIL achieved. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of R(t) and MTTF — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Failure rate definition (1 mark):**
The instantaneous failure rate is the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \frac{dR}{dt}$$

where $R(t)$ is the reliability function and $f(t) = -dR/dt$ is the failure density.

**Step 2 — Differential equation (1 mark):**
Rearranging:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Constant failure rate assumption (1 mark):**
For the useful-life region of the bathtub curve, $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant). Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with $R(0)=1$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — MTTF definition (1 mark):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Integration by parts (2 marks):**
Using $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}dt$:
$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = 0 + \left[\frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For constant failure rate, MTTF = 1/λ. The exponential reliability function is the cornerstone of probabilistic safety assessment.

---

**(b) Numerical SIL Identification — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr; $T = 8760$ hr; 1oo1 architecture.

**Step 1 — Compute PFDavg (3 marks):**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \frac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219 \approx 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute RRF (2 marks):**
$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{0.0219} \approx 45.7$$

**Step 3 — Identify SIL (2 marks):**
Comparing to the SIL table:
- SIL 1 requires $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$, i.e., RRF 10–100.
- Our PFD = 2.19 × 10⁻² and RRF ≈ 45.7 **fall within the SIL 1 band**.

**Conclusion:** The SIF achieves **SIL 1**. To reach SIL 2, the RRF must be ≥ 100, requiring either a shorter proof-test interval, a 1oo2 architecture, or a sensor with lower $\lambda_D$.

---

### Question B — Deterministic Approach & Comparative Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Suggested, June 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the deterministic approach to functional safety in detail. List its advantages and limitations. Why is it insufficient on its own for modern process-industry SIFs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIL 3 SIF in a refinery uses a 1oo2 sensor pair. Each sensor has $\lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr. The proof-test interval is 2 years (= 17 520 hours). Compute the PFDavg and verify whether the SIF meets the SIL 3 target. Suggest one improvement that would bring it into compliance, if it fails. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Deterministic Approach — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** The deterministic approach evaluates safety by assuming the *worst credible failure combination* and applying fixed safety factors or design rules. If the system holds under the worst case, it is declared safe.

**Methodology (2 marks):**
- Identify all credible single-point failures.
- Apply safety factors (e.g., derating, separation, HFT rules).
- Apply hardware fault-tolerance rules (e.g., 1oo2D for SIL 3, 2oo3 for SIL 4).
- Apply the single-fault criterion (no single failure shall cause loss of safety function).

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Simple — no failure-rate data required.
2. Conservative — guarantees a safety margin under all conditions.
3. Easy to audit and certify by third parties.
4. Decades of historical precedent in mechanical and civil engineering.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Cannot distinguish between marginal and massive over-design.
2. Does not allow cost-effective optimisation.
3. Cannot rank architectures (1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo3) on a common numerical basis.
4. Does not directly produce the PFD/PFH evidence demanded by IEC 61508/61511.

**Why Insufficient for Modern SIFs (1 mark):** IEC 61511 explicitly mandates *probabilistic verification* (target PFDavg per SIL). Deterministic design rules are retained as constraints (SFF, HFT, separation) but the *proof of safety* is probabilistic — hence a deterministic-only approach cannot certify SIL compliance.

---

**(b) Numerical SIL 3 Verification — 7 marks**

**Given:** 1oo2 architecture; $\lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr per channel; $T = 17\,520$ hr.

**Step 1 — Compute PFDavg for 1oo2 (3 marks):**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$$

Calculate the product first:
$$\lambda_D \cdot T = (1 \times 10^{-5}) \times 17520 = 0.1752$$

Square it:
$$(\lambda_D T)^2 = (0.1752)^2 = 0.03069$$

Divide by 3:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{0.03069}{3} \approx 0.01023 \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to SIL 3 target (2 marks):**
SIL 3 requires $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$, i.e., RRF 1000–10 000.
Our PFD = 1.02 × 10⁻² → falls in the **SIL 2** band (RRF ≈ 98), **not SIL 3**.

**Step 3 — Suggest an improvement (2 marks):**
Three possible improvements (any one acceptable for full marks):
1. **Reduce proof-test interval** to $T = 1$ year = 8760 hr → new $\lambda_D T = 0.0876$ → PFD = (0.0876)²/3 = 2.56 × 10⁻³ (still SIL 2). Need to go to ~ 1 month.
2. **Use 2oo3 voting** with three channels → only two must fail for system failure, with similar but slightly different math; effectively trades cost for availability.
3. **Add diagnostics (1oo2D)** to convert dangerous undetected failures to dangerous detected, reducing the effective $\lambda_D$ contribution and achieving SIL 3.
4. **Choose a sensor with lower $\lambda_D$** (e.g., 2 × 10⁻⁶/hr) — re-running the calc would drop PFD to the SIL 3 band.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Probabilistic Approach (Lose Marks Here):**
> - **Do not** confuse PFH (high-demand) with PFD (low-demand). Mixing units loses 2 marks.
> - **Do not** forget the factor of 2 in the 1oo1 PFD formula (integration of linear ramp → λT/2, not λT). This is a **top error** flagged by KTU examiners.
> - **Do not** use the 1oo2 formula (λ²T²/3) for 1oo1 or 2oo3 architectures.
> - **Always state the assumptions** when using approximations: constant λ, small λt, identical channels, independent failures.
> - **Do not** use the deterministic approach to *prove* a SIL — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether students know that the *proof* is probabilistic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Deterministic vs Probabilistic Approach**

- ✅ **Deterministic approach** = worst-case + safety factors + pass/fail verdict. Used in legacy standards, pressure-vessel design, traditional ESD systems.
- ✅ **Probabilistic approach** = failure-rate data → reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ → PFD/PFH → compare to SIL target.
- ✅ The two approaches are **complementary**, not opposing — modern IEC 61508/61511 use **probabilistic proof + deterministic constraints**.
- ✅ The **constant failure rate** assumption is the foundation of all probabilistic formulas in functional safety.
- ✅ Core formula memorisation required:
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
  - $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$
  - $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \lambda_D T/2$
  - $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} \approx (\lambda_D T)^2/3$
  - $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}$
- ✅ SIL bands (PFDavg): SIL 1 = 10⁻² to 10⁻¹, SIL 2 = 10⁻³ to 10⁻², SIL 3 = 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³, SIL 4 = 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴.
- ✅ The **derivation of R(t)** from a Poisson process and the **derivation of the 1oo1 PFD** via time-averaging are the two most-tested 14-mark derivations.
- ✅ **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is a deterministic quantity that gates which HFT route is acceptable for a given SIL.
- ✅ When asked "which approach is better?", answer: **probabilistic for proof, deterministic for design constraints** — never one without the other in modern functional safety.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 scheme exam, always show units, state assumptions, and finalise with a **SIL verdict** — partial credit depends on the verdict line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Functional Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Functional Safety # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Functional Safety** is the part of the overall safety of a system or piece of equipment that depends on the system or equipment operating correctly in response to its inputs, including the safe management of likely operator errors, hardware failures, and environmental changes. It is achieved when every safety function is correctly performed and the safety-related systems (SRS) achieve and maintain a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** under all stated operational conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (IEC 61508 Compliant):** > *"Functional Safety is the detection of a potentially dangerous condition resulting in the activation of a protective or corrective device or mechanism to prevent or mitigate the hazard."* > — IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.1.12 ### Key Terminology Distinctions | Term | Formal Definition | KTU-Style Distinction | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Safety** | Freedom from unacceptable risk of physical injury or of damage to the health of people, either directly or indirectly. | A broad, holistic state of protection. | | **Security** | The state of being free from danger or threat (focused on intentional malicious acts). | Cybersecurity; focused on malicious intent. | | **Functional Safety** | Safety achieved through the correct operation of safety-related systems responding to inputs. | Subset of overall safety, focused on **automatic** protective action. | | **Reliability** | The probability that an item performs a required function under stated conditions for a stated period of time. | Refers to continuous, intended function. | | **Risk** | The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm. | A measurable hazard metric. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steam Boiler Pressure Release" Imagine a traditional steam boiler in a 19th-century factory. Without any active protection, a pressure buildup could cause a catastrophic explosion. Engineers added a **pressure relief valve** — a purely mechanical device that physically opens when pressure exceeds a threshold. Now, in a modern nuclear power plant or a chemical reactor, the analogous protection is a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**: a network of sensors (pressure transmitter), logic solvers (PLC/safety controller), and final control elements (relief valves, emergency shutdown valves). If the sensor detects abnormal pressure, the system **automatically** commands a corrective action — even if no human operator is present or aware. > [!NOTE] > **Why this analogy matters for KTU exams:** When examiners ask *"Why is functional safety needed?"*, this boiler-to-SIS transformation captures the essence: from passive mechanical protection to **active, automated, sensor-driven safety** that must remain dependable during fault conditions. The chain of components that collectively perform a single safety function is termed the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**. ## 1.3 The Fundamental Premise A safety-related system must be designed so that even when **one, two, or more faults occur**, the system either continues to perform its safety function or transitions to a defined **safe state**. This principle is known as **fail-safe** or **fault-tolerant** operation, and it forms the bedrock of functional safety engineering. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Examiners — "SHE" Triad:** > - **S**everity (how bad can it get?) > - **H**azard rate (how often does it occur?) > - **E**xposure / probability (what is the chance it triggers?) > > Risk = f(Severity, Hazard, Exposure) — quantified as $R = \sum_{i} f_i \times C_i$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Functional Safety Modern functional safety rests on three philosophical pillars that the KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with **IEC 61508** (the umbrella standard for electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems): 1. **Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment** 2. **Risk Reduction (by Safety Functions)** 3. **Verification of Adequate Risk Reduction (via SIL)** ### 2.1.1 Hazard Identification A **hazard** is a physical situation with the potential to cause physical injury or damage. Hazards are identified through techniques such as: - **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study) - **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) - **FTA** (Fault Tree Analysis) - **LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis) ### 2.1.2 Risk Assessment **Risk** is mathematically expressed as: $$R = f(H, P, C)$$ where: - $H$ = hazard occurrence frequency (events per hour) - $P$ = probability of harm given the hazard - $C$ = consequence severity (typically in monetary or injury units) ### 2.1.3 Risk Reduction **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is the reciprocal of the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**: $$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD}$$ The **Tolerable Risk Target** is determined by society (regulatory), industry (standards), and corporate (ALARP principle) layers. ## 2.2 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The IEC 61508 Framework The **SIL** is a discrete level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety functions to be allocated to the **E/E/PE safety-related systems**. IEC 61508 defines two modes of operation: ### Mode A — Low Demand Mode The safety function is only exercised **on demand** (e.g., emergency shutdown ≤ once per year). ### Mode B — High Demand / Continuous Mode The safety function continuously maintains a safe state (e.g., aircraft flight control). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Point:** Many students confuse *Demand Rate* with *Failure Rate*. **Demand Rate** is how often the safety function is *called upon*; **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** is how often a component *fails internally*. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Variable Definitions | Applicable Mode | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $R = f \times C$ | $R$=risk, $f$=frequency, $C$=consequence | All | events × severity | | 2 | $PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}$ | $\lambda_D$=dangerous failure rate, $T_{proof}$=proof test interval | Low Demand | dimensionless | | 3 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Low Demand | dimensionless | | 4 | $PFH = \lambda_D$ (for single channel) | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour | High Demand / Continuous | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ | | 5 | $PFD_{system} = PFD_1 + PFD_2 - (PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2)$ | Independent SIFs in parallel | Low Demand | dimensionless | | 6 | $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | $\beta$-factor (CCF fraction), $\lambda_{DD}$=dangerous detected | Architecture | dimensionless | | 7 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Reliability | hours | | 8 | $DC = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | Architecture | fraction (0–1) | | 9 | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{D}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | Type A/B classification | fraction (0–1) | | 10 | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between/Between Repair | Reliability | fraction | ## 2.4 SIL Target Bands (IEC 61508-1 Table 2) | SIL | Low Demand Mode — PFD Range | High Demand / Continuous Mode — PFH Range | Risk Reduction | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | **10 to 100** | | **2** | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | **100 to 1 000** | | **3** | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | **1 000 to 10 000** | | **4** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | $\geq 10^{-9}$ to $< 10^{-8}$ | **10 000 to 100 000** | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Restriction Notice:** All $\vert \cdot \vert$ notations in equations above are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` to prevent markdown table parsing conflicts — *do not copy the pipe character directly*. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Functional Safety is the engineering backbone of every modern safety-critical domain: - **Process Industry (IEC 61511):** Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G) systems in refineries. - **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Anti-lock Braking (ABS), Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB). - **Railway (EN 50129 / EN 50128):** Automatic Train Protection (ATP), signaling interlocking. - **Machinery (ISO 13849 / IEC 62061):** Light curtains, two-hand controls, safety PLCs in robotic cells. - **Nuclear (IEC 61513):** Reactor protection systems. - **Aviation (DO-178C / DO-254):** Flight control software & hardware certification. - **Medical Devices (IEC 62304 / IEC 60601):** Infusion pumps, ventilators, MRI interlocks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$) ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement For a single channel of a low-demand safety instrumented function, the dangerous failure rate ($\lambda_D$) is constant. Proof testing is performed at regular intervals of duration $T_{proof}$. Derive the **average probability of failure on demand** for an unrevealed dangerous failure. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the unavailability $U(t)$ as the probability that the system is failed at time $t$ given it was operational at $t=0$. For a single, constant failure rate and a perfect proof test that restores the system to "as good as new": $$U(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the small-failure-probability approximation. Since $\lambda_D t \ll 1$ (typical values $\sim 10^{-6}$/hr), we use $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$: $$U(t) \approx \lambda_D t$$ **Step 3:** Compute the average unavailability over one complete proof-test interval by integrating from $0$ to $T_{proof}$ and dividing by the interval length: $$U_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_D t \, dt$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the integral: $$\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_D t \, dt = \lambda_D \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into the average expression: $$U_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}^{2}}{2} = \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}}{2}$$ **Step 6:** Identify that, for low-demand mode, the **probability of failure on demand** equals the average unavailability: $$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{proof}}$$ **Interpretation:** Halving the proof-test interval $T_{proof}$ halves the $PFD_{avg}$, thereby improving the SIL by one band (provided $\lambda_D$ remains constant). --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement Given the tolerable risk target $R_{t}$ for a process hazard and the inherent process risk $R_{process}$ without any safety function, derive the **required Risk Reduction Factor** for the safety instrumented function. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the inherent process frequency of occurrence of the hazardous event: $$f_{process} \; [\text{events per year}]$$ **Step 2:** The frequency after adding the safety function is the product of the inherent frequency and the PFD of the SIF: $$f_{with\,SIF} = f_{process} \times PFD_{SIF}$$ **Step 3:** The tolerable frequency target is given by the standard / regulator: $$f_{tolerable} \le f_{process} \times PFD_{SIF}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for the required PFD: $$PFD_{SIF} \le \frac{f_{tolerable}}{f_{process}}$$ **Step 5:** By definition of the Risk Reduction Factor as the inverse of PFD: $$\boxed{RRF = \frac{f_{process}}{f_{tolerable}} = \frac{1}{PFD_{required}}}$$ **Example:** If a runaway reaction occurs $10^{-2}$/year (once per 100 years) and the tolerable target is $10^{-6}$/year: $$RRF = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}} = 10^{4}$$ This corresponds to **SIL 3** in low-demand mode. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing SIL from Frequency & Consequence ### 3.3.1 Risk Matrix (Calibrated) | Severity $\rightarrow$
Frequency $\downarrow$ | **Catastrophic** | **Critical** | **Marginal** | **Negligible** | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Frequent ($>10^{-1}$/yr) | **SIL 4** | **SIL 4** | **SIL 3** | **SIL 2** | | Probable ($10^{-1}$–$10^{-2}$/yr) | **SIL 4** | **SIL 3** | **SIL 2** | **SIL 1** | | Occasional ($10^{-2}$–$10^{-3}$/yr) | **SIL 3** | **SIL 2** | **SIL 1** | **SIL 1** | | Remote ($10^{-3}$–$10^{-4}$/yr) | **SIL 3** | **SIL 2** | **SIL 1** | **SIL 1** | | Improbable ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-5}$/yr) | **SIL 2** | **SIL 1** | **SIL 1** | No SIF | | Incredible ($<10^{-5}$/yr) | **SIL 1** | No SIF | No SIF | No SIF | ### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem (KTU Practice):** A reactor overpressure hazard has an inherent frequency of $5 \times 10^{-3}$/year. The tolerable target set by the regulator is $10^{-5}$/year. The proposed SIS uses a single transmitter ($\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/hr), a logic solver ($\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-7}$/hr), and a single valve ($\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr), with a proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr (1 year). Determine whether SIL 2 is achieved. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute the required RRF: $$RRF_{req} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-5}} = 500$$ Step 2 — Compute $PFD_{avg}$ for each subsystem using $PFD_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{D,i} T_{proof}$: $$\begin{aligned} PFD_{sensor} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 2.19 \times 10^{-2} \\ PFD_{logic} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 8.76 \times 10^{-4} \\ PFD_{valve} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 1.314 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$$ Step 3 — Sum (series = sum) the subsystem PFDs: $$PFD_{sys} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2} + 8.76 \times 10^{-4} + 1.314 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$PFD_{sys} = 3.592 \times 10^{-2}$$ Step 4 — Compute achieved RRF: $$RRF_{achieved} = \frac{1}{3.592 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 27.84$$ Step 5 — Compare with SIL 2 target band ($\text{RRF} \in [100, 1000]$): $$27.84 < 100 \implies \text{SIL 2 NOT achieved (current: SIL 1)}$$ **Remediation:** Either reduce $T_{proof}$ to $\approx 1$ month ($730$ hr), which scales all PFDs by $\tfrac{1}{12}$, giving $PFD_{sys} \approx 2.99 \times 10^{-3}$, $RRF \approx 334$ (SIL 2), **or** add a redundant sensor channel. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Common Cause Failure (CCF) — The $\beta$-Factor Model For a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) redundant architecture, if both channels share the same $\beta$-fraction of dangerous failures, the system PFD with redundancy becomes: $$PFD_{1oo2} = \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}}{2} + (1-\beta)^{2} \cdot \frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^{2}}{3}$$ This penalizes redundancy. A common KTU derivation simplification: $$PFD_{1oo2,\,simplified} \approx \beta \cdot PFD_{1} + (1-\beta) \cdot \frac{PFD_{1}^{2}}{2}$$ where $PFD_{1}$ is the single-channel PFD. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Without the $\beta$-factor correction, students naively compute $PFD_{1oo2} = PFD_{1}^{2}$, which gives an **unrealistically optimistic** SIL rating — a guaranteed examiner-deduction in KTU valuation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61508 Overall V-Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD conceptA["Concept and Scope Definition"] hazardA["Hazard and Risk Analysis"] overallSafetyA["Overall Safety Requirements"] safetyReqAlloc["Safety Requirements Allocation to E/E/PE and Other Risk Reduction"] overallPlan["Overall Planning and Validation Plan"] eE_PE_Real["E/E/PE System Realisation"] otherTechReal["Other Risk Reduction System Realisation"] opInstall["Operation and Maintenance"] modify["Modification and Retrofit"] decomm["Decommissioning or Disposal"] installComm["Installation and Commissioning"] overallSafetyVer["Overall Safety Verification"] eE_PE_Ver["E/E/PE System Verification"] otherTechVer["Other Risk Reduction System Verification"] conceptA --> hazardA hazardA --> overallSafetyA overallSafetyA --> safetyReqAlloc safetyReqAlloc --> eE_PE_Real safetyReqAlloc --> otherTechReal eE_PE_Real --> eE_PE_Ver otherTechReal --> otherTechVer eE_PE_Ver --> overallSafetyVer otherTechVer --> overallSafetyVer overallSafetyVer --> installComm installComm --> opInstall opInstall --> modify modify --> decomm ``` **Reading the V-Model:** The left arm cascades requirements (decomposition from concept to subsystem design); the right arm performs **verification** by integration upward — each design level is tested against the requirements written at the same level on the left. ## 4.2 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Architecture — 1oo2 Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR processVar["Process Variable"] sensor1["Sensor Channel 1"] sensor2["Sensor Channel 2"] voter["1oo2 Logic Voter"] logicSolver["Safety Logic Solver PLC"] actuator1["Final Element A Valve"] actuator2["Final Element B Valve"] safeState["Safe State Actuated"] processVar --> sensor1 processVar --> sensor2 sensor1 --> voter sensor2 --> voter voter --> logicSolver logicSolver --> actuator1 logicSolver --> actuator2 actuator1 --> safeState actuator2 --> safeState ``` **Annotation:** In a 1oo2 voting configuration, the safety action is initiated if **either** channel signals an abnormal condition. This arrangement trades off spurious-trip rate (high) against probability of failure-to-act (low) — typical for **SIL 3 high-demand** applications. ## 4.3 Risk Reduction Concept — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) ```mermaid flowchart TD process["Process Layer Base Event"] bpcs["BPCS Layer Control Loop"] alarm["Alarm and Operator Intervention"] sis["Safety Instrumented Function SIF"] relief["Mechanical Relief Device"] community["Community Emergency Response"] mitigated["Residual Risk at Acceptable Level"] process --> bpcs bpcs --> alarm alarm --> sis sis --> relief relief --> community community --> mitigated ``` **Reading:** Each layer independently reduces risk by an order of magnitude. The cumulative risk reduction of an SIS operating alongside relief devices and procedural controls is what justifies the **tolerable risk** criterion. ## 4.4 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Top-Down Failure Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD top["TOP EVENT Reactor Overpressure"] gateA["OR Gate External or Internal Cause"] gateB["AND Gate SIS Fails AND Demand Occurs"] e1["Coolant Pump Failure"] e2["Control Valve Stuck"] e3["Sensor Fails Dangerously"] e4["Logic Solver Fault"] e5["Shutdown Valve Stuck"] top --> gateA gateA --> gateB gateA --> e1 gateA --> e2 gateB --> e3 gateB --> e4 e3 --> e5 ``` **Interpretation:** The top event (reactor overpressure) occurs if **any** base cause triggers OR if both the SIS fails AND a process demand occurs simultaneously. This visualization is a common 14-mark KTU question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Define Functional Safety. List any two standards governing it. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Functional Safety is defined as the part of overall safety relating to the EUC (Equipment Under Control) and the EUC control system that depends on the correct functioning of the **Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE)** safety-related systems, other technology safety-related systems, and external risk reduction facilities. **Two governing standards (1.5 marks each):** 1. **IEC 61508** — Generic functional safety of E/E/PE safety-related systems. 2. **ISO 26262** — Functional safety for automotive electrical/electronic systems. 3. *(Acceptable alternatives: IEC 61511, IEC 62061, EN 50129, DO-178C)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Differentiate between Safety Integrity Level (SIL) and Safety Integrity. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Safety Integrity | Safety Integrity Level (SIL) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | The probability of a safety-related system satisfactorily performing the required safety functions under all stated conditions within a stated period of time. | A discrete level (1 to 4) corresponding to a range of values for safety integrity. | | **Nature** | Continuous property (a probability). | Discrete banding (categorical). | | **Specified by** | Statistical / probabilistic calculation. | Standards (IEC 61508 tables). | | **Example** | $PFD = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$. | SIL 2. | (1.5 marks for definition each + 1 mark for distinction table) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** and identify the role of each subsystem. (7 Marks) **(b)** A pressure relief system has the following components in a single channel: | Subsystem | $\lambda_D$ (per hour) | $T_{proof}$ (hr) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Pressure Transmitter | $6 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 | | Logic Solver | $1 \times 10^{-7}$ | 8760 | | Shut-off Valve | $4 \times 10^{-6}$ | 4380 | Determine the achieved SIL under low-demand mode. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is the complete chain from sensor to final element that performs a single safety function. Block diagram: ``` [Process Variable] → [Sensor] → [Logic Solver] → [Final Element] → [Safe State] ``` **Roles (1 mark per subsystem, 1 mark for overall description):** 1. **Sensor / Transmitter (Input Subsystem):** Detects abnormal process condition (e.g., overpressure, over-temperature). Converts physical quantity into a standardised signal. *(2 marks)* 2. **Logic Solver / Safety PLC (Decision Subsystem):** Implements the safety logic (e.g., 2oo3 voting, trip logic). Generates the shutdown command when trip condition is met. *(2 marks)* 3. **Final Control Element (Output Subsystem):** Performs the corrective action — typically a shut-off valve, circuit breaker, or relay that brings the process to a safe state. *(2 marks)* 4. **Overall:** The SIF must achieve a specified **SIL** by limiting the **PFD** within the bounds of IEC 61508 Table 2. *(1 mark)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute $PFD_i$ for each subsystem using $PFD_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{D,i} \cdot T_{proof,i}$:** (2 marks) $$\begin{aligned} PFD_{PT} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 2.628 \times 10^{-2} \\ PFD_{LS} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-4} \\ PFD_{SV} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Total system PFD for series connection:** (1 mark) $$PFD_{sys} = 2.628 \times 10^{-2} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} + 8.76 \times 10^{-3} = 3.548 \times 10^{-2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute RRF:** (1 mark) $$RRF = \frac{1}{3.548 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 28.19$$ **Step 4 — Determine SIL from IEC 61508 Table 2:** (1 mark) | SIL | PFD Range | | :--- | :--- | | SIL 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | | SIL 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | | SIL 3 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | | SIL 4 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Since $PFD_{sys} = 3.548 \times 10^{-2}$ lies in $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$, the system achieves **SIL 1**. **Step 5 — Inference:** (2 marks) The valve and transmitter dominate the PFD contribution. To upgrade to **SIL 2**, either: - Reduce $T_{proof}$ for the transmitter and valve to 1 month (730 hr); or - Adopt a 1oo2 redundant sensor configuration with proper CCF ($\beta$) accounting. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` (INTERNAL CHOICE) **(a)** Explain the **ALARP principle** and describe the risk acceptance criteria used in functional safety standards. (7 Marks) **(b)** A Safety Instrumented System has a tolerable hazard rate of $10^{-7}$/hr and a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 10^{-5}$/hr. If a 1oo2 redundant sensor configuration is employed with $\beta = 10\%$ and proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr, compute the achieved PFH and verify whether SIL 3 is met. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable:** (3 marks) ALARP is a risk-management principle where risks must be reduced to a level "as low as reasonably practicable". It has two boundaries: - **Upper bound (Tolerable Risk):** Above this, the risk is intolerable and must be reduced regardless of cost. - **Lower bound (Negligible Risk):** Below this, further risk reduction is not required. Between them, the cost of further risk reduction must be **grossly disproportionate** to the benefit gained — this is the economic test for ALARP. **Risk Acceptance Criteria (4 marks):** 1. **ALARP / "Reasonably Practicable" Criterion (IEC 61511)** — cost-benefit analysis (EC.50 etc.). 2. **Memorandum of Understanding (MEM) Criterion** — collective vs individual risk. 3. **Numerical / Risk Matrix Criterion (IEC 61508 / 61511)** — fixed bands for tolerable frequency. 4. **GAME / GAMAB (Globalement Au Moins Aussi Bon)** — comparative with existing installations. 5. **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** — UK / EU regulatory standard. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Compute single-channel $PFD_1$:** (1 mark) $$PFD_1 = \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_{proof} = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute 1oo2 $PFD_{sys}$ with $\beta$-factor:** (2 marks) $$PFD_{1oo2} \approx \beta \cdot PFD_1 + (1-\beta) \cdot \frac{PFD_1^{2}}{2}$$ $$PFD_{1oo2} = 0.1 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-2} + 0.9 \times \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-2})^{2}}{2}$$ $$PFD_{1oo2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 8.63 \times 10^{-4} = 5.243 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 3 — Convert $PFD$ to $PFH$ for high-demand assessment:** (1 mark) For 1oo2 architecture in high-demand mode: $$PFH_{1oo2} \approx \beta \cdot \lambda_D + (1-\beta) \cdot \frac{\lambda_D^{2} T_{proof}}{3}$$ $$PFH_{1oo2} = 0.1 \times 10^{-5} + 0.9 \times \frac{(10^{-5})^{2} \times 8760}{3}$$ $$PFH_{1oo2} = 10^{-6} + 2.628 \times 10^{-7} = 1.263 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{hr}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with SIL 3 band ($\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$):** (1 mark) $$1.263 \times 10^{-6} \ge 10^{-7}$$ Since $PFH$ exceeds the SIL 3 upper bound by one decade, **SIL 3 is NOT met.** The system only achieves **SIL 2** ($\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$). **Step 5 — Verification against tolerable hazard rate:** (2 marks) $$10^{-6}\,/\text{hr} > 10^{-7}\,/\text{hr} = \text{tolerable target}$$ This confirms the system fails the safety requirement by a factor of 10. To meet SIL 3, $\lambda_D$ must be reduced by an order of magnitude, or a 2oo3 voting architecture employed. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Functional Safety Problems:** > > 1. **Naive sum $PFD = PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2$ for redundant channels** — this ignores CCF; the $\beta$-factor term $\beta \cdot PFD_1$ is *not* negligible. Loss: 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in $PFD = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{proof}$** — Examiner's instant 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Mixing up "low-demand PFD" with "high-demand PFH"** — they are different quantities; the SIL bands differ by orders of magnitude. > 4. **Wrong unit conversion in $T_{proof}$** — given in months, used as hours, or vice versa. Always write: $T_{proof} = 8760$ hr = 1 year. > 5. **Using $10^{-2}$ as SIL 1 boundary** — actual boundary is $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$. Off-by-one boundary losses are common. > 6. **Omitting Safe Failure Fraction ($SFF$) and Diagnostic Coverage ($DC$)** for Type A vs Type B subsystem classification — required by IEC 61508-2 Table 2. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Introduction to Functional Safety":** - ✓ **Functional Safety** is a *subset* of overall safety, focused on automatic, E/E/PE-system-driven risk reduction. - ✓ **IEC 61508** is the umbrella standard; **IEC 61511**, **ISO 26262**, **IEC 62061**, **EN 50129** are sector-specific derivatives. - ✓ **SIL** has 4 discrete levels; SIL 1 = lowest integrity, SIL 4 = highest. - ✓ **Low-Demand Mode** uses **PFD** ($\text{demand}^{-1}$); **High-Demand/Continuous Mode** uses **PFH** ($\text{hr}^{-1}$). - ✓ **Key formula:** $PFD_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{proof}$ for unrevealed dangerous failures with perfect proof test. - ✓ **Key formula:** $RRF = 1 / PFD$; chain PFDs in series by **addition**, in parallel by inclusion-exclusion. - ✓ **Redundancy benefit is limited by Common Cause Failures (CCF)** — quantified by the $\beta$-factor (typical 5–10%). - ✓ **ALARP** principle separates intolerable risk from negligible risk with a "grossly disproportionate" cost-benefit test in between. - ✓ **LOPA** is the structured method to determine required SIL by counting independent protection layers. - ✓ **Risk Matrix** maps severity × frequency to SIL targets (typical KTU 4×6 or 5×5 grid). - ✓ **V-Model** is the standard lifecycle: requirements on the left arm, verification on the right arm. - ✓ **Proof testing** restores a channel to "as good as new" — halving $T_{proof}$ halves $PFD_{avg}$. - ✓ **Diagnostic Coverage ($DC$)** and **Safe Failure Fraction ($SFF$)** determine acceptable hardware architectural constraints per IEC 61508-2. - ✓ Always state the **units** explicitly in numerical answers ($/\text{hr}$, $\text{dimensionless}$, $/year$).

topics.title: Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Functional Safety?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61508):** *Functional Safety* is defined as the part of the overall safety of a system or piece of equipment that depends on the system or equipment operating correctly in response to its inputs, including the safe management of likely operator errors, hardware failures, and environmental changes. It is achieved when every safety function is correctly performed and the system tolerates any single fault.

In simpler words, **Functional Safety** is the *automatic* protection of a system against failures. It is not about preventing failures (that is **reliability**) — it is about ensuring that *if something goes wrong*, the system automatically drives itself to a **safe state** without needing a human to intervene.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Building)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Modern Car with ABS:**
> Imagine an old car with no Anti-lock Braking System (ABS). When the driver brakes hard on a slippery road, the wheels may lock, causing a skid. The car becomes unsafe, but the driver *must* perform the right action.
>
> Now consider a car with ABS sensors, an Electronic Control Unit (ECU), and hydraulic actuators. The system **monitors** wheel speed 100 times per second. If a wheel locks, the ECU **automatically** releases and re-applies brake pressure many times per second. This is **functional safety** in action.
>
> - The car didn't *prevent* the skid (a failure condition occurred).
> - The safety function *detected* the unsafe state and *acted* automatically.
> - The driver was not needed to bring the system to a safe state.

### 1.3 Why is Functional Safety Important?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Functional Safety Matters:**
> 1. **Loss Prevention:** Prevents injury, fatality, environmental damage, and asset loss.
> 2. **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory in industries like automotive (ISO 26262), process control (IEC 61511), and machinery (ISO 13849).
> 3. **Legal Liability:** Manufacturers can be held legally responsible for unsafe products.
> 4. **Public Trust & Brand Value:** A single major accident can destroy decades of reputation.
> 5. **Cost of Recall:** Post-failure fixes are 10x–100x more expensive than designing safety in.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Foundation Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A potential source of harm (physical injury, damage to health, property, or environment). |
| **Risk** | The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm. |
| **Safety Function** | A function whose failure can result in an immediate increase of the safety risk. |
| **Safe State** | A state of the system where safety is assured (e.g., valve closed, motor stopped, power cut). |
| **Fault** | An abnormal condition that may cause a reduction in, or loss of, the capability of a functional unit to perform a required function. |
| **Error** | A discrepancy between a computed, observed, or measured value and the true, specified, or theoretically correct value. |
| **Failure** | The termination of the ability of a functional unit to perform a required function. |
| **SIL** | Safety Integrity Level — a discrete level (1 to 4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of safety functions. |
| **E/E/PE System** | Electrical / Electronic / Programmable Electronic system used to perform safety functions. |
| **BPCS** | Basic Process Control System — the system that performs the actual control task (not safety). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Language:** *Hazard* → *Risk* → *Safety Function* → *Safe State*. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for every Functional Safety exam answer.

### 1.5 The Origin of Functional Safety

The discipline of Functional Safety was formally codified in the international standard **IEC 61508**, first published in 1998 and revised in 2010. It is the *umbrella standard* for functional safety of E/E/PE safety-related systems.

| Standard | Domain |
|---|---|
| **IEC 61508** | Generic / Cross-industry umbrella standard |
| **ISO 26262** | Automotive |
| **IEC 61511** | Process Industry |
| **ISO 13849** | Machinery Safety |
| **IEC 62061** | Machinery (electrical) |
| **EN 50128 / EN 50129** | Railway (signalling & communication) |
| **IEC 61513** | Nuclear Power |
| **ISO 21448 (SOTIF)** | Road vehicles — Safety of the Intended Functionality |
| **IEC 62443** | Industrial Cyber Security (related, complementary) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PESFT635, the *primary* reference is **IEC 61508** (2010 edition), with references to **ISO 26262** for automotive and **IEC 61511** for process industries. Students must know the difference between these and apply the right one to industry case studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Functional Safety

Functional Safety rests on two non-negotiable concepts:

1. **Functional Correctness** — The safety function must *do the right thing* (correct logic, correct response).
2. **Integrity (Reliability of the Safety Function)** — The safety function must *do it reliably*, on demand, over the entire operational lifetime of the system.

This is why Functional Safety is best described by the equation:

$$
\text{Functional Safety} = f(\text{Correctness},\ \text{Integrity},\ \text{Determinism})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A system can be functionally correct (right logic) but unsafe if it is unreliable (low integrity). For example, a pressure relief valve that *works* but only 50% of the time is **not** functionally safe.

### 2.2 Risk — The Core Driver of Functional Safety

Risk is the *starting point* of all safety engineering. The **risk reduction** required from a safety function is calculated as:

$$
R = f \times C
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk
- $f$ = Frequency (or probability) of the hazardous event
- $C$ = Consequence (severity) of the hazardous event

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The goal of Functional Safety is **not** to eliminate risk (which is impossible), but to bring it down to a **tolerable level** — the level the society is willing to accept. This is called the **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** principle.

### 2.3 Risk Reduction — The Heart of Safety Engineering

The total risk of a process is the sum of three contributions:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{intrinsic}} + R_{\text{BPCS}} + R_{\text{SIS}}
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{intrinsic}}$ = Risk after inherent safe design (e.g., using less hazardous chemicals)
- $R_{\text{BPCS}}$ = Risk remaining after applying the Basic Process Control System
- $R_{\text{SIS}}$ = Risk remaining after applying the Safety Instrumented System

> [!NOTE]
> A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements whose purpose is to take the process to a safe state when predefined conditions are violated.

The **required risk reduction** is then:

$$
RR = \frac{R_{\text{after inherent design}}}{R_{\text{tolerable}}}
$$

This $RR$ value drives the determination of the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

### 2.4 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — The 4-Step Scale

A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is one of four discrete levels (SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4) that specify the required **probability of failure on demand (PFD)** of a safety function.

| SIL | PFD (Low Demand) | RRF (Risk Reduction Factor) | PFH (High Demand, per hour) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | $10$ to $100$ | $10^{-6} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$ |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | $100$ to $1{,}000$ | $10^{-7} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-6}$ |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | $1{,}000$ to $10{,}000$ | $10^{-8} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | $10{,}000$ to $100{,}000$ | $10^{-9} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-8}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for SIL → PFD:** *Higher the SIL, LOWER the PFD.* SIL 4 is the strictest — it must fail less than 1 time in 100,000 demands. Automotive (ISO 26262) instead uses **ASIL A through D**, where **ASIL D** is the most stringent.

### 2.5 Failure Categories

Functional Safety classifies failures very carefully because **some failures are detectable and some are not**:

$$
\text{Probability of Failure} = \lambda_{\text{DU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{SD}}
$$

| Failure Type | Meaning | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **$\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ (Dangerous Undetected)** | Failure that is dangerous AND not detected by diagnostics | Most dangerous — must be kept very low |
| **$\lambda_{\text{DD}}$ (Dangerous Detected)** | Failure that is dangerous BUT detected by diagnostics | Acceptable if diagnostic coverage is high |
| **$\lambda_{\text{SU}}$ (Safe Undetected)** | Failure that causes a safe trip but is not detected | Acceptable, may cause nuisance trips |
| **$\lambda_{\text{SD}}$ (Safe Detected)** | Failure that causes a safe trip and is detected | Best category — system is safe AND aware |

> [!NOTE]
> The average **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** for a single channel is dominated by the **DU** term, because detected failures are handled by the diagnostic system. KTU questions often ask you to compute the overall PFD for a redundant system.

### 2.6 Redundancy & Diagnostic Coverage

Two structural parameters define safety architecture:

- **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** The number of faults a system can tolerate *and still perform its safety function*.
  - HFT = 0 → 1oo1 (1-out-of-1) — single channel
  - HFT = 1 → 1oo2 or 2oo2 — dual channel
  - HFT = 1 → 1oo2D — dual channel with diagnostics

- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** The fraction of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostics.

$$
\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU favourite question type: *"A 1oo2D system has DC = 90%. Compute the effective PFD."* The standard formula is:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} \approx 2 \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2
$$

Where $T_{\text{proof}}$ is the proof test interval.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Risk | $R = f \times C$ | Dimensionless (rank) |
| PFD for 1oo1 | $\text{PFD} \approx \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} / 2$ | Per demand, dimensionless |
| PFD for 1oo2 | $\text{PFD} \approx 2 \cdot (\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} / 2)^2$ | Per demand, dimensionless |
| PFD for 1oo2D | $\text{PFD} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \text{DC})^2 \cdot (\lambda \cdot T)^2$ | Diagnostic-aware |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$ | Unitless |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $\text{DC} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} / (\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}})$ | Fraction (0 to 1) |
| Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = 1 / \lambda$ | Hours |
| PFH to PFD conversion (1 year) | $\text{PFD} \approx \text{PFH} \cdot T_{\text{mission}}$ | For high/continuous demand |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **PFD** (low-demand mode, $\leq 1$ demand/year) with **PFH** (high/continuous demand mode, $> 1$ demand/year). The numerical targets for the *same SIL* are *very different*. Always check which mode the question refers to.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** Airbag control unit, Electronic Stability Program (ESP), brake-by-wire, autonomous emergency braking.
- **Process Industry:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems, burner management systems, fire & gas detection.
- **Medical:** Infusion pumps, ventilators, dialysis machines — IEC 60601 / IEC 62304.
- **Railway:** Automatic Train Protection (ATP), interlocking systems.
- **Aerospace:** Flight Control Systems, autoland — DO-178C / DO-254.
- **Industrial Robots:** ISO 10218 (collaborative robots safety).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PFD for a 1oo1 (Single Channel) System

A 1oo1 system has **one** sensor, **one** logic solver, **one** final element. The safety function is performed by a single channel.

**Step 1 — Failure rate assumption**

For an exponential failure distribution, the failure rate is constant:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda_{\text{DU}} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of survival up to time $t$**

The reliability function is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of failure on demand (assuming a demand occurs uniformly during the proof test interval)**

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — For small $\lambda t$ (typical safety case, $\lambda t \ll 1$)**

Use the Taylor series expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2} - \dots$

$$
\text{PFD} = 1 - \left(1 - \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2} - \dots\right)
$$

Neglecting higher-order terms:

$$
\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from the fact that the *average* time since the last proof test is $T/2$, not $T$. The demand is equally likely to occur anywhere in the interval.

### 3.2 Derivation: PFD for a 1oo2 (Dual Channel, Voted) System

A 1oo2 system has **two independent channels**, both must fail for the system to fail (this is *voting logic*: 1-out-of-2). The system is highly available but has lower diagnostic capability than 1oo2D.

**Step 1 — System fails only if BOTH channels fail**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \text{PFD}_A \times \text{PFD}_B
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the 1oo1 expression for each channel**

For identical channels ($\lambda_A = \lambda_B = \lambda$):

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \left(\frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T}{2}\right) \times \left(\frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$
\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare the two architectures numerically. If $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 10^{-6}$/h and $T = 1$ year $\approx 8{,}760$ h:
>
> - 1oo1: $\text{PFD} = (10^{-6} \times 8760) / 2 = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ → SIL 2
> - 1oo2: $\text{PFD} = 2 \times (4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 3.84 \times 10^{-5}$ → SIL 3

### 3.3 Derivation: PFD for a 1oo2D (Dual Channel with Diagnostics)

A 1oo2D system has two channels AND high diagnostic coverage. Diagnostic coverage **dramatically reduces** the effective dangerous-undetected failure rate.

**Step 1 — Effective DU rate after diagnostics**

$$
\lambda_{\text{DU,eff}} = \lambda \cdot (1 - \text{DC})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into 1oo2 formula**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} \approx 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$
\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \text{DC})^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example — End-to-End Risk to SIL Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (typical 14-mark KTU question):** A chemical reactor operates at 450 °C and 30 bar. A temperature sensor and a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) must shut down the heater if temperature exceeds 500 °C.
>
> **Given:**
> - Failure rate of temperature sensor: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
> - Proof test interval: $T_{\text{proof}} = 1$ year
> - System architecture: 1oo2D with DC = 90%
> - Mode: Low demand

**Step 1 — Identify mode**

Mode is *low demand* (SIF trips on demand, not continuously). So we use **PFD**, not PFH.

**Step 2 — Apply 1oo2D formula**

$$
\text{PFD} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \text{DC})^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda \cdot T}{2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

T = 1 year = 8760 hours.

$$
\frac{\lambda \cdot T}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219
$$

$$
\text{PFD} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - 0.9)^2 \cdot (0.0219)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each factor**

$$
(1 - 0.9)^2 = (0.1)^2 = 0.01
$$

$$
(0.0219)^2 = 4.7961 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\text{PFD} \approx 2 \times 0.01 \times 4.7961 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result**

$$
\boxed{\text{PFD} \approx 9.59 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 6 — Determine SIL**

Compare to the table:

- SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ → not met
- SIL 4 requires PFD $< 10^{-4}$ → this is $9.59 \times 10^{-6}$, which is between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$ → **SIL 4** ✅

**Step 7 — Compute Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{9.59 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{5}
$$

This means the safety function reduces risk by approximately **104,000 times**.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Architecture Selection

| Required SIL | Recommended Architecture | HFT | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | 1oo1 or 2oo2 | 0 | Small furnaces, lab equipment |
| SIL 2 | 1oo2D or 2oo3 | 1 | Burner management, ESD for low hazard |
| SIL 3 | 1oo2D or 2oo3 with high DC | 1 or 2 | Refinery reactors, railway signalling |
| SIL 4 | 2oo3 or 1oo3 with very high DC + proof testing | 2 | Nuclear shutdown, large turbogenerators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **voting logic** notation "MooN" means *M out of N* channels must trip for the system to trip:
> - 1oo2 → trip if *any 1* of 2 channels trips (high availability, low spurious trip cost)
> - 2oo2 → trip only if *both* of 2 channels trip (high security, may miss single failures)
> - 2oo3 → trip if *any 2* of 3 channels trip (balance of availability and security)

### 3.6 The Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61508 Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> The IEC 61508 safety lifecycle consists of **16 phases** (numbered 1 to 16), grouped into three phases:
> 1. **Analysis Phases (1–5):** Concept, scope definition, hazard & risk analysis, overall safety requirements.
> 2. **Realization Phases (6–13):** Allocation to E/E/PE systems, design, integration, validation, installation, commissioning.
> 3. **Operation Phases (14–16):** Operation, maintenance, modification, decommissioning.
>
> KTU students are expected to draw this as a flow diagram in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Safety — High-Level Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant or Machine] --> B[Identify Hazards HAZOP HAZID]
    B --> C[Estimate Risk f x C]
    C --> D{Risk Acceptable}
    D -- No --> E[Apply Risk Reduction]
    E --> F[Safety Function SIF / SIS]
    F --> G[Calculate PFD or PFH]
    G --> H[Determine SIL 1 to 4]
    H --> I[Choose Architecture 1oo1 1oo2D 2oo3]
    I --> J[Validate Test Commission]
    J --> K[Safe State Achieved]
    D -- Yes --> L[No Additional Safety Needed]
```

### 4.2 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor A] --> LS[Safety Logic Solver PLC or Relay]
    S2[Sensor B] --> LS
    LS --> FE1[Final Element A Valve]
    LS --> FE2[Final Element B Valve]
    FE1 --> OUT[Process to Safe State]
    FE2 --> OUT
    DIAG[Diagnostics and Self Test] -.-> LS
    DIAG -.-> S1
    DIAG -.-> S2
```

### 4.3 Risk Reduction Strategy (Layer of Protection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Inherent Safe Design] --> L1[Process Design and Chemistry]
    L1 --> L2[BPCS Basic Process Control]
    L2 --> L3[Alarms and Operator Intervention]
    L3 --> L4[SIS Safety Instrumented System]
    L4 --> L5[Mechanical Protection Relief Valves]
    L5 --> L6[Emergency Response]
    L6 --> RESULT[Residual Risk Below Tolerable Limit]
```

### 4.4 Safety Lifecycle Phases (IEC 61508)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Concept] --> P2[Phase 2 Scope]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Hazard and Risk Analysis]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Overall Safety Requirements]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Safety Requirements Allocation]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6 E/E/PE System Design]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7 Software Design]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 8 Validation]
    P8 --> P9[Phase 9 Installation Commissioning]
    P9 --> P10[Phase 10 Operation]
    P10 --> P11[Phase 11 Maintenance]
    P11 --> P12[Phase 12 Modification]
    P12 --> P13[Phase 13 Decommissioning]
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree — PFD and SIL Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Dangerous Failure Rate lambda DU] --> Q2[Proof Test Interval T]
    Q2 --> Q3[Architecture 1oo1 1oo2 1oo2D 2oo3]
    Q3 --> Q4[Diagnostic Coverage DC]
    Q4 --> Q5[Compute PFD]
    Q5 --> Q6{Compare with SIL Table}
    Q6 -- 10-2 to 10-1 --> R1[SIL 1]
    Q6 -- 10-3 to 10-2 --> R2[SIL 2]
    Q6 -- 10-4 to 10-3 --> R3[SIL 3]
    Q6 -- 10-5 to 10-4 --> R4[SIL 4]
```

### 4.6 Failure Categorisation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Failure Event] --> F1[Dangerous]
    F --> F2[Safe]
    F1 --> F1A[Undetected DU]
    F1 --> F1B[Detected DD]
    F2 --> F2A[Undetected SU]
    F2 --> F2B[Detected SD]
    F1A --> X[Highest Risk Addressed by PFD]
    F1B --> Y[Handled by Diagnostic Shutdown]
    F2A --> Z[Causes Nuisance Trip]
    F2B --> W[Safe and Informative]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define Functional Safety. How is it different from reliability?**
*(3 Marks — CO1, Remember level)*

**Model Answer:**
Functional Safety is the part of the overall safety that depends on a system or equipment operating correctly in response to its inputs, including safe management of operator errors, hardware failures, and environmental changes (IEC 61508 definition).
*Reliability* is the probability that a system performs its intended function **without failure over time**. It is about *preventing* failures.
*Functional Safety* is about *what happens* when a failure occurs — it ensures the system automatically transitions to a **safe state** even if a component has failed. A system can be highly reliable but not functionally safe, and vice versa.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q2. List the four Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) and their corresponding Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) range in low-demand mode.**
*(3 Marks — CO1, Remember level)*

**Model Answer:**
In low-demand mode of operation, the SIL vs PFD targets are:

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Module 1 Choice Question]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
