| Aspect | Random Hardware Failure | Systematic Failure |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Physical degradation mechanisms | Design / specification / process error |
| Predictability of *occurrence* | Unpredictable | Predictable (deterministic) |
| Predictability of *rate* | Predictable (constant $\lambda$) | Cannot be quantified by $\lambda$ |
| Mitigation | Redundancy, proof testing | Design change, quality control, diversity |
| Example | Capacitor dielectric breakdown | Wrong firmware revision installed |

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Explain the bathtub curve. Why is Region II the relevant region for SIL calculation?**

**Model Answer:**
The bathtub curve plots the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operational time for a hardware population and has three regions:
- **Region I (Infant Mortality):** Decreasing $\lambda$, dominated by manufacturing defects. Mitigation by burn-in.
- **Region II (Useful Life):** Constant $\lambda$, dominated by random intrinsic failure mechanisms.
- **Region III (Wear-out):** Increasing $\lambda$, dominated by aging and fatigue. Mitigation by preventive replacement.

**Region II is used for SIL calculations** because in this region the failure rate is constant, which justifies the exponential distribution assumption ($R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$). This mathematically tractable assumption is the foundation of all PFD and PFH formulas used in IEC 61508/61511.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(7 Marks)** Explain the four categories of failure rates ($\lambda_{SD}, \lambda_{SU}, \lambda_{DD}, \lambda_{DU}$) with a suitable diagram. Derive the expressions for Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) and Diagnostic Coverage (DC).

**Model Answer:**

Every random hardware failure in a safety-related system can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **Consequence axis:** Is the failure *Safe* (no hazard) or *Dangerous* (prevents safety function)?
2. **Detection axis:** Is the failure *Detected* automatically by on-line diagnostics, or does it remain *Undetected* until a proof test?

The combination yields **four mutually exclusive failure rate categories**:

| Symbol | Category | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda_{SD}$ | Safe Detected | Safe failure, discovered by diagnostics |
| $\lambda_{SU}$ | Safe Undetected | Safe failure, hidden until proof test |
| $\lambda_{DD}$ | Dangerous Detected | Dangerous failure, caught immediately |
| $\lambda_{DU}$ | Dangerous Undetected | Dangerous failure, hidden — **most critical** |

**Mathematical relationships:**

The total safe failure rate and total dangerous failure rate are given by:

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}, \qquad \lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

The total failure rate of the channel is:

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_D = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

**Derivation of Diagnostic Coverage (DC):**
DC is the fraction of dangerous failures that are detected by automatic diagnostics:

$$\boxed{\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}}$$

It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%).

**Derivation of Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):**
SFF is the fraction of total failures (safe + dangerous) that are either *safe* or *dangerous-but-detected* (i.e., do not lead to a hidden dangerous failure):

$$\boxed{\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}}$$

SFF is also a dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\lambda_{DU}$ is the "king" parameter:** It is the only failure category that contributes to undetected dangerous failure. Every other category either is safe or is caught by diagnostics. Hence $\lambda_{DU}$ appears in **every** PFD and PFH formula.

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`
**(7 Marks)** A temperature sensor used in a SIF has the following failure data:
$\lambda_S = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /h, $\lambda_D = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ /h, $\lambda_{DD} = 7 \times 10^{-7}$ /h.
The proof-test interval is $T = 4380$ h (6 months). Compute (i) $\lambda_{DU}$, (ii) SFF, (iii) DC, and (iv) PFD_avg. Identify the achieved SIL.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Computation of $\lambda_{DU}$:**

$$\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D - \lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-7} - 7 \times 10^{-7} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /h}$$

**(ii) Computation of SFF:**

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6} + 8 \times 10^{-7} = 3.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

$$\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = 1 - \frac{1 \times 10^{-7}}{3.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 - 0.0263 = 0.9737 \;\;(97.37\%)$$

**(iii) Computation of DC:**

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{7 \times 10^{-7}}{8 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.875 \;\;(87.5\%)$$

**(iv) Computation of PFD_avg:**

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = (1 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{4380}{2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = (1 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 2190 = 2.19 \times 10^{-4}$$

**SIL Determination (IEC 61511-1, Low-Demand Mode):**

| SIL | PFD_range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **SIL 3** | **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$** |

Since $2.19 \times 10^{-4}$ lies between $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-3}$, the SIF achieves **SIL 3** (with margin).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2021]`
**(7 Marks)** Derive the relationship between MTTF, MTBF, MTTR, and the failure rate $\lambda$. Why is MTBF an inappropriate term for non-repairable systems?

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average operational time until the first (and final) failure for a *non-repairable* component.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time taken to diagnose, repair, and restore a failed *repairable* system.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average time interval between two consecutive failures for a *repairable* system in its useful life.

**Derivation of MTTF = $1/\lambda$:**

Assuming constant failure rate (Region II), the time-to-failure $T$ follows the exponential distribution with PDF $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. MTTF is the expected value:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using integration by parts (let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$):

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t \, e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = 0 + \left[ -\frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_0^{\infty}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Derivation of MTBF:**

For a *repairable* system in steady-state, the cycle time between failures equals the operational time plus the repair time:

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Why MTBF is inappropriate for non-repairable systems:**

A non-repairable system (e.g., a sensor cartridge, an IC chip, a fuse) is replaced *after* failure rather than repaired. Its lifetime ends at the first failure — there is no "time between failures" because there is only one failure event. Using MTBF for such a system would conceptually require an MTTR, which is undefined. Therefore the correct term is **MTTF**, not MTBF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write MTTF = MTBF. This is **only true when MTTR ≈ 0** (instantaneous repair). For practical systems MTTR is finite (often 8–24 hours), so MTTF is always *less than* MTBF. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2021]`
**(7 Marks)** A shut-off valve in a chemical plant has a failure rate of $\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ /h. It operates for 8760 hours per year. Calculate (i) the reliability after 1 year, (ii) the probability of failure within the first year, (iii) the MTTF in years.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Reliability after 1 year ($t = 8760$ h):**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(4 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8760)}$$

$$R(8760) = e^{-0.03504}$$

$$R(8760) = 0.9656 \quad (\text{i.e., } 96.56\%)$$

**(ii) Probability of failure within 1 year:**

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - 0.9656 = 0.0344 \quad (\text{i.e., } 3.44\%)$$

Equivalently:
$$F(8760) = 1 - e^{-0.03504} = 0.0344$$

**(iii) MTTF in years:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}} = 250{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

Converting to years:
$$\text{MTTF}_{years} = \frac{250{,}000}{8760} \approx 28.54 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** $\lambda t = 0.035 \ll 1$, so the Taylor approximation $F(t) \approx \lambda t = 0.035$ is very close to the exact 0.0344. This validates that for low failure rates the approximation $F(t) \approx \lambda t$ is excellent — a fact KTU examiners love to test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit mixing:** Using $\lambda$ in /h but $t$ in years without conversion. Always convert to a common unit first.
> 2. **Forgetting the Taylor approximation:** Writing $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ but then unable to simplify when $\lambda t$ is small.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda_S$, $\lambda_D$ vs $\lambda_{SD}$, $\lambda_{SU}$, $\lambda_{DD}$, $\lambda_{DU}$:** The subscripts matter. A "safe-detected" failure is *very different* from a "dangerous-undetected" failure for SIL purposes.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** PFD_avg uses $T/2$, not $T$. This is because the failure risk *averages* over the proof-test interval — using $T$ would be the worst case.
> 5. **MTBF vs MTTF:** Using MTBF for a sensor or non-repairable component is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Random Hardware Failure** = unpredictable in time, predictable in rate (constant $\lambda$ in useful life).
- [x] **Systematic Failure** = deterministic design/process flaw; cannot be reduced by redundancy.
- [x] **The exponential law** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the foundation of all SIL math.
- [x] **Four failure categories** — $\lambda_{SD}, \lambda_{SU}, \lambda_{DD}, \lambda_{DU}$; only $\lambda_{DU}$ drives PFD/PFH.
- [x] **MTTF = $1/\lambda$** (non-repairable); **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** (repairable).
- [x] **SFF** $= 1 - \lambda_{DU}/\lambda_{total}$; **DC** $= \lambda_{DD}/\lambda_D$.
- [x] **PFD_avg** $\approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T/2$ for a single channel with proof-test interval $T$.
- [x] **Series failure rates add:** $\lambda_{series} = \sum \lambda_i$.
- [x] **Bathtub curve:** Region II (constant $\lambda$) is the SIL-calculation zone.
- [x] **Units discipline:** $\lambda$ in per hour, $t$ in hours, $\text{PFD}_{avg}$ dimensionless — always convert before computing.
- [x] **SIL Tables (IEC 61511, Low Demand):** SIL 1 → $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4 → $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- [x] **High Demand Mode uses PFH** (per hour) instead of PFD (dimensionless): SIL 3 → $10^{-8} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$.
- [x] **Diagnostic Coverage** is what makes smart sensors "smart" — without it, every dangerous failure stays in the $\lambda_{DU}$ bucket.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Insight:** Module 1 is the *language foundation* of the entire course. Every later module — architectures, SIF design, SIL verification, ALARP — assumes you are fluent in the four-$\lambda$ taxonomy and the exponential reliability model. Master these and the rest of the syllabus becomes a layered extension.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systematic Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Systematic Failures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (IEC 61508 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Systematic Failure (as per IEC 61508:2010, Clause 3.4.5):** > A failure related in a deterministic way to a certain cause, which can only be eliminated by a modification of the design or of the manufacturing process, operational procedures, documentation, or other relevant factors. In simpler KTU-exam-ready words: > A **systematic failure** is a deterministic, repeatable failure that originates from a *built-in flaw* in the system — such as a design bug, a manufacturing defect, a documentation error, or an incorrect operational procedure. It is **NOT** caused by wear-and-tear or probabilistic component degradation. ## 1.2 The Two Families of Failure in Functional Safety Functional safety engineering (per IEC 61508) categorizes *every* failure into exactly two families: | Failure Family | Root Cause | Nature | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **Random Hardware Failure** | Physical degradation of components (aging, drift, overstress) | Stochastic (probabilistic) | Redundancy, diagnostics, periodic proof testing | | **Systematic Failure** | Human/engineering error during lifecycle phases | Deterministic (built-in) | Quality & process controls, competence management | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Typo in a Recipe" Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Family Recipe Book** > > Imagine your grandmother's recipe book. Two kinds of problems can ruin the dish: > - **Random Failure** = An egg goes bad because it was sitting in the fridge too long (unpredictable, physical decay). > - **Systematic Failure** = The recipe says "add 5 tablespoons of salt instead of sugar." No matter how many times you bake, the cake will be salty. The error is *designed into the recipe*. The only fix is to **rewrite the recipe** (modify the design). > > Systematic failures are like the typo — they will *always* reproduce under the same conditions and cannot be detected by "running it again and hoping for the best." ## 1.4 Why Systematic Failures Matter in Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) In a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), a systematic failure that prevents the protective action (e.g., a valve failing to close on demand) can directly lead to a hazardous event. Unlike random failures, **no amount of component-level redundancy will eliminate a systematic fault** if the same design error is replicated across all redundant channels. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Defining Characteristics of Systematic Failure - **Deterministic:** Same cause + same condition → same failure, *every time*. - **Latent until revealed:** The fault is *present* from day one but may only *manifest* under a specific input combination or operating state. - **Non-reducible by testing alone:** Proof tests can detect them once they occur, but cannot *prevent* them. - **Lifecycle-spanning:** Can be introduced at *any* phase — concept, design, manufacture, installation, operation, maintenance, modification, decommissioning. - **Common-cause coupling:** Systematic failures often defeat diversity/redundancy because they affect all channels simultaneously (e.g., identical software bug in all 2oo3 voters). ## 2.2 Classification of Systematic Failures (KTU-Exam Framework) Per IEC 61508, systematic failures are categorized by their **lifecycle phase of origin**: | Lifecycle Phase | Example of Systematic Fault | Avoidance Measure (per IEC 61508) | |---|---|---| | **Specification** | Wrong SIF specification (e.g., setpoint too high) | Competent engineering team; formal reviews | | **Design** | Incorrect logic solver algorithm | Use of certified design tools; design reviews | | **Implementation** | Software coding bug (off-by-one) | Coding standards (e.g., MISRA-C); static analysis | | **Manufacturing** | Wrong resistor value soldered on PCB | Quality control; traceability; audit | | **Installation** | Cable routed near high-EMI source | Site survey; installation procedures | | **Operation** | Operator follows wrong procedure | Operator training; clear HMI | | **Maintenance** | Bypassed interlock for convenience | Permit-to-work systems; audits | ## 2.3 Systematic Capability (per IEC 61508-2/3, Clause 4) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Systematic Capability (SC)** is a *measure* (expressed as a Safety Integrity Level: SC 1 to SC 4) of the confidence that the systematic safety integrity of an element meets the requirements of the given SIL. **Key Points:** - SC N is claimed for an element *only if* all the requirements for that level of rigour have been satisfied in the **avoidance** and **control** of systematic failures. - SC rating is tied to **process rigor**, not component reliability numbers. - A vendor claiming "SC 3" must demonstrate compliance with all IEC 61508 clauses mapped to SC 3. ## 2.4 Avoidance vs. Control of Systematic Failures (Core KTU Concept) IEC 61508 demands a **dual strategy**: 1. **Avoidance** — Reduce the *probability* of introducing a systematic fault (rigorous processes). 2. **Control** — Detect and *eliminate* systematic faults that have slipped in (verification, validation, diagnostics). > **Quality of process used to detect faults must, in general, be commensurate with the severity of consequences.** *(IEC 61508 principle)* ## 2.5 IEC 61508 Required Techniques for Systematic Failure Avoidance (Clause 7) For a typical KTU 14-mark question, the examiner expects knowledge of: - **Project Management** — Plans, lifecycles (V-model), documentation control. - **Documentation Standards** — Clear, traceable, version-controlled docs. - **Use of Qualified Personnel** — Competence matrices. - **Use of Suitable Tools** — Certified compilers, validated design tools. - **Structured Design** — Modular, hierarchical, with defined interfaces. - **Use of Coding Standards** — e.g., MISRA-C for safety software. - **Static Analysis** — Code review, abstract interpretation. - **Dynamic Analysis / Testing** — Unit, integration, system tests. - **Functional Testing** — Black-box / white-box. - **Failure Analysis** — FMEA, FTA applied during design. - **Verification & Validation (V&V)** — Independent of designer. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Expression | Description | |---|---|---| | Failure Rate (random) | $\lambda$ | Failures per hour (1/h) | | PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour) | $PFH$ | Top-line random-failure metric | | Systematic Capability | $SC\ N$ | $N \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ per IEC 61508 | | Safe Failure Fraction (related) | $SFF$ | Dimensionless ratio (0–1) | | Hardware Fault Tolerance | $HFT$ | Integer ≥ 0 | | Determinism check | $\Pr[F \mid C] = 1$ | Under same Cause $C$, failure $F$ always occurs | | Independence (systematic) | $\bot_{sys}$ | Independence from *systematic* common-cause sources | | Required Safety Integrity | $SIL\ N$ | $N \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ mapped to PFH ranges | > [!NOTE] > **Important boundary condition:** The systematic-failure contribution is **not** captured directly in $\lambda$ (failure-rate) equations. It is controlled *separately* through SC and lifecycle rigor. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** ISO 26262 (derived from IEC 61508) treats systematic faults via ASIL ratings. - **Aerospace:** DO-178C requires "Design Assurance Level" (DAL) for software. - **Process Industry:** A wrong P&ID (piping & instrumentation diagram) is a *systematic* hazard — caught only by HAZOP/LOPA review. - **Medical Devices:** IEC 62304 governs systematic software safety. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Logical Derivation: Why Redundancy Fails Against Systematic Faults We will prove (analytically) that duplication cannot eliminate systematic failures when channels share the same design. ### Setup Let a 1oo2 redundant system have two identical channels $A$ and $B$. Each channel has a *random* failure rate $\lambda_R$ and a *systematic* failure probability $\lambda_S$ (a probability per demand that the design flaw manifests). ### Step 1 — Random-Failure Performance The probability that *both* channels fail due to **random** hardware causes (assumed independent) over mission time $T$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{R,\text{both fail}} &= (1 - e^{-\lambda_R T}) \cdot (1 - e^{-\lambda_R T}) \end{aligned} $$ For small $\lambda_R T$ (high-reliability regime): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{R,\text{both fail}} &\approx (\lambda_R T)^2 \end{aligned} $$ This term can be made arbitrarily small by reducing $\lambda_R$ or using diagnostics — **redundancy helps.** ### Step 2 — Systematic-Failure Performance Let $p_S$ be the probability that the systematic design flaw *manifests* on a given demand in one channel. Because the channels are **identical**, the design flaw is correlated: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{S,\text{both fail}} &= p_S \quad \text{(NOT } p_S^2 \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Probability of Dangerous Failure (Per Demand) $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{1oo2} &\approx \underbrace{(\lambda_R T)^2}_{\text{random, reducible}} + \underbrace{p_S}_{\text{systematic, irreducible by redundancy}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation - The term $(\lambda_R T)^2$ tends to zero as redundancy increases. - The term $p_S$ **does NOT shrink** no matter how many identical channels we add. - Therefore: **redundancy only mitigates random failures; systematic failures require process/quality controls.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion of derivation:** > $$\boxed{PFD_{1ooN} \approx \underbrace{(\lambda_R T)^N}_{\text{random — diminishes}} + \underbrace{p_S}_{\text{systematic — persists}}}$$ > For large $N$, $PFD$ is *dominated* by $p_S$. This is the foundational justification for SC ratings. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Avoidance Techniques Mapped to SIL A frequently tested KTU grid — recall the mappings below. | Technique (IEC 61508-3, Table 5 / Annex A) | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | Formal project management | HR | HR | HR | HR | | Structured specification (semi-formal methods) | R | HR | HR | HR | | Structured design (modular) | R | HR | HR | HR | | Use of coding standards | R | HR | HR | HR | | Static analysis | R | HR | HR | HR | | Dynamic analysis / testing | R | R | HR | HR | | Functional / black-box testing | R | HR | HR | HR | | Performance testing | R | R | HR | HR | | Back-to-back testing | – | R | R | HR | | Failure analysis (FMEA/FTA) | R | R | HR | HR | *Legend:* **HR** = Highly Recommended; **R** = Recommended; **–** = Not required. ## 3.3 The Avoidance + Control Mathematical Model (Conceptual) The residual systematic failure probability can be modeled as: $$ \begin{aligned} p_S^{residual} &= p_S^{intro} \cdot (1 - D_{avoid}) \cdot (1 - D_{control}) \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $p_S^{intro}$ = Probability the fault is *introduced* in a given lifecycle phase. - $D_{avoid} \in [0,1]$ = Fraction *avoided* by process rigor (reviews, standards). - $D_{control} \in [0,1]$ = Fraction *detected-and-removed* by verification, V&V, diagnostics. For higher SIL, both $D_{avoid}$ and $D_{control}$ must approach **1**, demanding exponentially more rigorous process (hence the term *"systematic capability"*). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lifecycle View of Where Systematic Failures Are Introduced ```mermaid flowchart LR concept["CONCEPT
specification errors"] design["DESIGN
logic / architectural flaws"] impl["IMPLEMENTATION
coding bugs"] mfg["MANUFACTURE
wrong component"] install["INSTALLATION
wiring error"] operate["OPERATION
wrong procedure"] maintain["MAINTENANCE
bypassed interlock"] modify["MODIFICATION
uncontrolled change"] concept --> design design --> impl impl --> mfg mfg --> install install --> operate operate --> maintain maintain --> modify modify --> design review["AVOIDANCE
reviews, standards,
qualified personnel"] verify["CONTROL
V&V, testing,
diagnostics"] review -.guards.-> concept review -.guards.-> design review -.guards.-> impl verify -.verifies.-> impl verify -.verifies.-> mfg verify -.verifies.-> install ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Avoidance + Control Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph lifecycle["LIFECYCLE PHASES"] p1["Specification"] p2["Design"] p3["Implementation"] p4["Integration & Test"] p5["Operation & Maintenance"] end subgraph avoidance["AVOIDANCE LAYER"] a1["Process rigor"] a2["Coding standards"] a3["Design reviews"] a4["Competence management"] end subgraph control["CONTROL LAYER"] c1["V&V activities"] c2["Diagnostic tests"] c3["Independent assessment"] c4["Change management"] end subgraph outcome["OUTCOME"] out["SC N achieved
Systematic Capability rating"] end lifecycle --> avoidance lifecycle --> control avoidance --> out control --> out ``` ## 4.3 Systematic vs Random Failure Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Failure observed"] q1{"Failure caused by
design / process / human error?"} q2{"Failure caused by
physical degradation?"} sys["SYSTEMATIC FAILURE
Action: Modify design/process"] rand["RANDOM HARDWARE FAILURE
Action: Redundancy / Diagnostics / Proof test"] start --> q1 q1 -- Yes --> sys q1 -- No --> q2 q2 -- Yes --> rand q2 -- No --> sys ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Where Systematic Failures Inject into a SIF ```mermaid flowchart LR sensor1["SENSOR 1
hardware design"] sensor2["SENSOR 2
hardware design"] sensor3["SENSOR 3
hardware design"] logic["LOGIC SOLVER
software & firmware"] final["FINAL ELEMENT
valve & actuator"] sysf1["Systematic fault:
common firmware bug"] sysf2["Systematic fault:
identical spec error"] sysf3["Systematic fault:
shared installation mistake"] sysf1 -.affects all.-> sensor1 sysf1 -.affects all.-> sensor2 sysf1 -.affects all.-> sensor3 sysf2 -.affects all.-> sensor1 sysf2 -.affects all.-> sensor2 sysf2 -.affects all.-> sensor3 sysf3 -.affects all.-> sensor1 sysf3 -.affects all.-> sensor2 sysf3 -.affects all.-> sensor3 sysf1 -.affects.-> logic sysf2 -.affects.-> final ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define systematic failure. How is it different from random hardware failure?** **Model Answer:** > **Systematic failure:** A failure that is related in a deterministic way to a certain cause, which can only be eliminated by a modification of the design, manufacturing process, operational procedure, or documentation (IEC 61508:2010, Clause 3.4.5). > > **Differences:** > - *Cause:* Systematic → human/process error. Random → physical degradation of components. > - *Predictability:* Systematic → deterministic (will reproduce). Random → stochastic (probabilistic). > - *Reduction method:* Systematic → process controls, avoidance, control measures. Random → redundancy, diagnostics, proof testing. > - *Quantification:* Random failures are expressed via failure rates ($\lambda$). Systematic failures are *not* quantified by $\lambda$ but are controlled via Systematic Capability (SC) ratings. *(3 marks — full and crisp)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is Systematic Capability (SC)? Why is it not expressed in failure-rate units?** **Model Answer:** > **Systematic Capability (SC):** A measure (on a 1-to-4 scale) of confidence that the systematic safety integrity of an element meets the requirements of a stated SIL (IEC 61508-2/-3, Clause 4). > > **Why not in failure-rate units:** Systematic failures are *deterministic* — they either will or will not manifest given a particular cause. They cannot be modeled as Poisson processes; assigning a $\lambda$ would falsely imply a probabilistic occurrence. Instead, SC captures the **rigor of the lifecycle process** that prevents and detects such faults. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **one full 14-mark question with internal choice** is given. You answer EITHER **Q-A** OR **Q-B**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified]* **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **lifecycle phases** in which systematic failures can be introduced into a Safety Instrumented System. List any **six avoidance techniques** recommended by IEC 61508. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Derive (with appropriate reasoning) why **redundancy alone cannot eliminate systematic failures** in identical channels. Support your derivation with a final expression. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a)** *Systematic failures can be introduced at every phase of the SIS lifecycle. Refer to the figure in Section 4.1 of these notes for the schematic.* **Phases (referencing Section 2.2 table):** 1. **Specification phase** – Incorrectly defining the SIF (e.g., wrong shut-down setpoint). 2. **Design phase** – Wrong architecture, flawed logic solver algorithm. 3. **Implementation phase** – Software coding bugs, hardware interface errors. 4. **Manufacturing phase** – Wrong component soldered, calibration drift. 5. **Installation phase** – Cable routing near EMI sources, improper grounding. 6. **Operation phase** – Operator follows wrong procedure, misunderstanding alarms. 7. **Maintenance phase** – Bypassed interlock for convenience, skipped proof test. 8. **Modification phase** – Uncontrolled change that re-introduces faults. **Six IEC 61508 avoidance techniques (Clause 7):** 1. **Project management** – documented lifecycle, plan, change control. 2. **Documentation standards** – clear, version-controlled specifications. 3. **Use of qualified personnel** – competence management. 4. **Use of suitable tools** – validated compilers and design tools. 5. **Structured design** – modular hierarchy with defined interfaces. 6. **Coding standards** – e.g., MISRA-C for safety software. 7. *(Bonus)* Static analysis, FMEA/FTA, independent V&V. **(b)** *Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.* For an $1ooN$ identical-channel redundant system: $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{1ooN} &\approx (\lambda_R T)^N + p_S \end{aligned} $$ - The first term $(\lambda_R T)^N$ corresponds to **random hardware failures** and *decreases* as $N$ increases (assuming independence). Redundancy helps here. - The second term $p_S$ corresponds to the **probability of the systematic design flaw manifesting on demand**. Because the channels are identical, the systematic fault is *common-cause correlated*; both channels exhibit the same flaw with probability $p_S$, not $p_S^2$. - Therefore, $PFD$ is bounded below by $p_S$ regardless of how many identical redundant channels are added. - **Conclusion:** Redundancy *alone* cannot eliminate systematic failures. They must be addressed through **process rigor, design diversity, and verification/validation** — i.e., the systematic capability (SC) framework of IEC 61508. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Explain the **Avoidance and Control** strategy of systematic failures as mandated by IEC 61508. Differentiate between the two. **[7 marks]** **(b)** What is **Systematic Capability (SC)**? Discuss the **role of competence management, coding standards, and verification & validation** in achieving SC 3. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution **(a)** **Avoidance vs. Control of Systematic Failures:** | Aspect | Avoidance | Control | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | *Prevent* the fault from being introduced | *Detect and remove* faults that slipped in | | **Means** | Process rigor: coding standards, design reviews, qualified personnel, structured design, use of certified tools | V&V activities: static/dynamic analysis, functional testing, diagnostics, FMEA/FTA, independent assessment | | **Phase focus** | Early lifecycle (spec, design, implementation) | Throughout lifecycle, especially integration & operation | | **IEC 61508 mapping** | Clause 7 (lifecycle requirements) | Clause 7 + diagnostic coverage clauses | | **Effect on $p_S$** | Reduces $p_S^{intro}$ | Reduces *manifestation* via $(1-D_{control})$ | > The two strategies are **complementary**; neither alone is sufficient at higher SILs. **(b)** **Systematic Capability (SC):** Defined in Section 2.3 of these notes as a 1-to-4 scale of confidence in systematic safety integrity. **Role of three techniques in achieving SC 3:** 1. **Competence Management** – Establishes that only personnel with appropriate education, training, and experience participate in safety work. For SC 3, IEC 61508 expects demonstrable evidence (training records, certification). Reduces probability of human-introduced systematic faults. 2. **Coding Standards** – E.g., MISRA-C. Restricts unsafe constructs (e.g., dynamic memory allocation in safety code). For SC 3, the standard mandates use of a coding standard and adherence evidence. Eliminates whole classes of coding defects at source. 3. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** – Independent confirmation that each lifecycle phase's output meets its requirements. For SC 3, V&V must be done by personnel *independent of the designer*. Combines static analysis, dynamic analysis, functional testing, and back-to-back testing for higher SILs. > Together, these three techniques form the **engineering backbone** of SC 3 claims. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks on Systematic Failure questions:** > 1. **Confusing SC with SIL.** SC is an *element* rating of systematic integrity; SIL is a *function* rating of overall risk reduction. Both range 1–4 but represent different concepts. > 2. **Writing $\lambda_S$ as a "systematic failure rate."** Systematic failures are **deterministic**, not probabilistic. There is no $\lambda_S$. Express residual systematic risk through SC or via process metrics. > 3. **Listing techniques without grouping them** under Avoidance or Control. Examiners award marks for **classification**, not bare lists. > 4. **Omitting the duplicate-channel argument.** When asked "why does redundancy fail?", failing to mention *common-cause coupling* in identical channels is a guaranteed 3-mark loss. > 5. **Skipping the V&V independence criterion.** For SC 3+, the V&V *must be performed by an independent party*. Many students omit this and lose a mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Systematic failure = deterministic, repeatable, *design/process-induced* fault. - **Lifecycle-spanning:** Can be introduced at *any* phase — specification, design, implementation, manufacture, installation, operation, maintenance, modification. - **Not quantifiable by $\lambda$:** Express via Systematic Capability (SC 1–4). - **Two-pronged strategy:** **Avoidance** (prevent introduction) + **Control** (detect & remove). - **Redundancy limit:** Redundancy defeats *random* failures, NOT *systematic* ones, when channels share identical designs. - **Independent V&V** is mandatory for higher SC ratings. - **Coding standards** (MISRA-C, etc.) and **structured design** are the most effective avoidance tools in software-intensive SIFs. - **Human factors** — the most common root cause — are managed through competence management and procedural discipline. - **IEC 61508 cross-references:** Systematic failure is defined in Clause 3.4.5; systematic capability is addressed in Part 2 (Clause 4) and Part 3 (Annex A & Tables). - **Common derivative standards:** ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), EN 50128 (rail), DO-178C (aerospace) — all build on IEC 61508's systematic-failure philosophy. - **KTU exam keywords** to memorize: *deterministic, latent, common-cause, systematic capability, avoidance, control, V&V, coding standards, lifecycle, competence.*

topics.title: Common Cause and Common Mode Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Common Cause and Common Mode Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is defined as a failure resulting from a single shared root cause that simultaneously affects more than one component, channel, or sub-system in a redundant Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), causing the entire redundant group to lose its safety function.

**Common Mode Failure (CMF)** is a specific *subset* of Common Cause Failure in which multiple components fail in exactly the **same failure mode** (i.e., same direction, same mechanism, same magnitude) due to the same shared cause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Hierarchy:**
> **CCF (Common Cause Failure) ⊃ CMF (Common Mode Failure)**
> Every Common Mode Failure is a Common Cause Failure, but not every Common Cause Failure is a Common Mode Failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition Standard:**
> As per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 framework, CCF refers to failures of multiple items that would otherwise be considered independent in a redundant architecture, but fail together because they share a common root cause (design, environment, operation, or maintenance).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **twin-engine aircraft** with two engines (engine A and engine B) — designed so that if one fails, the other keeps the plane flying (redundancy).

- **Independent Failure:** Engine A fails due to a bird strike, while engine B keeps running. This is an *independent* failure. ✔️
- **Common Mode Failure:** Both engines seize at the exact same instant due to the *same* contaminated fuel batch — both fail in the *same* way (stall). ✖️
- **Common Cause Failure (broader):** Both engines fail on the same day — one stalls (fuel issue) and the other overheats (cooling pump fails) — same *cause* (poor maintenance crew) but *different* failure modes.

So redundancy is **defeated** when the very assumption of independence breaks down. That is the danger of CCF/CMF.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Functional Safety

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand (avg) | $PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless | Avg. probability SIF fails when demanded |
| CCF Beta Factor | $\beta$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of failures that are common-cause |
| Channel Equivalent Mean Downtime | $t_{CE}$ | hours (h) | Avg. time a channel remains in failed state |
| Dangerous Failure Rate | $\lambda_D$ | per hour (h⁻¹) | Rate of dangerous undetected failures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$\beta$ = 0** → perfectly independent channels (no CCF). **$\beta$ = 1** → total coupling (100% CCF). In real KTU problems, $\beta$ is typically between **1 % and 10 %** for well-designed systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Independence Fails — The Root Reasons

In any Safety Instrumented Function, designers **assume** channels (sensors, logic solvers, final elements) are statistically independent. In reality, this assumption fails due to:

- **Shared Design:** Identical components from same vendor (same silicon batch, same firmware).
- **Shared Environment:** Common temperature, humidity, vibration, EMI/EMC stress.
- **Shared Operating Procedures:** Same maintenance crew, same calibration cycle, same test rig.
- **Shared Manufacturing Defects:** Common latent defects in batches.
- **Shared Power Supply / Utility:** Single UPS, single air conditioning, single cabling.
- **Shared Human Interfaces:** Same operator, same HMI.

### 2.2 Difference Between CCF and CMF — Tabular Form

| Parameter | Common Cause Failure (CCF) | Common Mode Failure (CMF) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Multiple items fail from one shared cause | Multiple items fail in same mode from one shared cause |
| Failure Mode | May differ between components | **Identical** failure mode across all components |
| Scope | Broader (umbrella) concept | Strict subset of CCF |
| Example | One batch of relays → some fail open, some fail short | One batch of relays → **all** fail open |
| Impact on Redundancy | Defeats the safety case | Defeats the safety case **completely** |

### 2.3 CCF Modeling Approaches

#### (a) Beta Factor Model (Most Common — KTU Favorite)
The simplest and most KTU-relevant model. It assumes a fixed fraction $\beta$ of all component failures are common-cause failures.

Let:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate per channel
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous detected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate
- $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (proof test interval / 2 for undetected failures)

**Independent failure rate per channel:** $\lambda_{D,\text{indep}} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_D$

**Common cause failure rate per channel:** $\lambda_{D,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D$

#### (b) Multiple Beta Factor Model
Used for finer granularity — splits $\beta$ into $\beta_D$ (dangerous CCF), $\beta_{S}$ (safe CCF), etc.

#### (c) Alpha Factor Model
Uses $\alpha_k$ where $k$ = number of components that fail simultaneously. The sum of all $\alpha_k = 1$.

#### (d) Shock Model
Treats CCF as external environmental shocks affecting the entire system.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Architecture | $PFD_{avg}$ Formula (with $\beta$) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** (single channel) | $PFD_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | No redundancy |
| **1oo2** (1-out-of-2 voted low) | $PFD_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | One must work |
| **2oo2** (1-out-of-2 voted high) | $PFD_{2oo2} = 2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} t_{CE} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | Both must work |
| **2oo3** (majority voted) | $PFD_{2oo3} = 6(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | Two of three must work |

| CCF Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Beta Factor | $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda_{CCF}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Fraction of failures that are common-cause |
| Independent Failure Rate | $\lambda_{indep} = (1-\beta)\lambda_D$ | Failure rate treated as independent |
| CCF Failure Rate | $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D$ | Failure rate due to common cause |
| PFD Simplification | $q_{CCF} = \beta \cdot q_{indep}$ | CCF average unavailability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** CCF analysis is mandatory in **SIL verification calculations** for SIL 1/2/3 SIFs in oil & gas, nuclear, and chemical process plants. The $\beta$ factor is also used in **IEC 61508 Tables D.1–D.5** for scoring design robustness against CCF (lower $\beta$ ↔ higher score).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $\beta$ Factor — From First Principles

We start with the assumption that a *single channel* has dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ per hour, and the system uses $n$ identical, parallel redundant channels.

#### Step 1: Partition the Total Failure Rate

The total failure rate per channel can be split into two disjoint categories:

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{indep} + \lambda_{CCF}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Definition of $\beta$

The $\beta$ factor is defined as the **fraction** of failures attributed to a shared root cause:

$$\beta = \frac{\lambda_{CCF}}{\lambda_D}$$

#### Step 3: Solve for Independent Failure Rate

Rearranging the $\beta$ equation:

$$\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D$$

Substituting back into Step 1:

$$\lambda_{indep} = \lambda_D - \lambda_{CCF} = \lambda_D - \beta \cdot \lambda_D$$

Factoring $\lambda_D$:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{indep} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_D}$$

> **Logic:** This is the *effective* rate at which a channel fails in a way that does **not** affect its redundant partner — the only part of $\lambda_D$ that respects independence.

### 3.2 Derivation of $PFD_{1oo2}$ With CCF

For a **1oo2** architecture (1-out-of-2 voted low — system fails if BOTH channels fail), we must consider:

- **Both channels failing independently:** probability $q_1 \cdot q_2$ where $q_i$ is the average unavailability.
- **Both channels failing due to CCF:** probability $q_{CCF}$.

#### Step 1: Express Average Unavailability per Channel

For an undetected dangerous failure with periodic proof testing:

$$q_{indep} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$$

$$q_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$$

#### Step 2: Compute PFD for Independent Coincidence

For 1oo2, both must fail. Assuming independent events:

$$PFD_{indep} = q_{indep} \cdot q_{indep} = [(1 - \beta)\lambda_{DU} t_{CE}]^2$$

#### Step 3: Compute PFD for Common-Cause Path

Both channels fail from the same event — this is **a single failure event**, not a coincidence:

$$PFD_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$$

#### Step 4: Combine (Add Probabilities — Approximation Valid for Low q)

$$PFD_{1oo2} = PFD_{indep} + PFD_{CCF}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The $\beta$ term is **linear** in $\lambda_{DU}$, while the independent term is **quadratic**. This means for high-reliability systems with very small $\lambda_{DU}$, the **CCF term dominates** the entire PFD budget — exactly why IEC 61508 mandates CCF analysis.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A 1oo2 SIF is designed with:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- Proof test interval $T = 8760$ hours (1 year)
- $\beta = 0.05$ (5 %)
- $t_{CE} = T/2 = 4380$ hours

**Solution:**

#### Step 1: Independent failure rate

$$\lambda_{indep} = (1 - 0.05)(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 4.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$

#### Step 2: Independent PFD term

$$PFD_{indep} = 2 \cdot [(0.95)(5 \times 10^{-6})(4380)]^2$$

Evaluate inner term:

$$(0.95)(5 \times 10^{-6})(4380) = 0.02081$$

Square it:

$$(0.02081)^2 = 4.33 \times 10^{-4}$$

Multiply by 2:

$$PFD_{indep} = 2 \cdot 4.33 \times 10^{-4} = 8.66 \times 10^{-4}$$

#### Step 3: CCF term

$$PFD_{CCF} = (0.05)(5 \times 10^{-6})(4380) = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}$$

#### Step 4: Total PFD

$$PFD_{1oo2} = 8.66 \times 10^{-4} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3} = 1.961 \times 10^{-3}$$

#### Step 5: Interpret — Compare to SIL target

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **SIL 2** | **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$** |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

Our result $1.961 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in **SIL 2** range. ✔️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation for KTU 2024:** Without CCF ($\beta = 0$), $PFD = 8.66 \times 10^{-4}$ — which is **better**. So CCF **degrades** the SIL. The fraction due to CCF alone is $\dfrac{1.095 \times 10^{-3}}{1.961 \times 10^{-3}} = 55.8\,\%$. The system would have qualified for SIL 3 without CCF, but lands in SIL 2 *with* CCF. This is a classic KTU board scenario.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defenses Against CCF

| Defense | $\beta$ Reduction | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Diversity (different manufacturer)** | High | Use transmitters from two vendors |
| **Different technologies** | High | Use analog + digital sensors |
| **Physical separation** | Moderate | Spaced cable trays |
| **Different power feeds** | Moderate | Separate UPS circuits |
| **Different maintenance teams** | Low | Cross-trained shift crews |
| **Different proof test intervals** | Low | Staggered test dates |
| **Environmental hardening** | Low | EMI shielding, HVAC isolation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CCF in a 1oo2 SIF

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Physical Process Plant]
    SP[Shared Environmental Stressor eg heat vibration EMI]
    HMI[Shared Human Operator]
    CAL[Shared Calibration Source]
    PWR[Shared Power Supply]

    S1[Sensor Channel A]
    S2[Sensor Channel B]
    L1[Logic Solver Channel A]
    L2[Logic Solver Channel B]
    F1[Final Element A Valve]
    F2[Final Element B Valve]

    OUT1[Output to Field]
    OUT2[Output to Field]

    P1 --> S1
    P1 --> S2

    S1 --> L1
    S2 --> L2

    L1 --> F1
    L2 --> F2

    F1 --> OUT1
    F2 --> OUT2

    SP -.affects.-> S1
    SP -.affects.-> S2
    SP -.affects.-> L1
    SP -.affects.-> L2
    SP -.affects.-> F1
    SP -.affects.-> F2

    HMI -.interfaces.-> S1
    HMI -.interfaces.-> S2

    CAL -.calibrates.-> S1
    CAL -.calibrates.-> S2

    PWR -.powers.-> L1
    PWR -.powers.-> L2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the **intended signal flow** of the 1oo2 architecture. Dashed arrows represent **shared stressors** that can simultaneously defeat Channel A and Channel B — this is the *root cause layer* of CCF.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Concept Map — CCF vs CMF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Multiple Component Failures in a Redundant System]

    IND[Independent Failures]

    CCF[Common Cause Failure CCF]

    CMF[Common Mode Failure CMF subset of CCF]

    ROOT --> IND
    ROOT --> CCF
    CCF --> CMF

    IND_A[Example Bird strike on Engine A only]
    IND --> IND_A

    CCF_A[Example Single maintenance crew mistake]
    CCF --> CCF_A

    CMF_A[Example Bad batch fuel both engines stall]
    CMF --> CMF_A

    ROOT --> NOTE[Both fail but root cause differs]
    IND -.contrast.-> NOTE
    CCF -.contrast.-> NOTE
```

### 4.3 Beta Factor Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOT[Total Dangerous Failure Rate lambda D]

    BETA[Beta times lambda D]
    ONEBETA[(1 minus Beta) times lambda D]

    CCF_BLOCK[Common Cause Failure Channel]
    INDEP_BLOCK[Independent Failure Channel]

    TOT --> BETA
    TOT --> ONEBETA
    BETA --> CCF_BLOCK
    ONEBETA --> INDEP_BLOCK
    CCF_BLOCK -.add.-> PFD[PFD total]
    INDEP_BLOCK -.add.-> PFD
```

### 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SYS[Redundant Safety System]

    DEF1[Diversity Different Vendor]
    DEF2[Physical Separation Spaced Cabling]
    DEF3[Power Isolation Separate UPS]
    DEF4[Procedural Independence Different Crews]
    DEF5[Environmental Hardening EMI HVAC]
    DEF6[Staggered Proof Test]

    SYS --> DEF1
    SYS --> DEF2
    SYS --> DEF3
    SYS --> DEF4
    SYS --> DEF5
    SYS --> DEF6
```

> **Engineering Note:** Each defense reduces the *effective* $\beta$ value, which in turn lowers the PFD contribution from the CCF term. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to map specific defenses to specific $\beta$ reduction techniques.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Common Cause Failure (CCF) and Common Mode Failure (CMF). How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

**Common Cause Failure (CCF):** A failure where multiple components or channels of a redundant Safety Instrumented Function fail simultaneously due to a **single shared root cause**, defeating the redundancy assumption.

**Common Mode Failure (CMF):** A specific subset of CCF where multiple components fail in the **same failure mode** (same mechanism, same direction) due to the same shared cause.

**Relationship:** CMF is a *strict subset* of CCF. Every CMF is a CCF, but not every CCF is a CMF. For example, a bad batch of capacitors may cause some to fail short and others open (CCF, not CMF), while contaminated hydraulic fluid may cause all valves to stick closed (CCF that is also CMF).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the Beta factor model? State its significance in SIL verification.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Beta ($\beta$) factor model** is a quantitative technique to account for Common Cause Failures in redundant SIF architectures. It assumes a fixed fraction $\beta$ of all dangerous component failures occur due to shared root causes:

$$\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D \qquad \lambda_{indep} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_D$$

**Significance in SIL verification:**

1. Without CCF, the calculated PFD is *optimistic* and the SIF may appear to meet a higher SIL than it actually does.
2. IEC 61508 / 61511 require CCF evaluation as part of the SIL determination.
3. The $\beta$ term dominates the PFD for high-reliability channels, especially in 1oo2, 2oo3, and 1oo2D architectures.
4. It drives design choices — diversity, separation, power isolation — to reduce $\beta$.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Choose Either A or B**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a suitable example the concept of **Common Cause Failure** and **Common Mode Failure** in a Safety Instrumented Function. List at least **six (6) possible root causes** of CCF in industrial process plants.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 1oo2 SIF uses pressure transmitters with the following data:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- Proof test interval $T = 4380$ hours
- $\beta = 0.08$

Compute the $PFD_{1oo2}$ with and without CCF. Comment on the **SIL classification** of the SIF. State the channel equivalent mean downtime $t_{CE}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept Explanation [7 Marks]:**

In a 1oo2 (one-out-of-two) safety architecture, two independent channels are arranged so that the safety action is taken if *either* channel demands it. The design principle is that the channels are **statistically independent**, so the probability of both failing simultaneously is extremely small.

- **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** When a single shared root cause defeats *both* channels simultaneously, the redundancy assumption breaks down. Example: A common cooling fan failure in a control cabinet causes both PLCs to overheat and crash at the same time.
- **Common Mode Failure (CMF):** A special case of CCF where both channels fail in the *same* way. Example: Identical firmware bug causes both transmitters to output the same erroneous reading after power-up.

**Six (6) root causes of CCF in process plants:**

1. Shared power supply failure (common UPS, common battery).
2. Shared environment (heat, humidity, vibration, EMC/EMI stress).
3. Shared design (same component batch with same latent defect).
4. Shared maintenance procedure (same technician, same test rig, same calibration gas).
5. Shared utility supply (same instrument air, same nitrogen, same hydraulic line).
6. Shared human-machine interface (common HMI freeze, common operator error).

[Allocate ~1 mark per point plus concept clarity.]

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{CE}$:**

For a 1oo2 architecture with a single proof test interval $T$:

$$t_{CE} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{4380}{2} = 2190 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $PFD_{1oo2}$ WITHOUT CCF (assume $\beta = 0$):**

$$PFD_{1oo2,\text{no CCF}} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$$

Compute the inner term:

$$\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = (2 \times 10^{-6})(2190) = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$

Square it:

$$(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.918 \times 10^{-5}$$

Multiply by 2:

$$PFD_{1oo2,\text{no CCF}} = 2 \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{-5} = 3.84 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is in **SIL 3** range ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$) — actually closer to SIL 4.

**Step 3 — Compute $PFD_{1oo2}$ WITH CCF (using $\beta = 0.08$):**

Independent term:

$$PFD_{indep} = 2 \cdot (1 - 0.08)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2 = 2 \cdot (0.92)^2 \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$(0.92)^2 = 0.8464$$

$$PFD_{indep} = 2 \cdot 0.8464 \cdot 1.918 \times 10^{-5} = 3.25 \times 10^{-5}$$

CCF term:

$$PFD_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = 0.08 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 2190$$

$$PFD_{CCF} = 3.504 \times 10^{-4}$$

Total:

$$PFD_{1oo2,\text{with CCF}} = 3.25 \times 10^{-5} + 3.504 \times 10^{-4} = 3.83 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — SIL Classification:**

| PFD Value | SIL |
|---|---|
| $3.84 \times 10^{-5}$ (no CCF) | **SIL 4 territory** |
| $3.83 \times 10^{-4}$ (with CCF) | **SIL 3** |

**Comment:** The inclusion of CCF **demoted** the SIF from a high-SIL-3 / SIL-4 candidate down to a firm **SIL 3**. The CCF term contributes approximately $\dfrac{3.504 \times 10^{-4}}{3.83 \times 10^{-4}} = 91.5\,\%$ of the total PFD — proving CCF is the **dominant contributor** for high-reliability SIFs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **Beta Factor model** and the **Alpha Factor model** for quantifying Common Cause Failures. Compare their suitability for KTU-style SIL verification calculations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 2oo3 SIF has the following parameters:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T = 8760$ hours
- $\beta = 0.10$

Calculate the $PFD_{2oo3}$ with CCF. Comment on the impact of the CCF term on the SIL level.

---

**Model Solution (abbreviated):**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Beta Factor Model:** Simple; assumes a single fraction $\beta$ of all failures are common-cause. $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \lambda_D$. Easy to use in KTU numerical problems. Limitation: treats all multi-component failures identically.

**Alpha Factor Model:** Uses $\alpha_k$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$ where $k$ = number of components failing simultaneously. The constraint is:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k = 1$$

This gives more granularity. For a 2-channel system:

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda}, \quad \alpha_2 = \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda_1$ = single-component failure rate, $\lambda_2$ = both-components-CCF rate.

**Comparison for KTU-style verification:**

| Aspect | Beta Factor | Alpha Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Data required | 1 number ($\beta$) | Multiple $\alpha_k$ values |
| KTU exam suitability | **High** (preferred) | Moderate |
| Industry use | Common in IEC 61508 simple cases | Used in detailed nuclear studies |

**(b) Numerical [7 Marks]:**

$t_{CE} = T/2 = 4380$ hours

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 6(1 - \beta)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$$

Independent term:

$$\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = (3 \times 10^{-6})(4380) = 1.314 \times 10^{-2}$$

Square:

$$(1.314 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 1.727 \times 10^{-4}$$

Multiply by 6 and $(0.9)^2 = 0.81$:

$$PFD_{indep} = 6 \cdot 0.81 \cdot 1.727 \times 10^{-4} = 8.39 \times 10^{-4}$$

CCF term:

$$PFD_{CCF} = 0.10 \cdot (3 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 4380 = 1.314 \times 10^{-3}$$

Total:

$$PFD_{2oo3} = 8.39 \times 10^{-4} + 1.314 \times 10^{-3} = 2.153 \times 10^{-3}$$

This falls in **SIL 2** range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\beta$ term** — students often compute only the independent coincidence part. This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong $t_{CE}$** — must use $T/2$ for undetected dangerous failures with single proof test; not $T$.
> 3. **Mixing up architectures** — using 1oo2 formula for 2oo2 or 2oo3. Always recheck the architecture's voting logic.
> 4. **Confusing CMF with CCF in definitions** — examiners award only partial credit if you use the terms interchangeably without hierarchy.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — $\lambda_D$ in per-hour and $t_{CE}$ in hours is the KTU standard; mixing hours and years loses marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CCF** = failures of multiple components from one shared root cause.
- **CMF** = subset of CCF where failure *modes* are also identical.
- **Hierarchy:** CCF ⊃ CMF (always).
- **Beta factor** $\beta$ quantifies the fraction of CCF; $\beta \in [0, 1]$.
- **Independent failure rate** = $(1 - \beta) \lambda_D$.
- **CCF failure rate** = $\beta \lambda_D$.
- **Channel equivalent mean downtime** $t_{CE} = T_{proof}/2$ for undetected dangerous failures.
- **$PFD_{1oo2}$** = $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$.
- **$PFD_{2oo2}$** = $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} t_{CE} + \beta \lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$.
- **$PFD_{2oo3}$** = $6(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2 + \beta \lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$.
- **CCF term is linear**, **independent term is quadratic** — for high-reliability channels, CCF *dominates* the PFD.
- **Defenses against CCF:** diversity, physical separation, power isolation, different vendors, different maintenance crews, environmental hardening, staggered proof testing.
- **IEC 61508/61511 mandate CCF evaluation** for all SIL calculations.
- **SIL demotion:** Including CCF often moves a SIF from a higher SIL to a lower SIL — always report both with-CCF and without-CCF results in KTU answers.
- **Alpha factor model** uses $\alpha_k$ for $k$ simultaneously failing components; $\sum \alpha_k = 1$.
- **Real-world impact:** Without CCF analysis, safety claims are *optimistic* and may lead to unsafe plant operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Instrumented System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Instrumented System

# Safety Instrumented System (SIS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61511):** A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is a dedicated, independent system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined conditions (setpoints) are violated, thereby preventing or mitigating hazardous events.

In the framework of **KTU 2024 (Functional Safety)**, an SIS is fundamentally distinct from the **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)**. While the BPCS controls the *normal* operation of a plant, the SIS exists exclusively to handle *abnormal* and *emergency* conditions — it is the last line of automated defense before a hazard escalates into an accident.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **BPCS** = *Control* loop (setpoint tracking, regulation)
> - **SIS** = *Protection* loop (alarm, shutdown, interlock)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Car's Airbag System

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway:
- The **accelerator, brakes, and steering** are the **BPCS** — they help you *control* the car under normal driving.
- The **airbag, ABS, and seatbelt pretensioner** are the **SIS** — they exist *only* to protect you *when something goes wrong* (sudden collision, skidding).

Similarly, in a chemical plant:
- A **flow control loop** adjusting valve opening is the **BPCS**.
- An **emergency shutdown valve (ESD)** that slams shut when pressure exceeds a critical limit is the **SIS**.

The SIS must be **independent** (separate hardware, separate wiring, separate power) from the BPCS so that a single fault cannot disable both control *and* protection simultaneously — a principle called **separation / independence**.

---

### Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following globally recognized metrics govern SIS design per **IEC 61511** and **IEC 61508**:

- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** A dimensionless number between **0 and 1** representing the likelihood that the SIS will *fail to operate* when a hazardous demand occurs.
- **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor):** Defined as the inverse of PFD, expressed as $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$.
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** A discrete level (1 to 4) corresponding to a range of PFD values.
- **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour):** Used for high-demand / continuous-mode SIS, expressed in $\text{failures/hour}$.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** Average operational time before a component fails, typically in **hours**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**S**afety **I**nstrumented **S**ystem" → **S**ensors + **L**ogic solver + **F**inal element (the "SLF" mnemonic).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of an SIS (SLF Architecture)

Every SIS, regardless of industry application, contains exactly **three functional layers** operating in series. This is the **SLF architecture**:

### 1. **S — Sensor (Input Subsystem)**
- **Function:** Continuously measures the process variable (pressure, temperature, flow, level).
- **Examples:** Pressure transmitters, thermocouples, level switches, flame detectors.
- **Failure Modes:**
  - *Dangerous Detected (DD)* — sensor fails, but diagnostics catch it.
  - *Dangerous Undetected (DU)* — sensor fails silently — most critical for SIS.
  - *Safe Detected (SD)* / *Safe Undetected (SU)* — fail to a safe state.

### 2. **L — Logic Solver (Brain / PES)**
- **Function:** Receives sensor signals, executes the safety logic (e.g., 2oo3 voting), and commands the final element.
- **Examples:** Safety PLC (e.g., Siemens S7 F-CPU), Safety Shutdown System (SDS), Trip amplifiers.
- **Critical Property:** Must be **certified** to IEC 61508 SIL rating with proven **diagnostic coverage**.

### 3. **F — Final Element (Actuator)**
- **Function:** Physically changes the process state to bring it to a safe condition.
- **Examples:** Emergency Shutdown Valves (ESDVs), blowdown valves, motor contactors, circuit breakers.
- **Critical Property:** Must move to the safe position on **loss of signal/air/power** (fail-safe / de-energize-to-trip principle).

## 2.2 Why an SIS is Needed — The Risk Reduction Equation

The entire purpose of an SIS can be summarized in one equation:

$$R_{\text{acceptable}} = \frac{R_{\text{unmitigated}}}{RRF_{\text{total}}}$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{unmitigated}}$ = Risk of the process if **no protection** existed.
- $RRF_{\text{total}}$ = Total Risk Reduction Factor provided by all independent protection layers (IPLs).
- $R_{\text{acceptable}}$ = Tolerable risk threshold (defined by company / regulator / society).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An SIS is just **one** of several **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**. Other layers include relief valves, dikes, operator response, alarms, and inherent safe design. The SIS is typically the **last IPL** before the hazard reaches personnel/equipment/environment.

## 2.3 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The KTU PFD Table

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Board Table — Memorize This!**

| SIL | Low-Demand PFD Range | RRF Range | High-Demand PFH (per hour) | Risk Reduction |
|:---:|:--------------------:|:---------:|:--------------------------:|:--------------:|
| 1   | $10^{-2} \ge \text{PFD} \ge 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | $10^{-6} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$ | 10x – 100x |
| 2   | $10^{-3} \ge \text{PFD} \ge 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1,000 | $10^{-7} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-6}$ | 100x – 1,000x |
| 3   | $10^{-4} \ge \text{PFD} \ge 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | $10^{-8} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ | 1,000x – 10,000x |
| 4   | $10^{-5} \ge \text{PFD} \ge 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | $10^{-9} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-8}$ | 10,000x – 100,000x |

**Note:** There is **no SIL 5**. The maximum is **SIL 4**, reserved for nuclear/aviation.

## 2.4 SIS Operating Modes

| Mode | Demand Rate | Typical PFD Metric | Application |
|:----:|:-----------:|:------------------:|:-----------:|
| **Low Demand** | $\le 1$ demand/year | PFD$_{\text{avg}}$ | Process plants, refineries |
| **High Demand** | $> 1$ demand/year | PFH | Compressor control, burner management |
| **Continuous** | Demand is the normal state | PFH | Aircraft flight controls, reactor trips |

## 2.5 The Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511 Phases)

This is the **12-phase lifecycle** students must know for board questions:

1. **Concept & Scope Definition** — Identify the equipment under control (EUC).
2. **Hazard & Risk Assessment (HRA)** — Identify hazards, estimate severity & likelihood.
3. **Specification of Safety Requirements** — Define **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** and target SIL.
4. **SIS Design & Engineering** — Architecture, redundancy (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3).
5. **Installation, Commissioning & Validation** — Site acceptance test (SAT).
6. **Operation & Maintenance** — Proof Test scheduling.
7. **Modification** — Management of change (MOC).
8. **Decommissioning** — Safe disposal.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "*C**oncept, **H**azard, **S**pec, **D**esign, **I**nstall, **O**perate, **M**odify, **D**ecommission*" → **CHSDIOMD**

## 2.6 Voting Architectures (Nomenclature: MooN)

The notation **MooN** means *M out of N* channels must vote for a trip:

| Voting | Formula | Use Case |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------:|
| **1oo1** | Single channel | Low criticality |
| **1oo2** | 1 of 2 — any one trips | Higher availability, lower safety |
| **2oo2** | Both must trip | Higher safety, risk of dangerous failure |
| **2oo3** | Best of both — 2 of 3 trip | Highest integrity + availability (preferred for SIL 3) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

SIS design principles are deployed in:
- **Oil & Gas** — Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G) systems on offshore platforms.
- **Power Plants** — Reactor Protection System (RPS), Boiler trips.
- **Chemicals** — Toxic gas detection, overpressure protection.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Batch reactor over-temperature interlocks.
- **Automotive/Aerospace** — Drive-by-wire, fly-by-wire (ISO 26262 is the automotive twin of IEC 61508).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average PFD for a 1oo1 System

The SIS must operate reliably between **proof tests** (typically every 1, 2, or 5 years). Between tests, the probability of dangerous failure grows approximately linearly with time.

**Given:**
- $\lambda_D$ = Dangerous failure rate of the channel (failures/hour).
- $T$ = Proof Test Interval (hours).

**Step 1 — Express instantaneous unavailability $P(t)$:**

For a single channel that is restored to "as-good-as-new" at every proof test, the unavailability at time $t$ since the last test is:

$$P(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t$$

This is because failure rate $\lambda_D$ is the probability density of failure per unit time, integrated over the elapsed time $t$.

**Step 2 — Compute the average unavailability over the interval $T$:**

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $P(t) = \lambda_D t$:**

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Pull the constant $\lambda_D$ out of the integral:**

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T} \int_{0}^{T} t \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral of $t$:**

$$\int_{0}^{T} t \, dt = \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{T^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and simplify:**

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T} \cdot \frac{T^2}{2}$$

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula (1oo1 architecture):**
> $$PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$
> **Units:** $\lambda_D$ in failures/hour $\times$ $T$ in hours $\Rightarrow$ dimensionless. ✔

## 3.2 Worked Example — Finding SIL from PFD

**Problem:** A low-demand SIS safety function has a calculated $PFD_{\text{avg}} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$. What SIL does it satisfy? What is the Risk Reduction Factor?

**Step 1 — Identify the PFD range.**

$$3.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0035$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the SIL table.**

| SIL | PFD Range |
|:---:|:---------:|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ (0.01 to 0.1) |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ (0.001 to 0.01) |
| **SIL 3** | $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-3}}$ (**0.0001 to 0.001**) ← ← ← our value falls here |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ (0.00001 to 0.0001) |

Wait — $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is *inside* the SIL 2 range (since $10^{-3} \le 0.0035 \le 10^{-2}$). So the answer is **SIL 2**.

**Step 3 — Compute RRF:**

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$RRF = \frac{1000}{3.5} \approx 285.7$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

A RRF of ~286 means the SIS reduces the risk of the hazardous event by a factor of **about 286**, which is firmly within the **SIL 2 band (100 to 1,000)**. ✔

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Three Core SIS Relationships

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----:|
| 1 | Risk | $R = f \times C$ (frequency $\times$ consequence) | Foundation of any HRA |
| 2 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$ | Inverse relationship |
| 3 | Average PFD (1oo1) | $PFD_{\text{avg}} = (\lambda_D \cdot T) / 2$ | Increases linearly with T |
| 4 | SIL (low demand) | $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$ (rounded) | Quick mental check |
| 5 | Probability of Safe Failure | $\text{PFS} = 1 - \text{PFD}$ | Confidence level |

## 3.4 Derivation of the Trip Equation for a 2oo3 Voter

In a **2oo3 (2 out of 3)** voting system, the trip activates if any 2 of the 3 sensors agree. The probability of a **dangerous failure** occurs when **2 or more** sensors have failed dangerously.

**Step 1 — Probability that exactly 2 of 3 fail:**

$$P_{\text{exactly 2}} = \binom{3}{2} \cdot (PFD)^2 \cdot (1 - PFD)^1 = 3 \cdot PFD^2 \cdot (1 - PFD)$$

**Step 2 — Probability that all 3 fail:**

$$P_{\text{exactly 3}} = \binom{3}{3} \cdot (PFD)^3 \cdot (1 - PFD)^0 = PFD^3$$

**Step 3 — Total PFD for 2oo3 (dominant term when PFD $\ll$ 1):**

$$PFD_{\text{2oo3}} = 3 \cdot PFD^2 + PFD^3 \approx 6 \cdot PFD^2$$

$$PFD_{\text{2oo3}} \approx 6 \cdot PFD^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> For very small PFD (e.g., $10^{-2}$), $6 \cdot (10^{-2})^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$, which lands squarely in **SIL 3** territory. This is why 2oo3 is the industry gold standard for high-integrity protection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIS Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete SIS information flow from process measurement to safe-state actuation, including fault paths and diagnostic coverage.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Plant EUC] -->|Physical Variable| B1[Sensor 1]
    A -->|Physical Variable| B2[Sensor 2]
    A -->|Physical Variable| B3[Sensor 3]
    B1 --> C{Logic Solver PES Voting 2oo3}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -->|Trip Command| D1[Final Element Valve 1]
    C -->|Trip Command| D2[Final Element Valve 2]
    D1 --> E[Safe State Shutdown]
    D2 --> E
    C --> F[Operator HMI Alarm Annunciation]
    C --> G[Proof Test Scheduler T years]
    G -.->|Periodic Validation| C
    C --> H[Diagnostic Coverage DC]
    H -.->|Fault Detection| C
    styleA[fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px
    styleB1[fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:1px
    styleB2[fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:1px
    styleB3[fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:1px
    styleC[fill:#98fb98,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px
    styleD1[fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:1px
    styleD2[fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:1px
    styleE[fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    styleF[fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#6a5acd,stroke-width:1px
    styleG[fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:1px
    styleH[fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **gold process block (A)** is the Equipment Under Control.
> - **Blue sensor blocks (B1, B2, B3)** form a redundant triple.
> - The **green logic solver (C)** executes 2oo3 voting and feeds two parallel final elements.
> - **Pink valves (D1, D2)** are de-energize-to-trip.
> - **Diagnostic coverage (H)** continuously self-tests the PES and loops back.

## 4.2 Functional Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[1 Concept and Scope] --> P2[2 Hazard and Risk Assessment]
    P2 --> P3[3 Specification of Safety Requirements]
    P3 --> P4[4 SIS Design and Engineering]
    P4 --> P5[5 Installation and Commissioning]
    P5 --> P6[6 Operation and Maintenance]
    P6 --> P7[7 Modification]
    P7 --> P8[8 Decommissioning]
    P6 -.->|Periodic| PT[Proof Testing]
    PT -.->|Restore| P6
    styleP1[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP2[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP3[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP4[fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
    styleP5[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP6[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP7[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    styleP8[fill:#ffebcd,stroke:#8b4513
    stylePT[fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000
```

## 4.3 Risk Reduction Onion — IPL Layering

```mermaid
graph TB
    C[Community and Environment] --> P[Process Plant]
    P --> I1[Inherent Safe Design]
    I1 --> I2[BPCS Control Loop]
    I2 --> I3[Alarms and Operator Intervention]
    I3 --> I4[Mechanical IPL Relief Valves Dikes]
    I4 --> I5[SIS Safety Instrumented System]
    I5 --> I6[Emergency Response Fire Brigade]
    styleI1[fill:#ffd700
    styleI2[fill:#ffd700
    styleI3[fill:#ffd700
    styleI4[fill:#ffd700
    styleI5[fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px
    styleI6[fill:#ffd700
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SIS is the **penultimate (or last automated) layer** before the hazard reaches the community. Each preceding layer must be **independent** — no shared sensors, valves, logic, or power supply.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define Safety Instrumented System (SIS) as per IEC 61511. List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is an independent system comprising sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements, designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined trip conditions are exceeded, thereby reducing the risk of a hazardous event (1 mark).

The three main components are:
1. **Sensor subsystem** — measures process variables such as pressure, temperature, level, or flow (1 mark).
2. **Logic Solver** — a Safety PLC or PES that evaluates the sensor inputs against trip setpoints and executes the safety logic (0.5 marks).
3. **Final Element** — actuator (e.g., ESD valve, motor starter) that physically moves the process to a safe state (0.5 marks).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between BPCS and SIS. Why must an SIS be independent of the BPCS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | BPCS | SIS |
|:---------:|:----:|:---:|
| Purpose | Continuous process control | Emergency protection / shutdown |
| Operation | Modulating, setpoint tracking | Discrete trip / interlock |
| Failure response | Fault-tolerant, may continue | Must fail-safe to trip state |
| Standard | ISA 88/95 | IEC 61511 / IEC 61508 |

**Independence requirement (1 mark):** Independence ensures that a single common-cause failure (e.g., shared power supply, shared card, shared sensor) cannot disable both control and protection simultaneously. This is mandated by IEC 61511 to prevent loss of both the BPCS *and* the SIS in a single event — a principle known as **separation**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** concept as defined by IEC 61511. Tabulate the **PFD and RRF ranges for SIL 1 to SIL 4** in low-demand mode. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1oo1 pressure safety function has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The proof test interval is $T = 2$ years. Calculate (i) the average PFD, (ii) the achieved SIL, and (iii) the Risk Reduction Factor. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) used to specify the required risk reduction of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). It is defined in terms of either the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{\text{avg}}$)** for low-demand systems, or the **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** for high-demand/continuous systems.

**PFD and RRF Table (3 marks):**

| SIL | Low-Demand PFD$_{\text{avg}}$ Range | RRF Range |
|:---:|:-----------------------------------:|:---------:|
