| 1   | $\ge 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$  | 10 to 100 |
| 2   | $\ge 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$  | 100 to 1,000 |
| 3   | $\ge 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$  | 1,000 to 10,000 |
| 4   | $\ge 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$  | 10,000 to 100,000 |

**Explanation (2.5 marks):**
- SIL is determined through a **Hazard & Risk Assessment (HRA)** which combines severity, frequency of exposure, and possibility of avoidance.
- Higher SIL ⇒ stricter component qualification, higher diagnostic coverage, more frequent proof tests, and use of redundant architectures (e.g., 2oo3).
- SIL is **not** an attribute of a single component but of the **complete Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** including sensor, logic solver, and final element.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Architecture: 1oo1
- Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- Proof test interval: $T = 2$ years

**Step 1 — Convert T to hours (1 mark):**

$$T = 2 \text{ years} \times 365 \text{ days/year} \times 24 \text{ hours/day}$$

$$T = 17,520 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo1 PFD formula (2 marks):**

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$$

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17,520}{2}$$

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \frac{0.0876}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the SIL (1.5 marks):**

The value $4.38 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0438$ lies in the range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, which corresponds to **SIL 1**.

**Step 4 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor (1.5 marks):**

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{4.38 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 22.8$$

This RRF (≈ 22.8) falls within the SIL 1 band of 10–100, confirming the result. ✔

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three-tier architecture** of a Safety Instrumented System. Explain the function of each tier and two examples of components used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **MooN voting architectures** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3) in terms of safety, availability, and typical application. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.

**Three-Tier Architecture (5 marks):**

**Tier 1 — Sensor (Input) Subsystem:**
- Function: Continuously detects the process variable (pressure, temperature, flow) and converts it to a standardized electrical signal (4–20 mA or digital).
- Examples: Rosemount 3051S pressure transmitter (1 mark), Endress+Hauser radar level gauge, thermocouple RTD (1 mark).

**Tier 2 — Logic Solver (Brain):**
- Function: Receives sensor signals, compares against trip setpoints, executes safety logic (e.g., 2oo3 voting), and energizes/de-energizes the final element. Must be **TÜV-certified** to IEC 61508.
- Examples: Siemens S7-1500F Safety PLC (1 mark), ABB AC 800F HI Integrity controller, Honeywell FSC (1 mark).

**Tier 3 — Final Element (Actuator):**
- Function: Physically isolates the energy source — closes valves, trips motors, opens vents — to achieve the safe state.
- Examples: ESD ball valve with spring-return pneumatic actuator (1 mark), motor contactor, circuit breaker (1 mark).

**Design Principle (2 marks):** Each tier must be **independent** in hardware, software, and power. A single fault must not propagate across all three tiers. The principle of **de-energize-to-trip** is preferred: loss of air/signal/power must result in the safe state.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Architecture | Safety (PFD) | Availability | Spurious Trip Rate | Typical Application |
|:------------:|:------------:|:------------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:|
| **1oo1** | Low (worst) | Highest | Lowest | Non-critical alarms |
| **1oo2** | Low (worst) | Higher than 1oo1 | Higher | High availability, low safety |
| **2oo2** | High | Lower | Lower | High safety, low availability |
| **2oo3** | Highest (best) | Highest (best) | Moderate | **SIL 3** — burner mgmt, ESD, F&G |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **1oo1** — Single channel. Simplest and cheapest but no redundancy; any dangerous failure goes undetected.
- **1oo2** — Both channels in parallel; trip occurs if *either* channel demands. Reduces dangerous failure probability (improved safety) at the cost of higher spurious trip rate (reduced availability).
- **2oo2** — Both must trip; safer against dangerous failure but if one channel sticks, the system may not trip.
- **2oo3** — Industry gold standard. Tolerates one failed channel in either direction. Provides best balance of safety AND availability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing PFD with PFH:** PFD is dimensionless (for low-demand), PFH has units of $1/\text{hour}$ (for high-demand). Many students write the wrong units — this costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $PFD_{\text{avg}}$:** Always include the $\div 2$ in $\lambda_D T / 2$ — it comes from integrating unavailability over the proof interval. Omitting it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up 1oo2 and 2oo2:** 1oo2 = trip on any one (higher availability). 2oo2 = trip only if both demand (higher safety). This is a **favourite trick question** — clarify in your answer.
> 4. **Conflating SIL of a component vs SIL of a SIF:** A single transmitter may be SIL 2 *capable*, but the complete SIF (sensor + logic + valve) determines the achieved SIL. Don't claim "this valve is SIL 3" — say "this SIF achieves SIL 3."
> 5. **Not converting units:** $T$ in the PFD formula **must** be in hours, not years. Show the conversion step explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **SIS = Sensors + Logic Solver + Final Element** (the SLF architecture).
- ✅ SIS is the **last automated protection layer** before the hazard reaches people/environment.
- ✅ SIS is **independent** of the BPCS — separate hardware, power, and signal paths.
- ✅ **SIL 1 to SIL 4** — there is **no SIL 5**. Memorize the PFD ranges.
- ✅ **PFD** = Probability of Failure on Demand (dimensionless, 0 to 1). **PFH** = Probability of Failure per Hour (high-demand/continuous).
- ✅ **RRF = 1 / PFD.** Higher SIL ⇒ higher RRF ⇒ higher risk reduction.
- ✅ **$PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} = \lambda_D T / 2$.** Always show the integration derivation.
- ✅ **De-energize-to-trip** is the preferred SIS philosophy — loss of power = safe state.
- ✅ **Voting architectures:** 1oo1 (simplest), 1oo2 (high availability), 2oo2 (high safety), 2oo3 (best of both, SIL 3).
- ✅ **Proof testing** is mandatory at intervals $T$ to restore SIS to "as-good-as-new" condition.
- ✅ **IEC 61511** = process industry. **IEC 61508** = cross-industry umbrella. **ISO 26262** = automotive cousin.
- ✅ **Functional Safety Lifecycle** = Concept → HRA → Spec → Design → Install → Operate → Modify → Decommission.
- ✅ SIL is a **SIF property**, not a component property — never say "this PLC is SIL 3" without qualifying the SIF.
- ✅ Risk equation: **Risk = Frequency × Consequence**. The SIS reduces **frequency** of the hazardous event.
- ✅ **Dangerous Undetected (DU)** failure is the most critical — diagnostics (DC%) target this.
- ✅ For board exam: always **tabulate**, always **diagram**, always **show the unit conversion** for $T$ in hours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Integrity Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Integrity Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a **discrete level (one of four possible levels: SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4)** used to specify the **safety integrity requirements** of the **safety functions** to be allocated to the **Electrical / Electronic / Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE) safety-related systems**. The definition is taken from the international umbrella standard **IEC 61508**.

In the broader risk-management framework of **IEC 61511** (process industries) and **ISO 26262** (automotive), SIL is used analogously but with target probability ranges that are sector-specific. For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on Functional Safety, the **IEC 61508** target tables are mandatory.

There are **two flavours** of SIL depending on the **mode of operation** of the safety function:

1. **Low Demand Mode** (demand rate ≤ 1 per year) → SIL targets expressed in terms of **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD<sub>avg</sub>)**.
2. **High Demand / Continuous Mode** (demand rate > 1 per year) → SIL targets expressed in terms **Probability of a dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brake System of a Mountain Truck"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Imagine a heavy truck carrying explosives down a steep mountain.**
> - The truck has **brakes** as its safety function. The brakes must work on demand to prevent catastrophe.
> - A **1-layer brake (1oo1)** works most of the time, but if it fails, the truck is doomed. That is like **SIL 1**.
> - A **dual-brake system with two independent circuits (1oo2)** — even if one fails, the other stops the truck. That is **SIL 2 or SIL 3**.
> - Adding **a third redundant brake** plus continuous diagnostic gives near-certain stopping. That is **SIL 4**.
> - **SIL is therefore a "trust grade"** assigned to the brake system. The harsher the consequence of failure, the higher the SIL must be.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **PFD<sub>avg</sub> — Average Probability of Failure on Demand** (dimensionless, range 0 to 1)
- **PFH — Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour** (dimension: 1/hour, i.e. h⁻¹)
- **RRF — Risk Reduction Factor** = **1 / PFD<sub>avg</sub>**
- **λ<sub>DU</sub> — Dangerous Undetected failure rate** (FIT, i.e. failures per 10⁹ hours)
- **T<sub>proof</sub> — Proof Test Interval** (hours or months)
- **β-Factor — Fraction of common-cause failures** (dimensionless, between 0.01 and 0.10 for redundant channels)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four SIL Targets — IEC 61508 Quantitative Tables

The IEC 61508 standard defines the **target failure-measure ranges** as a **non-overlapping logarithmic decade mapping**. A student must memorise these exactly because they form the heart of KTU exam questions on SIL.

### 2.1.1 Low Demand Mode Targets (PFD<sub>avg</sub>)

| SIL Level | Target PFD<sub>avg</sub> Range | Equivalent RRF Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SIL 1** | ≥ 10⁻² to < 10⁻¹ | 10 to 100 |
| **SIL 2** | ≥ 10⁻³ to < 10⁻² | 100 to 1000 |
| **SIL 3** | ≥ 10⁻⁴ to < 10⁻³ | 1000 to 10 000 |
| **SIL 4** | ≥ 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁴ | 10 000 to 100 000 |

### 2.1.2 High Demand / Continuous Mode Targets (PFH)

| SIL Level | Target PFH Range (per hour) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **SIL 1** | ≥ 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ |
| **SIL 2** | ≥ 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ |
| **SIL 3** | ≥ 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ |
| **SIL 4** | ≥ 10⁻⁹ to < 10⁻⁸ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One full decade = One SIL level.** This is the **most common viva question**: *"If PFD = 3×10⁻³, what SIL?"* — Round down to the most restrictive decade that the value still satisfies. 3×10⁻³ lies between 10⁻³ and 10⁻² → **SIL 2**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of SIL Determination (Risk-Based Approach)

The KTU 2024 scheme syllabus follows the **semi-quantitative risk graph method** (IEC 61508-5). The procedural logic is:

1. **Identify the hazard** that the safety function must mitigate.
2. **Evaluate four risk parameters** for the hazardous event:
   - C — Consequence severity
   - F — Frequency of exposure to the hazard
   - P — Possibility of avoidance
   - W — Probability of occurrence of the unwanted event
3. **Read the SIL from the calibrated risk graph** for the chosen parameter combination.
4. **Allocate the SIL** to each subsystem in the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF): **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element**.
5. **Calculate the achieved PFD<sub>avg</sub>** of the SIF using the formula:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}$$
6. **Verify** that the calculated PFD<sub>SIF</sub> lies within the SIL target range.
7. If not, **upgrade architecture** (e.g., move from 1oo1 to 1oo2 or 2oo3).

## 2.3 Architectural Constraints

SIL alone is **not enough** — the **architecture** must also be **hardware-fault-tolerant (HFT)** compliant. The IEC 61508 table for minimum HFT:

| SIL | Minimum HFT (Type A subsystems) | Minimum HFT (Type B subsystems) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | 0 | 0 |
| **2** | 1 | 1 |
| **3** | 2 | 1 |
| **4** | 2 (with restrictions) | 2 (with restrictions) |

> Type A vs Type B refers to whether **failure modes of all components are well-defined and known** (Type A) or **not** (Type B).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Range | Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PFD<sub>avg, 1oo1</sub> | Avg. prob. of failure on demand (single channel) | 0 → 1 | $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ |
| t<sub>CE</sub> | Channel equivalent mean downtime | hours | $T_{proof} / 2$ (proof-test-only approx.) |
| PFD<sub>avg, 1oo2</sub> | Avg. prob. of failure (1-out-of-2) | 0 → 1 | $2 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ |
| PFD<sub>avg, 2oo2</sub> | Avg. prob. of failure (2-out-of-2) | 0 → 1 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ |
| PFD<sub>avg, 2oo3</sub> | Avg. prob. of failure (2-out-of-3) | 0 → 1 | $6 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ |
| RRF | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless | $1 / \text{PFD}_{avg}$ |
| PFH<sub>1oo1</sub> | Probability of dangerous failure / hr | h⁻¹ | $\lambda_{DU}$ |
| Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | Fraction of failures that are safe | % | $1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ |
| Total SIF PFD | Sum of subsystem PFDs | dimensionless | $\text{PFD}_S + \text{PFD}_L + \text{PFD}_F$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industry (Oil & Gas):** SIL is mandatory for Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G) systems, and High-Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS) per IEC 61511.
- **Nuclear Power:** Reactor trip systems are designed to **SIL 3** or higher (with hardware redundancy).
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 maps SIL to **ASIL A through D** for functional safety of electrical/electronic systems.
- **Railway Signalling:** SIL 4 is the standard for Automatic Train Protection (ATP) systems like ETCS Level 2/3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section presents the **mathematical derivation** of the PFD<sub>avg</sub> for a **single-channel (1oo1)** subsystem, which is the foundation for all higher-redundancy formulas. Every step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation: PFD<sub>avg</sub> for a 1oo1 (Single-Channel) Subsystem

### Step 1 — Define the dangerous failure rate

Let $\lambda_D$ be the dangerous failure rate per hour. For a single channel, the **probability of dangerous failure in a small time interval** $dt$ is:

$$P(\text{fail in } dt) = \lambda_D \cdot dt$$

### Step 2 — Unavailability as a function of time

Assuming the failure is **undetected** until the next proof test, the channel is in a **dangerous state** from the moment it fails until it is detected and repaired. If a failure occurs at time $t$ after the last proof test, the unavailability contribution is $(T_{proof} - t)$.

The **point unavailability** averaged uniformly over the proof-test interval:

$$U(t) = \lambda_D \cdot (T_{proof} - t), \quad 0 \leq t \leq T_{proof}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate over the proof-test cycle

The **average unavailability** = average probability of being in failed state:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_D \cdot (T_{proof} - t) \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_{proof}} \left[ T_{proof} \cdot t - \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_{proof}} \left( T_{proof}^2 - \dfrac{T_{proof}^2}{2} \right)$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_{proof}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{avg, \, 1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Express using t<sub>CE</sub>

By definition, the **channel equivalent mean downtime** is $t_{CE} = T_{proof} / 2$. Substituting:

$$\text{PFD}_{avg, \, 1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$$

This is the canonical IEC 61508-6 expression.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Typical Exam Style

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in an SIF has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h and is proof-tested every **2 years** (≈ **17 520 hours**). Find the achieved SIL.

**Step 1 — Compute t<sub>CE</sub>**

$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{17\,520}{2} = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute PFD<sub>avg</sub>**

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760$$

$$\text{PFD}_{avg} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Map to SIL**

The value $4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $\geq 10^{-3}$ and $< 10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative PFD<sub>avg</sub> Across Architectures

The table below compares three common architectures at $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h, $\beta_D = 0.05$.

| Architecture | Formula | Numerical PFD<sub>avg</sub> | SIL Achieved |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** | $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ | **SIL 2** |
| **1oo2** | $2 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | $1.10 \times 10^{-5}$ | **SIL 4** |
| **2oo3** | $6 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | $3.30 \times 10^{-5}$ | **SIL 4** |

**Observation:** Redundant architectures (1oo2, 2oo3) reduce PFD<sub>avg</sub> by **two orders of magnitude** because the dominant term becomes quadratic in $\lambda_{DU}$.

## 3.4 Risk Reduction Factor Derivation

By definition:

$$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{avg}}$$

For the worked example:

$$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{4.38 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 228$$

A RRF of 228 falls in the SIL 2 range (100 to 1000) → consistent with our earlier mapping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIL Determination Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hazardous Event Identified] --> B0[Assess Risk Parameters]
    B0 --> B1[C: Consequence Severity]
    B0 --> B2[F: Frequency of Exposure]
    B0 --> B3[P: Possibility of Avoidance]
    B0 --> B4[W: Probability of Occurrence]
    B1 --> C0[Apply Risk Graph Method]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    B4 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Determine Target SIL 1 to 4]
    D0 --> E0[Allocate SIL to Subsystems]
    E0 --> E1[Sensor PFD_S]
    E0 --> E2[Logic Solver PFD_L]
    E0 --> E3[Final Element PFD_F]
    E1 --> F0[Sum PFD Total]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    F0 --> G0{PFD Within Target SIL Range?}
    G0 -->|Yes| H0[SIL Achieved - Design Accepted]
    G0 -->|No| I0[Increase Redundancy]
    I0 --> E0
```

## 4.2 Safety Instrumented Function Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Channel 1] --> LOGIC[Logic Solver]
    S2[Sensor Channel 2] --> LOGIC
    LOGIC --> F1[Final Element 1]
    LOGIC --> F2[Final Element 2]
    LOGIC --> F3[Final Element 3]
    PROC[Process Under Control] --> S1
    PROC --> S2
    F1 --> PROC
    F2 --> PROC
    F3 --> PROC
    LOGIC --> SILCHECK{Compute Total PFD}
    SILCHECK --> SILD[SIL Determination]
```

## 4.3 SIL Target Mapping (Probability vs SIL)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[SIL 1] --> M1[PFD 10 to the minus 2 to 10 to the minus 1]
    L2[SIL 2] --> M2[PFD 10 to the minus 3 to 10 to the minus 2]
    L3[SIL 3] --> M3[PFD 10 to the minus 4 to 10 to the minus 3]
    L4[SIL 4] --> M4[PFD 10 to the minus 5 to 10 to the minus 4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (A0, B0, B1, … S1, S2, LOGIC, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). Mention the standards that define it. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (one of four: SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4) used to specify the safety integrity requirements of safety functions to be allocated to E/E/PE safety-related systems. It is defined by **IEC 61508** (generic) and adapted by sector standards such as **IEC 61511** (process) and **ISO 26262** (automotive). SIL 1 is the lowest integrity; SIL 4 is the highest.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Low Demand Mode and High Demand/Continuous Mode of operation. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low Demand Mode | High Demand / Continuous Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Demand Rate** | ≤ 1 per year | > 1 per year |
| **Failure Metric** | PFD<sub>avg</sub> | PFH |
| **Typical Application** | Emergency Shutdown | Burner Management, Aircraft Flight Control |
| **SIL Range (Target)** | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ | 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁹ per hour |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the SIL determination procedure as per IEC 61508 with a neat risk-graph description. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The IEC 61508-5 procedure uses a **semi-quantitative risk graph** to determine the target SIL. The procedure is:

1. **Identify the hazard** to be controlled.
2. **Assess four parameters:**
   - **C (Consequence):** C1 = minor injury; C2 = serious injury; C3 = death; C4 = catastrophe.
   - **F (Exposure frequency):** F1 = rarely; F2 = sometimes; F3 = frequent.
   - **P (Possibility of avoidance):** P1 = possible; P2 = hardly possible.
   - **W (Probability of unwanted event):** W1 = very low; W2 = low; W3 = relatively high.
3. **Combine the parameters** in the risk graph (Calibrated tree with logical AND/OR gates) to arrive at one of four terminal nodes labelled **a, b, c, d**.
4. **Map nodes to SIL:**
   - a → no safety requirement
   - b → **SIL 1**
   - c → **SIL 2**
   - d → **SIL 3**
   - (For d plus very high consequence → **SIL 4**)

The risk graph is essentially a **decision tree** that converts qualitative judgment into a numerical SIL requirement.

**[7 Marks – Standard KTU Mark Distribution: 2 for definition, 3 for parameters, 2 for graph logic]**

#### (b) A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a sensor ($\lambda_{DU,S} = 3 \times 10^{-7}$/h), logic solver ($\lambda_{DU,L} = 1 \times 10^{-8}$/h), and final element ($\lambda_{DU,F} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/h). All are single-channel (1oo1) with $T_{proof} = 1$ year (8760 h). Determine the achieved SIL. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute t<sub>CE</sub>**

$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{8760}{2} = 4380 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — PFD<sub>avg</sub> for each subsystem (1oo1 formula)**

$$\text{PFD}_{S} = \lambda_{DU,S} \cdot t_{CE} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380 = 1.314 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{L} = \lambda_{DU,L} \cdot t_{CE} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \times 4380 = 4.38 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{F} = \lambda_{DU,F} \cdot t_{CE} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380 = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Total SIF PFD**

$$\text{PFD}_{SIF} = 1.314 \times 10^{-3} + 4.38 \times 10^{-5} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{SIF} = 3.548 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — SIL Mapping**

$3.548 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2**.

---

### ✅ Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)

#### (a) Define Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). Explain its significance with a numerical example where a chemical reactor pressure relief SIF has $PFD_{avg} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) is the inverse of the average probability of failure on demand. It quantifies how much the safety system reduces the inherent process risk.

$$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{avg}}$$

**Significance:**
- RRF tells the engineer **how many times safer** the protected process becomes.
- It is the multiplicative factor used to reduce the unmitigated risk.
- It is the **first parameter a safety engineer quotes** to plant management.

**Numerical Example:**

$$\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500$$

So the SIF reduces the inherent reactor explosion risk by **500 times**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation Pitfall):** Students often quote RRF as a probability or write $\text{RRF} = \text{PFD}_{avg}$. The correct relation is **RRF = 1 / PFD<sub>avg</sub>**. Examiner deducts **1 mark** for this inversion.

#### (b) With suitable derivations, compare the PFD<sub>avg</sub> of a 1oo1 architecture and a 1oo2 architecture. Assume $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-7}$/h, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h, $\beta_D = 0.05$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — 1oo1 PFD<sub>avg</sub>**

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380 = 1.752 \times 10^{-3}$$

→ Falls in SIL 2 range.

**Step 2 — 1oo2 PFD<sub>avg</sub>**

The 1oo2 formula (IEC 61508-6) considers that:
- Both channels must fail dangerously for system failure → gives quadratic term.
- $\beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ accounts for common-cause failures.

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$$

Computing $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = 1.752 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \dfrac{2 \times (1.752 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} + 0.05 \times 1.752 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 3.07 \times 10^{-6}}{3} + 8.76 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$= 2.05 \times 10^{-6} + 8.76 \times 10^{-5} \approx 8.96 \times 10^{-5}$$

→ Falls in SIL 3 range.

**Step 3 — Comparison**

| Architecture | PFD<sub>avg</sub> | SIL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1oo1 | $1.752 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 |
| 1oo2 | $8.96 \times 10^{-5}$ | SIL 3 |

**Conclusion:** Adding **one redundant channel (1oo2)** improves the safety integrity by **one full SIL level** (factor of ~20 improvement), confirming that redundancy is the most cost-effective SIL upgrade path.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PFD and PFH** — KTU examiners explicitly test which mode (low vs high demand) applies. Wrong mode = **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $t_{CE}$** — A common omission; full derivation expected.
> 3. **Wrong decimal placement in SIL mapping** — $4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is **SIL 2**, not SIL 3. Memorise the decade boundaries.
> 4. **Skipping $\beta_D$ term** — For redundant systems, the **common-cause failure term is mandatory**. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 5. **Mixing IEC 61508 SIL table with IEC 61511/ISO 26262** — Use only the IEC 61508 table unless explicitly asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🎯 **SIL = Discrete level (1 → 4)** of safety integrity, defined by **IEC 61508**.
- 🎯 **Two modes:** Low Demand → **PFD<sub>avg</sub>**; High Demand → **PFH**.
- 🎯 **One decade = one SIL level** (logarithmic separation in failure probability).
- 🎯 **Low Demand Table (PFD<sub>avg</sub>):** SIL 1 = 10⁻²–10⁻¹, SIL 2 = 10⁻³–10⁻², SIL 3 = 10⁻⁴–10⁻³, SIL 4 = 10⁻⁵–10⁻⁴.
- 🎯 **High Demand Table (PFH per hour):** SIL 1 = 10⁻⁶–10⁻⁵, SIL 2 = 10⁻⁷–10⁻⁶, SIL 3 = 10⁻⁸–10⁻⁷, SIL 4 = 10⁻⁹–10⁻⁸.
- 🎯 **RRF = 1 / PFD<sub>avg</sub>** (always the inverse, never the same).
- 🎯 **1oo1 PFD<sub>avg</sub> = $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$** (where $t_{CE} = T_{proof} / 2$).
- 🎯 **1oo2 PFD<sub>avg</sub>** contains a quadratic $\lambda_{DU}^2$ term **plus** the $\beta_D \lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$ common-cause term.
- 🎯 **Total SIF PFD = PFD<sub>Sensor</sub> + PFD<sub>Logic</sub> + PFD<sub>Final Element</sub>** (simple linear sum).
- 🎯 **Architectural constraints** matter: SIL 2 minimum HFT = 1 for Type B subsystems.
- 🎯 **SIL is not just a probability** — it is also a **hardware architecture + systematic capability** constraint.
- 🎯 **Risk Graph** is the standard IEC 61508-5 method for SIL determination.
- 🎯 **Sector standards:** IEC 61511 (process), IEC 62061 (machinery), ISO 26262 (automotive), EN 50128/50129 (rail).
- 🎯 **Common units:** Failure rate in **FIT** (Failures In Time, i.e. per 10⁹ hours). Convert by: $\lambda_{\text{per hour}} = \lambda_{\text{FIT}} \times 10^{-9}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systematic Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systematic Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)

**Systematic Capability (SC)** of an element (hardware or software) is a measure of the confidence that the element's systematic faults (design faults, specification faults, implementation faults) will be avoided or controlled to a level consistent with a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. It is formally defined in **IEC 61508** and is denoted by **SC N**, where **N = 1, 2, 3, or 4**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Systematic Capability is NOT the same as the Safety Integrity Level of a safety function. **SC N is a property of an ELEMENT (e.g., a smart transmitter, a CPU, a software routine), not of the entire safety instrumented function.**

The exact statement from **IEC 61508-4:2010, Clause 3.5.4**:
*"The systematic capability of an element is a measure (on a scale of SC 1 to SC 4) of the confidence that the systematic safety integrity of an element meets the requirements of the specified SIL."*

> [!NOTE]
> **In IEC 61511 (Process Industry Sector)**, the levels are restricted to **SC 1, SC 2, and SC 3 only** (no SC 4), since the process industry is limited to SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Trust Index"

Imagine you are evaluating two chefs:
- **Chef A** has been trained at a 3-star Michelin school, follows a strict recipe book, has every dish double-checked by a senior chef before serving, and keeps detailed logs. You trust his kitchen up to a 3-star rating.
- **Chef B** has been informally trained, cooks from memory, and has no review process. You can only trust him up to a 1-star rating.

**Systematic Capability is the trust rating of the "kitchen process"** (design + verification + quality management), NOT the rating of any single dish (which would be random failure). Even if Chef A's dish never burned today, his process quality (systematic capability) determines what rating he can confidently claim.

In the same way, **SC N tells you the maximum SIL you can claim from the systematic-faults perspective**, regardless of the calculated random failure rate (PFH/PFD).

### 1.3 Why Systematic Capability Exists — A Core Distinction

Random hardware failures (e.g., a resistor burning out) can be quantified using **failure rate data (λ)**. Systematic faults (e.g., a CPU miscalculating due to a design bug) **cannot** — they only show up under specific conditions and cannot be reduced by adding redundancy (in fact, redundant identical CPUs can have *common* systematic faults).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** addresses random hardware failures and common-cause (beta-factor) failures. **Systematic Capability** addresses design faults, software bugs, and specification errors. **BOTH must be satisfied** for a safety function to claim a given SIL.

### 1.4 Key Boundary Definitions

- **SC 1:** No systematic fault requirements beyond standard quality management. (Confidence ≤ SIL 1 level)
- **SC 2:** Confidence that systematic faults are controlled to a level appropriate for **SIL 2**.
- **SC 3:** Confidence that systematic faults are controlled to a level appropriate for **SIL 3**.
- **SC 4:** Confidence that systematic faults are controlled to a level appropriate for **SIL 4**.

| SC Level | Maximum Claimable SIL (Systematic) | Achievable Industry Sectors |
|----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| SC 1     | SIL 1                             | All sectors                  |
| SC 2     | SIL 2                             | Process, machinery, nuclear |
| SC 3     | SIL 3                             | Process, machinery, nuclear |
| SC 4     | SIL 4                             | Nuclear, aerospace only      |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Two Pillars" of Safety Integrity

A safety function achieves SIL N only if **both** pillars are satisfied:

**Pillar 1 — Random Hardware Integrity**
- Quantified by **PFDavg** (low-demand) or **PFH** (high-demand/continuous mode)
- Governed by **HFT (Hardware Fault Tolerance)** + **SFF (Safe Failure Fraction)**
- Per IEC 61508-2, Table 2 / 61508-3 Annex E

**Pillar 2 — Systematic Integrity**
- Quantified qualitatively by **SC N** of each element
- Governed by **Quality Management, Design Methodology, Validation, Verification, Prior Use Evidence**

### 2.2 Routes to Achieve Systematic Capability (IEC 61508-2 / 61508-3)

For a **complex element** (e.g., microcontroller, ASIC, software) with **no prior use history**, IEC 61508 prescribes **Route 1s** to demonstrate SC N.

**Route 1s (Default Path):**
A combination of three concurrent activities, each scaled to the SC level:
1. **Quality Management** — based on ISO 9001 + IEC 61508-1 Annex B
2. **Design and Development Techniques** — formal methods, structured design, modularization (more rigorous for higher SC)
3. **Validation & Verification (V&V)** — testing, simulation, walk-throughs, formal proofs

**Route 2s (Alternative — Prior Use Justification):**
Demonstration of sufficient **operational history** in similar applications, with documented evidence that no dangerous systematic failures occurred.

**Route 3s (Software only, IEC 61508-3):**
Statistical evaluation of field data (very rarely used due to high sample requirements).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic to Determine Required SC

To assign SC N to an element used in a SIL N safety function:

**Step 1:** Identify the safety function and its required SIL (from risk assessment).

**Step 2:** Determine the element type:
- **Type A element** — failure modes fully known, behavior under fault conditions fully determined, well-proven design.
- **Type B element** — failure modes not fully known, complex component (e.g., microcontroller).

**Step 3:** Calculate the required **HFT** using Table 2 (IEC 61508-2):
- Type A element, SIL N → HFT = N (with adequate SFF)
- Type B element, SIL N → HFT = N + 1 (with adequate SFF)

**Step 4:** Demand SC N from each element, where **N = target SIL** of the safety function.

**Step 5:** Validate SC N by Route 1s (or Route 2s).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{dd}}{\lambda_d} = \dfrac{\lambda_{dd}}{\lambda_{dd} + \lambda_{du}}$ | Ratio of detected dangerous failures to total dangerous failures |
| 2 | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{du}}{\lambda_s + \lambda_d}$ | Must be ≥ 60% (Type B) or ≥ 90% (Type B HFT=N) |
| 3 | PFDavg for 1oo1 (low demand) | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_DU \cdot T_i}{2} + \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i)^2}{3}$ | $T_i$ = proof test interval |
| 4 | PFH for 1oo1 (high demand) | $PFH = \lambda_{DU}$ | Constant hazard rate |
| 5 | Common-cause (β-factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda_{DC}}{\lambda_D}$ | Typically 1%–10% |
| 6 | Total failure rate | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_s + \lambda_d$ | λ_s = safe, λ_d = dangerous |
| 7 | Systematic Fault Constraint | $SC_{element} \geq SIL_{function}$ | Both HFT and SC must be satisfied |
| 8 | Architectural Constraint (Type B, SIL 2) | $HFT \geq N$ if $SFF \in [60\%, 90\%)$; $HFT \geq N-1$ if $SFF \geq 90\%$ | From IEC 61508-2 Table 2 |
| 9 | PFH target for SIL 3 (high demand) | $10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$ per hour | Per IEC 61508-1 Table 3 |
| 10 | PFDavg target for SIL 2 (low demand) | $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$ | Per IEC 61508-1 Table 2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Many students confuse "SC 2 element" with "SIL 2 safety function." The element with SC 2 *contributes* to a SIL 2 safety function, but the function's actual SIL is the **minimum** of all elements' SCs AND the **PFH/PFDavg** achieved.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection of safety PLCs/SIS controllers:** Engineers choose products whose datasheet claims SC 3 to support SIL 3 loops in hydrocarbon processing plants.
- **Software certification:** IEC 61508-3 demands SC-aligned V&V techniques — for SC 3 software, you must use structured design, modularization, static analysis, and dynamic testing at specified coverage levels.
- **Sensors & transmitters:** Modern smart transmitters (e.g., pressure transmitters) are marketed as "SIL 2 capable, SC 2" or "SIL 3 capable, SC 3" — buyers verify this through FMEDA reports.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why an Element's SC Cannot Exceed Its Validation Confidence

Let us derive the **logical relationship between SC, Design Process (R), and Verification Evidence (V)**.

The probability that a systematic fault remains undetected after V&V is:

$$
P(\text{sys fault undetected}) = P(\text{fault exists}) \times P(\text{V\&V misses} \mid \text{fault exists})
$$

Let:
- $R$ = design rigor coefficient (closer to 1 means more rigorous techniques applied per IEC 61508-3)
- $V$ = verification coverage (probability of detecting a fault, $0 \leq V \leq 1$)

Then:

$$
P(\text{undetected}) = (1 - R) \cdot (1 - V)
$$

For SC N, IEC 61508 specifies minimum **R** and **V** thresholds. As N increases, both R and V must approach 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SC\ 1 &: R \geq 0.50,\ V \geq 0.50 \\
SC\ 2 &: R \geq 0.70,\ V \geq 0.70 \\
SC\ 3 &: R \geq 0.85,\ V \geq 0.85 \\
SC\ 4 &: R \geq 0.95,\ V \geq 0.95 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

These are illustrative values reflecting the spirit of the standard — actual IEC 61508-3 Annex A tables list specific technique-and-measure pairs.

Therefore the maximum **average probability of failure on demand attributable to systematic faults** scales as:

$$
PFD_{sys} = (1 - R)(1 - V)
$$

For **SC 3** with $R = 0.85, V = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{sys} &= (1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.85) \\
&= (0.15)(0.15) \\
&= 0.0225 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is on the **edge** of the SIL 3 boundary ($10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$). In practice, a margin is maintained by using techniques with $R, V > 0.85$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Relationship Between Pillar 1 and Pillar 2

| Safety Function Target | Random HW Integrity Required (Pillar 1) | Systematic Integrity Required (Pillar 2) | Both Required? |
|------------------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------|
| SIL 1                  | PFH: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ /h  OR PFD: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | All elements SC 1 (minimum) | Yes |
| SIL 2                  | PFH: $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ /h OR PFD: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | All elements SC 2 (minimum) | Yes |
| SIL 3                  | PFH: $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ /h OR PFD: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | All elements SC 3 (minimum) | Yes |
| SIL 4                  | PFH: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ /h OR PFD: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | All elements SC 4 (minimum) | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Overall SIL of a safety function = MIN(Random HW SIL, Systematic SIL)**
> If PFH suggests SIL 3, but one element has only SC 2, the function is at best **SIL 2**.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A safety shutdown function in a hydrocracker requires SIL 3. The system uses a **smart pressure transmitter** that the manufacturer certifies as **SC 3** and a **safety PLC** also certified **SC 3**. Both are Type B elements. The transmitter has $\lambda_s = 200$ FIT, $\lambda_{dd} = 150$ FIT, $\lambda_{du} = 50$ FIT. The proof test interval is $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year).

**Step 1:** Compute SFF of the transmitter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{total} &= \lambda_s + \lambda_{dd} + \lambda_{du} \\
&= 200 + 150 + 50 = 400\ \text{FIT} \\
SFF &= 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{du}}{\lambda_{total}} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{50}{400} \\
&= 0.875 \quad \text{(i.e., 87.5\%)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Check architectural constraint (Type B, SIL 3).

Per IEC 61508-2 Table 2 for Type B elements:
- SIL 3 with $SFF \in [60\%, 90\%)$ → require $HFT \geq N = 3$.
- SIL 3 with $SFF \geq 90\%$ → require $HFT \geq N - 1 = 2$.

Since $SFF = 87.5\%$, the architecture must achieve **HFT = 3** (e.g., 1oo3 voting). This is the **hardware fault tolerance** — independent of SC.

**Step 3:** Compute PFDavg for the transmitter in 1oo1 (per channel).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{1oo1} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 8760}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{4.38 \times 10^{-4}}{2} \\
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-4} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This is within the SIL 3 range ($10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$) for a single channel. In a 1oo3 architecture, the PFDavg per channel is acceptable, and the 1oo3 voted average can be calculated using the standard formula.

**Step 4:** Verify Systematic Capability.

Both transmitter and PLC are SC 3 ⇒ Systematic SIL ≥ 3 ✓

**Step 5:** Overall SIL = MIN(Random HW SIL, Systematic SIL).

Random HW SIL (from PFDavg) = SIL 3 (with 1oo3 architecture)
Systematic SIL = SIL 3

$$
\boxed{\text{Safety Function SIL} = \text{MIN}(3, 3) = \text{SIL 3} \quad \checkmark}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the PLC were only SC 2, the overall SIL would be **SIL 2**, even though the random PFH/PFD would have supported SIL 3. **Systematic Capability is the silent gatekeeper.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Two-Pillar Model of Safety Integrity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePillarA["PILLAR 1 - RANDOM HARDWARE INTEGRITY"]
    nodePillarB["PILLAR 2 - SYSTEMATIC INTEGRITY"]

    nodeHFT["HFT Hardware Fault Tolerance"]
    nodeSFF["SFF Safe Failure Fraction"]
    nodePFH["PFH or PFDavg Calculation"]

    nodeSC["SC N Systematic Capability"]
    nodeRoute1["Route 1s Quality + Design + V&V"]
    nodeRoute2["Route 2s Prior Use Evidence"]

    nodeFinal["OVERALL SAFETY FUNCTION SIL"]
    nodeMIN["MIN of both pillars"]

    nodePillarA --> nodeHFT
    nodePillarA --> nodeSFF
    nodePillarA --> nodePFH
    nodeHFT --> nodeFinal
    nodeSFF --> nodeFinal
    nodePFH --> nodeFinal

    nodePillarB --> nodeSC
    nodeSC --> nodeRoute1
    nodeSC --> nodeRoute2
    nodeRoute1 --> nodeFinal
    nodeRoute2 --> nodeFinal

    nodeFinal --> nodeMIN
```

### 4.2 Flowchart for Determining Required SC Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart(["Safety Function Requires SIL N"])
    nodeIdentify["Identify all elements in the safety function"]
    nodeType["Classify each element as Type A or Type B"]
    nodeHFTchk["Compute required HFT per IEC 61508-2 Table 2"]
    nodeSCchk["Demand SC N from each complex element"]
    nodeRoute["Validate SC N via Route 1s or Route 2s"]
    nodeCert["Check manufacturer certificate and FMEDA report"]
    nodePass{"All elements satisfy SC N and HFT?"}
    nodeYes["Function achieves SIL N"]
    nodeNo["Reduce target SIL or upgrade components"]

    nodeStart --> nodeIdentify --> nodeType --> nodeHFTchk --> nodeSCchk --> nodeRoute --> nodeCert --> nodePass
    nodePass -- Yes --> nodeYes
    nodePass -- No --> nodeNo
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of SC Validation per IEC 61508-3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSpec["Functional and Safety Requirements Specification"]
    nodeDesign["Design and Coding Following IEC 61508-3 Annex A"]
    nodeVerify["Verification Static and Dynamic Analysis"]
    nodeValidate["Validation against Safety Requirements"]
    nodeFMEDA["FMEDA Report and Manufacturer SC Claim"]
    nodeCertSC["Certified SC Level 1 to 4"]

    nodeSpec --> nodeDesign --> nodeVerify --> nodeValidate --> nodeFMEDA --> nodeCertSC
```

### 4.4 Comparative Table of the SC Routes

| Route | Applicable To | Evidence Required | Strength |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Route 1s** | Complex hardware & software without prior use | Quality Mgmt + Design Techniques + V&V per IEC 61508 | Default & strongest |
| **Route 2s** | Well-proven components in similar environments | Documented operational history (≥ 10⁶ operating hours, no dangerous failures) | Strong when data available |
| **Route 3s** | Software only (rarely used) | Statistical analysis of field failures | Weak; rarely accepted |

### 4.5 Hierarchy Diagram — From SIL to Component SC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSIL["Target SIL of Safety Function = N"]
    nodeSplit["Decompose into Elements"]
    nodeT1["Type A Element with HFT N and SFF sufficient"]
    nodeT2["Type B Element with HFT N+1 and SFF sufficient"]
    nodeT3["Complex SW or HW - Requires SC N"]
    nodeSC["Element must demonstrate SC N"]
    nodeFinal["Function SIL = MIN Random HW SIL, Systematic SIL"]

    nodeSIL --> nodeSplit --> nodeT1
    nodeSplit --> nodeT2
    nodeSplit --> nodeT3
    nodeT3 --> nodeSC --> nodeFinal
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
*Define Systematic Capability. Why is it necessary in functional safety assessment?*

**Model Answer:**
Systematic Capability (SC), as defined in **IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.5.4**, is a measure (on a scale of SC 1 to SC 4) of the confidence that an element's systematic safety integrity meets the requirements of a specified SIL. It is necessary because **systematic faults (design bugs, specification errors, software errors) cannot be quantified by random failure rate data**. SC ensures that such faults are controlled via quality management, design rigor, and validation to a confidence level appropriate for the safety function's target SIL. Without SC verification, a calculated low PFH/PFDavg value is insufficient to claim the SIL.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Systematic Capability (SC) and Safety Integrity Level (SIL).*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Systematic Capability (SC) | Safety Integrity Level (SIL) |
|--------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Scope** | Property of an individual element | Property of an entire safety function |
| **Scale** | SC 1, SC 2, SC 3, SC 4 | SIL 1, SIL 2, SIL 3, SIL 4 |
| **What it measures** | Confidence that systematic faults are controlled | Overall safety integrity (random + systematic) |
| **Verification** | Route 1s / 2s per IEC 61508-3 | Risk graph / risk matrix → derived |
| **Example** | Smart transmitter certified SC 3 | Emergency Shutdown System at SIL 3 |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Route 1s** approach used to demonstrate Systematic Capability for a complex hardware element as per IEC 61508-3. How does the technique selection differ between SC 1, SC 2, and SC 3? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SIL 2 overpressure protection function uses **two identical pressure transmitters** in a **1oo2 architecture**. Each transmitter has the following failure rates:
- $\lambda_s = 250 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- $\lambda_{dd} = 120 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- $\lambda_{du} = 30 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- Type B element
- Proof test interval $T_i = 4380$ hours (6 months)

Calculate: (i) SFF, (ii) Architectural constraint verification, (iii) PFDavg for 1oo2. State whether the SIL 2 target is met from the random hardware perspective. Assume both transmitters are certified **SC 2** by the manufacturer. State the final SIL of the function. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Route 1s for SC N (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Route 1s is the **default and most rigorous route** specified in IEC 61508-2 (Annex B) and IEC 61508-3 (Annex A and B) to demonstrate the Systematic Capability of a complex element when prior use evidence is not available. It is a **coherent package of three concurrent activities**.

**Three Pillars of Route 1s (3 Marks):**

1. **Quality Management System (QMS):**
   - Compliance with **ISO 9001** is necessary but not sufficient.
   - IEC 61508-1 Annex B mandates additional safety-specific QMS measures — safety lifecycle, independent functional safety assessment, configuration management, and document control.
   - Rigor increases with SC level.

2. **Design and Development Techniques:**
   - For **SC 1:** Structured specification, basic coding standards, simple modularization.
   - For **SC 2:** Structured design, modularization, defensive programming, formal review.
   - For **SC 3:** Above + formal methods, model-based design, semi-formal notations (e.g., UML with safety annotations), highly defensive coding.
   - Per IEC 61508-3, **Annex A Tables A.1 to A.14** prescribe specific techniques; higher SC requires **more "Highly Recommended"** techniques.

3. **Validation and Verification (V&V):**
   - **Testing coverage:** SC 1 → 50% branch coverage; SC 2 → 70%; SC 3 → 85%; SC 4 → 95%.
   - Includes static analysis, dynamic testing, simulation, hardware-in-the-loop, and **functional safety assessment** by an independent assessor.

**Difference between SC 1 / SC 2 / SC 3 (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | SC 1 | SC 2 | SC 3 |
|--------|------|------|------|
| QMS | ISO 9001 + basic safety plan | + safety audit + independent review | + multi-discipline review + certified assessors |
| Design | Structured design | + modular + defensive coding | + formal methods + model checking |
| V&V Coverage | ≥ 50% statement | ≥ 70% branch | ≥ 85% MC/DC (modified condition/decision) |
| Failure analysis | FMEDA basic | + dependent failure analysis | + complete FMEA + CCF + beta-factor |
| Validation | Functional test | + fault injection | + statistical testing + extensive fault injection |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $\lambda_s = 250 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- $\lambda_{dd} = 120 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- $\lambda_{du} = 30 \times 10^{-9}$ /h
- Type B element
- $T_i = 4380$ h
- Architecture: 1oo2
- Target SIL: 2

**(i) SFF Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{total} &= \lambda_s + \lambda_{dd} + \lambda_{du} \\
&= (250 + 120 + 30) \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 400 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{/h} \\
SFF &= 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{du}}{\lambda_{total}} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-9}}{400 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= 1 - 0.075 \\
&= 0.925 \quad \text{i.e., } 92.5\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Architectural Constraint Verification (2 Marks):**

For a Type B element targeting SIL 2, IEC 61508-2 Table 2 specifies:
- $HFT \geq 1$ if $60\% \leq SFF < 90\%$
- $HFT \geq 0$ if $SFF \geq 90\%$

Here $SFF = 92.5\% \geq 90\%$, and we are using **1oo2 architecture which provides HFT = 1**.

Hence **architectural constraint satisfied** ✓ (in fact, oversatisfied).

**(iii) PFDavg for 1oo2 (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{1oo1} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{(30 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4380}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{1.314 \times 10^{-4}}{2} \\
&= 6.57 \times 10^{-5} \\
PFD_{1oo2} &= 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_i}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_i}{2} \\
&\approx 2 \cdot PFD_{1oo1}\ \text{(ignoring CCF as not given)} \\
&\approx 2 \cdot 6.57 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 1.314 \times 10^{-4} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the textbook approximate formula for 1oo2:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3} \approx \text{very small} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-5}
$$

For an exam, the safe standard answer is:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} \approx 2 \times PFD_{1oo1} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-4}
$$

This lies in the SIL 2 range: $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ ✓ **SIL 2 from random HW perspective achieved.**

**(iv) Overall SIL (1 Mark):**
- Random HW SIL ≥ 2 ✓
- Systematic SIL = MIN(SC 2, SC 2) = SC 2 (i.e., SIL 2) ✓

**Overall Safety Function SIL = SIL 2 ✓**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **two pillars** of safety integrity. Explain with a diagram how Systematic Capability and Hardware Fault Tolerance together determine the overall SIL of a safety function. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A safety function targets SIL 3. It uses a **Type B complex subsystem** with the following data:
- $\lambda_s = 400$ FIT, $\lambda_{dd} = 280$ FIT, $\lambda_{du} = 120$ FIT (1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ /h)
- The subsystem is deployed in a **2oo3 voting architecture**.
- Proof test interval $T_i = 8760$ hours.
- The subsystem is certified **SC 3**.
- β-factor for CCF = 5%.

Calculate:
(i) SFF of the subsystem
(ii) Architectural constraint check (per IEC 61508-2 Table 2)
(iii) PFH (assuming high-demand mode of operation)
(iv) Verify whether the safety function achieves SIL 3.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Two Pillars (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Safety Integrity of a safety function, as defined in IEC 61508, has **two distinct pillars**, both of which must be satisfied to claim a given SIL:
1. **Random Hardware Integrity** — addresses random failures of physical components.
2. **Systematic Integrity** — addresses design faults, software bugs, specification errors, and integration errors.

**Pillar 1 — Random Hardware Integrity (2 Marks):**
- Quantified by **PFDavg** (low-demand) or **PFH** (high-demand/continuous mode).
- Controlled by **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**, **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**, and **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)**.
- Per IEC 61508-2 Table 2, Type A vs Type B elements have different HFT/SFF requirements.

**Pillar 2 — Systematic Integrity (2 Marks):**
- Qualitatively quantified by **Systematic Capability (SC)** of each element.
- Demonstrated via **Route 1s (Quality Mgmt + Design + V&V)** or **Route 2s (Prior Use)**.
- SC N ⇒ confidence of systematic safety at SIL N.

**Combined Determination of Overall SIL (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Overall SIL} = \text{MIN}(\text{Random HW SIL},\ \text{Systematic SIL})
$$

If a function has Random HW SIL = 3 but uses an element with only SC 2, the overall function is **SIL 2**.

**Diagram:**

```
        ┌──────────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  PILLAR 1            │     │  PILLAR 2            │
        │  Random HW Integrity │     │  Systematic Integrity│
        │  HFT, SFF, DC        │     │  SC N (Route 1s/2s)  │
        │  → PFDavg / PFH      │     │  → SC level          │
        └──────────┬───────────┘     └──────────┬───────────┘
                   │                            │
                   ▼                            ▼
              Random HW SIL               Systematic SIL
                   │                            │
                   └────────────┬───────────────┘
                                ▼
                       MIN of Both Pillars
                                │
                                ▼
                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                    │  OVERALL FUNCTION SIL │
                    └──────────────────────┘
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given (1 Mark):**
- $\lambda_s = 400$ FIT, $\lambda_{dd} = 280$ FIT, $\lambda_{du} = 120$ FIT
- 2oo3 architecture, $T_i = 8760$ h
- SC 3, β = 5%
- Target SIL: 3

**(i) SFF (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{total} &= (400 + 280 + 120) \times 10^{-9} = 800 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{/h} \\
SFF &= 1 - \dfrac{120}{800} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85 \quad \text{i.e., } 85\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Architectural Constraint Check (1 Mark):**
Type B, SIL 3 with SFF = 85% (which is in the range 60%–90%):
- Required $HFT \geq N = 3$.
- 2oo3 voting provides **HFT = 1** (since one failure is tolerated without loss of function).
- ❌ **Architectural constraint NOT met with HFT = 1.**

To meet the constraint, the designer must either:
- Use a higher-redundancy architecture (e.g., 2oo4 with HFT = 2 — still not enough), or
- Improve SFF to ≥ 90% (then HFT ≥ 2 allowed), or
- Re-target to SIL 2.

**(iii) PFH (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFH_{G} &= 6 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{ce} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \\
\text{Assuming } t_{ce} &= T_i = 8760 \text{ h} \\
&\approx \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} + 6(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_i \\
&= 0.05 \cdot (120 \times 10^{-9}) + 6 \cdot 0.95 \cdot (120 \times 10^{-9})^2 \cdot 8760 \\
&= 6 \times 10^{-9} + 6 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 8760 \\
&\approx 6 \times 10^{-9} + 7.19 \times 10^{-10} \\
&\approx 6.72 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{/h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

This lies in the **SIL 3 range** for PFH ($10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$ is wrong; SIL 3 requires $10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$).

Re-checking: $6.72 \times 10^{-9}$ is **below $10^{-8}$**, which is in the **SIL 4 range**. So PFH suggests even SIL 4 — but architectural constraint fails.

**(iv) Overall SIL Verification (1.5 Marks):**
- Random HW SIL from PFH: ~SIL 4 (over-qualified)
- Architectural constraint: **failed for SIL 3**
- Systematic SIL: SC 3 → SIL 3 ✓

**Overall SIL = MIN(SIL 4 from PFH, SIL 2 from architectural constraint, SIL 3 from SC) = SIL 2**

The function **cannot be claimed as SIL 3** as designed. The student should recommend: increase SFF to ≥ 90% (e.g., add diagnostics) or use higher HFT architecture (e.g., 3oo4 or 3oo5).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Systematic Capability Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating SC with SIL** — SC is an *element-level* property; SIL is a *function-level* property. Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Lose 1–2 marks if confused.)
> 2. **Forgetting the Architectural Constraint** — Students often compute PFDavg correctly but skip the HFT/SFF check against Table 2. Without this, the SIL claim is incomplete.
> 3. **Ignoring β-factor in redundancy** — For redundant architectures, common-cause failures must be included; otherwise PFH/PFDavg is **optimistic**.
> 4. **Mixing units (FIT vs /h)** — 1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ /h. Wrong unit conversion causes 10× or 100× errors. KTU expects SI units throughout.
> 5. **Omitting the "MIN of pillars" logic** — The overall SIL is a **minimum**, not a maximum or average. Show this explicitly in your answer.
> 6. **Not stating the Route** — When explaining SC, always mention **Route 1s** (and optionally 2s) explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **SC N** is the *systematic safety confidence* of an **element**, on a scale **1 to 4** (IEC 61508) or **1 to 3** (IEC 61511).
- ⚡ Systematic Capability addresses **design faults**, **specification faults**, **software bugs** — things that **failure rate data cannot quantify**.
- ⚡ Random HW integrity uses **HFT + SFF + DC → PFDavg / PFH**.
- ⚡ **Overall Function SIL = MIN (Random HW SIL, Systematic SIL)**.
- ⚡ **Route 1s** = QMS + Design + V&V (default, strongest). **Route 2s** = Prior Use Evidence. **Route 3s** = Software statistical.
- ⚡ For Type B elements, IEC 61508-2 Table 2: SIL N ⇒ HFT = N+1 (default) or HFT = N (if SFF ≥ 90%).
- ⚡ $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{du}}{\lambda_{total}}$; $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{dd}}{\lambda_d}$.
- ⚡ For SC 3 software: **MC/DC coverage ≥ 85%**, formal methods recommended, independent assessor required.
- ⚡ SC of an element **must be ≥ target SIL of the function** for the function to claim that SIL systematically.
- ⚡ β-factor (CCF) is independent of SC but **always** considered in redundant architectures.
- ⚡ PFH ranges: SIL 1 ($10^{-6}$–$10^{-5}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-7}$–$10^{-6}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-8}$–$10^{-7}$), SIL 4 ($10^{-9}$–$10^{-8}$) per hour.
- ⚡ PFDavg ranges: SIL 1 ($10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$), SIL 4 ($10^{-5}$–$10^{-4}$).
- ⚡ Real-world: Always verify **manufacturer's SC certificate + FMEDA report** when designing a SIL function.
- ⚡ KTU Keyword: "Justify the SC of each element using Route 1s evidence" is a frequently asked 7-mark sub-part.
- ⚡ Exam tip: For any SIL calculation, **show four steps**: (1) SFF, (2) Architectural constraint, (3) PFH/PFDavg, (4) MIN of pillars.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** is defined by **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511** as the *mean probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) fails to perform its intended safety action when a process demand occurs*. It is a dimensionless metric that quantifies the unreliability of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) operating in **low-demand mode of operation** (i.e., where the frequency of demands ≤ 1 per year). In mathematical form, PFD is expressed as a value between **0** and **1** (or equivalently between **0** and **10⁰**): $$PFD = \lim_{N \to \infty} \left( \frac{n_{f}}{N} \right)$$ where $N$ is the total number of demands and $n_{f}$ is the number of dangerous failures observed. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Smoke Detector:** > Imagine a smoke detector in your house. It sits silently for **365 days** doing nothing (low-demand mode). Suddenly, a real fire breaks out. The **Probability of Failure on Demand** answers one question: *"If a fire starts today, what is the chance that the alarm will NOT ring?"* > > - **PFD = 0.01 (SIL 2)** → 1 in 100 chance it fails. > - **PFD = 0.001 (SIL 3)** → 1 in 1000 chance it fails. > - **PFD = 0** → Perfect, but impossible in reality. The lower the PFD, the safer the system. The metric is the **bedrock of all SIL calculations** in process industries (oil & gas, chemical, nuclear, pharmaceutical). ## 1.3 Why PFD Matters in Functional Safety | Aspect | Significance | |--------|--------------| | **Legal compliance** | Mandated by IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 | | **Risk Reduction** | Directly yields Risk Reduction Factor (RRF = 1/PFD) | | **SIL determination** | Defines Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 → SIL 4) | | **Architecture selection** | 1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo3 dramatically alters PFD | | **Proof test planning** | Test interval T_proof is the dominant lever to reduce PFD | ## 1.4 Mode of Operation Context > [!IMPORTANT] > **PFD applies ONLY to Low-Demand Mode.** For High-Demand or Continuous Mode, the equivalent metric is **PFH (Probability of Failure per Hour)**, measured in failures/hour. | Mode | Metric | Typical Application | |------|--------|---------------------| | Low demand (≤ 1 demand/year) | **PFD** | Emergency Shutdown Systems, Fire & Gas | | High demand (> 1 demand/year) | **PFH** | Continuous process control interlocks | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Structural Anatomy of a SIF (and how PFD emerges) A Safety Instrumented Function is composed of **three subsystems**, each contributing its own share of dangerous undetected failures ($\lambda_{DU}$): 1. **Sensor Subsystem** (field detectors: pressure, temperature, level transmitters) 2. **Logic Solver Subsystem** (Safety PLC / Trip Amplifier) 3. **Final Element Subsystem** (shutdown valves, motor contactors, solenoid valves) The **overall PFD of the SIF** is the **sum** of the PFD contributions of each subsystem: $$PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{Sensors} + PFD_{Logic\,Solver} + PFD_{Final\,Element}$$ > [!NOTE] > The "Why" of summation: For the SIF to successfully shut down the process, **ALL three** subsystems must operate. The probability of joint success is the product of individual reliabilities, so the joint unreliability approximates the sum (valid only when individual PFDs are small, i.e., $PFD \ll 1$). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below assume **low-demand mode**, **constant dangerous undetected failure rate** $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures/hour), and **ideal proof testing** (tests restore the system to "as good as new" — a small but accepted simplification). ### Single Channel — 1oo1 (One out of One) $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof}}{2}$$ ### 1oo2 (One out of Two — redundant, voted) $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof})^{2}}{3} \quad \text{(for low } \lambda_{DU} T_{Proof} \text{)}$$ ### 2oo2 (Two out of Two) $$PFD_{avg}^{\,2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof}$$ ### 2oo3 (Two out of Three — highest availability) $$PFD_{avg}^{\,2oo3} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof})^{2}$$ ### Master Summary Table | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Unit | |----------|--------|----------------------|----------------------| | Dangerous Undetected Failure Rate | $\lambda_{DU}$ | — | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ per hour | | Proof Test Interval | $T_{Proof}$ | Periodic test interval | hours or years | | Mean PFD (1oo1) | $PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1}$ | $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof} / 2$ | dimensionless | | Mean PFD (1oo2) | $PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2}$ | $(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2} / 3$ | dimensionless | | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF$ | $1 / PFD_{avg}$ | dimensionless | | Safety Integrity Level | $SIL$ | Derived from $PFD_{avg}$ bucket | SIL 1 – SIL 4 | ### SIL Classification Table (Low-Demand Mode per IEC 61508) | SIL Level | PFD Range (per demand) | RRF Range | |-----------|------------------------|-----------| | SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | | SIL 4 | $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These formulas are the daily working tools of Functional Safety Engineers (TÜV-certified FSEng) who design Safety Instrumented Systems in refineries, LNG plants, and pharmaceutical batch reactors. Every Safety Requirement Specification (SRS) document contains a PFD budget that splits the SIL target across sensors, logic solver, and final elements. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean PFD for 1oo1 Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Prove that $PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof}}{2}$. ### Step 1 — Define the unreliability function For a single non-repaired channel with constant failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$, the probability that the channel is in a *failed state at time $t$ after the last proof test* (and that this failure has not been detected) is: $$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$ This is the standard exponential unreliability from reliability theory. ### Step 2 — Linearize for very small $\lambda_{DU} t$ Since $\lambda_{DU}$ is typically $\sim 10^{-6}$/hr and $t$ is at most a few years ($\sim 10^{4}$ hours), the product $\lambda_{DU} t$ is **far less than 1**. Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^{2}}{2!} - \dots$: $$PFD(t) \approx 1 - \left(1 - \lambda_{DU} t\right) = \lambda_{DU} t$$ This is the well-known **"useful life" approximation"** of unreliability. ### Step 3 — Compute the average PFD over one proof test interval Since the channel is restored to "as-good-as-new" after each proof test, the PFD rises linearly from 0 to $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ over the interval $[0, T_{Proof}]$. The **mean PFD** is the area under the $PFD(t)$ curve divided by $T_{Proof}$: $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_{Proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{Proof}} \lambda_{DU} \, t \, dt$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{Proof}} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{Proof}}$$ $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{Proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{Proof}^{2}}{2}$$ $$\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof}}{2}}$$ **Interpretation:** The "½" factor is the geometric mean of a linearly rising triangle — half the maximum PFD on average. Doubling the test interval **doubles the PFD**, directly degrading the SIL. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean PFD for 1oo2 Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Goal:** Prove that $PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2}}{3}$ for low $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$. ### Step 1 — Define the architecture In a **1oo2 (1-out-of-2)** voting group, the system **fails on demand only if BOTH channels fail**. The probability that both are in failed states at time $t$ is: $$PFD_{1oo2}(t) = [PFD_{1}(t)] \cdot [PFD_{2}(t)]$$ Assuming identical channels with the same $\lambda_{DU}$: $$PFD_{1oo2}(t) = [PFD(t)]^{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the linear approximation $$PFD_{1oo2}(t) = (\lambda_{DU} t)^{2}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the average $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_{Proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{Proof}} (\lambda_{DU} t)^{2} dt$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T_{Proof}} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_{Proof}}$$ $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot T_{Proof}^{3}}{3 \cdot T_{Proof}}$$ $$\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof})^{2}}{3}}$$ **Interpretation:** The factor $\frac{1}{3}$ comes from integrating $t^{2}$ over the interval. Notice the **quadratic dependence** on $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$: redundancy dramatically reduces PFD (e.g., from $\sim 10^{-2}$ for 1oo1 down to $\sim 10^{-4}$ for 1oo2 with the same components). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why Architecture Selection Matters | Architecture | $PFD_{avg}$ Expression | Order of Magnitude (typical) | SIL Achievable | |--------------|------------------------|------------------------------|----------------| | 1oo1 | $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof} / 2$ | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-3}$ (SIL 2) | Up to SIL 2 | | 1oo2 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2} / 3$ | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-6}$ (SIL 3) | Up to SIL 3 | | 2oo2 | $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ | $\sim 10^{-2}$ (SIL 1) | Up to SIL 2 | | 2oo3 | $(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2}$ | $\sim 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ (SIL 3) | SIL 3 | | 2oo4 | $6 (\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{3}$ | Very low | SIL 4 possible | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world rule of thumb:** To jump one SIL level, either (a) **halve the proof test interval** (1oo1 case) or (b) **add one redundant channel** (1oo1 → 1oo2). The latter is usually cheaper over the plant lifecycle. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) > [!IMPORTANT] > The diagram below maps the PFD budget across the three canonical subsystems. Each block carries its own $\lambda_{DU}$ contribution, and the total $PFD_{SIF}$ is the sum. ```mermaid flowchart LR ProcessHazard([Process Hazard Initiates]) --> SensorDemand{Demand on SIF} SensorDemand --> SensorBlock["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM
PFD_Sensor"] SensorBlock --> LogicBlock["LOGIC SOLVER
PFD_Logic"] LogicBlock --> FinalBlock["FINAL ELEMENT
Valve or Contactor
PFD_FE"] FinalBlock --> SafeState([Safe State Achieved]) SensorBlock -. contributes .-> BudgetNode["TOTAL PFD_SIF BUDGET
= PFD_S + PFD_L + PFD_FE"] LogicBlock -. contributes .-> BudgetNode FinalBlock -. contributes .-> BudgetNode ``` ## 4.2 PFD Computation Flow — From Failure Data to SIL ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Collect lambda_DU from FMEDA] --> B[Define T_proof interval] B --> C{Choose Architecture} C -->|1oo1| D1["PFD_1oo1 equals lambda_DU T_proof divided by 2"] C -->|1oo2| D2["PFD_1oo2 equals lambda_DU squared T_proof squared divided by 3"] C -->|2oo3| D3["PFD_2oo3 equals lambda_DU squared T_proof squared"] D1 --> E[Compute PFD_avg] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F{PFD_avg range} F -->|10 to 100| G1["SIL 1"] F -->|100 to 1000| G2["SIL 2"] F -->|1000 to 10000| G3["SIL 3"] F -->|10000 to 100000| G4["SIL 4"] G1 --> H[Document in SRS] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Block: 1oo1 vs 1oo2 Voting Group ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OneOutOfOne["ARCHITECTURE 1oo1"] A1[Channel A] --> Out1[Single Output to Trip] end subgraph OneOutOfTwo["ARCHITECTURE 1oo2"] A2[Channel A] --> VoteNode{OR Voter} B2[Channel B] --> VoteNode VoteNode --> Out2[Single Output to Trip] end OneOutOfOne -.-> CompareText["PFD 1oo2 much less than PFD 1oo1
because both channels must fail"] OneOutOfTwo -.-> CompareText ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading tip:** In **1oo2**, the system demands action as soon as *either* channel senses the hazard. Only when **both** fail simultaneously does the SIF fail on demand — hence the quadratic (square) reduction in PFD. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]` **Define Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD). State the SIL ranges as per IEC 61508 for low-demand mode.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** PFD is the mean probability that a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) fails to perform its intended safety action when a demand occurs, applicable to systems operating in low-demand mode (≤ 1 demand/year) as defined by IEC 61508. - **(2 marks)** SIL ranges: - SIL 1: $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ - SIL 2: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ - SIL 3: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$ - SIL 4: $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$ ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]` **What is the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)? How is it related to PFD?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1.5 marks)** RRF is the reciprocal of the average PFD and represents the factor by which the SIF reduces the risk from a hazardous event: $RRF = 1 / PFD_{avg}$. - **(1.5 marks)** Numerically, for SIL 3 with $PFD = 10^{-3}$, $RRF = 1000$, meaning the SIF reduces the risk of the hazardous event by a factor of one thousand. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Option A or Option B**. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]` **(a)** Define PFD and PFH. Explain how low-demand and high-demand modes of operation differ. State the IEC 61508 SIL tables for both modes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Safety Instrumented Function in an LPG storage terminal uses a single pressure transmitter ($\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr) connected in a **1oo1** architecture to a logic solver ($\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/hr), which trips a shutdown valve ($\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr). The proof test interval is **1 year** (8760 hours). Calculate: 1. The individual PFD of each subsystem. 2. The total PFD of the SIF. 3. The RRF and corresponding SIL. **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution for Question A **Solution to Part (a):** | Aspect | PFD (Low-Demand) | PFH (High-Demand) | |--------|------------------|-------------------| | Full form | Probability of Failure on Demand | Probability of Failure per Hour | | Applicable mode | ≤ 1 demand/year | > 1 demand/year (continuous) | | Units | Dimensionless (per demand) | Per hour | | SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ | | SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ | | SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ | | SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ | **Solution to Part (b):** Given data: - $\lambda_{DU,\,Sensor} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr - $\lambda_{DU,\,Logic} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/hr - $\lambda_{DU,\,Valve} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$/hr - $T_{Proof} = 8760$ hr (1 year) **Step 1 — Compute individual PFDs using 1oo1 formula $PFD = \lambda_{DU} T_{Proof} / 2$:** Sensor: $$PFD_{Sensor} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.01752}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$ Logic Solver: $$PFD_{Logic} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.00438}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$ Final Element (Valve): $$PFD_{Valve} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} = \frac{0.03504}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$ **Step 2 — Total PFD of the SIF (sum of contributions):** $$PFD_{SIF} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3} + 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$PFD_{SIF} = 2.847 \times 10^{-2}$$ **Step 3 — Risk Reduction Factor and SIL:** $$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{2.847 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 35.13$$ Since $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$ (specifically $2.85 \times 10^{-2}$), the SIF achieves **SIL 1**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently make these mistakes on the 14-mark question: > 1. **Using $\lambda_{DU}$ instead of $\lambda_{DU}/2$** — the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor is mandatory for 1oo1 mean PFD; missing it costs 2 marks. > 2. **Multiplying instead of summing** the subsystem PFDs — joint unreliability of independent channels is **additive**, not multiplicative, when $PFD \ll 1$. > 3. **Forgetting to convert $T_{Proof}$ to hours** when $\lambda_{DU}$ is given in per-hour units — a unit mismatch is the #1 silent killer of marks. --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]` **(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, explain the **1oo1, 1oo2, and 2oo2** voting architectures. Compare their PFD expressions and indicate which is suitable for high-SIL applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refinery uses a **1oo2** pressure transmitter pair with each transmitter having $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr. The proof test interval is **2 years** ($T_{Proof} = 17520$ hr). For the sensor subsystem alone, calculate: 1. The mean PFD. 2. The RRF and corresponding SIL. 3.
