Comment on whether the architecture achieves SIL 3. **(7 marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution for Question B **Solution to Part (a):** | Architecture | Diagram Description | PFD Formula | Typical SIL | |--------------|---------------------|-------------|-------------| | **1oo1** | Single channel; single output | $PFD = \lambda_{DU} T_{Proof} / 2$ | SIL 1 – SIL 2 | | **1oo2** | Two channels in parallel; either triggers trip | $PFD = (\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2} / 3$ | SIL 2 – SIL 3 | | **2oo2** | Two channels in series; both must trigger trip | $PFD = \lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ | SIL 1 – SIL 2 | **Best for high SIL:** **1oo2** is preferred because its PFD scales **quadratically** with $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$, offering one SIL level of improvement over 1oo1 with the same components. **Solution to Part (b):** Given: $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/hr per channel, $T_{Proof} = 17520$ hr. **Step 1 — Compute mean PFD for 1oo2:** $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof})^{2}}{3}$$ Compute the product first: $$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof} = (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17520 = 5.256 \times 10^{-2}$$ Square it: $$(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{Proof})^{2} = (5.256 \times 10^{-2})^{2} = 2.762 \times 10^{-3}$$ Divide by 3: $$PFD_{avg}^{\,1oo2} = \frac{2.762 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 9.21 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 2 — RRF and SIL:** $$RRF = \frac{1}{9.21 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1086$$ Since $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$ (specifically $9.21 \times 10^{-4}$ falls just under $10^{-3}$), the architecture lies **at the boundary between SIL 2 and SIL 3** — essentially achieving **SIL 3** marginally. **Step 3 — Comment:** With a PFD of $9.21 \times 10^{-4}$, the value is just below $10^{-3}$, placing it **firmly in SIL 3 territory**. To improve safety margin or accommodate the contribution of logic solver and final element (which will increase total SIF PFD), the engineer should either: - Reduce $T_{Proof}$ to 1 year, which halves $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ and quarters the squared PFD (giving $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$, comfortably SIL 3), or - Upgrade to 2oo3 architecture for further redundancy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Forgetting to square the product** — the 1oo2 formula has a **square**, not a single multiplication; missing this is a 3-mark deduction. > 2. **Mixing up the factor** ($\frac{1}{3}$ for 1oo2 vs $\frac{1}{2}$ for 1oo1) — board examiners specifically test whether the student knows which architecture applies. > 3. **Not commenting** on the SIL boundary — the question explicitly asks for a "comment," and a simple "SIL 3 achieved" without justification loses 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall — bookmark these bullets.** - ✅ **PFD** = Probability that a SIF fails when demanded; used in **low-demand mode** (≤ 1 demand/year). - ✅ **PFD is dimensionless** and lies between 0 and 1. - ✅ **PFH** (failures/hour) is used in **high-demand / continuous mode** — do NOT confuse the two. - ✅ **1oo1 PFD** = $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}}{2}$ — linear with $T_{Proof}$. - ✅ **1oo2 PFD** = $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2}}{3}$ — quadratic with $T_{Proof}$ → massive SIL improvement. - ✅ **2oo2 PFD** = $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ — no SIL improvement, useful for *spurious trip reduction*. - ✅ **2oo3 PFD** = $(\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof})^{2}$ — high availability AND high safety. - ✅ **Total SIF PFD** = sum of sensor + logic solver + final element PFDs. - ✅ **RRF** = $1 / PFD_{avg}$. - ✅ **SIL buckets (low-demand):** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$, SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$. - ✅ **Proof test interval** ($T_{Proof}$) is the **most economical lever** to lower PFD in operating plants. - ✅ **Assumptions baked into the formulas:** ideal proof testing (as-good-as-new), constant $\lambda_{DU}$, low $\lambda_{DU} T_{Proof}$ regime (so $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$). - ✅ **No cross-talk between PFH and PFD** in calculations — the SIL table for one does not apply to the other. ---

topics.title: Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** is the quantitative reliability metric used in functional safety engineering (defined rigorously in **IEC 61508** and adopted by **IEC 61511**, **ISO 26262**, and **IEC 62061**) that describes the **average frequency of dangerous (hazardous) failures of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) per hour of continuous operation in high-demand or continuous mode of operation**.

Mathematically, PFH is expressed as:

$$PFH = \frac{1}{MTBF_{dangerous}}$$

where $MTBF_{dangerous}$ is the **Mean Time Between Dangerous Failures** of the Safety-Related System (SRS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PFH applies *only* to **high-demand mode** (demand rate > 1 per year) and **continuous mode** of operation. For low-demand systems, the equivalent metric is **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)**. This distinction is a frequently tested concept in KTU University Examinations.

The formal units of PFH are:

$$[PFH] = \frac{1}{hour} = h^{-1}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you own a **commercial passenger aircraft autopilot system** that must actively prevent the plane from stalling. The autopilot operates **continuously** (every second of the flight). 

- If the autopilot's safety function (e.g., automatic nose-down trim to prevent stall) has a PFH of $10^{-7}$/h, this means: *in a fleet of 10 million such systems operating simultaneously, statistically one dangerous failure is expected every hour of cumulative operation.*
- Equivalently, in a single aircraft, you would expect **one dangerous autopilot failure roughly every 10,000 years** of continuous operation.

**Another Analogy — The Car Airbag System:** A modern airbag control unit (ACU) monitors crash sensors continuously. Its PFH tells the manufacturer: "Out of 1 billion hours of driving, how many hours will the airbag fail to deploy when needed?" A low PFH means extremely high reliability of the safety function.

## 1.3 Why PFH Matters in Functional Safety

PFH is the **primary determinant of the Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** classification for high-demand and continuous-mode safety functions. The lower the PFH, the higher the integrity, and the more rigorous the design, diagnostic coverage, and proof-testing requirements imposed by IEC 61508.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (Target Failure Measures) — IEC 61508-1 Table 2:**
> - **SIL 1:** PFH $\in [10^{-6}, 10^{-5})$ per hour
> - **SIL 2:** PFH $\in [10^{-7}, 10^{-6})$ per hour
> - **SIL 3:** PFH $\in [10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$ per hour
> - **SIL 4:** PFH $\in [10^{-9}, 10^{-8})$ per hour
>
> *Note: SIL 4 is rarely used in process industries; it is most common in nuclear and aerospace domains.*

## 1.4 Failure Categories Relevant to PFH

PFH is computed using only the **dangerous undetected** failure component, since:

1. **Safe failures (S):** Cause a false trip → system goes into safe state, no hazard.
2. **Dangerous detected failures (DD):** Diagnosed by automatic diagnostics → system forces a safe shutdown.
3. **Dangerous undetected failures (DU):** *These are the killers.* They remain latent until a real demand occurs or a proof test reveals them.

Therefore:

$$PFH = f(\lambda_{DU}, \text{architecture}, \text{diagnostic coverage}, \text{CCF}, T_{proof})$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Failure Rate Decomposition

Every component in a safety system has a total failure rate $\lambda$ (in failures per hour, FIT — **F**ailures **I**n **T**ime, where $1\,FIT = 10^{-9}/h$). This decomposes as:

$$\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_D = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$$

Where the indices mean:
- **S** = Safe failure, **D** = Dangerous failure
- **U** = Undetected, **D** = Detected (by automatic diagnostics)

For PFH calculation, **only the dangerous undetected portion $\lambda_{DU}$ matters**, because detected dangerous failures lead to a safe shutdown, while safe failures (detected or undetected) do not contribute to a hazardous event.

## 2.2 Modes of Operation — Why PFH Exists Separately from PFD

IEC 61508 explicitly defines **three modes of operation** for a safety function:

| Mode of Operation | Demand Rate | Primary Metric | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Demand** | $\le 1$ demand/year | **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) | Process interlocks, ESD valves |
| **High Demand** | $> 1$ demand/year | **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) | Turbine overspeed protection, burner management |
| **Continuous** | Demand keeps safety function active | **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) | Aircraft flight controls, process control loops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical justification: For high-demand/continuous mode, the *probability* of failure on a specific demand approaches the *frequency* of dangerous failures per unit time, hence PFH $\approx \lambda_{DU}$ (for a single-channel non-redundant system).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$ | Failure rate decomposition | Universal |
| 2 | $PFH_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}$ | Single-channel non-redundant | No diagnostics |
| 3 | $PFH_{1oo1,D} = \lambda_{DU}(1 - DC)$ | Single-channel with diagnostics | $DC$ = Diagnostic Coverage |
| 4 | $PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | 1-out-of-2 voted | $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime |
| 5 | $PFH_{2oo2} = 2\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | 2-out-of-2 voted (de-energize) | Used in de-energize-to-trip systems |
| 6 | $PFH_{2oo3} = 6\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | 2-out-of-3 voted | IEC 61508-6 formula |
| 7 | $t_{CE} = \frac{T_1}{2} + MTTR$ | Channel equivalent downtime | $T_1$ = proof test interval |
| 8 | $PFH = \frac{1}{MTBF_D}$ | Inverse of dangerous MTBF | Definition |
| 9 | $\beta = \beta_D + (1-\beta_D)\beta$ | Common cause factor split | IEC 61508-6 Annex D |
| 10 | $RRF = \frac{1}{PFH}$ | Risk Reduction Factor (continuous mode) | Engineering utility |
| 11 | $DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage ratio | $0 \le DC \le 1$ |
| 12 | $SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | Hardware fault tolerance constraint |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students frequently confuse $t_{CE}$ with $T_1$ (the proof test interval). $t_{CE}$ is the *equivalent* downtime used in PFH formulas, defined as $t_{CE} = T_1/2 + MTTR$, which represents the *average* time a channel will be in the dangerous-failed state before being detected and repaired.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of PFH in the Real World

PFH is the cornerstone of:
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) verification** during HAZOP/LOPA studies.
- **Quantitative risk assessment** — combining PFH with hazard rate $H$ yields the **event frequency** $f = H \times PFH$.
- **Hardware reliability budgeting** — allocating the maximum tolerable PFH to each subsystem (sensors, logic solver, final elements).
- **Functional safety audits** — IEC 61508 SIL certification requires demonstrated PFH values for the entire Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
- **Regulatory compliance** — process industries, railway signaling (EN 50128/50129), automotive (ISO 26262 → PMHF), and machinery (ISO 13849 → PFH$_{d}$) all mandate PFH computation.

## 2.5 Diagnostic Coverage and Its Effect on PFH

Diagnostic Coverage ($DC$) is the proportion of dangerous failures detected by automatic online diagnostics:

$$DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

For a 1oo1 architecture, increasing $DC$ **reduces PFH linearly**:

$$PFH_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}(1 - DC) = \lambda_D (1 - DC)$$

This is why **self-diagning smart sensors** dramatically improve PFH. For example, a smart pressure transmitter with $DC = 90\%$ reduces its PFH contribution to the SIF by an order of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PFH of a 1oo1 Architecture (Single Channel, No Diagnostics)

**Definition:** A 1oo1 (1-out-of-1) architecture is the simplest non-redundant safety channel. The safety function is performed by one component; if it fails dangerously, the safety function is lost.

**Step 1 — Start with the failure rate density.**

Let $\lambda_{DU}$ be the constant dangerous-undetected failure rate (in failures/hour). For a non-repaired system, the probability that the system is operating correctly at time $t$ is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the unreliability (probability of dangerous failure by time $t$):**

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}$$

**Step 3 — For small $\lambda_{DU} t$ (which is the typical operational regime, since $PFH \sim 10^{-7}$ and $t$ is hours), use the Taylor expansion:**

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} t + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} t)^2}{2!} - \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Truncate to first order (valid because $\lambda_{DU} t \ll 1$):**

$$F(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} t$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that PFH is the time-averaged rate of failure in continuous operation:**

$$PFH_{1oo1} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{F(T)}{T} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} T}}{T}$$

**Step 6 — Apply L'Hôpital's rule (numerator and denominator both $\to 0$ as $T \to \infty$ in the failure rate sense, but more precisely via the hazard rate definition):**

The instantaneous failure rate (hazard function) for an exponential distribution is **constant** and equal to $\lambda_{DU}$. For continuous operation, the average rate of dangerous failures per unit time converges to this hazard rate:

$$PFH_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{PFH_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \quad [\text{failures/hour}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PFH of a 1oo1 Architecture **With Diagnostics**

**Setup:** Diagnostics detect a fraction $DC$ of dangerous failures instantaneously, forcing the system to a safe state. Only $(1 - DC)$ of dangerous failures remain undetected and contribute to PFH.

**Step 1 — Split the dangerous failure rate into detected and undetected portions:**

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

$$\lambda_{DD} = DC \cdot \lambda_D, \quad \lambda_{DU} = (1 - DC) \cdot \lambda_D$$

**Step 2 — Only undetected dangerous failures contribute to the average failure-per-hour rate:**

$$PFH_{1oo1,DC} = \lambda_{DU} = (1 - DC) \cdot \lambda_D$$

**Step 3 — Numerical illustration:** If $\lambda_D = 10^{-6}/h$ and $DC = 0.9$:

$$PFH = (1 - 0.9) \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-7}/h$$

**Conclusion:** Adding 90% diagnostics improved the SIL classification from SIL 1 ($10^{-6}$) to SIL 2 ($10^{-7}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PFH of a 1oo2 Architecture (1-out-of-2 Voted)

**Architecture:** Two parallel channels; safety function is performed if **at least one** channel works. The system fails dangerously only when **both** channels have failed dangerously.

**Step 1 — Define variables:**
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate of each channel
- $\beta_D$ = fraction of failures that are common-cause dangerous (both channels fail simultaneously)
- $t_{CE}$ = channel equivalent mean downtime (average time a channel remains in the dangerous-failed state before being detected and repaired)
- $T_1$ = proof test interval
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair

**Step 2 — Decompose the total PFH into two terms:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = PFH_{CCF} + PFH_{Independent}$$

where:
- $PFH_{CCF}$ = contribution from common-cause dangerous failures
- $PFH_{Independent}$ = contribution from independent dangerous failures of both channels

**Step 3 — Compute the common-cause term.** A common-cause failure takes out both channels at once, with rate $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$:

$$PFH_{CCF} = \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the independent-failure term.** Both channels must fail independently. Channel A fails at rate $(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU}$ and remains undetected for time $t_{CE}$. The probability that Channel B fails during this window is $(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$.

The average rate of double-failure events per hour is the *product* of the single-channel failure rate and the probability that the second channel also fails within the downtime window:

$$PFH_{Independent} = (1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = (1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}$$

But considering both orders (A fails first, then B; or B fails first, then A), we get a factor of 2:

$$PFH_{Independent} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $t_{CE} = T_1/2 + MTTR$:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 \left(\frac{T_1}{2} + MTTR\right) + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example:** Let $\lambda_{DU} = 10^{-6}/h$, $\beta_D = 0.05$, $T_1 = 8760\,h$ (1 year), $MTTR = 8\,h$.

$t_{CE} = 8760/2 + 8 = 4388\,h$

Independent term: $2(0.95)^2 (10^{-6})^2 (4388) = 2 \times 0.9025 \times 10^{-12} \times 4388 = 7.92 \times 10^{-9}/h$

CCF term: $0.05 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-8}/h$

$$PFH_{1oo2} \approx 5.79 \times 10^{-8}/h \quad \Rightarrow \quad SIL\,3$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 1oo2 architecture dramatically reduces the **independent** failure term (order $\lambda_{DU}^2$), but the **common-cause** term remains linear in $\lambda_{DU}$. This is why IEC 61508 mandates a maximum $\beta$-factor for each SIL level (e.g., $\beta \le 5\%$ for SIL 2, $\beta \le 2\%$ for SIL 3).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: PFH of a 2oo2 Architecture

**Architecture:** Both channels must trip to perform the safety function (de-energize-to-trip, e.g., two relay contacts in series). The system fails dangerously if **either** channel has failed dangerously undetected.

**Step 1 — A single dangerous undetected failure in either channel is sufficient to disable the safety function.**

**Step 2 — Total PFH is the sum of failure rates of both channels (with common-cause adjustment):**

$$PFH_{2oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

Wait — this is approximately equal to $2\lambda_{DU}$ (no redundancy benefit!). But IEC 61508-6 includes a small second-order correction for the case where both channels are failed but the common-cause contribution differs.

The more rigorous IEC 61508-6 expression (for 2oo2 with the dangerous-failure-of-both-channels consideration) is:

$$PFH_{2oo2} = 2\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

This second-order term accounts for the rare case where both channels fail *independently* in the dangerous direction. However, the dominant term is $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$, meaning 2oo2 provides **little PFH improvement** over 1oo1 — its advantage is mainly in **fault tolerance** (the system can still detect faults and perform diagnostics).

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{PFH_{2oo2} = 2\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: PFH of a 2oo3 Architecture

**Architecture:** Three parallel channels; safety function is performed if **at least 2** of 3 work (majority voting). Common in turbine overspeed trips and railway signaling.

**Step 1 — System fails dangerously if 2 of 3 channels have failed dangerously undetected.**

**Step 2 — Number of ways to choose 2 failed channels out of 3: $\binom{3}{2} = 3$. Each pair contributes an independent-failure term, and the *reverse-order* product (A→B, B→A) doubles the count to 6.**

**Step 3 — Apply the same logic as 1oo2 to each pair:**

$$PFH_{Independent,2oo3} = 6 (1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}$$

**Step 4 — Add the common-cause term:**

$$PFH_{2oo3} = 6(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{PFH_{2oo3} = 6(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — End-to-End PFH Computation

**Problem:** A SIL 3 high-demand safety function uses a 1oo2D pressure transmitter arrangement, with each transmitter having $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}/h$, $\beta_D = 0.02$, proof test interval $T_1 = 4380\,h$ (6 months), and $MTTR = 4\,h$. Compute the PFH of the sensor subsystem.

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{CE}$:**

$$t_{CE} = \frac{T_1}{2} + MTTR = \frac{4380}{2} + 4 = 2190 + 4 = 2194\,h$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo2 formula:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1 - 0.02)^2 (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 (2194) + (0.02)(5 \times 10^{-7})$$

**Step 4 — Compute term-by-term:**

- $(1-0.02)^2 = (0.98)^2 = 0.9604$
- $(5 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-14} = 2.5 \times 10^{-13}$
- $2 \times 0.9604 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \times 2194 = 2 \times 0.9604 \times 5.485 \times 10^{-10} = 1.0536 \times 10^{-9}$
- $\beta_D \lambda_{DU} = 0.02 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}$

**Step 5 — Sum the contributions:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 1.0536 \times 10^{-9} + 1.0 \times 10^{-8} = 1.106 \times 10^{-8}/h$$

**Step 6 — Convert to a familiar unit (FIT):**

$$PFH = 1.106 \times 10^{-8}/h = 11.06\,FIT$$

**Step 7 — SIL classification:**

Since $10^{-8} \le PFH < 10^{-7}$, the **sensor subsystem achieves SIL 3**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PFH Computation Workflow for 1oo2 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start PFH Computation] --> B[Define System Architecture]
    B --> C[Collect Component Failure Data]
    C --> D[Extract lambda_DU per Channel]
    D --> E[Determine Beta_D Factor]
    E --> F[Compute t_CE = T1/2 + MTTR]
    F --> G{Architecture Type?}
    G -->|1oo1| H1[Apply PFH = lambda_DU x 1-DC]
    G -->|1oo2| H2[Apply PFH = 2 1-Beta^2 lambda_DU^2 t_CE + Beta_D lambda_DU]
    G -->|2oo2| H3[Apply PFH = 2 lambda_DU^2 t_CE + Beta_D lambda_DU]
    G -->|2oo3| H4[Apply PFH = 6 1-Beta^2 lambda_DU^2 t_CE + Beta_D lambda_DU]
    H1 --> I[Sum Subsystem PFH Values]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Compute Total SIF PFH]
    J --> K[Compare with SIL Target Range]
    K --> L{PFH within SIL Target?}
    L -->|Yes| M[SIL Achieved - Document Evidence]
    L -->|No| N[Redesign: Increase DC, Reduce T1, Add Redundancy]
    N --> C
    M --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Safety Function Decomposition Showing PFH Contribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Subsystem PFH_S] --> SUM[Total SIF PFH]
    L1[Logic Solver Subsystem PFH_L] --> SUM
    F1[Final Element Subsystem PFH_F] --> SUM
    SUM --> R{Risk Reduction Factor}
    R --> SIL[SIL Classification]
    SIL --> OP1[SIL 1: 10-6 to 10-5]
    SIL --> OP2[SIL 2: 10-7 to 10-6]
    SIL --> OP3[SIL 3: 10-8 to 10-7]
    SIL --> OP4[SIL 4: 10-9 to 10-8]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Rate Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Total Failure Rate lambda] --> L1[Safe Failures lambda_S]
    L0 --> L2[Dangerous Failures lambda_D]
    L1 --> L1A[Safe Undetected lambda_SU]
    L1 --> L1B[Safe Detected lambda_SD]
    L2 --> L2A[Dangerous Detected lambda_DD]
    L2 --> L2B[Dangerous Undetected lambda_DU]
    L2A --> DCC[Diagnostic Coverage DC = lambda_DD / lambda_D]
    L2B --> PFF[Used for PFH and PFD Calculations]
    DCC --> PFF
    PFF --> SILT[SIL Target Compliance Check]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Proof Test Interval Impact on PFH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Proof Test Interval T1] --> CE[t_CE = T1/2 + MTTR]
    MTTR[MTTR] --> CE
    CE --> IND[Independent Failure Term lambda_DU squared times t_CE]
    IND --> PFH12[1oo2 PFH]
    IND --> PFH23[2oo3 PFH]
    CCF[Beta_D lambda_DU] --> PFH12
    CCF --> PFH23
    PFH12 --> RSLT[SIL Verification]
    PFH23 --> RSLT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram (4.1) is the master computation workflow a Functional Safety Engineer would follow during the SIL verification phase of a project. The second (4.2) shows how subsystem-level PFH values combine (as a sum, assuming independence) to yield the total Safety Instrumented Function PFH. The third (4.3) decomposes the failure rate, identifying that only $\lambda_{DU}$ matters for PFH. The fourth (4.4) shows how $T_1$ and $MTTR$ influence PFH through $t_{CE}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH). In which modes of operation is it used as the primary safety metric, and what is its unit?**

**Model Answer:**

PFH is defined as the average frequency of dangerous failures of a Safety-Related System per hour of operation in a high-demand or continuous mode. It is the reciprocal of the Mean Time Between Dangerous Failures ($MTBF_D$). The unit is $\text{failures per hour}$ (h$^{-1}$) or equivalently **FIT** (1 FIT = $10^{-9}$/h). PFH is the primary metric for safety functions operating in **high-demand mode** (demand rate > 1 per year) and **continuous mode**, as per IEC 61508.

> [!TIP]
> Marks split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for modes of operation, 1 mark for unit.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**List the SIL target failure measure ranges for PFH as defined by IEC 61508-1, and explain why a 1oo2 architecture achieves a lower PFH than a 1oo1 architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

As per IEC 61508-1 Table 2, the SIL target failure measures for PFH are:

- **SIL 1:** $10^{-6} \le PFH < 10^{-5}$ per hour
- **SIL 2:** $10^{-7} \le PFH < 10^{-6}$ per hour
- **SIL 3:** $10^{-8} \le PFH < 10^{-7}$ per hour
- **SIL 4:** $10^{-9} \le PFH < 10^{-8}$ per hour

A 1oo2 architecture achieves a lower PFH than 1oo1 because the system fails dangerously only when **both channels have failed dangerously**, making the dominant independent-failure term proportional to $\lambda_{DU}^2$ rather than $\lambda_{DU}$. The redundancy reduces the *independent* failure contribution by orders of magnitude; however, the common-cause failure term $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ still limits the achievable PFH reduction.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Choice 1: Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the PFH expression for a 1oo2 (1-out-of-2) safety architecture, clearly defining all symbols used. Discuss the role of common-cause failures ($\beta$-factor) in limiting the achievable PFH.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A SIL 3 high-demand safety function uses a 1oo2D sensor subsystem. Each sensor has $\lambda_{DU} = 8 \times 10^{-7}/h$, $\beta_D = 0.03$, and the system uses a proof test interval $T_1 = 4380\,h$ with $MTTR = 6\,h$. Compute the PFH of the sensor subsystem and verify the SIL achievement.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define symbols:**
- $\lambda_{DU}$: Dangerous undetected failure rate per channel (per hour)
- $\beta_D$: Common-cause dangerous failure fraction (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $T_1$: Proof test interval (hours)
- $MTTR$: Mean Time To Repair (hours)
- $t_{CE}$: Channel equivalent mean downtime (hours)

**Step 2 — Express $t_{CE}$:**

$$t_{CE} = \frac{T_1}{2} + MTTR$$

The $T_1/2$ term arises because, on average, a dangerous undetected failure occurs at the midpoint of the proof test interval.

**Step 3 — Split the failure mechanism:**

The total PFH consists of:
- **Independent double failure:** Both channels fail independently
- **Common-cause failure:** A single event takes out both channels

**Step 4 — Common-cause term:**

A common-cause dangerous failure occurs with rate $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$:

$$PFH_{CCF} = \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 5 — Independent failure term:**

Consider Channel A failing first at rate $(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU}$. It remains undetected for an average time $t_{CE}$. During this window, Channel B must also fail independently at rate $(1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU}$:

$$PFH_{A\text{-then-}B} = (1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1-\beta_D)\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = (1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}$$

**Step 6 — Symmetry multiplier:**

By symmetry, Channel B can fail first, then A. The factor of 2 gives:

$$PFH_{Independent} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}$$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Step 8 — Role of $\beta_D$:**

The common-cause term $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ is **linear** in $\lambda_{DU}$ and **dominant** for high $\beta_D$. Even with perfect redundancy, PFH cannot be reduced below $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$. This is why IEC 61508 specifies maximum permissible $\beta$-factors per SIL level (e.g., $\beta \le 5\%$ for SIL 2 and $\beta \le 2\%$ for SIL 3 when used in 1oo2 architecture). Methods to reduce $\beta$ include physical separation, diverse technologies, and environmental isolation.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $t_{CE}$:**

$$t_{CE} = \frac{4380}{2} + 6 = 2190 + 6 = 2196\,h$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1oo2 formula:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$PFH_{1oo2} = 2(0.97)^2 (8 \times 10^{-7})^2 (2196) + (0.03)(8 \times 10^{-7})$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term:**

- $(0.97)^2 = 0.9409$
- $(8 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 64 \times 10^{-14} = 6.4 \times 10^{-13}$
- $2 \times 0.9409 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-13} \times 2196 = 2 \times 0.9409 \times 1.4054 \times 10^{-9} = 2.645 \times 10^{-9}$
- $\beta_D \lambda_{DU} = 0.03 \times 8 \times 10^{-7} = 2.4 \times 10^{-8}$

**Step 5 — Sum:**

$$PFH = 2.645 \times 10^{-9} + 2.4 \times 10^{-8} = 2.665 \times 10^{-8}/h$$

**Step 6 — Convert to FIT:**

$$PFH = 2.665 \times 10^{-8}/h = 26.65\,FIT$$

**Step 7 — Verify SIL:**

Since $10^{-8} \le 2.665 \times 10^{-8} < 10^{-7}$, the sensor subsystem **achieves SIL 3**. ✓

---

### Choice 2: Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the PFH expressions for 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3 architectures. Explain which architecture is most suitable for (i) an energize-to-trip burner management system, and (ii) a de-energize-to-trip emergency shutdown valve.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A safety logic solver has a dangerous undetected failure rate of $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h$ with a Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) of 85% and Diagnostic Coverage (DC) of 60%. Compute the PFH, and discuss how the PFH would change if the DC were improved to 90%.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Architecture Comparison Table:**

| Architecture | PFH Expression | Redundancy Benefit | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\lambda_{DU}(1-DC)$ | None (single channel) | Low-criticality, low-demand |
| 1oo2 | $2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | Very high (independent term is $\lambda^2$) | Energize-to-trip, high-demand |
| 2oo2 | $2\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | Low (linear $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ dominates) | De-energize-to-trip with diagnostics |
| 2oo3 | $6(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ | Very high (factor of 6 in independent term) | Turbine overspeed, railway signaling |

**Step 2 — For an energize-to-trip burner management system (i):**

Use **1oo2 architecture**. In an energize-to-trip system, the safety function requires *at least one* channel to be active (energy present to keep the burner running safely). Redundancy ensures that the system tolerates a single channel failure. The 1oo2 architecture provides excellent PFH because the dominant independent-failure term is quadratic in $\lambda_{DU}$.

**Step 3 — For a de-energize-to-trip ESD valve (ii):**

Use **1oo2 architecture with diagnostics**, but the underlying principle favors a **1oo2D or 2oo2 with continuous diagnostics**. Since the system is fail-safe (loss of energy = trip), the safety function is preserved even if one channel fails. The 2oo2 architecture is also used to provide **fault tolerance against dangerous detected failures** that would otherwise cause a spurious trip.

**Step 4 — Summary justification:**

For high-demand safety functions requiring **maximum risk reduction**, the 1oo2 or 2oo3 architectures are preferred due to the $\lambda^2$ scaling. For fail-safe systems (de-energize to trip), 2oo2 with strong diagnostics is appropriate because fault tolerance is the priority rather than PFH reduction.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Recall the definitions:**

- $SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$
- $DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$

**Step 2 — Given data:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h$
- $SFF = 85\% = 0.85$
- $DC = 60\% = 0.60$

**Step 3 — Compute $\lambda_{total}$ from SFF:**

$$SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

$$\lambda_{total} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{1 - SFF} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{0.15} = 1.333 \times 10^{-7}/h$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\lambda_D$:**

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{total} - \lambda_S = \lambda_{total} - (1 - SFF)\lambda_{total} = SFF \cdot \lambda_{total} - \lambda_{SU}$$

Actually, using a simpler route: $SFF = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD})/\lambda_{total}$, so $\lambda_D = (1-SFF) \cdot \lambda_{total} = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h$ (which equals $\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$). However, $\lambda_{DD}$ is part of $\lambda_D$:

$$\lambda_D = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{1 - DC} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{0.4} = 5 \times 10^{-8}/h$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\lambda_{DD}$:**

$$\lambda_{DD} = \lambda_D \cdot DC = 5 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.6 = 3 \times 10^{-8}/h$$

(Verification: $\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-8} + 2 \times 10^{-8} = 5 \times 10^{-8} = \lambda_D$ ✓)

**Step 6 — Compute PFH for 1oo1 with $DC = 60\%$:**

$$PFH_{DC=60\%} = \lambda_{DU}(1 - DC) = 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.4 = 8 \times 10^{-9}/h$$

> Wait — careful. The formula $PFH = \lambda_{DU}(1-DC)$ only applies when $\lambda_{DU}$ is the *total* dangerous rate. Here $\lambda_{DU}$ is already specified as the undetected portion. The PFH is therefore simply:
>
> $$PFH = \lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h \quad (\text{with } DC = 60\%)$$
>
> Diagnostics reduces the *demand* on the system but does not retroactively change $\lambda_{DU}$ for the 1oo1 case. The effect of DC is more pronounced in redundant systems.

**Step 7 — Re-compute the effect of improving $DC$ to 90%:**

If we keep $\lambda_{total} = 1.333 \times 10^{-7}/h$ fixed, and $SFF$ improves, then the *original* $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h$ was based on a $DC = 60\%$ assumption. The component's intrinsic dangerous failure rate is $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-8}/h$.

With $DC = 90\%$:

$$\lambda_{DU,new} = (1 - 0.9) \times 5 \times 10^{-8} = 0.5 \times 10^{-8} = 5 \times 10^{-9}/h$$

**Step 8 — Compare PFH values:**

- With $DC = 60\%$: $PFH = 2 \times 10^{-8}/h$ (SIL 3)
- With $DC = 90\%$: $PFH = 5 \times 10^{-9}/h$ (SIL 4)

**Step 9 — Conclusion:**

Improving the Diagnostic Coverage from 60% to 90% **reduces the PFH by a factor of 4**, moving the logic solver from SIL 3 to SIL 4 compliance. This demonstrates the immense value of robust online diagnostics in functional safety design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost on PFH Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit errors (–1 mark):** Writing PFH as a dimensionless probability. PFH is a *rate* with units of $h^{-1}$. Some students mistakenly give it as a probability.
> 2. **Confusing PFD and PFH (–2 marks):** PFD applies to low-demand mode; PFH applies to high-demand/continuous mode. The examiner will deduct for using PFD formulas in a PFH question.
> 3. **Omitting $\beta_D$ term (–2 marks):** The common-cause failure term must always be included in redundant architectures. Forgetting $\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$ is a common, costly mistake.
> 4. **Wrong $t_{CE}$ formula (–1 to 2 marks):** $t_{CE} = T_1/2 + MTTR$, not $T_1/2$, not $T_1$. Marks are deducted for incorrect use.
> 5. **Summing PFH linearly across subsystems without context (–1 mark):** The SIF-level PFH is the *sum* of subsystem PFH values (sensor + logic solver + final element), but only when those subsystems are architecturally independent.
> 6. **Confusing DC and SFF (–1 mark):** $DC$ refers to fraction of *dangerous* failures detected; $SFF$ refers to fraction of *all* failures that are safe. They are different ratios.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH):**
>
> - **PFH** is the **frequency** of dangerous failures per hour, used in **high-demand** and **continuous** modes of operation.
> - PFH is the **inverse of MTBF for dangerous failures**; its unit is $h^{-1}$ or FIT ($10^{-9}/h$).
> - **SIL classification from PFH:**
>   * SIL 1: $[10^{-6}, 10^{-5})$
>   * SIL 2: $[10^{-7}, 10^{-6})$
>   * SIL 3: $[10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$
>   * SIL 4: $[10^{-9}, 10^{-8})$
> - **Only $\lambda_{DU}$ contributes to PFH**; safe failures and detected dangerous failures do not.
> - **1oo1 PFH:** $\lambda_{DU}(1 - DC)$
> - **1oo2 PFH:** $2(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$
> - **2oo2 PFH:** $2\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$
> - **2oo3 PFH:** $6(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta_D \lambda_{DU}$
> - **$t_{CE} = T_1/2 + MTTR$** — the equivalent channel downtime used in redundant architecture PFH formulas.
> - **Redundancy** reduces the **independent** failure term quadratically (order $\lambda_{DU}^2$).
> - **Common-cause failures ($\beta_D \lambda_{DU}$)** set a **floor** on PFH; cannot be reduced by redundancy.
> - **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** reduces PFH by lowering the effective $\lambda_{DU}$.
> - **Total SIF PFH** = sum of sensor + logic solver + final element PFH contributions.
> - **PFH in continuous mode** is equivalent to the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** inverse: $RRF = 1/PFH$.
> - **Standards:** IEC 61508 (generic), IEC 61511 (process), IEC 62061 (machinery), ISO 26262 (automotive → PMHF), EN 50128/50129 (railway).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Safety Time (PST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Process Safety Time (PST) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (IEC 61511 / KTU 2024 Terminology) **Process Safety Time (PST)** is defined by **IEC 61511-1 (Clause 3.2.65)** as *the time period between a failure occurring in the process (or its control system) and the occurrence of a hazardous event if no protective action is initiated by the Safety Instrumented System (SIS)*. In simpler KTU-board language: > [!IMPORTANT] > **PST is a property of the PROCESS, not of the Safety Instrumented System (SIS).** It is the "deadline" within which the SIF must finish its safety action — otherwise the process crosses into a hazardous state. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Boiling Milk on the Stove Imagine milk boiling over on a gas stove: | Stage | Real-Life Event | SIS Equivalent | |---|---|---| | **t = 0** | You turn the stove on but forget to watch | Process deviation begins | | **t = 1 min** | Milk starts rising | Sensor detects high level | | **t = 1.5 min** | You turn off the gas | Final element closes valve | | **t = 2 min** | Milk would have overflowed | **Process crosses into hazard** | The interval between *forgetting the stove* (failure) and *milk boiling over* (hazard) is your **Process Safety Time (PST ≈ 2 min)**. Your corrective response (turning off gas) **must** happen within that 2-minute window. If you take 3 minutes, the safety function has failed its purpose. ### Why PST is the Cornerstone of SIS Design > [!NOTE] > Without knowing PST, an engineer **cannot** determine: > 1. The required **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** of the SIF > 2. The maximum permissible **response time** of sensors, logic solver, and final elements > 3. The required **test interval** (proof test interval) of the SIF **Key Metrics in Bold** (KTU 2024 expected knowledge): - **Process Safety Time (PST)** — measured in **seconds** - **SIF Response Time (tSIF)** — sum of sensor + logic + final element delays - **Demand Rate (λ)** — frequency of process demands on the SIF, per year - **PFDavg** — Average Probability of Failure on Demand (dimensionless, between 0 and 1) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of PST The PST framework rests on three sequential building blocks: 1. **Initiating Event Occurs** (process upset: overpressure, high temperature, loss of cooling, etc.) 2. **Process Propagates Toward Hazard** — during this propagation window, the SIF *must* complete its action 3. **Hazardous State Reached** — point of no return; SIF action arriving here is TOO LATE > [!IMPORTANT] > The SIF must **detect → decide → act** all within PST. This is the cardinal rule of functional safety design per **IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.5.1.2**. ### 2.2 The Three Time Budgets Inside PST $$ PST = t_{sensor} + t_{logic\ solver} + t_{final\ element} + t_{margin} $$ - **t_sensor** — detection lag of the field sensor (e.g., pressure transmitter response) - **t_logic solver** — scan time + I/O processing of the Safety PLC / relay - **t_final element** — stroke time of the shut-down valve (including purge, actuator extension) - **t_margin** — engineering safety margin (typically 20–50% of calculated time) ### 2.3 Why PST is NOT a Function of the SIF This is a **favorite KTU conceptual question**. The PST depends purely on: - **Process dynamics** (volume, flow rate, heat capacity, reaction kinetics) - **Inventory** of hazardous material - **Mitigation** provided by the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) before the SIF triggers The SIF is *designed to fit inside* PST — not the other way around. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with **IEC 61511** and the KTU 2024 Functional Safety syllabus (Module 1). | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $PST \geq t_{SIF} = t_{S} + t_{L} + t_{FE}$ | SIF must finish before PST expires | seconds (s) | | 2 | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} PFD(t)\, dt$ | Average probability of failure on demand over proof-test interval | dimensionless | | 3 | $PFD_{avg} \leq 10^{-(\text{SIL}+1)}$ | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor demanded of the SIF | dimensionless | | 5 | $MTTFS = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Mean Time To Fail Spurious (for diagnostic coverage) | hours | | 6 | $\lambda_{D} = \dfrac{PFD_{avg}}{T_{proof}}$ (approx.) | Dangerous failure rate (low-demand mode) | per hour | | 7 | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2}$ | Channel Equivalent Mean Down Time (IEC 61508) | hours | | 8 | $f_{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}}$ | Average demand frequency on proof-test interval | per hour | > [!WARNING] > In **markdown tables**, never use the bare vertical bar `|` for absolute value. Use **\vert** or **\mid** instead, e.g. $PST = \vert t_{hazard} - t_{init} \vert$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Oil & Gas:** Designing High-Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS) — PST is typically 5–30 seconds for a fired heater tube rupture case. - **Chemical Reactors:** Exothermic runaway reactions can have PST as short as **2–10 seconds**. - **Power Plants:** Steam drum level protection — PST can extend to several minutes. - **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** PST is often the **critical design constraint** when selecting SIL of the SIF. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: SIF Response Time Constraint Inside PST **Starting Premise:** For a SIF to successfully protect the process, the worst-case SIF response time must be less than or equal to PST. $$ t_{SIF}^{worst} = t_{S}^{max} + t_{L}^{max} + t_{FE}^{max} $$ The constraint becomes: $$ t_{S}^{max} + t_{L}^{max} + t_{FE}^{max} \leq PST $$ Rearranging to find the maximum allowable final-element stroke time (a common KTU problem): $$ t_{FE}^{max} \leq PST - t_{S}^{max} - t_{L}^{max} $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A chemical reactor has the following data: > - Process Safety Time (PST) = **12 s** > - Sensor detection time (max) = **1.5 s** > - Logic solver scan time (max) = **0.5 s** > - Final element stroke time (max) = **8 s** > - Required safety margin = **20% of PST** > > **Determine:** > (a) Is the existing SIF compliant with PST? > (b) If not, what is the maximum permissible final-element stroke time? **Step 1 — Compute total SIF response time (without margin):** $$ t_{SIF} = 1.5 + 0.5 + 8 = 10\ \text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute required safety margin:** $$ t_{margin} = 0.20 \times 12 = 2.4\ \text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute effective available time for the SIF:** $$ t_{available} = PST - t_{margin} = 12 - 2.4 = 9.6\ \text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Compare:** $$ t_{SIF} = 10\ \text{s} \quad \text{vs.} \quad t_{available} = 9.6\ \text{s} $$ Since $10 > 9.6$, the SIF is **NON-COMPLIANT**. **Step 5 — Compute the maximum allowable final-element stroke time:** $$ t_{FE}^{max} = t_{available} - t_{S}^{max} - t_{L}^{max} $$ $$ t_{FE}^{max} = 9.6 - 1.5 - 0.5 = 7.6\ \text{s} $$ **Conclusion:** The final element must be replaced with one whose stroke time is **≤ 7.6 s**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between PFDavg, Demand Rate, and PST In low-demand mode, the SIF must operate before the process crosses into the hazard. From reliability theory: $$ PFD_{avg} \leq \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{demand} \cdot PST} $$ **Derivation logic:** - Probability that a demand occurs in any given second: $\lambda_{demand}$ (per year, converted to per second) - Probability that the SIF is in a dangerous-failed state at that instant: $PFD_{avg}$ - Joint probability of (demand occurs) AND (SIF is unavailable): $\lambda_{demand} \cdot PFD_{avg}$ - For acceptable risk: this joint probability must be less than 1/PST > [!NOTE] > This equation is **fundamental** in **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** as taught in KTU Module 2, but the foundation is laid in Module 1 (PST). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PST Categories in Industry | Industry Sector | Typical PST Range | Criticality | Typical SIL Demanded | |---|---|---|---| | Exothermic batch reactor | 2 – 10 s | **Very High** | SIL 2 to SIL 3 | | Oil & Gas separator (HIPPS) | 5 – 30 s | High | SIL 2 to SIL 3 | | Fired heater tube | 10 – 60 s | High | SIL 2 | | Steam boiler drum level | 60 – 300 s | Medium | SIL 1 to SIL 2 | | LNG storage tank overflow | 600 – 1800 s | Medium | SIL 1 | | Storage tank overfill (low reactivity) | 1800 – 3600 s | Low | SIL 1 | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: PST as the Time Envelope Around a SIF Action ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process in Normal Operation] --> B[Initiating Event: Process Upset] B --> C{Time equals zero} C --> D[Sensor Detects Deviation] D --> E[Logic Solver Triggers SIF] E --> F[Final Element Acts: Valve Closes] F --> G[Process Returns to Safe State] B --> H[Process Continues to Worsen] H --> I[Hazardous Event Occurs] I -.->|Time equals PST| J[PST Window Expired] style A fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32 style G fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32 style J fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828 style I fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828 ``` ### 4.2 Timing Diagram: How PST Encompasses the SIF Action ```mermaid gantt title PST Timeline Showing SIF Sub-Intervals dateFormat X axisFormat %S section Process Process Deviation (Initiating Event) :a1, 0, 1s Process Propagating Toward Hazard :a2, 1s, 12s section SIF Sub-Tasks Sensor Detection :crit, b1, 1s, 1.5s Logic Solver Processing :crit, b2, 2.5s, 0.5s Final Element Stroke :crit, b3, 3s, 8s Safety Margin 20 percent :crit, b4, 11s, 1s section Outcome Process Returns to Safe State :milestone, c1, 11s, 0s Hazardous Event Reached :milestone, c2, 12s, 0s ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PST Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart TD p1[Process Dynamics] --> q1[Determine PST from
Process Simulation] q1 --> r1[Identify SIF Components] r1 --> r2[Sensor] r1 --> r3[Logic Solver] r1 --> r4[Final Element] r2 --> s1[Sum up Worst Case Times] r3 --> s1 r4 --> s1 s1 --> t1{tSIF within PST?} t1 -->|Yes| u1[SIF Compliant
Proceed to SIL Verification] t1 -->|No| v1[Redesign SIF
Reduce Response Time] v1 --> r1 u1 --> w1[Compute PFDavg] w1 --> x1[Verify PFDavg within
SIL Target Band] style p1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style u1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32 style v1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c62828 style x1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q1a]** > **Q1.** Define Process Safety Time (PST). Why is it considered a property of the *process* and not the *SIS*? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Process Safety Time (PST) is the time period between the occurrence of a process failure (or its initiating event) and the point at which the process reaches a hazardous state, assuming no safety action is taken. It is a property of the **process** because it is governed by process parameters such as inventory, reaction kinetics, flow rates, heat capacity, and the propagation rate of the disturbance — *not* by the SIF hardware. The SIF is *designed to operate within* the PST, and if the SIF's response time exceeds PST, protection is impossible. **(3 marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1b]** > **Q2.** List the three sub-components whose response times must be summed to compare against PST. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three sub-components are: 1. **Sensor detection time (tS)** — time taken by the field sensor to detect the deviation 2. **Logic solver processing time (tL)** — scan time of the Safety PLC / relay logic 3. **Final element stroke time (tFE)** — time taken by the shut-down valve to fully actuate Their sum must satisfy: $t_{S} + t_{L} + t_{FE} \leq PST$. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q2]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of Process Safety Time (PST) and show how the SIF response time must fit within it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydrocracker reactor has the following data: PST = 8 s, sensor delay = 1 s, logic solver delay = 0.3 s, final element stroke time = 5 s, safety margin required = 25% of PST. Check whether the SIF is compliant and, if not, find the maximum permissible final element stroke time. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Conceptual Block Diagram (7 marks):** A process is initially in normal state. At t = 0, an initiating event (e.g., loss of cooling) occurs. The process begins deviating from safe operating limits. Three parallel sub-tasks of the SIF must complete before the process reaches the hazardous condition: - Sensor detects the deviation (typical delay 0.5–2 s) - Logic solver processes input and energises output (typical 50–500 ms) - Final element (e.g., shut-down valve) moves to safe position (typical 2–10 s) The total of these three, plus an engineering safety margin, must be **strictly less than PST**. If the sum equals or exceeds PST, the SIF cannot protect the process. The SIF must be redesigned to reduce any of the three sub-component times. *(Full block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 above should be reproduced on the answer script for full 7 marks.)* **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Compute the safety margin. $$ t_{margin} = 0.25 \times 8 = 2\ \text{s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the effective time available for SIF action. $$ t_{available} = PST - t_{margin} = 8 - 2 = 6\ \text{s} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the actual SIF response time. $$ t_{SIF} = 1 + 0.3 + 5 = 6.3\ \text{s} $$ **Step 4:** Compare. $$ t_{SIF} = 6.3\ \text{s} \quad > \quad t_{available} = 6\ \text{s} $$ **Verdict:** SIF is **NON-COMPLIANT** with PST. **(1 mark)** **Step 5:** Find the maximum permissible final-element stroke time. $$ t_{FE}^{max} = t_{available} - t_{S} - t_{L} = 6 - 1 - 0.3 = 4.7\ \text{s} $$ **Conclusion:** The final element must be replaced with one whose stroke time is **at most 4.7 s**. **(1 mark for the final statement, 5 marks for working)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the relationship between PST, demand rate, and PFDavg. Derive the inequality that must be satisfied for the SIF to provide adequate risk reduction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a SIF, the following data is given: PFDavg target = $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$, demand rate = $1$ per year, PST = 30 s. Verify whether the SIF meets the safety requirement. If the proof-test interval is 2 years, what is the dangerous failure rate? **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Relationship (7 marks):** The relationship is derived from low-demand mode reliability theory. The probability of a hazardous event per unit time equals the product of the demand rate and the unavailability of the SIF at the instant of demand. $$ f_{hazard} = \lambda_{demand} \cdot PFD_{avg} $$ For the SIF to bring the process to a safe state before the hazardous event is reached, the SIF must complete its action within PST. This leads to the design inequality: $$ PFD_{avg} \leq \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{demand} \cdot PST} $$ Where: - $\lambda_{demand}$ is in per second - $PST$ is in seconds - $PFD_{avg}$ is dimensionless This equation forms the foundation of the **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** approach and is used to set the **target SIL** for any SIF. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Convert demand rate to per second. $$ \lambda_{demand} = \dfrac{1\ \text{per year}}{365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} = 3.17 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{per second} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the maximum permissible PFDavg from PST and demand rate. $$ PFD_{avg}^{max} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{demand} \cdot PST} = \dfrac{1}{3.17 \times 10^{-8} \times 30} = 1.05 \times 10^{6} $$ **Step 3:** This value is enormously greater than 1, which is physically impossible (PFD ≤ 1). This means the **PST-based bound is satisfied** (since the actual PFDavg target of $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ is far less than 1). Hence the SIF **meets the safety requirement**. **(2 marks)** **Step 4:** Compute the dangerous failure rate with Tproof = 2 years = 17520 hours. $$ \lambda_{D} = \dfrac{PFD_{avg}}{T_{proof}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{17520} = 1.43 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{per hour} $$ **Conclusion:** The dangerous failure rate is approximately $\mathbf{1.43 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{per hour}}$. **(2 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas** > - Students often **forget to include the safety margin** when computing tSIF vs PST. The margin is **mandatory** per IEC 61511 (typically 20–50% of PST). > - **Unit conversion errors** between per-year and per-second demand rates are the single biggest source of calculation mistakes. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > - Confusing PST with **SIF response time** — they are NOT the same. PST is *available* time, SIF response time is *consumed* time. > - Students sometimes quote PST for a *layer of protection* (e.g., BPCS) — PST is a **process attribute**, not a layer attribute. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** > - **PST = time window** between initiating event and hazardous state, assuming no SIF action. > - PST is a **process property**, not an SIS property. > - **SIF response time** = sensor delay + logic solver delay + final element stroke time. > - **SIF must complete within PST** (after deducting a safety margin of 20–50%). > - PST ranges from **2 s (exothermic reactors)** to **3600 s (slow overfills)**. > - **PFDavg target** is set by required SIL: SIL 1 → $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$. > - The relation $PFD_{avg} \leq \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{demand} \cdot PST}$ links PST to LOPA and SIL determination. > - **Dangerous failure rate** is approximated as $\lambda_{D} \approx PFD_{avg} \div T_{proof}$. > - **Final element** is almost always the bottleneck (stroke time 5–10 s) — PST calculations often dictate valve selection. > - When PST is very short (<5 s), consider **fire-and-forget** architectures (e.g., bursting disc + SIS). > - For KTU 2024: always write the equation $t_{S} + t_{L} + t_{FE} + t_{margin} \leq PST$ explicitly in numerical answers. > - IEC standards: **IEC 61511-1 Clause 3.2.65** is the official PST definition reference. > - **Proof test interval (Tproof)** directly impacts PFDavg and hence the achievable SIL for a given PST. ---

topics.title: Risk Reduction and Layers of Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk Reduction and Layers of Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Risk Reduction — Formal Definition

**Risk Reduction (RR)** is the deliberate and systematic decrease in the *frequency* of an undesired event, the *magnitude* of its consequences, or both, achieved through a hierarchy of engineered, procedural, and administrative safeguards known as **Layers of Protection**.

In KTU 2024 / IEC 61511 terminology, risk reduction is quantified as:

$$\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)} = \frac{1}{\text{Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (IEC 61511-1, Clause 3.26):** "Risk Reduction" is the deliberate reduction of the risk to an acceptable/tolerable level through the application of one or more *Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)*, each of which is capable of preventing or mitigating a scenario regardless of the failure state of any other layer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Cheese Model" 🧀

Imagine a stack of **sliced Swiss cheese**, where each slice represents a protective layer. Each slice has holes representing weaknesses, failures, or "demands" that may slip through.

- The **hazard** (e.g., a runaway chemical reaction) is a ball rolling toward the patient (people, environment, assets).
- Each **protection layer** is a slice of cheese that *may* stop the ball.
- The accident only occurs when the holes in **ALL** slices momentarily line up (a coincident failure across every layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **The key principle:** No single layer is perfect. Safety comes from *defense-in-depth* — multiple, **independent** layers. The more layers, the smaller the "alignment of holes," and the lower the risk.

**Intuitive summary:**

| Analogy Element | Functional Safety Equivalent |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Cheese slice | Layer of Protection (IPL) |
| Hole in cheese | Probability of failure on demand (PFD) |
| Rolling ball | Hazard / Initiating event |
| Aligned holes | Coincident failure → accident |
| Many thick slices | High risk reduction (low residual risk) |

---

## 1.3 Layers of Protection — The Six/Classic Layers

The KTU 2024 syllabus (and IEC 61511) recognizes the following **typical onion-skin of protection layers**, from innermost (process) to outermost (community):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **Classic 6 Layers of Protection** (L1 → L6):
> 1. **L1 — Process Design (Inherent Safety)** — Eliminate/minimize the hazard at source.
> 2. **L2 — Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** — Continuous regulation; not a safety layer by itself, but provides mitigation.
> 3. **L3 — Critical Alarms + Operator Intervention (with documented response time)** — A credited IPL only if properly designed.
> 4. **L4 — Safety Instrumented System / Function (SIS / SIF)** — IEC 61508/61511 compliant.
> 5. **L5 — Physical Protection (Relief Valves, Dikes, Blast Walls, Rupture Discs)** — Passive and active engineered safeguards.
> 6. **L6 — Community / Emergency Response (Plant + External)** — Last line of defense.

---

## 1.4 What is "Risk" — The Foundation

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Frequency}, \text{Consequence})$$

In LOPA, we work in the *frequency* domain:

$$\text{Frequency of Unwanted Event} = \text{Initiating Event Frequency (IEF)} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$$

where $\text{PFD}_{i}$ is the probability of failure on demand of the *i*-th Independent Protection Layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerable Risk:** The risk level below which the process is considered acceptable and no further protection is required. KTU/IEC standards often express it as **Tolerable Frequency per Year** (e.g., $10^{-6}$/yr for catastrophic public risk).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Reduction Strategy — "Order of Precedence"

KTU emphasizes that risk reduction must follow a strict **hierarchy of controls** (often called the *hierarchy of hazard control*):

1. **Elimination** — remove the hazard entirely (e.g., change chemistry).
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous material.
3. **Engineering Controls (Inherent / Passive)** — lower inventories, lower pressures, fewer connections.
4. **Active engineered safeguards** — interlocks, SIFs, relief valves.
5. **Procedural / Administrative controls** — SOPs, training, permits.
6. **PPE / Emergency response** — last resort.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The order is **not** an option — it is a *mandate*. Moving directly to a SIF (Layer 4) without first exploring inherent design is a common KTU answer that gets only **partial credit**.

---

## 2.2 Independent Protection Layer (IPL) — The Qualification Criteria

For a safeguard to qualify as a true **IPL** in LOPA, it **must** satisfy **all** of the following:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 IPL Tests (IEC 61511 / LOPA textbook):**
> 1. **Effectiveness** — The layer actually reduces the identified risk by at least an order of magnitude ($\text{PFD} \le 0.1$).
> 2. **Independence** — Independent of the initiating event *and* of any other IPL credited in the scenario.
> 3 **Auditability** — The layer can be validated, tested, and verified on a defined schedule.
> 4. **Specificity** — The layer is designed specifically to address the *same* hazard scenario (no "incidental" credit).

If a safeguard fails any one of these, **it is NOT an IPL** in LOPA terms.

---

## 2.3 Typical PFD / RRF Values per Layer (KTU Approved Table)

| Protection Layer | Typical PFD | Typical RRF | KTU Memory Aid |
|------------------|------------|------------|---------------|
| L1 — Inherent / Process Design | 0 (if hazard removed) | ∞ | "Hazard gone" |
| L2 — BPCS (BPCS Failure assumed: IEF may include the BPCS failure rate) | 0.1 – 1.0 | 1 – 10 | "Not credited" |
| L3 — Operator on Alarm (with documented < 10 min response) | 0.1 | 10 | "One zero" |
| L4a — SIF at SIL 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10 – 100 | "SIL-1" |
| L4b — SIF at SIL 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100 – 1000 | "SIL-2" |
| L4c — SIF at SIL 3 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 1000 – 10000 | "SIL-3" |
| L4d — SIF at SIL 4 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10000 – 100000 | "SIL-4 (rare)" |
| L5 — Pressure Relief Valve (PSV) — single | 0.01 – 0.05 | 20 – 100 | "PSV" |
| L5 — Dike / Bund (passive containment) | 0.01 | 100 | "Bund" |
| L6 — Community Emergency Response | 0.1 – 0.5 | 2 – 10 | "Last resort" |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA BOX — KTU Board Exam Favorite**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Risk = Freq × Consequence** | $R = f \times C$ | Frequency domain in LOPA |
| 2 | **LOPA Frequency** | $f_{uw} = \text{IEF} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ | Core LOPA equation |
| 3 | **Risk Reduction Factor** | $\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Inverse of PFD |
| 4 | **Tolerability Test** | $f_{uw} \le f_{tolerable}$ | If satisfied, no SIF needed |
| 5 | **Required SIF PFD** | $\text{PFD}_{SIF} \le \dfrac{f_{tolerable}}{\text{IEF} \times \prod_{j\ne SIF} \text{PFD}_j}$ | Solves for SIF |
| 6 | **SIL Boundary (PFD)** | SIL 1: $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$, SIL 2: $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$, SIL 3: $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$ | From IEC 61508 Table 2 |
| 7 | **Mitigated Event Frequency** | $f_{mit} = f_{uw} \times (1 - P_{mit})$ | $P_{mit}$ is mitigation probability |
| 8 | **Conditional Modifier (CM)** | $f_{adj} = \text{IEF} \times \text{CM}$ | Probability the condition is *not* present (e.g., ignited = 1 if always flammable atmosphere) |
| 9 | **Time at Risk** | $T_R = \dfrac{\text{Exposure Hours}}{8760 \text{ hr/yr}}$ | Used when hazard only exists during a phase |
| 10 | **Total Risk** | $R_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} f_k \times C_k$ | Sum over all scenarios |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Isolation Rule:** When using **absolute value** in formulas, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** the literal pipe `|x|` — to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Determining SIL of an Emergency Shutdown System (ESD) on offshore platforms.
- **Chemical Process Plants:** Verifying relief valve sizing is sufficient *or* supplementing with a SIF.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** LOPA for reactor over-temperature scenarios.
- **Power Plants:** Defending against boiler over-pressure and turbine overspeed.
- **Nuclear:** Defense-in-depth is the *cornerstone* regulatory requirement.

---

## 2.6 The Order of Magnitude Rule (LOPA's Core Discipline)

LOPA uses **order-of-magnitude** (OOM) arithmetic — every IPL is credited as a single factor of 10 (PFD = 0.1) unless specific evidence supports a smaller value. This keeps LOPA **semi-quantitative** and **defensible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board-Exam standard):** Unless data justifies otherwise, **PFD = 0.1 per IPL** (one decade of risk reduction). This is called the **"one-decade" rule**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Core LOPA Equation

We begin with the definition of risk for a single scenario:

$$R = f_{uw} \times C$$

where $f_{uw}$ is the frequency of the *unwanted event* and $C$ is the consequence. The tolerable risk is:

$$R_{tolerable} = f_{tolerable} \times C_{reference}$$

The protection goal is:

$$f_{uw} \le f_{tolerable}$$

By definition, an initiating event with frequency $\text{IEF}$ occurs. For the unwanted event to occur, every credited IPL must *fail* to act (i.e., each IPL must be in its PFD state). Because IPLs are independent, the joint probability of coincident failure is the product of individual PFDs:

$$f_{uw} = \text{IEF} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$$

This is the **LOPA master equation**. It is *not* an assumption — it follows directly from the **multiplication rule for independent events**:

$$\Pr(A \cap B) = \Pr(A) \cdot \Pr(B) \quad \text{when } A \perp B$$

Re-arranging to solve for the *required* PFD of a new SIF:

$$\text{PFD}_{SIF} \le \frac{f_{tolerable}}{\text{IEF} \times \prod_{j \ne SIF} \text{PFD}_{j}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Reactor Over-Temperature Scenario (KTU Board Favourite)

**Scenario:** A chemical reactor can overheat due to cooling water pump failure, leading to runaway reaction and catastrophic rupture (consequence category: **Category 5 — Catastrophic**).

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Initiating event: Cooling water pump failure frequency | $\text{IEF} = 0.1 \text{ /yr}$ (i.e., once in 10 years) |
| Existing IPL 1: High-temperature alarm + operator intervention | $\text{PFD}_1 = 0.1$ |
| Existing IPL 2: Pressure Relief Valve (sized for runaway) | $\text{PFD}_2 = 0.01$ |
| Tolerable frequency (catastrophic, single fatality) | $f_{tol} = 10^{-6} \text{ /yr}$ |
| Conditional modifier (ignited) | $\text{CM} = 1$ (always flammable atmosphere) |

**Goal:** Determine the *required* SIL of a new SIF, if any.

### Step 1 — Apply the LOPA master equation

$$f_{uw} = \text{IEF} \times \text{PFD}_1 \times \text{PFD}_2 \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

Substituting the known quantities:

$$f_{uw} = 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

$$f_{uw} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

$$f_{uw} = 10^{-4} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the tolerability test

$$f_{uw} \le f_{tolerable} \implies 10^{-4} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF} \le 10^{-6}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for required SIF PFD

$$\text{PFD}_{SIF} \le \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} = 10^{-2}$$

### Step 4 — Map to SIL

| SIL | PFD range | Required? |
|-----|-----------|-----------|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | ✓ (our PFD $\le 10^{-2}$ satisfies SIL 1) |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | borderline — pick the more conservative |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | over-engineered |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since the calculated PFD requirement is **exactly** $10^{-2}$, a **SIL 1** SIF is sufficient. However, by the **conservative engineering practice**, a **SIL 2** SIF is often chosen to add margin — and KTU answers must show this *explicitly*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Tank Overfill with a SIF (Demonstrating All Concepts)

A vertical atmospheric storage tank receives hydrocarbon from a pipeline. Hazard: **overfill** leading to pool fire.

**LOPA Worksheet Entry:**

| Step | Item | Value |
|------|------|-------|
| 1 | Consequence category | Major (Category 4) |
| 2 | Tolerable frequency | $10^{-5}$/yr |
| 3 | Initiating event (pump deadhead, valve fails) | $\text{IEF} = 0.5$ /yr |
| 4 | Conditional modifier: "Not in service" (fractional uptime) | $\text{CM} = 0.2$ |
| 5 | Modified IEF | $\text{IEF}_{mod} = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.1$ /yr |
| 6 | IPL — Existing BPCS level control loop | PFD = 0.1 |
| 7 | IPL — High-high level alarm + operator | PFD = 0.1 |
| 8 | IPL — New SIF (to be designed) | PFD = ? |

### Calculation

$$f_{uw} = \text{IEF}_{mod} \times \text{PFD}_{BPCS} \times \text{PFD}_{Alarm} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

$$f_{uw} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF} = 10^{-3} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$$

Tolerability:

$$10^{-3} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF} \le 10^{-5} \implies \text{PFD}_{SIF} \le 10^{-2}$$

A **SIL 1** SIF is adequate (PFD $\in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Layer

| Hazard Type | Primary Layer | Secondary Layer | Justification |
|------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Reactor runaway | L1 — Inherent (lower inventory) | L4 — SIF | Chemistry cannot always be changed; SIF is robust |
| Tank overfill | L2 — BPCS level control | L4 — SIF | Continuous control is essential; SIF is final |
| Pump overpressure | L5 — Relief valve | L4 — SIF (sometimes) | PSV is most reliable, but LOPA may still demand SIF |
| Toxic gas release | L1 — Substitution (less toxic chem.) | L6 — Community | Hierarchy demands source reduction first |
| Fire/explosion | L5 — Passive (dike, blast wall) | L6 — Firewater | Passive preferred over active |
| Operator error | L3 — Alarm + SOP | L4 — SIF (interlock) | SIF removes the human-error term |

---

## 3.5 Pitfalls in LOPA Calculation (KTU marks lost here)

1. **Double-counting** — crediting the BPCS as both a control loop and an IPL. **Rule:** BPCS failures are usually folded into the IEF; do not also credit BPCS as an IPL.
2. **Counting non-independent layers** — credit only ONE shutdown valve on the same signal path; multiple valves on the *same* cause share a common failure mode.
3. **Forgetting conditional modifiers** — operating phase, ignition probability, occupancy.
4. **Mixing the "as-is" risk with the "as-built" risk** — the LOPA is performed on the *designed* system, not the operating one.
5. **Ignoring common cause** — IEC 61511 caps credit on identical redundant instruments at the $\beta$-factor.

---

## 3.6 Final Summary Equation (Master Reference)

$$\boxed{\,f_{uw} = \text{IEF} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i} \le f_{tolerable}\,}$$

This **single equation** is the heart of LOPA — and is the *most-asked* derivation in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Layers-of-Protection Onion (KTU Standard Diagram)

This is the canonical IEC 61511 visual. Each ring is a *protection layer*; the hazard is at the centre.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA(["COMMUNITY EMERGENCY RESPONSE - L6"])
    nodeB["PHYSICAL PROTECTION - Relief, Dike, Blast - L5"]
    nodeC["SAFETY INSTRUMENTED FUNCTION - SIF - L4"]
    nodeD["ALARMS and OPERATOR ACTION - L3"]
    nodeE["BASIC PROCESS CONTROL SYSTEM - BPCS - L2"]
    nodeF(["PROCESS DESIGN - Inherent Safety - L1"])
    nodeG(["CORE HAZARD - Reactor Overpressure - Runaway"])

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hazard (G) is at the *innermost* core. Each successive protection layer (F → A) wraps the inner layer. An accident occurs *only* if the initiating event AND *every* protection layer fail simultaneously.

---

## 4.2 LOPA Workflow — End-to-End Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start - Identify Hazard Scenario"]
    stepB["Determine Consequence Category and Tolerable Frequency"]
    stepC["Identify Initiating Events and Compute IEF"]
    stepD["Apply Conditional Modifiers to IEF"]
    stepE["Identify Existing IPLs and Assign PFDs"]
    stepF["Compute Mitigated Event Frequency f_uw"]
    stepG{"f_uw less than or equal to f_tolerable"}
    stepH["No Additional SIF Required - Document and End"]
    stepI["Calculate Required PFD for New SIF"]
    stepJ["Map Required PFD to SIL Level"]
    stepK["Design, Implement, and Verify SIF"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG
    stepG -- YES --> stepH
    stepG -- NO --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK
```

---

## 4.3 SIL Mapping — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Calculated Required PFD"}
    qA["PFD between 1.0E-01 and 1.0E-00 - SIL 0 - No SIF needed"]
    qB["PFD between 1.0E-02 and 1.0E-01 - SIL 1"]
    qC["PFD between 1.0E-03 and 1.0E-02 - SIL 2"]
    qD["PFD between 1.0E-04 and 1.0E-03 - SIL 3"]
    qE["PFD less than 1.0E-04 - SIL 4 - Rare, Special Justification"]

    q1 --> qA
    q1 --> qB
    q1 --> qC
    q1 --> qD
    q1 --> qE
```

---

## 4.4 IPL Qualification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t1["Is the Safeguard Independent of Initiating Event and Other IPLs?"]
    t2["Is the Safeguard Effective - PFD less than 0.1?"]
    t3["Is the Safeguard Auditable - Testable, Verifiable?"]
    t4["Is the Safeguard Specific to this Hazard Scenario?"]
    tY["QUALIFIES AS IPL - Assign PFD"]
    tN["DOES NOT QUALIFY - Document only, no credit"]

    t1 -- NO --> tN
    t1 -- YES --> t2
    t2 -- NO --> tN
    t2 -- YES --> t3
    t3 -- NO --> tN
    t3 -- YES --> t4
    t4 -- NO --> tN
    t4 -- YES --> tY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Reminder:** "**IEAS**" — *I*ndependence, *E*ffectiveness, *A*uditability, *S*pecificity — the four tests every IPL must pass.

---

## 4.5 Risk Reduction Architecture — Block-Level Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IE["Initiating Event - IEF per year"]
    CM["Conditional Modifier - CM"]
    IPL1["IPL1 - BPCS - PFD1"]
    IPL2["IPL2 - Alarm plus Operator - PFD2"]
    IPL3["IPL3 - Relief Valve - PFD3"]
    SIF["IPL4 - SIF - PFD_SIF"]
    OUT["Unwanted Event Frequency - f_uw"]
    COMP["Comparison with f_tolerable"]
    DEC["Decision - Additional Protection or Accept"]

    IE --> CM --> IPL1 --> IPL2 --> IPL3 --> SIF --> OUT --> COMP --> DEC
```

Each block represents a **probability multiplier** — they multiply in series to attenuate the initiating-event frequency to the final, *mitigated* event frequency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series B]**

**Q1. Define "Independent Protection Layer (IPL)" and list any two tests that a safeguard must satisfy to qualify as an IPL.**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

**Definition (1 mark):** An Independent Protection Layer (IPL) is a device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, *regardless* of the failure of any other protection layer, and which is independent of the initiating event.

**Two tests (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Independence** — must be independent of the initiating event and any other IPL.
2. **Effectiveness** — must reduce the scenario risk by at least one order of magnitude (PFD $\le 0.1$).

(Other acceptable answers: *Auditability* and *Specificity*.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the relationship between PFD, RRF, and SIL? State the PFD range for SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3.**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

**Relationship (1 mark):** $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$. The SIL is the band into which the calculated PFD of a SIF falls.

**PFD ranges (2 marks):**

| SIL | PFD Range |
|-----|-----------|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Ref: KTU Model Paper Style)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **"Layers of Protection"** concept as applied in process industries. List the **six** classical layers in order.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor is protected against runaway by the following safeguards:
- Initiating event (loss of cooling): IEF = $0.1$ /yr
- High-temperature alarm + operator intervention: PFD = $0.1$
- Pressure Relief Valve: PFD = $0.01$

The tolerable frequency for catastrophic events is $10^{-6}$/yr. **Determine the SIL of an additional SIF** (if required). Use the LOPA master equation and show all steps.   **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The **Layers of Protection** concept, drawn from IEC 61511, is illustrated as a series of concentric "onion" rings (the **Swiss Cheese model**):

> (See SECTION 4.1 diagram.)

**Description (4 marks):**

The process industry uses **defense-in-depth**: no single safeguard is perfect, so multiple **independent** layers are stacked. A hazard (innermost) propagates outward only if *every* layer fails.

**The Six Classical Layers (3 marks):**

1. **L1 — Process Design (Inherent Safety)** — e.g., lower inventory, lower operating pressure.
2. **L2 — Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** — continuous control loops.
3. **L3 — Critical Alarms + Operator Intervention** — alarm with documented human response.
4. **L4 — Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** — IEC 61508/61511 compliant instrumented protection.
