<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the core mathematical relationship used in Bow Tie risk quantification — the formula that converts a Bow Tie diagram into numerical risk figures used in **LOPA** and **SIL allocation**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Top Event Frequency from a Bow Tie

The frequency at which a specific **Top Event** occurs in a Bow Tie diagram is computed by combining:

- The **frequency of each threat path** (initiating cause frequency, $f_{T_i}$), and
- The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of each preventive barrier on that path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption (per IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5):** The barriers on a given threat path are modelled as **independent** AND-logic elements — i.e., all barriers must fail for the threat to escalate to the Top Event.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1 — Consider a single threat path $i$** (say *“Loss of cooling water”*) reaching the Top Event (say *“Reactor runaway”*). Suppose this path has $m$ preventive barriers.

**Step 2 — Probability of success of the $j$-th barrier** on this path:

$$
P_{success,j} = 1 - PFD_j
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of failure of the $j$-th barrier** (which is, by definition, $PFD_j$).

**Step 4 — Probability that the threat escapes ALL $m$ barriers** (AND logic — all must fail):

$$
P_{escape,i} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} PFD_{ij}
$$

**Step 5 — Frequency at which this single threat $i$ generates the Top Event**:

$$
f_{TE,i} = f_{T_i} \cdot P_{escape,i} = f_{T_i} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} PFD_{ij}
$$

**Step 6 — The overall Top Event frequency is the sum over all $n$ threat paths** (OR logic — any one path is sufficient):

$$
f_{TE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{T_i} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} PFD_{ij}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numeric Example (typical KTU 14-mark question style):**
> A reactor has 2 threats leading to a Top Event "Loss of Containment":
> - **Threat A** (overpressure) — initiating frequency $f_{A} = 0.1\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, passes through 2 barriers with $PFD_{A1} = 10^{-2}$ and $PFD_{A2} = 10^{-1}$.
> - **Threat B** (external fire) — initiating frequency $f_{B} = 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, passes through 3 barriers with $PFD_{B1} = 10^{-2}$, $PFD_{B2} = 10^{-1}$, $PFD_{B3} = 10^{-1}$.

Compute $f_{TE}$.

**Computation — Threat A path:**

$$
f_{TE,A} = f_{A} \cdot PFD_{A1} \cdot PFD_{A2} = 0.1 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Computation — Threat B path:**

$$
f_{TE,B} = f_{B} \cdot PFD_{B1} \cdot PFD_{B2} \cdot PFD_{B3} = 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Total Top Event Frequency:**

$$
f_{TE} = f_{TE,A} + f_{TE,B} = 10^{-4} + 10^{-7} \approx 1.001 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

Since $f_{TE,A} \gg f_{TE,B}$, the dominant contributor is Threat A — a typical KTU question will then ask *"Which threat should be prioritised for additional barriers?"*

## 3.2 Derivation: RRF for an SIF Inside the Bow Tie

The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** of a Safety Instrumented Function is the inverse of its PFD. For an SIF containing $n$ subsystems in low-demand mode (per IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5.2):

$$
PFD_{SIF} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} PFD_{k}
$$

Therefore the RRF contributed by the SIF is:

$$
RRF_{SIF} = \frac{1}{PFD_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} PFD_{k}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The SIF is allocated to one of the SIL brackets: **SIL 1** ($PFD \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$), **SIL 2** ($PFD \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$), **SIL 3** ($PFD \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$), or **SIL 4** ($PFD \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-4})$). Per IEC 61511-1 §5.2.6.1.1, **SIL 4 is not recommended** in the process industry because the hardware fault tolerance required becomes uneconomic.

## 3.3 Derivation: Consequence Frequency from the Event-Tree Side

For a specific consequence pathway $k$ on the right side of the Bow Tie, with $q$ mitigative barriers in series:

$$
f_{Outcome,k} = f_{TE} \cdot \prod_{l=1}^{q} PFD_{kl}
$$

If we have the **risk matrix** with the company's **tolerable frequency** for that consequence ($f_{tol,k}$), then the **ALARP** requirement is:

$$
f_{Outcome,k} \le f_{tol,k}
$$

Substituting the Bow Tie expressions, additional mitigative barrier PFDs are added until the inequality is satisfied.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bow Tie Elements vs. KTU 2024 Course Outcomes

| Bow Tie Element | What the Student Must Know (CO) | Bloom's Level | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top Event identification | CO1 — Hazard identification | Apply (L3) | 3–4 marks |
| Threat listing | CO1 — Hazard identification | Analyse (L4) | 4–5 marks |
| Preventive barrier design | CO2 — Barrier design & independence | Design (L5) | 7 marks |
| Mitigative barrier (recovery) | CO2 — Consequence mitigation | Apply (L3) | 4–5 marks |
| PFD / RRF calculation | CO3 — Quantitative safety | Evaluate (L5) | 7 marks |
| SIL allocation via Bow Tie | CO3 — SIL determination per IEC 61511 | Evaluate (L5) | 7–10 marks |
| ALARP demonstration | CO4 — Risk acceptance | Create (L6) | 5–7 marks |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Numerical Bow Tie Build-up

Consider the KTU-style exam scenario:

> *"A pump in a refinery circulates hot oil at 280 °C. Identify a Top Event, list two threats, propose two preventive and two mitigative barriers, and compute the Top Event frequency assuming $f_{T1} = 0.5\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, $PFD_{1,1} = 10^{-1}$, $PFD_{1,2} = 10^{-2}$, $f_{T2} = 10^{-2}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, $PFD_{2,1} = 10^{-2}$, $PFD_{2,2} = 10^{-3}$."*

**Solution Outline (model answer):**

- **Top Event:** *Loss of hot-oil containment from pump P-101.*
- **Threat 1:** Mechanical seal failure.
- **Threat 2:** External corrosion of pump casing.
- **Preventive Barrier 1.1:** Vibration monitoring with trip.
- **Preventive Barrier 1.2:** Routine mechanical integrity inspection.
- **Mitigative Barrier (right side):** Bunded containment, firewater deluge.

**Numerical calculation:**

$$
f_{TE,1} = 0.5 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
f_{TE,2} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

$$
f_{TE} = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-7} \approx 5.001 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

The dominant threat (Threat 1) is **three orders of magnitude** more risky — an additional $10^{-1}$ PFD barrier (e.g., improved trip) would reduce $f_{TE,1}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$, achieving a **further risk reduction factor (RRR) of 10×**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked answer cross-check:** All quantities in $\text{yr}^{-1}$ are consistent with IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5 which uses a **1-year operational time basis**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Bow Tie Diagram (Mermaid Block-Diagram Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Threat A1: Pump Mechanical Seal Failure] -->|via| B1(Preventive Barrier 1: Vibration Trip)
    A2[Threat A2: External Corrosion] -->|via| B2(Preventive Barrier 2: Scheduled Inspection)
    A3[Threat A3: Overpressure Surge] -->|via| B3(Preventive Barrier 3: Pressure Relief Valve)
    A4[Threat A4: Operator Error] -->|via| B4(Preventive Barrier 4: Permits to Work)

    B1 --> C{{TOP EVENT: Loss of Hot Oil Containment}}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C

    C -->|via| D1(Mitigative Barrier 1: Bunded Area)
    C -->|via| D2(Mitigative Barrier 2: Firewater Deluge)
    C -->|via| D3(Mitigative Barrier 3: Emergency Shutdown ESD)
    C -->|via| D4(Mitigative Barrier 4: Site Evacuation Plan)

    D1 --> E1[Consequence 1: Pool Fire]
    D2 --> E2[Consequence 2: Toxic Vapour Cloud]
    D3 --> E3[Consequence 3: Asset Damage]
    D4 --> E4[Consequence 4: Personnel Injury or Fatality]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow into the **Top Event** represents a *threat* that has passed all preventive barriers. Each arrow out of the Top Event represents a *consequence path* that survives all mitigative barriers. The **Top Event** is the "knot" of the bow tie, and the diagram's overall shape mimics a horizontally-tied bow tie (the diagram is read left-to-right).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Barrier Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[PREVENTIVE SIDE - LEFT WING]
        direction TB
        T[Threats]
        PB1[P1: Hardware Barrier - Sensor + SIF]
        PB2[P2: Procedural Barrier - Inspection]
        PB3[P3: Independent Barrier - Relief Device]
        T --> PB1 --> PB2 --> PB3
    end

    TE{{TOP EVENT - LOSS OF CONTROL}}

    subgraph RIGHT[MITIGATIVE SIDE - RIGHT WING]
        direction TB
        M1[M1: Hardware Barrier - ESD]
        M2[M2: Procedural Barrier - Emergency Plan]
        M3[M3: Active Barrier - Fire Suppression]
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
    end

    OUT[Consequences - Severity Outcomes]

    PB3 --> TE
    TE --> M1
    M3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Swiss-Cheese Layered Defence in Bow Tie (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hazard Initiator] -->|travels| S1[Slice 1: SIF / Hardware Barrier]
    S1 -->|hole?| S2[Slice 2: Procedural Control]
    S2 -->|hole?| S3[Slice 3: Independent Protection Layer IPL]
    S3 -->|hole?| S4[Slice 4: Emergency Response]
    S4 -->|hole?| C[Consequence Manifests]

    H1[Degradation Factor 1] -.->|drill?| S1
    H2[Degradation Factor 2] -.->|drill?| S2
    H3[Degradation Factor 3] -.->|drill?| S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the Swiss-Cheese Concept inside Bow Tie:** Each slice is a barrier; each "hole" is a degradation factor (e.g., maintenance skipped, sensor uncalibrated, valve stuck). A hazard "escapes" only when the holes in **all** slices line up.

## 4.4 Bow Tie → SIL Allocation Mapping (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BTM[Bow Tie Diagram]
    BTM --> P[Preventive Barriers]
    BTM --> M[Mitigative Barriers]

    P --> SIF1[SIF Candidate 1: Sensor + Logic + Final Element]
    P --> SIF2[SIF Candidate 2: Trip Valve Loop]

    SIF1 --> CALC1[PFD Calculation per IEC 61511-1 5.2.5]
    SIF2 --> CALC2[PFD Calculation per IEC 61511-1 5.2.5]

    CALC1 --> SIL1{Compare to Tolerable Frequency}
    CALC2 --> SIL2{Compare to Tolerable Frequency}

    SIL1 -->|achieved| OUT1[SIL 1, 2 or 3 Allocated]
    SIL2 -->|achieved| OUT2[SIL 1, 2 or 3 Allocated]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** Bow Tie does NOT compute the SIL — it identifies *which barrier should be* a SIF. The actual SIL value is calculated separately using the **PFD formula** above. This is a common KTU 14-mark question: *"Using a Bow Tie, identify the candidate SIF and compute its PFD."*

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the Bow Tie Method and list its four main components.*

**Model Answer:**

The Bow Tie Method is a graphical risk assessment technique that combines a **fault tree** (causes) on the left with an **event tree** (consequences) on the right, joined at a central **Top Event**. Its four main components are:

1. **Threats** (left tail — initiating causes)
2. **Preventive Controls / Barriers** (left wing — reduce top event frequency)
3. **Top Event** (central node — moment of loss of control)
4. **Consequences** (right tail — outcomes)
5. **Mitigative / Recovery Controls / Barriers** (right wing — reduce consequence severity)

> [!NOTE]
> Full-marks answer must include both the **preventive and mitigative** barrier types — students frequently forget mitigative barriers in 3-mark questions.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
*What is the relationship between the Bow Tie Method and the Swiss-Cheese Model?*

**Model Answer:**

The Swiss-Cheese Model (proposed by James Reason) describes safety as a **series of imperfect barrier layers** each containing latent "holes" (degradation factors). The Bow Tie Method **operationalises** this model by drawing each barrier as a separate "slice" on either the preventive (left) or mitigative (right) side of the diagram. A hazard propagates through the slices only when their holes align, mirroring the layered-defence principle of **defence-in-depth (per IEC 61511-1 §5.2.1)**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, Module 2 Part B must contain an internal choice. Both options are provided below with full model solutions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular Exam — Modified Model Paper]**

*(a)* Draw and explain the **Bow Tie diagram** for a hydrocarbon storage tank with the **Top Event** defined as *"Loss of tank containment leading to hydrocarbon release."* Identify at least **two threats**, **two preventive barriers**, and **two mitigative barriers** (with their barrier types). **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* For the same scenario, the company has identified the following data: Threat 1 (*external impact*, e.g., vehicle collision) has a frequency $f_{T1} = 10^{-2}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$ and passes through 2 preventive barriers with $PFD_1 = 10^{-1}$ and $PFD_2 = 10^{-2}$. Threat 2 (*internal corrosion*) has $f_{T2} = 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$ and passes through 2 preventive barriers with $PFD_3 = 10^{-1}$ and $PFD_4 = 10^{-3}$. **Compute the Top Event frequency** and identify which threat dominates. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Top Event identification (1 mark):**
**Top Event:** Loss of tank containment (hydrocarbon release to atmosphere).

**Step 2 — Threats (2 marks):**
- **Threat 1:** External impact — e.g., vehicle collision, falling object.
- **Threat 2:** Internal corrosion — metal loss in the tank wall.

**Step 3 — Preventive Barriers (2 marks):**
- **Preventive Barrier 1 (Hardware):** Bund wall with concrete crash barrier (independent of process) — protects from vehicle impact.
- **Preventive Barrier 2 (Hardware/Procedural):** Scheduled thickness-measurement inspections (e.g., API 653 internal inspections) and corrosion inhibitor dosing.

**Step 4 — Mitigative Barriers (2 marks):**
- **Mitigative Barrier 1 (Hardware):** Secondary containment / bund with sufficient capacity (110 % of largest tank, per NFPA 30 / Indian Oil Industry Safety Directorate).
- **Mitigative Barrier 2 (Active Hardware):** Foam pourer / firewater deluge system actuated by flame detector.

*(Note: Each barrier must specify its type — hardware, procedural, or human — to earn full 2 marks per category.)*

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (1 mark):**

$$
f_{TE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{T_i} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} PFD_{ij}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Threat 1 contribution to Top Event (2 marks):**

$$
f_{TE,1} = f_{T1} \cdot PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2 = 10^{-2} \cdot 10^{-1} \cdot 10^{-2} = 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Threat 2 contribution to Top Event (2 marks):**

$$
f_{TE,2} = f_{T2} \cdot PFD_3 \cdot PFD_4 = 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-1} \cdot 10^{-3} = 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and identify dominant threat (2 marks):**

$$
f_{TE} = f_{TE,1} + f_{TE,2} = 10^{-5} + 10^{-7} = 1.01 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Dominant Threat:** Threat 1 (external impact) is dominant — it contributes **99 %** of the Top Event frequency, which is two orders of magnitude higher than Threat 2. The Bow Tie therefore shows that investment in additional barriers on the Threat 1 path (e.g., reinforced bund wall, enhanced crash protection) will yield a **greater risk reduction** per unit cost than barriers on Threat 2.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Variation]**

*(a)* Explain with a **labelled diagram** how the Bow Tie Method integrates with the **SIL allocation process** as per **IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5**. In your answer, describe the role of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** and how they map to Bow Tie barriers. **[7 Marks]**

*(b)* A candidate SIF inside a Bow Tie consists of a single sensor subsystem ($PFD_{sensor} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$), a logic solver ($PFD_{logic} = 10^{-3}$), and a final element ($PFD_{final} = 10^{-2}$). **Compute the SIL** of this SIF and comment on whether it meets the typical industry tolerable frequency of $f_{tol} = 10^{-6}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$ when the demand rate is $0.1\ \text{yr}^{-1}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Integration of Bow Tie and SIL allocation (2 marks):**
The Bow Tie Method is the **qualitative framework** that identifies which barriers need protection. The SIL allocation process is the **quantitative layer** that determines how much risk reduction each barrier (specifically the SIF) must provide. Per **IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5.2**, a candidate SIF becomes a barrier on the Bow Tie once a Target SIL is determined from the gap between the unmitigated demand rate and the tolerable frequency.

**Step 2 — Role of IPLs (2 marks):**
An **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** is a device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, **independent of** the initiating event and any other layer. IPLs correspond directly to Bow Tie barriers — every preventive barrier is in principle an IPL, provided it satisfies the **independence, effectiveness, and auditability** criteria of CCPS LOPA Guide (2001).

**Step 3 — Mapping table (3 marks):**

| Bow Tie Element | SIL / IPL Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Preventive Barrier (Hardware) | IPL — e.g., SIF with SIL 1, 2, or 3 | Reduce top event frequency |
| Preventive Barrier (Procedural) | Administrative IPL (typically PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$) | Operator action / inspection |
| Mitigative Barrier (Hardware) | IPL — e.g., ESD, blowdown, foam system | Reduce consequence frequency |
| Mitigative Barrier (Procedural) | Emergency response IPL (PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$) | Evacuation, fire brigade |

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute the total SIF PFD (3 marks):**
For a low-demand SIF per **IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5.2**:

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{final}
$$

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 5 \times 10^{-3} + 10^{-3} + 10^{-2} = 1.6 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Map to SIL bracket (2 marks):**
Comparing $1.6 \times 10^{-2}$ with the IEC 61511-1 Table 3 ranges:

- SIL 1: $PFD \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$ — **$1.6 \times 10^{-2}$ falls in this range**.

**Therefore the SIF achieves SIL 1.**

**Step 3 — Compute achieved risk reduction and comment (2 marks):**
Achieved Top Event frequency (assuming single threat path):

$$
f_{achieved} = f_{demand} \cdot PFD_{SIF} = 0.1 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-2} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

Compare to tolerable frequency:

$$
1.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1} > 10^{-6}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
$$

**Conclusion:** The SIF achieves **SIL 1** but the resulting risk ($1.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$) is **three orders of magnitude higher** than the tolerable frequency. The Bow Tie therefore indicates that **additional barriers** (e.g., a second independent IPL, redundant sensor, or improved final element) are required to bring the residual risk within ALARP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Mixing PFD of independent barriers with multiplication** — In the AND-logic (preventive) case, $PFD$ values **multiply** across the path; in the SIF subsystem case, PFDs **add**. Students commonly multiply when they should add, or vice versa. **Rule of thumb:** *different barriers on a path → multiply; subsystems of one SIF → add.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — Some PFDs are given per hour (PFH); always confirm the operational time basis (typically **1 year = 8 760 hours**).
> 3. **Ignoring independence** — Two barriers that share a common sensor or maintenance procedure are NOT independent and CANNOT both be counted as separate IPLs. Always state "**independent**" when listing Bow Tie barriers.
> 4. **Confusing ALARP with ALARA** — ALARP = "**As Low As Reasonably Practicable**" (UK HSE / IEC 61511); ALARA = "**As Low As Reasonably Achievable**" (older radiation-safety term). KTU questions on functional safety expect ALARP.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Bow Tie Method**

- ✅ **Bow Tie = Fault Tree (left) + Event Tree (right) + Top Event (centre) + Barriers on both sides.**
- ✅ The diagram visually implements **James Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model** — each barrier is a "slice" with potential **degradation factors** (holes).
- ✅ **Top Event** is the central node — the moment of loss of control. Bow Tie does NOT compute risk; it identifies **where** to put controls.
- ✅ **Preventive barriers** reduce $f_{TE}$ (top event frequency). **Mitigative barriers** reduce the severity of consequences.
- ✅ **Barriers must be independent** (per CCPS LOPA Guide) — share no common failure mode, sensor, maintenance crew, or test procedure.
- ✅ **PFD of barriers on a single threat path (AND logic) = product of individual PFDs.**
- ✅ **PFD of subsystems within a single SIF = sum of individual PFDs** (low-demand mode).
- ✅ **RRF = 1 / PFD.** Standard SIL brackets: **1 → 10–100, 2 → 100–1000, 3 → 1000–10 000, 4 → 10 000–100 000**.
- ✅ IEC 61511-1 limits process-industry SIFs to **SIL 1, 2, or 3**; **SIL 4 is not recommended** (per §5.2.6.1.1).
- ✅ **ALARP** is the risk acceptance principle; tolerable frequencies are company-specific but typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$ for major hazards.
- ✅ Standard reference: **IEC 61511-1 §5.2.5** (Low-demand mode PFD formulation); **IEC 61511-1 §5.2.1** (Risk and ALARP).
- ✅ Bow Tie origins: **ICI (1979)**; popularised by **UK HSE (Health and Safety Executive)**; supported by **CCPS** (Center for Chemical Process Safety).
- ✅ Bow Tie is the **bridge** between **HAZOP** (hazard identification) and **LOPA** (quantitative risk layer of protection analysis).
- ✅ Common errors: forgetting the **mitigative** side, confusing **PFD multiplication vs. addition**, mixing **ALARP and ALARA**, omitting **independence** declaration.

> **End of Module 2 — Bow Tie Method Notes** (Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education framework)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prevention Safeguard and Protection Safeguard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prevention Safeguard and Protection Safeguard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the domain of Functional Safety and the broader framework of **Risk Reduction** (as defined under **IEC 61508** and **ISO 12100** — the international standard for safety of machinery), every industrial process, machine, or safety instrumented system is designed to bring the residual risk of a hazardous event down to a **Tolerable Risk (T)** level from its inherent (unmitigated) risk level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Safeguard**
> A **Safeguard** is any deliberate design measure, device, system, or procedure that is implemented to reduce risk to an acceptable (tolerable) level. Safeguards are broadly classified into two complementary categories: **Prevention Safeguards** and **Protection Safeguards**.

### 1.1.1 Prevention Safeguard (Preventive Safeguard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Prevention Safeguard (P)**
> A **Prevention Safeguard** is a measure, device, or system whose primary function is to **reduce the *likelihood* (frequency/probability) of a hazardous event** from occurring in the first place. It operates **before** the hazardous event is initiated, and its goal is to **prevent the hazard from being released** or to **prevent the dangerous condition from arising**. Examples include: safety interlocks, guards preventing access to moving parts, over-speed trips in turbines, safe operating procedures, and training of personnel.

### 1.1.2 Protection Safeguard (Protective Safeguard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Protection Safeguard (P)**
> A **Protection Safeguard** is a measure, device, or system whose primary function is to **reduce the *consequences (severity)* of harm to people, property, or the environment** once a hazardous event has already occurred. It operates **after** the hazardous event is initiated, and its goal is to **mitigate, contain, or limit the damage**. Examples include: emergency stop circuits, pressure relief valves, fire suppression systems, airbags in automobiles, catch nets on construction sites, and personal protective equipment (PPE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Crash" Intuition

Imagine driving a car. Two distinct safety philosophies apply:

| Domain | Prevention Safeguard (Before) | Protection Safeguard (After) |
|---|---|---|
| **Analogy: Driving a Car** | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), lane-keep assist, traction control — they prevent the crash from happening. | Airbags, crumple zones, seatbelts — they reduce injury *after* the crash has occurred. |
| **Analogy: A House Fire** | Fire-resistant wiring, avoiding open flames near curtains, smoke detectors with auto-cutoff gas valves — they prevent the fire from starting. | Fire extinguishers, sprinklers, fire-resistant doors, escape routes — they limit damage once the fire starts. |
| **Analogy: Industrial Reactor** | High-integrity pressure transmitter tripping a feed pump at unsafe pressure, interlocking on temperature — prevents runaway reaction. | Pressure relief valve (PSV), rupture disc, emergency vent — releases pressure *after* a dangerous overpressure has developed. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for Students:** Prevention = **"Stop it from happening."** Protection = **"Limit the damage once it does happen."** Both are needed because no prevention safeguard is 100% perfect; the residual risk must be handled by protective measures.

## 1.3 Why Both Are Necessary — The Risk Reduction Equation

The fundamental risk equation in functional safety is:

$$R = f \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk**
- $f$ = **Frequency (likelihood) of the hazardous event** (per unit time)
- $C$ = **Consequence (severity) of harm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Prevention Safeguard** primarily acts by **reducing $f$ (the frequency)**, while a **Protection Safeguard** primarily acts by **reducing $C$ (the consequence)**. To achieve the overall Tolerable Risk, both must work together.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Risk Reduction Framework

The standards **ISO 12100** and **IEC 61511** define a **layered (defense-in-depth) approach** to risk reduction, often visualized as the "**Onion Model**":

1. **Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design:** Eliminate the hazard itself (e.g., use lower-voltage power supply, replace hazardous chemical).
2. **Layer 2 — Prevention Safeguards (Engineering Control):** Reduce the *likelihood* of the hazardous event.
3. **Layer 3 — Protection Safeguards (Engineering Control):** Reduce the *consequence* if the event occurs.
4. **Layer 4 — Information for Use (Procedural):** Warnings, signs, training, operating procedures.
5. **Layer 5 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Last line of defense — protects the individual.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 students must memorize: **Inherent safe design > Prevention > Protection > Information > PPE**. Higher-priority layers must be exhausted before lower-priority layers are relied upon.

## 2.2 Detailed Characteristics — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | **Prevention Safeguard** | **Protection Safeguard** |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage of Action** | Before the hazardous event | After the hazardous event is initiated |
| **Risk Parameter Reduced** | Frequency ($f$) of occurrence | Consequence severity ($C$) |
| **Primary Goal** | Stop the event from happening | Limit the damage once it happens |
| **Trigger Condition** | Pre-event (precautionary, continuous monitoring) | Post-event (reactive, on-demand) |
| **Typical Activation Mode** | Continuous / demand-based trip (e.g., interlock opens a circuit when guard is removed) | On-demand during emergency (e.g., relief valve opens on overpressure) |
| **Examples (Industrial)** | Safety interlocks, guards, over-speed trips, burner management system | Pressure relief valve, fire suppression, deluge system, E-stop, containment |
| **Examples (Machinery)** | Two-hand control, light curtain, fixed perimeter guard | Emergency stop, hold-to-run, cushion, brake |
| **Failure Effect** | A failure may allow the hazardous event | A failure may increase the consequence of the event |
| **Dependability Metric** | PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) or PFH (per hour) | Availability, Response Time, Capacity |
| **Standardization** | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 / ISO 13849-1 | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 / API 520/521 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **essential for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination)**. Memorize the variables, units, and applicability.

### 2.3.1 Core Risk Equation

$$R = f \times C$$

The fundamental risk equation where risk $R$ is the product of frequency $f$ and consequence $C$.

### 2.3.2 Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{R_{\text{inherent}}}{R_{\text{residual}}}$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{inherent}}$ = Risk before any safeguard is applied
- $R_{\text{residual}}$ = Risk after safeguard is applied

The **Risk Reduction** (in decibels/logarithmic) is sometimes expressed as:

$$\text{Risk Reduction (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{RRF})$$

### 2.3.3 SIL Assignment Equation (IEC 61508)

The target Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) is determined by the required **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**:

$$\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$$

| SIL | Target PFD (Low-demand mode) | PFH (per hour, High-demand mode) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | **10 to 100** |
| **SIL 2** | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | **100 to 1 000** |
| **SIL 3** | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | **1 000 to 10 000** |
| **SIL 4** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | $\geq 10^{-9}$ to $< 10^{-8}$ | **10 000 to 100 000** |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, "$\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$" means: PFD must be at least 0.01 but less than 0.1.

### 2.3.4 PFD for a 1oo1 (One-out-of-One) System

A single channel with no redundancy — a typical **prevention safeguard** is often designed this way when high reliability is required:

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1}(t) = \lambda_D \cdot \frac{T}{2} + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T$$

Where:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous detected failure rate (per hour)
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $T$ = proof test interval (hours)

### 2.3.5 PFD for a 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two) Redundant System

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2}(t) = 2 \cdot \left( (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T}{2} \right)^2 \cdot \frac{T}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

Where $\beta$ is the **beta factor** (fraction of failures that are common-cause).

### 2.3.6 Total Risk Reduction — Combined Safeguards

When multiple safeguards (prevention + protection) are applied in series:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$$

OR for independent layers in a **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**:

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries (Oil & Gas, Petrochemical):** Prevention safeguards include Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) such as Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), High Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS), and Burner Management Systems (BMS). Protection safeguards include Pressure Safety Valves (PSV), flares, fire & gas systems, and dikes.
- **Machinery Safety (Manufacturing):** Prevention safeguards include light curtains, interlocks, two-hand controls, and guards. Protection safeguards include emergency stops, mechanical brakes, and energy-absorbing cushions.
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Prevention — electronic stability control, anti-lock braking. Protection — airbags, seatbelts, crumple zones, parachute systems.
- **Nuclear Power:** Prevention — multiple redundant trip systems. Protection — containment structure, emergency core cooling system (ECCS).

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Examination:** When asked "give two examples of prevention and protection safeguards," use **two different examples** for each (one from process industry, one from machinery is a safe combination).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Risk Equation to Safeguard Effectiveness

Starting from the basic risk equation, we want to show formally how a prevention safeguard and a protection safeguard each contribute differently to risk reduction.

### Step 1: State the Inherent Risk

Before any safeguard is applied, the process has an inherent risk:

$$R_{\text{inherent}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times C_{\text{unmitigated}}$$

For example, an unprotected chemical reactor may have a runaway reaction frequency of $f_{\text{inherent}} = 10^{-1}$ per year and unmitigated consequence (injury to operator) of $C = 1$ (normalized). Then:

$$R_{\text{inherent}} = 10^{-1} \times 1 = 10^{-1}$$

### Step 2: Apply a Prevention Safeguard

A prevention safeguard (e.g., high-integrity temperature interlock with PFD = $10^{-2}$) reduces the *frequency* of the hazardous event:

$$f_{\text{reduced}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{prevention}}$$

$$f_{\text{reduced}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-3} \text{ per year}$$

### Step 3: Apply a Protection Safeguard

A protection safeguard (e.g., pressure relief valve) reduces the *consequence* by venting the overpressure before catastrophic rupture:

$$C_{\text{reduced}} = C_{\text{unmitigated}} \times (1 - \text{Mitigation Efficiency})$$

If the protection safeguard is 90% effective:

$$C_{\text{reduced}} = 1 \times (1 - 0.9) = 0.1$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Residual Risk

$$R_{\text{residual}} = f_{\text{reduced}} \times C_{\text{reduced}}$$

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 10^{-4}$$

### Step 5: Risk Reduction Factor

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{R_{\text{inherent}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{10^{-1}}{10^{-4}} = 10^{3} = 1\,000$$

In decibels:

$$\text{Risk Reduction (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{3}) = 30 \text{ dB}$$

This achieves **SIL 2** level performance (RRF 100 to 1 000).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFD Calculation for a Prevention Safeguard (1oo1 Architecture)

A temperature interlock is installed as a prevention safeguard. Given:
- $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $T = 8\,760$ hours (1 year)

The simplified PFD formula (assuming dangerous detected failures cause automatic shutdown, so they do not contribute to PFD on demand):

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = (1 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{8\,760}{2}$$

$$= (1 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (4\,380)$$

$$= 4.38 \times 10^{-4}$$

This is **within SIL 3** target ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Combined Safeguards

A LOPA study on a tank overfill scenario has the following independent protection layers (IPLs):

| Layer | Type | PFD |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Process Control System (BPCS) | Prevention | $10^{-1}$ |
| Operator response to alarm | Prevention | $10^{-1}$ |
| Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) | Prevention | $10^{-2}$ |
| Pressure relief valve (PSV) | Protection | $10^{-1}$ |
| Bund/dike (containment) | Protection | Mitigates 50% consequence |

### Step 1: Determine the Mitigated Event Frequency

The mitigated frequency combines all prevention layers multiplicatively:

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{operator}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$$

Assume $f_{\text{inherent}} = 10^{-1}$ per year:

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

### Step 2: Consequence After Protection Layers

If the PSV fails (probability 0.1) and bund mitigates 50% of consequence:

$$C_{\text{residual}} = C_{\text{max}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PSV}} \times (1 - \text{Bund Efficiency})$$

$$C_{\text{residual}} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$$

### Step 3: Final Residual Risk

$$R_{\text{residual}} = f_{\text{mitigated}} \times C_{\text{residual}}$$

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$

This is well below most tolerable risk thresholds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Safeguard Selection

| Step | Question | If YES → Action | If NO → Continue |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Can the hazard be eliminated by inherent safe design? | Use inherent safe design — done | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is the frequency of occurrence still too high? | Add a **Prevention Safeguard** (interlock, trip, control) | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | Is the consequence still too severe if the event occurs? | Add a **Protection Safeguard** (relief, containment, PPE) | Stop — risk is tolerable |
| 4 | Do operators need procedural guidance? | Add warnings, signs, training (informational layer) | Stop |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Onion Model of Risk Reduction (Layered Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Layer5["LAYER 5 - PPE\nLast line of defense\nHard hats, gloves, masks"]
    Layer4["LAYER 4 - INFORMATION\nWarnings, signs, training\nOperating procedures"]
    Layer3["LAYER 3 - PROTECTION SAFEGUARD\nReduces CONSEQUENCE\nPressure relief valve, E-stop, sprinkler, airbag"]
    Layer2["LAYER 2 - PREVENTION SAFEGUARD\nReduces LIKELIHOOD\nInterlocks, trips, light curtains, BPCS, SIF"]
    Layer1["LAYER 1 - INHERENT SAFE DESIGN\nEliminates the hazard itself\nLower voltage, safer chemistry, smaller energy"]
    Hazard["HAZARD SOURCE\nHigh pressure, high temperature, moving parts, toxic gas"]
    Out1["Safe State\nResidual Risk within Tolerable Limit"]
    Hazard --> Layer1
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
    Layer3 --> Layer4
    Layer4 --> Layer5
    Layer5 --> Out1
    style Layer1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style Layer2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style Layer3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style Layer4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style Layer5 fill:#e2d5e8,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000
    style Hazard fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style Out1 fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The outermost (largest) layer is PPE — the last line of defense. The innermost (closest to the hazard) is the most effective. **Higher layers must be exhausted before lower layers are used.**

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Prevention vs Protection Safeguard Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Process["INDUSTRIAL PROCESS - e.g. Chemical Reactor"]
        InP["Process Input\nFeed, Heat, Agitation"]
        Reactor["Reactor\nHazard Source"]
        OutP["Process Output\nProduct"]
        InP --> Reactor --> OutP
    end
    subgraph Prev["PREVENTION SAFEGUARD LAYER"]
        Sensor1["Temperature Sensor"]
        Trip1["High Integrity Trip\nSIL 2 SIF"]
        Valve1["Shutdown Valve\nCloses feed on trip"]
        Sensor1 --> Trip1 --> Valve1
    end
    subgraph Prot["PROTECTION SAFEGUARD LAYER"]
        Sensor2["Pressure Sensor"]
        Valve2["Pressure Relief Valve PSV\nOpens on overpressure"]
        Vent["Vent to Safe Location\nFlare or scrubber"]
        Sensor2 --> Valve2 --> Vent
    end
    subgraph Op["OPERATOR LAYER"]
        Alarm["Alarm and Indicator"]
        Estop["Emergency Stop Button"]
        Alarm --- Estop
    end
    Reactor -.monitors.-> Sensor1
    Reactor -.monitors.-> Sensor2
    Valve1 -.closes feed to.-> InP
    Valve2 -.vents from.-> Reactor
    style Prev fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style Prot fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style Op fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style Process fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Risk Reduction Flow — From Inherent to Residual Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["STEP 0 - INHERENT RISK\nR_inherent = f_inherent x C_unmitigated\nHIGH RISK"]
    R1["STEP 1 - INHERENT SAFE DESIGN\nHazard reduced at source\nR1 = R_inherent x k1"]
    R2["STEP 2 - PREVENTION SAFEGUARD\nFrequency reduced\nf_reduced = f x PFD_prevention\nR2 = f_reduced x C"]
    R3["STEP 3 - PROTECTION SAFEGUARD\nConsequence reduced\nC_reduced = C x Mitigation\nR3 = f_reduced x C_reduced"]
    R4["STEP 4 - RESIDUAL RISK\nR_residual within TOLERABLE LIMIT\nAcceptable for operation"]
    R0 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    style R0 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#ffeeba,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style R4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Safeguard Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Identify Hazard and Estimate Risk"]
    Q1{"Can hazard be\neliminated at source?"}
    A1["Use INHERENT SAFE DESIGN\nEnd of risk reduction effort"]
    Q2{"Is frequency\ntoo high?"}
    A2["Install PREVENTION SAFEGUARD\nInterlock, Trip, Guard, SIF"]
    Q3{"Is consequence\nstill too severe?"}
    A3["Install PROTECTION SAFEGUARD\nRelief valve, E-stop, Containment, PPE"]
    Q4{"Residual risk within\nTOLERABLE limit?"}
    Done["Document and Operate\nResidual Risk Acceptable"]
    Re["Re-evaluate or Stop Operation\nRisk still too high"]
    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> A1
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2
    Q2 -- YES --> A2
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3
    A2 --> Q3
    Q3 -- YES --> A3
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4
    A3 --> Q4
    Q4 -- YES --> Done
    Q4 -- NO --> Re
    style Start fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style Done fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    style Re fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Prevention Safeguard and a Protection Safeguard. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Prevention Safeguard:** A safeguard that operates **before** the hazardous event occurs and **reduces the *likelihood* (frequency)** of the event. It prevents the hazard from being released.
> - *Example:* A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) that trips a feed pump when reactor temperature exceeds a safe limit, preventing runaway reaction.

> **Protection Safeguard:** A safeguard that operates **after** the hazardous event has been initiated and **reduces the *consequence (severity)* of harm** to people, property, or environment.
> - *Example:* A Pressure Relief Valve (PSV) on a chemical reactor that opens automatically on overpressure to vent gases safely, limiting damage.

| Basis | Prevention | Protection |
|---|---|---|
| Stage of action | Before event | After event |
| Risk parameter reduced | Frequency ($f$) | Consequence ($C$) |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for example, 1 for distinction)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**Q: State the fundamental risk equation used in functional safety. Explain how a prevention safeguard and a protection safeguard each reduce risk.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental risk equation is:

$$R = f \times C$$

Where $R$ = Risk, $f$ = Frequency of hazardous event (per year), $C$ = Consequence severity (normalized).

- A **Prevention Safeguard** reduces risk by decreasing the **frequency $f$** of the hazardous event. For example, an interlock prevents a press from starting when the guard is open, thus preventing the injury event.
- A **Protection Safeguard** reduces risk by decreasing the **consequence $C$** when the event occurs. For example, an emergency stop limits the duration of harm during a machinery incident, reducing injury severity.

The combined effect of both safeguards brings residual risk to a tolerable level.

**(3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 for prevention, 1 for protection)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Pattern: Each module typically has a 14-mark question with **internal choice (either/or)** between two sub-questions. Each sub-question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### ⭐ CHOICE QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Prevention Safeguard and Protection Safeguard in detail. Discuss with a **chemical reactor overpressure** scenario, how each type of safeguard is applied to bring the residual risk to a tolerable level. Use a labeled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A chemical reactor operates at 50 bar and 200 °C. Inherent risk is high because overpressure could rupture the vessel, releasing toxic chemicals.

**Prevention Safeguards (Reduce Frequency):**
1. **High Integrity Pressure Trip (SIF, SIL 2):** A pressure transmitter continuously monitors reactor pressure. If pressure exceeds 45 bar, the SIF trips the feed pump and closes the inlet isolation valve within seconds, preventing further pressure rise.
2. **Temperature Interlock:** A high-temperature trip stops the heater if temperature exceeds 210 °C, preventing the secondary cause of overpressure.
3. **BPCS Control Loop:** The Basic Process Control System maintains pressure at 50 bar using a control valve on the vent line.

These prevention measures act **before** the overpressure event by reducing the *frequency* of occurrence.

**Protection Safeguards (Reduce Consequence):**
1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PSV):** A spring-loaded PSV sized for 100% blocked outlet case opens at 48 bar to vent gases to a safe location (flare or scrubber), preventing vessel rupture.
2. **Rupture Disc:** A secondary protection device that fails at 52 bar if the PSV fails, providing redundancy.
3. **Bund/Dike:** A containment wall around the reactor that holds any spill, preventing spread to other equipment.
4. **Fire Suppression System:** A deluge system that activates if a fire is detected after a release.

These protection measures act **after** the overpressure event by limiting the consequence of the release.

**Block Diagram:** (See Section 4.2 for reference architecture)

**Conclusion:** The combination of prevention safeguards (reducing frequency) and protection safeguards (reducing consequence) reduces the inherent risk of reactor overpressure to a tolerable level as required by IEC 61511.

**(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 3 for reactor scenario, 2 for block diagram/conclusion)**

---

**(b)** A refinery distillation column has the following parameters. Compute the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** and identify the SIL level achieved. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

Given:
- Inherent frequency of overpressure event, $f_{\text{inherent}} = 10^{-1}$ per year
- Prevention safeguard: SIL 2 SIF with PFD = $10^{-2}$
- Protection safeguard: PSV with mitigation efficiency of 90% (i.e., reduces consequence by factor of 10)
- Unmitigated consequence, $C = 1$ (normalized)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Mitigated Frequency (After Prevention Safeguard)**

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}}$$

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-3} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2: Compute Mitigated Consequence (After Protection Safeguard)**

$$C_{\text{mitigated}} = C \times (1 - 0.90) = 1 \times 0.10 = 0.10$$

**Step 3: Compute Inherent and Residual Risk**

$$R_{\text{inherent}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times C = 10^{-1} \times 1 = 10^{-1}$$

$$R_{\text{residual}} = f_{\text{mitigated}} \times C_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-3} \times 0.10 = 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Compute Risk Reduction Factor**

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{R_{\text{inherent}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{10^{-1}}{10^{-4}} = 10^{3} = 1\,000$$

**Step 5: Identify SIL Level**

From the IEC 61508 table:
- RRF = 1 000 → lies in range **100 to 1 000 → SIL 2**

**Step 6: Express in Decibels (Optional)**

$$\text{Risk Reduction (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{3}) = 30 \text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** The combined prevention and protection safeguards achieve an overall risk reduction factor of **1 000**, corresponding to **SIL 2** safety integrity level.

**(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–5, 1 for dB calculation, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### ⭐ CHOICE QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Alternative]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of the **"Onion Model" of risk reduction**, explain the hierarchy of safeguards. Place prevention and protection safeguards in the correct layer and justify their position. Give **two real-world examples** for each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Onion Model** of risk reduction (per ISO 12100) is a layered defense-in-depth strategy. The closer a safeguard is to the hazard source, the more effective and reliable it is. Layers, from innermost to outermost:

**Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design (Most Effective)**
Eliminates the hazard itself rather than controlling it.
- *Example 1:* Using a 24V DC control circuit instead of 230V AC to eliminate electrocution hazard.
- *Example 2:* Substituting a flammable solvent with a water-based non-flammable cleaner.

**Layer 2 — Prevention Safeguards (Engineering Control)**
Reduces the *likelihood* of the hazardous event.
- *Example 1:* A **light curtain** on a hydraulic press that detects a hand entering the danger zone and immediately stops the press.
- *Example 2:* A **safety interlock** on a microwave oven door that prevents magnetron operation if the door is open.

**Layer 3 — Protection Safeguards (Engineering Control)**
Reduces the *consequence* if the hazardous event occurs.
- *Example 1:* A **fire sprinkler system** that activates after a fire starts, limiting damage.
- *Example 2:* A **crumple zone** in a car that absorbs crash energy, reducing injury to occupants.

**Layer 4 — Information for Use (Procedural)**
Warnings, signs, training, and operating procedures.
- *Example 1:* A **"High Voltage"** warning sign on a transformer.
- *Example 2:* **Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO)** procedures for maintenance.

**Layer 5 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — Last Resort**
Protects the individual if all other layers fail.
- *Example 1:* A **safety helmet** on a construction site.
- *Example 2:* **Insulated gloves** for an electrician.

**Justification of Position:**
- Prevention safeguards are placed **in Layer 2** because they act *before* the event and are more reliable than post-event protection.
- Protection safeguards are placed in **Layer 3** because they act *after* the event as a backup to prevention.
- Information and PPE are outer layers because they depend on human action and are least reliable.

**(7 marks: 1 for onion model explanation, 2 for prevention layer with examples, 2 for protection layer with examples, 2 for justification and remaining layers)**

---

**(b)** A 1oo1 (single-channel) prevention safeguard has the following failure data:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- Proof test interval $T = 4\,380$ hours (6 months)

Calculate the **PFD** and determine the **SIL level** achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the PFD Formula for 1oo1**

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

(We assume that dangerous **detected** failures $\lambda_{DD}$ lead to automatic safe shutdown, so they do not contribute to PFD on demand. This is the standard assumption for a single-channel SIL-rated system.)

**Step 2: Substitute the Given Values**

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = (2 \times 10^{-7}) \times \frac{4\,380}{2}$$

$$= (2 \times 10^{-7}) \times 2\,190$$

$$= 4.38 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Decimal Form**

$$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = 4.38 \times 10^{-4} = 0.000438$$

**Step 4: Identify SIL Level from IEC 61508 Table**

Recall the PFD ranges for each SIL (low-demand mode):

- SIL 1: PFD between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: PFD between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$
- SIL 3: PFD between $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-3}$

Since $4.38 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$, the achieved SIL is **SIL 3**.

**Step 5: Compute the Risk Reduction Factor**

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{4.38 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2\,283$$

This RRF (2 283) corresponds to **SIL 3** (range 1 000 to 10 000).

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation**

A proof test interval of 6 months is sufficient to maintain SIL 3 performance for this 1oo1 prevention safeguard. To improve reliability further, a redundant 1oo2 architecture could be adopted, which would lower the PFD by approximately an order of magnitude.

**(7 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for calculation, 1 for SIL identification, 1 for RRF, 1 for engineering interpretation, 1 for units/dimensional check)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "frequency" with "consequence" reduction:** Many students write that "prevention safeguard reduces severity." This is incorrect. Prevention = **frequency**; Protection = **consequence**. (Lose 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Giving only definitions without examples:** KTU examiners expect **at least one example per safeguard type**. A 7-mark question without examples may receive only 4–5 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state the units** of frequency (per year or per hour) and PFD (dimensionless). Always include units in the final answer.
>
> 4. **Confusing SIL with RRF:** SIL is a **discrete level (1, 2, 3, 4)** while RRF is a **continuous numerical factor**. State the relationship explicitly: $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Always draw a labeled block diagram for any "explain with example" question. KTU awards 1–2 marks specifically for a clear diagram.
>
> 6. **In PFD calculations, forgetting the $T/2$ factor** — it represents the average probability of failure during the proof test interval. Using $T$ instead of $T/2$ is a frequent error.
>
> 7. **Not stating which standard is being referenced** (IEC 61508, IEC 61511, ISO 12100, ISO 13849-1). Examiners expect at least one standard reference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins for KTU ESE 2024:**

- [x] **Risk Equation:** $R = f \times C$ — the foundation of all functional safety analysis.
- [x] **Prevention Safeguard** → acts **before** event → reduces **frequency ($f$)**.
- [x] **Protection Safeguard** → acts **after** event → reduces **consequence ($C$)**.
- [x] **Onion Model order** (innermost to outermost): Inherent safe design → Prevention → Protection → Information → PPE.
- [x] **Key Standards:** IEC 61508 (general), IEC 61511 (process industry), ISO 12100 (machinery), ISO 13849-1 (machinery control systems).
- [x] **SIL ↔ PFD relationship:** $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD})$ — memorize the table for SIL 1 to 4.
- [x] **SIL 1 PFD range:** $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; **SIL 2:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; **SIL 3:** $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; **SIL 4:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- [x] **1oo1 PFD formula:** $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$.
- [x] **Risk Reduction Factor:** $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{R_{\text{inherent}}}{R_{\text{residual}}}$.
- [x] **Risk reduction in dB:** $10 \log_{10}(\text{RRF})$.
- [x] **Industrial examples to memorize:**
  - *Prevention:* Interlock, Light curtain, Two-hand control, BPCS, SIF, HIPPS, Over-speed trip, Guard.
  - *Protection:* Pressure Relief Valve, Rupture Disc, Emergency Stop, Fire Sprinkler, Airbag, Crumple Zone, Bund/Dike, PPE.
- [x] **Hierarchy principle:** Always apply higher-priority layers first. PPE is the **last resort**, never the primary safeguard.
- [x] **Defense-in-depth:** Use **multiple independent protection layers (IPLs)** — never rely on a single safeguard.
- [x] **LOPA equation:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{inherent}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ (multiplicative for prevention layers).
- [x] **Tolerable Risk ($T$):** The risk level deemed acceptable by society/regulators — the goal of all safeguards.
- [x] **Beta factor ($\beta$):** Fraction of failures common to all channels in a redundant system — relevant for 1oo2 PFD calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercise on HAZOP and Bow Tie, Allocation of Safety Functions to the Protection Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Exercise on HAZOP and Bow Tie, Allocation of Safety Functions to the Protection Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)

**Formal Definition:**
HAZOP is a structured and systematic technique for examining a defined process or operation, originally developed for the chemical process industry, in which a multi-disciplinary team uses a series of guide words (e.g., NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) applied to process parameters (flow, pressure, temperature, level, etc.) to identify potential deviations from the design intent that could lead to hazards or operability problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** HAZOP is a qualitative risk identification technique. It does NOT provide numerical risk values, but identifies *what can go wrong*, *why*, and *what are the consequences*.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a doctor examining a patient. Instead of just asking "are you sick?", you systematically go through every body system — heart (more beats? fewer beats? no beats?), lungs (more breath? less breath?), etc. Each combination reveals a potential health issue. HAZOP does exactly the same for a chemical plant: it systematically asks about every process variable whether it could be TOO HIGH, TOO LOW, ABSENT, or WRONG, and traces the consequences. It is essentially a "medical check-up" for a process plant.

### 1.2 Bow Tie Analysis

**Formal Definition:**
The Bow Tie method is a graphical risk evaluation technique that displays the relationship between the causes of an undesirable event (the "Top Event") on the left side and the consequences of that event on the right side, with barriers (preventive barriers on the left, mitigative barriers on the right) placed in between to reduce the likelihood or impact of the event. It is essentially a combination of a Fault Tree (causes) and an Event Tree (consequences).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** A Bow Tie diagram is shaped literally like a bow tie — causes fan out on the left, consequences fan out on the right, and the knot in the middle is the "Top Event." Barriers (controls) are placed along the threat-consequence pathway.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a dangerous river (the Top Event) flowing from a mountainous threat (cause) toward a village (consequence). The Bow Tie model is like a dam system:
- **Upstream of the dam (left side):** Where threats originate (heavy rain, dam cracks, earthquakes).
- **The dam (Top Event):** The catastrophic event (e.g., dam break).
- **Downstream of the dam (right side):** Where consequences occur (flooding, loss of life, economic loss).
- **Barriers:** Levies, warning systems, evacuation plans that either prevent the dam break (prevention) or reduce the impact (mitigation).

### 1.3 Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers

**Formal Definition:**
The allocation of safety functions to protection layers is the systematic process of determining which safety functions are needed, what performance level (e.g., Safety Integrity Level — SIL) they must achieve, and assigning each function to an appropriate protection layer (e.g., process design, basic process control system (BPCS), safety instrumented system (SIS), physical protection, mitigation systems). This is typically done using techniques like the Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) as per IEC 61511.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle — Independent Protection Layer (IPL):** An IPL is a device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, AND is independent of the initiating event and any other layer in the scenario. Independence is the cornerstone of protection layer allocation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a multi-layered Swiss cheese model. Each slice of cheese represents a protection layer. A single slice has holes (failures), but if you stack multiple slices, the chances of all holes aligning to allow a hazard to pass through become vanishingly small. The "allocation of safety functions" is the engineering decision of *which* slice each safety function should belong to and *how thick* (reliable) each slice must be.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HAZOP — Detailed Operational Logic

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Define the scope and objectives** — Identify the system, node boundaries, and study intent.
2. **Form a multi-disciplinary team** — Includes process engineer, control engineer, safety engineer, operations, and maintenance.
3. **Divide the plant into nodes** (sections) — Each node has defined design intent and process parameters.
4. **Apply guide words to each parameter** for each node:
   - **NO / NOT** → Complete negation (e.g., NO FLOW)
   - **MORE** → Quantitative increase (e.g., MORE PRESSURE)
   - **LESS** → Quantitative decrease (e.g., LESS TEMPERATURE)
   - **AS WELL AS** → Additional activity (e.g., FLOW + CONTAMINANTS)
   - **PART OF** → Partial achievement (e.g., PART OF COMPOSITION)
   - **REVERSE** → Logical opposite (e.g., REVERSE FLOW)
   - **OTHER THAN** → Complete substitution (e.g., OTHER THAN WATER)
5. **Identify causes** for each deviation.
6. **Identify consequences** of each deviation.
7. **Identify safeguards** already in place.
8. **Generate recommendations** for unacceptable risks.
9. **Document** in a standard HAZOP worksheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard HAZOP Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Time, Sequence, Mixing, Concentration, pH, Speed, Viscosity, etc.

### 2.2 Bow Tie — Detailed Component Logic

**Core elements of a Bow Tie diagram:**

- **Top Event:** The central loss-of-control event (e.g., release of flammable material, loss of containment, overpressure).
- **Threats (Left side):** The causes or initiating events that can lead to the top event. Each threat has a pathway.
- **Preventive Barriers (Left):** Controls that reduce the likelihood of a threat progressing to the top event (e.g., BPCS alarm, operator intervention, safety instrumented function, mechanical relief device).
- **Consequences (Right side):** The undesirable outcomes if the top event occurs (e.g., fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental damage).
- **Mitigative Barriers (Right):** Controls that reduce the severity of the consequences (e.g., fire protection, emergency shutdown, evacuation, bund/dike).
- **Escalation Factors:** Conditions that can degrade a barrier's effectiveness (e.g., maintenance bypass, common cause failure, training gaps).

### 2.3 Allocation of Safety Functions — LOPA Logic

The Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) is a semi-quantitative risk assessment method that uses simplified categories of initiating event frequency, consequence severity, and protection layer Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) to determine the required Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

**LOPA Logic Steps:**

1. Identify a scenario (cause → consequence).
2. Determine the **Target Mitigated Event Likelihood (TMEL)** — the acceptable frequency of the consequence.
3. Identify the **Initiating Event Frequency (IEF)**.
4. Identify all **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** and assign their PFDs.
5. Calculate the **Mitigated Event Likelihood (MEL)**:

$$MEL = IEF \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

6. Compare MEL to TMEL. If MEL > TMEL, an additional risk reduction is required:

$$Required\ Risk\ Reduction\ Factor\ (RRF_{req}) = \frac{MEL}{TMEL}$$

7. The required RRF is the inverse of the required PFD for the SIF:

$$PFD_{SIF,req} = \frac{1}{RRF_{req}}$$

8. **Map PFD to SIL** as per IEC 61511:

| SIL | Target PFD (Average Probability of Failure on Demand) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|-----|------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1   | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$                        | 10 to 100                    |
| 2   | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$                        | 100 to 1000                  |
| 3   | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$                        | 1000 to 10000                |
| 4   | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$                        | 10000 to 100000              |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** For 14-mark questions involving LOPA, you MUST show the full sequence: IEF → IPL multiplication → MEL → compare to TMEL → Calculate RRFreq → Map to SIL. Skipping any one step costs 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| HAZOP Guide Words | NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN | Applied to process parameters |
| Bow Tie Top Event | Central loss-of-control event | Single point in diagram |
| LOPA Mitigated Event Likelihood | $MEL = IEF \times \prod PFD_i$ | Frequency per year |
| Required Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF_{req} = \dfrac{MEL}{TMEL}$ | Dimensionless |
| SIF Required PFD | $PFD_{SIF,req} = \dfrac{1}{RRF_{req}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| SIL to PFD Mapping | $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-SIL}$ (order of magnitude) | IEC 61511 |
| SIL RRF Range | $RRF = 10^{SIL}$ to $10^{SIL+1}$ | Per IEC 61511 Table 3 |
| Number of IPLs | Typically 3–6 in onion model | Process, BPCS, Alarms, SIS, Mechanical, Mitigative |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Exercise: HAZOP on a Pump Section

**Scenario Setup:**
A centrifugal pump takes suction from a storage tank and discharges to a heat exchanger. The design intent is to transfer 50 m³/h of process fluid at 4 barg discharge pressure.

**HAZOP Worksheet (Exhaustive Entry):**

| Node | Parameter | Guide Word | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Pump Section | Flow | NO | NO FLOW | Pump failure, closed valve, line blockage | Loss of production, possible pump overheating/damage | Low flow alarm, pump trip | Add redundancy, install flow switch |
| Pump Section | Flow | LESS | LESS FLOW | Partial blockage, pump wear, partial valve closure | Reduced production, increased residence time, possible process upset | Low flow alarm | Periodic maintenance |
| Pump Section | Flow | MORE | MORE FLOW | Pump overspeed, valve passing | Erosion, noise, downstream overloading | High flow alarm | Verify pump capacity match |
| Pump Section | Pressure | MORE | HIGH PRESSURE | Downstream blockage, thermal expansion | Pipe rupture, gasket failure, fire | Pressure relief valve (PRV) | Verify PRV sizing and certification |
| Pump Section | Temperature | MORE | HIGH TEMPERATURE | Pump dead-heading, ambient rise | Seal failure, fire | Temperature indicator, PRV | Add interlock to stop pump on dead-head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** In exam answers, the HAZOP worksheet should be presented in a tabular form as above. Drawing the table properly is worth 2 marks alone.

### 3.2 Worked Exercise: Bow Tie Construction

**Scenario:** Overpressure in a chemical reactor.

**Bow Tie Development (Step-by-step):**

1. **Define the Top Event:** Loss of containment of reactor due to overpressure rupture.

2. **Identify Threats (Causes):**
   - T1: External fire (heating jacket failure)
   - T2: Runaway exothermic reaction
   - T3: Blocked outlet
   - T4: Utility failure (cooling water loss)
   - T5: Operator overcharging reactants

3. **Identify Preventive Barriers (for each threat):**
   - For T1 (External fire): PB1: Fire detection system, PB2: Emergency deluge
   - For T2 (Runaway): PB3: Temperature control loop, PB4: High temperature SIF (SIL 2), PB5: Emergency cooling
   - For T3 (Blocked outlet): PB6: Flow indication, PB7: High pressure SIF
   - For T4 (Utility failure): PB8: Utility failure alarm, PB9: Backup cooling pump
   - For T5 (Overcharging): PB10: Mass flow meter, PB11: Operator training

4. **Identify Consequences:**
   - C1: Toxic gas release to atmosphere
   - C2: Fire/explosion
   - C3: Equipment damage
   - C4: Personnel injury/fatality
   - C5: Environmental contamination

5. **Identify Mitigative Barriers (for each consequence):**
   - For C1: MB1: Gas detection, MB2: Containment (bund)
   - For C2: MB3: Foam suppression, MB4: Emergency shutdown
   - For C3: MB4: Emergency shutdown, MB6: Emergency venting
   - For C4: MB5: Evacuation plan, MB7: Safe assembly point
   - For C5: MB8: Bund/dike, MB9: Spill response team

6. **Identify Escalation Factors:**
   - E1: Maintenance bypass of SIF
   - E2: Common cause failure (CCF) of redundant sensors
   - E3: Inadequate proof testing

### 3.3 Worked Exercise: LOPA Calculation for SIL Determination

**Scenario:** A reactor has the following parameters:
- Consequence: Toxic release (Major, off-site impact)
- Initiating Event: Cooling water pump failure
- IEF (per year): 0.1
- Existing IPLs: 
  - IPL1: BPCS temperature control loop (PFD = 0.1)
  - IPL2: High temperature alarm with operator response (PFD = 0.1)
  - IPL3: Pressure Relief Valve (PFD = 0.01)
  - IPL4: Bund containment for mitigation (PFD = 0.1)
- TMEL: $10^{-6}$ per year

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the LOPA equation.

$$MEL = IEF \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} \times PFD_{IPL3} \times PFD_{IPL4}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MEL &= 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate step by step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MEL &= 0.1 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.01 \\
&\text{then } \times 0.1 = 0.001 \\
&\text{then } \times 0.01 = 0.00001 \\
&\text{then } \times 0.1 = 0.000001 \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare MEL to TMEL.

$$MEL = 10^{-6} = TMEL$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion. The risk is already at the target. No additional SIF is required (RRFreq = 1, i.e., no safety instrumented function needed at this time).

**Step 6:** Suppose management wants to add a SIF as defense-in-depth. The required PFD would be:

$$PFD_{SIF,req} = \frac{1}{RRF_{req}} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

This shows that no SIF is required because the current IPLs already meet the target.

### 3.4 Alternative LOPA Example (SIL Required)

**Modified Scenario:** TMEL = $10^{-7}$ per year (stricter), IEF = 0.1, same IPLs.

**Step 1:** MEL = $10^{-6}$ per year (from previous calculation).

**Step 2:** Calculate required risk reduction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF_{req} &= \frac{MEL}{TMEL} \\
&= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-7}} \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate required PFD for the SIF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{SIF,req} &= \frac{1}{RRF_{req}} \\
&= \frac{1}{10} \\
&= 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Map to SIL.

From the table: PFD between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$ corresponds to **SIL 1**.

Therefore, a **SIL 1 SIF** must be implemented.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HAZOP vs Bow Tie vs LOPA

| Aspect | HAZOP | Bow Tie | LOPA |
|--------|-------|---------|------|
| **Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative |
| **Output** | Deviation list and recommendations | Visual diagram of risk pathways | Numerical risk with SIL |
| **Stage** | Early design / HAZOP phase | Risk communication | Detailed design / SIL allocation |
| **Team** | Multi-disciplinary | Risk specialists | Process safety + control engineers |
| **Standard** | IEC 61882 | ISO 31000 (extension) | IEC 61511 |
| **Numerical?** | No | No | Yes (orders of magnitude) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HAZOP Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Define Scope and Nodes] --> node2[Form Multidisciplinary Team]
    node2 --> node3[Select Process Parameter]
    node3 --> node4[Apply Guide Word]
    node4 --> node5{Deviation Identified?}
    node5 -- Yes --> node6[Identify Causes]
    node6 --> node7[Identify Consequences]
    node7 --> node8[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    node8 --> node9{Risk Acceptable?}
    node9 -- No --> node10[Generate Recommendation]
    node10 --> node11[Document in HAZOP Worksheet]
    node9 -- Yes --> node11
    node11 --> node12{More Nodes or Parameters?}
    node12 -- Yes --> node3
    node12 -- No --> node13[HAZOP Report Complete]
    node5 -- No --> node3
```

### 4.2 Bow Tie Structure — Reactor Overpressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Left side threats
    T1[Threat 1 External Fire] --> TE
    T2[Threat 2 Runaway Reaction] --> TE
    T3[Threat 3 Blocked Outlet] --> TE
    T4[Threat 4 Cooling Water Loss] --> TE
    T5[Threat 5 Overcharging] --> TE

    TE[Top Event Loss of Containment Overpressure] --> C1[Consequence 1 Toxic Release]
    TE --> C2[Consequence 2 Fire Explosion]
    TE --> C3[Consequence 3 Equipment Damage]
    TE --> C4[Consequence 4 Personnel Fatality]
    TE --> C5[Consequence 5 Environmental Damage]

    %% Preventive barriers on threat lines
    T1 -.-> PB1[PB1 Fire Detection]
    T2 -.-> PB2[PB2 Temperature Control BPCS]
    T3 -.-> PB3[PB3 Flow Indication]
    T4 -.-> PB4[PB4 Utility Failure Alarm]
    T5 -.-> PB5[PB5 Mass Flow Meter]

    PB1 -.-> TE
    PB2 -.-> TE
    PB3 -.-> TE
    PB4 -.-> TE
    PB5 -.-> TE

    %% Mitigative barriers on consequence lines
    TE -.-> MB1[MB1 Gas Detection]
    TE -.-> MB2[MB2 Bund Containment]
    TE -.-> MB3[MB3 Foam Suppression]
    TE -.-> MB4[MB4 Emergency Shutdown]
    TE -.-> MB5[MB5 Evacuation Plan]

    MB1 -.-> C1
    MB2 -.-> C5
    MB3 -.-> C2
    MB4 -.-> C3
    MB5 -.-> C4
```

### 4.3 Layer of Protection Onion (Protection Layer Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Layer 1 Process Design Inherent Safety] --> B[Layer 2 Basic Process Control System BPCS]
    B --> C[Layer 3 Operator Alarms and Supervision]
    C --> D[Layer 4 Safety Instrumented System SIS SIF]
    D --> E[Layer 5 Physical Protection PSV Rupture Disc Dike]
    E --> F[Layer 6 Emergency Response Fire and Gas Detection]
    F --> G[Layer 7 Community Response Evacuation Plan]

    IEF[Initiating Event Frequency] --> A
    A -->|Reduces Frequency| H[Mitigated Event Likelihood MEL]
    B -->|Reduces Frequency| H
    C -->|Reduces Frequency| H
    D -->|Reduces Frequency| H
    E -->|Reduces Frequency| H
    F -->|Reduces Frequency| H
    H --> Cmp{TMEL Achieved?}
    Cmp -- No --> AddSIF[Add Additional SIF and Recalculate]
    AddSIF --> A
    Cmp -- Yes --> End[Risk Acceptable]
```

### 4.4 SIL Determination Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Identify Hazard Scenario] --> node2[Determine Consequence Severity]
    node2 --> node3[Assign TMEL from Risk Matrix]
    node3 --> node4[Identify Initiating Event Frequency IEF]
    node4 --> node5[Identify Existing IPLs and PFDs]
    node5 --> node6[Calculate MEL]
    node6 --> node7{MEL greater than TMEL?}
    node7 -- Yes --> node8[Calculate Required RRF]
    node8 --> node9[Determine Required PFD for SIF]
    node9 --> node10[Map PFD to SIL 1 2 3 4]
    node10 --> node11[Assign SIF and Specify Proof Test Interval]
    node7 -- No --> node12[No Additional SIF Required Risk Acceptable]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define HAZOP and list the seven standard guide words used in a HAZOP study.**

**Model Answer:**
HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured and systematic technique used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant by applying guide words to process parameters and examining deviations from the design intent.

The seven standard HAZOP guide words are:
1. **NO / NOT** — Complete negation of design intent.
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase.
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease.
4. **AS WELL AS** — Additional activity or component present.
5. **PART OF** — Only part of design intent achieved.
6. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite occurs.
7. **OTHER THAN** — Complete substitution occurs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is a Bow Tie diagram? Name its key components.**

**Model Answer:**
A Bow Tie diagram is a graphical risk assessment tool that displays the pathway from causes (threats) to consequences through a central Top Event, with preventive and mitigative barriers placed along the pathway to reduce likelihood or severity.

Key components:
1. **Top Event** — Central loss-of-control event.
2. **Threats** — Causes that can lead to the top event (left side).
3. **Consequences** — Outcomes if the top event occurs (right side).
4. **Preventive Barriers** — Controls that prevent the top event from occurring.
5. **Mitigative Barriers** — Controls that reduce severity post-top-event.
6. **Escalation Factors** — Conditions that degrade barrier effectiveness.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the methodology of conducting a HAZOP study with the help of a worked example for a heat exchanger node.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a Bow Tie diagram for the scenario "Release of flammable material from a storage tank" showing at least three threats, three consequences, and suitable barriers.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — HAZOP Methodology and Worked Example:**

**HAZOP Methodology (Steps):**
1. Define scope, objective, and node boundaries.
2. Form a multi-disciplinary team (process, instrumentation, operations, safety).
3. Acquire design intent and P&ID for the node.
4. Apply guide words to each process parameter.
5. Identify deviations, causes, and consequences.
6. Identify existing safeguards.
7. Generate recommendations for unacceptable risks.
8. Document in a standard HAZOP worksheet.

**Worked Example — Heat Exchanger Node:**

Design intent: Cool 100 m³/h of process fluid from 150 °C to 60 °C using cooling water at 30 °C inlet.

| Parameter | Guide Word | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Temperature (Outlet) | MORE | HIGH OUTLET TEMP | Cooling water failure, fouling, low flow | Downstream equipment damage, process upset | TT, alarm | Install interlock on cooling water |
| Flow (Process) | NO | NO FLOW | Pump failure, blocked line | Loss of cooling, tube overheating | FT, low flow alarm | Add low flow trip |
