| Flow (Cooling Water) | LESS | LOW CW FLOW | CW pump issue, valve closed | Insufficient cooling | FT, alarm | Redundant CW pump |
| Pressure (CW Side) | MORE | HIGH CW PRESSURE | Blocked outlet, valve closure | Tube rupture, leak | PRV | Verify PRV sizing |

**Part (b) — Bow Tie Construction for Flammable Storage Tank Release:**

**Top Event:** Loss of containment of flammable material from storage tank.

**Threats (Causes):**
- T1: Tank overfill
- T2: External impact (vehicle collision)
- T3: Corrosion / wall thinning
- T4: Piping failure at connection

**Consequences:**
- C1: Pool fire
- C2: Vapor cloud explosion
- C3: Toxic exposure to personnel
- C4: Environmental damage

**Preventive Barriers:**
- PB1: Level control loop (BPCS)
- PB2: High level alarm with operator response
- PB3: High level SIF (SIL 2) — automated shutoff
- PB4: Bund/dike around tank
- PB5: Inspection and corrosion monitoring program
- PB6: Vehicle barrier / wheel stoppers

**Mitigative Barriers:**
- MB1: Foam fire suppression system
- MB2: Gas detection system
- MB3: Emergency shutdown (ESD) of transfer pumps
- MB4: Emergency evacuation plan
- MB5: Spill containment and recovery

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) method as per IEC 61511. Explain the concept of Independent Protection Layer (IPL) with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reactor has the following LOPA data: Initiating Event (cooling water failure) frequency = 0.1/year. Existing IPLs: BPCS temperature control (PFD = $10^{-1}$), Operator response to high temperature alarm (PFD = $10^{-1}$), Pressure Relief Valve (PFD = $10^{-2}$). The Target Mitigated Event Likelihood (TMEL) is $10^{-6}$/year. Determine the required SIL for the additional SIF.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — LOPA Description:**

**Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a semi-quantitative risk assessment method that uses simplified categories of initiating event frequency, consequence severity, and the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of each Independent Protection Layer (IPL) to determine if the existing layers of protection are sufficient to reduce the risk to an acceptable level, and if not, to determine the required Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for additional Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs).

**Key Steps in LOPA:**
1. Identify the hazard scenario (cause → consequence).
2. Determine the consequence severity category.
3. Assign a Target Mitigated Event Likelihood (TMEL).
4. Identify the Initiating Event Frequency (IEF).
5. Identify all existing IPLs and their PFDs.
6. Calculate the Mitigated Event Likelihood (MEL).
7. Compare MEL to TMEL; if MEL > TMEL, calculate the required RRF and PFD for additional SIF.
8. Map required PFD to SIL (1, 2, 3, or 4).

**Independent Protection Layer (IPL):**
An IPL is a device, system, or action that:
- Is **independent** of the initiating event and other protection layers.
- Is **effective** in preventing the scenario from progressing to the consequence.
- Has a **validated PFD** within the allowed range.
- Is **auditable** — its functionality can be checked and verified.

**Examples of IPLs:**
- BPCS control loop (PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$)
- Operator response to alarm (PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$)
- Safety Instrumented Function (PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$)
- Pressure Relief Valve (PFD $\approx 10^{-2}$)
- Bund/dike (PFD $\approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$)
- Fire and gas detection with suppression (PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$)

**Part (b) — SIL Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- IEF = 0.1/year = $10^{-1}$/year
- $PFD_{IPL1}$ (BPCS) = $10^{-1}$
- $PFD_{IPL2}$ (Operator) = $10^{-1}$
- $PFD_{IPL3}$ (PRV) = $10^{-2}$
- TMEL = $10^{-6}$/year

**Step 1: Calculate Mitigated Event Likelihood (MEL)**

$$MEL = IEF \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} \times PFD_{IPL3}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MEL &= 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 10^{-1-1-1-2} \\
&= 10^{-5} \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare MEL to TMEL**

$$MEL = 10^{-5} > TMEL = 10^{-6}$$

Risk is higher than acceptable. Additional SIF is required.

**Step 3: Calculate Required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RRF_{req} &= \frac{MEL}{TMEL} \\
&= \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-6}} \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Required PFD for the SIF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{SIF,req} &= \frac{1}{RRF_{req}} \\
&= \frac{1}{10} \\
&= 10^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Map to SIL**

From IEC 61511 Table 3:
- PFD in range $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ → **SIL 1**

**Conclusion:** A **SIL 1** Safety Instrumented Function is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to convert IEF to scientific notation (writing 0.1 instead of $10^{-1}$) — **Loses 1 mark**.
> - Failing to multiply ALL IPLs (missing the PRV term) — **Loses 2 marks**.
> - Skipping the comparison step (MEL vs TMEL) and directly going to SIL — **Loses 1 mark**.
> - Mapping the PFD to the wrong SIL (e.g., writing SIL 2 instead of SIL 1 for PFD = 0.1) — **Loses 1 mark**.
> - Not writing the formula in the answer — **Loses 1 mark** for lack of methodology.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HAZOP** is a **qualitative** technique using **7 guide words** applied to process parameters; it identifies *what can go wrong*, *why*, and the *consequences*.
- **Bow Tie** is a **graphical risk visualization** with Threats → Top Event → Consequence, sandwiched by **Preventive** and **Mitigative Barriers**.
- **LOPA** is a **semi-quantitative** method that uses **orders-of-magnitude** frequencies and PFDs to determine **SIL**.
- The **LOPA equation** is: $MEL = IEF \times \prod PFD_i$.
- The **Required SIF PFD** is: $PFD_{SIF,req} = \dfrac{TMEL}{MEL} \times \text{(product of all IPL PFDs)} = \dfrac{T}{RRF_{req}}$.
- **SIL mapping** as per **IEC 61511 Table 3**:
  - SIL 1: PFD in $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ (RRF 10–100)
  - SIL 2: PFD in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ (RRF 100–1000)
  - SIL 3: PFD in $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ (RRF 1000–10000)
  - SIL 4: PFD in $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ (RRF 10000–100000)
- **IPL Independence** is a strict requirement — no shared sensors, common cause failures, or operational dependencies.
- **HAZOP worksheets must be tabular**; the standard columns are: Node, Parameter, Guide Word, Deviation, Cause, Consequence, Safeguards, Recommendations.
- **Bow Tie diagram must clearly show**: Threats on the left, Top Event at the center, Consequences on the right, and barriers as labeled ovals on the connecting lines.
- **LOPA** cannot be used alone; it must build on **HAZOP outputs** (the scenarios identified in HAZOP are the input to LOPA).
- The "Onion Model" of protection layers (Inherent Design → BPCS → Alarms → SIS → Physical Protection → Emergency Response) must be referenced when describing protection layer allocation.
- **Common cause failures (CCF)** and **escalation factors** must always be considered when assessing barrier effectiveness in Bow Tie analysis.
- For 14-mark questions: present the **complete solution path** — every formula, every substitution, every comparison step. Skipping steps loses marks even if the final answer is correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Demand Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low Demand Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IEC 61508 / IEC 61511)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low Demand Mode of Operation** is a condition under which the frequency of demands on a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is **less than or equal to one per year** (i.e., demand rate $\le 1/\text{year}$), or where the total risk tolerance is dominated by the *probability of failure on demand* rather than by continuous exposure to hazardous conditions.

The Safety Integrity Level (SIL) under Low Demand Mode is quantified by the metric:

$$ \boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \quad \text{(Average Probability of Failure on Demand)}} $$

This is a **dimensionless probability** (range: $0 \rightarrow 1$), representing the *expected likelihood* that the safety function will fail to perform its required task when a hazardous demand actually occurs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fire Extinguisher on the Wall"

Imagine a fire extinguisher mounted in your college corridor.

- It sits there, **unused, for months or years** (Low Demand).
- You only hope it works **on the rare day** an actual fire breaks out.
- Because it is rarely tested for *real* action, you only know its health through **periodic inspections** (Proof Tests).
- If it fails silently between two inspections, no one knows — this is analogous to a **dangerous undetected failure** ($\lambda_{DU}$).

The fire extinguisher is the **SIF**, the rare fire is the **demand**, the inspection is the **proof test**, and the silent failure is the **$\lambda_{DU}$** that drives the PFD calculation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Boundaries

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Demand Rate** | Frequency of hazardous events requiring SIF action | $\le 1/\text{year}$ qualifies as Low Demand |
| **PFD** | Probability of Failure on Demand | Core KTU metric for Low Demand |
| **RRF** | Risk Reduction Factor $= 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Shows how much the SIF lowers risk |
| **Proof Test Interval ($T_{\text{proof}}$)** | Time between manual functional tests | Drives PFD growth between inspections |
| **$\lambda_{DU}$** | Dangerous Undetected failure rate | The *silent killer* of safety functions |
| **$\lambda_{DD}$** | Dangerous Detected failure rate | Caught by diagnostics, repaired quickly |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, you will frequently be asked to *contrast* Low Demand Mode with **High Demand / Continuous Mode**. Memorize the boundary: **demand rate $\le 1/\text{year}$ → Low Demand**, **demand rate $> 1/\text{year}$ → High Demand/Continuous**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Low Demand"? — The Operational Logic

A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) operates in Low Demand Mode when **all three** of the following engineering conditions are simultaneously met:

1. The SIF is **idle for long durations** (most of its operational life).
2. Demands arrive **rarely** (e.g., overpressure in a chemical reactor occurs once every several years).
3. The **process risk tolerance** is dominated by the *consequence* of one missed demand rather than continuous exposure.

In such scenarios, the relevant safety metric is **not failure rate per hour** (which would apply to continuous mode), but rather the **probability that the SIF fails to act on demand**.

### 2.2 SIL Mapping to PFD (Low Demand — IEC 61508-1 Table 2)

This is the **single most important table** for KTU theory and numerical questions:

| SIL Level | Target PFD$_{avg}$ Range | Target RRF | Typical Application |
|:---------:|:-----------------------:|:----------:|:--------------------|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1}$ | $10 \rightarrow 100$ | Simple protective interlocks |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$ | $100 \rightarrow 1000$ | Burner management, ESD valves |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3}$ | $1000 \rightarrow 10000$ | Turbine overspeed, reactor trips |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-4}$ | $10000 \rightarrow 100000$ | Niche (e.g., nuclear) — rarely demanded |

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Low Demand)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers $\sim 70\%$ of PESFT635 numerical and 14-mark questions.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} + \lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR$ | $\lambda_{DU}$, $T_{\text{proof}}$, $\lambda_{DD}$, $MTTR$ | Single-channel PFD (most common KTU formula) |
| 2 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot (\lambda_{DU})^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}}{3}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$, $T_{\text{proof}}$ | Two parallel channels, **both** must fail (voting: 1-out-of-2) |
| 3 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 2oo2}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$, $T_{\text{proof}}$ | Two channels in series, **any single** failure = dangerous |
| 4 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SYS}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}}$ | Sum of three subsystems | **Total SIF PFD** (always summed, NOT multiplied, for low demand) |
| 5 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | — | Risk Reduction Factor |
| 6 | $\text{SIL}_{\text{SIF}} = f(\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}})$ | Look up in Table above | Map calculated PFD to a SIL |

> **KTU Pitfall:** In Low Demand Mode, the system PFD is the **arithmetic SUM** of subsystem PFDs (because the failure probabilities are tiny and independent). In Continuous Mode, you would use a different formulation.

### 2.4 Physical Meaning of the Formula

- The term $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ comes from **averaging a linearly rising failure probability** between two proof tests. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ represents the *expected* time-of-failure within the interval (uniform distribution).
- The term $\lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR$ accounts for **diagnostically detected dangerous failures** that are repaired within a short Mean Time To Restoration.
- For **1oo2 redundant architectures**, PFD becomes *quadratic* in $\lambda_{DU}$ (and $T_{\text{proof}}$), because *both* channels must fail simultaneously — a huge SIL advantage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PFD$_{avg}$ for a 1oo1 Channel Under Proof Testing

**Problem Setup:** Consider a single safety channel with dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures per hour). After each proof test, the channel is restored to an "as good as new" state. Between proof tests, no failure is detected.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Probability of Failure.**
At time $t$ after a successful proof test, the probability that the channel has *failed dangerously* (and not been noticed) is:

$$ PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Low-Probability Approximation.**
Because $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ for typical industrial values ($\lambda_{DU} \sim 10^{-6}/\text{hr}$ and $t \le T_{\text{proof}} \sim 1\text{ year} = 8760\text{ hr}$), we use the first-order Taylor expansion:

$$ PFD(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t $$

**Step 3 — Average Over the Proof Test Interval.**
The average PFD across one full proof test cycle is the *integral average*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} PFD(t)\, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{\text{proof}}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the Detected-Failure Contribution.**
Detected dangerous failures cause a *short outage* of duration $MTTR$. Their average contribution is:

$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, detected}} \approx (\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot \frac{MTTR}{2} $$

**Step 5 — Combine Both Contributions (Final Master Formula).**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \;+\; (\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot \frac{MTTR}{2}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,/\text{hr}$
- $\lambda_{DD} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{hr}$
- $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760\, \text{hr}$ (1 year)
- $MTTR = 8\, \text{hr}$
- Architecture: 1oo1

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7})(8760)}{2} + \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-6})(8)}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} + \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-6})(8)}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-3} + 6.0 \times 10^{-6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.196 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $10^{-3} \le 2.196 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$, this single channel meets **SIL 2** requirements (barely).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "Which Formula Do I Use?"

| Architecture | Voting | Single Failure Consequence? | Formula Pattern | Order in $\lambda$ |
|:------------:|:-----:|:---------------------------:|:----------------|:------------------:|
| **1oo1** | 1-out-of-1 | Yes | Linear in $\lambda_{DU}$ | $O(\lambda)$ |
| **1oo2** | 1-out-of-2 | No (need both) | Quadratic in $\lambda_{DU}$ | $O(\lambda^{2})$ |
| **2oo2** | 2-out-of-2 | Yes (one fails → trip) | Linear in $\lambda_{DU}$ | $O(\lambda)$ |
| **2oo3** | 2-out-of-3 | No (1 safe failure OK) | Quadratic | $O(\lambda^{2})$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — A SIF in Low Demand Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR["Sensor Subsystem"] -->|Process Variable| LOGIC["Logic Solver PLC"]
    LOGIC -->|Shutdown Command| FINAL["Final Element Valve"]
    FINAL -->|Hazard Mitigation| PROCESS["Process Under Protection"]
    PROCESS -.->|Rare Hazardous Demand| SENSOR
    PROOF["Periodic Proof Test"] -.->|Restore to As Good As New| SENSOR
    PROOF -.->|Restore| LOGIC
    PROOF -.->|Restore| FINAL
    DIAG["Diagnostics DC"] -.->|Detect lambda_DD| LOGIC
    SENSOR --> PFD1["PFD Sensor"]
    LOGIC --> PFD2["PFD Logic"]
    FINAL --> PFD3["PFD Final Element"]
    PFD1 --> SYS["PFD SYS Sum of All"]
    PFD2 --> SYS
    PFD3 --> SYS
```

### 4.2 Failure Rate Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LAM["Total Failure Rate lambda"] --> LAMD["Dangerous Failures lambda_D"]
    LAM --> LAMS["Safe Failures lambda_S"]
    LAMD --> LAMDD["Dangerous Detected lambda_DD"]
    LAMD --> LAMDU["Dangerous Undetected lambda_DU"]
    LAMS --> LAMSD["Safe Detected lambda_SD"]
    LAMS --> LAMSU["Safe Undetected lambda_SU"]
    LAMDU -->|Drives PFD in Low Demand| FORMULA["PFD avg equals lambda_DU times T_proof over 2"]
    LAMDD -->|Short Outage MTTR| FORMULA2["PFD contrib equals lambda_DD times MTTR"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Time-vs-PFD Graph (Description for Board Drawing)

```
   PFD
    |
1.0|                                *
    |                            *
    |                        *
    |                    *
    |                *
    |            *
    |        *
    |    *
0.0 *----+----+----+----+----+----> Time (hr)
    0   T    2T   3T   4T   5T
        (Sawtooth — Proof Tests Reset PFD to Zero)
```

**Reading the graph:** Between every proof test, PFD rises linearly as $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ accumulates. At each proof test, undetected failures are discovered and PFD *resets* to zero (as-good-as-new). The **average height** of these sawtooth ramps equals $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ — exactly our formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking "Sketch PFD vs time for a Low Demand system with periodic proof testing." Draw the **sawtooth** and **label** the average line at half-height — full marks guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Low Demand Mode of operation as per IEC 61508. State the corresponding safety integrity metric.*

**Model Answer:**
> Low Demand Mode is defined under **IEC 61508** as a mode of operation where the safety function is demanded **no more than once per year** (i.e., the demand rate $\le 1/\text{year}$). The corresponding safety integrity metric is the **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$)**. It is a dimensionless probability representing the likelihood that the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) fails to perform its safety action when a hazardous demand occurs. The metric targets a target range based on the SIL level — e.g., SIL 2 requires $10^{-3} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between $\lambda_{DU}$ and $\lambda_{DD}$. Why is $\lambda_{DU}$ the dominant term in Low Demand PFD calculation?*

**Model Answer:**
> **$\lambda_{DD}$ (Dangerous Detected failures)** are failures of the safety function that are *automatically identified by built-in diagnostics* and result in a safe shutdown or alarm within seconds. **$\lambda_{DU}$ (Dangerous Undetected failures)** are dangerous failures that *remain hidden* until the next proof test or actual demand.
>
> In Low Demand Mode, $\lambda_{DU}$ dominates the PFD calculation because **the demand itself is rare** — a failure cannot "reveal itself" by causing a spurious trip. The diagnostics catch $\lambda_{DD}$ quickly (within $MTTR$, typically hours), making their contribution small. But $\lambda_{DU}$ failures *accumulate silently* across the entire proof test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$ (often a year), driving PFD to values like $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ — orders of magnitude larger.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper — KTU 2024 Scheme]* ⭐ Recommended Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for PFD$_{avg}$ of a **1oo1 (single-channel) Safety Instrumented Function** operating in Low Demand Mode, clearly stating all assumptions. Include the contribution from both dangerous undetected and dangerous detected failures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo1 pressure transmitter-based SIF is to be designed for **SIL 2** compliance. Given the following data, calculate the maximum permissible proof test interval $T_{\text{proof}}$:

- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\,/\text{hr}$
- $\lambda_{DD} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,/\text{hr}$
- $MTTR = 8\, \text{hr}$
- Target: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \le 10^{-2}$ (SIL 2 upper bound, conservative)

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Channel is restored to "as-good-as-new" after each proof test.
2. Failures follow a constant failure rate (exponential distribution).
3. $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ throughout the cycle (low-probability approximation).
4. $MTTR \ll T_{\text{proof}}$.
5. Common-cause failures are neglected (idealized).

**Derivation:**

*Step 1:* Instantaneous PFD at time $t$ after proof test:
$$ PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \quad \text{(since } \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1\text{)} $$

*Step 2:* Average over one proof test cycle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, undetected}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt \\
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}}{2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3:* Detected dangerous failures contribute during $MTTR$ window:
$$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, detected}} \approx \frac{(\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot MTTR}{2} $$

*Step 4:* Final expression:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot MTTR}{2}\;}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &\le 10^{-2} \\
\frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot MTTR}{2} &\le 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the detected term:
$$
\frac{(2 \times 10^{-7} + 5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 8}{2} = \frac{7 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 8}{2} = 2.8 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Substitute and isolate $T_{\text{proof}}$:
$$
\frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} + 2.8 \times 10^{-6} \le 10^{-2}
$$

$$
1 \times 10^{-7} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} \le 10^{-2} - 2.8 \times 10^{-6} \approx 9.9972 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
T_{\text{proof}} \le \frac{9.9972 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 99{,}972\, \text{hr} \approx 11.4\, \text{years}
$$

**Answer:** The proof test interval must be **less than approximately 11 years** (in practice, engineers round down to **1 year** with a strong safety margin).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how **Periodic Proof Testing** affects the PFD of a Low Demand SIF. Describe the "sawtooth" model in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two architectures are proposed for the same SIL 2 safety function: a **1oo1** single channel and a **1oo2** redundant channel. Given:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,/\text{hr}$
- $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760\, \text{hr}$
- $MTTR = 8\, \text{hr}$ (1oo1 only)

Compare the PFD$_{avg}$ of both architectures and state which one is more suitable for SIL 2. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Proof Test Sawtooth (7 Marks):**

The PFD of a Low Demand channel rises **linearly** with time as $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ (since failures accumulate silently between tests). When a proof test is performed, all hidden dangerous failures are revealed and repaired — the channel returns to an "as-good-as-new" state, so PFD drops back to **zero**. This creates a **sawtooth waveform**.

*Key Points to Write in Exam:*
- Average PFD over one cycle = area of triangle ÷ base = $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} \div T_{\text{proof}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$.
- Shorter $T_{\text{proof}}$ → smaller average PFD → higher achievable SIL.
- Proof testing is the **only practical means** to control $\lambda_{DU}$ accumulation in Low Demand Mode.

**Part (b) — Architecture Comparison (7 Marks):**

**1oo1 PFD:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} &= \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6})(8760)}{2} + \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6} + 0)(8)}{2} \\
&= 4.38 \times 10^{-3} + 4 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 4.384 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

This falls in the SIL 2 range ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$). ✅

**1oo2 PFD** (assume $\lambda_{DD} \approx 0$ for simplicity):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &\approx \frac{2 (\lambda_{DU})^{2} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}}{3} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \cdot (8760)^{2}}{3} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 10^{-12} \cdot 7.674 \times 10^{7}}{3} \\
&= \frac{1.535 \times 10^{-4}}{3} \\
&\approx 5.12 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

This falls in the **SIL 3** range. ✅✅

**Conclusion:** Both architectures meet SIL 2, but **1oo2 offers a much larger safety margin** and effectively achieves SIL 3 — recommended for higher-criticality applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\lambda_{DU}$ with $\lambda_{D}$** — students forget that *only the undetected portion* drives long-term PFD; if you use $\lambda_{D}$ (total dangerous), your PFD is **overestimated** by ~10×.
> 2. **Summing instead of multiplying** subsystem PFDs — in Low Demand, PFDs are summed (since they're tiny and independent); multiplying gives absurdly small (and wrong) numbers.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$** in the average — losing 2 marks per occurrence.
> 4. **Wrong architecture formula** — confusing 1oo2 (redundant, both fail) with 2oo2 (both required, any single fails). Read the voting notation **carefully**.
> 5. **Not converting units** — failure rates in /hr vs /year. Always convert: 1 year = 8760 hours.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Low Demand Mode**

- ✅ Low Demand Mode = demand rate $\le 1/\text{year}$.
- ✅ Core metric = **PFD$_{avg}$** (dimensionless probability).
- ✅ Risk Reduction Factor = $\text{RRF} = 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$.
- ✅ SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ · SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ · SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ · SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ✅ Master formula for 1oo1: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} + \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}) \cdot MTTR}{2}$.
- ✅ Master formula for 1oo2: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{2 (\lambda_{DU})^{2} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^{\,2}}{3}$.
- ✅ System PFD = **sum** of sensor + logic + final element PFDs (Low Demand only).
- ✅ Proof testing is the **only way** to "reset" $\lambda_{DU}$ accumulation.
- ✅ PFD vs Time plot = **sawtooth** with average at half-height.
- ✅ Redundancy (1oo2) provides **quadratic** PFD reduction — huge SIL advantage.
- ✅ Always **convert failure rates** to consistent units (per hour) before computing.
- ✅ Diagnostic coverage affects $\lambda_{DU}/\lambda_{DD}$ split — higher DC = lower $\lambda_{DU}$ = better SIL.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Demand Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Demand Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 / IEC 61508 Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Demand Mode** is defined by **IEC 61508** as the mode of operation in which the **demand rate on a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is greater than one (1) per year** (i.e., the safety function is required more frequently than once annually), but is **not continuous**. The integrity of the SIF is characterized by the metric **PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour)**, expressed in units of **per hour (h⁻¹)**.

For a clear boundary comparison (KTU Board-Standard Triplet):

| Operating Mode | Demand Frequency | Reliability Metric | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low Demand | ≤ 1 demand / year | **PFD** (avg) | dimensionless |
| **High Demand** | **> 1 demand / year** | **PFH** | **per hour (h⁻¹)** |
| Continuous | safety function always required | **PFH** | per hour (h⁻¹) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **smoke detector** in a busy **industrial kitchen** of a hotel.

- **Low Demand Analogy**: A fire alarm in a forest cabin — it might never ring in 20 years (low demand). We just need a *small chance of failing when the rare fire happens*.
- **High Demand Analogy**: A smoke detector in a **commercial kitchen** where smoke events are common (multiple times a day due to cooking). The detector must be *almost always working*, because the demand (smoke event) happens frequently.
- **Continuous Analogy**: An **aircraft altimeter sensor** — it is needed every single second to keep the plane flying safely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When a SIF is "demanded" often (e.g., burner management in a furnace cycling on/off, or an Emergency Shutdown System in a refinery), waiting for an *annual proof test* is too risky. We must compute the **probability that the system fails within ANY given hour**, not just per demand.

### 1.3 Standard PFH Targets per SIL (Safety Integrity Level)

For High Demand and Continuous Modes, the achievable Safety Integrity Levels are bounded as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SIL 1} &: \ 10^{-6} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-5} \ \text{per hour} \\
\text{SIL 2} &: \ 10^{-7} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-6} \ \text{per hour} \\
\text{SIL 3} &: \ 10^{-8} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-7} \ \text{per hour} \\
\text{SIL 4} &: \ 10^{-9} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-8} \ \text{per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit:** PFH is **per hour (h⁻¹)**. The smallest permissible PFH (SIL 4) is **10⁻⁹ h⁻¹**, equivalent to roughly **1 failure in 114,000 years**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does High Demand Mode Exist as a Separate Category?

In Low Demand Mode, the SIL target is achieved by ensuring the **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** is low enough — basically, what is the chance the system fails *exactly when the rare event happens*?

In High Demand Mode, the **demand rate λ_D** itself becomes a first-order contributor to risk:

$$
\text{Risk} \approx \text{PFD} \times \lambda_D
$$

When λ_D is large (> 1/year), even a small PFD gives a significant risk per hour. Hence, we bypass PFD and directly quantify **PFH (average dangerous failure frequency per hour)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High Demand Mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU Board Examination.** Symbols use **β_D** for the common-cause fraction (dangerous) and **t_CE** for the channel equivalent mean downtime.

| Architecture | PFH Formula (High Demand / Continuous Mode) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** (single channel) | $\text{PFH}_G = \lambda_{DU}$ |
| **1oo2** (1 out of 2 voting) | $\text{PFH}_G = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}$ |
| **2oo2** (2 out of 2 voting) | $\text{PFH}_G = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}$ |
| **2oo3** (2 out of 3 voting) | $\text{PFH}_G = 6 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}$ |
| Channel Downtime | $t_{CE} \approx \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2}$ (for perfect periodic proof test) |

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions and Variable Definitions

- **λ_DU** = Dangerous Undetected failure rate per hour. *Must include contribution from all sub-systems (sensor + logic solver + final element).*
- **β_D** = Common-cause failure fraction for dangerous failures; typically **2% to 10%** (KTU assumes β_D = 0.05 to 0.10 unless stated otherwise).
- **t_CE** = Channel Equivalent mean Downtime (in hours). Represents the average time a channel remains in a failed state before being restored.
- **T_proof** = Proof Test Interval (hours). For perfect testing, $t_{CE} = T_{proof} / 2$.
- **SIL** is achieved when computed PFH falls within the **KTU 2024 Table** listed in §1.3.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical High Demand SIF Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Oil & Gas Refining | Hydrocracker reactor protection (cycles frequently) |
| Power Plants | Boiler flame-failure detection (every restart is a demand) |
| Chemical Batch Reactors | Over-temperature trip during exothermic phases |
| Railway Signalling | Automatic Train Protection (ATP) systems |
| Robotics / CNC | Safety-rated reduced-speed monitoring in collaborative cells |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters to a KTU engineer:** In industries like Kerala's process plants (BPCL Kochi, FACT, etc.), High Demand SIFs are common. Improper PFH calculation directly leads to incorrect SIL — either **over-engineered (costly)** or **under-engineered (unsafe)** systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of PFH for a 1oo1 (Single Channel) SIF

In a 1oo1 architecture, the SIF fails dangerously if the single channel fails dangerously in a way that is undetected.

**Step 1 — Identify the failure mechanism:**
A 1oo1 channel fails when it incurs a dangerous undetected failure at rate **λ_DU**.

**Step 2 — Calculate the average number of dangerous failures per hour:**
Since failures occur randomly (Poisson process assumption), the average rate is simply the rate itself:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{1oo1} &= \text{(average rate of dangerous failures)} \\
&= \lambda_{DU} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** For 1oo1, T_proof and β_D have **no effect** on PFH — this is why 1oo1 rarely achieves more than SIL 1 in High Demand.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of PFH for a 1oo2 Architecture (KTU's Most Tested Case)

A 1oo2 system fails dangerously when:
- **Path A:** Both channels fail dangerously from independent causes (rare).
- **Path B:** A common-cause failure takes out both channels simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Independent failure of both channels:**
For each channel, the fraction of time it spends failed (undetected) is:

$$
\text{Avg. unavailable fraction} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}
$$

Both channels simultaneously unavailable (independent events):

$$
(\lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE})^2 \approx \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2
$$

The *rate of dangerous system failure* is obtained by dividing by t_CE (since a system fails "into" a state, then stays for ~t_CE):

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} \approx \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}
$$

For 1oo2, there are 2 channel pairs to consider → multiply by 2:

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}
$$

**Step 2 — Common-cause failure contribution:**
A fraction β_D of the dangerous failures are common to both channels, immediately disabling the SIF:

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{CCF}} = \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine (additivity of failure rates for small rates):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} &= \text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} + \text{PFH}_{\text{CCF}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Full Step-by-Step Numerical)

**Problem:** A 1oo2 SIF operates in High Demand Mode with the following data:

- λ_DU = 1 × 10⁻⁶ per hour
- β_D = 5% = 0.05
- T_proof = 8760 hours (1 year)

**Step 1 — Compute the channel equivalent mean downtime:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{CE} &= \frac{T_{proof}}{2} \\
&= \frac{8760}{2} \\
&= 4380 \ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the independent-failure PFH term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} &= 2 \cdot (\lambda_{DU})^2 \cdot t_{CE} \\
&= 2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 4380 \\
&= 2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4380 \\
&= 8760 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 8.76 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the common-cause PFH term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{CCF}} &= \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \\
&= 0.05 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFH:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} &= 8.76 \times 10^{-9} + 5 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 0.876 \times 10^{-8} + 5 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 5.876 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Map to SIL:**

$$
5.876 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1} \in [10^{-8}, \ 10^{-7}) \Rightarrow \textbf{SIL 3} \ \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Proof Test Interval on SIL

Continuing the same example with T_proof doubled:

| T_proof (hours) | t_CE (hours) | PFH_ind (h⁻¹) | PFH_CCF (h⁻¹) | PFH_total (h⁻¹) | SIL |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 8760 (1 yr) | 4380 | 8.76 × 10⁻⁹ | 5.0 × 10⁻⁸ | **5.876 × 10⁻⁸** | **SIL 3** |
| 43800 (5 yr) | 21900 | 4.38 × 10⁻⁸ | 5.0 × 10⁻⁸ | **9.38 × 10⁻⁸** | **SIL 3** |
| 87600 (10 yr) | 43800 | 8.76 × 10⁻⁸ | 5.0 × 10⁻⁸ | **1.376 × 10⁻⁷** | **SIL 2** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Takeaway:** Lengthening the proof test interval in High Demand Mode causes **SIL degradation** because t_CE grows linearly, amplifying the independent-failure PFH term. This is the inverse of intuition (one might think "longer T_proof means less testing cost = better"), but mathematically PFH worsens.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mode Selection Logic Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Hazard Analysis Initiated] --> B[Determine Demand Rate on SIF]
    B --> C{Demand Frequency Check}
    C --> D[Demand Rate less than or equal to 1 per year]
    C --> E[Demand Rate greater than 1 per year]
    C --> F[Safety Function required continuously]
    D --> G[LOW DEMAND MODE]
    E --> H[HIGH DEMAND MODE]
    F --> I[CONTINUOUS MODE]
    G --> J[Compute PFD average using lambda DU times T proof]
    H --> K[Compute PFH using lambda DU and t CE]
    I --> L[Compute PFH using lambda DU and t CE]
    J --> M[Map to SIL 1 to SIL 4 Table]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[SIF Architecture Designed to meet Target SIL]
```

### 4.2 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Block Diagram — High Demand Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Sensor primary] --> C[Logic Solver Input Module]
    A2[Process Sensor secondary] --> C
    C --> D[Logic Solver CPU executing SIF Logic]
    D --> E[Output Module to Final Element]
    E --> F1[Final Element Valve A]
    E --> F2[Final Element Valve B]
    F1 --> G[Process shutdown achieved]
    F2 --> G
    H[Dangerous Undetected Failure lambda DU per hour] -.affects.-> A
    H -.affects.-> A2
    H -.affects.-> C
    H -.affects.-> D
    H -.affects.-> E
    H -.affects.-> F1
    H -.affects.-> F2
    I[Proof Test every T proof hours] -.restores.-> H
    J[Common Cause Failure beta D] -.affects.-> A2
    J -.affects.-> C
    J -.affects.-> E
    J -.affects.-> F2
```

### 4.3 PFH vs PFD Threshold Diagram (Conceptual Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demand Occurs on SIF] --> B{PFD - failure on single demand}
    A --> C{PFH - failure frequency per hour}
    B --> D[Low Demand Mode - sporadic]
    C --> E[High Demand Mode - frequent]
    C --> F[Continuous Mode - always needed]
    D --> G[SIL 1: 1.0E-02 to 1.0E-01]
    D --> H[SIL 2: 1.0E-03 to 1.0E-02]
    D --> I[SIL 3: 1.0E-04 to 1.0E-03]
    D --> J[SIL 4: 1.0E-05 to 1.0E-04]
    E --> K[SIL 1: 1.0E-06 to 1.0E-05 per hour]
    E --> L[SIL 2: 1.0E-07 to 1.0E-06 per hour]
    E --> M[SIL 3: 1.0E-08 to 1.0E-07 per hour]
    E --> N[SIL 4: 1.0E-09 to 1.0E-08 per hour]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define **High Demand Mode of operation** as per IEC 61508. State the metric used to characterize the safety integrity for a SIF operating in this mode.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
High Demand Mode is the operating condition in which the **demand rate on a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is greater than one (1) per year**, but the safety function is not required continuously. The metric used to characterize safety integrity in this mode is **PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour)**, expressed in **per hour (h⁻¹)**. For example, SIL 3 corresponds to PFH between 10⁻⁸ and 10⁻⁷ per hour. *(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition, 1 mark for metric, 0.5 mark for unit/example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Compare **High Demand Mode** and **Low Demand Mode** with respect to demand rate and the safety integrity metric used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Parameter | Low Demand Mode | High Demand Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Demand Rate | ≤ 1 per year | > 1 per year |
| Metric | PFD (avg) — dimensionless | PFH — per hour (h⁻¹) |
| Typical Application | Fire & gas detection | Burner management, hydrocrackers |

*(1 mark each row.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Define the three modes of operation of a safety instrumented function as per IEC 61508. Explain with one industry example each, why High Demand Mode requires a different reliability metric than Low Demand Mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 SIF operates in High Demand Mode with the following parameters:
   - λ_DU = 5 × 10⁻⁷ per hour
   - β_D = 10% = 0.10
   - T_proof = 17520 hours (2 years)

   Calculate the PFH and determine the **SIL achieved**. Also, comment on how the SIL will change if T_proof is reduced to 8760 hours (1 year). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Three Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | Demand Rate | Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low Demand | ≤ 1 per year | PFD |
| High Demand | > 1 per year | PFH |
| Continuous | safety function always required | PFH |

**Step 2 — Why different metric? (2 marks):**
In High Demand Mode, the demand rate λ_D is high, so the **risk** = PFD × λ_D becomes unacceptable even with low PFD. Hence PFH (probability per hour) directly quantifies risk regardless of when the next demand occurs.

**Step 3 — Industry Example (2 marks):**
- *Low Demand:* Forest fire alarm system in a sparsely populated area — may be demanded less than once per decade.
- *High Demand:* Furnace flame-failure detection system — restarts occur daily/weekly, so the SIF must be available most of the time.
- *Continuous:* Aircraft altimeter for terrain avoidance — must always function.

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute t_CE for T_proof = 17520 h:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{CE} &= \frac{17520}{2} = 8760 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute independent-failure PFH term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} &= 2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot 8760 \\
&= 2 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 8760 \\
&= 438000 \times 10^{-14} \\
&= 4.38 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute common-cause PFH term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{CCF}} &= 0.10 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-7}) \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFH and SIL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} &= 4.38 \times 10^{-9} + 5.0 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 0.438 \times 10^{-8} + 5.0 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 5.438 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $5.438 \times 10^{-8} \in [10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$, the **SIL achieved is SIL 3**.

**Step 5 — Effect of halving T_proof to 8760 h:**

New t_CE = 4380 h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} &= 2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot 4380 \\
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

PFH_CCF remains $5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ (independent of T_proof).

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{new}} = 2.19 \times 10^{-9} + 5.0 \times 10^{-8} = 5.219 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Comment:** Reducing T_proof from 2 years to 1 year **lowers PFH only marginally** (by ~4%) because the dominant term is the **common-cause PFH** which is independent of T_proof. Therefore, in a 1oo2 architecture, simply increasing testing frequency has **diminishing returns** — to significantly improve SIL, the architecture must be improved (e.g., to 2oo3 with diagnostic coverage) or β_D must be reduced via diversity. *(SIL remains SIL 3.)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable derivations how **PFH is calculated for a 1oo1 and a 1oo2 architecture** operating in High Demand Mode. Discuss the role of the proof test interval (T_proof). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process plant has two redundant pressure transmitters feeding a Safety Shutdown System (SDS) in a 1oo2 voting arrangement, with:
   - λ_DU for each transmitter = 2 × 10⁻⁷ per hour
   - β_D = 5% = 0.05
   - T_proof = 4380 hours (6 months)

   Calculate the PFH and verify whether the SIF meets **SIL 2** requirements. If the proof test interval is increased to 17520 hours (2 years), determine the new SIL. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 Marks

**1oo1 Derivation (3 marks):**
A 1oo1 SIF has only one channel. It fails dangerously whenever an undetected dangerous failure occurs. The failure rate per hour is exactly λ_DU:

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}
$$

T_proof has **no effect** because the failure is "silent" — once it occurs, the channel is permanently failed (until proof test detects it, but in continuous operation, the next demand may occur at any moment).

**1oo2 Derivation (3 marks):**
A 1oo2 SIF fails if **both channels fail independently** OR if a **common-cause failure** disables both.

- Independent simultaneous failure: rate ≈ λ_DU × (λ_DU × t_CE) per channel-pair; 2 such pairs exist → 2 × λ_DU² × t_CE
- Common-cause contribution: β_D × λ_DU

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU}
$$

with $t_{CE} \approx T_{proof} / 2$.

**Role of T_proof (1 mark):**
T_proof enters only through t_CE. **Shorter T_proof → smaller t_CE → smaller PFH_ind**, but does not affect the common-cause term. For High Demand Mode, T_proof is typically **shorter (6 months to 2 years)** than for Low Demand (5–10 years), because undetected failures must be detected quickly given the high demand rate.

---

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute t_CE for T_proof = 4380 h:**

$$
t_{CE} = \frac{4380}{2} = 2190 \ \text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PFH_ind:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind}} &= 2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot 2190 \\
&= 2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 2190 \\
&= 1.752 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PFH_CCF:**

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{CCF}} = 0.05 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFH and SIL check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} &= 1.752 \times 10^{-10} + 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 0.01752 \times 10^{-8} + 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \\
&= 1.01752 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $1.01752 \times 10^{-8} \in [10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$ → **SIL 3** achieved. Since SIL 3 > SIL 2 requirement, the SIF **meets SIL 2 with margin** (it actually exceeds it). *(3 marks for correct calculation, 1 mark for SIL mapping.)*

**Step 5 — Recalculation for T_proof = 17520 h:**

New t_CE = 8760 h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_{\text{ind,new}} &= 2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot 8760 \\
&= 2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 8760 \\
&= 7.008 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

PFH_CCF is unchanged (independent of T_proof):

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{new}} = 7.008 \times 10^{-10} + 1.0 \times 10^{-8} = 1.0701 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**New SIL:** Still within $[10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$ → **SIL 3** is maintained.

**Conclusion:** Increasing T_proof from 6 months to 2 years increased PFH by only ~5%. The system still meets SIL 2 (and SIL 3) because the common-cause term dominates and is unaffected by proof testing.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU Board marks:**
>
> 1. **Using PFD formula in High Demand questions:** Many students write `PFD = λ_DU × T_proof` — this is the **Low Demand formula**. In High Demand, you must use PFH, where the **square term** `λ_DU²` appears.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in 1oo2:** The independent-failure term for 1oo2 has a **coefficient of 2** (two channel pairs), not 1.
> 3. **Forgetting t_CE = T_proof/2:** Many students plug T_proof directly instead of halving it. **Always use t_CE = T_proof / 2 for perfect periodic proof testing.**
> 4. **Wrong SIL boundaries:** SIL 3 in High Demand is **10⁻⁸ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁷**, NOT 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ (that is Low Demand territory).
> 5. **Ignoring the common-cause term:** β_D × λ_DU term is often missed in textbook shortcuts — it is the **dominant term** for most 1oo2 designs.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU Board Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** High Demand Mode = demand rate **> 1 per year** on a SIF.
- [ ] **Metric:** **PFH** (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour), unit is **h⁻¹**.
- [ ] **SIL Boundary (High Demand):** SIL 1 → 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵; SIL 2 → 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶; SIL 3 → 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁷; SIL 4 → 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁸.
- [ ] **1oo1 PFH** = **λ_DU** (T_proof irrelevant, β_D irrelevant).
- [ ] **1oo2 PFH** = **2 λ_DU² t_CE + β_D λ_DU**.
- [ ] **t_CE = T_proof / 2** (assumes perfect periodic proof testing with full restoration).
- [ ] **Common-cause term** (β_D λ_DU) is **independent of T_proof** — cannot be reduced by more frequent testing.
- [ ] **Independent-failure term** is **proportional to T_proof** — shorter testing improves this term.
- [ ] **PFH vs PFD:** PFD is dimensionless; PFH is in per-hour units (h⁻¹).
- [ ] **High Demand ≠ Continuous:** High Demand has a definable interval between demands; Continuous has essentially no interval.
- [ ] **Typical applications:** Burner management, hydrocracker reactor protection, batch reactor overtemperature trips, automatic train protection.
- [ ] **Architectural insight:** 1oo1 cannot typically exceed SIL 1 in High Demand; 1oo2 commonly achieves SIL 2 or SIL 3; 2oo3 is needed for SIL 4.
- [ ] **Diagnostic coverage (DC)** is critical in High Demand to convert dangerous undetected (λ_DU) failures into dangerous detected (λ_DD) failures, but λ_DD failures must still be handled (safe-failure fraction considerations).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU/IEC 61508 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Mode of Operation:** A mode of operation where the safety function is *permanently active* and continuously controls the EUC (Equipment Under Control) to maintain a safe state, such that any dangerous failure of the safety function results in an immediate hazardous event. The target measure of risk reduction is the **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)** of the safety function.

The relevant standards — **IEC 61508** (functional safety of electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems) and **IEC 61511** (process industry) — distinguish three operating modes:

| Mode | Trigger Frequency | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Low Demand Mode | ≤ 1 demand per year | PFD<sub>avg</sub> (avg. prob. of failure on demand) |
| High Demand Mode | > 1 demand per year | PFH (prob. of failure per hour) |
| **Continuous Mode** | **Demand is effectively constant / safety function never released** | **PFH** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In continuous mode, the safety function is *not* a "standby" interlock — it is part of the live control loop. Think of an aircraft fly-by-wire envelope protection, an automated train braking system, or the over-speed trip of a turbine. The process cannot be safely operated even for one second without that function being live.

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Always-On Lifeguard"

Imagine three lifeguards at a swimming pool:

- **Low Demand** = A lifeguard sitting on a chair who is *called* only when someone appears to be drowning (he must respond to a *request*).
- **High Demand** = A lifeguard on patrol who is asked to jump in more than once a year.
- **Continuous Mode** = A lifeguard who is *already in the water*, actively keeping a struggling swimmer's head above the surface. The moment he loses his grip (fails), the swimmer immediately goes under — there is no "demand" to respond to because the danger is constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** Because the dangerous event is *immediate* upon failure, KTU / IEC 61508 requires the *probability of failure per hour* (PFH), not PFD<sub>avg</sub>. Failure rate (λ) — measured in **per hour (h⁻¹)** or **FIT (Failures In Time, 1 FIT = 10⁻⁹/h)** — becomes the dominant variable.

## Standard Numerical Targets (IEC 61508 Table 2 / Table 3)

| SIL | PFH (per hour) | Equivalent "FIT budget" |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | ≥ 10⁻⁶ to < 10⁻⁵ | 1,000 – 10,000 FIT |
| SIL 2 | ≥ 10⁻⁷ to < 10⁻⁶ | 100 – 1,000 FIT |
| SIL 3 | ≥ 10⁻⁸ to < 10⁻⁷ | 10 – 100 FIT |
| SIL 4 | ≥ 10⁻⁹ to < 10⁻⁸ | 1 – 10 FIT |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Safety Function in Continuous Mode

A safety function in continuous mode is realised by a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** composed of three subsystems in series:

1. **Sensor subsystem (S)** – detects process variable continuously (e.g., pressure transmitter, speed probe).
2. **Logic solver (L)** – computes the safety action (e.g., Safety PLC, relay logic).
3. **Final Element (F)** – executes the safety action (e.g., shut-off valve, circuit breaker).

> [!NOTE]
> Each subsystem has its own failure rate **λ** and is decomposed into **safe** (**λ<sub>S</sub>**) and **dangerous** (**λ<sub>D</sub>**) fractions. The dangerous fraction is further split into **detected** (**λ<sub> DD</sub>**) and **undetected** (**λ<sub>DU</sub>**).

## 2.2 The Failure-Rate Decomposition (Foundational Equation)

$$
\lambda \;=\; \lambda_{S} \;+\; \lambda_{D} \;=\; \lambda_{S} \;+\; \lambda_{DD} \;+\; \lambda_{DU}
$$

Where the diagnostic coverage **DC** is defined as:

$$
DC \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_{DD} \;=\; DC \cdot \lambda_{D},\;\; \lambda_{DU} \;=\; (1-DC)\cdot \lambda_{D}
$$

## 2.3 Common-Cause Failure (β-Factor Model)

In redundant architectures (1oo2, 2oo3, etc.), the **β-factor** quantifies the fraction of failures that defeat the redundancy:

$$
\lambda_{D,\text{independent}} = (1-\beta)\,\lambda_{D}, \qquad \lambda_{D,\text{CCF}} = \beta\,\lambda_{D}
$$

A typical engineering value is **β = 10 %** for well-separated, diverse channels; **β = 2 %** is the optimistic lower limit per IEC 61508.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **the single most important reference for KTU board problems on continuous mode**. Memorise the PFH expressions for each architecture — they appear every year.

| Architecture | PFH Expression (per hour) | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** (single channel) | $\text{PFH}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}$ | No redundancy; λ<sub>DU</sub> is the dangerous-undetected rate of the only channel. |
| **1oo2** (1-out-of-2, voted) | $\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2\,(1-\beta)\,\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE} \;+\; \beta\,\lambda_{DU}$ | Both must fail; very low PFH. $\text{t}_{CE}$ = channel-equivalent mean downtime. |
| **2oo2** (2-out-of-2) | $\text{PFH}_{2oo2} = 2\,\lambda_{DU}$ | Both must operate; one failure = system fail. Used where spurious trip cost is high. |
| **2oo3** (2-out-of-3) | $\text{PFH}_{2oo3} = 6\,(1-\beta)^{2}\,\lambda_{DU}^{3}\,t_{CE}^{2} \;+\; 3\beta(1-\beta)\,\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE} \;+\; \beta^{2}\,\lambda_{DU}$ | Highest availability with low PFH; aerospace / nuclear typical. |
| **1oo2D** (1oo2 with diagnostic) | $\text{PFH}_{1oo2D} = 2\lambda_{DU,\text{single}} \quad\text{or}\quad 6\lambda_{DD}\lambda_{DU}\,t_{CE} + \beta\lambda_{DU}$ (depending on whether DD is a safe or dangerous failure) | Diagnostic converts many DD to a controlled shutdown. |

### Supplementary Equations

**Mean downtime / proof-test interval (T):**

$$
t_{CE} \;=\; \frac{T}{2} \;+\; \text{MTTR} \quad\text{(channel-equivalent mean downtime)}
$$

For continuous mode, the **proof-test interval T** is replaced by the **diagnostic test interval τ** (often much shorter than T), because proof-testing during operation is impractical.

**Subsystem PFH combination (series, pessimistic):**

$$
\text{PFH}_{\text{SIF}} \;\approx\; \text{PFH}_{\text{Sensor}} \;+\; \text{PFH}_{\text{Logic}} \;+\; \text{PFH}_{\text{Final Element}}
$$

**Probability of dangerous failure per hour to SIL mapping:**

$$
\text{SIL}_{n} \;:\; 10^{-(n+5)} \;\le\; \text{PFH} \;<\; 10^{-(n+6)} \quad\text{for SIL }n
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Continuous-mode SIL design is mandatory in:

- **Aerospace** (DO-178C / DO-254, ARP 4761) — flight-control envelope protection.
- **Automotive** (ISO 26262 ASIL) — electronic stability control, autonomous emergency braking.
- **Rail** (EN 50128/50129) — ETCS / ATP train protection.
- **Process industry** — burner-management systems (NFPA 85), turbomachinery overspeed, compressor anti-surge.
- **Medical devices** (IEC 62304) — radiation therapy beam interlock, ventilator pressure limiting.

In all of these, the safety function **never "turns off"**, so its PFH must be verified for the **whole mission time**, not just a stochastic demand rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — PFH of a 1oo1 Channel in Continuous Mode

A 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) system has a single channel. The dangerous event occurs the *first instant* a dangerous failure manifests. Therefore, the average rate of dangerous failure events is the **dangerous-undetected failure rate**:

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU}
$$

**Step 1.** A channel has total failure rate λ, with dangerous fraction λ<sub>D</sub>.
**Step 2.** Diagnostic coverage DC reveals λ<sub>DD</sub> = DC·λ<sub>D</sub>; the remainder is undetected: λ<sub>DU</sub> = (1−DC)·λ<sub>D</sub>.
**Step 3.** Detected failures are either brought to a *safe state* (λ<sub>DD</sub> counted in safe failure fraction) or repaired before they become hazardous, so they do **not** contribute to PFH.
**Step 4.** Only λ<sub>DU</sub> contributes.

$$
\boxed{\text{PFH}_{1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} = (1-DC)\,\lambda_{D}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Maximising DC and minimising λ are the **two engineering levers** for SIL achievement. Doubling DC roughly halves PFH (linear); halving λ roughly halves PFH (linear). Combining both gives multiplicative improvement.

## 3.2 Derivation — PFH of a 1oo2 System

A 1oo2 system requires *both* channels to fail dangerously for a system-level dangerous failure. Consider the *first* dangerous failure to occur (rate 2·λ<sub>DU</sub>). The system survives only if it is **repaired** before the second failure. The mean time the first failed channel is "down" is the channel-equivalent mean downtime **t<sub>CE</sub>**. The probability that the second channel fails in that window is (1−β)·λ<sub>DU</sub>·t<sub>CE</sub> (independent failures only).

**Step 1.** Rate of first failure = 2 λ<sub>DU</sub>.
**Step 2.** Probability of second failure during repair window = (1−β)·λ<sub>DU</sub>·t<sub>CE</sub>.
**Step 3.** Multiply (events per hour × dimensionless probability):

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2}^{(\text{indep})} = 2 \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1-\beta)\,\lambda_{DU}\,t_{CE} = 2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE}
$$

**Step 4.** Add the common-cause term (β-fraction of failures defeats redundancy):

$$
\boxed{\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta)\,\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE} \;+\; \beta\,\lambda_{DU}}
$$

### Worked Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark sub-question)

A 1oo2 sensor channel has λ<sub>DU</sub> = 5 × 10⁻⁷ /h, β = 0.1, t<sub>CE</sub> = 8760 h (1 year). Find PFH and the SIL.

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2(1-0.1)(5\!\times\!10^{-7})^{2}(8760) \;+\; 0.1 \times 5\!\times\!10^{-7}
$$

**Compute the squared term:**

$$
(5\!\times\!10^{-7})^{2} = 25\!\times\!10^{-14} = 2.5\!\times\!10^{-13}
$$

**Compute the independent term:**

$$
2 \times 0.9 \times 2.5\!\times\!10^{-13} \times 8760 = 1.8 \times 2.5\!\times\!10^{-13} \times 8760
$$

$$
= 4.5\!\times\!10^{-13} \times 8760 = 3.942\!\times\!10^{-9} \;/\text{h}
$$

**Compute the CCF term:**

$$
0.1 \times 5\!\times\!10^{-7} = 5\!\times\!10^{-8} \;/\text{h}
$$

**Add them (note: independent term is 2 orders of magnitude smaller):**

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} \approx 3.942\!\times\!10^{-9} + 5\!\times\!10^{-8} = 5.39\!\times\!10^{-8} \;/\text{h}
$$

**SIL mapping:** 10⁻⁸ ≤ 5.39 × 10⁻⁸ < 10⁻⁷ → **SIL 2**.

## 3.3 Derivation — PFH of a 2oo2 System

A 2oo2 system requires *both* channels to operate for the safety function. The loss of **any single** channel causes a system-level failure. Thus:

$$
\boxed{\text{PFH}_{2oo2} = 2\,\lambda_{DU}}
$$

This is **worse than 1oo1** for safety but better for *availability* (no spurious trip on first failure). It is the right choice when the *safe state* itself causes economic loss but the *dangerous state* is also catastrophic — the design tradeoff is well-examined in IEC 61508 §5.2.5.

## 3.4 Derivation — PFH of a 2oo3 System (Sketch)

A 2oo3 system fails dangerously when 2 of 3 channels fail. Combinatorial counting gives 3 ways to lose 2 channels out of 3 (choose 2 of 3 = 3). With pair-wise independence and a CCF term for common-cause double failures:

$$
\text{PFH}_{2oo3} = \underbrace{3\,\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE}}_{\text{one-channel-already-down terms}} + \underbrace{\beta^{2}\,\lambda_{DU}}_{\text{common-cause double failure}} + \underbrace{\beta(1-\beta)\,\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,t_{CE}\,\text{terms}}_{\text{mixed}}
$$

The full IEC 61508-6 formula is:

$$
\boxed{\text{PFH}_{2oo3} = 6(1-\beta)^{2}\lambda_{DU}^{3}t_{CE}^{2} + 3\beta(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^{2}t_{CE} + \beta^{2}\lambda_{DU}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cubic term makes 2oo3 attractive for **SIL 3 / SIL 4** continuous applications where t<sub>CE</sub> is large (e.g., un-attended plants with 8760 h diagnostic intervals).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | 1oo1 | 1oo2 | 2oo2 | 2oo3 | 1oo2D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PFH (SIL potential)** | Moderate (SIL 1–2) | Highest (SIL 3–4) | Low (SIL 1) | High (SIL 3–4) | Highest (SIL 3–4) |
| **Spurious trip rate** | 1·λ<sub>S</sub> | 2·λ<sub>S</sub> (high) | 1·λ<sub>S</sub> (low) | 3·λ<sub>S</sub> (highest) | 2·λ<sub>S</sub> |
| **Tolerance to one fail** | No | Yes (single) | No (both must work) | Yes (single) | Yes (single) |
| **Diagnostic effort** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Cost / complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Best continuous use** | Simple loops | Critical loops | High-availability loops | Aerospace, nuclear | Modern SIS with smart diagnostics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Continuous-Mode Safety Loop — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable\nPressure, Temp, Speed] --> B[Sensor 1]
    A --> C[Sensor 2]
    B --> D[Safety Logic Solver\nPLC / Relay]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Final Element 1\nValve / Breaker]
    D --> F[Final Element 2\nValve / Breaker]
    E --> G[Actuator\nShuts Down EUC]
    F --> G
    D -.feedback.-> H[Diagnostics\nProof Test Interval t]
    H -.DC coverage.-> D
    classDef safe fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef proc fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef diag fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class A,G proc
    class B,C,D,E,F safe
    class H diag
```

**Read this diagram as follows:**
- The **process variable** is sensed **continuously** by redundant sensors.
- The **logic solver** evaluates the safety action **every scan** (typically 10–100 ms in a safety PLC).
- The **final element** is *always energised*; de-energising it is the safe state.
- The **diagnostics block** is not optional — it is the only way to detect dangerous failures before they accumulate.

## 4.2 PFH Decision Tree for Architecture Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Required SIL for continuous mode?] --> Q{Is SIL 1 sufficient?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use 1oo1 with diagnostics]
    Q -- No --> S{Is spurious-trip cost very high?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Use 2oo2 with CCF analysis]
    S -- No --> U{Is SIL 4 or high availability needed?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Use 2oo3 with diverse redundancy]
    U -- No --> W[Use 1oo2 or 1oo2D]
    W --> X[Validate with IEC 61508-6 PFH formulas]
    V --> X
    T --> X
    R --> X
    X --> Y[Verify PFH target met\nDocument in SRS]
    classDef defStyle fill:#e7e6e6,stroke:#000
    classDef goodStyle fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P,Q,R,S,T,U,V,W,X goodStyle
    class Y defStyle
```

## 4.3 Failure-Rate Decomposition — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Total Channel Failure Rate lambda] --> B[Safe lambda_S]
    A --> C[Dangerous lambda_D]
    C --> D[Detected lambda_DD\nDC fraction]
    C --> E[Undetected lambda_DU\n1-DC fraction]
    D --> F[Brought to safe state\nDoes NOT count to PFH]
    E --> G[Counts directly to PFH\nTHIS is the killer]
    H[Common Cause Factor beta] --> I[Defeats redundancy\nin 1oo2, 2oo3, etc.]
    I --> G
    classDef dangerStyle fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef safeStyle fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef neutralStyle fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#000
    class A,H neutralStyle
    class B,D,F,I safeStyle
    class C,E,G dangerStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The only "killer" path is the **λ<sub>DU</sub>** arrow that bypasses diagnostics. Every other failure path either results in a safe shutdown (acceptable) or is repaired quickly. **Continuous-mode SIL design is fundamentally a battle against λ<sub>DU</sub>.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between "Low Demand Mode" and "Continuous Mode" of safety instrumented function operation. State the appropriate target measure of risk reduction for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Low Demand Mode | Continuous Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Safety function operates only on *demand*; otherwise inactive. | Safety function is *permanently active* in the live control loop. |
| Demand frequency | ≤ 1 demand/year | Demand is effectively continuous (no interval without risk) |
| Target measure | **PFD<sub>avg</sub>** (Average Probability of Failure on Demand) | **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) |
| Failure rate role | Less critical (only matters at demand instant) | Critical — every hour is a potential failure event |
| Typical example | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) on a process upset | Aircraft envelope protection; turbomachinery overspeed trip |

**(1 mark each for: definition, target measure, example.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Define the β-factor model for common-cause failures (CCF). Why is it critical for continuous-mode redundant architectures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **β-factor model** (IEC 61508-6 Annex D) assumes that a fraction **β** of channel failures is *common to all channels* in a redundant group (defeats redundancy), while the remaining fraction (1−β) is *statistically independent*.

$$
\lambda_{D,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{D}, \qquad \lambda_{D,\text{indep}} = (1-\beta)\cdot \lambda_{D}
$$

**Criticality for continuous mode (1 mark):** In continuous mode, the safety function runs every hour. Redundancy only protects against *independent* failures. If β = 10 %, then for a 1oo2 system, the CCF term (β·λ<sub>DU</sub>) **dominates** the PFH — often by 2–3 orders of magnitude — and is frequently the limiting factor preventing SIL 3 achievement. **(2 marks for explanation.)**

---

## **Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)**

> KTU 2024 ESE module questions carry **internal choice**. Students answer **one** of two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) and (b), each for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper based on Dec 2023 pattern]**

A turbo-compressor in a hydrocarbon plant is protected by a **2oo3 overspeed trip** in continuous mode. Each speed probe channel has:

- Total failure rate λ = 2 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- Diagnostic coverage DC = 90 %
- Safe failure fraction = 50 % of total failures
- Common-cause β-factor = 5 %
- Channel-equivalent mean downtime t<sub>CE</sub> = 8760 h (1 year)
- Logic solver and final element are certified SIL 3 individually.

**(a)** Compute the **PFH of the 2oo3 sensor subsystem** and determine the achievable SIL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **two engineering measures** to improve the achieved SIL if the requirement is SIL 3 but the calculated PFH is borderline. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute λ<sub>DU</sub> of a single channel. (2 marks)**

Safe failure fraction = 50 % → λ<sub>S</sub> = 0.5 · λ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /h
Dangerous failure rate λ<sub>D</sub> = 0.5 · λ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /h
Diagnostic coverage DC = 90 % → λ<sub>DD</sub> = 0.9 · 1×10⁻⁶ = 9 × 10⁻⁷ /h
λ<sub>DU</sub> = (1 − DC) · λ<sub>D</sub> = 0.1 · 1 × 10⁻⁶ = **1 × 10⁻⁷ /h**

**Step 2: Apply the 2oo3 PFH formula. (3 marks)**

$$
\text{PFH}_{2oo3} = 6(1-\beta)^{2}\lambda_{DU}^{3}t_{CE}^{2} + 3\beta(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^{2}t_{CE} + \beta^{2}\lambda_{DU}
$$

Plug in: β = 0.05, (1−β) = 0.95, λ<sub>DU</sub> = 1×10⁻⁷, t<sub>CE</sub> = 8760.

**Term 1 (cubic):**

$$
6 \times (0.95)^{2} \times (10^{-7})^{3} \times (8760)^{2}
$$

$$
= 6 \times 0.9025 \times 10^{-21} \times 7.677 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= 5.415 \times 10^{-21} \times 7.677 \times 10^{7} = 4.16 \times 10^{-13} \;/h
$$

**Term 2 (quadratic):**

$$
3 \times 0.05 \times 0.95 \times (10^{-7})^{2} \times 8760
$$

$$
= 0.1425 \times 10^{-14} \times 8760 = 1.25 \times 10^{-11} \;/h
$$

**Term 3 (linear CCF):**

$$
(0.05)^{2} \times 10^{-7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-10} \;/h
$$

**Sum:**

$$
\text{PFH}_{2oo3} = 4.16\!\times\!10^{-13} + 1.25\!\times\!10^{-11} + 2.5\!\times\!10^{-10} \approx 2.63 \times 10^{-10} \;/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3: SIL mapping. (2 marks)**

PFH = 2.63 × 10⁻¹⁰ /h falls in the band **10⁻¹⁰ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁹**, which is the **SIL 3 band**.

| Band | PFH range | SIL |
|---|---|---|
| 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵ | SIL 1 | |
| 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶ | SIL 2 | |
| **2.63 × 10⁻¹⁰ (here)** | **SIL 3 ✓** | |
| 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁸ | SIL 4 | |

**Conclusion:** The 2oo3 sensor subsystem achieves **SIL 3**, comfortably meeting the typical turbomachinery overspeed requirement.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Engineering Measure 1: Increase Diagnostic Coverage (DC) from 90 % → 99 %. (3 marks)**

New λ<sub>DU</sub> = (1 − 0.99) · 1 × 10⁻⁶ = **1 × 10⁻⁸ /h** (10× reduction).

**New Term 3 (CCF, dominant):** (0.05)² · 1×10⁻⁸ = **2.5 × 10⁻¹¹ /h**.
**New PFH ≈ 2.5 × 10⁻¹¹ /h** — solidly SIL 3 with margin, or even approaching SIL 4.

This is achieved by adding **on-line self-test pulses** (e.g., reference-speed check every cycle) to the speed probe electronics.

**Engineering Measure 2: Reduce the channel-equivalent downtime t<sub>CE</sub> by shortening the proof-test interval. (2 marks)**

Reducing T from 1 year to 1 month reduces t<sub>CE</sub> by ~12×, which **squares** in the cubic term and **linearly** reduces the quadratic term.

**Engineering Measure 3 (Bonus 2 marks):** Use **diverse redundancy** — different speed-probe technologies (magnetic pickup + optical encoder) — to lower effective β to ~1 %. Eliminates the CCF dominance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute only the **cubic** term of the 2oo3 formula and forget the **CCF (β²·λ<sub>DU</sub>) linear term**, which is often the *largest* contributor when β > 5 %. **Always show all three terms explicitly in the board answer and identify the dominant one.** Marks are awarded for the breakdown, not just the final number.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper based on June 2024 pattern]**

A burner-management system (BMS) operates a burner in **continuous mode** with a **1oo2 flame-failure safety function**. Each flame-scanner channel has:

- Total λ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- DC = 60 %
- Safe-failure fraction = 40 %
- β = 10 %
- t<sub>CE</sub> = 4380 h (6 months)
- Required SIL = 2.

**(a)** Determine whether the 1oo2 architecture meets the **SIL 2** PFH target. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the target is upgraded to **SIL 3**, recommend with justification the **most suitable redundant architecture** (1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, or 1oo2D) and state one design change to achieve it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Single-channel λ<sub>DU</sub>. (1 mark)**

$$
\lambda_{S} = 0.4 \times 5\!\times\!10^{-6} = 2\!\times\!10^{-6} \;/\text{h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{D} = 0.6 \times 5\!\times\!10^{-6} = 3\!\times\!10^{-6} \;/\text{h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DD} = 0.6 \times 3\!\times\!10^{-6} = 1.8\!\times\!10^{-6} \;/\text{h}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU} = 0.4 \times 3\!\times\!10^{-6} = 1.2\!\times\!10^{-6} \;/\text{h}
$$

**Step 2: 1oo2 PFH. (3 marks)**

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^{2}t_{CE} + \beta\,\lambda_{DU}
$$

$$
= 2(0.9)(1.2\!\times\!10^{-6})^{2}(4380) + 0.1 \times 1.2\!\times\!10^{-6}
$$

**Indep term:**

$$
(1.2\!\times\!10^{-6})^{2} = 1.44\!\times\!10^{-12}
$$

$$
2 \times 0.9 \times 1.44\!\times\!10^{-12} \times 4380 = 1.8 \times 1.44\!\times\!10^{-12} \times 4380
$$

$$
= 2.592\!\times\!10^{-12} \times 4380 = 1.135\!\times\!10^{-8} \;/\text{h}
$$

**CCF term:** 0.1 × 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ = **1.2 × 10⁻⁷ /h** (dominates by 1 order of magnitude).

**Sum:**

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2} \approx 1.2\!\times\!10^{-7} + 1.1\!\times\!10^{-8} \approx 1.31 \times 10^{-7} \;/\text{h}
$$

**Step 3: SIL check. (3 marks)**

SIL 2 band: 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶ → 1.31 × 10⁻⁷ falls **inside** this band.

$$
\boxed{\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 1.31 \times 10^{-7} \;/\text{h} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{SIL 2 achieved. ✓}}
$$

However, the margin is thin (factor ~7.6 from the SIL 3 boundary). **CCF dominates** — note how the β = 10 % term is the limiting factor.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Recommended Architecture: 1oo2D (1oo2 with high-coverage diagnostics). (4 marks)**

**Justification:**

1. **PFH math:** A 1oo2D architecture, when the diagnostic converts dangerous-detected failures into a controlled (safe) shutdown, replaces the dangerous-undetected rate with a much smaller one. The IEC 61508-6 simplified form is:

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo2D} \approx 2\,\lambda_{DU,\text{channel}}
$$

But with DC raised to 95 %, the *new* λ<sub>DU</sub> = 0.05 × 3 × 10⁻⁶ = **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ /h**, giving PFH ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁷ /h — still only SIL 2.

2. **Better alternative — 2oo3 with diverse channels:** Reduces β to ~2 % and uses the cubic term. A rough recomputation with β = 0.02, λ<sub>DU</sub> = 1.5 × 10⁻⁷ /h (improved DC), t<sub>CE</sub> = 4380 h:

CCF term: (0.02)² × 1.5 × 10⁻⁷ = **6 × 10⁻¹¹ /h** → firmly SIL 3.

3. **Rationale for *not* choosing 2oo2:** A 2oo2 architecture has PFH = 2·λ<sub>DU</sub> = 2·(1.2×10⁻⁶) = 2.4 × 10⁻⁶ → **SIL 1 only**. The "vote-to-trip" logic of 2oo2 actually *worsens* safety, only improving availability.

**Single Most Effective Design Change (3 marks):**

**Replace single flame-scanner technology with diverse sensors (UV + IR flame scanners).** This drops the effective β from 10 % to ~2 %, which:
- reduces the dominant CCF term by 25×,
- moves the system from the borderline SIL 2 into SIL 3 with ~3 decades of margin,
- is explicitly recommended by IEC 61508-2 §7.4.4 for CCF control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Question B specific):** A common error is recommending 2oo2 because the student confuses *availability* (where 2oo2 is best) with *safety* (where 1oo2/2oo3 is best). Always state the metric you are optimising **first** before choosing architecture. The board examiner will deduct 2 marks if the metric is not declared.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Mode — Final Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam.)**

- **Definition (1 line):** Safety function permanently active; failure → immediate hazard → metric is **PFH (per hour)**, not PFD<sub>avg</sub>.
- **SIL → PFH mapping:** SIL 1 = 10⁻⁶, SIL 2 = 10⁻⁷, SIL 3 = 10⁻⁸, SIL 4 = 10⁻⁹ (per hour, upper bound of each band).
- **Failure rate decomposition:** λ = λ<sub>S</sub> + λ<sub>DD</sub> + λ<sub>DU</sub>; only λ<sub>DU</sub> contributes to PFH.
- **DC equation:** λ<sub>DU</sub> = (1 − DC)·λ<sub>D</sub>. **DC is the single most powerful design lever.**
- **β-factor model:** β = 10 % is the *default* per IEC 61508-6; β = 2 % requires *proven diversity*.
- **PFH formulas to memorise (in order of exam frequency):**
    1. 1oo1 → λ<sub>DU</sub>
    2. 1oo2 → 2(1−β)λ<sub>DU</sub>²·t<sub>CE</sub> + β·λ<sub>DU</sub>
    3. 2oo2 → 2·λ<sub>DU</sub> (worse for safety!)
    4. 2oo3 → 6(1−β)²λ<sub>DU</sub>³·t<sub>CE</sub>² + 3β(1−β)λ<sub>DU</sub>²·t<sub>CE</sub> + β²·λ<sub>DU</sub>
