- **Step 5 — Consequence Modelling:** Estimate harm (deaths, injury, asset loss) for each outcome.
- **Step 6 — Risk Aggregation:** Compute $IR$ and plot $F$–$N$ curves.
- **Step 7 — ALARP / Tolerability Check:** Compare computed risk against the regulator's acceptance criteria.

> **The 'Why' Behind the Logic:**
> The fundamental philosophy is **Risk = Frequency × Consequence**. We cannot reduce either independently forever; the question is whether their product lies below tolerable limits.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for QRA Exercises)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = f_i \times C$ | Risk = frequency × consequence | fatalities/yr |
| 2 | $PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ (1oo1) | Average PFD for single-channel system | dimensionless |
| 3 | $PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof})^2}{3}$ | PFD for 1-out-of-2 voted system | dimensionless |
| 4 | $PFD_{2oo3} = 3 \cdot (1-\lambda_D T_p)^2 (\lambda_D T_p)^2$ | PFD for 2-out-of-3 voted system | dimensionless |
| 5 | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{top} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$ | Top event probability for OR-gate (independent paths) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{top} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Top event probability for AND-gate (independent events) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f_{outcome} = f_{init} \times P_{IPL1} \times P_{IPL2} \times \dots$ | Outcome frequency (Event Tree) | events/yr |
| 9 | $IR = \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{death \vert j}$ | Individual Risk per year | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $f_{final} = f_{init} \times PFD_{SIS}$ | Frequency after SIS layer | events/yr |
| 11 | $f_{CET} = \lambda \cdot t$ | Conditional Event Tree base (for time-based components) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (for exponential dist.) | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown table cells.** This breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead (e.g., write $p_{death \vert j}$ as shown above).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

QRA exercises are not academic — they directly feed into:
- **IEC 61511 SIL determination** for the process industry (oil & gas, petrochemical, refining).
- **ISO 26262 ASIL determination** in automotive functional safety.
- **Comah / Seveso compliance** for major hazard installations in the EU.
- **OSHA 1910.119 PSM** in the United States.
- **Indian Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO)** for storage of petroleum class installations.

## 2.4 The "Independent Protection Layer (IPL)" Concept — A Closer Look

> [!NOTE]
> An **IPL** is any device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, **independent of the initiating event and any other IPL.** Every IPL has three mandatory properties:
> 1. **Independence** — does not share a common cause failure with other layers.
> 2. **Effectiveness** — quantified by a Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-5}$.
> 3. **Auditability** — performance can be verified during plant life.

The standard IPL stack (from bottom to top, **LOPA - Layer of Protection Analysis**):
- **Layer 1:** Basic Process Control System (BPCS) — PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$
- **Layer 2:** Operator intervention (alarms) — PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$
- **Layer 3:** Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ (depends on SIL)
- **Layer 4:** Mechanical relief (PSV, rupture disc) — PFD $\approx 10^{-2}$
- **Layer 5:** Physical protection (bunds, dikes) — PFD $\approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$
- **Layer 6:** Community emergency response — PFD $\approx 10^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Event Tree Risk Computation (Full Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A refinery compressor has an estimated leak frequency of $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ per year for a major hydrocarbon release. The following independent protection layers (IPLs) are present, in this order:
> 1. **BPCS (flow shutdown):** PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$
> 2. **Operator intervention (alarm):** PFD = $5 \times 10^{-2}$
> 3. **SIS (High-High pressure trip, SIL 2):** PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
> 4. **Bund / dike (mechanical IPL):** PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$
>
> If all IPLs fail, a **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is expected, with an estimated $N_{fatalities} = 3$ persons on site.
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) The frequency of the VCE outcome.
> (b) The Individual Risk (IR) per year for an operator working 2000 hrs/yr on the compressor deck.
> (c) Comment on whether the IR is tolerable (tolerable IR $\le 10^{-5}$/yr per KTU referenced standard).

### Step 3.1.1 — Drawing the Event Tree Logic

Each IPL either **succeeds** (stops escalation) or **fails** (allows escalation). Since they are independent:

$$
P(\text{all fail}) = PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_4
$$

### Step 3.1.2 — Numerical Evaluation of Combined PFD

$$
P_{combined} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
$$

Group terms in pairs to keep precision:

$$
P_{combined} = (1 \times 5) \times (1 \times 1) \times 10^{-1-2-2-2}
$$

$$
P_{combined} = 5 \times 10^{-7}
$$

### Step 3.1.3 — Compute VCE Frequency

$$
f_{VCE} = f_1 \times P_{combined}
$$

$$
f_{VCE} = (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (5 \times 10^{-7})
$$

$$
f_{VCE} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per year}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A VCE is expected roughly once in **200 million years** at this facility. This is an extremely low frequency.

### Step 3.1.4 — Compute Individual Risk (IR)

The operator's exposure fraction:

$$
E_{frac} = \frac{2000 \text{ hrs/yr}}{8760 \text{ hrs/yr}} = 0.2283
$$

Probability of being in the affected zone during a VCE (conservative = 1, since they are in the compressor deck):

$$
p_{death \vert VCE} = 1.0
$$

The IR for this scenario:

$$
IR = f_{VCE} \times p_{death \vert VCE} \times E_{frac}
$$

$$
IR = (5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 1.0 \times 0.2283
$$

$$
IR = 1.14 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per year}
$$

### Step 3.1.5 — Tolerability Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The computed $IR = 1.14 \times 10^{-9}$ /yr is **far below** the typical tolerable individual risk criterion of $10^{-5}$ /yr.
> **Conclusion:** Risk is **ACCEPTABLE / Broadly Acceptable** (well into the ALARP green zone).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fault Tree Top-Event Probability

> **Problem Statement:**
> A simple Fault Tree has two parallel OR paths leading to a TOP event:
> - **Path A** = AND gate of events $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_3$ with $P_{A1} = 0.01$, $P_{A2} = 0.02$, $P_{A3} = 0.01$.
> - **Path B** = AND gate of events $B_1$, $B_2$ with $P_{B1} = 0.05$, $P_{B2} = 0.04$.
> - The TOP gate is an OR combining Path A and Path B.
>
> Compute $P_{TOP}$.

### Step 3.2.1 — Compute Probability of Each Path

For AND gates (independent events), probabilities multiply:

$$
P_A = P_{A1} \times P_{A2} \times P_{A3}
$$

$$
P_A = 0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.01 = 2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
P_B = P_{B1} \times P_{B2} = 0.05 \times 0.04 = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 3.2.2 — Apply the OR-gate Probability Formula (Exact)

For independent paths in OR, the exact non-trivial formula is:

$$
P_{TOP} = 1 - (1 - P_A)(1 - P_B)
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
P_{TOP} = 1 - (1 - 2 \times 10^{-6})(1 - 2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
P_{TOP} = 1 - (0.999998)(0.998)
$$

$$
P_{TOP} = 1 - 0.997998004 = 2.001996 \times 10^{-3}
$$

> **Engineering Approximation Note:** Since $P_A \ll P_B$, the dominant contribution comes from Path B. A common shortcut (valid for small probabilities) is the rare-event approximation: $P_{TOP} \approx P_A + P_B$, giving $2.002 \times 10^{-3}$ — essentially identical.

$$
\boxed{P_{TOP} \approx 2.002 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SIL Verification (1oo2 Architecture)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr and a proof test interval $T_p = 8760$ hrs (1 year). A SIL-2 shutdown is required.
> **Compute** the average PFD for a 1oo1 architecture and a 1oo2 architecture, and identify which SIL band they fall into.

### Step 3.3.1 — 1oo1 PFD Formula Derivation

For an unrevealed dangerous failure with proof testing, assuming failures are detected only at proof test, the average unavailability is the area under the triangular failure probability over one test interval, divided by $T_p$:

$$
PFD_{1oo1}(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T_p
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_0^{T_p} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_p^2}{2 T_p} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_p}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 3.3.2 — 1oo2 PFD Formula (voted — fails only if both fail)

For a 1oo2 voting logic, the system fails if **both** channels fail. Assuming both are identical and independent:

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = (\lambda_D \cdot t)^2
$$

Averaging over the interval:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_0^{T_p} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_p^3}{3 T_p} = \frac{(\lambda_D T_p)^2}{3}
$$

Substituting:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{[(5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760]^2}{3}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{3}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = 6.39 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Step 3.3.3 — Map to SIL Bands (IEC 61511)

| SIL | Target $PFD_{avg}$ (low demand mode) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

> **Result:**
> - 1oo1: $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ falls in **SIL 2** band.
> - 1oo2: $6.39 \times 10^{-6}$ falls in **SIL 3** band.
>
> **Conclusion:** 1oo2 redundancy is required if SIL 3 is the target.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (All Worked Examples)

| Example | $f_{init}$ | $\sum PFD_{IPL}$ | $f_{outcome}$ | $IR$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Compressor VCE | $1 \times 10^{-2}$/yr | $5 \times 10^{-7}$ | $5 \times 10^{-9}$/yr | $1.14 \times 10^{-9}$/yr | Acceptable |
| 2. Fault Tree OR | — | $P_A = 2\times10^{-6}$, $P_B = 2\times10^{-3}$ | $P_{TOP} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | — | Path B dominant |
| 3. SIL 1oo1 vs 1oo2 | $\lambda = 5\times 10^{-7}$/hr | $T_p = 8760$ hr | $2.19\times10^{-3}$ vs $6.39\times10^{-6}$ | — | 1oo1 = SIL 2, 1oo2 = SIL 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Standard QRA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Hazard Identification] --> nodeB[Frequency Assignment f_i per year]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Event Tree Branching with IPL stack]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Each IPL succeeds or fails?}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute branch PFDs multiply]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Outcome Frequency f_out]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Consequence Estimation deaths injuries]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Risk Metric: IR = f_out x p_death]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Compare to Tolerability Criteria}
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Tolerable: ALARP demonstration]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Not Tolerable: Add IPL or Reduce frequency]
    nodeK --> nodeB
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Final QRA Report]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The QRA workflow is **iterative**. If the computed risk is not tolerable, engineers add more IPLs (or strengthen the SIS) and re-evaluate — this is the heart of the ALARP cycle.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Independent Protection Layer Stack (LOPA Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1[Layer 1 BPCS PFD 10^-1] --> layer2[Layer 2 Operator PFD 10^-1]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3 SIS PFD 10^-2 to 10^-5]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4 Mechanical Relief PFD 10^-2]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5 Physical Bund PFD 10^-2]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6 Community Response PFD 10^-1]
    layer6 --> outcome[Final Consequence: VCE, Fire, Toxic Release]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Fault Tree Reduction (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEvt[TOP EVENT: Loss of Containment] --> orGate1{OR Gate}
    orGate1 --> andPathA[AND Gate Path A]
    orGate1 --> andPathB[AND Gate Path B]
    andPathA --> a1[P_A1]
    andPathA --> a2[P_A2]
    andPathA --> a3[P_A3]
    andPathB --> b1[P_B1]
    andPathB --> b2[P_B2]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Risk Tolerability Framework (F-N Curve Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zoneA[UNACCEPTABLE Cannot operate] --> zoneB[ALARP region: Reduce risk if reasonably practicable]
    zoneB --> zoneC[BROADLY ACCEPTABLE: No further action needed]
    zoneA -.Rejected.- zoneB
    zoneB -.Mitigation or cost-benefit.- zoneC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **F-N curve** plots cumulative frequency $F$ (events/yr with $N$ or more fatalities) against $N$ (number of fatalities). The slope of the boundary line reflects society's aversion to large-scale accidents.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA) and list any four numerical outputs typically produced by a QRA study.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA) is the systematic numerical estimation of risk in industrial installations by combining failure frequency data with consequence modelling to produce objective risk metrics for decision-making.
>
> **Four typical numerical outputs:**
> 1. **Individual Risk (IR)** per year for an exposed person.
> 2. **Societal Risk (F-N curve).**
> 3. **Expected Loss of Life (ELL) per year.**
> 4. **Hazard Rate / Frequency** of a specific accident outcome (e.g., VCE per year).

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is an Independent Protection Layer (IPL)? State the three properties that any device/system must satisfy to qualify as an IPL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An IPL is a device, system, or human action that prevents or mitigates the escalation of an unwanted event to a more severe consequence.
>
> **Three mandatory properties:**
> 1. **Independence** — it does not share a common cause failure with any other IPL or the initiating event.
> 2. **Effectiveness** — its performance can be quantified by a Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD).
> 3. **Auditability** — its performance can be verified and maintained throughout the plant life.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> A chemical reactor operates at 25 bar pressure. A **runaway reaction** has an estimated initiating frequency of $f_i = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. Three independent protection layers are installed in series:
>
> 1. **Basic Process Control System** (reactor temperature control loop) with $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
> 2. **Operator intervention** based on high-temperature alarm with $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
> 3. **Safety Instrumented System (SIL-1)** pressure relief interlock with $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
>
> If all IPLs fail, the consequence is a **thermal runaway explosion** with an expected fatality count of $C = 4$ persons.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **frequency of the thermal runaway explosion** per year. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the **Individual Risk (IR)** per year, assuming continuous on-site presence, and comment on its **tolerability** against a $10^{-5}$/yr criterion. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(a) — Frequency of Runaway

The probability of all three IPLs failing simultaneously (assuming independence):

$$
P_{all\,fail} = PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3
$$

$$
P_{all\,fail} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
P_{all\,fail} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

The frequency of the runaway event:

$$
f_{runaway} = f_i \times P_{all\,fail}
$$

$$
f_{runaway} = (4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{runaway} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}}
$$

#### Solution A(b) — Individual Risk

For continuous presence and probability of death given runaway = 1 (worst-case exposure):

$$
IR = f_{runaway} \times p_{death} \times E_{frac}
$$

With $E_{frac} = 1$ (continuous presence):

$$
IR = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 1 = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}
$$

**Tolerability comparison:**

$$
IR = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ll 10^{-5} \text{ per year (criterion)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Risk is **BROADLY ACCEPTABLE** — no further risk reduction is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> A **pressure transmitter** in a safety shutdown system has the following reliability data:
> - Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
> - Proof test interval: $T_p = 4380$ hours (6 months)
> - The transmitter is part of a **1oo1** architecture.
>
> **(a)** Derive the formula for the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg)** for a 1oo1 architecture, and compute its numerical value. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** If a **1oo2 redundant** architecture is used instead (same $\lambda_D$, same $T_p$), derive the new PFD_avg formula and compute the value. Identify the **SIL band** achieved in each case using the IEC 61511 target table. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution B(a) — Derivation of 1oo1 PFD

For a single channel with dangerous failures, the probability of being in failed state at time $t$ between proof tests is approximately $\lambda_D t$. The average over the test interval $T_p$:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_0^{T_p} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt
$$

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_p} \left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^{T_p} = \frac{\lambda_D T_p}{2}
$$

**Numerical value:**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7}) \times 4380}{2} = \frac{8.76 \times 10^{-4}}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg,1oo1} = 4.38 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

#### Solution B(b) — 1oo2 Derivation and SIL Mapping

For 1oo2, system fails only when **both** channels have failed. The instantaneous PFD squared:

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2
$$

Averaging:

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_p} \int_0^{T_p} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt = \frac{\lambda_D^2 T_p^2}{3}
$$

**Numerical value:**

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{[(2 \times 10^{-7}) \times 4380]^2}{3}
$$

$$
PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(8.76 \times 10^{-4})^2}{3} = \frac{7.674 \times 10^{-7}}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg,1oo2} = 2.56 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

**SIL Mapping (IEC 61511 Low-Demand Mode):**

| Architecture | $PFD_{avg}$ | SIL Band |
|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $4.38 \times 10^{-4}$ | **SIL 3** ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$) |
| 1oo2 | $2.56 \times 10^{-7}$ | **SIL 3** (just below $10^{-7}$, marginal) |

> **Conclusion:** 1oo1 already meets SIL 3 with this failure rate. 1oo2 dramatically improves the margin, providing protection against common cause failures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **multiply** the IPL PFDs in an Event Tree — students sometimes add them. Mark deduction: **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Using the **rare-event approximation** $P_{OR} = P_A + P_B$ without stating that it is valid only for small probabilities. Examiner may deduct **−1 mark** if not justified.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** For the PFD formula derivation, failing to show the **integration step** explicitly. Examiner expects to see $\int_0^{T_p} \lambda_D t \, dt$ on the answer sheet. Mark deduction: **−2 marks** if skipped.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying **units** (per year vs per hour) in frequency. Always convert carefully: $1$ yr $= 8760$ hrs.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting the **exposure fraction $E_{frac}$** when computing Individual Risk for part-time workers. Examiner may deduct **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Risk = Frequency × Consequence** is the foundational equation. Memorize it.
- [ ] **Event Tree multiplies PFDs**; **Fault Tree AND-gate multiplies PFDs**, **OR-gate uses $1 - \prod(1-P_i)$**.
- [ ] **PFD_avg for 1oo1** = $\lambda_D T_p / 2$ (proof test interval in hours).
- [ ] **PFD_avg for 1oo2** = $(\lambda_D T_p)^2 / 3$ — derive on paper before every exam.
- [ ] **SIL bands** (low-demand): SIL 1 = $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$; SIL 4 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$.
- [ ] **Individual Risk** = $f \times p_{death} \times E_{frac}$; tolerable criterion typically $10^{-5}$ /yr.
- [ ] **F-N curve** plots cumulative frequency vs. number of fatalities — distinguishes individual vs societal risk.
- [ ] **IPL rule of three** — Independence, Effectiveness, Auditability.
- [ ] **Common Cause Factor ($\beta$)** is not modelled in this exercise set, but remember to **mention it** if asked in viva.
- [ ] **Exposure fraction** = hours-on-site / 8760 (year total).
- [ ] Always **show integration steps** for PFD derivations in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Check units** of $\lambda_D$ (per hour is the standard IEC 61511 unit).
- [ ] **Rare-event approximation** $P_A + P_B$ is valid only if both $P_A, P_B \ll 1$.
- [ ] **LOPA** = Layer of Protection Analysis — the practical QRA tool for SIL selection.
- [ ] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the regulatory decision principle.
- [ ] **MTTF** = $1/\lambda$ for exponential failure distribution (exams test this).
- [ ] Final tip: **Practice 5–6 worked problems** on Event Tree + Fault Tree + SIL verification before the KTU ESE — the marks in this topic are nearly free if your formulas are memorized.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIS Safety Requirement Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SIS Safety Requirement Specifications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** is the formal, documented specification document that precisely defines all the functional and integrity requirements of a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**. It is a critical deliverable in the **Safety Lifecycle** as defined by **IEC 61511 (Process Industry)** and **IEC 61508 (Functional Safety of E/E/PE Systems)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **IEC 61511-1, Clause 10.3**, the SRS shall specify the required **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** in terms of: > - **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — for each SIF > - **Process Safety Time**, **Demand Rate**, and **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** > - **Safe State** definition and process variable set points > - **Proof Test Interval**, **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**, and **Response Time** The SRS serves as the **single source of truth** between the process design, instrumentation, and operations teams, and acts as the binding contract for SIS designers, integrators, and validators. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Think of the SRS like the **architect's blueprint and legal contract** combined, when building a fire-protection system for a skyscraper: | Real-World Analogy | SIS Equivalent | |---|---| | The fire code that says "sprinklers must activate in 10 seconds" | **Process Safety Time** requirement | | The insurance company demanding 99.99% reliability | **SIL Target (e.g., SIL 3)** | | The building plans showing sprinkler locations | **Logic Solver I/O List & Field Device Mapping** | | The annual fire-drill inspection | **Proof Test Interval** | | The legal document specifying penalties for non-compliance | **Auditability & Documentation Requirement** | In the same way, the SRS does **not** say *how* to build the SIS (that's the design phase). It strictly says **what** the SIS **must do**, **when** it must do it, and **how reliably** it must do it. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Favourite Question):** > - **BPCS (Basic Process Control System)** → Maintains *normal* operation (e.g., temperature control loop). > - **SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** → Takes *emergency* action when BPCS fails or process exceeds safe limits. > The SRS defines **only the SIS** — never the BPCS. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following metrics are universally referenced in any SRS document and must be quoted using exact IEC/ISA values: - **SIL 1:** Average Probability of Failure on Demand (**PFDavg**) = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ ; **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** = **10 to 100** - **SIL 2:** PFDavg = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ ; RRF = **100 to 1,000** - **SIL 3:** PFDavg = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ ; RRF = **1,000 to 10,000** - **SIL 4:** PFDavg = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ ; RRF = **10,000 to 100,000** - **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** for SIL 2/3 subsystems = **1 (redundant)** - **Operating Mode**: SIS in process industry is predominantly **Low-Demand Mode** (demand rate $\le 1$ per year) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of the SRS The SRS is logically decomposed into **four fundamental pillars**. Every KTU question on this topic is mapped to one of these pillars: ### Pillar A — Functional Requirements (the "WHAT") - Description of each **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** with a unique tag (e.g., SIF-101, SIF-102). - Identification of **cause(s)** and **consequence(s)**. - Definition of the **Safe State** (e.g., "Close ESD valve XV-101 to isolate reactor feed"). - Specification of **process variables**, their **trip set points**, and **reset logic** (auto-reset vs. manual reset). ### Pillar B — Integrity Requirements (the "HOW RELIABLE") - Target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** for each SIF. - Calculation of **PFDavg** and **RRF**. - Specification of **HFT (Hardware Fault Tolerance)** per route. - **Architectural Constraints** (Type A vs Type B sensors, logic solver, final elements). ### Pillar C — Operational Requirements (the "WHEN") - **Demand Rate** (expected frequency of hazardous events). - **Process Safety Time** (max time before unsafe condition causes harm). - **Response Time** of the SIF (typically < Process Safety Time / 2). - **Proof Test Interval (T_proof)** — typically 1 to 5 years. ### Pillar D — Environmental & Interface Requirements - **Environmental conditions**: temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC, hazardous area classification (ATEX/IECEx). - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** requirements: alarms, bypass keys, overrides. - **Modes of Operation**: maintenance bypass, partial-stroke testing, override procedures. - **Commissioning and Validation** requirements (per IEC 61511-1 Clause 15). ## 2.2 Why Each Pillar Matters — The "Why & How" | Pillar | Why It Exists | How It Is Used | |---|---|---| | Functional | Prevents ambiguity during FAT/SAT | Used by designers to build logic diagrams | | Integrity | Quantifies risk reduction | Used to select redundant architecture | | Operational | Ensures SIF responds in time | Used to tune proof-test schedules | | Environmental | Ensures devices survive field conditions | Used for vendor selection & installation | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every formula you may need to solve numerical problems on SIS specifications. Memorize this for the ESE exam. | # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundaries | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg) | $\overline{PFD}_{SIF} = \overline{PFD}_{S} + \overline{PFD}_{L} + \overline{PFD}_{FE}$ | Dimensionless; summed across sensor, logic, final element | | 2 | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Range: 10 (SIL 1) to 100,000 (SIL 4) | | 3 | SIL Determination (from PFDavg) | $SIL \leftrightarrow \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}\right)$ | Maps PFDavg band to discrete SIL 1–4 | | 4 | SIL from Hazard Frequency | $RRF_{required} = \dfrac{f_{hazard}}{f_{tolerable}}$ | Demand-rate approach (LOPA) | | 5 | Hardware Fault Tolerance (IEC 61508) | $HFT = N - (N-1)$ ; where $N$ = redundancy | HFT 0 = single, 1 = 1oo2/2oo3 | | 6 | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD}}$ | Fraction; typical $\ge 60\%$ for SIL 1 | | 7 | Response Time Constraint | $t_{response} \le \dfrac{t_{process\_safety}}{2}$ | Seconds; safety margin of 50% | | 8 | Proof Test Effectiveness | $PFD_{avg} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ (1oo1 arch.) | $T_{proof}$ in years | | 9 | Architectural Constraint (Route 1H) | $SFF \ge 60\%$ (Type B, SIL 1, HFT 0) | Tabulated per IEC 61508-2 Table 2 | | 10 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for sensor | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{D}}$ | Years; inverse of dangerous failure rate | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trick:** Notice the difference between $\lambda_{DD}$ (Dangerous Detected) and $\lambda_{DU}$ (Dangerous Undetected). Only $\lambda_{DU}$ contributes to PFDavg. This is a guaranteed 2-mark KTU question. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The SRS is the most **audited and legally significant** document in any functional safety project. Regulators (e.g., OSHA in the US, HSE in the UK, PESO/CCOE in India) routinely request the SRS to verify that the SIS is fit-for-purpose. In **petrochemical refineries**, the SRS dictates the design of **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** systems, **Fire & Gas (F&G)** systems, and **High-Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS)**. In **nuclear power**, it aligns with **IEC 61513**; in **railway signaling**, with **EN 50128/50129**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the SIL Determination from Risk Graph / LOPA The most common analytical procedure in any KTU exam question is the **SIL determination via the Risk Graph (per IEC 61511-1, Clause 9.4)**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation used by safety engineers in industry. ### Step 1 — Establish the Tolerable Hazard Frequency ($f_{tolerable}$) $$f_{tolerable} = \dfrac{1}{T_{tolerable}}$$ where $T_{tolerable}$ is the tolerable return period (e.g., $10^{-5}$ per year = once in 100,000 years). ### Step 2 — Determine the Unmitigated Hazard Frequency ($f_{init}$) $$f_{init} = f_{init,BPCS} \cdot f_{independent\_layer}$$ This is the frequency of the hazardous event assuming the BPCS performs normally but no SIS is present. ### Step 3 — Identify the Required Risk Reduction Factor ($RRF_{required}$) $$RRF_{required} = \dfrac{f_{init}}{f_{tolerable}}$$ **Reasoning:** The SIS must reduce the hazard frequency *from* $f_{init}$ *down to* $f_{tolerable}$. ### Step 4 — Convert RRF to PFDavg $$PFD_{required} = \dfrac{1}{RRF_{required}}$$ ### Step 5 — Map PFD to SIL using IEC 61511 Table $$SIL = f(PFD_{required}) = \begin{cases} SIL\ 1 & 10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1} \\ SIL\ 2 & 10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2} \\ SIL\ 3 & 10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3} \\ SIL\ 4 & 10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4} \end{cases}$$ ## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A reactor cooling-water pump failure causes a runaway reaction. Assume: - Unmitigated hazard frequency $f_{init} = 10^{-2}$ per year - Tolerable hazard frequency $f_{tolerable} = 10^{-5}$ per year - Calculate the required SIL of the cooling-water SIF. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Identify the two key frequencies. $$ f_{init} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{per year} $$ $$ f_{tolerable} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{per year} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). $$ \begin{aligned} RRF_{required} &= \dfrac{f_{init}}{f_{tolerable}} \\ &= \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-2}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{\,(-2) - (-5)} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{\,3} \\ &= 1{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the required PFDavg of the SIF. $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{required} &= \dfrac{1}{RRF_{required}} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{1{,}000} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Map PFDavg to SIL. $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{required} &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \\ \text{Compare with bands:} \quad 10^{-4} &\le PFD < 10^{-3} \;\Rightarrow\; SIL\ 3 \\ \text{Thus} \quad SIL_{target} &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A PFD of exactly $10^{-3}$ falls at the *boundary* between SIL 2 and SIL 3. By convention in IEC 61511, **the more conservative (higher) SIL is chosen**, i.e., **SIL 3**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SRS Specification Traceability The following matrix maps every SRS element to its source standard clause and verification method. This is precisely what examiners look for in 14-mark answers. | SRS Element | Source Standard | Verification Method | |---|---|---| | Definition of Safe State | IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.3.1 | HAZOP & LOPA Study | | Process Safety Time | IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.3.5 | Dynamic Process Simulation | | Target SIL per SIF | IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.3.3 | LOPA / Risk Graph Workshop | | Proof Test Interval | IEC 61511-1 Clause 16 | Maintenance Schedule Audit | | Bypass & Override Limits | IEC 61511-1 Clause 11.5 | Operations Procedures Audit | | HFT & SFF Requirements | IEC 61508-2 Tables 2/3 | Vendor Datasheet Cross-Check | | Response Time | IEC 61511-1 Clause 11.4 | Bench Testing & SIL Verification | | Environmental Specs | IEC 61511-1 Clause 11.5.3 | Equipment Datasheet Review | ## 3.4 Derivation of the 1oo2 Architecture PFD (Common Exam Variant) For a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two)** sensor voting group: $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{1oo2} &= 2 \cdot (1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} \\ &\approx 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot T_{proof}^{\,2} \end{aligned} $$ For a **2oo3** voting group: $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{2oo3} &= 6 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{\,3} \cdot T_{proof}^{\,3} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 1oo2 is preferred over 1oo1:** A single dangerous failure in 1oo1 trips the SIF. In 1oo2, the *other* healthy channel can override the spurious trip — significantly reducing both spurious trip rate *and* PFD. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SRS Lifecycle Position in the Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Process Design
and Hazard Analysis] --> B[Phase 2: Allocation of
Safety Functions to Layers] B --> C[Phase 3: SIS Design
and Engineering] C --> D[Phase 4: SIS Installation
and Commissioning] D --> E[Phase 5: SIS Operation,
Maintenance, and Modification] E --> F[Phase 6: Decommissioning] A --> SIF1[Identify SIFs] SIF1 --> SRS[Generate Safety
Requirement Specification] SRS --> LOPA[Determine SIL
via LOPA / Risk Graph] LOPA --> ARCH[Select Architecture
1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3] ARCH --> VAL[SIL Verification
Calculation] VAL --> DOC[SRS Documentation
and Approval] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SRS Document ```mermaid flowchart LR DOC[SRS Master Document
Document Number and Revision] DOC --> SEC1[Section 1
Functional Requirements] DOC --> SEC2[Section 2
Integrity Requirements] DOC --> SEC3[Section 3
Operational Requirements] DOC --> SEC4[Section 4
Environmental and Interface] SEC1 --> SIF1[SIF-101 Reactor ESD] SEC1 --> SIF2[SIF-102 Overpressure Protection] SEC1 --> SIF3[SIF-103 Fire and Gas Detection] SEC2 --> SIL1[SIL Target: 1 to 4] SEC2 --> PFD1[PFDavg Calculation] SEC2 --> HFT1[Hardware Fault Tolerance] SEC3 --> PST1[Process Safety Time] SEC3 --> RT1[Response Time] SEC3 --> PTI1[Proof Test Interval] SEC4 --> ENV1[ATEX Zone 1 Area Classification] SEC4 --> HMI1[Operator HMI Bypass Keys] SEC4 --> MODE1[Maintenance Bypass Mode] ``` ## 4.3 PFDavg Distribution Across SIS Subsystems ```mermaid pie title Typical PFDavg Distribution in a 1oo2 Sensor / 1oo1 Logic / 1oo2 Valve Architecture "Field Sensors (1oo2)" : 40 "Logic Solver (1oo1)" : 10 "Final Elements (1oo2 valves)" : 50 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Notice that **final elements (valves) typically contribute ~50% of the total PFD**. This is why the SRS must always specify valve diagnostic coverage and partial-stroke testing — a very common 7-mark exam question. ## 4.4 Information Flow Inside an SRS-Driven SIS Project ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT[SRS Input Sources
HAZOP, LOPA, PHA, P&ID] --> PROC[SRS Compilation
by Safety Engineer] PROC --> REVIEW[Peer Review
by Independent Assessor] REVIEW --> APPROVE[Management Approval
and Sign-Off] APPROVE --> DESIGN[SIS Detailed Design
Logic Diagrams, Cause-Effect] DESIGN --> FAT[Factory Acceptance Test] FAT --> SAT[Site Acceptance Test] SAT --> OPER[Plant Operation
with Proof Testing] OPER --> AUDIT[Periodic Functional
Safety Audit] AUDIT --> REVISE[SRS Revision
if Required] REVISE --> PROC ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Define the Safety Requirement Specification (SRS). List any four elements that must be included in an SRS document as per IEC 61511.** **Model Answer:** The **Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** is the formal specification document that defines all functional and integrity requirements for each **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** within a Safety Instrumented System, in accordance with **IEC 61511-1, Clause 10.3**. Four essential elements: 1. **Functional Requirements** — Description of each SIF, its cause(s), consequence(s), and safe-state definition. 2. **Integrity Requirements** — Target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**, **PFDavg**, and **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**. 3. **Process Safety Time and Response Time** — Maximum permissible time before unsafe consequences occur. 4. **Proof Test Interval** — Frequency at which hidden failures must be revealed by inspection/testing. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list "sensor model" or "valve manufacturer" — these are **design** details, not SRS details. The SRS specifies *what* and *how reliably*, not *which vendor*. You lose 1 mark if you confuse this. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between the BPCS and the SIS. Why is the SRS developed only for the SIS?** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | BPCS | SIS | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Maintain *normal* operation | Take *emergency* action on unsafe condition | | Mode | Continuous control | Discrete, dormant until demanded | | Failure philosophy | Fail-as-is (control continues) | Fail-safe (reach safe state) | | Standard | ISA-88, IEC 61131 | **IEC 61511 / IEC 61508** | | Integrity target | No formal SIL | Target **SIL 1–4** | **Why SRS only for SIS:** The BPCS is a *control* system operating under normal conditions, while the SIS is a *protection* system that must meet a quantifiable risk-reduction target. Since the SIS independently reduces risk, its requirements must be **formally specified, documented, and auditable** — which is exactly the role of the SRS. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **contents of a Safety Requirement Specification** as per IEC 61511. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A reactor over-pressure hazard has an unmitigated frequency of $5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. The tolerable frequency is $10^{-5}$ per year. Determine the **target SIL** and required **RRF** for the over-pressure SIF. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution #### (a) Contents of SRS The SRS document, per **IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.3**, contains the following sections: 1. **Functional Requirements** - Description of each SIF (unique tag, cause, consequence). - **Safe State** definition (e.g., close block valve, stop pump). - **Process variables** and their **trip set points**. - **Reset logic** — automatic or manual. - **Bypass/override** allowances and limits. 2. **Integrity Requirements** - Target **SIL** per SIF (1, 2, 3, or 4). - Required **PFDavg** per SIF. - **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** for sensor, logic solver, and final element. - **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** requirements. - **Diagnostic coverage** requirements. 3. **Operational Requirements** - **Process Safety Time** (PST). - **SIF Response Time** requirement ($t_{response} \le PST / 2$). - **Demand Rate** (assumed demand on the SIF per year). - **Proof Test Interval** ($T_{proof}$). 4. **Environmental & Interface Requirements** - Ambient conditions (temperature, humidity, corrosion). - **Hazardous area classification** (ATEX/IECEx zone). - **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** and **alarm philosophy**. - **Bypass philosophy** during maintenance. 5. **Validation & Verification Requirements** - **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** criteria. - **SIL Verification** methodology. - **Documentation traceability**. #### (b) SIL Determination **Given:** $$ f_{init} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{per year}, \quad f_{tolerable} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{per year} $$ **Step 1: Compute RRF** $$ \begin{aligned} RRF_{required} &= \dfrac{f_{init}}{f_{tolerable}} \\ &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} \\ &= 5 \times 10^{2} \\ &= 500 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute PFDavg** $$ PFD_{required} = \dfrac{1}{500} = 2 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 3: Map to SIL** Since $10^{-3} \le 2 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2}$, this falls in the **SIL 2 band**. > [!NOTE] > **However**, since RRF = 500 is closer to SIL 3's lower bound (1000) than to SIL 1's upper bound (100), and the conservative practice in IEC 61511 is to **round up** when borderline, many engineers would specify **SIL 2** but verify with redundancy. The expected KTU answer: **SIL 2**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Discuss the **importance and objectives** of the SRS in the safety lifecycle. Why is the SRS considered the **most critical document** in functional safety projects? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 1oo1 sensor has dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year). Calculate the **PFDavg** of the sensor. If the same sensor is reconfigured to **1oo2**, what is the new PFDavg? Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution #### (a) Importance and Objectives of SRS **Objectives:** 1. Translate the *output* of the hazard analysis (HAZOP/LOPA) into unambiguous engineering requirements. 2. Provide a **contractual** basis between process, instrumentation, and operations teams. 3. Enable **SIL verification** through quantitative calculation of PFDavg. 4. Serve as the **audit baseline** for regulatory compliance. 5. Establish **traceability** from hazard → SIF → installation → operation → decommissioning. **Why SRS is the most critical document:** - It is the **only place** where functional and integrity requirements are formally captured. - Errors in the SRS **propagate** into every downstream phase (design, installation, operation). - It is the **primary evidence** used in incident investigations and legal proceedings. - Without an SRS, **SIL verification, validation, and audits are impossible**. #### (b) PFDavg Calculation **Step 1: Convert units** $$ \lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour} $$ $$ T_{proof} = 8760 \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ year} $$ **Step 2: PFDavg for 1oo1 architecture** $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{1oo1} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (8760)}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{4.38 \times 10^{-2}}{2} \\ &= 2.19 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: PFDavg for 1oo2 architecture** $$ \begin{aligned} PFD_{1oo2} &\approx 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{\,2} \cdot T_{proof}^{\,2} \\ &= 2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \cdot (8760)^{2} \\ &= 2 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot (7.677 \times 10^{7}) \\ &= 2 \cdot 1.919 \times 10^{-3} \\ &\approx 3.84 \times 10^{-3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Comment** | Architecture | PFDavg | Maps to | |---|---|---| | 1oo1 | $2.19 \times 10^{-2}$ | SIL 1 | | 1oo2 | $3.84 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 | **Comment:** The 1oo2 architecture improves PFDavg by roughly **a factor of 6**, moving the SIF from SIL 1 to SIL 2 capability — at the cost of increased complexity and a slightly higher spurious-trip rate. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Unit trap:** If you forget to convert $\lambda_{DU}$ from /hour to /year, you will lose 2 marks. > 2. **Architecture formula:** Writing $PFD_{1oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ (the 1oo1 formula) is the most common error. Always use the **square of failure probability** for 1oo2. > 3. **Missing comment:** Numerical answers without engineering interpretation lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE** - [x] **SRS = Specification of WHAT + HOW RELIABLE**, not HOW to design. - [x] **Standard:** IEC 61511-1 Clause 10.3 (process industry) / IEC 61508 (general). - [x] **Four pillars:** Functional, Integrity, Operational, Environmental. - [x] **PFDavg formula:** $PFD = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ for 1oo1. - [x] **RRF = 1/PFDavg**. - [x] **SIL 3** band: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$. - [x] **Process Safety Time** $\ge 2 \times$ SIF Response Time (50% safety margin). - [x] **Dangerous Undetected ($\lambda_{DU}$)** is the only contributor to PFDavg. - [x] **HFT = 1** required for SIL 2/3 subsystems in Type B devices. - [x] **Final elements (valves) typically consume ~50% of the budgeted PFDavg**. - [x] **BPCS ≠ SIS** — SRS is **only for SIS**, never for BPCS. - [x] **SRS is the audit baseline** for OSHA/HSE/regulators. - [x] **1oo2 architecture** dramatically reduces PFDavg at the cost of spurious-trip rate. - [x] **Proof Test Interval** is a **mandatory** SRS element — never omit. - [x] **Validation** (per Clause 15) is done **after** the SRS is approved, but **before** plant operation.

topics.title: Independency of the Protective Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Independency of the Protective Layers # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / IEC 61511 / IEC 61508 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Independency of the Protective Layers (IPL Independency)** is the property by which two or more Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) do not influence each other's ability to perform their intended safety functions, do not share a common operational mechanism, do not rely on the same sensor, logic solver, or final element, and do not fail from a single root cause (Common Cause Failure — CCF). For an IPL to be considered valid in Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA), it must satisfy the **independence requirement** with respect to (a) the initiating event, and (b) every other credited IPL in the same scenario. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PESFT635 — Functional Safety**, the concept is positioned as a foundational qualifier of any IPL claimed in a safety instrumented function (SIF) or risk-reduction credit assignment. The standard reference is **IEC 61511-1:2016, Clause 5.2.6 / 9.2** and **IEC 61508-2:2010, Annex D**. ### The Three Independent Layer Axioms | # | Axiom | Meaning | |---|-------|---------| | 1 | **Independence from the Initiating Event** | The IPL must not be triggered, controlled, or defeated by the same process deviation that causes the initiating event. | | 2 | **Independence from other IPLs** | Two IPLs must not share equipment, software, power supply, calibration, operators, or procedures. | | 3 | **Auditability / Effectiveness** | An IPL must have a **detectable, documented, and quantified PFD** (typically PFD < **10⁻²** for a single IPL under LOPA rules). | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss Cheese Stack" Made of Different Cheeses > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Commercial Airplane Landing System** Imagine a passenger aircraft on final approach. There are **six independent safety layers** protecting the landing: 1. **Air Traffic Control (ATC)** gives correct landing headings. 2. **On-board Autopilot** holds the glide slope. 3. **Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)** shouts "PULL UP" if terrain comes too close. 4. **Pilot's visual judgment** through the windshield. 5. **Anti-collision radar** detecting other aircraft. 6. **The pilot's manual override** (the physical hand on the yoke). Each layer is built by a **different vendor**, runs on **different software**, uses **different sensors**, and is operated by **different humans (or none)**. None of them will fail because one of the others failed. **That is functional independence.** If all six layers were powered from the same battery and ran the same software chip — they would look like six layers, but they would be just **one layer multiplied six times**. A short circuit in the shared battery would knock out all of them simultaneously. **That is the illusion of independence, which IEC 61508 explicitly forbids.** This is the same trap KTU examiners test when they ask: *"Is a transmitter + DCS logic + valve that all sit on the same Profibus segment truly two independent IPLs?"* — **Answer: No, they are one IPL.** --- ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) | Metric | Typical Range / Value | |--------|----------------------| | **PFDavg** of a single, valid IPL | **10⁻¹ to 10⁻²** | | **PFDavg** of a Type A, SIL-2 SIF | **10⁻² to 10⁻³** | | **CCF Beta-factor (β)** | typically **0.05 to 0.10** | | **Minimum Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** per IPL | **10** (LOPA convention) | | **SIL** reduction credit per independent IPL | one SIL level, if truly independent | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architecture of Independence — A Layered View An IPL is a device, system, or action that is **capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence**, *provided* it: - Operates **independently** of the initiating event and of the other layers, - Is **auditable** (its PFD can be verified and documented), and - Has a **single, well-defined safety function** it performs when demanded. ### Types of Independence — The Four Pillars > [!IMPORTANT] > **Four Pillars of IPL Independence (must hold simultaneously)** 1. **Physical Independence** — separate equipment, separate wiring, separate I/O cards, separate cabinets. The most basic form. 2. **Functional Independence** — different operating principles (e.g., a pressure transmitter vs. a temperature transmitter detecting the same over-pressure event through two different physics). 3. **Human / Operational Independence** — different operators, different procedures, different shift teams, different communication channels. No single human error can disable multiple IPLs. 4. **Common-Cause-Failure (CCF) Independence** — the IPL must be robust against shared root causes like lightning strikes, common power supplies, flooding, software bugs, calibration drift, environmental stress, and design defects. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $PFD_{\text{total}} \approx PFD_1 + PFD_2 + PFD_3$ | Sum of independent PFDs (valid when each $PFD_i \ll 1$) | dimensionless | | 2 | $PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - PFD_i)$ | Exact PFD for $n$ independent IPLs in series | dimensionless | | 3 | $RRF_i = \dfrac{1}{PFD_i}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of layer $i$ | dimensionless | | 4 | $RRF_{\text{total}} \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} RRF_i$ | RRF multiplication for small PFDs | dimensionless | | 5 | $PFD_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot PFD_i$ | PFD component contributed by common-cause failure of layer $i$ | dimensionless | | 6 | $PFD_{\text{independent}} = (1-\beta) \cdot PFD_i$ | Independent failure portion | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(PFD_{\text{avg}})$ | SIL to PFD mapping | — | | 8 | $\Delta R \geq R_{\text{required}} - R_{\text{current}}$ | LOPA risk gap equation (each independent IPL must fill part of $\Delta R$) | dimensionless risk units | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Condition for Formula #1**: The approximation $PFD_{\text{total}} \approx \sum PFD_i$ is valid **only when each $PFD_i < 0.1$**. For IPLs with $PFD_i > 0.1$, students must use the exact Formula #2. --- ## 2.3 The "AND-Logic" of Independent Protection Layers In LOPA, multiple IPLs are connected in **logical AND** in the *event tree* sense: the consequence occurs **only if all IPLs fail simultaneously**. For independent layers, this AND-gate becomes statistically multiplicative in risk reduction. $$P(\text{Consequence}) = P(\text{Initiating Event}) \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$ This is the **mathematical beauty of independence**: two independent IPLs, each with $PFD = 10^{-2}$, give a combined $PFD \approx 10^{-4}$, achieving **SIL 4** behavior from two SIL-2 layers — *but only if they are truly independent.* ### Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of Independent Layers | |--------|---------------------------| | **Oil & Gas** | Pressure Safety Valve (PSL) + SIF + Operator action — three IPLs on a fired heater | | **Chemical** | BPCS control loop + Alarm + SIS shutdown — overlapping protection | | **Pharmaceutical** | BPCS + redundant DCS + mechanical relief — common in batch reactor safety | | **Power Plant** | Boiler trips, relief valves, operator response, containment — turbine overspeed protection | | **KTU Exam** | LOPA questions on scenario risk reduction — *this is your bread and butter* | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total PFD for Two Independent IPLs **Statement.** For two independent IPLs, IPL-A and IPL-B, with individual probabilities of failure on demand $PFD_A$ and $PFD_B$, derive the total probability that *both* fail (i.e., the consequence is realized). **Step 1 — Define the failure events.** Let $F_A$ be the event "IPL-A fails to act on demand" and $F_B$ be the event "IPL-B fails to act on demand". Independence implies: $$P(F_A \cap F_B) = P(F_A) \cdot P(F_B)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the PFDs.** $$PFD_{\text{total}} = PFD_A \cdot PFD_B$$ **Step 3 — Generalize to *n* independent IPLs.** $$PFD_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$ **Step 4 — Express in complement form** (probability that *at least one* layer succeeds): $$PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - PFD_1)(1 - PFD_2)\cdots(1 - PFD_n)$$ **Step 5 — Approximation for small PFDs.** Expand the product: $$\begin{aligned} PFD_{\text{total}} &= 1 - \left[1 - (PFD_1 + PFD_2 + \cdots + PFD_n) + \text{second-order terms}\right] \\ &= (PFD_1 + PFD_2 + \cdots + PFD_n) - \sum_{i **Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2024 style]**: > A reactor over-pressure scenario has: > - Initiating Event Frequency (IEF) $= 10^{-1}\ \text{per year}$ > - IPL-1: Pressure Safety Valve with $PFD = 10^{-2}$ > - IPL-2: Independent SIF (SIL-2) with $PFD = 10^{-2}$ > - IPL-3: Operator action with $PFD = 10^{-1}$ > - Target tolerable frequency $= 10^{-5}\ \text{per year}$ **Step 1 — Total PFD of three independent IPLs.** $$PFD_{\text{total}} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-5}$$ **Step 2 — Mitigated event frequency.** $$f_{\text{mitigated}} = IEF \times PFD_{\text{total}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-5} = 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to target.** $$10^{-6} \ll 10^{-5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{✓ ACCEPTABLE — Risk gap is closed with a safety margin of 10.}$$ --- ## 3.3 The Common-Cause Failure (CCF) Penalty When two "independent" layers are *not truly* independent, the IEC 61508 Beta-factor model applies. **Step 1 — Decompose each PFD into independent + dependent fractions.** $$PFD_i = PFD_{i,\text{independent}} + PFD_{i,\text{CCF}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Beta factor.** With $\beta = 0.10$ (typical for same-vendor, same-technology redundancy): $$PFD_{i,\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot PFD_i \quad ; \quad PFD_{i,\text{independent}} = (1-\beta) \cdot PFD_i$$ **Step 3 — Effective combined PFD accounting for CCF** (two layers A & B): $$PFD_{\text{eff}} = PFD_{A,\text{independent}} \cdot PFD_{B,\text{independent}} + \text{CCF contribution}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical illustration.** Let $PFD_A = PFD_B = 10^{-2}$, $\beta = 0.10$. $$PFD_{\text{eff}} = (0.9)(10^{-2}) \cdot (0.9)(10^{-2}) + 0.10 \cdot 10^{-2} = 8.1 \times 10^{-5} + 10^{-3} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-3}$$ Notice: the **ideal independent value** would have been $10^{-4}$, but CCF degraded it to $1.08 \times 10^{-3}$ — almost an **order of magnitude worse**. **This is why independence is non-negotiable in IEC 61511.** --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Independence Checklist | Failure Category | Question to Test Independence | Pass Criterion | |------------------|-------------------------------|----------------| | **Shared Sensor** | Do the two IPLs use the same physical sensor? | No — must be different | | **Shared Logic Solver** | Same PLC/SIS controller? | No | | **Shared Final Element** | Same valve, breaker, relay? | No | | **Shared Power** | Same UPS, same battery, same MCC feeder? | No | | **Shared Software** | Same firmware, same version? | No | | **Shared Operator** | Same human, same procedure? | No | | **Shared Maintenance** | Same technician on same day? | No | | **Shared Environment** | Same cabinet, same flooded area, same cable tray? | No | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often assume that two different *brands* of PLC give independence. They do not, if both run the same application logic and are fed by the same process variable. **Brand diversity ≠ functional independence.** # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Event-Tree Representation of Independent Protection Layers ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeIE["Initiating Event
Overpressure in Reactor"] nodeIPL1["IPL 1 Pressure Safety Valve
PFD = 1e-2"] nodeIPL2["IPL 2 Safety Instrumented Function
PFD = 1e-2"] nodeIPL3["IPL 3 Operator Intervention
PFD = 1e-1"] nodeSafe["Safe State Achieved"] nodeHazard["Hazardous Consequence
Reactor Rupture"] nodeIE -- succeeds --> nodeSafe nodeIE -- fails --> nodeIPL1 nodeIPL1 -- succeeds --> nodeSafe nodeIPL1 -- fails --> nodeIPL2 nodeIPL2 -- succeeds --> nodeSafe nodeIPL2 -- fails --> nodeIPL3 nodeIPL3 -- succeeds --> nodeSafe nodeIPL3 -- fails --> nodeHazard style nodeSafe fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeHazard fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 style nodeIE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000 style nodeIPL1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 style nodeIPL2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 style nodeIPL3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Independence Verification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSensA["Sensor A
Pressure xmtr 1"] nodeSensB["Sensor B
Pressure xmtr 2
Different vendor"] nodeLogicA["Logic Solver A
BPCS"] nodeLogicB["Logic Solver B
SIS Independent"] nodeFinalA["Final Element A
Control Valve"] nodeFinalB["Final Element B
Block Valve"] nodeProcess["Reactor Process"] nodeProcess -- PV signal 1 --> nodeSensA nodeProcess -- PV signal 2 --> nodeSensB nodeSensA --> nodeLogicA nodeSensB --> nodeLogicB nodeLogicA -- shutdown cmd --> nodeFinalA nodeLogicB -- shutdown cmd --> nodeFinalB style nodeSensA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000 style nodeSensB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000 style nodeLogicA fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000 style nodeLogicB fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000000 style nodeFinalA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeFinalB fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000000 style nodeProcess fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 CCF Coupling Diagram — What Makes Layers *Not* Independent ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeRoot["Common Root Cause
e.g. Lightning Strike"] nodeCCF["Common Cause Failure
Beta factor beta"] nodeIPL1a["IPL 1A
Fails due to CCF"] nodeIPL1b["IPL 1B
Fails due to CCF"] nodeLoss["Both layers lost
despite nominal independence"] nodeRoot --> nodeCCF nodeCCF --> nodeIPL1a nodeCCF --> nodeIPL1b nodeIPL1a --> nodeLoss nodeIPL1b --> nodeLoss style nodeRoot fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 style nodeCCF fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000 style nodeIPL1a fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeIPL1b fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF style nodeLoss fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"Define Independent Protection Layer (IPL). State any two conditions required for an IPL to be considered independent in LOPA."** **Model Answer:** > An **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** is a device, system, or human action that is capable of preventing a scenario from progressing to its undesired consequence, with a **quantifiable, auditable probability of failure on demand (PFD)** and with **demonstrated independence** from the initiating event and from every other IPL credited in the same scenario. > > **Two conditions for independence:** > 1. **Independence from the initiating event** — the IPL is not activated, defeated, or made inoperable by the same process deviation that triggers the initiating event. > 2. **Independence from other credited IPLs** — the IPL does not share sensors, logic solvers, final elements, power supplies, software, operators, or maintenance personnel with any other credited IPL. *(Examiner's note: A third condition — **auditable PFD with detectable failure mode** — if added, fetches full 3 marks.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **"What is Common Cause Failure (CCF)? How does it affect the credit assigned to multiple protective layers in LOPA?"** **Model Answer:** > **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** is the failure of two or more apparently independent protection layers due to a **shared root cause**, such as a common power supply, common software defect, common calibration drift, common environmental stress, or a shared operator error. > > In LOPA, CCF reduces the **effective risk reduction factor (RRF)** because the true combined $PFD_{\text{total}}$ is **greater than the ideal independent value** $PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2 \cdot \ldots \cdot PFD_n$. The **Beta-factor model** is used to quantify this penalty, where a fraction $\beta$ of each PFD is attributed to CCF. **Practically, this means students must not over-credit overlapping layers**; only layers with documented independence are entitled to multiplicative credit. --- ## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four pillars of IPL independence: physical, functional, human/operational, and CCF independence. Illustrate with one example of a violation of each pillar from a typical process plant. **(b) [7 Marks]** A LOPA study on a hydrocarbon storage tank gives the following data: | Item | Value | |------|-------| | Initiating Event Frequency (overfilling) | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ /year | | IPL-1: Independent level SIF (SIL-2) | $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ | | IPL-2: Independent overflow alarm + operator | $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ | | IPL-3: Bund / dyke (passive IPL) | $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$ | | Target tolerable frequency | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ /year | Compute the **total PFD**, the **mitigated event frequency**, and **verify whether the SIL target is met**. If the SIF and the alarm were found to share the same level transmitter (a CCF), comment on how this would alter the conclusion. --- ### **Model Answer — Question A** **Part (a) — The Four Pillars of IPL Independence:** 1. **Physical Independence** — Separation of equipment, cabinets, wiring, and signal paths. - *Example violation*: A SIS sensor and a BPCS sensor mounted in the same impulse line tapping the same nozzle. A plugged tap knocks out both. 2. **Functional Independence** — Use of different operating principles and different measured variables. - *Example violation*: Two "independent" pressure transmitters using the same diaphragm material and same supplier, both subject to identical corrosion fatigue failure. 3. **Human / Operational Independence** — Separation of operators, procedures, training, and shift teams. - *Example violation*: A single operator tasked with both the alarm acknowledgement and the manual valve closure during the same incident — a single human error defeats both IPLs. 4. **Common-Cause-Failure (CCF) Independence** — Robustness against shared root causes such as common power, common software, common flooding, common fire, and shared maintenance windows. - *Example violation*: Two ESD valves both fed from the same 24 VDC power supply. A short circuit at the supply disables both. > *(For each pillar, the KTU answer expects: Definition + Example + Why it matters. Award 7/7 only if all four are clearly distinguished.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **Step 1 — Total PFD (independent layers, using exact formula):** $$\begin{aligned} PFD_{\text{total}} &= 1 - (1 - 10^{-2})(1 - 10^{-1})(1 - 10^{-1}) \\ &= 1 - (0.99)(0.9)(0.9) \\ &= 1 - 0.8019 \\ &= 0.1981 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Mitigated event frequency:** $$f_{\text{mit}} = IEF \times PFD_{\text{total}} = 10^{-1} \times 0.1981 = 1.981 \times 10^{-2}\ /\text{year}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to target:** $$1.981 \times 10^{-2} \gg 10^{-5}$$ ❌ **Target NOT met.** The risk gap $\Delta R = f_{\text{mit}} / f_{\text{target}} = 1981$ is unacceptably large. **Step 4 — Reframe the three IPLs as a multiplicative chain (assuming independence holds):** If the three IPLs are treated as strictly independent barriers in series, students often quote $PFD_{\text{total}} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-4}$. **This is a common KTU trap.** The exact AND-gate formula (Step 1) must be used whenever the PFD values are not all $<10^{-2}$. **Step 5 — Effect of CCF (shared level transmitter):** If the SIF (IPL-1) and the alarm (IPL-2) share the same level transmitter, then **IPL-1 and IPL-2 are no longer independent.** They collapse into a single IPL whose PFD must be combined via the Beta model or worst-case assumed: $$PFD_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1 - 10^{-2})(1 - 10^{-1}) = 1 - 0.891 = 0.109$$ **New total PFD with CCF collapse:** $$PFD_{\text{total, CCF}} = 1 - (1 - 0.109)(1 - 10^{-1}) = 1 - 0.109 \times 0.9 = 1 - 0.0981 = 0.9019$$ $$f_{\text{mit, CCF}} = 10^{-1} \times 0.9019 \approx 9 \times 10^{-2}\ /\text{year}$$ **Conclusion:** The CCF collapses the safety case to almost no protection. This demonstrates **why independence verification is non-negotiable.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **independent protection layers** and **redundant elements within a single IPL**. Why is this distinction critical in LOPA credit assignment? **(b) [7 Marks]** A reactor has two layers of protection against runaway: - IPL-A: BPCS control loop with $PFD = 5 \times 10^{-1}$ - IPL-B: Independent SIF (SIL-2) with $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ - Initiating event frequency of runaway $= 10^{-1}$/year. The company policy mandates a tolerable frequency of $10^{-6}$/year. **(i)** Compute the mitigated frequency. **(ii)** If a third layer, IPL-C (an operator manual response with $PFD = 10^{-1}$), is added and is *fully independent*, recompute and comment. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B** **Part (a) — IPL vs Redundancy:** | Aspect | IPLs (Independent Protection Layers) | Redundant Elements within an IPL | |--------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------| | Definition | Two *separate* safety barriers acting on the same hazard | Two or more *parallel* elements *within* one safety function (e.g., 2oo3 voting) | | Independence | Independent by definition (IEC 61511 requirement) | Usually dependent — designed to fail together (e.g., same manufacturer, same logic) | | LOPA Credit | Each independent IPL receives its own PFD credit | Redundancy is used to *reduce* the PFD of *one* IPL; it does *not* create a new IPL | | Effect on SIL | Multiple SIL-2 IPLs can collectively achieve SIL 3 or SIL 4 behavior | Redundancy can boost one IPL from SIL 2 to SIL 3 internally | | Example | Pressure Safety Valve + SIF shutdown | Two pressure transmitters in 1oo2D inside one SIF | **Why critical:** If a LOPA analyst wrongly treats redundant elements *within* a SIF as a *new* IPL, the credit is artificially inflated, the SIL claim is unsupported, and the safety case fails validation. The **independence test** is the diagnostic. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) With IPL-A and IPL-B only (independent):** $$PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1 - 5\times 10^{-1})(1 - 10^{-2}) = 1 - (0.5)(0.99) = 1 - 0.495 = 0.505$$ $$f_{\text{mit}} = 10^{-1} \times 0.505 = 5.05 \times 10^{-2}\ /\text{year}$$ **Comparison:** $5.05 \times 10^{-2} \gg 10^{-6}$. ❌ **Target NOT met.** **Note the critical insight:** Even though IPL-B is SIL-2 ($PFD = 10^{-2}$), the *BPCS loop* ($PFD = 0.5$) is so weak that the combined risk reduction is only about a factor of 2, **not** a factor of $100 \times 2 = 200$. This is the **LOPA trap of weak BPCS** — IEC 61511 caps BPCS credit at $PFD \ge 10^{-1}$ for a single layer. **(ii) Adding IPL-C (operator, $PFD = 10^{-1}$):** $$PFD_{\text{total,3}} = 1 - (1 - 0.505)(1 - 10^{-1}) = 1 - (0.495)(0.9) = 1 - 0.4455 = 0.5545$$ $$f_{\text{mit,3}} = 10^{-1} \times 0.5545 = 5.545 \times 10^{-2}\ /\text{year}$$ **Comment:** Adding IPL-C barely helps because IPL-A is so weak that the dominant risk comes from IPL-A failing. The lesson — **strengthen the weakest IPL before adding new ones** — is a recurring KTU and industry insight. *(For full 7 marks on part (b), students must show both numerical computations AND the qualitative interpretation about weak-IPL dominance.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points** > 1. **Treating the BPCS as an IPL with PFD < 0.1** — IEC 61511 caps BPCS PFD at $10^{-1}$. Writing $PFD_{\text{BPCS}} = 10^{-2}$ costs marks. > 2. **Adding PFDs of redundant elements instead of multiplying** — within one SIF, redundant transmitters reduce PFD through architecture formulas (1oo2, 2oo3), they do **not** sum. > 3. **Forgetting CCF Beta-factor** — when asked about independence, simply ignoring CCF = automatic 2-mark loss. > 4. **Confusing event-tree AND logic with simple addition** — the additive shortcut $PFD_{\text{total}} = \sum PFD_i$ is valid **only** when each $PFD_i < 0.1$. > 5. **Calling "different brand" the same as "independent"** — vendor diversity ≠ functional independence. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **IPL** = a safety layer that is **independent**, **auditable**, and **effective**. - ✅ **Independence** is judged against (a) initiating event, (b) other IPLs, and (c) common-cause failures. - ✅ **Four pillars** of independence: Physical, Functional, Human/Operational, CCF. - ✅ **BPCS PFD capped at $10^{-1}$** per IEC 61511 LOPA rules. - ✅ **Minimum PFD of any single IPL** must be **≤ $10^{-2}$** to be credited. - ✅ **Exact formula**: $PFD_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod (1 - PFD_i)$. **Approximate formula**: $PFD_{\text{total}} \approx \sum PFD_i$ (valid only when each $PFD_i < 0.1$). - ✅ **CCF Beta factor**: typically $\beta = 0.05$ to $0.10$; it penalizes apparent independence. - ✅ **Redundancy inside a SIF ≠ new IPL** — 1oo2, 2oo3 are internal voting, not separate layers. - ✅ **Two SIL-2 independent IPLs → combined SIL 4 behavior** — but only with verified independence. - ✅ **Vendor diversity ≠ functional independence**. - ✅ **Weakest IPL dominates** — strengthen the weakest link before stacking new ones. - ✅ **Risk gap equation**: $\Delta R = f_{\text{mitigated, target}} / f_{\text{event}}$; LOPA must close the gap. - ✅ **Documentation is mandatory**: every credited IPL must have a written independence check-list signed off. - ✅ **No credit for un-auditable layers**: operator action without documented procedure is **not** a valid IPL. - ✅ **CCF coupling is the silent killer** — always verify power, software, environment, and operator independence. > **Final KTU Takeaway**: Independence is **not** a paperwork formality — it is the mathematical foundation that makes the AND-logic of multiple protection layers *actually multiplicative*. Lose independence, and you lose the entire risk-reduction argument. **Verify, document, and audit every IPL.**

topics.title: Demand Rate and Operational Modes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Demand Rate and Operational Modes

## 1.1 Demand Rate — Formal Definition

In the context of **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511**, the **Demand Rate** is defined as the frequency at which a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) or Safety-Related System is *called upon* to perform its protective action. Mathematically, it is expressed as the **number of demands placed on a safety function per unit time** (typically per year).

$$\text{Demand Rate} \; (\lambda_d) = \frac{\text{Number of Demands}}{\text{Unit Time}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Demand Rate is the *master switch* that determines whether a SIF operates in **Low-Demand Mode** or **High-Demand/Continuous Mode**, which in turn dictates whether we evaluate the system using **PFDavg** (Probability of Failure on Demand — average) or **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour).

## 1.2 Operational Modes — Formal Definition

The **Operational Mode** of a safety function describes the relationship between the rate at which demands occur and the *proof-test interval* (T_proof) or mission time of the system. IEC 61508 explicitly classifies operational modes into three categories:

| Mode | Demand Frequency | Risk Metric Used |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| **Low Demand Mode** | ≤ **1 demand per year** | PFDavg |
| **High Demand Mode** | > **1 demand per year** | PFH |
| **Continuous Mode** | Safety function always active | PFH |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Smoke Detector

Imagine a **smoke detector** in your house:
- A **low demand rate** scenario is like the detector sitting idle for years — it rarely needs to sound. When it does finally alarm (perhaps from a kitchen mishap), it must work perfectly on that *one* demand. We measure this as "How likely is it to fail when asked?" → **PFDavg**.
- A **high demand rate** scenario is like the smoke detector in a busy industrial furnace room where smoke or heat appears frequently. Here, we care about "How many times per hour might the system fail to operate?" → **PFH**.
- A **continuous mode** scenario is like the **brake system** in a moving car — the safety function is *always* required to be operational.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Anchor:** In a refinery, an **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** valve protecting a hydrocarbon storage tank may only see a demand once every 5–10 years (low demand). But a **turbine overspeed protection system** may face dozens of demands per hour (high demand/continuous).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Demand Rate — Structural Breakdown

The concept of Demand Rate is rooted in the **demand model** of a safety function. The key engineering insight is that the *acceptable probability of failure* is **inversely proportional** to the demand rate. A system demanded often must be extraordinarily reliable *per unit time*, because failures accumulate rapidly.

### 2.1.1 Why Demand Rate Matters (The Core Logic)

Consider two safety functions:
- **SIF-A**: Demanded once every 5 years → tolerable failure probability on demand = **10⁻²** (SIL 1).
- **SIF-B**: Demanded 100 times per hour → tolerable failure probability per hour ≈ **10⁻⁸** (SIL 3).

If SIF-B had only SIL 1 reliability, the cumulative failure rate over a year would be catastrophic.

## 2.2 Relationship: Demand Rate ↔ PFDavg ↔ PFH

The transition from PFDavg to PFH occurs at the **demand rate boundary of 1/year**.

For a given **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**, the target failure measure is split:

$$\text{Demand Rate} \le 1/\text{year} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use } PFD_{avg}$$

$$\text{Demand Rate} > 1/\text{year} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use } PFH \; (\text{Probability of Failure per Hour})$$

## 2.3 SIL Allocation Table (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 61508 Table 2 / Table 3 — Target Failure Measures for Safety Functions**

| Safety Integrity Level (SIL) | PFDavg (Low Demand) | PFH (High Demand / Continuous) [per hour] | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6} \le PFH < 10^{-5}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7} \le PFH < 10^{-6}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8} \le PFH < 10^{-7}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \le PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9} \le PFH < 10^{-8}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

## 2.4 The Demand Rate Boundary Formula

The average number of demands placed on a safety function over its **proof test interval** (T_proof) determines the operational mode:

$$N_{demands} = \lambda_d \times T_{proof}$$

- If $N_{demands} \le 1$, the system operates in **Low Demand Mode**.
- If $N_{demands} > 1$, the system operates in **High Demand Mode**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Test Interval (T_proof):** The periodic test performed to reveal hidden (dangerous undetected) failures in a safety system. Standard values range from 1 year to 10 years depending on the process.

## 2.5 Why the Distinction Matters — Engineering Utility

In Low Demand Mode, the safety function has long idle periods where **diagnostic testing** catches hidden failures. The dominant failure metric is **PFDavg**, computed over the proof test interval.

In High Demand / Continuous Mode, the safety function operates so frequently that we cannot rely on a single "average demand" — instead we use the **probability of failure per hour (PFH)**, derived from the dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ of the subsystem.

$$PFH \approx \lambda_D \quad \text{(simplified, for a 1oo1 architecture)}$$

$$PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D \times T_{proof} \quad \text{(simplified, for a 1oo1 architecture in low demand)}$$

These two equations elegantly show why PFH grows linearly with time (and thus demands), while PFDavg grows only across proof test intervals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Dangerous Failure Rate to PFDavg

We begin with a **single-channel (1oo1)** safety system in low demand mode. The dangerous failure rate (per hour) of the channel is $\lambda_D$.

**Step 1 — Define the unavailability function $U(t)$.**

A channel is unavailable if it has failed dangerously *and* the failure remains undetected. The probability that the channel fails within time $t$ follows the exponential reliability law:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous unavailability.**

The instantaneous unavailability at time $t$ for a system that fails at some random time uniformly in the interval $[0, T_{proof}]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U(t) &= 1 - R(t) \\
     &= 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average unavailability over the proof test interval.**

We integrate the unavailability over the entire interval and divide by $T_{proof}$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} U(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right) dt \\
          &= \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[ t + \frac{1}{\lambda_D} e^{-\lambda_D t} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} \\
          &= \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \left[ T_{proof} + \frac{1}{\lambda_D} e^{-\lambda_D T_{proof}} - 0 - \frac{1}{\lambda_D} \right] \\
