          &= 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}} \left( e^{-\lambda_D T_{proof}} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the engineering approximation.**

Since $\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof} \ll 1$ (typical value: $10^{-6} \times 8760 \approx 0.008$), we use the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &\approx 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_D T_{proof}} \left( 1 - \lambda_D T_{proof} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^2}{2} - 1 \right) \\
          &\approx 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_D T_{proof}} \left( -\lambda_D T_{proof} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_{proof})^2}{2} \right) \\
          &\approx 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda_D T_{proof}}{2} \\
          &\approx \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{proof}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Simplified Result:**
> $$\boxed{PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_{proof}}$$
> This is the **KTU board-exam golden formula** for a 1oo1 architecture in low demand mode.

## 3.2 Derivation: PFH from Dangerous Failure Rate

For a 1oo1 channel operating in **high demand or continuous mode**, every dangerous failure that occurs results in a failure of the safety function. Thus, the probability of failure per hour is simply:

$$PFH = \lambda_D$$

For more complex architectures like **1oo2** (one out of two), the PFH is approximated as:

$$PFH_{1oo2} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \times T_{proof}}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** For redundant architectures, PFH scales with the *square* of $\lambda_D$, which is why redundancy is so powerful in high-demand applications.

## 3.3 Demand Rate Boundary — Numerical Demonstration

Let us consider a SIF with:
- Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- Proof test interval: $T_{proof} = 1$ year $= 8760$ hours

**Case 1: Low Demand Mode (1 demand/year)**

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760 \\
          &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.38 \times 10^{-3} \\
          &= 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

This falls within the **SIL 2** band ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$). ✓

**Case 2: High Demand Mode (>1 demand/year)**

$$PFH = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$

This also falls within the **SIL 2** band ($10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The same hardware can satisfy SIL 2 in either mode — but the *meaning* of the failure probability is fundamentally different. In low demand, we tolerate ~2 failures in 1000 demands; in high demand, we tolerate ~5 failures per 10 million operating hours.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Demand Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Safety Function SIF"] --> B["Determine Demand Rate lambda_d"]
    B --> C{"Compare lambda_d with 1 per year"}
    C -- "lambda_d less than or equal to 1 per year" --> D["LOW DEMAND MODE"]
    C -- "lambda_d greater than 1 per year" --> E{"Check continuous operation"}
    E -- "Yes always active" --> F["CONTINUOUS MODE"]
    E -- "No frequent periodic" --> G["HIGH DEMAND MODE"]
    D --> H["Use PFDavg metric"]
    G --> I["Use PFH metric"]
    F --> I
    H --> J["Compute SIL target from PFDavg table"]
    I --> K["Compute SIL target from PFH table"]
    J --> L["Allocate Hardware and Software"]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Failure Probability vs Demand Rate Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Low Demand n less than 1 per year"] --> B["PFDavg 10 to the power minus 5 to 10 to the power minus 1"]
    C["High Demand n greater than 1 per year"] --> D["PFH 10 to the power minus 9 to 10 to the power minus 6 per hour"]
    E["Continuous always active"] --> F["PFH same as high demand"]
    B --> G["SIL 1 to SIL 4 selection"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.3 Architecture Block Diagram — Demand Mode Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Process Variable Layer"]
        I1["Sensor 1"]
        I2["Sensor 2"]
    end
    subgraph LOGIC["Logic Solver Layer"]
        L1["Safety PLC with diagnostics"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Final Element Layer"]
        O1["Actuator A"]
        O2["Actuator B"]
    end
    subgraph DEMAND["Demand Assessment Module"]
        D1["Count demands per year"]
        D2["Compare against boundary of 1 per year"]
        D3["Select PFDavg or PFH framework"]
    end
    I1 --> L1
    I2 --> L1
    L1 --> O1
    L1 --> O2
    L1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> L1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Demand Rate. How is it used to classify the operational mode of a safety function?**

**Model Answer:**
The Demand Rate is defined as the frequency (number per unit time) at which a Safety Instrumented Function is required to actuate. According to **IEC 61508**, if the demand rate is ≤ **1 demand per year**, the SIF is classified as operating in **Low Demand Mode** and the failure metric used is **PFDavg**. If the demand rate exceeds 1 demand per year, the SIF operates in **High Demand Mode** (or Continuous Mode if it is always active), and the failure metric used is **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour). The demand rate thus dictates the entire analytical framework of the safety lifecycle.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between PFDavg and PFH. Under what demand conditions is each applied?**

**Model Answer:**
| Parameter | PFDavg | PFH |
|-----------|--------|-----|
| **Full Form** | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | Probability of Failure per Hour |
| **Mode** | Low Demand Mode | High Demand / Continuous Mode |
| **Demand Rate** | ≤ 1/year | > 1/year |
| **Formula (1oo1)** | $PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_{proof}$ | $PFH \approx \lambda_D$ |
| **SIL Range** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$ /hr |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 3 — Internal Choice**

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Demand Rate** as per IEC 61508. Discuss how demand rate determines whether a safety function operates in **Low Demand Mode** or **High Demand Mode**, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo1 pressure transmitter has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and the proof test interval is 2 years. If the safety function is demanded **once every 3 years**, determine the operational mode and compute the **PFDavg**. Comment on the achieved SIL. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Detailed Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Demand Rate ($\lambda_d$) is the number of demands placed on a safety function per unit time, expressed in demands/year.
2. **Mode Classification (2 marks):** Per IEC 61508:
   - $\lambda_d \le 1$/year → **Low Demand Mode** (metric: PFDavg)
   - $\lambda_d > 1$/year → **High Demand Mode** (metric: PFH)
   - Always active → **Continuous Mode** (metric: PFH)
3. **Examples (3 marks):**
   - *Low Demand*: Emergency Shutdown valve on a hydrocarbon storage tank (demand once in years due to overpressure).
   - *High Demand*: Fire & Gas detection system in a chemical plant (frequent nuisance alarms).
   - *Continuous*: Aircraft flight control surface actuators — safety function always active.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the operational mode.**

Demand interval = 3 years → Demand rate $\lambda_d = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$/year.

Since $\lambda_d = 0.333$/year ≤ 1/year → **Low Demand Mode**.

**Step 2 — Compute PFDavg.**

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2} \times \lambda_D \times T_{proof} \\
          &= \frac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2 \times 8760) \\
          &= \frac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17520 \\
          &= \frac{1}{2} \times 3.504 \times 10^{-2} \\
          &= 1.752 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — SIL Classification.**

$PFD_{avg} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$ falls in the range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ → **SIL 1**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The pressure transmitter achieves SIL 1 in a 1oo1 configuration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the relationship between **Demand Rate, Operational Mode, PFDavg, PFH, and SIL** in functional safety. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A safety valve on a reactor is operated in **High Demand Mode** with a demand rate of 50 demands per year. The dangerous failure rate of the valve is $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour. Compute the **PFH** and the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**. Identify the achieved SIL. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Conceptual Diagram + Explanation (7 Marks):**

The relationship forms a logical chain:

**Demand Rate → Operational Mode → Failure Metric → SIL Target**

1. **Demand Rate** is computed from process hazard analysis (HAZOP, LOPA).
2. Based on the threshold of **1 demand/year**, the mode is selected.
3. In Low Demand Mode, **PFDavg** is used; in High Demand / Continuous Mode, **PFH** is used.
4. From the chosen metric, the target **SIL** is read from the IEC 61508 tables.
5. The required **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is then derived as $RRF = 1/PFD$ or $RRF = 1/PFH \times T_{mission}$.

A Mermaid flow diagram (see Section 4.1) summarizes this chain.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Confirm High Demand Mode.**

Demand rate = 50/year > 1/year → **High Demand Mode confirmed**.

**Step 2 — Compute PFH.**

$$PFH = \lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 3 — Determine SIL.**

PFH = $3 \times 10^{-7}$ falls within $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ → **SIL 2**.

**Step 4 — Compute RRF.**

For high demand mode, the Risk Reduction Factor is computed using the average annual demands and the proof-test interval. Assuming $T_{proof} = 1$ year:

$$\begin{aligned}
RRF &= \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}} \\
PFD_{avg} &= PFH \times T_{proof} = (3 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760 = 2.628 \times 10^{-3} \\
RRF &= \frac{1}{2.628 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 380.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The safety valve achieves **SIL 2** with an RRF of approximately **380**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up PFDavg and PFH:** A common mistake is computing PFDavg for a high-demand system. Always check the demand rate first.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $T_{proof}$ must be in **hours** when multiplied with $\lambda_D$ (which is in /hour). 1 year = **8760 hours** (not 365 × 24 with the wrong approximation).
> 3. **Incorrect SIL boundary interpretation:** The SIL ranges are inclusive on the lower bound. For example, $PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3}$ falls in **SIL 2**, not SIL 3.
> 4. **Skipping the mode identification step:** Board examiners explicitly look for the statement "Since demand rate is ≤ 1/year → Low Demand Mode" before the calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Demand Rate** = Number of demands per unit time on a SIF, measured in demands/year.
- ✅ **Boundary value:** 1 demand/year separates Low Demand Mode from High Demand Mode.
- ✅ **Low Demand Mode** uses **PFDavg** (Probability of Failure on Demand — average).
- ✅ **High Demand / Continuous Mode** uses **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour).
- ✅ **PFDavg for 1oo1:** $PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_{proof}$.
- ✅ **PFH for 1oo1:** $PFH \approx \lambda_D$.
- ✅ **SIL 1:** PFDavg = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ ; PFH = $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$/hr.
- ✅ **SIL 2:** PFDavg = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ ; PFH = $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$/hr.
- ✅ **SIL 3:** PFDavg = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ ; PFH = $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$/hr.
- ✅ **SIL 4:** PFDavg = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ ; PFH = $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$/hr.
- ✅ **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** = 1/PFD for low demand; 1/(PFH × T) for high demand.
- ✅ **Proof Test Interval (T_proof)** is critical: shorter intervals reduce PFDavg but increase maintenance cost.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** ESD valves (low demand), Fire & Gas systems (high demand), Aircraft controls (continuous).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIF Structure Redundancies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SIF Structure Redundancies

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SIF Redundancy

A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is a designated set of sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements that together perform a single safety function intended to bring a process to a safe state when a hazardous condition is detected. Within the architecture of a SIF, **SIF Structure Redundancies** refer to the deliberate duplication or multiplicity of the constituent hardware channels (sensors, logic solver I/O, valves, etc.) such that the loss of any single channel does not result in the loss of the safety function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Redundancy is the principal hardware mechanism used to satisfy the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** or **Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)** targets mandated for a given **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 3)** as defined in **IEC 61511** (process industries) and **IEC 61508** (general functional safety).

The redundant structure is characterized by two fundamental design parameters:

1. **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** — The maximum number of faults a sub-system can tolerate without loss of the safety function. A sub-system with HFT = N can suffer N dangerous hardware faults and still execute its safety action.
2. **Voting Logic (M out of N)** — A logic arrangement where **M** out of **N** parallel channels must agree before the safety action is initiated (for low demand) or to keep the system available (for high/continuous demand).

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel versus Sub-system:** A *channel* is an independent single path of measurement or actuation. A *sub-system* is the complete assembly of redundant channels (e.g., three pressure transmitters forming the sensor sub-system).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Pilots in the Cockpit"

Imagine an airliner that requires a critical safety decision — *deploy the emergency landing gear*. A single tired pilot (analogous to a **1oo1** single-channel system) may make a mistake or fall asleep. So the airline assigns **three** independent pilots (**N = 3**). The airline's rulebook states that *at least two pilots must agree* before pulling the lever. This is a **2-out-of-3 (2oo3)** voting system.

- If one pilot becomes incapacitated, the remaining two can still unanimously decide → safety preserved.
- If one pilot "lies" (sends a wrong signal — analogous to a *dangerous undetected failure*), the other two override him → false trip avoided.
- The redundancy cost (more pilots) buys you **fault tolerance** *and* **diagnostic immunity**.

This is precisely what a SIF does in a refinery, a chemical reactor, or a burner management system.

## 1.3 Standard Vocabulary Anchors

| Term | Standard Symbol | KTU Friendly Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand | **PFD** | For low-demand mode: probability the SIF fails to act when a demand occurs |
| Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour | **PFH** | For high/continuous-demand mode: dangerous failures per hour |
| Safe Failure Fraction | **SFF** | Percentage of failures that are either safe-detected or safe-undetected |
| Diagnostic Coverage | **DC** | Fraction of failures automatically diagnosed by self-tests |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance | **HFT** | Number of faults tolerated without loss of safety action |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the translation between **PFD**, **PFH**, **SIL tables**, and **voting architecture**, so internalizing these abbreviations is non-negotiable for the examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Redundant SIF Subsystem

A SIF is divided into three sub-systems. Each sub-system may have its own redundancy arrangement:

1. **Sensor Sub-system** — Detects the hazardous process variable (pressure, temperature, level, flow).
2. **Logic Solver Sub-system** — PLC / Safety PLC / Trip amplifier that executes the voted trip logic.
3. **Final Element Sub-system** — Shutdown valve, blowdown valve, isolation damper, motor contactor.

The total PFD/PFH of the SIF is the sum of the contributions from all three sub-systems:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}$$

$$\text{PFH}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFH}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFH}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFH}_{\text{Final Element}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU formula is **additive in the dangerous failure space** because the three sub-systems are in series from a safety perspective: any one failing ends the SIF.

## 2.2 Voting Notation Decoded

The IEC notation **MooN** (read as "M out of N") has strict meaning:

- **N** = total number of parallel channels.
- **M** = number of channels that must agree to (a) trip the process (low demand) or (b) keep the process running (high demand).
- The lowercase letters "oo" simply indicate "out of."

| Voting | Name | Trip Behavior | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** | Single channel | Single failure causes loss of safety | Non-critical trips |
| **1oo2** | One-out-of-two, single channel voted | Either channel can trip; one failure tolerated | High-SIL sensor sub-system |
| **2oo2** | Two-out-of-two | Both must trip; tolerant of *no* single dangerous failure | Fire & gas detectors |
| **1oo2D** | 1oo2 *with Diagnostic* | Like 1oo2 but with diagnostic that detects dangerous faults | SIL 3 sensor systems |
| **2oo3** | Two-out-of-three, best of three | Best of two agreements required; one dangerous failure tolerated and detected | Burner Management |
| **2oo4** | Two-out-of-four | Pairs voting; very high spurious-trip resistance | Reactor protection |

## 2.3 Why Redundancy? The Underlying Justification

A single non-redundant channel has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and a proof-test interval $T_i$. Its average unavailability (PFDavg) under a low-demand regime is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D\,T_i \quad \text{(assuming DC = 0)}$$

For SIL 2, the required PFDavg must lie between **10⁻⁷ ≤ PFDavg < 10⁻⁶** per IEC 61511. Plugging a typical $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h and $T_i = 8760$ h (one year):

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2\times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \approx 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

This is **four orders of magnitude worse** than SIL 2. Redundancy, shorter proof tests, and diagnostics are the only engineering levers to bring this number into compliance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe character) when writing absolute values in tables.

| # | Architecture | Operating Mode | PFDavg Formula (low demand) | PFH Formula (high demand) | HFT | Diagnostic Trap |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **1oo1** | Single | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D\,T_i$ | $\lambda_D$ | 0 | Any dangerous fault = loss of safety |
| 2 | **1oo2** | Low-demand | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D^{\,2}\,T_i^{\,2}$ | $2\lambda_D^{\,2}\,T_i + \dots$ | 1 | Dangerous fault in *both* needed |
| 3 | **2oo2** | Low-demand | $\lambda_D\,T_i$ | $2\lambda_D$ | 0 | Higher PFD than 1oo1; no redundancy benefit |
| 4 | **1oo2D** | Low-demand | $\tfrac{1}{2}\,\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_i^{\,2} + \beta\,\lambda_{DU}\,\tfrac{T_i}{2}$ | $\beta\,\lambda_{DU}$ | 1 | Tolerates *one* dangerous failure |
| 5 | **2oo3** | Low-demand | $6(\lambda_{DU}\,T_i)^2$ (approx, DC=0) | $6\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_i$ | 1 | Best spurious-trip vs. PFD compromise |
| 6 | **2oo4** | Low-demand | (depends on grouping) | $\approx 4\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_i$ | 1 | Very low spurious trip rate |

**Common Cause Failure (CCF) Factor** — $\beta$ (beta) and $\beta_D$:

| Factor | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | Fraction of failures common to all redundant channels | 0.05 – 0.10 (scoreable per IEC 61511) |
| $\beta_D$ | Fraction common & *dangerous* | 0.01 – 0.05 |
| $\lambda_{DU}$ | Dangerous Undetected failure rate per channel | $5\times 10^{-7}$/h typical |
| $\lambda_{DD}$ | Dangerous Detected failure rate per channel | $\lambda_D \times DC$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (IEC 61511)** — SIF redundancy decisions on HIPPS (High Integrity Pressure Protection Systems) and ESD (Emergency Shutdown) systems.
- **Burner Management Systems (BMS)** — 2oo3 flame scanners and pressure transmitters protect furnace explosions.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262, derived from IEC 61508)** — Redundant braking, steering torque, and airbag sensors.
- **Nuclear (IEC 61513)** — Reactor trip systems rely on 2oo4 or 2-out-of-3-coincident logic.
- **Process Reactors** — Over-temperature and over-pressure SIFs in exothermic reactors typically require SIL 2–3 with 2oo3 sensor redundancy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFDavg for a Single Non-Redundant Channel (1oo1)

The foundation on which all redundancy math rests.

**Step 1 — Define the failure density.**
Let $\lambda_D$ be the constant dangerous failure rate of the channel. The probability density of failing during an interval $t$ after a successful proof test is:

$$f(t) = \lambda_D\,e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D \quad \text{(since } \lambda_D t \ll 1\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the average unavailability on demand.**
Suppose the channel is tested at interval $T_i$. At any instant $t$ after the test, the *fraction of the interval during which the channel has been failed* is, on average, $\tfrac{t}{T_i}$.

**Step 3 — Integrate over the test interval.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} \lambda_D\,t\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_i}\left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{T_i} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_i}\cdot\frac{T_i^{\,2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Final simplified result.**

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1, avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\lambda_D\,T_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *baseline*. Every redundancy formula in Section 3 reduces (or attempts to reduce) this number by orders of magnitude.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo2 System

In **1oo2**, the safety action is initiated if *either* channel trips (OR-logic for trip). The system fails on demand only if **both** channels have a dangerous undetected failure simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Express the joint failure probability.**
For two independent channels A and B with dangerous undetected rates $\lambda_{DU_A}$ and $\lambda_{DU_B}$, the joint failure density is:

$$P_{AB}(t) \approx \lambda_{DU_A}\lambda_{DU_B}\,t \quad \text{(second-order, small failure rates)}$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof-test interval.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2, avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i}\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,t\,dt = \lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,\frac{T_i}{2}$$

**Step 3 — General form (with separate $\lambda_{DU}$ values):**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2, avg}} = \frac{T_i^{\,2}}{3}\,(\lambda_{DU_1}\lambda_{DU_2} + \lambda_{DU_1}\lambda_{DU_3} + \lambda_{DU_2}\lambda_{DU_3}) \quad \text{(in 1oo3 generalization)}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with 1oo1.**
With identical channels and $T_i = 8760$ h, $\lambda_{DU} = 5\times 10^{-7}$/h:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\times 5\times 10^{-7}\times 8760 \approx 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = (5\times 10^{-7})^2\times \tfrac{8760}{2} \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-9}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1oo2 is approximately **six orders of magnitude safer** than 1oo1 — but it has a much *higher* spurious-trip rate because any single safe failure trips the process.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo2D System

The "D" in **1oo2D** stands for *Diagnostic*. The diagnostic auto-tests the channel and converts dangerous failures into detected failures ($\lambda_{DD} = DC \times \lambda_D$). The mathematical model is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D, avg}} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}T_i^{\,2}}_{\text{independent failures}} + \underbrace{\beta_{D}\,\lambda_{DU}\,\tfrac{T_i}{2}}_{\text{common-cause failures}}$$

**Step-by-step construction:**

**Step 1 — Independent dangerous undetected failures.**
Same as 1oo2 derivation, but now applied only to the *undetected* portion of dangerous failures ($\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D(1-DC)$).

**Step 2 — Common-cause dangerous failure contribution.**
A fraction $\beta_D$ of dangerous failures affects *both* channels simultaneously (e.g., shared power supply, shared calibration error, shared environmental stress). This reduces the redundancy to an effective single channel.

**Step 3 — Combine with the standard $\beta$-factor model.**

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D, avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}T_i^{\,2} + \beta_{D}\,\lambda_{DU}\,\tfrac{T_i}{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> A frequent KTU pitfall: students forget the $\beta_D$ term and obtain an over-optimistic PFD that can be *less than physically achievable*. Always include CCF in the exam answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PFDavg for a 2oo3 System

In **2oo3**, the safety action fires when at least two of the three channels agree to trip. The SIF fails only when two or more channels have dangerous undetected failures.

**Step 1 — Enumerate failure cases.**
With three channels, the dangerous undetected combinations are:
- Channels 1 & 2 fail
- Channels 1 & 3 fail
- Channels 2 & 3 fail

**Step 2 — Sum the joint probabilities (identical channels).**

$$P_{\text{fail}}(t) = 3\,\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,t \quad \text{(order-2 approximation)}$$

**Step 3 — Average over $T_i$.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3, avg}} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i} 3\,\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,t\,dt = 3\,\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,\frac{T_i}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2}\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}T_i$$

**Step 4 — Refined formula with $\beta$ factor and DC.**

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3, avg}} \approx 6(\lambda_{DU}T_i)^2 + \beta_D\,\lambda_{DU}\,\tfrac{T_i}{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Numeric example.**
With $\lambda_{DU} = 1\times 10^{-6}$/h, $T_i = 8760$ h, $\beta_D = 0.05$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3, avg}} \approx 6(1\times 10^{-6}\times 8760)^2 + 0.05\times 1\times 10^{-6}\times \tfrac{8760}{2}$$

$$= 6(8.76\times 10^{-3})^2 + 2.19\times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 6\times 7.67\times 10^{-5} + 2.19\times 10^{-4} \approx 4.6\times 10^{-4} + 2.19\times 10^{-4} \approx 6.8\times 10^{-4}$$

This is borderline for SIL 1, requiring either better $\lambda_{DU}$ or shorter $T_i$.

---

## 3.5 High-Demand Mode (PFH) Derivations

For continuous or high-demand mode (PFH in failures/hour), the time-dependent integral is replaced by the limit of the time-to-failure density times the failure rate:

| Architecture | PFH Formula |
|---|---|
| **1oo1** | $\lambda_{D}$ |
| **1oo2** | $2\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_i + \beta_{D}\lambda_{DU}$ |
| **1oo2D** | $\beta_{D}\lambda_{DU}$ (ideal diagnostic) |
| **2oo2** | $2\lambda_{DU}$ |
| **2oo3** | $6\lambda_{DU}^{\,2}\,T_i + \beta_{D}\lambda_{DU}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from PFDavg to PFH is a frequent KTU Part-A question. The key distinction: PFDavg is *dimensionless probability*, PFH has units of **per hour**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Architecture

| Engineering Goal | Best Architecture | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest PFDavg | 1oo2D | Two independent channels + diagnostic |
| Lowest spurious trip | 2oo3 or 2oo4 | Voting tolerates single-channel failure |
| Lowest hardware cost | 1oo1 | Single channel only |
| SIL 3 with high availability | 2oo3 | Fault tolerance 1, low spurious trip |
| SIL 3 in continuous mode | 1oo2D | $\beta_D\lambda_{DU}$ can hit SIL 3 |
| Fire & gas detection | 2oo2 (voting for "no fire") | Coincident logic avoids single-spurious alarm |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A SIL 2 ESD valve SIF uses 2oo3 pressure transmitters with identical $\lambda_{DU} = 5\times 10^{-7}$/h, $T_i = 4380$ h (6 months), $\beta_D = 0.07$. Compute PFDavg and identify the achieved SIL.

**Solution:**

$$(\lambda_{DU}T_i)^2 = (5\times 10^{-7}\times 4380)^2 = (2.19\times 10^{-3})^2 = 4.796\times 10^{-6}$$

$$\text{Independent term} = 6\times 4.796\times 10^{-6} = 2.878\times 10^{-5}$$

$$\text{CCF term} = 0.07\times 5\times 10^{-7}\times \tfrac{4380}{2} = 0.07\times 1.095\times 10^{-3} = 7.665\times 10^{-5}$$

$$\text{PFDavg} = 2.878\times 10^{-5} + 7.665\times 10^{-5} = 1.054\times 10^{-4}$$

**SIL classification (IEC 61511):**

| SIL | PFDavg Range |
|---|---|
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |

$\text{PFDavg} = 1.05\times 10^{-4}$ → **SIL 3 achieved** ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a 1oo2 Sensor Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Variable] --> TX1[Pressure TX Channel A]
    P --> TX2[Pressure TX Channel B]
    TX1 --> OR{1oo2 OR Logic}
    TX2 --> OR
    OR --> PLC[Safety PLC Logic Solver]
    PLC --> V[Shutdown Valve Final Element]

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000;
    classDef logic fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000;
    classDef final fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000;
    class TX1,TX2 sensor;
    class PLC logic;
    class V final;
```

**Reading the diagram:** Either TX-A *or* TX-B can independently initiate a trip. The SIF fails on demand only if *both* channels suffer a dangerous undetected failure.

## 4.2 2oo3 Voting Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[Process Variable] --> SA[Sensor A]
    PV --> SB[Sensor B]
    PV --> SC[Sensor C]
    SA --> VOTE{2oo3 Voter}
    SB --> VOTE
    SC --> VOTE
    VOTE --> SOL[Safety PLC]
    SOL --> FV[Final Valve]

    style VOTE fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style SOL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style FV fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two of the three sensors must "agree to trip" before the SIF activates the final element. This voting architecture balances **low PFD** with **low spurious trip rate**.

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Selecting Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Target SIL] --> B{Low Spurious Trip Required?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Target SIL Level?}
    B -- No --> D[Use 1oo2D]
    C -- SIL 1 or 2 --> E[Use 2oo3]
    C -- SIL 3 --> F[Use 2oo4 or 2oo3 with short proof test]
    D --> G[Validate via PFH calc]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[SIL Target Achieved?]
    H -- Yes --> I[Issue Documentation]
    H -- No --> J[Improve DC or shorten Ti]
    J --> G
```

## 4.4 Redundancy Stack Across the Three Sub-systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR[Sensor Sub-system]
        S1[Channel A] --- S2[Channel B]
        S2 --- S3[Optional Channel C]
    end
    SENSOR --> L[Logic Solver PLC]
    subgraph LOGIC[Logic Solver Sub-system]
        L1[CPU A] --- L2[CPU B]
    end
    LOGIC --> F[Final Element]
    subgraph FINAL[Final Element Sub-system]
        F1[Valve A] --- F2[Valve B]
    end
    style SENSOR fill:#cfe2ff
    style LOGIC fill:#fff3cd
    style FINAL fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.5 Spurious-Trip vs PFD Trade-off Chart (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRIP_HIGH[High Spurious Risk]
        X1[1oo2]
        X2[1oo2D]
    end
    subgraph TRIP_MED[Balanced]
        Y1[2oo3]
        Y2[2oo4]
    end
    subgraph TRIP_LOW[Low Spurious Risk]
        Z1[2oo2]
    end
    TRIP_HIGH --> TRIP_MED --> TRIP_LOW
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define SIF redundancy and explain its necessity in functional safety.**

**Model Answer:**
A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is the complete set of equipment (sensor → logic solver → final element) that performs a single safety action. **SIF redundancy** is the deliberate duplication of channels within one or more sub-systems so that the loss of any single channel does not result in loss of the safety function. It is necessary because a single non-redundant channel has a PFDavg that is several orders of magnitude worse than the SIL-2 or SIL-3 targets mandated by IEC 61511/61508. Redundancy, combined with diagnostics and proof testing, brings the PFDavg into the safe-integrity range.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between 1oo2 and 2oo2 voting architectures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 1oo2 | 2oo2 |
|---|---|---|
| Trip action | Trip if either channel demands it | Trip only if *both* channels demand it |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance | 1 | 0 |
| PFDavg | Very low (~ $\tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i$) | High (~ $\lambda_D T_i$) |
| Spurious trip rate | High | Low |
| Use case | SIL 3 sensor sub-system | Fire & gas detection, where coincidence is required |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the structural redundancy of 1oo2D and 2oo3 sensor sub-systems. Highlight the role of diagnostics and CCF.

**Model Solution:**

The **1oo2D** architecture (one-out-of-two with diagnostics) consists of two parallel channels, each equipped with an automatic self-tester that detects dangerous faults and forces a trip or alarm. The OR-vote means *either* channel can demand a trip. Because diagnostic coverage (DC) converts dangerous-undetected failures ($\lambda_{DU}$) into detected failures ($\lambda_{DD}$), the PFDavg is reduced to the joint-independent failure probability plus a $\beta$-factor CCF term:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D, avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2 + \beta_D \lambda_{DU} \tfrac{T_i}{2}$$

The **2oo3** architecture consists of three independent channels, with a 2-out-of-3 voter. The SIF trips only when at least two channels agree. The system tolerates any single dangerous failure (HFT = 1) without loss of the safety function, and the third channel maintains availability:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{2oo3, avg}} \approx 6(\lambda_{DU}T_i)^2 + \beta_D \lambda_{DU} \tfrac{T_i}{2}$$

> **Role of Diagnostics:** Diagnostics reduce $\lambda_{DU}$ to $\lambda_D(1-DC)$, lowering the squared term exponentially.
> **Role of CCF ($\beta$):** Common-cause failures (shared power, environment, calibration) defeat independence; the $\beta$-factor models this loss.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SIF uses 2oo3 pressure transmitters with $\lambda_{DU} = 2\times 10^{-7}$/h, $\lambda_{DD} = 1\times 10^{-7}$/h, $T_i = 4380$ h, $\beta_D = 0.05$, and DC = 0.6. Compute PFDavg and determine the achieved SIL.

**Model Solution:**

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD} = 2\times 10^{-7} + 1\times 10^{-7} = 3\times 10^{-7}\text{/h}$$

Check: $DC = \lambda_{DD}/\lambda_D = 1\times 10^{-7}/3\times 10^{-7} = 0.333$. Since the problem states DC = 0.6, we use it to recompute:

$$\lambda_{DU, \text{eff}} = \lambda_D(1-DC) = 3\times 10^{-7}\times 0.4 = 1.2\times 10^{-7}\text{/h}$$

**Independent failure term:**

$$6(\lambda_{DU, \text{eff}}\,T_i)^2 = 6(1.2\times 10^{-7}\times 4380)^2 = 6(5.256\times 10^{-4})^2 = 6\times 2.762\times 10^{-7} = 1.657\times 10^{-6}$$

**Common-cause term:**

$$\beta_D\,\lambda_{DU, \text{eff}}\,\tfrac{T_i}{2} = 0.05\times 1.2\times 10^{-7}\times 2190 = 1.314\times 10^{-5}$$

**Total:**

$$\text{PFDavg} = 1.657\times 10^{-6} + 1.314\times 10^{-5} = 1.480\times 10^{-5}$$

This places the SIF between **$10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$**, hence **SIL 3** is achieved.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the PFDavg formula for a 1oo2D architecture from first principles. Clearly state the role of $\beta_D$ and DC.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify failure categories. Each channel has dangerous failures of two types: *dangerous detected* ($\lambda_{DD}$, eliminated by diagnostic within proof-test interval) and *dangerous undetected* ($\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D(1-DC)$, only caught at proof test).

**Step 2:** Express the SIF failure on demand. The 1oo2 OR-vote requires *both* channels to be dangerously failed AND undetected:

$$\text{PFD}(t) \approx \lambda_{DU}^2\,t^2 \quad \text{(two independent channels failing in } [0,t]\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Account for common-cause failures. A fraction $\beta_D$ of dangerous failures hits *both* channels simultaneously, behaving like a single channel:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{CCF}}(t) = \beta_D \lambda_D\,t$$

**Step 4:** Combine and average over the proof-test interval.

$$\text{PFDavg} = \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{T_i}\!\bigl(\lambda_{DU}^2 t^2 + \beta_D \lambda_D t\bigr)\,dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{T_i}\!\left[\frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^3}{3} + \frac{\beta_D \lambda_D T_i^2}{2}\right]$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{3}\lambda_{DU}^2 T_i^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta_D \lambda_D T_i$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_D(1-DC)$ if required.

**Role of $\beta_D$:** Models CCF — failures defeating redundancy (shared power supply, common environment, identical design). Without $\beta_D$, the redundancy is over-idealized.
**Role of DC:** Diagnostic Coverage fraction; higher DC lowers $\lambda_{DU}$ and exponentially improves PFDavg.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the spurious-trip behaviour and PFDavg of 1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3 architectures using a comparative table and a labelled diagram. State one industrial application of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Architecture | Trip Logic | HFT | PFDavg | Spurious Trip | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1oo2** | OR | 1 | Very low | **High** | SIL 3 sensor sub-system in ESD |
| **2oo2** | AND | 0 | Higher than 1oo1 | **Low** | Fire & gas detector voting |
| **2oo3** | 2-OR-of-3 | 1 | Very low | Medium-low | Burner Management Systems, HIPPS |

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph X1[1oo2]
        X1a[Sensor A] --> X1V{OR Voter}
        X1b[Sensor B] --> X1V
        X1V --> X1T[Trip Output]
    end
    subgraph X2[2oo2]
        X2a[Sensor A] --> X2V{AND Voter}
        X2b[Sensor B] --> X2V
        X2V --> X2T[Trip Output]
    end
    subgraph X3[2oo3]
        X3a[Sensor A] --> X3V{2oo3 Voter}
        X3b[Sensor B] --> X3V
        X3c[Sensor C] --> X3V
        X3V --> X3T[Trip Output]
    end
```

**Inference:** 1oo2 gives the best PFDavg but doubles the spurious-trip probability. 2oo2 suppresses spurious trips but offers no HFT for dangerous failures. 2oo3 strikes a balance — the *voting principle* in critical process industries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not omit the $\beta_D$ term** in any redundancy formula — examiners specifically check whether you remember the common-cause failure contribution.
> 2. **Do not confuse HFT with redundancy factor.** A 2oo2 system has N=2 channels but HFT=0, because a single dangerous failure defeats the AND vote.
> 3. **Do not claim 2oo2 has lower PFD than 1oo1** — the formula $\lambda_D T_i$ is *worse*, not better.
> 4. **Always state units** in PFH answers (per hour); marks are lost for stating a bare number.
> 5. **Distinguish PFDavg (dimensionless) from PFH (per hour)** clearly in tabular answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **SIF** is sensor + logic solver + final element; redundancy is applied **within each sub-system**.
- **MooN** notation: N parallel channels, M needed to agree for trip.
- **HFT = N − M** for most architectures.
- **1oo1**: HFT 0, lowest cost, lowest safety.
- **1oo2**: HFT 1, best PFDavg, *high* spurious trips.
- **2oo2**: HFT 0, *worst* PFD, low spurious trips (used for fire/gas coincidence).
- **1oo2D**: HFT 1 with diagnostic — best for SIL 3 in continuous/high-demand mode.
- **2oo3**: HFT 1, balanced PFD + spurious — Burner Management & HIPPS.
- **2oo4**: HFT 1, two pairs voting — large reactor protection.
- $\beta$ and $\beta_D$ must always appear in PFD formulas; never ignore CCF.
- DC converts $\lambda_D$ → $\lambda_{DU}(1-DC)$, exponentially improving PFDavg.
- PFDavg is dimensionless; PFH has units of /h.
- SIL achieved by PFDavg: SIL1 (10⁻²–10⁻¹), SIL2 (10⁻³–10⁻²), SIL3 (10⁻⁴–10⁻³), SIL4 (10⁻⁵–10⁻⁴).
- Shortening proof-test interval $T_i$ linearly improves PFDavg (1oo1) and quadratically improves PFDavg (1oo2, 1oo2D, 2oo3).
- KTU loves the comparison "1oo2 vs 2oo2 vs 2oo3" — memorize the table.
- Always conclude with "achieved SIL" by mapping PFDavg against the IEC 61511 table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workshop Reliability and Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip, Design and Realization of SIS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Workshop Reliability and Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip, Design and Realization of SIS

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Workshop Reliability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workshop Reliability** is defined as the probability that a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) will perform its required safety function successfully under the stated conditions within an industrial workshop/process environment, taking into account **hardware failures**, **software integrity**, **human factors**, and **environmental stressors**.

Mathematically, it is the complement of the sum of all probabilities of dangerous failures combined with operational downtimes:

$$
R_{workshop}(t) = 1 - \left[ P_{dangerous\_failure}(t) + P_{downtime}(t) \right]
$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a workshop reliability study like a **fire alarm inspection service**. The alarm sitting in a box on a shelf (manufacturer reliability) is one thing, but once it is installed in a real, dusty, hot, vibration-prone workshop where operators may forget to test it — its true reliability in the field is quite different. Workshop reliability captures the *real-world* performance under actual operating stresses, maintenance routines, and operator behavior.

### Key Distinctions in Reliability Terminology

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | Average time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system | hours |
| **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure) | Average time to first failure of a non-repairable component | hours |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | Average time to restore a system after a failure | hours |
| **λ (Lambda)** | Failure rate of a component per unit time | failures/hour (FIT) |
| **PFH / PFD** | Probability of Failure per Hour / on Demand | dimensionless |
| **R(t)** | Reliability at time $t$ | dimensionless |

### Physical & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)
- **1 FIT (Failures In Time) = 1 failure per 10⁹ device-hours**
- **IEC 61511** is the governing standard for SIS in the process industry sector.
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** ranges from **SIL 1 to SIL 4** (SIL 4 is the highest, mostly used in nuclear/aviation).

---

## 1.2 Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Spurious Trip** (also called a *nuisance trip* or *false trip*) is an unintended activation of the safety instrumented function (SIF) when the process is actually operating within safe limits. The **Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip Rate (ASTR)** is the maximum tolerable rate at which such unwanted trips are permitted to occur, balancing safety against plant availability.

The spurious trip rate is conventionally expressed as:

$$
STR = \frac{1}{MTTF_{spurious}} \quad [\text{trips per year}]
$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **smoke detector at home** going off every time you make toast. The detector is *technically working* (high safety integrity), but the nuisance trips make you want to disconnect it — increasing real danger. Spurious trips in a process plant are identical: they shut down a multi-million dollar furnace or refinery unit, costing huge production losses, possibly encouraging operators to *bypass* the safety system, ironically reducing safety.

### Why Spurious Trips Matter
- **Direct Cost:** Lost production, wasted raw materials, restart costs.
- **Indirect Cost:** Equipment wear, plant stress, environmental release of partially processed materials.
- **Human Cost:** Operator frustration leading to **bypassing**, defeating the very purpose of the SIS.
- **Reputation:** Insurance premium increases, regulatory scrutiny.

### Quantitative Acceptance Thresholds (Industry Typical)

| Industry Sector | Acceptable Spurious Trip Rate |
| :--- | :--- |
| Oil & Gas (Refinery) | 1 spurious trip per **10 to 50 years** |
| Chemical Plants | 1 spurious trip per **5 to 20 years** |
| Power Generation | 1 spurious trip per **20 to 100 years** |
| Pharmaceutical | 1 spurious trip per **2 to 10 years** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are **typical industry benchmarks**, not KTU-mandated values. Actual acceptable rates must be derived from a **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** study performed on the specific process unit.

---

## 1.3 Design and Realization of SIS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Design and Realization of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** refers to the complete engineering lifecycle — from conceptual safety requirement definition, through detailed hardware/software design, integration, validation, installation, commissioning, and final hand-over to operations — as specified in **IEC 61511 clauses 5 through 16**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Designing an SIS is like **engineering a car's airbag system**. You cannot just bolt an airbag onto a steering wheel — you must specify the crash sensor, the deployment algorithm, the inflation mechanism, install it correctly, test it under crash conditions, then ensure it works reliably for the next 15 years. Every step is documented, verified, and validated. The SIS lifecycle follows exactly this discipline.

### SIS Functional Architecture (3-Subsystem View)
1. **Sensor Subsystem** — Detects the hazardous condition (e.g., pressure, temperature, flow).
2. **Logic Solver Subsystem** — Processes sensor inputs and decides on the protective action (e.g., PLC, PES, Trip Amplifier).
3. **Final Element Subsystem** — Implements the protective action (e.g., shutdown valve, vent, interlock relay).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Workshop Reliability — Theoretical Framework

Workshop reliability is built upon four interdependent pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Hardware Failure Behavior
Components fail according to the well-known **Bathtub Curve**:
1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** High but decreasing failure rate.
2. **Useful Life (Random Failures):** Approximately constant failure rate $\lambda$.
3. **Wear-out Phase:** Increasing failure rate due to aging.

For a constant failure rate regime, reliability follows the **exponential distribution**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Pillar 2 — Software Integrity
Unlike hardware, software does not "fail randomly." It either contains a **systematic fault** (specification, design, or coding error) or it does not. Software reliability is achieved through:
- **Formal methods** (e.g., CCF analysis, FMEA on code paths).
- **Rigorous testing** (unit, integration, SIL-qualification testing).
- **Diversity & redundancy** (N-version programming, dissimilar logic solvers).
- **Coding standards** (IEC 61131-3, MISRA-C).

### Pillar 3 — Environmental & Operational Stressors
Workshop reliability must account for:
- **Temperature, humidity, vibration, dust, EMI/RFI.**
- **Power supply quality** (sags, surges, brown-outs).
- **Process medium effects** (corrosion, fouling, plugging of impulse lines).
- **Operator competency and human intervention frequency.**

### Pillar 4 — Maintenance Philosophy
- **Proof Test Interval (PTI):** The periodic manual test that reveals hidden/dangerous undetected failures.
- **Coverage Factor (C):** The fraction of dangerous failures actually revealed by the proof test (typically $C = 0.5$ to $1.0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof testing is the single most powerful lever** to improve workshop PFD without changing hardware SIL. Reducing PTI from 5 years to 1 year can drop PFDavg by an order of magnitude.

---

## 2.2 Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip — Theoretical Analysis

### Failure Rate Decomposition

Every component has **two failure paths** in an SIS:

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{safe} + \lambda_{dangerous}
$$

- **Safe Failure (λs):** Causes a spurious trip (over-reaction). Detected by diagnostic coverage usually.
- **Dangerous Failure (λd):** Causes the SIS to fail to act when needed. Hidden until proof test.

The **Spurious Trip Rate (STR)** for a 1oo1 (one-out-of-one) configuration is:

$$
STR_{1oo1} = \lambda_{safe} + \lambda_{dd\_diagnosed}
$$

For a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted system**, both channels must fail safe simultaneously:

$$
STR_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{safe}^2 \cdot t \quad \text{(approximation)}
$$

### Trade-off: Safety vs Availability

| Architecture | PFD (Safety) | STR (Availability) | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** | High (poor) | Low (good) | Non-critical trips |
| **1oo2** | Moderate | Higher (poor) | Safety-critical, low-spurious priority |
| **2oo2** | High (poor) | Low (good) | High-spurious-cost environments |
| **2oo3** | Best | Moderate | Highest integrity, mid cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **inverse relationship**: architectures that improve dangerous-failure tolerance typically increase spurious trip risk, and vice versa. This is the central engineering trade-off in SIS design.

---

## 2.3 SIS Design and Realization — Theoretical Framework

### Phase-Wise SIS Lifecycle (IEC 61511 Compliance)

| Phase | Clause | Key Deliverable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Concept & Scope** | Clause 5 | Safety Life Cycle definition |
| **2. Hazard & Risk Analysis** | Clause 6 | PHA, HAZOP, LOPA reports |
| **3. Allocation of SIFs to SIL** | Clause 7 | SIL Target table |
| **4. SIS Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** | Clause 8 | Formal SRS document |
| **5. SIS Detailed Engineering & Design** | Clause 9 | Hardware design package |
| **6. SIS Installation** | Clause 10 | Installation records |
| **7. SIS Commissioning** | Clause 11 | SAT report |
| **8. SIS Validation** | Clause 12 | Validation report proving SIL achieved |
| **9. Operation, Maintenance & Modification** | Clauses 13–16 | Procedures, audit reports |

### Detailed Engineering — Subsystem Design Rules

#### A. Sensor Subsystem
- **Selection:** Choose sensors whose **range, accuracy, and failure mode** match the safety function.
- **Redundancy:** Use 1oo2 or 2oo3 voting for higher SIL.
- **Diagnostic:** Use SMART sensors with continuous self-diagnostics (HART, FOUNDATION Fieldbus).
- **Separation:** Physical separation to defeat **Common Cause Failures (CCF)**.

#### B. Logic Solver Subsystem
- **Certification:** Must be **SIL-certified** by TÜV/TÜV-Süd/Exida/Factory Mutual.
- **Type:** Relay-based trip amplifier, Safety PLC (PES), or hybrid logic.
- **Programming:** Use certified function block libraries; avoid custom ladder logic where possible.
- **Determinism:** Deterministic execution cycle time verified for worst-case response.

#### C. Final Element Subsystem
- **Failure Mode Selection:** **Fail-closed** valves for over-pressure protection; **fail-open** for cooling water.
- **Actuator:** Spring-return pneumatic preferred for guaranteed energy-independent movement.
- **Position Feedback:** Critical for partial-stroke testing.
- **Proof Testing:** Provision for in-line partial stroke test (PST) capability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the formulas in this table. They appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (exponential model) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate from MTBF | failures/hr |
| 3 | $PFD_{avg} \approx \frac{\lambda_d \cdot T_i}{2}$ | Average PFD for 1oo1, low demand | dimensionless |
| 4 | $PFD_{avg,1oo2} \approx \frac{\lambda_d^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3}$ | PFD for 1oo2 voted architecture | dimensionless |
| 5 | $PFD_{avg,2oo3} \approx \frac{6\lambda_d^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3}$ | PFD for 2oo3 voted architecture | dimensionless |
| 6 | $RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $STR_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_s$ | Spurious trip rate, 1oo1 | trips/hr |
| 8 | $STR_{1oo2} \approx 2\lambda_s^2 \cdot t$ | Spurious trip rate, 1oo2 | trips/hr |
| 9 | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_s + \lambda_d$ | Failure rate decomposition | failures/hr |
| 10 | $SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{dd}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | dimensionless |
| 11 | $PFD_{G} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - PFD_i)$ | PFD of entire SIF (sensor+logic+final) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $PFH_{avg} = 2\lambda_{DU}$ | PFH for 1oo1 architecture | failures/hr |
| 13 | $C = \frac{\text{Failures detected}}{\text{Total failures}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage factor | dimensionless |

Where:
- $T_i$ = Proof Test Interval
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = Dangerous Detected failure rate
- SFF = Safe Failure Fraction

### Real-World Utility
These formulas are used by **Functional Safety Engineers** worldwide in **HAZOP/LOPA teams**, **SIL determination workshops**, and **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Verification** studies per IEC 61511. Mastery is essential for employment in **Oil & Gas, Petrochemical, Power Generation, and Pharmaceutical** sectors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFD_avg for a 1oo1 Architecture (Proof Test Model)

This is the **most important derivation** in functional safety, and a guaranteed KTU exam favorite.

### Step 1: Define the System

Consider a **single-channel (1oo1) SIS** with:
- Constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_d$.
- Proof test interval $T_i$.
- All dangerous failures are **undetected** until proof test.
- No automatic diagnostics.

### Step 2: Probability of Being Failed at Time t

The probability that the system has experienced a dangerous failure by time $t$, but it has not yet been revealed by the proof test, is:

$$
P_{failed}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_d t}
$$

### Step 3: Average the Probability Over the Proof Test Interval

The average unavailability (PFDavg) is the time-averaged probability over one full proof test cycle $[0, T_i]$:

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left( 1 - e^{-\lambda_d t} \right) dt
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_i} 1 \, dt - \int_{0}^{T_i} e^{-\lambda_d t} \, dt \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda_d} e^{-\lambda_d t} \Big\vert_{0}^{T_i} \right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i + \frac{1}{\lambda_d} \left( e^{-\lambda_d T_i} - 1 \right) \right] \\
&= 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_d T_i} \left( e^{-\lambda_d T_i} - 1 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Simplify Using Taylor Series Expansion

For small values of $\lambda_d T_i$ (the realistic case — failure rate × test interval is much less than 1), we use the Taylor series:

$$
e^{-\lambda_d T_i} \approx 1 - \lambda_d T_i + \frac{(\lambda_d T_i)^2}{2} - \frac{(\lambda_d T_i)^3}{6} + \cdots
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &\approx 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_d T_i} \left( \left[ 1 - \lambda_d T_i + \frac{(\lambda_d T_i)^2}{2} \right] - 1 \right) \\
&= 1 + \frac{1}{\lambda_d T_i} \left( -\lambda_d T_i + \frac{(\lambda_d T_i)^2}{2} \right) \\
&= 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda_d T_i}{2} \\
&= \frac{\lambda_d T_i}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Final Result

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg,\,1oo1} \approx \frac{\lambda_d \cdot T_i}{2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the celebrated **"lambda-T over 2"** formula. It tells us that PFD grows linearly with both the failure rate and the proof test interval. Halve $T_i$, halve the PFD.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) and Safety Integrity Level (SIL)

The Risk Reduction Factor is mathematically defined as:

$$
RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}
$$

Since $PFD_{avg}$ is dimensionless, RRF is also dimensionless. It tells us how much the SIS reduces the unmitigated risk.

The IEC 61511 standard defines SIL targets based on $PFD_{avg}$ ranges:

| SIL | $PFD_{avg}$ Range (Low Demand) | RRF Range | PFH Range (High Demand) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10 to 100 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ per hr |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100 to 1000 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ per hr |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 1000 to 10000 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ per hr |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10000 to 100000 | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ per hr |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Examination Style

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a 1oo1 SIS has $\lambda_d = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The proof test interval is $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year). The spurious failure rate is $\lambda_s = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. Calculate:
> (a) $PFD_{avg}$
> (b) The SIL achieved.
> (c) The expected Spurious Trip Rate (STR) per year.
> (d) If the workshop operates 24/7, what is the **mean time between spurious trips (MTBFspurious)**?

### Given Data
- $\lambda_d = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $\lambda_s = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T_i = 8760$ hours
- Architecture: 1oo1

### Part (a): PFD_avg

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{\lambda_d \cdot T_i}{2} \\
&= \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 8760}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.01752}{2} \\
&= 8.76 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b): SIL Determination

Comparing with the SIL table:
- $8.76 \times 10^{-3}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$.

Therefore, the SIL achieved is **SIL 2**.

### Part (c): Spurious Trip Rate (per year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{1oo1} &= \lambda_s \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to trips per year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{yearly} &= \lambda_s \times 8760 \text{ hours/year} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \\
&= 0.035 \text{ trips/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (d): MTBFspurious

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_{spurious} &= \frac{1}{STR_{yearly}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.035} \\
&\approx 28.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Interpretation: This single transmitter is expected to cause a spurious trip approximately every **28.5 years**, which is generally acceptable in oil & gas. If the workshop requires less than 1 spurious trip per 100 years, then a 1oo2 voted transmitter architecture must be used.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Architecture | $PFD_{avg}$ (this example) | STR (per year) | SIL | Spurious Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** | $8.76 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.035 | SIL 2 | Every ~28 years |
| **1oo2** | $\frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | $2 \cdot \lambda_s^2 \cdot t \cdot 8760$ — computed below | SIL 3 | Every ~3,300 years |
| **2oo2** | $\approx 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.070 (≈2× of 1oo1) | SIL 2 | Every ~14 years |

For the 1oo2 spurious trip rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{1oo2,\,yearly} &= 2 \cdot \lambda_s^2 \cdot T_i \\
&= 2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 8760 \\
&= 2 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 8760 \\
&= 2.80 \times 10^{-7} \text{ trips/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single comparison illustrates the central design trade-off: **moving from 1oo1 to 1oo2 dramatically improves safety (SIL 2 → SIL 3) while simultaneously making spurious trips extremely rare.** However, the cost roughly doubles and diagnostic complexity rises.

```mermaid

    PROCESS["Process Under Protection e.g. Reactor"]

    LOGIC --> FINAL1["Final Element 1 SDV"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Failure Rate Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L["Lambda Total Failure Rate"] --> LS["Lambda Safe Failures"]
    L --> LD["Lambda Dangerous Failures"]
    LD --> LDD["Lambda DD Detected by Diagnostics"]
    LD --> LDU["Lambda DU Undetected until Proof Test"]
    LS --> STR["Spurious Trip Rate"]
    LDD --> STR
    LDU --> PFD["Probability of Failure on Demand PFD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This diagram precisely captures the duality of failure rates in any SIS. Engineers must always design for *both* the dangerous failure path (governed by PFD) and the safe failure path (governed by STR). They are inversely coupled by architectural choice.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip" in the context of an SIS. Why is it a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Acceptable Level of Spurious Trip Rate (ASTR) is the maximum tolerable rate at which a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is permitted to produce an unintended / nuisance activation when the process is actually operating within safe limits.
>
> **Why Critical (2 Marks):**
> 1. Spurious trips cause production losses, equipment wear, and increased operating costs.
> 2. Frequent spurious trips create operator frustration and may lead to dangerous bypassing of the SIS — paradoxically *reducing* plant safety.
> 3. It governs the **availability** vs **safety trade-off** that defines the entire SIS architecture (1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo3 voting).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "Workshop Reliability" of an SIS? List any two factors that differentiate it from manufacturer-rated reliability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Workshop reliability is the probability that an SIS will perform its required safety function under actual field/installation conditions, encompassing hardware, software, environmental, and human factors over its installed lifetime.
>
> **Two Differentiating Factors (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Environmental Stresses:** Workshop reliability accounts for temperature, vibration, humidity, dust, and process medium effects, whereas manufacturer reliability is measured under controlled lab conditions.
> 2. **Human & Maintenance Factors:** Workshop reliability includes proof-test discipline, diagnostic coverage, and operator competency — all absent from manufacturer data sheets.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse PFD with PFH. **PFD** is for *low-demand* mode (demand rate < 1/year), while **PFH** is for *high-demand / continuous* mode (demand rate > 1/year). Mixing these up will cost you 4 marks minimum. Also, units must be in **failures/hour** consistently; do not mix FIT and per-year without conversion.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete IEC 61511 Safety Life Cycle** for the Design and Realization of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Discuss the deliverables of each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a 1oo2 voted pressure transmitter assembly, a SIL 3 certified Safety PLC logic solver, and a single spring-return shut-down valve. The following data is given:
- Each transmitter: $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr, $\lambda_S = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Logic Solver: $PFD_{LS} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
- Shut-down valve: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /hr, $\lambda_S = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Proof Test Interval $T_i = 4380$ hours (6 months)

Calculate:
(i) The $PFD_{avg}$ of the complete SIF.
(ii) The achieved Safety Integrity Level (SIL).
(iii) The Spurious Trip Rate per year of the complete SIF. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — IEC 61511 SIS Lifecycle (7 Marks)

The IEC 61511 Safety Life Cycle has **16 clauses** spread across **three broad phases**:

| Phase | Clauses | Key Activities |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Analysis Phase** | 1–7 | Concept, Scope, Hazard & Risk Analysis, Allocation of SIFs |
| **Realization Phase** | 8–12 | SRS, Design, Installation, Commissioning, Validation |
| **Operation Phase** | 13–16 | Operation, Maintenance, Modification, Decommissioning |

**Detailed Phase Description:**

1. **Clause 5 – Concept & Scope:** Define the boundary, project scope, and applicable codes.
2. **Clause 6 – Hazard & Risk Analysis:** Conduct PHA / HAZOP / What-If analysis to identify hazards.
3. **Clause 7 – Allocation of SIF to SIL:** Use Risk Graph, LOPA, or Calibrated Risk Matrix to assign SIL.
4. **Clause 8 – SIS Safety Requirements Specification (SRS):** Document all SIFs, their SILs, response times, proof-test requirements.
5. **Clause 9 – Detailed Engineering & Design:** Design sensor, logic solver, final element subsystems; specify redundancy.
6. **Clause 10 – Installation:** Install per design drawings; verify separation and segregation.
7. **Clause 11 – Commissioning:** Site Acceptance Testing (SAT); calibrate instruments; verify wiring.
8. **Clause 12 – Validation:** Demonstrate that the installed SIS meets the SRS and the SIL targets.
9. **Clauses 13–16 – Operation & Maintenance:** Establish proof-test procedures, training, audits, and modification management.

**Deliverables:** PHA report, LOPA report, SIL determination matrix, SRS document, hardware design package, SAT report, Validation report, Operating procedures, Proof-test records, Audit reports.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Analysis] → [Allocation] → [SRS] → [Design] → [Installation]
              ↑ Risk Graph / LOPA                              ↓
              ← [Operation & Maintenance] ← [Validation] ← [Commissioning]
```

---

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- 1oo2 transmitter: $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-7}$/hr, $\lambda_S = 6 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- Logic Solver: $PFD_{LS} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
- Shut-down valve: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/hr, $\lambda_S = 8 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $T_i = 4380$ hours

**Step (i): PFD_avg of Each Subsystem**

*Transmitter (1oo2 voted):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{TX,1oo2} &= \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_i^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{(3 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot (4380)^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{(9 \times 10^{-14}) \cdot (1.918 \times 10^{7})}{3} \\
&= \frac{1.726 \times 10^{-6}}{3} \\
&= 5.75 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic Solver (given):* $PFD_{LS} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$

*Shut-down Valve (1oo1):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{SDV} &= \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2} \\
&= \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 4380}{2} \\
&= \frac{2.19 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= 1.095 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Total SIF PFD (series combination):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{SIF} &= PFD_{TX} + PFD_{LS} + PFD_{SDV} \\
&= 5.75 \times 10^{-7} + 1.5 \times 10^{-4} + 1.095 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.245 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): SIL Determination**

Comparing $1.245 \times 10^{-3}$ with the SIL table: it lies between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2**.

**Step (iii): Spurious Trip Rate per Year**

*Transmitter (1oo2 voted) STR per hour:*

$$
STR_{TX,1oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_S^2 \cdot T_i = 2 \cdot (6 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 4380 = 3.15 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}
$$

*Logic Solver STR:* Typically negligible or taken from vendor data — assume $STR_{LS} \approx 1 \times 10^{-7}$/hr.

*SDV STR per hour:* $STR_{SDV} = \lambda_S = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr.

*Total STR per hour:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{SIF,\,hourly} &= STR_{TX} + STR_{LS} + STR_{SDV} \\
&= 3.15 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-7} + 8 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 8.42 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Total STR per year:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{SIF,\,yearly} &= 8.42 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \\
&= 7.38 \times 10^{-2} \text{ trips/year} \\
&\approx 1 \text{ spurious trip every } 13.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The SIF achieves SIL 2 with a spurious trip approximately every 13.5 years — generally acceptable for oil & gas but not for pharmaceutical-grade continuous processes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **trade-off between Safety Integrity and Availability** in SIS design. With suitable equations, compare 1oo1, 1oo2, and 2oo3 voting architectures in terms of PFD and STR. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature SIF uses a single RTD sensor with $\lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/hr and $\lambda_S = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr. The proof test interval is 4380 hours.
(i) Calculate the SIL achieved.
(ii) If the management mandates a maximum of 1 spurious trip per 50 years, determine whether the current 1oo1 architecture is acceptable.
(iii) If not, suggest and justify an alternative architecture. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Safety vs Availability Trade-off (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** In any SIS, **safety integrity** (low PFD) and **availability** (low STR) are typically inversely related. An architecture that reduces dangerous failures (improving safety) often increases safe failures (more spurious trips). The choice is dictated by the cost of downtime vs the cost of an accident.

**Comparative Table:**

| Architecture | PFD Equation | STR Equation | Safety Impact | Availability Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1oo1** | $\frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}$ | $\lambda_S$ | Poor | Best |
| **1oo2** | $\frac{\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{3}$ | $2\lambda_S^2 T_i$ | Best | Worst |
| **2oo3** | $\frac{6\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{3}$ | Moderate | Very Good | Moderate |

**1oo1 — Simplex:** Single channel. Simplest and cheapest. Either fail-safe (spurious trip) or fail-dangerous. No diagnostics between channels.

**1oo2 — Safety Optimized:** Both channels must fail to create a dangerous condition. Lowest PFD but highest STR — both channels independently failing safe cause trip.

**2oo2 — Availability Optimized:** Both channels must command trip to shut down. Worst PFD, lowest STR. Used in plants where restart costs are enormous.

**2oo3 — Compromised (Best Overall):** Three channels voted; two must trip. Used in nuclear and high-integrity applications.

**Design Rule:** Use 1oo1 for low-risk SIFs where spurious cost is high; 1oo2 for safety-critical low-demand; 2oo3 for highest-integrity continuous processes.

---

#### Part (b) — Architecture Suitability Analysis (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- 1oo1 RTD sensor
- $\lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $\lambda_S = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr
- $T_i = 4380$ hr

**Step (i): SIL Achieved**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,\,1oo1} &= \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2} \\
&= \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 4380}{2} \\
&= 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with the SIL table: $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$ → **SIL 2**.

**Step (ii): Spurious Trip Rate vs Mandate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
STR_{yearly} &= \lambda_S \times 8760 \\
&= (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 \\
&= 1.752 \times 10^{-2} \text{ trips/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mandate: max 1 trip per 50 years → $STR_{mandate} = 1/50 = 0.02$ trips/year $= 2 \times 10^{-2}$ trips/year.

Comparison: $1.752 \times 10^{-2} < 2 \times 10^{-2}$ — **just within mandate** (margin is too thin for safety buffer). **NOT acceptable** for robust engineering practice.

**Step (iii): Alternative Architecture Recommendation**

**Recommendation: Use a 2oo3 voted RTD assembly.**

Justification:
1. **PFD improvement:** $PFD_{2oo3} \approx \frac{6\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{3} = 6 \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (4380)^2 / 3 = 3.84 \times 10^{-5}$ → **SIL 3**.
2. **STR improvement:** Spurious trip requires two safe failures simultaneously → far less frequent.
3. **Diagnostics:** Mismatch between any two of three sensors triggers diagnostic alarm without spurious trip.
4. **CCF tolerance:** Three diverse sensors reduce common cause failure probability.
5. **Cost-benefit:** Marginal cost increase (one extra RTD) for order-of-magnitude safety improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert $\lambda$ from FIT to per-hour. **1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ per hour**, and **1 year = 8760 hours**. Always verify units before substituting into formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this and review before exams.**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Workshop Reliability** = Real-world SIS reliability accounting for hardware + software + environment + human factors.
- ☐ **Spurious Trip** = Unintended SIF activation; quantified by Spurious Trip Rate (STR).
- ☐ **Acceptable STR** is industry-driven: 1 per 10–100 years typical for process industries.
- ☐ **SIS** = Sensor + Logic Solver + Final Element — all three must be designed cohesively.

### Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- ☐ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- ☐ $PFD_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D T_i}{2}$
- ☐ $PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 T_i^2}{3}$
- ☐ $STR_{1oo1} = \lambda_S$
- ☐ $STR_{1oo2} = 2\lambda_S^2 T_i$
- ☐ $PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{logic} + PFD_{final}$

### Critical Numbers
- ☐ **1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour**
- ☐ **1 year = 8760 hours**
- ☐ **SIL 1:** $10^{-2} \geq PFD > 10^{-1}$
- ☐ **SIL 2:** $10^{-3} \geq PFD > 10^{-2}$
- ☐ **SIL 3:** $10^{-4} \geq PFD > 10^{-3}$

### Standards to Remember
- ☐ **IEC 61508** — Generic Functional Safety standard (electrical/electronic/programmable).
- ☐ **IEC 61511** — Process industry sector-specific application of IEC 61508.
- ☐ **IEC 61131-3** — PLC programming languages standard.

### Design Trade-offs
- ☐ Safety ↑ ⇔ Spurious Trip ↑ (typically)
- ☐ Proof Test Interval ↓ ⇔ PFD ↓ (always)
- ☐ Diagnostic Coverage ↑ ⇔ PFD ↓ AND STR ↓
- ☐ Redundancy ↑ ⇔ Cost ↑ AND Availability ↑ (with proper voting)

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Mixing PFD and PFH (low-demand vs high-demand)
- ☐ Forgetting to convert units (FIT vs per-hour)
- ☐ Confusing $PFD_{avg}$ with instantaneous PFD
- ☐ Adding PFHs instead of averaging them
- ☐ Not justifying architecture choice with both safety and availability metrics

### Recommended Memory Anchors
- ☐ **"Lambda-T over 2"** for 1oo1 — engraved in stone for SIS engineers.
- ☐ **"Square-law for redundancy"** — both PFD and STR scale as $\lambda^2$ in 1oo2.
- ☐ **"SRS is sacred"** — every SIF must have a documented Safety Requirements Specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always state the governing equation first, show substitution, compute intermediate results, and conclude with units and an interpretive sentence.** Examiners reward systematic working, not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIS Safety Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SIS Safety Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: SIS Safety Plan — Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — SIS Safety Plan**
> The **SIS Safety Plan** (also called the *Safety Lifecycle Plan* or *Safety Requirements Specification Implementation Plan*) is a formal, living document that defines **how a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) project will be executed** in compliance with the **IEC 61511 / IEC 61508** functional safety standards. It prescribes the scope, objectives, responsibilities, deliverables, procedures, schedules, tools, and resources required to bring a SIS from concept through operation, modification, and decommissioning.

The plan is the **master governing document** for every phase of the safety lifecycle. Without it, the project has no auditable, traceable roadmap — and **auditors (KTU board examiners and certification bodies) will treat the project as non-compliant.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Blueprint" of Functional Safety

> [!NOTE] **Intuition for Students**
> Think of the **SIS Safety Plan** as the **architectural blueprint + project management Gantt chart** for a hospital's emergency power system.
> - Before the electricians arrive, the hospital needs a written document that says: *"Who is in charge of installing the backup generator? What standards must the wiring meet? How will we TEST that the generator kicks in within 10 seconds of a power failure? What happens if a component fails during surgery?"*
> - That document is the **Safety Plan**. It does NOT contain the actual electrical schematic (that's the SRS or Logic Solver design). It contains the **rules of engagement, the checklist of tasks, the list of people responsible, and the proof of completion criteria**.
> - In the same way, the SIS Safety Plan tells the engineering team **"here is HOW we will design, install, operate, and decommission a safety system"** — but the **WHAT** (the actual safety functions, SIL targets, PFD values) is documented separately in the SRS.

### 1.3 Why the SIS Safety Plan is Mandatory in KTU 2024 Curriculum

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Standard Mandate** | IEC 61511 Clause **5.2.1.1** explicitly requires a Safety Lifecycle Plan |
| **Auditability** | Provides documentary evidence for TÜV / certification audits |
| **Risk Reduction Traceability** | Maps each phase back to the original Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) |
| **Knowledge Preservation** | Survives personnel turnover; institutional memory |
| **Legal Defense** | Serves as evidence of due diligence in incident investigations |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Frequently appears in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) |

### 1.4 Position of the Safety Plan in the Safety Lifecycle

The Safety Plan is initiated at **Phase 1 (Concept)** of the IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle and is **continuously updated** through every subsequent phase until decommissioning. It is the **only document that spans the entire lifecycle** — all other documents (PHA, SRS, validation reports) are deliverables of the plan.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3: SIS Safety Plan — Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Mandatory Contents of a Compliant SIS Safety Plan

According to **IEC 61511-1:2016, Clause 5.2.1.1**, a compliant Safety Plan **must** address the following structured elements:

### 2.1.1 Scope Definition
- **Physical boundary**: Which process unit, plant, or skid does the SIS cover?
- **Logical boundary**: Which hazards are addressed? Which are explicitly *out of scope*?
- **Time boundary**: Project start, commissioning date, operational handover.

> [!NOTE] A well-defined scope prevents "scope creep" — a frequent cause of project failure flagged by KTU examiners.

### 2.1.2 Objectives and Targets
- Target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** for each Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
- Target **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** per SIF.
- Target **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**.
- Target **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**.

### 2.1.3 Roles, Responsibilities, and Competency Requirements
A RACI matrix is mandatory, identifying:

$$
\text{RACI} = \{\text{Responsible},\ \text{Accountable},\ \text{Consulted},\ \text{Informed}\}
$$

Typical roles include: **Process Safety Engineer, Instrument Engineer, Operations Lead, Maintenance Lead, Project Manager, Independent Assessor, and Plant Manager**.

### 2.1.4 SIS Safety Lifecycle Strategy
The plan must describe how the organization will execute **all 11 phases** (in IEC 61511) or **16 phases** (in IEC 61508):

$$
