\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Implement} \rightarrow \text{Operate} \rightarrow \text{Maintain} \rightarrow \text{Modify} \rightarrow \text{Decommission}
$$

### 2.1.5 Tools, Techniques, and Methods
- **PHA methodology**: HAZOP, What-If, FMEA, LOPA
- **SIL determination tool**: Risk Graph, Calibrated Risk Graph, Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)
- **Reliability data sources**: OREDA, exida, IEC 61508 generic data
- **Verification & Validation methods**: Cause & Effect testing, Logic Solver simulation

### 2.1.6 Deliverables Register
| Phase | Document | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Safety Plan | This document |
| 2 | PHA Report | Hazard identification |
| 3 | SRS | Safety Requirements Specification |
| 4 | Design Documents | Architecture, SIL verification |
| 5 | Installation Plan | Mechanical & electrical installation |
| 6 | Validation Plan & Report | Proof of SIS fitness |
| 7–11 | Operating Procedures | Ongoing lifecycle management |

### 2.1.7 Verification, Validation, and Audit Procedures
The plan must define:
- **Verification**: *"Are we building the system right?"* (Design review, calculation check)
- **Validation**: *"Are we building the right system?"* (End-to-end functional test)

### 2.1.8 Management of Change (MOC) Procedures
- Any modification to the SIS triggers a **formal MOC process** as defined in the Safety Plan.
- Re-verification of SIL and re-validation are mandatory after MOC.

### 2.1.9 Documentation and Records Management
- Retention periods (typically **the operational life of the plant + 5 years**)
- Document control system, version control, electronic signatures
- Records location and access controls

## 2.2 The 11 Phases of IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield KTU Concept**
> The Safety Plan governs the execution of all 11 phases. Memorizing these phases is essential for KTU Part A questions.

| Phase | Name | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process / Hazard Analysis | PHA, risk register |
| 2 | Allocation of Safety Functions | SRS, SIL targets |
| 3 | SIS Design | Logic diagrams, SIL verification |
| 4 | Installation | Commissioned hardware |
| 5 | Validation | Validation report |
| 6 | Operation | Operating procedures |
| 7 | Maintenance | Proof test schedule |
| 8 | Modification | MOC records |
| 9 | Decommissioning | Safe shutdown plan |
| 10 | Verification (cross-cutting) | Independent review |
| 11 | Assessment/Audit (cross-cutting) | Compliance audit |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Safety Plan Risk Metrics

> [!NOTE] The Safety Plan itself does not contain engineering calculations, but it **mandates** the formulas below. KTU examiners often test whether students know **which formulas the plan must reference**.

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Avg. probability SIS fails to act on demand | dimensionless |
| Average Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour | $\text{PFH} = \lambda_{D}$ | For high/continuous demand mode | $\text{h}^{-1}$ |
| SIL from PFD | $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}})$ | Mapping to integrity level | dimensionless |
| Safe Failure Fraction | $\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Fraction of failures that are safe | % |
| Hardware Fault Tolerance (route 1H) | $\text{HFT} = N - N_{D}$ | Required redundancy | integer |
| Total PFD for redundant system | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SYS}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_{i}$ | Sum of subsystem PFDs | dimensionless |
| Proof Test Interval | $T_{\text{proof}}$ | Maximum interval between tests | hours / months |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SIS Safety Plan is **not academic paperwork** — it is the operational backbone of:
- **Oil & Gas refineries** (Kuwait, Mangalore, Kochi refineries use it for ESD systems)
- **Chemical plants** (Fertilizer plants in Kerala — FACT, Cochin Minerals)
- **Power plants** (NTPC boiler protection, turbine overspeed trips)
- **Pharmaceuticals** (Sterilizer autoclave overpressure protection)
- **KTU Case Studies**: Often set in LPG bottling plants (Indian Oil, Bharat Petroleum)

## 2.5 Failure Mode of a Missing or Poor Safety Plan

| Missing Element | Consequence |
|---|---|
| No RACI matrix | Unclear ownership → incidents |
| No MOC procedure | Unauthorized bypasses → Bhopal-like disasters |
| No validation plan | System fails on demand → catastrophic |
| No proof test schedule | PFD drifts above target → SIL violation |
| No documentation retention | Audit failure → plant shutdown |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3: SIS Safety Plan — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Derivation: Mapping a SIF to the Safety Plan Phases

The Safety Plan does not derive engineering values — it **prescribes the derivation methodology**. Below is the step-by-step logic a student should be able to articulate in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1 — Identify the Hazard (Governed by Plan Phase 1)

> [!NOTE] **Logic Step**
> A process upset is identified, e.g., a centrifugal pump discharge overpressure event. The Safety Plan mandates that a **HAZOP node** be opened.

**Conversion Logic:** The PHA team records:
- **Cause**: Blocked discharge valve
- **Consequence**: Pipe rupture, $P > 1.6 \times \text{MAWP}$
- **Severity Category**: Catastrophic (multiple fatalities)

### Step 2 — Determine SIL Target (Governed by Plan Phase 2)

Using the **Calibrated Risk Graph** or **LOPA**, the team computes:

$$
\text{Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)} = \frac{f_{n}^{\text{unc}}}{\text{Tolerable Risk}}
$$

For example, if the unmitigated event frequency is:

$$
f_{n}^{\text{unc}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{per year}
$$

And the tolerable risk is:

$$
f_{n}^{\text{tol}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}
$$

Then:

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Mapping RRF to SIL** (per IEC 61511 Table 3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RRF} &\in [10, 100] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 1} \\
\text{RRF} &\in [100, 1000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 2} \\
\text{RRF} &\in [1000, 10000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 3} \\
\text{RRF} &\in [10000, 100000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since RRF = $10^{4}$, the SIF must be designed to **SIL 3**.

### Step 3 — Allocate the SIL to a SIF Architecture (Governed by Plan Phase 3)

The Safety Plan mandates that the **PFD for each SIF** be calculated and verified to meet:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{target}}
$$

For **SIL 3**, the target band is:

$$
10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} < 10^{-3}
$$

For a **1oo2 (1-out-of-2) voted sensor subsystem** with identical sensors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &\approx 2 \cdot \lambda_{D} \cdot t_{CE} \cdot \frac{t_{CE}}{3} \\
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &\approx \frac{2}{3} \cdot \lambda_{D} \cdot t_{CE}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This formula accounts for the redundancy masking dangerous failures until **both** sensors fail — giving a much lower PFD than a single channel.

For a **logic solver** with very low dangerous failure rate:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} \approx \lambda_{DU}^{\text{Logic}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}
$$

For a **final element** (e.g., a shutdown valve):

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} = \lambda_{DU}^{\text{FE}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Subsystem PFDs (Plan Mandates Aggregation)

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{Sensor}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Final Element}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The SIF is only as good as its weakest link. The plan requires summation because failures in **any** subsystem cause the SIF to fail on demand.

### Step 5 — Verification (Plan Phase 4)

The Safety Plan mandates an **independent check** that:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{calculated}} < \text{PFD}_{\text{target}}
$$

If violated, the plan prescribes design iteration (add redundancy, shorten proof test interval, or use a more reliable component).

### Step 6 — Validation (Plan Phase 5)

The plan mandates end-to-end testing of the **installed SIF** to prove it operates as designed. Typical acceptance criteria:

- **Response time** of SIF ≤ safety time of process
- **No spurious trips** during commissioning
- **All proof test bypasses** properly logged and removed
- **Independent witness** signs the validation report

### Step 7 — Operation, Maintenance, and Proof Testing (Plan Phases 6–7)

The plan defines:

$$
T_{\text{proof}} = \text{Maximum interval such that PFD stays within SIL target}
$$

For SIL 3 with a 1oo2D system, $T_{\text{proof}}$ might be **12 months**; for SIL 1 it could be **5 years**.

### Step 8 — Modification (Plan Phase 8)

Any change — even replacing a transmitter with an identical model from a different vendor — triggers the **MOC procedure** in the Safety Plan, requiring:

1. Documented change request
2. Re-calculation of PFD with new failure rates
3. Re-validation if the architecture changes
4. Approval by the **Process Safety Engineer** and **Independent Assessor**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
> A reactor has a **high-pressure deviation** scenario. The PHA concludes that without protection, the event frequency is $f = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ /year, with **catastrophic** consequences. The tolerable risk is $f_{tol} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /year. The proposed SIF is **1oo2 pressure transmitters → Logic Solver → Shutdown Valve**, with:
> - $\lambda_{D}^{\text{sensor}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h (per transmitter)
> - $\lambda_{DU}^{\text{logic}} = 1 \times 10^{-8}$ /h
> - $\lambda_{DU}^{\text{valve}} = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ /h
> - $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760$ h (1 year)
> - $t_{CE} = 720$ h (1 month, for the 1oo2 sensor)

**Show that the SIF meets the SIL target set in the Safety Plan.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step (a): Determine SIL target**

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{\text{unc}}}{f_{\text{tol}}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
\text{RRF} = 10^{4} \Rightarrow \text{SIL 3 (per IEC 61511 Table 3)}
$$

**Step (b): PFD for 1oo2 sensor**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \frac{2}{3} \cdot \lambda_{D} \cdot t_{CE}^{2} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (720)^{2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= \frac{2}{3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 518400 \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.2592 \\
&= 0.1728 \times 10^{-0} \\
\text{Wait — recalculation:} \\
5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 518400 &= 0.2592 \\
\frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.2592 &= 0.1728
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Note on units**: $\lambda_{D}$ is in /h, $t_{CE}$ is in h, so $t_{CE}^{2}$ is in h², and the result is dimensionless. This $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 0.1728$ is **NOT** within SIL 3 band; the sensor architecture is **over-redundant or mis-applied**. Let's correct: a 1oo2 **with diagnostics** is typically written as:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{t_{CE}}{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}
$$

Assuming $\beta = 0.05$ (common for modern smart transmitters) and using $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ /h (assuming 90% of dangerous failures are detected):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} &\approx 2 \cdot (0.95) \cdot 5 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \frac{720}{2} + 0.05 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 720 \\
&\approx 2 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 360 + 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 3.42 \times 10^{-5} + 3.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 3.78 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): PFD for logic solver**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} = \lambda_{DU}^{\text{Logic}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \frac{8760}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step (d): PFD for shutdown valve**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{Valve}} = \lambda_{DU}^{\text{Valve}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2} = 3 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \frac{8760}{2} = 1.314 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step (e): Total SIF PFD**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &= \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Valve}} \\
&= 3.78 \times 10^{-5} + 4.38 \times 10^{-5} + 1.314 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\approx 1.396 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (f): Check against SIL 3 target**

$$
10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} < 10^{-3}
$$

But $1.396 \times 10^{-3} > 10^{-3}$, so the SIF **FAILS** to meet SIL 3.

**Step (g): Safety Plan's prescribed corrective action**

The Safety Plan mandates a re-design iteration. Options:
- Increase HFT of the valve (use 2oo2 voted valves)
- Shorten the proof test interval
- Select a valve with lower $\lambda_{DU}$

**Conversion Logic Recap:** The example demonstrates that the Safety Plan is the **executive document** that triggers these re-design activities when engineering calculations fail to meet SIL targets.

## 3.3 Summary of Derivation Logic Flow

$$
\text{Hazard Identified} \rightarrow \text{Risk Quantified} \rightarrow \text{SIL Target Set} \rightarrow \text{Architecture Allocated} \rightarrow \text{PFD Calculated} \rightarrow \text{SIL Verified} \rightarrow \text{SIF Validated} \rightarrow \text{Operate / Maintain}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3: SIS Safety Plan — Structural Diagrams

## 4.1 SIS Safety Plan Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE] This Mermaid block illustrates the **document hierarchy** that the Safety Plan sits at the apex of.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SIS Safety Plan Master Document] --> B[Process Hazard Analysis PHA]
    A --> C[Safety Requirements Specification SRS]
    A --> D[SIS Design Documentation]
    A --> E[Installation and Commissioning Plan]
    A --> F[Validation Plan and Report]
    A --> G[Operation and Maintenance Procedures]
    A --> H[Management of Change MOC Procedure]
    A --> I[Audit and Assessment Procedure]
    A --> J[Decommissioning Plan]
    B --> K[Risk Register and SIL Targets]
    C --> L[SIF List with PFD and SFF Targets]
    D --> M[Logic Diagrams and Cause Effect]
    E --> N[Loop Checks and Site Acceptance]
    F --> O[End to End Functional Tests]
    G --> P[Proof Test Schedule]
    H --> Q[Re Verification Trigger]
    I --> R[Independent Safety Assessor]
    J --> S[Safe Shutdown Sequence]
```

## 4.2 Safety Plan Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase 1 Concept] --> B[Phase 2 Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Phase 3 SRS Generation]
    C --> D[Phase 4 SIS Design]
    D --> E[Phase 5 Installation]
    E --> F[Phase 6 Validation]
    F --> G[Phase 7 Operation]
    G --> H[Phase 8 Maintenance]
    H --> I[Phase 9 Modification]
    I --> J[Phase 10 Decommission]
    K[SIS Safety Plan Governs All Phases] -.-> A
    K -.-> B
    K -.-> C
    K -.-> D
    K -.-> E
    K -.-> F
    K -.-> G
    K -.-> H
    K -.-> I
    K -.-> J
```

## 4.3 RACI Matrix Structure for the Safety Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Safety Engineer] -->|Responsible| B[Safety Plan Updates]
    C[Instrument Engineer] -->|Accountable| D[SIS Design Compliance]
    E[Operations Lead] -->|Consulted| F[Operating Procedures]
    G[Maintenance Lead] -->|Consulted| H[Proof Test Plans]
    I[Project Manager] -->|Responsible| J[Schedule and Resources]
    K[Independent Assessor] -->|Informed| L[Audit Findings]
    M[Plant Manager] -->|Accountable| N[Final Approval]
    B --> O[Safety Plan Document]
    D --> O
    F --> O
    H --> O
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.4 Verification vs Validation Distinction (Common KTU Exam Topic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[SIS Project Lifecycle] --> B{Question Asked}
    B -->|Are we building the system RIGHT| C[Verification]
    B -->|Are we building the RIGHT system| D[Validation]
    C --> E[Design Reviews, Calculations, Simulations]
    D --> F[End to End Functional Testing on Installed Hardware]
    E --> G[Outputs to Design Documents]
    F --> H[Outputs to Validation Report]
    G --> I[Safety Plan Phase 4]
    H --> J[Safety Plan Phase 6]
```

## 4.5 Management of Change Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Change Request Raised] --> B{Risk Impact Assessment}
    B -->|Low Impact| C[Engineering Approval]
    B -->|Medium Impact| D[Process Safety Engineer Review]
    B -->|High Impact| E[Independent Safety Assessment]
    C --> F[Implement Change]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Re verify SIL PFD]
    G --> H[Re validate SIF Function]
    H --> I[Update Safety Plan]
    I --> J[Close Out MOC Record]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3: SIS Safety Plan — KTU 2024 Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Q: Define an SIS Safety Plan and state any two mandatory contents as per IEC 61511.**

**Model Answer:**
An **SIS Safety Plan** is a formal document that defines how a Safety Instrumented System will be designed, installed, validated, operated, maintained, modified, and decommissioned in compliance with IEC 61511.

Two mandatory contents:
1. **Scope definition** — physical, logical, and time boundaries of the SIS.
2. **Roles, responsibilities, and competency requirements** — RACI matrix identifying who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed for each lifecycle activity.

*(Alternate acceptable items: deliverables register, tools/methodology, MOC procedure, verification & validation strategy, documentation management.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Q: Distinguish between verification and validation as defined in the SIS Safety Plan.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | "Are we building the system right?" | "Are we building the right system?" |
| **Phase in Plan** | Phase 4 (Design) | Phase 6 (Post-installation) |
| **Method** | Design review, calculation check, simulation | End-to-end functional test on installed hardware |
| **Output** | Verification report | Validation report with witness signatures |

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Modeled)]**

**(a) (7 Marks) List and briefly explain the 11 phases of the IEC 61511 SIS Safety Lifecycle as governed by the Safety Plan.**

**Model Answer:**

The Safety Plan prescribes execution of the following **11 phases**:

1. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** — Identify all process hazards using HAZOP, What-If, or FMEA. Output: PHA report with risk register.

2. **Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers** — Decide which risks are reduced by the SIS and which by other layers (BPCS, alarms, relief valves). Output: SRS with SIL targets per SIF.

3. **SIS Design** — Specify logic solver, sensor and final element architecture, and verify PFD. Output: Design documents, logic diagrams, SIL verification calculations.

4. **Installation** — Mounting, wiring, grounding, and commissioning of hardware. Output: Installation records and loop checks.

5. **Validation** — End-to-end functional testing to prove the installed SIS meets the SRS. Output: Validation report with witness sign-offs.

6. **Operation** — Operating procedures, bypass management, alarm management. Output: Operating manuals.

7. **Maintenance** — Preventive maintenance, proof testing, spare parts. Output: Proof test records and maintenance logs.

8. **Modification** — Management of Change (MOC) process for any design changes. Output: Updated SRS, MOC records.

9. **Decommissioning** — Safe shutdown and disposal of the SIS at end-of-life. Output: Decommissioning report.

10. **Verification (Cross-Cutting)** — Independent review activities across all phases.

11. **Assessment / Audit (Cross-Cutting)** — Compliance audit against IEC 61511, including independent safety assessment.

> [!NOTE] **Conversion Logic:** The Safety Plan is the **only document** that references all 11 phases; all other deliverables (PHA, SRS, validation report) are outputs of specific phases.

---

**(b) (7 Marks) With a suitable example, explain how the Safety Plan drives SIL determination and verification of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification (Plan Phase 1)**
A reactor overpressure event is identified during PHA. Unmitigated frequency: $f_{unc} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ /year. Severity: catastrophic (multiple fatalities).

**Step 2 — Tolerable Risk Definition (Plan Phase 2)**

$$
f_{tol} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Risk Reduction Factor Calculation**

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{unc}}{f_{tol}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — SIL Target Mapping (IEC 61511 Table 3)**

$$
\text{RRF} = 10^{4} \Rightarrow \text{SIL 3}
$$

The SIF must achieve:

$$
10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} < 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Architecture Allocation & PFD Calculation (Plan Phase 3)**

For a 1oo2D voted sensor system, logic solver, and shutdown valve:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2D}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{Valve}}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Plan Phase 4)**

The Safety Plan mandates that an **independent assessor** verify:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{calculated}} < \text{PFD}_{\text{target}}
$$

If not met, the plan prescribes design iteration (redundancy increase, shortened proof test, or component replacement).

**Conversion Logic Recap:** The Safety Plan **drives** every step by mandating the methodology, tools, responsibilities, and acceptance criteria. Without the plan, SIL determination becomes ad-hoc and non-auditable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modeled)]**

**(a) (7 Marks) Explain the contents of an SIS Safety Plan as per IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.1.1.**

**Model Answer:**

The IEC 61511 standard mandates that a Safety Plan include the following key contents:

1. **Scope** — Defines physical, logical, and time boundaries of the SIS project.

2. **Objectives and Targets** — SIL targets, PFD targets, SFF, HFT.

3. **Roles, Responsibilities, and Competency** — RACI matrix, competency matrix for safety engineers, proof test engineers, and assessors.

4. **SIS Safety Lifecycle Strategy** — How the 11 phases will be executed, including the methodology for each phase.

5. **Tools, Techniques, and Methods** — HAZOP, LOPA, Risk Graph, SIL verification software, OREDA reliability data.

6. **Deliverables Register** — A master list of all documents to be produced (PHA, SRS, design docs, validation report, etc.).

7. **Verification and Validation Procedures** — Methods, acceptance criteria, and witness requirements.

8. **Management of Change (MOC) Procedure** — The formal process for authorizing, documenting, and re-verifying any modification.

9. **Documentation and Records Management** — Retention periods, document control, access controls, version control.

10. **Audit and Review Schedule** — Frequency and scope of internal and external audits.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Note:** Each of the above is a **mandatory element** per IEC 61511. Omission of any one is grounds for audit non-compliance.

---

**(b) (7 Marks) A SIF is designed as 1oo2 voted pressure transmitters feeding a Safety PLC operating a single shutdown valve. The unmitigated event frequency is $1 \times 10^{-2}$/year and tolerable risk is $1 \times 10^{-5}$/year. Given: $\lambda_{DU}^{\text{sensor}} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$/h (per transmitter), $\lambda_{DU}^{\text{logic}} = 1 \times 10^{-8}$/h, $\lambda_{DU}^{\text{valve}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$/h, $T_{proof} = 8760$ h, $T_{CE} = 4380$ h. Verify if the SIF meets the required SIL.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Determine Required SIL**

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
\text{RRF} = 10^{3} \Rightarrow \text{SIL 2} \quad [\text{per IEC 61511 Table 3}]
$$

SIL 2 target band:

$$
10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} < 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensor PFD (1oo2 voted)**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{CE}}{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{CE}
$$

Assuming $\beta = 0.1$ (typical for pressure transmitters with diagnostics):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &= 2 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 2190 + 0.1 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 4380 \\
&= 7.884 \times 10^{-4} + 8.76 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 8.76 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Logic Solver PFD**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{Logic}} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \frac{8760}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Element (Valve) PFD**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{Valve}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \frac{8760}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Total SIF PFD**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &= 8.76 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-5} + 2.19 \times 10^{-3} \\
&\approx 3.117 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify against SIL 2 Target**

$$
10^{-3} \leq 3.117 \times 10^{-3} < 10^{-2} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The SIF **meets SIL 2** requirements. The dominant contributor is the **shutdown valve** (~70% of total PFD), which is a typical finding. The Safety Plan's next iteration may prescribe valve redundancy or shortened proof test for the valve if higher SIL is needed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "Safety Plan" with "SRS"** — The Safety Plan is the *executive plan*; the SRS is the *technical specification*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Omitting the RACI matrix** — Many students list activities but forget the **roles and responsibilities** section. Mandatory per Clause 5.2.1.1.
> 3. **Forgetting the MOC procedure** — A SIF without a MOC procedure is **non-compliant** with IEC 61511.
> 4. **Wrong SIL band mapping** — Confusing the RRF ranges (e.g., stating RRF = 1000 maps to SIL 3, when it maps to SIL 2).
> 5. **Ignoring documentation retention** — Examiners specifically test whether students know the "operational life + 5 years" retention rule.
> 6. **Using $\lambda_{D}$ instead of $\lambda_{DU}$ in PFD calculations** — Only **undetected** dangerous failures contribute to PFD; using $\lambda_{D}$ overestimates failure probability and may show the system failing SIL verification.
> 7. **Forgetting unit conversions in numerical problems** — $T_{proof}$ must be in **hours** when $\lambda$ is in /h; mixing years and hours is the #1 calculation error in KTU answer sheets.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- The **SIS Safety Plan** is the **master governing document** of the entire functional safety lifecycle, mandated by **IEC 61511 Clause 5.2.1.1**.
- It is initiated in **Phase 1 (Concept)** and updated continuously through **decommissioning**.
- **Mandatory contents**: Scope, Objectives, Roles (RACI), Lifecycle Strategy, Tools/Methods, Deliverables, V&V procedures, MOC procedure, Documentation, Audit schedule.
- The plan governs **all 11 phases** of IEC 61511 — from PHA to Decommissioning.
- **Verification** = "Are we building the system right?" (Design review)
- **Validation** = "Are we building the right system?" (Installed functional test)
- **SIL mapping**:
  - $\text{RRF} \in [10, 100] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 1}$
  - $\text{RRF} \in [100, 1000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 2}$
  - $\text{RRF} \in [1000, 10000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 3}$
  - $\text{RRF} \in [10000, 100000] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 4}$
- **PFD formula** (low demand): $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{proof}} / 2$
- **PFH formula** (high demand): $\text{PFH} = \lambda_{DU}$ (or summed for redundant)
- **SFF formula**: $\text{SFF} = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}) / (\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$
- The **dominant contributor** to a SIF's PFD is typically the **final element (valve)** — a key KTU insight.
- Any change to a SIF requires **MOC** + **re-verification of SIL** + **re-validation**.
- The Safety Plan must define **documentation retention** for the operational life of the plant + 5 years.
- **Auditor focus areas**: RACI matrix, MOC procedure, V&V records, proof test intervals.
- **Common KTU trap**: Mixing up $\lambda_{D}$ and $\lambda_{DU}$ in calculations.
- The Safety Plan is **a living document** — not a one-time deliverable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification and Validation Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Verification and Validation Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Verification and Validation (V&V) Plan** is a formally documented, lifecycle-spanning engineering artifact that defines the **strategies, methods, responsibilities, resources, schedules, pass/fail criteria, and traceability mechanisms** employed to demonstrate — with documented evidence — that a safety-related system satisfies its specified **functional safety requirements** and that the implemented design correctly realizes those requirements.

In the context of **IEC 61508** and **ISO 26262**, the V&V Plan is a mandatory deliverable within the broader **Safety Lifecycle** and is referenced explicitly in clauses governing **Verification (ensures "we built the product right")** and **Validation (ensures "we built the right product")**. For KTU's functional safety curriculum, the V&V Plan is treated as the **controlling document** that converts abstract safety goals into concrete, auditable, and reproducible test/analysis activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A V&V Plan is a *living document* under configuration management that prescribes the techniques (analysis, testing, simulation, formal proof, inspection), the entry/exit criteria for each safety phase, the SIL/ASIL target metrics, and the evidence collection strategy required by the safety standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are building a **fire alarm system for a 10-storey building**. Before you hand it over to the building owner, you must prove two distinct things:

1. **Verification** — *"Did the engineers follow the blueprints exactly?"*  
   You check whether every wire was soldered as designed, whether the smoke sensor is the exact model specified, and whether the microcontroller code matches the flowchart approved by the safety architect. You are comparing the **work product** against the **design specification**.

2. **Validation** — *"Will the building actually be safe in a real fire?"*  
   You ignite a controlled test fire in a real room and check whether the alarm triggers within 30 seconds, whether the sprinkler activates, and whether the fire department gets notified. You are comparing the **final system behavior** against the **user's true need (life safety)**.

A **V&V Plan** is the **master checklist + procedure manual + sign-off sheet** that orchestrates both of these activities. It tells the team: *what* to verify, *how*, *when*, *who* is responsible, *what evidence* to capture, and *what thresholds* (e.g., diagnostic coverage ≥ 90%) must be met.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** Verification = proofreading your essay against the assignment rubric. Validation = asking your friend whether your essay actually persuades the reader.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars — V&V Distinct Goals

| Pillar | Question Answered | Lifecycle Phase | Core Activity | Standards Reference |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Verification** | Are we building the product correctly? | Design + Implementation | Reviews, inspections, static analysis, unit tests, integration tests | IEC 61508 Clause 7, ISO 26262-4, -5, -6 |
| **Validation** | Are we building the correct product? | Final integration + deployment | Functional safety tests, fault injection, field trials, statistical analysis | IEC 61508 Clause 7, ISO 26262-4, -7 |

## 1.4 Why a Plan (and not just ad-hoc testing)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A V&V Plan exists because functional safety is **evidence-based**, not opinion-based. Standards require *demonstrable* and *auditable* proof. Without a plan, you cannot prove to a TÜV/SGS auditor, a regulatory body, or a KTU examiner that your safety claims are *justified*.

**Five driving reasons:**
1. **Traceability** — every requirement → design → test case link is documented.
2. **Completeness** — ensures no safety requirement is left untested.
3. **Independence** — assigns verification roles to people *not* involved in design (mandated at higher SILs).
4. **Repeatability** — tests can be re-executed for regression or re-certification.
5. **Resource estimation** — budgets, tools, rigs, and personnel are committed in advance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V-Model of Functional Safety (Foundation)

The V&V Plan is organized along the **V-Model**, where each design phase on the descending arm has a corresponding verification activity on the ascending arm, and the entire system undergoes validation at the apex.

```
Concept (Idea)
  → Overall Safety Requirements
    → System-Level Safety Design
      → Sub-system Design
        → Hardware Design  |  Software Design
          → Module Implementation
            → Unit Verification
              → Integration Verification
                → System Validation  ◄── APEX
              → Integration Verification
            → Module Implementation
          → Hardware Design | Software Design
        → Sub-system Design
      → System-Level Safety Design
    → Overall Safety Requirements
  → Concept
```

## 2.2 Structural Components of a V&V Plan

A KTU/IEC-compliant V&V Plan must contain the following **eight core sections**:

1. **Scope and Purpose** — which subsystem, which SIL/ASIL, which standards.
2. **References** — applicable clauses of IEC 61508 / ISO 26262 / IEC 61511.
3. **Roles, Responsibilities, and Independence Levels** — who verifies what, and at what organizational separation.
4. **V&V Strategy** — choice of techniques (Table B.5 of IEC 61508-2 for hardware, Table B.5 of IEC 61508-3 for software).
5. **Entry and Exit Criteria** — when an activity may start and when it is deemed complete.
6. **Test Cases, Test Procedures, and Test Data** — derived from requirements via traceability matrix.
7. **Pass/Fail Criteria and Metrics** — quantified acceptance thresholds.
8. **Tool Qualification, Environment, and Schedule** — version of tools, hardware rigs, calendar timeline.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The V&V Plan does not have "formulas" in the classical sense, but it relies on **quantitative metrics** borrowed from reliability and safety engineering. The following table is the **exam-relevant cheat sheet** you must memorize.

| Metric / Symbol | Definition | Formula | Typical Bound (KTU) | Used In |
|-----------------|------------|---------|----------------------|---------|
| **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) | Avg. probability that a safety function fails when demanded | $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \text{PFD}(t)\,dt$$ | SIL 1: $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $<10^{-1}$ | Verification of low-demand modes |
| **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) | Avg. frequency of dangerous failures per hour | $$\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_{DU}$$ | SIL 3: $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $<10^{-7}$ /h | High-demand / continuous modes |
| **SFF** (Safe Failure Fraction) | Fraction of failures that lead to a safe state | $$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}}$$ | SIL 2 minimum: 60%–90% | Hardware architectural constraints |
| **DC** (Diagnostic Coverage) | Fraction of dangerous failures detected by diagnostics | $$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$ | Type B, SIL 2: ≥ 60% | Verification of self-tests |
| **PFD$_{avg}$ for 1oo1** | Single-channel architecture | $$\text{PFD}_{1oo1}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx \lambda_{DU}\,t$$ | — | Simplest baseline |
| **PFD$_{avg}$ for 1oo2** | Two-channel with parallel voting (low demand) | $$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{3}$$ | — | Redundancy improvement |
| **2H → 1oo2** | Two-out-of-two converted to 1oo2 after first fault | $$\text{PFD}_{2H} = 2\,(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}\,t + \beta\,\lambda_{DU}^{2} t^{2}$$ | $\beta$ = common-cause factor | Common verification target |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | Used in $\text{PFD}_{avg}$ calculations | $$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{repair,i}$$ | Typically 8 h | Repairable systems |
| **TI** (Test Interval) | Time between automatic diagnostic tests | $$\text{PFD} \approx \lambda_{DU}\cdot\frac{TI}{2}$$ | — | Proof-test design |
| **Coverage (E/E/PE)** | Required for IEC 61508 route 1H | $$\text{Route } 1H \iff \text{DC} \geq 90\% \text{ for SIL 3}$$ | — | Architectural constraint |

## 2.4 Independence Levels (Organizational)

The V&V Plan **must** specify the degree of independence. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

| Independence Level | Description | Minimum SIL/ASIL Allowed (IEC 61508) |
|--------------------|-------------|--------------------------------------|
| **IR0** | Self-verification by the designer | Not allowed for safety functions |
| **IR1** | Verified by another person in the same team | Up to SIL 1 (limited) |
| **IR2** | Verified by a person from a different department | Up to SIL 2 |
| **IR3** | Verified by a person from a different organization | Up to SIL 3 |
| **IR4** | Verified by an independent third-party assessor | SIL 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write *"verification is done by the QA team"* — this is **insufficient**. The plan must specify the **independence level (IRx)** explicitly, mapped to the SIL target.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industries, the V&V Plan is the **single document** that an external auditor (TÜV, SGS, Bureau Veritas) reads first during a functional safety certification audit. It establishes the **chain of trust** that propagates from the safety requirement to the physical test result. Without it, a perfectly safe system cannot be *proven* safe, and a perfectly unsafe system cannot be *proven* unsafe — and in safety engineering, *unproven safety is equivalent to no safety*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFD$_{avg}$ for a 1oo1 Architecture

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU functional safety papers.

**Given:**
- A single-channel subsystem (one sensor + one logic + one actuator).
- Constant dangerous-uncovered failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures/hour).
- A perfect proof-test interval $T_{proof}$ (the system is restored to "as good as new" at every proof test).
- The system operates in low-demand mode.

**Step 1 — Define instantaneous unavailability.**
The probability that the channel is failed at time $t$ after a perfect proof test, assuming exponential failure distribution, is:

$$P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU}\,t}$$

**Step 2 — Linearize for small $\lambda_{DU} t$.**
Since safety integrity demands $\lambda_{DU} t \ll 1$ (otherwise the system is unusable):

$$P(t) \approx \lambda_{DU}\, t$$

**Step 3 — Average unavailability over the proof interval.**
The mean PFD over one complete proof-test cycle is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU}\,t\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^{2}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}\;}$$

> This expresses a fundamental trade-off: **halving the proof-test interval (T/2) halves the average probability of failure on demand**, but doubles the maintenance cost.

## 3.2 Derivation: PFD$_{avg}$ for a 1oo2 Architecture

**Given:**
- Two identical channels in parallel, both required to fail for system failure.
- Each channel has $\lambda_{DU}$.

**Step 1 — Joint failure probability.**
For both channels to fail simultaneously (and unrevealed):

$$P_{1oo2}(t) \approx (\lambda_{DU}\,t)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof interval.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} (\lambda_{DU}\,t)^{2}\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo2} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T_{proof}} \left[ \frac{t^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}\,T_{proof}^{2}}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU}\,T_{proof})^{2}}{3}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Compare $1oo1$ and $1oo2$ numerically. If $\lambda_{DU} T_{proof} = 10^{-2}$, then $1oo1$ gives $5 \times 10^{-3}$ while $1oo2$ gives $3.3 \times 10^{-5}$ — a **150× safety improvement** at the cost of doubling the hardware.

## 3.3 Derivation: Diagnostic Coverage Impact on PFH

When a fraction $DC$ of dangerous failures is detected and the system transitions to a safe state within $\tau$ (diagnostic test interval), the residual dangerous failure rate becomes:

$$\lambda_{DU}^{\text{residual}} = \lambda_{D}\,(1 - DC)$$

Therefore the **Probability of Failure per Hour** is:

$$\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_{DU}^{\text{residual}} = \lambda_{D}\,(1 - DC)$$

If the diagnostic test runs every $TI$ hours and the diagnostic is perfect (DC = 100% for detectable failures), the maximum dangerous exposure is $TI/2$ hours, so the unavailability contribution per detected failure is:

$$P_{\text{detected}} \approx \lambda_{DD} \cdot \frac{TI}{2}$$

**Combined PFH:**

$$\text{PFH} = \underbrace{\lambda_{DU}\,(1-DC)}_{undetected} + \underbrace{\lambda_{DD} \cdot DC \cdot \frac{1}{TI}}_{detected\, but\, still\, contributes\, if\, not\, fast\, enough}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the foundation of the **"Route 1H" architecture** in IEC 61508, where the *only* way to reach SIL 3 in a single channel is to demonstrate DC ≥ 90% with a sufficiently short test interval.

## 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Mapping Requirement to V&V Activity

This matrix is the **blueprint** the V&V Plan must produce for every safety requirement.

| Requirement ID | Safety Function | SIL | Verification Technique | Validation Technique | Pass Criterion | Evidence Artifact |
|----------------|-----------------|-----|------------------------|----------------------|----------------|--------------------|
| SR-001 | Emergency stop within 100 ms | 2 | Static code analysis + unit test | Fault injection on HIL rig | Reaction time ≤ 100 ms | Test report + traceability |
| SR-002 | Sensor short-circuit detection | 2 | FMEA + Diagnostic coverage calc | Long-duration burn-in | DC ≥ 90% | FMEA spreadsheet |
| SR-003 | Safe state on power loss | 3 | Hardware review + walkthrough | Power-down HIL scenario | Output < 24 V within 50 ms | Oscilloscope traces |
| SR-004 | Software loop integrity check | 3 | Static analysis + back-to-back test | Defect injection campaign | 0 undetected loop faults | Test log |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above is the **exact artifact** a KTU examiner expects when a question asks you to "design a V&V plan for a given safety function."

## 3.5 Worked Example: Complete V&V Plan Numerical Sketch

A SIL 2 emergency shutdown system has:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h
- $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (1 year)
- $DC = 80\%$
- Architecture: 1oo1 with diagnostics

**Step 1 — Calculate PFD$_{avg}$ without diagnostic credit.**

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — SIL 2 demand bound check.**

$$\text{SIL 2 range: } 10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Diagnostic improvement.**
With $DC = 80\%$, the dangerous undetected rate reduces to $0.20 \times \lambda_{DU}$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{effective}} \approx 0.20 \times 2.19 \times 10^{-3} = 4.38 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Result interpretation.**
Effective PFD drops by an order of magnitude, providing a **safety margin** well within SIL 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The V&V Plan would mandate the following evidence:
> 1. FMEA report proving $\lambda_{DU}$ calculation.
> 2. Diagnostic test report proving $DC \geq 80\%$.
> 3. Proof-test procedure document.
> 4. Sign-off by an IR2 (or higher) independent verifier.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 V&V Plan Lifecycle Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Safety Requirements Specification]
    B --> C[Design and Implementation]
    C --> D[Verification Phase]
    D --> D1[Unit Verification]
    D1 --> D2[Integration Verification]
    D2 --> D3[System Verification]
    D3 --> E[Validation Phase]
    E --> E1[Functional Safety Validation]
    E1 --> E2[Field Validation and Site Acceptance]
    E2 --> F{All Pass Criteria Met?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Release and Certification]
    F -->|No| H[Reopen Defects to Design]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Configuration Management and Archiving]

    classDef verify fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0d2c5c
    classDef validate fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0f3d22
    classDef release fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#5a3a00
    class D,D1,D2,D3 verify
    class E,E1,E2 validate
    class G,I release
```

## 4.2 V&V Plan Document Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1. Scope and Purpose] --> P2[2. References and Standards]
    P2 --> P3[3. Roles and Independence]
    P3 --> P4[4. V&V Strategy and Techniques]
    P4 --> P5[5. Entry and Exit Criteria]
    P5 --> P6[6. Test Cases and Traceability]
    P6 --> P7[7. Pass or Fail Metrics]
    P7 --> P8[8. Tools, Schedule, and Environment]
    P8 --> P9[9. Risk and Defect Management]
    P9 --> P10[10. Approvals and Sign-Off]
```

## 4.3 Verification vs Validation — A Functional Architecture View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[VERIFICATION DOMAIN]
        R[Safety Requirements] --> SP[Design Specification]
        SP --> IM[Implementation]
        IM --> CHK[Check: Does implementation match design?]
    end

    subgraph RIGHT[VALIDATION DOMAIN]
        USER[User Need and Operational Context] --> NEED[Real-World Safety Need]
        NEED --> VAL[Validate: Does the system satisfy the need?]
    end

    CHK --> VAL
    VAL --> CERT[Certification and Release]
    CHK --> CERT
```

## 4.4 Traceability Flow — Requirements to Evidence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Stakeholder Need] --> SR[Safety Requirement SR-XXX]
    SR --> DES[Design Element D-XXX]
    DES --> IM[Implementation Code or Hardware C-XXX]
    IM --> TC[Test Case TC-XXX]
    TC --> TR[Test Result TR-XXX]
    TR --> EV[Evidence Package for Auditor]
    EV --> SIGN[Independent Sign-Off]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safety compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`, `chkVerify`) and all quoted labels are plain uppercase text, in accordance with the diagram compilation safeguard rules of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Verification and Validation in a functional safety lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Verification (1 mark):** The process of evaluating whether the work products of a given phase satisfy the specifications established in the previous phase. It answers *"Are we building the product right?"* Activities include reviews, inspections, static analysis, and unit tests.
- **Validation (1 mark):** The process of evaluating whether the final system fulfills the user and safety needs under realistic operating conditions. It answers *"Are we building the right product?"* Activities include end-to-end functional safety tests, fault injection, and site acceptance.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** Verification is *phase-local and static*; validation is *system-level and dynamic*. Verification is internal-facing; validation is external-facing (user/regulator).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three mandatory sections of a V&V Plan as required by IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **V&V Strategy and Techniques** — selection of techniques from Tables B.5/B.6 of IEC 61508 mapped to the target SIL.
2. **Roles, Responsibilities, and Independence Levels (IRx)** — formal assignment of verification personnel with explicit independence.
3. **Entry and Exit Criteria for Each Lifecycle Phase** — quantified thresholds that gate progression from one phase to the next.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Pass/Fail criteria, traceability matrix, test procedures, tool qualification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(7 marks) Prepare a Verification Plan for the software component of a SIL 2 fire-suppression controller. Specify at least five verification techniques, the independence level required, and the entry/exit criteria for the software unit verification phase.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Scope and Independence (1 mark):**  
The software verification is performed at **IR2** (independent department within the same organization) as per IEC 61508-3 Table 5, SIL 2 requirement. The lead verifier is from the Safety QA team and is **not** the developer.

**2. Verification Techniques (3 marks — 0.5 each minimum five):**
| # | Technique | Standard Reference | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Static analysis** (MISRA-C, linting) | IEC 61508-3 Table B.5 | Detect coding standard violations |
| 2 | **Dynamic analysis and unit testing** | IEC 61508-3 Table B.5 | Verify code path behavior against spec |
| 3 | **Walk-through and inspection** | IEC 61508-3 Table B.5 | Peer review of design and code |
| 4 | **Failure assertion programming** | IEC 61508-3 Table B.5 | Self-test mechanisms within code |
| 5 | **Code coverage measurement** (branch + MC/DC) | IEC 61508-3 Table B.9 | SIL 2 mandates ≥ 100% statement, ≥ 80% branch |
| 6 | **Back-to-back testing** with diverse implementation | IEC 61508-3 Table B.5 | Detect systematic faults |

**3. Entry Criteria (1.5 marks):**
- Approved Software Safety Requirements Specification (SSRS).
- Source code under configuration control with version tag.
- Approved Software Design Specification.
- Test environment (compiler, simulator) baselined.

**4. Exit Criteria (1.5 marks):**
- 100% of test cases executed with results recorded.
- Branch coverage ≥ 80% and MC/DC coverage ≥ 50% achieved.
- Zero unresolved "critical" or "high" defects.
- Static analysis: zero violations of "required" MISRA rules.
- Traceability matrix complete: every requirement → test case link closed.

---

### Question A (b) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(7 marks) For a 1oo1 architecture sensor channel with $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-7}$/h and $T_{proof} = 4380$ h (6 months), compute PFD$_{avg}$ and determine whether the system meets SIL 2. If a 1oo2 architecture is adopted, recalculate PFD$_{avg}$ and comment on the safety improvement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PFD$_{avg}$ for 1oo1 (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo1} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 8.76 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — SIL 2 Bound Check (1 mark):**
SIL 2 requires $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2}$.  
Our value $8.76 \times 10^{-4}$ is **slightly below the SIL 2 lower bound**, i.e., it actually achieves **SIL 3** performance for the channel — but this is *unsafe to claim* without considering the full sensor + logic + actuator system, and the proof-test coverage (often < 100%).

> **Comment (1 mark):** The 1oo1 channel *numerically* satisfies SIL 3 in isolation, but architectural constraints of IEC 61508 (Type A/B, SFF, hardware fault tolerance) must also be satisfied. The V&V Plan must include SFF calculation and DC analysis before SIL 3 is claimed.

**Step 3 — PFD$_{avg}$ for 1oo2 (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},\,1oo2} &= \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4380)^{2}}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(1.752 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.069 \times 10^{-6}}{3} \\[6pt]
&= 1.023 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Engineering Insight (1 mark):**
- 1oo1: $8.76 \times 10^{-4}$
- 1oo2: $1.02 \times 10^{-6}$
- **Improvement factor:** $\dfrac{8.76 \times 10^{-4}}{1.02 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 859\times$

The 1oo2 architecture provides a **three-orders-of-magnitude** improvement in PFD, well within SIL 3 bounds, but at the cost of doubled hardware, common-cause failure analysis, and increased proof-test complexity. The V&V Plan must therefore include a **CCF (β-factor) analysis** and a **diversity-of-channel-types** argument to justify the redundancy gain.

---

### Question B (a) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(7 marks) Construct a V&V traceability matrix template for a SIL 3 over-speed protection function of a turbogenerator. Clearly identify columns, the linking mechanism, and explain how you would close the loop between a failed validation test and a corrective action on the design.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Matrix Columns (3 marks):**

| Column | Content | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Safety Requirement ID** | SR-OS-001 |
| 2 | **Requirement Description** | "Trip turbine when speed exceeds 3600 rpm" |
| 3 | **Derived Design Element** | D-OS-001 (Speed sensor + comparator + trip relay) |
| 4 | **Implementation Artifact** | Source file `speed_trip.c` lines 120-180 |
| 5 | **Verification Test Case** | TC-OS-001 (HIL over-speed injection) |
| 6 | **Validation Test Case** | TV-OS-001 (Full system load-rejection field trial) |
| 7 | **Pass/Fail Metric** | Trip time ≤ 200 ms; no spurious trip |
| 8 | **Evidence Reference** | Doc-ID `EVD-2024-0421` |
| 9 | **Status** | PASS / FAIL / DEFERRED |

**2. Linking Mechanism (1.5 marks):**  
Each row is **uniquely keyed** by the Safety Requirement ID. The same ID appears in:
- Safety Requirements Specification (source)
- Design Specification
- Source code (header comment)
- Test plan
- Test report
- Audit dossier

This forms a **bidirectional traceability graph** that an auditor can walk forward (requirement → evidence) and backward (defect → requirement).

**3. Defect Close-the-Loop Process (2.5 marks):**
1. Validation test TV-OS-001 fails (e.g., trip time = 320 ms).
2. Failure is logged in the **Defect Management System** (e.g., Jira) with link to TR-OS-001 and to SR-OS-001.
3. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** is opened — e.g., "comparator threshold set to 3650 rpm, design spec said 3600 rpm."
4. **Corrective Action** issued to design team: change threshold, re-derive $\lambda_{DU}$, update FMEA.
5. Re-verification: TC-OS-001 re-run.
6. Re-validation: TV-OS-001 re-run on HIL.
7. Sign-off by IR3 independent verifier.
8. Document update approved through change control.

---

### Question B (b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(7 marks) A SIL 3 system uses a single-channel (1oo1) architecture. The dangerous failure rate is $\lambda_{D} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h, and the diagnostic coverage achieved is DC = 95% with a diagnostic test interval TI = 1 hour. Compute the PFH and verify whether the system satisfies SIL 3. State two techniques from IEC 61508-3 that you would include in the V&V Plan to control systematic failures.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the undetected dangerous failure rate (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU} &= \lambda_{D} \cdot (1 - DC) \\[6pt]
&= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times (1 - 0.95) \\[6pt]
&= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 \\[6pt]
&= 1 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the detected dangerous failure contribution (1.5 marks):**
For detected failures, the maximum exposure is one diagnostic test interval:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{detected}} &= \lambda_{D} \cdot DC \cdot TI \\[6pt]
&= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.95 \times 1 \\[6pt]
&= 1.9 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{failures per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total PFH (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH} &= \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD} \cdot DC \cdot \frac{1}{TI} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1 \times 10^{-7} + 1.9 \times 10^{-6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — SIL 3 Bound Check (1 mark):**  
SIL 3 requires $10^{-8} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ per hour.  
Our computed PFH of $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ is **two orders of magnitude too high** and **does not satisfy SIL 3**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often take $\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_{DU}$ and report $1 \times 10^{-7}$, which *would* be SIL 3. However, this is the **undetected-only** value and ignores the detected-but-still-active failures between diagnostic cycles. The full PFH formula must be applied. **This is a 1–2 mark penalty trap.**

**Step 5 — Systematic Failure Control Techniques from IEC 61508-3 (2 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Diversity of design (different algorithms, different compilers, different programmers)** — reduces common-cause software faults.
2. **Defensive programming with failure-assertion checks** — runtime detection of illegal states and forced transition to safe state.
*(Other valid techniques: formal methods, back-to-back testing, structured programming, certified tools, detailed code review by IR3.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in V&V Plan questions:**
> 1. **Confusing V and V** — writing "validation = testing, verification = reviewing" without the lifecycle linkage. Always tie each to a specific phase.
> 2. **Omitting independence levels** — every V&V activity must declare an IRx. Generic "QA team" wording is **not** acceptable.
> 3. **Skipping exit criteria** — students often list test cases but forget the *quantitative thresholds* (coverage %, defect counts, PFD bound).
> 4. **Mixing units in PFH/PFD formulas** — $\lambda$ in per-hour, $T$ in hours, $\text{PFD}$ dimensionless. Always state units.
> 5. **Ignoring common-cause failures** — in 1oo2, never forget the $\beta$ factor and CCF analysis.
> 6. **Forgetting evidence storage** — a V&V plan that doesn't describe *how* test results are archived, version-controlled, and retrievable is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **V&V Plan** = living, configuration-managed document specifying all verification and validation activities for a safety-related system.
- [x] **Verification** = "built the product right" — phase-local, static, document-to-document.
- [x] **Validation** = "built the right product" — system-level, dynamic, against operational need.
- [x] **Eight mandatory sections:** scope, references, roles, strategy, entry/exit, tests, criteria, tools/schedule.
- [x] **Independence levels IR0–IR4** must be assigned per SIL target.
- [x] **Key formulas:** $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}}{2}$, $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_{proof})^{2}}{3}$, $\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{SU}+\lambda_{SD}+\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}}$.
- [x] **DC × TI tradeoff** is the foundation of Route 1H architectures.
- [x] **Traceability matrix** is the single most important V&V artifact — every requirement must link to design, code, test, and evidence.
- [x] **Defect close-the-loop** must involve RCA, re-verification, re-validation, and IR3 sign-off.
- [x] **Tools used in V&V must be qualified** (T1, T2, T3 categories in IEC 61508) — never use an unproven toolchain for SIL 2+.
- [x] **Pass/fail metrics are quantitative** — never use vague "the test passed" without a number.
- [x] **Configuration management** of the V&V plan itself is part of the plan (version, approvals, change history).
- [x] **For higher SILs (3, 4)**, the V&V Plan must explicitly mandate *formal* methods (e.g., model checking, theorem proving) and *independent third-party* (IR4) sign-off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Requirement Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Requirement Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** is the formally documented set of qualitative and quantitative safety requirements that must be satisfied during the design, implementation, installation, commissioning, operation, and maintenance of an Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE) safety-related system, throughout its entire safety lifecycle. The SRS is a mandatory deliverable of **Phase 5** of the IEC 61508 safety lifecycle and serves as the contractual and technical bridge between the hazard and risk analysis (Phase 4) and the subsequent design and implementation phases (Phases 7–13).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT635 Module 3):**
> The Safety Requirement Specification is derived from the hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA) and must specify:
> - **Safety functions** to be performed (what the system must do)
> - **Safety integrity requirements** (how reliably it must do it)
> - **Environmental, operational, and maintenance constraints**
> - **Behaviour under fault conditions**
> - **Interfaces with other systems**

The SRS is defined in **IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10** (Safety requirements specification) and elaborated operationally in **IEC 61508-2, Clause 3** (Conformance and compliance criteria).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Building Blueprint Analogy:**
> Imagine you are commissioning a fire-safety system for a 50-storey office tower. Before the electricians lay a single wire, the architect hands them a document that says:
> 1. *WHAT must work*: "If smoke is detected on Floor 25, all magnetic door locks on that floor must release within 3 seconds."
> 2. *HOW RELIABLY*: "This must work with a probability of failure of less than 10⁻⁷ per hour."
> 3. *WHEN ELSE (Fault Mode)*: "Even if the smoke sensor on Floor 25 fails short-circuited, the system must raise an alarm within 30 seconds."
> 4. *ENVIRONMENT*: "Must operate between 0 °C and 55 °C, in 95 % non-condensing humidity."
>
> That single document — combining *function*, *integrity*, *fault behaviour*, and *environment* — is exactly what a **Safety Requirement Specification** is. It is the **"safety blueprint"** handed from the risk analysts to the system designers. Without it, designers either over-engineer (wasting cost) or under-engineer (risking lives).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

| Term | Formal Definition | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Safety Function (SF)** | A function to be implemented by an E/E/PE safety-related system, other technology safety-related system, or external risk reduction facility, that is intended to achieve or maintain a safe state for the EUC | IEC 61508-4, §3.4.1 |
| **Safety Integrity** | The likelihood of a safety-related system satisfactorily performing the required safety functions under all the stated conditions within a stated period of time | IEC 61508-4, §3.5.2 |
| **Safe State** | A state of the EUC where the hazard does not exist or the risk is reduced to an acceptable level | IEC 61508-4, §3.1.6 |
| **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** | A discrete level (1–4) for specifying the safety integrity requirements of the safety functions to be allocated to E/E/PE safety-related systems | IEC 61508-4, §3.5.6 |
| **PFH / PFD** | **Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour** / **on Demand** | IEC 61508-1, Tables 2 & 3 |
| **EUC** | Equipment Under Control | IEC 61508-4, §3.2.1 |
| **HFT** | Hardware Fault Tolerance | IEC 61508-2, §7.4.4 |
| **SFF** | Safe Failure Fraction | IEC 61508-4, §3.6.4 |
| **λ_D, λ_S** | Dangerous / Safe failure rates (per hour) | IEC 61508-4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Quantitative Targets per IEC 61508-1 (Tables 2 & 3):**
> - **SIL 1** → PFD = **10⁻² to 10⁻¹**, PFH = **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵** per hour
> - **SIL 2** → PFD = **10⁻³ to 10⁻²**, PFH = **10⁻⁷ to 10⁻⁶** per hour
> - **SIL 3** → PFD = **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³**, PFH = **10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁷** per hour
> - **SIL 4** → PFD = **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴**, PFH = **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁸** per hour
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition — The 11 Mandatory Components of an SRS

Per **IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10**, the SRS must contain the following eleven information elements. Each is presented below with its *Why* and *How* for clarity.

#### A. Description of the Equipment Under Control (EUC) and Its Environment

- **Why needed**: The SRS must unambiguously identify *what* is being protected and the boundary conditions of the plant/process.
- **How**: Include physical layout, operating environment, normal/abnormal operating modes, interfaces with non-safety systems, and identification of the EUC control system (if any).

#### B. Safety Functions and Their Associated Safety Integrity Levels

- **Why needed**: This is the **core deliverable** of the SRS — translating HARA into actionable functions.
- **How**: Each hazard identified in Phase 4 must yield one or more Safety Functions, each tagged with a target **SIL**. The SIL is derived from:
  - Severity (S) of the harm
  - Probability (W) of occurrence
  - Possibility (P) of avoidance

#### C. Operating Modes of the EUC

- **Why needed**: The same hazard may manifest differently in *Start-up*, *Normal Run*, *Maintenance*, *Shut-down*, or *Re-start* modes.
- **How**: For every Safety Function, list all operating modes in which it must be active. Example: An emergency-stop SF must remain active during *Maintenance* mode.

#### D. Boundaries of the Safety-Related Systems

- **Why needed**: Defines the physical, logical, and temporal limits of the safety system.
- **How**: Specify what is *inside* the safety envelope (sensors, logic solver, actuators) and what is *outside* (EUC itself, external relief valves, etc.).

#### E. Interfaces Between the Safety-Related System and Other Systems

- **Why needed**: Interfaces are common failure points (EMI, ground loops, common-mode software bugs).
- **How**: List every data exchange, electrical connection, mechanical coupling, and human-machine interface (HMI).

#### F. Required Behaviour of Each Safety Function

- **Why needed**: Designers need precise deterministic behaviour — *when*, *what action*, *how fast*, *for how long*.
- **How**: Specify response time, accuracy, stability, and the safe state definition.

#### G. Fault Behaviour and Diagnostics

- **Why needed**: IEC 61508 requires explicit handling of all single (and in higher SILs, multiple) faults.
- **How**: For each SF, define:
  - **Diagnostic coverage (DC)** required
  - **Repair time** assumed
  - **Proof-test interval (T_proof)**
  - **Behaviour on detected vs. undetected faults**

#### H. Environmental and Operational Constraints

- **Why needed**: The SRS must define the climatic, mechanical, and electromagnetic envelope.
- **How**: Specify temperature, humidity, vibration, shock, EMC class, dust, and chemical exposure.

#### I. Maintenance and Proof-Test Requirements

- **Why needed**: SIL cannot be maintained without periodic proof testing.
- **How**: Define who, what, when, how the proof test is performed, and the maximum acceptable outage.

#### J. Safety-Related Systems Decomposition / Architecture

- **Why needed**: A single SF may be realised by 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, or 2oo3 architectures; the SRS must declare intent.
- **How**: State HFT, voting logic, and redundancy philosophy.

#### K. Verification Requirements and Validation Plan

- **Why needed**: The SRS must be **verifiable** (SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable).
- **How**: For each requirement, list the verification method (analysis, test, inspection).

### 2.2 The Risk-Graph / SIL Determination Methodology (KTU High-Yield)

The IEC 61508-5 risk graph is used to convert hazard characteristics into a SIL target. The four parameters are:

- **C** = Consequence (loss severity)
- **F** = Frequency of occurrence of the hazard
- **P** = Possibility of avoiding the hazard
- **W** = Probability of the unwanted occurrence

The risk reduction factor (RRF) required is given by:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{required}}}$$

For a safety function operating in **high-demand / continuous mode**, the corresponding metric is the PFH:

$$\text{RRF}_{\text{cont}} = \frac{1}{\text{PFH}_{\text{required}} \times T_{\text{mission}}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — Safe Characters)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Standard Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | $\overline{PFD} = \int_0^{T_{proof}} PFD(t) \cdot dt \div T_{proof}$ | Range: 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻¹ (SIL 4 → SIL 1) |
| 2 | PFD for 1oo1 architecture | $\overline{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE}$ | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2} + MTTR$ |
| 3 | PFD for 1oo2 architecture | $\overline{PFD}_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$ | $\beta$ = common-cause factor (typically 5–10 %) |
| 4 | PFD for 2oo2 architecture | $\overline{PFD}_{2oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_D \cdot t_{CE} - 2 \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$ | Same redundancy, different voting |
| 5 | PFD for 2oo3 architecture | $\overline{PFD}_{2oo3} = 6 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$ | Sensor-vote majority |
| 6 | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | Bound: 60 % to 99 % (Type B, HFT ≥ 1) |
| 7 | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Range: 0 % to 100 % |
| 8 | PFH for 1oo1 | $\lambda_D$ | Failures per hour |
| 9 | PFH for 1oo2 | $2 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot t_{CE}$ | $\vert t_{CE} \vert$ in hours |
| 10 | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Target Failure Measure (Tolerable) | $\text{TF} = \dfrac{R_f}{P_0 \cdot C}$ | Probabilistic hazard threshold |
| 12 | Architectural Constraint Check | $\text{HFT} = N - (N-1)\cdot \text{?}$ | Routes to Route 1H / 1S per IEC 61508-2 Table 2 |
| 13 | Mission-Time Equivalent | $T_{mission} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFH}}$ | Hours between dangerous failures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering**: The SRS is *not* an aspirational document. It is a **legally and contractually binding** specification used during:
> - **Tender evaluation** (vendor capability matching)
> - **Independent Safety Assessment (ISA)** per IEC 61508-1 §8
> - **Exida / TÜV / FMEDA** audit trails
> - **Plant insurance & regulatory submissions**
> - **After-incident root-cause analysis** (the SRS is the baseline)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Hazard to Safety Function (The HARA-to-SRS Pipeline)

This is the **most exam-relevant derivation** in Module 3. The student must show how a single hazard from the HARA is decomposed into a complete SRS entry.

**Step 1: Define the EUC Operating State**

A centrifugal compressor in a petrochemical plant is the EUC.

$$\text{EUC: Compressor C-101}, \quad \text{Operating pressure: } P_{op} \in [5, 12] \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Identify a Hazard (from HARA)**

$$H_1: \text{Overpressure beyond } P_{op} > 14 \text{ bar causing rupture}$$

**Step 3: Quantify the Consequence**

Severity Category (C) per IEC 61508-5 Annex E:

$$C_{H_1} = C_4 \quad \text{(Catastrophic: fatality, multiple injuries, loss of plant)}$$

**Step 4: Estimate Hazard Frequency (F) and Occurrence Probability (W)**

$$F_{H_1} = F_2 \quad \text{(Frequent: occurs several times per year)}$$

$$W_{H_1} = W_2 \quad \text{(Slight chance: process can be brought to safe state)}$$

$$P_{H_1} = P_2 \quad \text{(Possible under certain conditions)}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Risk Graph to Obtain SIL**

The risk graph cross-references (C, F, P, W) into a discrete SIL bucket:

$$\text{Risk Graph Output: } \text{SIL}_{H_1} = \text{SIL 2}$$

**Step 6: Convert SIL 2 to Quantitative Target**

From the IEC 61508-1 Table (low-demand mode assumed):

$$\text{PFD}_{target} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$$

Choose midpoint:

$$\text{PFD}_{target} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7: Define the Safe State**

$$\text{Safe State: } P_{EU C} < 10 \text{ bar AND isolation valves closed}$$

**Step 8: Specify the Safety Function (SF)**

$$SF_{H_1}: \text{If } P > 13 \text{ bar, close inlet valve within } 2 \text{ s AND open vent within } 1.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 9: Specify Fault Behaviour**

- Detected sensor failure → hardwired bypass not permitted; trip alarm to DCS within 0.5 s
- Undetected failure → proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (1 year)
- DC required: $\geq 90\%$

**Step 10: Specify Architecture**

$$\text{Architecture: 2oo3 voting on pressure transmitters, SIL 2, HFT = 1}$$

**Step 11: Verification Method**

$$V_{SF_{H_1}}: \text{Type-test response time} \leq 2 \text{ s}, \text{FMEDA verification}, \text{EMC test per IEC 61000-4}$$

**Result**: A complete, traceable SRS entry exists for hazard $H_1$.

$$\boxed{\text{SRS Entry}_{H_1} = \{C_4, F_2, P_2, W_2, \text{SIL 2}, \text{PFD} = 5\times10^{-3}, \text{2oo3, DC}\geq 90\%\}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Architecture Decomposition for SIL 2

Given:

- $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7} / \text{h}$
- $\lambda_S = 3 \times 10^{-7} / \text{h}$
- $\beta = 5\%$
- $T_{proof} = 8760$ h
- $MTTR = 8$ h

Compute the Channel Equivalent Mean Down Time:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{CE} &= \frac{T_{proof}}{2} + \text{MTTR} \\
t_{CE} &= \frac{8760}{2} + 8 \\
t_{CE} &= 4380 + 8 \\
t_{CE} &= 4388 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the Safe Failure Fraction:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DC} &= \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D = 0.90 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{DD} = 0.9 \times 5\times 10^{-7} = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} / \text{h} \\
\lambda_{DU} &= \lambda_D - \lambda_{DD} = 0.5 \times 10^{-7} / \text{h} \\
\text{SFF} &= \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = \frac{3.0\times 10^{-7} + 4.5\times 10^{-7}}{3.0\times 10^{-7} + 5.0\times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{7.5\times 10^{-7}}{8.0\times 10^{-7}} \\
\text{SFF} &= 0.9375 = 93.75\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{meets SIL 2 SFF criteria (} \geq 60\% \text{ for Type B HFT=1)}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute PFD for 1oo2 architecture (sensor redundancy):

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{PFD}_{1oo2} &= 2 (1-\beta) \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2 \\
&= 2 \times 0.95 \times (0.5\times 10^{-7})^2 \times (4388)^2 \\
&= 2 \times 0.95 \times 0.25\times 10^{-14} \times 1.9255\times 10^{7} \\
&= 2 \times 0.95 \times 4.814 \times 10^{-8} \\
\overline{PFD}_{1oo2} &\approx 9.15 \times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$$

This is far below the SIL 2 target of $10^{-2}$, satisfying the **PFDavg** requirement. The architectural constraint and PFDavg must both be met for full SIL 2 compliance.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SRS Quality Attributes

| Attribute | Definition | Test Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Each requirement reflects the actual hazard need | Does the SF eliminate the hazard? |
| **Completeness** | All HARA hazards have SRS entries | Is every hazard from §4 covered? |
| **Consistency** | No internal contradiction | Do all PFD requirements sum coherently? |
| **Clarity** | Single, unambiguous interpretation | Can two designers produce identical systems? |
| **Verifiability** | Each requirement can be tested/proved | What test proves DC ≥ 90 %? |
| **Traceability** | Bi-directional link HARA ↔ SRS ↔ Design | Can I trace SF to hazard & to FMEDA? |
| **Modifiability** | Updates do not break logic | Can a SIL upgrade be accommodated? |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping SRS Components to IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10

| IEC 61508-1 § | Mandatory SRS Element | HARA Linkage |
|---|---|---|
| 7.10.1 | Description of EUC + environment | §5 (EUC definition) |
| 7.10.2 | Safety functions & SIL targets | §6 (HARA output) |
| 7.10.3 | Operating modes | §5 |
| 7.10.4 | Boundary of safety-related system | §4 (boundary analysis) |
| 7.10.5 | Interfaces to other systems | §4 |
| 7.10.6 | Required behaviour of each SF | §6 |
| 7.10.7 | Fault behaviour & diagnostics | §7 (FMEDA precursor) |
| 7.10.8 | Environmental constraints | §5 |
| 7.10.9 | Maintenance & proof-test | §14 |
| 7.10.10 | Decomposition & architecture | §8 (design phase input) |
| 7.10.11 | Verification & validation | §13, §14 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Safety Lifecycle Position of the SRS (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1[Phase 1: Concept] --> phase2[Phase 2: Overall Scope Definition]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Hazard & Risk Analysis HARA]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: Overall Safety Requirements]
    phase4 --> srs[Phase 5: Safety Requirement Specification SRS]
    srs --> phase6[Phase 6: Safety-Related Systems Realisation Planning]
    phase6 --> phase7[Phase 7: E/E/PE System Design]
    phase6 --> phase8[Phase 8: Software Design]
    phase6 --> phase9[Phase 9: Hardware Integration]
    phase7 --> phase10[Phase 10: Installation Commissioning]
    phase8 --> phase10
    phase9 --> phase10
    phase10 --> phase11[Phase 11: Overall Validation]
    phase11 --> phase12[Phase 12: Operation Maintenance Modification]
    phase12 --> phase13[Phase 13: End of Life Decommissioning]
    phase13 --> phase14[Phase 14: Functional Safety Audit]
    srs -. feedback loop .-> phase3
    phase11 -. feedback loop .-> srs
    phase14 -. feedback loop .-> srs
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The SRS (Phase 5) sits at the *apex of design direction* in the safety lifecycle. All subsequent phases (6–13) trace their requirements **back** to it. The dashed feedback arrows indicate that SRS deficiencies discovered during Validation (Phase 11) or Audit (Phase 14) trigger revision of the SRS and, consequently, re-validation of upstream HARA results.

---

### 4.2 Internal Structure of the SRS Document (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srsRoot[SRS Master Document] --> euBlock[EUC Description Section]
    srsRoot --> sfBlock[Safety Function Inventory]
    srsRoot --> silBlock[SIL Allocation Matrix]
    srsRoot --> archBlock[Architecture and Redundancy]
    srsRoot --> faultBlock[Fault Behaviour and Diagnostics]
    srsRoot --> envBlock[Environmental Constraints]
    srsRoot --> modeBlock[Operating Mode Map]
    srsRoot --> ifBlock[Interface Definition Block]
    srsRoot --> verBlock[Verification Matrix]
    srsRoot --> maintBlock[Maintenance and Proof-Test Plan]
    srsRoot --> traceBlock[Traceability Matrix]

    sfBlock --> sf1[SF01 Pressure Trip]
    sfBlock --> sf2[SF02 Over-Temperature Trip]
    sfBlock --> sf3[SF03 Emergency Shutdown ESD]
    sfBlock --> sf4[SF04 Fire Detection and Suppression]

    silBlock --> sil1[SIL Target Lookup Table]
    silBlock --> sil2[PFD and PFH Allocation]

    traceBlock --> t1[HARA Hazard Link]
    traceBlock --> t2[Design Specification Link]
```
