```mermaid
    traceBlock --> t3[Validation Test Link]
```

---

### 4.3 SRS Requirements Hierarchy (Top-Down Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    goal[Overall Functional Safety Goal] --> pri1[Primary Safety Requirement 1]
    goal --> pri2[Primary Safety Requirement 2]
    goal --> pri3[Primary Safety Requirement 3]

    pri1 --> sec1[Secondary SR 1.1 SIL 2]
    pri1 --> sec2[Secondary SR 1.2 SIL 1]
    pri1 --> sec3[Secondary SR 1.3 SIL 3]

    sec1 --> ter1[Tertiary SR 1.1.1 PFD target]
    sec1 --> ter2[Tertiary SR 1.1.2 Response time]
    sec1 --> ter3[Tertiary SR 1.1.3 Diagnostic coverage]

    sec3 --> ter4[Tertiary SR 1.3.1 Architecture 2oo3]
    sec3 --> ter5[Tertiary SR 1.3.2 Proof-test interval]
    sec3 --> ter6[Tertiary SR 1.3.3 SFF target]

    pri2 --> sec4[Secondary SR 2.1 SIL 2]
    pri3 --> sec5[Secondary SR 3.1 SIL 1]
```

---

### 4.4 SRS Verification Flow (V-Model Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srs[SRS Document] --> v1[Verification by Inspection]
    srs --> v2[Verification by Analysis]
    srs --> v3[Verification by Type Test]
    srs --> v4[Verification by Statistical FMEDA]
    srs --> v5[Verification by Operating Experience]

    v1 --> isa[Independent Safety Assessment]
    v2 --> isa
    v3 --> isa
    v4 --> isa
    v5 --> isa

    isa --> accept{Acceptance Decision}
    accept -->|Pass| signOff[Document Sign-off]
    accept -->|Fail| revise[Revise SRS and Repeat]
    revise --> srs
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway**: The SRS is the *single document* that ties every phase of the safety lifecycle together. It is the "contract" between the **analyst** (HARA), the **designer** (E/E/PE), the **validator** (test), and the **operator** (maintenance). A weak SRS propagates weakness into every downstream phase.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

> **Define Safety Requirement Specification (SRS) as per IEC 61508. List any four mandatory elements that must be included in an SRS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Safety Requirement Specification (SRS)** is a formally documented set of qualitative and quantitative safety requirements derived from the hazard and risk analysis that must be satisfied by the E/E/PE safety-related system throughout its safety lifecycle, as mandated by **IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10**.

Four mandatory elements:

1. Description of the **Equipment Under Control (EUC)** and its environment.
2. The **safety functions** to be performed and their associated **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)**.
3. The **required behaviour** of each safety function, including response time and diagnostic coverage.
4. The **fault behaviour** under single and (where applicable) multiple faults, including proof-test interval.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A)**: Students often write "SRS lists the safety functions" *only*. To get full 3 marks you **must** explicitly name the standard (IEC 61508) and provide **four** distinct items.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

> **What is the difference between SIL and SFF? Why is the SFF parameter relevant while writing the SRS for a Type B safety-related system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** is a *discrete level (1 to 4)* indicating the *target reliability* (PFD/PFH) a safety function must achieve. It is a **performance target**.
- **SFF (Safe Failure Fraction)** is the *ratio of safe + diagnostically detected dangerous failures to total failures*. It is a **hardware characteristic** of the subsystem: $\text{SFF} = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}) / (\lambda_S + \lambda_D)$.

For a **Type B safety-related system** with HFT ≥ 1, IEC 61508-2 Table 3 mandates an SFF **≥ 60 %** for SIL 2 and **≥ 90 %** for SIL 3. Without meeting SFF, the system **fails the architectural constraint** even if its calculated PFDavg meets the SIL target. Therefore, SRS writers must specify the *minimum acceptable SFF* for every subsystem.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 3 Choice A]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **eleven mandatory elements of the Safety Requirement Specification** as per IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pressure transmitter subsystem of a SIL 2 safety instrumented function has the following parameters:
> - $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h
> - $\lambda_S = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ /h
> - Diagnostic Coverage (DC) = 90 %
> - Proof-test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ h
> - MTTR = 8 h
> - Common-cause factor $\beta = 5\%$
>
> Determine (i) $\lambda_{DU}$, (ii) SFF, (iii) $\overline{PFD}_{1oo2}$, and (iv) verify whether the system meets SIL 2 requirements for a Type B sensor with HFT = 1. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**:

The SRS document, per **IEC 61508-1, Clause 7.10**, must contain the following eleven mandatory elements:

1. **Description of the EUC** and its operating environment (physical, functional, operational).
2. **Safety functions** to be performed by the safety-related system, with each function having a **defined safe state**.
3. **Safety integrity requirements** (SIL targets) for each safety function.
4. **Operating modes** of the EUC (start-up, normal, maintenance, shut-down) during which the safety function must operate.
5. **Boundaries** of the safety-related system (sensors, logic solver, actuators).
6. **Interfaces** between the safety-related system and other systems (electrical, mechanical, data).
7. **Required behaviour** of each safety function — response time, accuracy, stability, robustness.
8. **Fault behaviour and diagnostics** — diagnostic coverage, repair time, proof-test interval, response to detected vs. undetected faults.
9. **Environmental and operational constraints** — temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC, dust.
10. **Maintenance and proof-test requirements** — frequency, method, test coverage, acceptable outage.
11. **Verification requirements** and the validation plan, including test method and acceptance criteria.

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer script)**:

```
                ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
                │     SAFETY REQUIREMENT SPECIFICATION   │
                ├────────────────────────────────────────┤
                │  1.  EUC Description                   │
                │  2.  Safety Functions (SF List)        │
                │  3.  SIL Targets (PFD/PFH Allocation)  │
                │  4.  Operating Modes                   │
                │  5.  System Boundaries                 │
                │  6.  Interface Definitions             │
                │  7.  Required Behaviour (Response, Acc)│
                │  8.  Fault Behaviour + Diagnostics     │
                │  9.  Environmental Constraints         │
                │ 10.  Maintenance + Proof-Test Plan     │
                │ 11.  Verification & Validation Plan    │
                └────────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │   HARA Traceability Matrix │
                │ (Hazard → SF → SIL → SF ID)│
                └────────────────────────────┘
```

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**:

**(i) Compute $\lambda_{DU}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DD} &= \text{DC} \times \lambda_D = 0.90 \times 5\times 10^{-7} = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /h} \\
\lambda_{DU} &= \lambda_D - \lambda_{DD} = 5\times 10^{-7} - 4.5\times 10^{-7} \\
\boxed{\lambda_{DU}} &= 0.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Compute SFF**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SFF} &= \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} \\
&= \frac{3.0\times 10^{-7} + 4.5\times 10^{-7}}{3.0\times 10^{-7} + 5.0\times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{7.5\times 10^{-7}}{8.0\times 10^{-7}} \\
\boxed{\text{SFF}} &= 0.9375 = 93.75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Compute $\overline{PFD}_{1oo2}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{CE} &= \frac{T_{proof}}{2} + \text{MTTR} = \frac{8760}{2} + 8 = 4388 \text{ h} \\
\overline{PFD}_{1oo2} &= 2 (1-\beta) \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2 \\
&= 2 \times 0.95 \times (0.5\times 10^{-7})^2 \times (4388)^2 \\
&= 2 \times 0.95 \times 0.25\times 10^{-14} \times 1.9255\times 10^{7} \\
\boxed{\overline{PFD}_{1oo2}} &\approx 9.15 \times 10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) SIL 2 Compliance Check**

- **PFDavg target (SIL 2, low-demand)** = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$.
- **Calculated** = $9.15 \times 10^{-8}$ ✅ (well within range).
- **SFF target (Type B, HFT=1, SIL 2)** = $\geq 60\%$.
- **Calculated SFF** = $93.75\%$ ✅.

**Conclusion**: The subsystem satisfies **both** the probabilistic (PFDavg) and architectural-constraint (SFF) requirements for **SIL 2**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 3 Choice B]

> **(a)** Explain the **risk-graph method** of determining the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) for a safety function. Describe each of the four parameters (C, F, P, W) and their standard categorisation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **attributes of a good Safety Requirement Specification**. Why is **traceability** considered the most important attribute in a Functional Safety Management System? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**:

The **Risk-Graph Method** is a semi-quantitative technique (described in **IEC 61508-5, Annex E** and IEC 61511-3) used to translate qualitative hazard characteristics into a target SIL for each safety function. It is preferred over pure numerical methods when data are sparse or when a project must be rapidly assessed.

**The Four Parameters**:

| Parameter | Name | Categories & Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | Consequence | C1: minor injury; C2: serious injury (one person); C3: death (one person); C4: multiple fatalities / catastrophe |
| **F** | Frequency of exposure to hazard | F1: rare; F2: occasional (yearly); F3: frequent (monthly); F4: continuous (always present) |
| **P** | Possibility of avoidance | P1: avoidable under all conditions; P2: avoidable under some conditions; P3: hardly avoidable |
| **W** | Probability of unwanted occurrence | W1: very low probability; W2: low; W3: relatively high |

The risk-graph combines these in a directed graph and outputs one of five possible decisions:

$$\text{Risk Graph: } \{C, F, P, W\} \longrightarrow \text{SIL} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

The output SIL is then translated into a quantitative PFD/PFH target via the IEC 61508-1 Table 2/3.

**Worked Example**:

A chemical reactor has: Catastrophic consequence (C4), Frequent exposure (F3), Avoidable with early warning (P2), Relatively high occurrence (W3).

$$\text{Risk Graph}(C_4, F_3, P_2, W_3) \longrightarrow \text{SIL 3}$$

The SRS must then specify for the corresponding safety function:

$$\text{PFD}_{target} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}], \quad \text{SFF} \geq 90\%$$

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**:

The seven canonical attributes of a good SRS are:

1. **Correctness** — Each requirement correctly addresses the identified hazard.
2. **Completeness** — All hazards from the HARA are covered.
3. **Consistency** — No internal contradictions (e.g., two SFs cannot require mutually exclusive actions).
4. **Clarity** — Single, unambiguous interpretation by any qualified designer.
5. **Verifiability** — Each requirement can be checked by analysis, test, or inspection.
6. **Modifiability** — Changes in HARA propagate cleanly to SRS updates.
7. **Traceability** — Every requirement has a bi-directional link to its origin (hazard) and its implementation (design, code, test).

**Why Traceability is Paramount in Functional Safety**:

1. **Audit Defence**: Regulators (TÜV, FMEDA auditors, KTU examiners) demand a chain of evidence. Traceability provides it in a single matrix.
2. **Change Impact Analysis**: A SIL change in one hazard propagates through traceability links to every affected SF, design, and test case.
3. **Incident Investigation**: After a real-world incident, the SRS-to-HARA traceability matrix lets investigators identify whether the *requirement* was missing, the *design* failed, or the *operation* violated the spec.
4. **Compliance with IEC 61508-1, Clause 5** which explicitly requires traceability of safety requirements throughout the lifecycle.
5. **Independent Safety Assessment (ISA)** under IEC 61508-1 §8 is *impossible* without traceability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B — 14 Marks)**:
> - Students frequently **forget to specify PFD/PFH units** (per hour for high-demand, dimensionless for low-demand) — expect **2-mark penalty** per occurrence.
> - In architecture calculations, students often **omit the $(1-\beta)$ factor** for 1oo2/2oo3 PFD — this is a **3-mark penalty**.
> - Students confuse **SIL (performance)** with **HFT (architecture)** — the SRS must specify **both**. Missing HFT = 2-mark penalty.
> - In the SRS elements list, only naming 5 of the 11 elements will cap your mark at 3 of 7 for that sub-part.
> - Always show **all four steps** of SIL verification (PFDavg, SFF, architectural constraint, systematic capability) for full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Bank** (for KTU ESE Module 3):

- ✅ **SRS Definition** = Output of IEC 61508 Phase 5, derived from HARA, input to Phases 6–13.
- ✅ **SRS stands on 11 elements** as per IEC 61508-1 §7.10 — memorise the list (EUCC, SFs, SILs, Modes, Boundaries, Interfaces, Behaviour, Faults, Environment, Maintenance, Verification).
- ✅ **SIL is discrete (1–4)**; **SFF is a ratio (%)**; **HFT is architecture (integer)**; **DC is a ratio (%)**; **PFD is dimensionless**; **PFH is per-hour**.
- ✅ **Low-demand mode** uses PFD; **high-demand / continuous mode** uses PFH.
- ✅ **1oo2 PFD formula**: $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2$ — multiply by $\beta$ correction for common-cause failures.
- ✅ **t_CE = T_proof/2 + MTTR** — the channel-equivalent mean downtime.
- ✅ **SFF ≥ 60 % (Type B, HFT=1, SIL 2)**; **SFF ≥ 90 % (Type B, HFT=1, SIL 3)**.
- ✅ **Risk-Graph parameters** are C, F, P, W — never confuse them.
- ✅ **Traceability** is the *most-examined attribute* in KTU Module 3 questions.
- ✅ The SRS is **verifiable** (SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable).
- ✅ **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** is satisfied by **redundancy**, not by diagnostics.
- ✅ **Diagnostics improve SFF and DC**, but do **not** reduce HFT requirement.
- ✅ An SRS that omits **fault behaviour** automatically fails IEC 61508 conformance.
- ✅ The SRS **must be revised** if HARA outputs change; this triggers a re-validation cycle (Phase 11).
- ✅ **Kerala KTU-specific**: Always cite **IEC 61508-1 Clause 7.10** explicitly in answers; examiners award bonus credit for standard clause references.
- ✅ For **modular exam preparation**: Memorise the **11-element list**, the **4 risk-graph parameters**, and **at least three PFD formulas** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2).
- ✅ The SRS is a **living document** — it is version-controlled, signed off by an Independent Safety Assessor, and reviewed at every phase boundary.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Topper's Strategy for Module 3**: In every long-answer question, open with the standard reference (e.g., "Per IEC 61508-1 §7.10..."), define each symbol used, show the formula with **all substituted numbers**, include **units** at every step, and **end with a compliance verdict** ("Therefore the system meets SIL X requirement").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIS Failure Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SIS Failure Management

# SIS Failure Management — Comprehensive KTU Study Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context:** This topic falls under **Module 3** of PESFT635 (Functional Safety) and directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Apply IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 principles for SIS failure classification, proof testing, and reliability quantification."* Expect high-weightage questions (14-mark full modules) on this topic.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is SIS Failure Management?

**Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Failure Management** is the systematic engineering discipline of identifying, classifying, detecting, predicting, and mitigating failures within the hardware and software components of a Safety Instrumented System, so that the probability of the SIS failing to perform its safety function (i.e., a *dangerous failure*) remains within the tolerable risk boundary defined by the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

Formally, per **IEC 61508-1 (2010)**, failure management encompasses the lifecycle activities that ensure:

$$
\text{Risk Reduction}_{\text{SIS}} \;\geq\; \text{Required Risk Reduction}_{\text{SIL Target}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.6.7):**
> A **failure** is the *termination of the ability of a functional unit to perform a required function*. **SIS Failure Management** is the structured set of technical and procedural measures to prevent, detect, control, and document such failures throughout the safety lifecycle (Phases 1–16 of IEC 61508-1).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airbag System" in Your Car

Imagine the **airbag system** in your car:

- The airbag is an SIS — it sits dormant but is ready to fire.
- A **safe failure** = the airbag warning light comes on. The car refuses to drive (system goes to a safe state). *Annoying but no accident.*
- A **dangerous failure** = the airbag warning light is **off**, but internally the igniter wire is corroded. The system *thinks* it is healthy, but it will not fire when needed. *This is the killer.*
- **Failure management** = the self-diagnostic circuit that runs a check every time you start the car (diagnostic coverage), the periodic crash-test certification by manufacturers (proof testing), and the design choice of having *two independent igniters* (hardware fault tolerance).

**The hidden killer of safety systems is not the failure you see — it is the failure you don't see.**

### 1.3 The Failure Universe at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Failure Decomposition (IEC 61508 Framework):**
>
> **Total Failure Rate:**
> $$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_{\text{safe}} + \lambda_{\text{dangerous}}$$
>
> **Each split further by Detection:**
> $$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$$

Where the standard notations are:

| Symbol | Meaning | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **λ_SD** | Safe Detected | System trips → safe state, operator alerted |
| **λ_SU** | Safe Undetected | Spurious trip only, no danger |
| **λ_DD** | Dangerous Detected | Auto-shutdown via diagnostics — failure contained |
| **λ_DU** | Dangerous Undetected | **MOST CRITICAL** — system silently unsafe |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Failure Classification Logic (Hierarchical Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Categorize by Effect on Safety Function:**
- **Safe failure (λ_S):** Causes the SIS to move to a safe (often tripped) state without a process demand. Examples: relay coil short-to-ground in a de-energize-to-trip system; sensor output stuck at high.
- **Dangerous failure (λ_D):** Prevents the SIS from executing its safety function when a process demand occurs. Examples: solenoid valve stuck open; transmitter diaphragm rupture reading low pressure.

**Step 2 — Subcategorize by Diagnostics:**
- **Detected failure:** Automatically revealed by the SIS's internal self-tests (e.g., watchdog timer, stuck-bit check, current loop monitoring) within the diagnostic test interval τ.
- **Undetected failure:** Lies dormant until discovered manually or until a proof test reveals it.

**Step 3 — Why "Why" Matters:**
The classification drives the **PFH/PFDavg** calculation. Only **λ_DU** directly degrades the probability of failure on demand. **λ_DD** is mitigated by diagnostic response time. **λ_SU/SD** affect spurious trip rate (STR) but not the dangerous metric.

### 2.2 Diagnostic Coverage (DC) and Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** is the *ratio of detected dangerous failures to total dangerous failures*. Per IEC 61508-2, Annex A, DC can only take standardized values: **0%, 60%, 90%, 99%**.

$$
\text{DC} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is the *fraction of all failures (safe + dangerous) that do NOT result in a dangerous-undetected state*. It is the master gatekeeper for SIL capability.

$$
\text{SFF} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Real-world utility:** SFF is the single number a safety engineer reports on a component datasheet. A transmitter rated **SFF = 95%** with **DC = 90%** can support SIL 2 in a 1oo1 architecture but only after the proof test interval is verified.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** In Part B questions, examiners expect you to **state the formula, define every symbol, substitute with units, and present the answer with correct significant figures**. Memorize the table below verbatim.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Unit / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ | Per failure mode | per hour (FIT = 10⁻⁹/h) | FMEDA breakdown |
| 2 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | % (0–99) | Diagnostics effectiveness |
| 3 | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | % (0–100) | SIL capability (Route 2H) |
| 4 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours / years | Reliability block |
| 5 | $\text{MTTR}$ = Diagnostic Test Interval (ideal) | Mean Time To Repair | hours | PFDavg input |
| 6 | $\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | Avg Prob. Failure on Demand | dimensionless | Low-demand SIL 1 |
| 7 | $\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T)^2}{3}$ | Avg PFD for 1oo2 voted | dimensionless | Low-demand SIL 2/3 |
| 8 | $\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_{\text{DU}}$ | Prob. Failure per Hour | per hour (h⁻¹) | High-demand / SIL 1 |
| 9 | $\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo2}} = 2\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot T$ | PFH for 1oo2 voted | per hour | High-demand SIL 2/3 |
| 10 | $\text{STR}_{\text{1oo1}} = \lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}}$ | Spurious Trip Rate | per hour | Availability analysis |
| 11 | $\beta\text{-factor model: } \lambda_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{D}}$ | Common Cause Failure | fraction | Redundancy penalty |
| 12 | $\text{PFDavg}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{PFDavg}_i$ | SIS loop summation | dimensionless | Entire loop budget |

### 2.4 Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) — The Architecture Lever

A failure is dangerous **only if** it overwhelms the architecture's tolerance. **HFT = N** means the system can survive **N** dangerous faults *without* loss of the safety function.

- **HFT = 0 (1oo1):** Single channel. One fault → dangerous failure.
- **HFT = 1 (1oo2 / 2oo2):** Two channels. Tolerates one fault. 2oo2 demands agreement; 1oo2 tolerates one disagreement.
- **HFT = 2 (1oo3 / 2oo3):** Three channels. Tolerates two faults. IEC 61508-2 allows SIL 3 even with low SFF when HFT = 2.

**The Why:** Architectures reduce PFH/PFDavg by orders of magnitude. **PFDavg(1oo2) ≪ PFDavg(1oo1)** because both channels must fail simultaneously.

### 2.5 Proof Testing (T_proof)

A **proof test** is a periodic, manual or semi-automatic verification that the SIS can still perform its safety function. It is the *only* practical method to reveal accumulated **λ_DU** faults.

$$
T_{\text{proof}} \;\leq\; \frac{2 \cdot \text{PFDavg}_{\text{required}}}{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** A SIL 3 low-demand system might require $\text{PFDavg} \leq 10^{-3}$. If $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 10^{-6}$/h, then $T_{\text{proof}} \leq 2 \times 10^{-3} / 10^{-6} = 2000$ hours ≈ **83 days**. *Most plants run proof tests annually — verify they truly meet the budget!*

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo1 Architecture

**Premise:** A single channel has a constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$. The system is restored to "as good as new" by a proof test every interval $T$. Failures occur randomly.

**Step 1 — Probability of a single failure in time $t$:**
For a Poisson process with constant failure rate, the probability density of failure occurring at time $t$ is:

$$
f(t) \;=\; \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} t}
$$

**Step 2 — Average Probability of being unable to respond over interval $[0, T]$:**
Given that a failure occurred at time $\tau$ (where $0 \leq \tau \leq T$), the system is unsafe for the remaining duration $(T - \tau)$. The average probability of being in the dangerous state is:

$$
\text{PFDavg} \;=\; \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (T - \tau) \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} \tau} \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the engineering approximation $\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T \ll 1$:**

This assumption holds for low-demand SIS (typical $\lambda_{\text{DU}} < 10^{-5}$/h, $T < 1$ year $\approx 8760$ h). Then $e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}}\tau} \approx 1$.

$$
\text{PFDavg} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (T - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\int_{0}^{T} (T - \tau) \, d\tau \;=\; \left[ T\tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2} \right]_0^T \;=\; T^2 - \frac{T^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{T^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final 1oo1 PFDavg formula:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo1}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T}{2}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/h and $T = 8760$ h (1 year), then PFDavg = $5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 / 2 = 0.0219$. This corresponds to **SIL 1** (PFDavg range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$). Correct.

### 3.2 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo2 Architecture

**Premise:** Two identical independent channels; system fails only when *both* have dangerous undetected faults.

**Step 1 — Joint failure probability density:**
The probability that channel 1 fails at $\tau_1$ and channel 2 fails at $\tau_2$ is:

$$
f(\tau_1, \tau_2) \;=\; \lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}}(\tau_1 + \tau_2)}
$$

**Step 2 — The system is unsafe only after the *second* failure:**
Let $t = \max(\tau_1, \tau_2)$. The unsafe duration is $T - t$.

$$
\text{PFDavg} \;=\; \frac{1}{T^2} \int_0^T \int_0^T (T - \max(\tau_1, \tau_2)) \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \, d\tau_1 \, d\tau_2
$$

**Step 3 — Symmetry reduction:**
By symmetry, integrate over the region $\tau_1 > \tau_2$ and double:

$$
\text{PFDavg} \;=\; \frac{2\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2}{T^2} \int_0^T \int_0^{\tau_1} (T - \tau_1) \, d\tau_2 \, d\tau_1
$$

**Step 4 — Inner integral over $\tau_2$ gives $\tau_1$:**

$$
\text{PFDavg} \;=\; \frac{2\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2}{T^2} \int_0^T \tau_1 (T - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the outer integral:**

$$
\int_0^T \tau_1 (T - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1 \;=\; \left[ \frac{T\tau_1^2}{2} - \frac{\tau_1^3}{3} \right]_0^T \;=\; \frac{T^3}{2} - \frac{T^3}{3} \;=\; \frac{T^3}{6}
$$

**Step 6 — Final 1oo2 PFDavg formula:**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot T^2}{3}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative comparison:** For $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 10^{-5}$/h and $T = 8760$ h:
> - 1oo1 PFDavg = $10^{-5} \times 8760 / 2 = 0.0438$ → SIL 1
> - 1oo2 PFDavg = $(10^{-5})^2 \times (8760)^2 / 3 = 0.00256$ → SIL 2
>
> **Redundancy improves SIL by one level.** This is the core engineering trade-off in SIS design.

### 3.3 Derivation of β-Factor for Common Cause Failures

**Premise:** Redundancy assumes independence. In practice, common cause failures (CCF) violate this — e.g., both pressure transmitters plug with process condensate.

**Step 1 — Split dangerous failures:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{D}} \;=\; \lambda_{\text{D,independent}} + \lambda_{\text{D,common}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define β-factor:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{D,common}}}{\lambda_{\text{D,independent}} + \lambda_{\text{D,common}}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{D,common}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}}
$$

**Step 3 — The CCF component for redundant systems:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{\text{effective}} \;=\; \lambda_{\text{DU}} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} \;=\; (1 + \beta) \lambda_{\text{DU}} \quad \text{(single channel)}
$$

For the redundant 1oo2 channel pair, both channels contribute:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2,CCF}} \;=\; \frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^2}{3} \;+\; \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical demonstration:**
With $\beta = 10\%$ (typical industry value per IEC 61508-6 Annex D), $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 10^{-5}$/h, $T = 8760$ h:

$$
\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2,CCF}} = \frac{(0.0876)^2}{3} + 0.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 = 0.00256 + 0.00876 = 0.01132
$$

Notice that **CCF dominates**: it contributes **3.4× more** to the PFDavg than the independent term. *Ignoring β-factor is the single most common KTU answer penalty.*

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Architecture Selection

| Architecture | HFT | SIL Cap (Route 1H) | PFDavg Formula (low-demand) | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | 0 | SIL 2 (with SFF + DC) | $\frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} T}{2}$ | Non-critical loops, cost-sensitive |
| 1oo2 | 1 | SIL 3 | $\frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^2}{3}$ | High availability, low spurious |
| 2oo2 | 0 | SIL 3 | $\frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^2}{3}$ | High integrity, accept spurious |
| 2oo3 | 1 | SIL 3 | $\approx \frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^2}{3} \cdot \text{factor}$ | Voting, fire & gas systems |
| 1oo3 | 2 | SIL 3 (any SFF) | $\frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^3}{4}$ | Nuclear, highest integrity |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
>
> **Q1.** Define **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** and **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** with reference to IEC 61508. Why are these metrics critical for SIS failure management?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** The fraction of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostics. Formula: $\text{DC} = \lambda_{\text{DD}} / (\lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}})$. Per IEC 61508-2, DC takes standardized values of **0%, 60%, 90%, 99%**. (1 mark)
> - **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** The fraction of *all* failures that do not result in a dangerous-undetected state. Formula: $\text{SFF} = 1 - \lambda_{\text{DU}}/\lambda_{\text{total}}$. (1 mark)
> - **Criticality:** Together, DC and SFF determine the maximum achievable SIL via Route 2H (architectural constraints). Without adequate SFF/DC, even redundant hardware cannot achieve high SIL. (1 mark)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **λ_DD** and **λ_DU**. Why is **λ_DU** called the "silent killer" of SIS?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **λ_DD (Dangerous Detected):** Failure that impairs safety function but is *automatically identified* by internal diagnostics, leading to a controlled safe shutdown. (1 mark)
> - **λ_DU (Dangerous Undetected):** Failure that impairs safety function but *lies hidden* until revealed by proof test or accidental demand. (1 mark)
> - **"Silent Killer" justification:** Because undetected, the system *appears healthy* during routine operation and only fails catastrophically when the process demands protection. It directly enters PFDavg and PFH formulas, dominating the safety budget. No warning, no auto-correction. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 3]`**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A 1oo1 pressure transmitter SIS loop has the following failure data (per IEC 61508 FMEDA):
- $\lambda_{\text{SD}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{\text{SU}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{\text{DD}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- Proof test interval $T = 8760$ h (1 year)

Calculate **(i)** Diagnostic Coverage (DC), **(ii)** Safe Failure Fraction (SFF), **(iii)** PFDavg, and **(iv)** state the achieved SIL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a labelled block diagram how the failure management strategy differs for **low-demand** versus **high-demand** modes of operation. Include the role of PFH in the latter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step:**

**(i) Diagnostic Coverage:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{D}} \;=\; \lambda_{\text{DD}} + \lambda_{\text{DU}} \;=\; 1.8 \times 10^{-6} + 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 2.2 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{/h}
$$

$$
\text{DC} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{D}}} \;=\; \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-6}}{2.2 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 0.818 \;=\; 81.8\%
$$

DC rounded to standard value = **80% (closest to 90% tier; treat as 90% per IEC 61508-2 conservative rounding)**. **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Safe Failure Fraction:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.5 + 1.2 + 1.8 + 0.4 \;=\; 3.9 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{/h}
$$

$$
\text{SFF} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{0.4}{3.9} \;=\; 1 - 0.1026 \;=\; 0.8974 \;=\; 89.74\%
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) PFDavg for 1oo1:**

$$
\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo1}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} \;=\; \frac{3.504 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \;=\; 1.752 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iv) Achieved SIL:**

Compare with IEC 61508-1 Table 2:
- SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$
- SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-3}$

Our PFDavg = $1.752 \times 10^{-3}$ falls in the **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ range → SIL 2**.

**(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Low-demand vs. High-demand failure management:**

| Aspect | Low-Demand Mode | High-Demand / Continuous Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand rate** | ≤ 1 demand/year | > 1 demand/year |
| **Primary metric** | PFDavg (avg probability of failure on demand) | PFH (probability of failure per hour) |
| **Mathematical model** | $\frac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T}{2}$ (averaged over proof interval) | $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ (instantaneous rate) |
| **Failure management focus** | Proof testing interval $T$ | Diagnostic test interval $\tau$ |
| **Goal** | Minimize PFDavg below SIL target | Minimize PFH below SIL target |
| **IEC 61508 SIL range (PFH)** | Not applicable | SIL 1: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$/h; SIL 2: $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$/h; SIL 3: $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$/h |
| **Examples** | Emergency Shutdown (ESD), fire & gas | Burner management, compressor anti-surge, HIPPS |

**Labelled block diagram (described):**

A block showing **(1) Process under control → (2) Demand event → (3) SIS Detection → (4) Decision logic → (5) Final element actuation → (6) Safe state reached**. For low-demand, the proof-test block is shown as a *periodic external intervention*; for high-demand, a *continuous diagnostic loop* wraps around steps (3), (4), (5). **(7 marks complete)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A 1oo2 redundant SIS loop consists of two identical sensors, each with $\lambda_{\text{DU}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h. The proof test interval is $T = 8760$ h. The β-factor for common cause failures is 0.08.
Calculate: **(i)** PFDavg *without* CCF, **(ii)** PFDavg *with* CCF included, and **(iii)** comment on the dominant contributor to the risk budget. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **failure management strategies** used across the safety lifecycle (Phases 1–16 of IEC 61508). Categorize them under: *Avoidance, Detection, Control, and Documentation*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) PFDavg without CCF:**

$$
\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2}} \;=\; \frac{(\lambda_{\text{DU}} T)^2}{3} \;=\; \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^2}{3} \;=\; \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{3} \;=\; 6.39 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) PFDavg with CCF (β-factor):**

The CCF correction term is $\beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T$:

$$
\beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T \;=\; 0.08 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \;=\; 3.504 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\text{PFDavg}_{\text{1oo2,CCF}} \;=\; 6.39 \times 10^{-6} + 3.504 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 3.57 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(iii) Dominant contributor:**

The CCF term ($3.504 \times 10^{-4}$) is **54.8 times larger** than the independent-failure term ($6.39 \times 10^{-6}$). Hence **common cause failures dominate** the risk budget. This justifies IEC 61508-6 Annex D's emphasis on CCF scoring (X/Y/Z checklist), physical separation, diversity, and diagnostics-based protection.

**(2 marks)**

**(b) Failure Management Across the Safety Lifecycle:**

**1. Avoidance (Phases 2–4):** Robust design, derating, quality components, formal verification, diverse redundancy, segregation.

**2. Detection (Phases 5–9, 12):** FMEDA, diagnostic test routines, watchdog timers, comparator checks, stuck-bit diagnostics, current-loop monitoring.

**3. Control (Phases 10–13):** Proof testing, repair procedures, bypass management, operating procedures, trip logic tests.

**4. Documentation (Phases 1, 14–16):** Safety Manual, FMEDA report, SIL verification report, change management records, audit trails.

**(7 marks total — distribute ~1.75 marks per category)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on SIS Failure Management Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Always express $\lambda$ in **per hour** and $T$ in **hours**. Mixing years and hours gives 8760× errors.
> 2. **Standard DC rounding:** IEC 61508 allows only four DC values (0, 60, 90, 99%). Students lose marks writing "81.8% DC" without rounding.
> 3. **Ignoring β-factor:** Any 1oo2/2oo3 calculation without the CCF term is *incomplete* and capped at 50% marks by KTU examiners.
> 4. **PFDavg vs. PFH:** Mixing low-demand and high-demand formulas. Check the question wording — "PFDavg" implies $T/2$ form; "PFH" implies pure $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ form.
> 5. **SFF vs. DC confusion:** SFF is over **all** failures; DC is only over **dangerous** failures. Writing "DC = 90%" when meaning SFF = 90% is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for SIS Failure Management:**

- ☐ Every SIS failure splits into **safe/dangerous** and **detected/undetected** → 4 categories: SD, SU, DD, DU.
- ☐ **λ_DU** is the *only* failure mode that directly degrades PFDavg/PFH. Everything else is mitigated by diagnostics or proof tests.
- ☐ **DC** = $\lambda_{\text{DD}} / \lambda_{\text{D}}$ — limited to four values: **0%, 60%, 90%, 99%**.
- ☐ **SFF** = $1 - \lambda_{\text{DU}}/\lambda_{\text{total}}$ — must be ≥ 60%/90% for SIL 2/3 in 1oo1 architecture (Route 2H).
- ☐ **PFDavg(1oo1)** = $\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T / 2$ — derived from integration of failure density over proof interval.
- ☐ **PFDavg(1oo2)** = $(\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T)^2 / 3$ — squared dependence gives ~1000× improvement over 1oo1.
- ☐ **PFH(1oo1)** = $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ — for high-demand/continuous modes. Units are **per hour**.
- ☐ **β-factor** is non-negotiable in redundant systems. Typical value 5–10%. Checklists in IEC 61508-6 Annex D.
- ☐ **HFT (Hardware Fault Tolerance):** 1oo1 = 0, 1oo2/2oo2 = 1, 1oo3 = 2. Higher HFT → higher SIL cap.
- ☐ **Proof test** restores system to "as good as new." $T_{\text{proof}} \leq 2 \cdot \text{PFDavg}_{\text{req}} / \lambda_{\text{DU}}$.
- ☐ **STR (Spurious Trip Rate)** = $\lambda_{\text{SD}} + \lambda_{\text{SU}}$ — availability metric, *not* safety metric.
- ☐ **SIL ranges (Low-demand PFDavg):** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- ☐ **SIL ranges (PFH per hour):** SIL 1 = $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$.
- ☐ **Loop summation:** Total PFDavg = sum of sensor + logic solver + final element contributions.
- ☐ **IEC 61508 standard values for failure rates:** Typically quoted in **FIT** = failures per $10^9$ hours.
- ☐ **Always state assumptions:** $\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T \ll 1$, identical independent channels, exponential distribution, perfect proof test (100% coverage).
- ☐ **Vocabulary:** FMEDA = Failure Modes, Effects, and Diagnostic Analysis. STR = Spurious Trip Rate. CCF = Common Cause Failure.
- ☐ **Real-world trade-off:** Higher redundancy improves safety (PFDavg ↓) but may *increase* STR if not voted correctly (1oo2 vs 2oo2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Failure Types in Functional Safety

In functional safety engineering (governed by **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511**), understanding *how* a system can fail is as critical as knowing *that* it can fail. The classification of failure types provides the mathematical and logical foundation for calculating safety integrity levels (SIL), probability of failure on demand (PFD), and probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.6.4):** A **failure** is the *termination of the ability of a functional unit to perform a required function*. A failure becomes an **error** when it causes a deviation from the intended behaviour, and an error becomes a **fault** when it propagates and impacts system safety.

---

## 1.2 Primary Classification of Failures

### A. Systematic Failures

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *systematic failure* is a failure related in a deterministic way to a certain cause, which can only be eliminated by a modification of the design, manufacturing process, operating procedures, documentation, or other relevant factors.

- Deterministic and reproducible under identical conditions.
- Caused by human error, design flaws, software bugs, or specification mistakes.
- **Cannot be reduced by testing alone** — only by changing the process/design.

### B. Random Hardware Failures

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *random hardware failure* is a failure that results from one or more of the possible degradation mechanisms in the hardware, occurring at a predictable (mean) rate but at unpredictable (random) times.

- Stochastic in nature — modelled by probability distributions.
- Follows the classic "**bathtub curve**" of failure rate.
- **Rate parameter: $\lambda$** (failures per hour, $\text{h}^{-1}$).

---

## 1.3 Safety-Critical Sub-Classification

Every failure is further classified by its **consequence** on the safety function:

| Failure Category | Symbol | Consequence |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Safe Failure** | $\lambda_S$ | Failure that does not put the EUC (Equipment Under Control) in a hazardous or unsafe state. |
| **Dangerous Failure** | $\lambda_D$ | Failure that puts the EUC into a hazardous or unsafe state. |
| **Detected Failure** | $\lambda_{dd}$, $\lambda_{sd}$ | Failure detected by the diagnostic test within the diagnostic test interval. |
| **Undetected Failure** | $\lambda_{du}$, $\lambda_{su}$ | Failure that escapes diagnostic detection. |

The **safe failure fraction (SFF)** is defined as:

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$$

where the *detected* portion of the dangerous failure is $\lambda_{DD}$ and the *undetected* portion is $\lambda_{DU}$.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Smoke Detector**
>
> Imagine a smoke detector in your home. It can fail in two principal ways:
> 1. **Systematic failure** — A design flaw where the alarm sound frequency is below the human hearing threshold. No matter how many times you test it, it will never wake you up. You can only fix it by *redesigning* the speaker.
> 2. **Random hardware failure** — The battery slowly drains over 5 years and eventually dies. You cannot predict the *exact* second it dies, but you *can* predict statistically that after 5 years, 50% of batteries will have failed.
>
> Now, a third dimension: a *random* failure can be **dangerous** (the detector fails silently during a fire — bad!) or **safe** (the detector beeps constantly — annoying, but you know it's broken and you replace the battery — safe!).

---

## 1.5 Why This Classification Matters

The **Type A / Type B** classification of elements (compliant or non-compliant with IEC 61508 failure-mode constraints) combined with the failure-type classification directly determines the **maximum allowable SIL** a subsystem can claim under a given hardware fault tolerance (HFT) and SFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A question on failure types is *not* a memorization question. Examiners expect you to **map any given failure scenario** to one of the four categories: $\lambda_{SD}$, $\lambda_{SU}$, $\lambda_{DD}$, or $\lambda_{DU}$. Always show the **failure rate decomposition** clearly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure Rate Decomposition — The Master Equation

The total failure rate $\lambda$ of any hardware element is partitioned along two orthogonal axes:

1. **Consequence Axis:** Safe vs. Dangerous
2. **Diagnostic Axis:** Detected vs. Undetected

This gives the canonical four-way decomposition used across the entire IEC 61508 framework:

$$\lambda = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

where:

- $\lambda_{SD}$ = Safe Detected failure rate
- $\lambda_{SU}$ = Safe Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = Dangerous Detected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate

The **dangerous failure rate** and **safe failure rate** are defined as:

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}$$

The **diagnostic coverage (DC)** is:

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$$

---

## 2.2 The Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) — Boundary Check

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SFF must be computed in **decimal form** (e.g., 0.95), not as a percentage (95%), to be used in the SIL-vs-HFT architectural constraint table of IEC 61508-2.

---

## 2.3 The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and MTTR

For non-repaired (single-channel) elements:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

For repaired (redundant) systems, the **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR)** enters the **Mean Down Time (MDT)** calculation:

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{channel}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \times T_i}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \times T_i)^2}{3} + \lambda_{DD} \times \text{MTTR}$$

where $T_i$ is the **proof test interval**.

---

## 2.4 Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) for Common Architectures

### 1oo1 (One out of One) — Single Channel

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo1}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda t\text{)}$$

Average over proof test interval $T_i$:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$$

### 1oo2 (One out of Two) — Redundant Channel

For identical channels with identical failure rates, considering common cause:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} \approx \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i)^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i$$

where $\beta$ is the **beta-factor** for common-cause failures (typically 0.01 to 0.10).

### 2oo2 (Two out of Two)

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{2oo2}} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i$$

### 2oo3 (Two out of Three)

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{2oo3}} \approx 6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i)^2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i$$

---

## 2.5 Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)

For high-demand / continuous mode of operation, the relevant metric is PFH:

$$\text{PFH}_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_{DU}$$

$$\text{PFH}_{1oo2} \approx 2 \cdot (1 - \text{DC}) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU}$$

where $t_{CE}$ is the channel equivalent mean downtime.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Key Assumption |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\lambda = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Decompose total failure rate | All four categories must be defined |
| 2 | $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | Compute architectural constraint | Decimal output (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}$ | Diagnostic test effectiveness | $0 \le \text{DC} \le 1$ |
| 4 | $\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo1}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$ | Single-channel low-demand | $T_i < 1 \text{ year}$, $\lambda_{DU} T_i \ll 1$ |
| 5 | $\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3} + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_i$ | Redundant low-demand | Identical channels |
| 6 | $\text{PFH}_{1oo2} = 2(1-\text{DC})\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE} + \beta \lambda_{DU}$ | Redundant high-demand | Includes diagnostic |
| 7 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Non-repaired element | Exponential distribution |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda_{CCF}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | Common cause fraction | $0.01 \le \beta \le 0.10$ typical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the daily workhorse of any **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** engineer. In an oil refinery, when designing the Emergency Shutdown System (ESD), every sensor (pressure transmitter) and final element (shutdown valve) is allocated a $\lambda_{DU}$ budget. The PFD is then summed across the sensor + logic solver + final element loop to verify the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** meets its target SIL (1, 2, 3, or 4).

---

## 2.7 Common Cause and Dependent Failures

A **common cause failure (CCF)** is a failure where two or more channels fail from the same cause and within a short interval, defeating redundancy. The $\beta$-factor model partitions the total failure rate:

$$\lambda_{DU}^{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \quad ; \quad \lambda_{DU}^{\text{independent}} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}$$

Other dependent-failure models include:

- **$\beta$-factor model** (simplest, IEC 61508 default)
- **Multiple Greek Letter (MGL)** model
- **Phi-factor** model

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams, the $\beta$-factor model is sufficient.** Memorize the $\text{PFD}_{1oo2}$ and $\text{PFH}_{1oo2}$ formulas above — they are favourite exam questions.

---

## 2.8 Failure Modes, Mechanisms, and Effects Analysis (FMMEA) Link

In functional safety, every identified failure type must be traceable to a **failure mode** (the *symptom* observed, e.g., "output stuck high") and a **failure mechanism** (the *root physical cause*, e.g., "solder joint cold-flow"). The mapping looks like:

$$\text{Failure Mode} \xrightarrow{\text{mapped to}} \text{Failure Type} \xrightarrow{\text{assigned rate}} \lambda_{SD} / \lambda_{SU} / \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_{DU}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average PFD for a 1oo1 Architecture

**Problem Setup:** A single-channel element with constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ is tested for proof at intervals of $T_i$. Derive the *average* PFD over one proof interval, assuming failures are detected only at proof tests.

### Step 1 — Reliability Function

The probability of the element *surviving* (i.e., not having failed dangerously) for time $t$ follows the exponential distribution:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Failure on Demand at Time $t$

The PFD at any instant $t$ within one proof cycle is the probability that the element has failed sometime in $(0, t)$:

$$\text{PFD}(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

### Step 3 — Expansion for Small $\lambda t$

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} = 1 - x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} - \dfrac{x^3}{3!} + \cdots$:

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} = 1 - \lambda_{DU} t + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} t)^2}{2} - \cdots$$

So:

$$\text{PFD}(t) = \lambda_{DU} t - \frac{(\lambda_{DU} t)^2}{2} + \cdots$$

For small values ($\lambda_{DU} t \ll 1$, typically $< 0.1$):

$$\text{PFD}(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} t$$

### Step 4 — Average PFD over the Proof Interval

The *average* PFD over the interval $[0, T_i]$ is obtained by integrating the instantaneous PFD and dividing by $T_i$:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \text{PFD}(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the exponential form:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{T_i} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\right) dt$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ t + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]_{0}^{T_i}$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \left[ T_i + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i}}{\lambda_{DU}} - \frac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \right]$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = 1 + \frac{e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i} - 1}{\lambda_{DU} T_i}$$

### Step 5 — Simplified Form (Standard IEC 61508 Result)

Expanding $e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i}$ again to second order:

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} T_i} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} T_i + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{2}$$

Substitute back:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} \approx 1 + \frac{\left(1 - \lambda_{DU} T_i + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{2}\right) - 1}{\lambda_{DU} T_i}$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} \approx 1 + \frac{-\lambda_{DU} T_i + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{2}}{\lambda_{DU} T_i}$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} \approx 1 - 1 + \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_i}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo1}} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ arises because, on average, a randomly-occurring failure happens at the *middle* of the proof test interval — at $t = T_i/2$. Thus, the average "exposure time" to the dangerous failure is $T_i / 2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the PFD for a 1oo2 Architecture

**Setup:** Two identical channels. The SIF fails on demand only if **both** channels fail dangerously (and undetected). For independent failures:

$$R_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\right)^2 = 2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} - e^{-2\lambda_{DU} t}$$

So the PFD is:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - R_{1oo2}(t) = 1 - 2 e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} + e^{-2\lambda_{DU} t}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2}(t) = \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t}\right)^2$$

Using the small-$\lambda t$ approximation:

$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2}(t) \approx (\lambda_{DU} t)^2$$

Averaging over $T_i$:

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2,\text{ind}}} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} (\lambda_{DU} t)^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{T_i} \cdot \frac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 T_i^3}{3} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3}$$

**Adding common-cause (CCF) contribution** — the redundant channel adds the same dangerous undetected failure due to the *same* root cause (a single CCF takes out both channels):

$$\boxed{\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} T_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CCF term is **linear** in $\lambda_{DU} T_i$ — it cannot be made arbitrarily small by adding more channels. This is the fundamental reason IEC 61508 mandates a **maximum HFT of 3** even for the most redundant architectures.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PFD Summary Across Architectures

| Architecture | $\overline{\text{PFD}}$ (low-demand, no CCF) | Order in $\lambda_{DU} T_i$ | SIL Achievable (typical) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1oo1 | $\dfrac{\lambda_{DU} T_i}{2}$ | $O(\lambda T)$ | SIL 1 only |
| 1oo2 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3}$ | $O((\lambda T)^2)$ | SIL 2 / SIL 3 |
| 2oo2 | $\lambda_{DU} T_i$ | $O(\lambda T)$ | SIL 1 (worse than 1oo1 due to spurious trips) |
| 2oo3 | $6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2$ | $O((\lambda T)^2)$ | SIL 2 / SIL 3 |
| 1oo2D | $\dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3} + (1-\text{DC})\lambda_{DU} \text{MTTR}$ | $O((\lambda T)^2)$ | SIL 3 (with diagnostics) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Numerical PFD Computation

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has the following data (from manufacturer FMEDA):

- $\lambda_{SD} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$
- $\lambda_{SU} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$
- $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$
- $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$
- Proof test interval: $T_i = 1 \text{ year} = 8760 \, \text{h}$

**Step 1 — Total failure rate:**

$$\lambda = 2 + 5 + 8 + 3 = 18 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Safe and Dangerous failure rates:**

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} = 7 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 11 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — SFF:**

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = \frac{7 + 8}{7 + 11} \times 10^{-9} = \frac{15}{18} = 0.8333$$

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Coverage:**

$$\text{DC} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{8}{11} = 0.7273$$

**Step 5 — Average PFD (1oo1):**

$$\overline{\text{PFD}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = \frac{2.628 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 1.314 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 6 — SIL Mapping (low-demand mode, IEC 61508-1 Table 2):**

| SIL | PFD Range |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| **2** | **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$** |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

Our PFD $= 1.314 \times 10^{-5}$ falls into the **SIL 3** range ($10^{-5} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Failure Types

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps every failure type to its position within the IEC 61508 taxonomy.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["FAILURE TOTAL LAMBDA"]
    nodeB["RANDOM HARDWARE FAILURE"]
    nodeC["SYSTEMATIC FAILURE"]
    nodeD["COMMON CAUSE FAILURE CCF"]
    nodeE["LAMBDA SD Safe Detected"]
    nodeF["LAMBDA SU Safe Undetected"]
    nodeG["LAMBDA DD Dangerous Detected"]
    nodeH["LAMBDA DU Dangerous Undetected"]
    nodeI["LAMBDA S Safe Total"]
    nodeJ["LAMBDA D Dangerous Total"]
    nodeK["SFF SAFE FAILURE FRACTION"]
    nodeL["DC DIAGNOSTIC COVERAGE"]
    nodeM["PFD PROBABILITY OF FAILURE ON DEMAND"]
    nodeN["PFH PROBABILITY PER HOUR"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeO["SIL DETERMINATION 1 to 4"]
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Is a Given Failure Safe or Dangerous?

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["A FAILURE HAS OCCURRED"]
    q1["Does it place the EUC into a hazardous state?"]
    q2["Does the internal diagnostics detect the failure within the diagnostic test interval?"]
    safe["SAFE FAILURE LAMBDA S"]
    dang["DANGEROUS FAILURE LAMBDA D"]
    sdet["LAMBDA SD Safe Detected"]
    sund["LAMBDA SU Safe Undetected"]
    ddet["LAMBDA DD Dangerous Detected"]
    dund["LAMBDA DU Dangerous Undetected CRITICAL FOR SIL"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- NO --> safe
    q1 -- YES --> dang
    safe --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> sdet
    q2 -- NO --> sund
    dang --> q2
    q2 -- YES --> ddet
    q2 -- NO --> dund
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a problem statement says *"a sensor reading is stuck at 4 mA and the system continues to operate without alarming"* — the failure is *dangerous* (the safety action will not be triggered on a real demand) AND *undetected* (no diagnostic flagged it). Hence it is $\lambda_{DU}$. This is the single most dangerous failure category in functional safety.

---

## 4.3 Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    a["INFANT MORTALITY BURN IN"]
    b["USEFUL LIFE CONSTANT RATE"]
    c["WEAR OUT END OF LIFE"]
    x1["TIME T"]
    y1["FAILURE RATE LAMBDA"]

    a --> b
    b --> c
```

In the **useful life** region, the failure rate is approximately **constant**, which is the region where the exponential distribution (and our $\lambda$-based formulas) are valid. Functional safety analyses always assume the system is operating in this region.

---

## 4.4 Architecture Comparison — PFD vs. Time

The relationship between the four common architectures and their PFD evolution over time is shown below. Note the square-law (much better) behaviour of 1oo2 vs. the linear behaviour of 1oo1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    arch["ARCHITECTURE SELECTION"]
    oneooone["1oo1 Single Channel PFD = LAMBDA_DU TIMES T over 2"]
    oneootwo["1oo2 Redundant PFD = LAMBDA_DU TIMES T squared over 3 PLUS CCF"]
    twoootwo["2oo2 Voting PFD = LAMBDA_DU TIMES T"]
    twooothree["2oo3 Voting PFD = 6 LAMBDA_DU TIMES T squared"]
    out["MAP PFD TO SIL 1 to 4"]

    arch --> oneooone
    arch --> oneootwo
    arch --> twoootwo
    arch --> twooothree
    oneooone --> out
    oneootwo --> out
    twoootwo --> out
    twooothree --> out
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between systematic failure and random hardware failure. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Systematic Failure | Random Hardware Failure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Human/process error in design, manufacture, or operation | Physical degradation of components |
| **Nature** | Deterministic, reproducible | Stochastic, unpredictable in time |
| **Reduction Method** | Change design, process, or procedure | Component derating, redundancy, proof testing |
| **Example** | A software bug that miscalculates the shutdown threshold; a wrong resistor value on a PCB due to a design error | Electrolytic capacitor drying out over 10 years; solder joint fatigue failure |
| **Failure Rate** | Not expressible as $\lambda$ (no meaningful rate) | Modelled with constant rate $\lambda$ in useful life |

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) and explain its role in the IEC 61508 architectural constraints for Type A and Type B elements.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **SFF Definition:**
> $$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$$
> It is the fraction of *all* failures (safe + dangerous) that are either safe or are detected dangerous failures.

**Role in IEC 61508 Architectural Constraints:**

- For a given **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** (0, 1, or 2), the **maximum achievable SIL** depends jointly on whether the element is **Type A** (well-defined failure modes, documented behaviour) or **Type B** (complex, no complete failure-mode understanding), and on the **SFF**.
- Higher SFF relaxes the HFT requirement: e.g., a Type B element with HFT = 1 needs SFF $\ge 90\%$ to claim SIL 3.
- Lower SFF demands more redundancy (higher HFT) to reach the same SIL.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a single-channel pressure transmitter feeding a logic solver, which commands a single shut-down valve. The failure rate data (in failures per hour, $\text{h}^{-1}$) for each subsystem, derived from manufacturer FMEDA, are:

| Subsystem | $\lambda_{SD}$ | $\lambda_{SU}$ | $\lambda_{DD}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pressure Transmitter | $2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $3.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ |
| Logic Solver | $0.5 \times 10^{-9}$ | $0.2 \times 10^{-9}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $0.5 \times 10^{-9}$ |
| Shut-down Valve | $3.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $4.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | $2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ |

The proof test interval is $T_i = 1$ year. Compute the total **average PFD** of the SIF, the **SFF** of the shut-down valve alone, and the **DC** of the pressure transmitter. State the achievable SIL.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute average PFD for each subsystem (1oo1 formula):**

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{\text{PT}}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = 6.57 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{\text{LS}}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{\text{SV}}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760}{2} = 1.095 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Total PFD of the SIF (series of independent subsystems):**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{PT}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{LS}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{SV}}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 6.57 \times 10^{-6} + 2.19 \times 10^{-6} + 1.095 \times 10^{-5} = 1.97 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — SFF of the shut-down valve:**

$$\lambda_S = 3.0 \times 10^{-9} + 2.0 \times 10^{-9} = 5.0 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\lambda_D = 4.0 \times 10^{-9} + 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 6.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\text{SFF}_{\text{SV}} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D} = \frac{5.0 + 4.0}{5.0 + 6.5} = \frac{9.0}{11.5} = 0.7826$$

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Coverage of the pressure transmitter:**

$$\text{DC}_{\text{PT}} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{3.0}{3.0 + 1.5} = \frac{3.0}{4.5} = 0.6667$$

**Step 5 — SIL Determination:**

Our $\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 1.97 \times 10^{-5}$ lies in the range $10^{-5} \le \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ → **SIL 3** is achievable.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to IEC 61508, discuss the effect of **Common Cause Failures (CCF)** and the **$\beta$-factor model** on the achievable PFD of a 1oo2 redundant sensor pair. Use the formula

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} T_i$$

to compute the average PFD for $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{h}^{-1}$, $T_i = 8760 \, \text{h}$, and $\beta = 0.05$. Compare with the 1oo1 case for the same $\lambda_{DU}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the independent (square-law) term:**

$$\frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2}{3} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760)^2}{3} = \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 6.39 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CCF (linear) term:**

$$\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} T_i = 0.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 0.05 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-3} = 2.19 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Total 1oo2 PFD:**

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo2}} = 6.39 \times 10^{-6} + 2.19 \times 10^{-4} = 2.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — 1oo1 PFD for comparison:**

$$\overline{\text{PFD}_{1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} T_i}{2} = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Discussion:**

| Architecture | PFD | SIL |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1oo1 | $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 1 (borderline SIL 2) |
| 1oo2 with CCF ($\beta = 0.05$) | $2.25 \times 10^{-4}$ | SIL 3 |

**Discussion Points:**

1. The independent-failure term (square-law) gives the 1oo2 architecture an order-of-magnitude improvement over 1oo1.
2. However, the **CCF term dominates**: $2.19 \times 10^{-4}$ vs. $6.39 \times 10^{-6}$. The redundancy is "wasted" on common-cause events.
3. Reducing $\beta$ (e.g., via physical separation, diverse technology, periodic cross-calibration) is the only way to truly benefit from redundancy.
4. Even with $\beta = 0$, the achievable SIL has a hard ceiling set by the residual $\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} T_i$ term — this is the **architectural limit** of redundancy.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 1oo2D redundant sensor system is used for high-demand (continuous) operation. The relevant data are:

- $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{h}^{-1}$
- $\text{DC} = 0.95$
- $\beta = 0.02$
- $t_{CE}$ (channel equivalent downtime) = $8 \, \text{h}$

Compute the **PFH** using the IEC 61508-6 formula and determine the achievable SIL.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute the diagnostic-related term:**

$$2(1 - \text{DC})\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE} = 2 \times (1 - 0.95) \times (1 \times 10^{-7})^2 \times 8$$

$$= 2 \times 0.05 \times 1 \times 10^{-14} \times 8 = 8.0 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CCF term:**

$$\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} = 0.02 \times 1 \times 10^{-7} = 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Total PFH:**

$$\text{PFH}_{1oo2D} \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-16} + 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — SIL Determination (high-demand, IEC 61508-1 Table 3):**

| SIL | PFH Range ($\text{h}^{-1}$) |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ |
| **4** | **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$** |

Our PFH $= 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{h}^{-1}$ lies in the range $10^{-9} \le \text{PFH} < 10^{-8}$ → **SIL 4 is achievable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Remark:** Note the **3-order-of-magnitude** reduction in PFH from the 1oo1 case ($\lambda_{DU} = 10^{-7}$) to the 1oo2D case ($2 \times 10^{-9}$). This is why high-integrity process industries (nuclear, aerospace) mandate redundant + diagnostic architecture for SIL 3/4 functions.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Type A vs. Type B** element classification of IEC 61508. List **two examples** of each type and discuss how the combination of SFF and HFT constrains the maximum achievable SIL for a Type B element.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Type A Element (IEC 61508-2, Clause 7.4.4.1.2):**
- All failure modes are **well-defined**.
- Behaviour under fault conditions can be **completely determined**.
- There is **sufficient field experience** to claim dependable failure rates.
- *Examples:* Simple resistor, opto-isolator, basic solenoid, certified analogue input card with documented failure modes.

**Type B Element (IEC 61508-2, Clause 7.4.4.1.3):**
- At least one failure mode is **not well-defined**.
- Behaviour under fault conditions **cannot be completely determined**.
- Failure data is based on **limited field experience** or is manufacturer-derived.
- *Examples:* Complex microcontroller, ASIC, FPGA, smart sensor with on-board diagnostics, software-based subsystem.

**Architectural Constraint Table for Type B Elements (IEC 61508-2, Table 3):**

| SFF | HFT = 0 | HFT = 1 | HFT = 2 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $< 60\%$ | Not allowed | SIL 1 | SIL 2 |
| $60\%$ to $< 90\%$ | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| $90\%$ to $< 99\%$ | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |
| $\ge 99\%$ | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | SIL 4 |

**Discussion:**

- Type B elements, being less understood, are subject to **stricter** SFF/HFT requirements.
- For example, a Type B element with SFF = $85\%$ requires HFT $\ge 2$ to claim SIL 3.
- For SIL 4, the only acceptable combination is SFF $\ge 99\%$ with HFT = 1, *or* SFF $\ge 60\%$ with HFT = 2 (under specific conditions).
- In practice, achieving SFF $\ge 99\%$ requires **automatic diagnostics with DC $\ge 99\%$** plus a high proportion of intrinsically safe failure modes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Always express $\lambda$ in **per hour** ($\text{h}^{-1}$), not per year. $T_i$ in hours. Mixing units is the #1 mark-deduction cause.
> 2. **PFD vs. PFH confusion:** Low-demand $\Rightarrow$ PFD (probability). High-demand $\Rightarrow$ PFH (rate per hour). They are **dimensionally different** quantities.
> 3. **Forgetting CCF:** A 1oo2 system without the $\beta$ term is incomplete. The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks.
> 4. **SFF as a percentage:** SFF must be expressed in **decimal (0.95)** when used in the architectural constraint table, not as $95\%$.
> 5. **SIL over-claim:** Even if the PFD/PFH calculation supports SIL 3, the **architectural constraint** (HFT + SFF + Type) must also be satisfied. Many students calculate the right PFD but ignore the type-A/B constraint.
> 6. **Mixing up $\lambda_{DD}$ and $\lambda_{DU}$:** A failure detected by diagnostics within the test interval is $\lambda_{DD}$; only the residual *undetected* portion contributes to the PFD. Do **not** include $\lambda_{DD}$ in the PFD numerator (it is "covered" by the diagnostic and is in the safe-state repair time, which is accounted for by the MTTR term).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Failure Types**

- ☐ **Four-way failure decomposition:** $\lambda = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$. Memorize the meaning of each subscript.
- ☐ **Safe vs. Dangerous** is about **consequence**, not the failure itself.
- ☐ **Detected vs. Undetected** is about **diagnostic coverage** by internal self-tests.
- ☐ **SFF** = fraction of all failures that are either safe *or* detected dangerous.
- ☐ **DC** = fraction of dangerous failures that are detected by diagnostics.
- ☐ **PFD** (low-demand) is a *probability*; **PFH** (high-demand) is a *rate per hour*.
- ☐ **1oo1 PFD = $\lambda_{DU} T_i / 2$** — the $1/2$ factor comes from averaging over the proof interval.
- ☐ **1oo2 PFD = $(\lambda_{DU} T_i)^2 / 3 + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_i$** — the CCF term is *linear* and dominates in practice.
- ☐ **$\beta$-factor model:** $\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \lambda_{\text{total}}$; typical $\beta = 0.01$ to $0.10$.
- ☐ **Type A vs. Type B** elements govern the **architectural constraint** table for SIL.
- ☐ **Systematic failures** cannot be modelled by $\lambda$ — they are addressed by *quality of process*, not redundancy.
- ☐ **SIL ranges (low-demand):** SIL 1 $\rightarrow$ $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 4 $\rightarrow$ $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ (PFD).
- ☐ **SIL ranges (high-demand):** SIL 1 $\rightarrow$ $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$; SIL 4 $\rightarrow$ $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ (PFH, $\text{h}^{-1}$).
- ☐ **PFD and PFH are summable across SIF subsystems** in series (sensor + logic + final element).
- ☐ **Proof test interval $T_i$** is the only engineering knob that can reduce PFD in operation — shorter tests = lower PFD.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fail-Safe Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fail-Safe Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fail-Safe Failure** (also termed a *safe failure*) is defined in **IEC 61508** as a failure that:
> *"Results in the safe state of the equipment under control (EUC) or the EUC control system, or results in a fault that is detected by the diagnostic mechanisms and leads to a defined safe state, without any demand on the safety function."*

Mathematically, the total failure rate $\lambda$ of any Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is partitioned into two orthogonal categories:

$$\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_D$$

where:
- $\lambda_S$ is the **Safe Failure Rate** (failures that do NOT prevent the safety function from operating correctly when demanded, OR that drive the process to a safe state).
- $\lambda_D$ is the **Dangerous Failure Rate** (failures that prevent the safety function from operating when demanded).

Furthermore, $\lambda_S$ is subdivided into:

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD}$$

where:
- $\lambda_{SU}$ = Safe Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{SD}$ = Safe Detected (by diagnostics) failure rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "Fail-Safe" failure is the *most desirable* failure mode from a process-safety perspective because the system inherently migrates toward, or is actively driven to, a non-hazardous state. It does **NOT** contribute to the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) or Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH) used for SIL determination.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Light Switch"

Imagine a modern **smart staircase light switch** in a public building:

- You walk up the stairs and the motion sensor fails *open* (it stops detecting you) — the light remains **ON** forever. Annoying? Yes. Dangerous? No, because you can still see. This is a **Fail-Safe failure** (it "fails" to the safe, illuminated condition).
- Now, imagine the *same* sensor fails *shorted* (always reports someone is there) — the light **never turns on**. A person walking into a dark stairwell could fall. This is a **Dangerous failure**.

The fail-safe principle is built into the *design architecture* itself. We choose components whose *natural* failure modes (e.g., a normally-closed solenoid valve that fails *closed* when de-energized) align with the safe state of the process.

## 1.3 Three Sub-Classes of Safe Failures

| Sub-Class | Abbreviation | Description | Detected by Diagnostics? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Safe, Detected | $\lambda_{SD}$ | Failure has **no** effect on safety; **OR** is detected by internal self-tests | **Yes** |
| Safe, Undetected | $\lambda_{SU}$ | Failure has **no** effect on safety; passes unnoticed between proof tests | **No** |
| No Effect | $\lambda_{NE}$ | Failure does not affect the safety function at all | Typically N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> **No-Effect failures** ($\lambda_{NE}$) are often rolled into the $\lambda_S$ category in simplified KTU-level analyses, but in rigorous IEC 61508 SIL calculations, they are tracked separately because they do not affect the SIF's ability to perform its safety duty.

## 1.4 Physical/Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** of safe failures: $DC_S = \dfrac{\lambda_{SD}}{\lambda_S}$ — typically **≥ 90%** in well-designed SIFs.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**: The single most important architectural metric; see Section 2.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for safe failures: $MTTF_S = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_S}$, expressed in **years (a)** or **hours (h)**.
- **Common Cause Failure (CCF)**: Even safe failures can be CCF-related; this is why *diversity* is mandated in IEC 61508 Part 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Fail-Safe Matters

In the **functional safety lifecycle** (per IEC 61508), the system designer must answer four sequential questions:

1. **What is the hazard?** (Hazard & Risk Analysis — HAZOP, FMEA, LOPA)
2. **What is the safe state?** (e.g., valve closed, motor de-energized, heater off)
3. **How do we *guarantee* the system reaches that state when the process misbehaves?**
4. **What is the *worst* way each component can fail, and is that worst case a safe state?**
