Question 4 is the *fail-safe design checkpoint*. The designer evaluates every component (sensor, logic solver, final element) for its **failure mode and effects**. If the natural failure mode is *not* safe, the designer must add:
- **Diagnostics** (to detect the dangerous failure and force a trip), OR
- **Redundancy** (1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3 voting — discussed in Module 4), OR
- **Proof testing** (periodic manual inspection to catch undetected failures).

---

## 2.2 The Fail-Safe Failure Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Fault occurs in a component.**
A resistor in an analog input card drifts, a relay contact welds, a sensor element cracks.

**Step 2 — Effect on the safety function is classified.**
Is the affected parameter moving *toward* the safe trip point, or *away* from it?
- *Toward safe trip* → **Safe failure** ($\lambda_S$).
- *Away from safe trip* → **Dangerous failure** ($\lambda_D$).

**Step 3 — Diagnostic mechanisms evaluate the fault.**
If the fault is detected:
- The system executes the **safe-state action** (e.g., de-energize the final element).
- A **proof test interval** countdown may be reset.
- The failure is logged in $\lambda_{SD}$.

If undetected:
- The fault remains latent.
- It accumulates into $\lambda_{SU}$ until the next proof test reveals it.

**Step 4 — Sum the failure rates to compute the Safe Failure Fraction (SFF).**
This determines the *archetype* (Type A or Type B) and the *permitted hardware fault tolerance* for the target SIL — see Section 2.3.

**Step 5 — Iterate the design.**
If SFF is too low, the engineer must:
- Add **internal diagnostics** (raise $DC_S$ and $DC_D$).
- Select components with **higher inherent reliability** (lower total $\lambda$).
- Apply **redundancy** to vote out single-point failures.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU BOARD INSTRUCTION:** Do **NOT** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` in your exam answer sheets to denote absolute value or conditional terms. Use the word "such that" or the symbols `\vert` and `\mid` in your LaTeX drafts. Board examiners deduct marks for ambiguous expressions like `P(D|U)`.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_D$ | Total failure rate partition | failures per hour (fph) |
| 2 | $\lambda_S = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD}$ | Safe failure rate decomposition | fph |
| 3 | $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$ | Dangerous failure rate decomposition | fph |
| 4 | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Total failure rate (all four categories) | fph |
| 5 | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}} \times 100\%$ | **Safe Failure Fraction** | **%** (0 – 100) |
| 6 | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | Alternate compact SFF form | dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $DC_S = \dfrac{\lambda_{SD}}{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}}$ | Diagnostic coverage of safe failures | % (0 – 100) |
| 8 | $DC_D = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic coverage of dangerous failures | % (0 – 100) |
| 9 | $PFD_{avg} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Avg. Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1, low-D) | dimensionless (0 – 1) |
| 10 | $PFH \approx \lambda_{DU}$ | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (1oo1, high-D) | fph |
| 11 | $MTTF_S = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_S}$ | Mean Time To Safe Failure | hours |
| 12 | $T_{proof}$ | Proof Test Interval | months or years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations 5 and 6 are the *most tested* formulas in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 3. Board examiners **expect** students to know that SFF is the gateway metric that determines the *minimum hardware fault tolerance* for a given SIL per **IEC 61508 Tables 2 & 3** (and ISO 13849-1 for machinery safety).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (ESD valves):** A spring-return, air-to-open shutdown valve is the canonical fail-safe element. Loss of instrument air → valve fails closed → well is isolated safely.
- **Nuclear Reactor Protection (RPS):** Control rods are held *up* by electromagnets. Loss of power (a fail-safe failure of the hold circuit) → rods drop in by gravity → reactor scrammed.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** An electronic stability control sensor failing "stuck at last valid value" is a *safe failure* (no spurious intervention), whereas "stuck at 0" may be *dangerous* (loss of control).
- **Railway Signaling (EN 50128/50129):** Trackside relays are *de-energize-to-stop*. Power loss = train stops = fail-safe.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) — The Workhorse Equation

**Starting point:** The total failure rate $\lambda_{total}$ is the sum of all four mutually exclusive failure categories from the IEC 61508 failure model.

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

**Definition step:** Safe Failure Fraction is, by IEC 61508 Part 4 Clause 3.6.12, the ratio of *all safe failures + all detected dangerous failures* to the *total failure rate*. The detected dangerous failures are *counted as contributing to safety* because the diagnostic function that detected them has already forced the safe state.

$$SFF = \frac{\text{(Safe + Detected Dangerous) Failures}}{\text{Total Failures}}$$

**Substitution step:** Replace numerator and denominator with the lambda symbols.

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Simplification step:** Numerator = (Total – $\lambda_{DU}$). Substitute and factor.

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{total} - \lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

$$SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

**Final canonical form (the version board examiners prefer):**

$$\boxed{\,SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}} \times 100\%\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Time To Safe Failure (MTTF_S)

**Starting premise:** Failure events follow an exponential distribution when failures are independent and memoryless (a standard assumption in IEC 61508 reliability block diagrams). The probability density function is:

$$f(t) = \lambda_S \cdot e^{-\lambda_S \cdot t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Expected value integral:** MTTF is the *expected* (mean) time until the next safe failure event.

$$MTTF_S = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda_S \cdot e^{-\lambda_S \cdot t} \, dt$$

**Integration by parts** (let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda_S e^{-\lambda_S t} dt$):

$$MTTF_S = \left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda_S t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_S t} \, dt$$

**Evaluate the boundary term** (exponential decay dominates linear growth, so the term vanishes at $\infty$, and equals 0 at t = 0):

$$MTTF_S = 0 + \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda_S t}}{\lambda_S} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Compute the bounds:**

$$MTTF_S = \left( 0 - \frac{-1}{\lambda_S} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda_S}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,MTTF_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S}\,}$$

**Conversion to years** (a common KTU board question):

$$MTTF_S \, [years] = \frac{1}{\lambda_S \, [fph] \times 8760 \, [h/year]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example:** If a sensor has $\lambda_S = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ fph, then $MTTF_S = 10^6$ hours $\approx$ **114.16 years**. This is the expected *safe* lifetime, **not** the dangerous lifetime — students must not confuse the two.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PFD Formula for a 1oo1 Architecture (Safe + Undetected Safe)

For a *single-channel* (1oo1) sensor, the average Probability of Failure on Demand is the area under the dangerous-failure probability curve over one proof test interval $T$:

**Dangerous failure probability at time t** (assuming exponential distribution):

$$P_{D}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \cdot t} \approx \lambda_D \cdot t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda_D \cdot t \text{)}$$

**Average over the proof test interval $T_{proof}$:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

**Evaluate the integral:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_{proof}} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Restrict to undetected dangerous failures** (the safe/detected ones are revealed by diagnostics and handled by the safe-state action):

$$\boxed{\,PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Rate Examples (KTU Board Reference)

| Component | $\lambda_{SD}$ (fph) | $\lambda_{SU}$ (fph) | $\lambda_{DD}$ (fph) | $\lambda_{DU}$ (fph) | SFF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Pressure Transmitter (with diagnostics) | $1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ | $0.3 \times 10^{-7}$ | $0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ | $0.2 \times 10^{-7}$ | **~98.6%** |
| Solenoid Valve (spring-return, de-energize to trip) | $4.0 \times 10^{-7}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ | $5.0 \times 10^{-8}$ | **~92.0%** |
| Simple Thermocouple (no diagnostics) | $0$ | $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $0$ | $3.0 \times 10^{-7}$ | **~87.0%** |
| PLC Logic Solver (modern, type B) | $5.0 \times 10^{-7}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{-7}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | **~99.8%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** When a board question gives a *single* failure rate $\lambda$ (e.g., $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ fph from a manufacturer datasheet), you **must** first partition it into $\lambda_S$ and $\lambda_D$ using the manufacturer's stated failure-mode distribution. Assume **50/50** as a worst-case default *only* if no other data is given. The board often tests whether the student knows the *partitioning step* precedes the SFF calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Failure Classification Hierarchy (IEC 61508 View)

This block diagram traces the IEC 61508 failure taxonomy from the top-level $\lambda$ down to the four leaf categories. Note that every node ID is alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[lambda TOTAL Failure Rate] --> B[lambda S Safe Failures]
    A --> C[lambda D Dangerous Failures]
    B --> D[lambda SD Safe Detected]
    B --> E[lambda SU Safe Undetected]
    C --> F[lambda DD Dangerous Detected]
    C --> G[lambda DU Dangerous Undetected]
    D --> H[Drives system to safe state OR no effect]
    E --> I[Latent until next proof test]
    F --> J[Diagnostic forces safe state]
    G --> K[CRITICAL: contributes to PFD or PFH]
    H --> L[COUNTED IN SFF numerator]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> M[NOT counted in SFF numerator]
```

## 4.2 Figure 2: Functional Architecture of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) with Fail-Safe Properties

This diagram shows the canonical three-stage SIF (Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element) and labels each stage with its dominant fail-safe design choice. This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* that the KTU Module 3 syllabus explicitly references.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Sensor Stage: Smart 4-20mA Transmitter] -->|Analog signal| LS1[Logic Solver: SIL-rated PLC]
    S2[Sensor Stage: Voting 1oo2 Redundancy] -->|Backup signal| LS1
    LS1 -->|Trip command| FE1[Final Element: Spring Return Solenoid Valve]
    LS1 -->|Trip command| FE2[Final Element: De energize to Trip Contactor]
    FE1 --> SV[Safe State: Valve CLOSED process isolated]
    FE2 --> MS[Safe State: Motor STOPPED no mechanical hazard]
    DIAG[Internal Diagnostic Processor: continuous self test] -.->|Detects faults| LS1
    DIAG -.->|Alarms| HMI[Operator HMI for proof test scheduling]
```

## 4.3 Figure 3: Safe Failure Decision Flowchart (Design Logic)

This decision tree walks the safety engineer through the classification of any failure they encounter. The KTU board expects students to be able to *narrate* this logic in a 7-mark answer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Start: A fault occurs in a component] --> Q1{Does the fault affect the ability of the SIF to perform its safety function when demanded?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the fault detected by internal diagnostics?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Is the dangerous failure detected by diagnostics?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[lambda SD Safe Detected]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[lambda SU Safe Undetected]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[lambda DD Dangerous Detected drives safe state]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[lambda DU Dangerous Undetected contributes to PFD PFH]
    R1 --> SFF[Compute SFF using canonical formula]
    R2 --> SFF
    R3 --> SFF
    R4 --> SFF
    SFF --> MAP{MAP SFF to IEC 61508 Table 2 to find permitted hardware fault tolerance for target SIL}
```

## 4.4 Figure 4: Proof Test Interval vs. PFD Trade-off

The board frequently includes a graph of $PFD_{avg}$ vs. proof test interval. Reproduced here as a Mermaid-styled conceptual block since a continuous plot cannot be rendered in Mermaid. The relationship is *linear* for low values.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PT1[Proof Test Interval 1 month] --> P1[PFD avg approx 1.15 times 10 to power minus 4]
    PT2[Proof Test Interval 6 months] --> P2[PFD avg approx 6.9 times 10 to power minus 4]
    PT3[Proof Test Interval 1 year] --> P3[PFD avg approx 1.38 times 10 to power minus 3]
    PT4[Proof Test Interval 2 years] --> P4[PFD avg approx 2.76 times 10 to power minus 3 outside SIL 1 range]
    P1 --> Z[Compare to SIL target band SIL 1 needs PFD between 10 to power minus 2 and 10 to power minus 1]
    P2 --> Z
    P3 --> Z
    P4 --> Z
    Z --> DEC[Design Decision: shorten proof test interval OR add redundancy to meet SIL target]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]
**Define the term "Fail-Safe Failure" as per IEC 61508. How does it differ from a "Dangerous Failure" in the context of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Fail-Safe Failure* is a failure that (i) does not prevent the SIF from performing its safety function when a process demand occurs, **or** (ii) results in the EUC/EUC control system moving to (or remaining in) a defined **safe state** without the safety function being demanded. (1.5 marks)

In contrast, a *Dangerous Failure* prevents the SIF from executing its safety function when demanded, *or* increases the probability of a hazardous event by failing in a direction that *opposes* the safe state (e.g., a valve failing *open* when it should fail *closed*). (1 mark)

The fundamental difference: safe failures are *inherently* aligned with process protection and are excluded from the PFD/PFH budget; dangerous failures are the only ones that *contribute* to the probability of dangerous failure used for SIL determination. (0.5 marks)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]
**List the four sub-categories into which the total failure rate of a SIF component is partitioned. Write the formula for Safe Failure Fraction (SFF).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four sub-categories are (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks):
1. $\lambda_{SD}$ — Safe, Detected
2. $\lambda_{SU}$ — Safe, Undetected
3. $\lambda_{DD}$ — Dangerous, Detected
4. $\lambda_{DU}$ — Dangerous, Undetected

Safe Failure Fraction formula (1 mark):

$$SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**Question A(a) — 7 marks:**
A pressure transmitter used in a level safety function has the following failure data (per IEC 61508 vendor datasheet):
- $\lambda_{SD} = 1.20 \times 10^{-6}$ fph
- $\lambda_{SU} = 3.00 \times 10^{-8}$ fph
- $\lambda_{DD} = 5.00 \times 10^{-7}$ fph
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2.00 \times 10^{-8}$ fph

Compute: (i) the total failure rate, (ii) the Safe Failure Fraction, (iii) the Mean Time To Safe Failure, and (iv) the diagnostic coverage of dangerous failures.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total failure rate:** Sum all four categories.

$$\lambda_{total} = (1.20 \times 10^{-6}) + (3.00 \times 10^{-8}) + (5.00 \times 10^{-7}) + (2.00 \times 10^{-8})$$

Converting all to a common exponent ($10^{-8}$):

$$\lambda_{total} = 120 \times 10^{-8} + 3 \times 10^{-8} + 50 \times 10^{-8} + 2 \times 10^{-8} = 175 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{total} = 1.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ fph}} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**(ii) SFF calculation:**

$$SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = 1 - \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-8}}{1.75 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$SFF = 1 - 0.01143 = 0.98857$$

$$\boxed{SFF \approx 98.86\%} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**(iii) MTTF_S:**

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} = 1.20 \times 10^{-6} + 3.00 \times 10^{-8} = 1.23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ fph}$$

$$MTTF_S = \frac{1}{1.23 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.13 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours}$$

Converting to years:

$$MTTF_S = \frac{8.13 \times 10^{5}}{8760} \approx \boxed{92.8 \text{ years}} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**(iv) Diagnostic coverage of dangerous failures:**

$$DC_D = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \frac{5.00 \times 10^{-7}}{5.00 \times 10^{-7} + 2.00 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$DC_D = \frac{5.00 \times 10^{-7}}{5.20 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.9615$$

$$\boxed{DC_D \approx 96.15\%} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

---

**Question A(b) — 7 marks:**
Explain with a suitable block diagram how a "fail-safe" design philosophy is achieved in a typical 3-stage Safety Instrumented Function (Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element). Give **two** real-world industrial examples.

**Model Answer:**

A fail-safe design philosophy is the *deliberate selection* of components and architectures such that the *most probable* or *natural* failure mode drives the process to its safe state, *even when the safety function is not actively demanded*. (1 mark)

**Block Diagram of the 3-Stage SIF with fail-safe considerations:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    SS[Sensor Stage: fail safe element chosen e.g. normally closed limit switch] --> LSG[Logic Solver: fail safe programming e.g. de energize to trip, internal diagnostics, redundancy 1oo2]
    LSG --> FE[Final Element: fail safe actuator e.g. spring return valve, gravity drop contactor]
    SS -.->|Loss of signal = safe state| LSG
    LSG -.->|Loss of power = safe state| FE
    FE -.->|Mechanical spring or gravity| SAFE[Safe State: process isolated or de energized]
```

(3 marks for the diagram and the explanation that accompanies each block)

**Industrial Example 1 — Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Valve in an Oil & Gas Plant:**
A spring-return, air-to-open ball valve is installed on a hydrocarbon line. The safe state is "valve closed, line isolated". If instrument air is lost, the spring *mechanically* drives the valve to the closed position. Loss of air is therefore a *fail-safe* failure of the actuator — the process is protected without any electronic intervention. (1.5 marks)

**Industrial Example 2 — Nuclear Reactor Protection System (RPS) Control Rod Insertion:**
Control rods are held *above* the reactor core by electromagnetic coils. The safe state is "rods inserted, fission rate suppressed". Loss of electrical power to the coils (a fail-safe failure of the hold circuit) releases the rods, and they drop into the core by *gravity* — the reactor scrams. The natural failure mode of the *holding mechanism* is therefore aligned with the safe state. (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *draw* the diagram but *forget to label each block with the fail-safe mechanism* (spring, gravity, de-energize-to-trip). A diagram without annotations earns **at most 1 of 3 marks** for the figure. Always annotate every block with the *mechanism* by which the fail-safe property is achieved.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Question B(a) — 7 marks:**
Derive the formula for Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) starting from the total failure rate partition. Show **every** algebraic step clearly. How is SFF used to determine the permitted hardware fault tolerance for SIL 2 in a Type B subsystem per IEC 61508 Table 2?

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Total failure rate partition:** The IEC 61508 model partitions all failures into four mutually exclusive categories.

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of SFF:** SFF is defined as the fraction of failures that are *either* safe *or* detected dangerous, divided by the total failure rate.

$$SFF = \frac{\text{Safe Failures} + \text{Detected Dangerous Failures}}{\text{Total Failures}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the lambda symbols:**

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the numerator equals total minus undetected dangerous:**

$$\text{Numerator} = \lambda_{total} - \lambda_{DU}$$

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{total} - \lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

**Step 5 — Split the fraction:**

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_{total}}{\lambda_{total}} - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

**Final canonical form:**

$$\boxed{SFF = \left(1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}\right) \times 100\%} \quad (4 \text{ marks for the derivation})$$

**Step 6 — Application to Type B subsystem, SIL 2:**

Per IEC 61508 Part 2 Table 2 (Type B subsystems — complex components with non-deterministic behavior, e.g., microcontrollers, ASICs):

- For SIL 2, a Type B subsystem requires:
  - **SFF ≥ 60%** (per the table), AND
  - **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) = 1**

If the computed SFF is **60% to < 90%** → HFT = 1 is required (i.e., the architecture must tolerate one fault — 1oo2 or 2oo3 voting).
If the computed SFF is **≥ 90%** → HFT = 0 may be permitted (single-channel 1oo1 is acceptable for SIL 2 in Type B). (3 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students confuse Type A and Type B tables. **Type A** = simple, well-defined components (resistors, capacitors, relays with no software). **Type B** = complex components (microprocessors, FPGAs, ASICs with embedded software). The SFF/HFT thresholds *differ* between the two tables. Always state the Type explicitly in your answer.

---

**Question B(b) — 7 marks:**
A 1oo1 architecture safety sensor has $\lambda_{DU} = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ fph and a proof test interval $T_{proof} = 1$ year (8760 hours). Compute the average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$). If the process is to be upgraded to SIL 2 (which requires $PFD_{avg}$ between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$), is this design acceptable as-is? If not, suggest **two** design changes to bring the $PFD_{avg}$ within SIL 2 limits.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the 1oo1 PFD formula:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1.314 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 6.57 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} \approx 6.57 \times 10^{-4}} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3 — Compare to SIL 2 range:**

The SIL 2 PFD range is $10^{-2} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$ (i.e., 0.01 to 0.1). (1 mark)

Our value $6.57 \times 10^{-4} = 0.000657$ is **below the lower bound of SIL 2** — meaning the design is *safer than SIL 2* (it is comfortably in the SIL 3 region of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$). Therefore the design is **acceptable** and exceeds the SIL 2 requirement with significant margin. (2 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner note:** Board evaluators will accept both "yes, it is acceptable" *and* "it is over-engineered; consider whether the added cost is justified". A student who *critically* evaluates the result earns full marks.

**Step 4 — If the result had been *too high* (i.e., if $\lambda_{DU}$ were larger), the two standard design changes would be:** (1 mark)

1. **Reduce the proof test interval** $T_{proof}$ — halving $T_{proof}$ halves the $PFD_{avg}$ (linear relationship). The trade-off is increased maintenance cost and process downtime for testing.
2. **Add diagnostic coverage to convert $\lambda_{DU}$ to $\lambda_{DD}$** — installing internal self-test circuitry reduces $\lambda_{DU}$ (the dangerous-undetected category) by detecting and alarming the failure, thereby transferring it to $\lambda_{DD}$. The trade-off is hardware complexity and potential for additional CCFs.

(1 mark for the two suggestions)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students mistakenly use the *total* $\lambda_D$ (including $\lambda_{DD}$) in the PFD formula. **Only $\lambda_{DU}$ contributes to PFD** in the standard 1oo1 derivation, because $\lambda_{DD}$ is *already detected* and the system has *already* forced the safe state. Using the wrong lambda inflates $PFD_{avg}$ by 1–2 orders of magnitude and *fails* the SIL check even when the design is good.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fail-Safe Failure** = a failure that does *not* prevent the safety function from operating, *or* that drives the system to the safe state. It is the *desirable* failure mode.
- ✅ The total failure rate $\lambda$ splits into **Safe ($\lambda_S$)** and **Dangerous ($\lambda_D$)** categories.
- ✅ Each of $\lambda_S$ and $\lambda_D$ further splits into **Detected** and **Undetected**, giving four sub-categories: $\lambda_{SD}$, $\lambda_{SU}$, $\lambda_{DD}$, $\lambda_{DU}$.
- ✅ **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is the key architectural metric: $SFF = 1 - \lambda_{DU}/\lambda_{total}$.
- ✅ SFF and **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** together determine the *maximum achievable SIL* per IEC 61508 Tables 2 & 3.
- ✅ **Type A** components (simple, deterministic) have *less stringent* SFF thresholds than **Type B** components (complex, with software/FPGAs).
- ✅ **Mean Time To Safe Failure**: $MTTF_S = 1/\lambda_S$ (units: hours or years; remember to divide by 8760 for years).
- ✅ **1oo1 architecture PFD formula** (low-demand mode): $PFD_{avg} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} / 2$.
- ✅ **High-demand mode PFH** (continuous mode, e.g., 1oo1): $PFH \approx \lambda_{DU}$.
- ✅ **Fail-safe design** is achieved by: spring-return actuators, gravity-operated devices, de-energize-to-trip logic, normally-closed contacts, and 1oo2 / 2oo3 voting redundancy (Module 4).
- ✅ **Proof Test Interval** $T_{proof}$ directly multiplies PFD — shorter $T_{proof}$ = lower PFD = higher SIL achievable.
- ✅ **Common Cause Failures (CCF)** are a *hidden danger*; even safe failures can be CCF-induced — always apply the β-factor model and demand diversity.
- ✅ **Board Pitfall:** Always partition $\lambda$ into the four sub-categories *first*; never use the manufacturer-stated single $\lambda$ directly in the SFF formula.
- ✅ **Board Pitfall:** Always state whether the component is Type A or Type B before quoting SFF/HFT thresholds.
- ✅ **Board Pitfall:** $PFD_{avg}$ uses *only* $\lambda_{DU}$, never $\lambda_{DD}$ or $\lambda_{total}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dangerous Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dangerous Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (IEC 61508-4:2010, Clause 3.6.5):** A **dangerous failure** is a *failure* of an *element*, *subsystem*, or *system* that *prevents* the safety function from operating correctly when demanded, OR that *prevents* the safety function from being performed when demanded. The failure causes the EUC (Equipment Under Control) to move to, or remain in, a hazardous state without the protective action of the safety function being triggered.

A dangerous failure has two operational sub-categories mandated by the KTU 2024 Functional Safety syllabus:

1. **Detected Dangerous Failure (DD):** A dangerous failure that is *recognized* by the automatic diagnostic tests built into the SIS (Safety Instrumented System). It is typically revealed within the Diagnostic Test Interval $\left(T_{D}\right)$, and the system can raise an alarm or force a safe shutdown.

2. **Undetected Dangerous Failure (DU):** A dangerous failure that *escapes* the automatic diagnostic coverage. It remains latent (hidden) in the subsystem and is only revealed either by:
   - A **Proof Test** (manual inspection/calibration at the Proof Test Interval $T_{proof}$), or
   - An **actual process demand** that the system fails to handle.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The DU fraction is the *dominant* contributor to the average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$) and Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH). Examiners specifically test whether students can decompose the total failure rate $\lambda$ into its four canonical components.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smoke Detector" Mental Model

Imagine a **smoke detector** in your hostel room:

- **Safe failure** = the battery dies (no fire present, but the alarm sounds). Inconvenient, but nobody dies.
- **Dangerous failure** = the sensor electronics silently burn out so that *when a real fire starts*, the alarm never rings. This is the catastrophic scenario functional safety engineering is designed to prevent.
- **Detected Dangerous** = the detector has a self-test (chirps every 30 seconds). The moment the sensor dies, the chirp alerts you.
- **Undetected Dangerous** = the detector has *no* self-test. The sensor dies silently, and you only discover the problem when a real fire occurs and nothing happens.

The goal of every safety engineer is to **maximize the detected portion (DD)** and **minimize the undetected portion (DU)** of dangerous failures, because DU failures are the silent killers that the proof test must hunt down periodically.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** When asked to "define dangerous failure," always explicitly mention **both conditions** (failure to operate on demand AND failure to place the EUC in a safe state). Half-marks are deducted if only one clause is quoted.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | KTU Significance |
|:------:|:---------|:-------------|:-----------------|
| $\lambda$ | Total failure rate | $\text{failures} / 10^6 \text{ hours}$ (FIT) | Decomposed into four sub-fractions |
| $\lambda_D$ | Dangerous failure rate | FIT | $= \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ |
| $\lambda_S$ | Safe failure rate | FIT | $= \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}$ |
| $DC$ | Diagnostic Coverage | % ($0 \leq DC \leq 100$) | Fraction of failures caught by diagnostics |
| $SFF$ | Safe Failure Fraction | % ($0 \leq SFF \leq 100$) | Architectural constraint driver |
| $T_{proof}$ | Proof Test Interval | years / months / hours | Periodically reveals $\lambda_{DU}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Quadrant Decomposition of $\lambda$

Every failure of an electronic / electro-mechanical component in a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is classified along **two independent binary axes**, producing **four mutually exclusive categories**. This is the conceptual cornerstone of Module 3.

**Axis 1 — Effect on the safety function:**
- **Dangerous (D):** Failure impedes the SIF from executing its protective duty.
- **Safe (S):** Failure causes a false trip but does not impede protection.

**Axis 2 — Diagnostic detection status:**
- **Detected (D):** Failure is identified by the SIS's automatic self-diagnostics.
- **Undetected (U):** Failure lies latent; only a proof test reveals it.

This produces the **canonical four-quadrant decomposition** that IEC 61508 mandates:

$$
\lambda = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conservation Law of Failure Rates:** This equation is exact and must always balance. In any KTU numerical problem, if one of the four terms is missing, the other three do *not* add to the total. Examiners commonly provide 3 of the 4 terms and ask for the 4th.

## 2.2 Diagnostic Coverage ($DC$) — The Detection Efficiency

> [!NOTE]  
> **Definition:** Diagnostic Coverage is the *ratio* of the failure rate of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostics to the *total* dangerous failure rate. It is expressed as a dimensionless fraction (typically as a percentage).

For the **dangerous** subset of failures:

$$
DC_D = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D}
$$

For the **safe** subset of failures:

$$
DC_S = \frac{\lambda_{SD}}{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}} = \frac{\lambda_{SD}}{\lambda_S}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Common student pitfall:** Students frequently confuse $DC_D$ with $DC_S$. The KTU board uses different symbols. Always state the subscript.

## 2.3 Safe Failure Fraction ($SFF$) — The Architectural Gatekeeper

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition (IEC 61508-2):** The Safe Failure Fraction of a subsystem is the ratio of the *average* failure rates of safe failures *plus* detected dangerous failures to the *total* failure rate of the subsystem.

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda} = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda}
$$

Expanded for clarity:

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why SFF is the gatekeeper:** Together with the **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**, SFF determines whether a subsystem satisfies the **architectural constraints** of Route 1$_{H}$ (per IEC 61508-2, Tables 2 and 3). A high SFF allows the use of lower HFT, which directly affects cost.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Golden Six" for Dangerous Failures

> [!WARNING]  
> **Table formatting rule:** Absolute value bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown. Do NOT use `|`.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Exam Use |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|:---------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}$ | Total failure rate decomposition | FIT | Numerical completion |
| 2 | $\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Total dangerous failure rate | FIT | Required for PFD |
| 3 | $DC_D = \lambda_{DD} \, / \, \lambda_D$ | Diagnostic coverage (dangerous) | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Derivation step |
| 4 | $SFF = 1 - \lambda_{DU} / \lambda$ | Safe failure fraction | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Architectural check |
| 5 | $\text{PFD}_{avg} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} / 2$ | 1oo1 subsystem PFD (low-demand mode) | dimensionless | 7-mark problems |
| 6 | $\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_{DU}$ | 1oo1 subsystem PFH (high-demand mode) | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ | 7-mark problems |

## 2.5 Relationship Between $\lambda_{DU}$, Proof Test Interval, and SIL

The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$)** for a 1oo1 architecture in low-demand mode is fundamentally driven by the **average downtime of a dangerous undetected failure**, which equals $T_{proof}/2$ (assuming a uniform failure distribution over the proof test interval):

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}
$$

In high-demand / continuous mode, the system never gets a "rest," and PFH replaces PFD:

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_{DU}
$$

This makes $\lambda_{DU}$ the **single most important quantity** in the entire SIL calculation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFD$_{avg}$ for a 1oo1 Subsystem (Dangerous Undetected Failures)

**Starting premise:** Consider a single channel (1oo1) subject only to dangerous *undetected* failures with constant rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (FIT converted to $\text{hr}^{-1}$), operating in low-demand mode with a proof test interval $T_{proof}$ (hours).

**Step 1 — Fraction of channel downtime due to a DU failure:**

A DU failure that occurs at time $t$ (measured from the most recent proof test) will lie undetected until the next proof test at $T_{proof}$. The *average* downtime is:

$$
\text{Average downtime} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t \, dt = \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{T_{proof}^2}{2}
$$

Dividing by the interval:

$$
\text{Average downtime} = \frac{T_{proof}^2}{2 \, T_{proof}} = \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the dangerous failure rate:**

The expected fraction of time the channel is in a dangerous-failed state equals (dangerous failure rate) $\times$ (average downtime):

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Converting $\lambda_{DU}$ from FIT to $\text{hr}^{-1}$:**

Since 1 FIT $= 1 \text{ failure per } 10^6 \text{ hours} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1}$, the equation often appears as:

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \quad \text{with } \lambda_{DU} \text{ in } \text{hr}^{-1} \text{ and } T_{proof} \text{ in hours}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Examiner's key step:** Always show the integration step explicitly in a 7-mark derivation. Half the marks are for setting up the integral; the other half are for evaluation and final substitution.

## 3.2 Derivation of Diagnostic Coverage from Given Data

**Worked KTU-style problem:** A pressure transmitter has the following data sheet:

- $\lambda_{DD} = 250$ FIT
- $\lambda_{DU} = 50$ FIT
- $\lambda_{SD} = 300$ FIT
- $\lambda_{SU} = 100$ FIT
- $T_{proof} = 1 \text{ year}$

**Step 1 — Compute total $\lambda$:**

$$
\lambda = 250 + 50 + 300 + 100 = 700 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\lambda_D$ and $\lambda_S$:**

$$
\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = 250 + 50 = 300 \text{ FIT}
$$

$$
\lambda_S = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} = 300 + 100 = 400 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Diagnostic Coverage for the dangerous subset:**

$$
DC_D = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{250}{300} = 0.833 \approx 83.3\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Safe Failure Fraction:**

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda} = 1 - \frac{50}{700} = 1 - 0.0714 = 0.9286 \approx 92.86\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute PFD$_{avg}$ (low-demand mode, 1oo1):**

Convert $T_{proof}$: $1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ hours}$.
Convert $\lambda_{DU}$: $50 \text{ FIT} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1}$.

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 8760}{2}
$$

Intermediate multiplication:

$$
50 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 50 \times 8.76 \times 10^{-3} = 438 \times 10^{-3} = 0.438
$$

Divided by 2:

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = 0.219 = 2.19 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Map PFD to SIL per IEC 61511-1, Table 3:**

| SIL | PFD$_{avg}$ range | Decision |
|:---:|:------------------|:--------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | — |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | — |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | — |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | — |

Our value $0.219$ falls in the **SIL 1** range. ✅

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "What if I change one parameter?"

| Scenario | Effect on $\text{PFD}_{avg}$ | Effect on SIL | KTU Board Question Type |
|:---------|:----------------------------|:--------------|:------------------------|
| Halve $T_{proof}$ (test more often) | Halves $\text{PFD}_{avg}$ | May upgrade by 1 SIL | "Suggest 2 ways to upgrade SIL" |
| Improve $DC_D$ from 70% → 90% | Reduces $\lambda_{DU}$ by ~67% | Major upgrade possible | "Role of diagnostics in SIL" |
| Migrate 1oo1 → 1oo2 voted | $\text{PFD}^2$ (square law) | Up to 2 SIL upgrades | "Redundancy justification" |
| Replace Type A with Type B component | Adds +1 to HFT requirement | Restricts architectural options | "Type A vs Type B trade-off" |
| Increase $\lambda$ (older/stressed component) | Both $\lambda_{DU}$ and $\lambda_{SD}$ grow | Usually degrades SIL | "Aging equipment safety case" |

> [!NOTE]  
> **The Square-Law Insight (1oo2):** For a 1oo2D architecture, the PFD$_{avg}$ for dangerous undetected failures becomes approximately:
> $$\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} \approx \frac{2}{3} \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} / 2)^2$$
> This is why redundancy is so powerful — failures must now occur in *both* channels in the same proof-test window.

## 3.4 Type A vs Type B Subsystems — Architectural Constraint Interaction

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **IEC 61508-2 distinguishes two component classes that interact with SFF and HFT to determine whether a SIL is achievable:**

| Class | Definition | Failure Modes | Diagnostic Assumed? |
|:------|:-----------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| **Type A** | Well-tried, simple components (resistors, capacitors, relays, opto-couplers with known behavior) | Fully characterized | Not assumed |
| **Type B** | Complex components (microcontrollers, ASICs, custom VLSI) where failure modes cannot be completely characterized | Incompletely characterized | Assumed only if explicitly implemented |

**Example constraint check:** To achieve **SIL 2** in a 1oo1 architecture:
- **Type A subsystem:** Requires $SFF \geq 60\%$
- **Type B subsystem:** Requires $SFF \geq 90\%$

This is why the SFF formula — driven by the $\lambda_{DU}$ term — is structurally critical.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Failure Rate Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#98D8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef du fill:#FFA500,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A["Total Failure Rate lambda"]:::root
    A --> B["Dangerous Failures lambda_D"]:::branch
    A --> C["Safe Failures lambda_S"]:::branch

    B --> D["Detected Dangerous lambda_DD"]:::leaf
    B --> E["Undetected Dangerous lambda_DU"]:::du
    C --> F["Detected Safe lambda_SD"]:::leaf
    C --> G["Undetected Safe lambda_SU"]:::leaf

    D --> H["Caught by Diagnostics - Auto Alarm / Shutdown"]:::danger
    E --> I["Latent - Revealed only by Proof Test"]:::danger
    F --> J["False Trip - Auto Detected"]:::safe
    G --> K["Spurious Trip - Hidden"]:::safe
```

> [!TIP]  
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace any failure from the **root** (total $\lambda$) downward. The two columns (Dangerous vs Safe) split first, then the diagnostic axis splits again. The **orange DU block** is the one that must be minimized in every safety design.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — From Component Fault to SIL Achievement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef startnode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef calcnode fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#7ED321,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef danger fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px

    S1[Component Failure Event]:::startnode
    S2{Does it prevent safety function action?}:::decision
    S3[Dangerous Failure - lambda_D]:::danger
    S4[Safe Failure - lambda_S]:::calcnode
    S5{Are diagnostics present and effective?}:::decision
    S6[Detected Dangerous lambda_DD - safe shutdown triggered]:::result
    S7[Undetected Dangerous lambda_DU - LATENT RISK]:::danger
    S8[Apply Proof Test at T_proof]:::calcnode
    S9[Compute PFD_avg and map to SIL]:::result

    S1 --> S2
    S2 -->|Yes| S3
    S2 -->|No| S4
    S3 --> S5
    S5 -->|Yes DC high| S6
    S5 -->|No or low DC| S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9
    S6 --> S9
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain View — Undetected Failure Accumulation Over Proof Test Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef tnode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef fnode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef anode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    T0["t = 0 hr - Proof Test Complete - Channel Healthy"]:::anode
    T1["t = 1000 hr - DU Failure Occurs - Latent"]:::fnode
    T2["t = 2000 hr - Process Demand - DU failure causes hazardous event"]:::fnode
    T3["t = 8760 hr - Next Proof Test - DU failure detected and repaired"]:::tnode
    T4["Cycle Restarts - Channel Healthy again"]:::anode

    T0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Visual takeaway:** The horizontal distance between the failure occurrence (T1) and its detection (T3) is the *exposure window*. The **average** length of this window across all possible failure times is $T_{proof}/2$, which is exactly what produced the factor of $1/2$ in the PFD$_{avg}$ formula.

## 4.4 Architecture Block Diagram — 1oo1 vs 1oo2D

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef proc fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef vote fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef trip fill:#EF5350,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px

    P1[Process Sensor]:::proc
    L1[Logic Solver]:::ch
    F1[Final Element - Valve]:::ch
    T1[Safe Shutdown]:::trip

    subgraph SUB1["Architecture 1oo1 - Single Channel"]
        direction LR
        P1 --> L1 --> F1 --> T1
    end

    P2[Process Sensor x2]:::proc
    L2[Logic Solver - 1oo2D Voting]:::vote
    F2[Final Element - 2 Valves]:::ch
    T2[Safe Shutdown]:::trip

    subgraph SUB2["Architecture 1oo2D - Dual Channel with Diagnostics"]
        direction LR
        P2 --> L2 --> F2 --> T2
    end
```

> [!TIP]  
> **Reading the architecture diagram:** In **1oo1**, any single DU failure causes system failure. In **1oo2D**, *two* simultaneous DU failures are required. This is the *physical* origin of the square-law reduction in PFD$_{avg}$ for redundant architectures.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define **dangerous failure** as per IEC 61508. How is it different from a **safe failure**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **dangerous failure** is a failure of an element or subsystem that *prevents* the safety instrumented function (SIF) from performing its intended protective action when a process demand occurs, thereby allowing the EUC to enter a hazardous state **(1.5 marks)**.
A **safe failure** is one that *does not* prevent the safety function but instead causes a false/spurious trip, moving the system to a safe state without an actual demand **(1 mark)**.
**Key contrast:** A dangerous failure is failure-to-danger; a safe failure is failure-to-safe (no actual hazard, just nuisance) **(0.5 mark)**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Distinguish between **detected** and **undetected dangerous failures** with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **detected dangerous failure (DD)** is identified by the SIS's automatic self-diagnostics within the diagnostic test interval, raising an alarm or initiating a safe shutdown **(1 mark)**. *Example:* A transmitter with self-test that flags an internal ADC failure on the next scan **(0.5 mark)**.
An **undetected dangerous failure (DU)** lies dormant because the diagnostic system does not detect it; it is only revealed by a manual proof test or by an actual process demand the system fails to handle **(1 mark)**. *Example:* A stuck relay contact that passes all voltage checks but fails to open under real process demand **(0.5 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> Per the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, each module contains an internal choice. Both options are provided below with full model solutions.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A level transmitter in a SIF has the following failure data:**

- $\lambda_{DD} = 180$ FIT, $\lambda_{DU} = 20$ FIT
- $\lambda_{SD} = 120$ FIT, $\lambda_{SU} = 80$ FIT
- $T_{proof} = 2 \text{ years}$, demand mode = **low**

**(a)** Compute the **Diagnostic Coverage ($DC_D$)** and **Safe Failure Fraction ($SFF$)** of the transmitter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{avg}$)** assuming a 1oo1 architecture, and identify the achievable **SIL level** per IEC 61511-1. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute total failure rate $\lambda$** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda = 180 + 20 + 120 + 80 = 400 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute dangerous and safe failure rates** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda_D = 180 + 20 = 200 \text{ FIT}
$$

$$
\lambda_S = 120 + 80 = 200 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $DC_D$** (2 marks):

$$
DC_D = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $SFF$** (3 marks):

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda} = 1 - \frac{20}{400} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert units** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda_{DU} = 20 \text{ FIT} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

$$
T_{proof} = 2 \text{ years} = 2 \times 8760 = 17{,}520 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 1oo1 PFD formula** (3 marks):

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 17{,}520}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate** (1 mark):

$$
20 \times 10^{-6} \times 17{,}520 = 350{,}400 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3504
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{0.3504}{2} = 0.1752
$$

**Step 4 — Map to SIL per IEC 61511-1 Table 3** (2 marks):

| SIL | PFD$_{avg}$ range |
|:---:|:------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

Since $0.0175 < 0.1752 \leq 0.10$? No — $0.1752$ falls between $10^{-1}$ and $10^0$, which lies **just above SIL 1**. The system does **not** meet SIL 1; it is **below SIL 1** in this configuration. ❌

> **Examiner's Note:** To bring this into SIL 1 ($\leq 10^{-1}$), $T_{proof}$ must be reduced to approximately $\leq 1$ year, OR the transmitter must be migrated to a 1oo2D voted architecture.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a suitable **block diagram**, explain the four-quadrant decomposition of failure rate $\lambda$ into dangerous/safe × detected/undetected categories. State the formula for **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** and explain its role in determining the achievable **SIL** for a Type B subsystem in 1oo1 architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety valve subsystem is rated at $\lambda = 800$ FIT, with $SFF = 85\%$, $DC_D = 70\%$, and proof test interval $T_{proof} = 1 \text{ year}$. If the system operates in **high-demand mode** as a 1oo1 channel, calculate the **PFH** and determine the SIL per IEC 61511-1. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Four-quadrant decomposition** (3 marks):

Every failure of a SIF component is classified along two independent binary axes:

$$
\lambda = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}
$$

(Block diagram reference: see Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Step 2 — SFF formula and definition** (2 marks):

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}}{\lambda} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Role in SIL achievement (Type B, 1oo1)** (2 marks):
- For SIL 1, the minimum SFF for a Type B 1oo1 subsystem is **60%**.
- For SIL 2, the minimum SFF for a Type B 1oo1 subsystem is **90%**.
- The high SFF requirement for complex (Type B) components forces the designer to either improve diagnostics, increase redundancy, or accept a lower achievable SIL.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Extract $\lambda_{DU}$ from given data** (2 marks):

$$
SFF = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda} \implies 0.85 = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{800}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU} = 800 \times 0.15 = 120 \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $\lambda_{DU}$ to PFH units** (1 mark):

$$
\lambda_{DU} = 120 \text{ FIT} = 120 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — PFH for 1oo1 high-demand mode** (2 marks):

$$
\text{PFH}_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_{DU} = 120 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Map PFH to SIL per IEC 61511-1 Table 2** (2 marks):

| SIL | PFH range ($\text{hr}^{-1}$) |
|:---:|:----------------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ |

Our value $1.2 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{hr}^{-1}$ falls between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$, which is **above** the SIL 1 range. Hence the system does **not** achieve SIL 1. ❌

> **Examiner's Note:** To reach SIL 1 ($\text{PFH} \leq 10^{-5} \, \text{hr}^{-1}$), $\lambda_{DU}$ must be reduced by a factor of at least 12 — typically requiring redundancy (1oo2) and/or better diagnostics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Dangerous Failures" Questions (per KTU Board Examiner feedback):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $\times 10^{-6}$ conversion** for FIT to $\text{hr}^{-1}$. If you write $\lambda_{DU} = 20$ in your PFD formula without the conversion factor, your answer will be off by **6 orders of magnitude**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $DC_D$ with $SFF$.** $DC_D = \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D$ (dangerous only); $SFF$ uses the full $\lambda$ in the denominator. Mixing these up will cost 2–3 marks instantly.
> 
> 3. **Quoting only half the dangerous failure definition.** Always state BOTH conditions: (i) failure to operate on demand, AND (ii) failure to place EUC in a safe state.
> 
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram for the 4-quadrant decomposition.** Even if the question gives marks for "explain with diagram," examiners expect the diamond/tree structure explicitly. 2 marks are typically reserved for the figure.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up PFD (low-demand) and PFH (high-demand) formulas.** Low-demand uses $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} / 2$; high-demand uses $\lambda_{DU}$ directly. Always re-read the question stem for the demand mode.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- ✅ A **dangerous failure** *prevents* the SIF from executing its protective action; it is the failure that functional safety exists to prevent.
- ✅ Failures split along **two axes**: dangerous vs safe, and detected vs undetected → **four sub-fractions**.
- ✅ The **canonical decomposition** is $\lambda = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU}$.
- ✅ **$\lambda_{DU}$ is the single most important quantity** in any SIL calculation; it drives both PFD$_{avg}$ (low-demand) and PFH (high-demand).
- ✅ **Diagnostic Coverage** $DC_D = \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_D$ measures the *efficiency* of automatic self-tests.
- ✅ **Safe Failure Fraction** $SFF = 1 - \lambda_{DU} / \lambda$ is the *architectural gatekeeper* for Type B components.
- ✅ **Low-demand mode** (PFD): PFD$_{avg,1oo1} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} / 2$.
- ✅ **High-demand mode** (PFH): PFH$_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_{DU}$.
- ✅ **1 FIT** $= 1$ failure per $10^6$ hours $= 1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{hr}^{-1}$. Never forget this conversion.
- ✅ **1 year** $= 8760$ hours; **5 years** $= 43{,}800$ hours.
- ✅ Reducing $T_{proof}$ is the **cheapest** SIL improvement; redundancy is the **most powerful** SIL improvement (square law).
- ✅ For a **Type B 1oo1** subsystem, SFF must be $\geq 60\%$ for SIL 1 and $\geq 90\%$ for SIL 2.
- ✅ The square-law PFD for 1oo2D voted systems is the reason redundancy dominates SIL-3 and SIL-4 designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Failure Fraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safe Failure Fraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

The **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is a quantitative hardware metric defined in the international functional safety standard **IEC 61508** that expresses the ratio of failures which do *not* lead to an unsafe state of the Equipment Under Control (EUC), relative to the *total* failure population of a safety-related subsystem or channel.

In formal notation, SFF is the ratio of the sum of *safe* failure rate (λ_S) and *detected dangerous* failure rate (λ_DD) to the total failure rate (λ_total) of the considered subsystem:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **SFF is NOT a probability of failure.** It is a *fractional reliability index* describing the share of "non-hazardous" failures within the entire failure space. It is a pure number expressed as a percentage between **0%** and **100%** (or 0 to 1 in decimal form).

The standard categorises failures into four orthogonal classes based on two binary attributes:
- **Effect**: Safe (S) vs. Dangerous (D)
- **Detection**: Detected (D) vs. Undetected (U)

Combining these yields the four canonical buckets used throughout IEC 61508-2: **λ_S, λ_DD, λ_DU** (and implicitly λ_SU, which is included inside λ_S for SFF purposes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trustworthy Doctor" Analogy

Imagine you are evaluating a doctor (the safety subsystem) who treats 1,000 patients per year. Out of these 1,000 cases, suppose the doctor makes mistakes, but most mistakes are caught early by a vigilant nurse (diagnostic system) and corrected.

- **Safe failures (λ_S)** = Cases where the doctor's own mistake is *intrinsically harmless* (e.g., prescribing a vitamin instead of a needed drug — patient is not endangered, just sub-optimally treated).
- **Detected dangerous failures (λ_DD)** = Cases where a *potentially serious* mistake is made but the vigilant nurse catches it *in time* and intervenes (the patient is saved).
- **Undetected dangerous failures (λ_DU)** = Cases where a serious mistake is made and *nobody catches it* until it is too late — this is what genuinely endangers the patient.

The **Safe Failure Fraction** of this doctor is therefore:

> $$\text{Doctor's SFF} = \frac{\text{Harmless mistakes} + \text{Caught serious mistakes}}{\text{All mistakes}}$$

A doctor with a high SFF is one whose dangerous mistakes are either rare or are reliably caught by the diagnostic system. **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Examiners frequently quote this analogy in 2-mark sub-parts, so internalise the S/DD/DU split before proceeding.

### 1.3 Why SFF Exists in Functional Safety

IEC 61508 introduced SFF because the *absolute* failure rate of a component is meaningless on its own — a relay that fails once every 100 years but always fails dangerously is far worse than a sensor that fails every 10 years but whose failures are always safe or detected. **SFF enables the engineer to grade the *quality* of failures, not just the quantity.** This quality dimension is what determines whether a hardware element is suitable for a given **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 → SIL 4)**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Takeaway for KTU 2024 Scheme:** SFF is the *bridge* between a component's failure-mode distribution and the architectural constraints (Hardware Fault Tolerance) imposed by IEC 61508-2 Tables 2 and 3. You cannot solve any KTU numerical on SIL determination without it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Failure-Rate Universe — Decomposing λ_total

Every safety-related element has a *total* failure rate λ, which IEC 61508 partitions as follows. The notation used throughout the KTU Functional Safety module is summarised in the table below.

| Symbol | Failure Category | Effect on EUC | Detected by Diagnostics? |
|:------:|:-----------------|:-------------:|:------------------------:|
| λ_S    | Safe failure     | None / benign | Irrelevant (already safe) |
| λ_DD   | Detected dangerous failure | Hazardous *if uncorrected* | **Yes** |
| λ_DU   | Undetected dangerous failure | Hazardous *and uncorrected* | **No** |
| λ_SU   | Safe undetected  | None          | Irrelevant               |
| λ_SD   | Safe detected    | None          | Irrelevant               |
| λ_D    | Total dangerous  | Hazardous in principle | Sum of DD + DU |

The *total* failure rate of the subsystem is therefore:

$$\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

Note carefully: in the KTU/IEC formulation, **λ_S already encompasses both λ_SU and λ_SD**, so the elementary decomposition above is the *minimal* three-term form. Examiners will mark you down if you treat λ_SD and λ_SU as separate from λ_S.

### 2.2 The Two Governing Equations

**Equation 1 — Safe Failure Fraction:**

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Equation 2 — Diagnostic Coverage (DC):**

$$DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D}}$$

These two equations are the *entire* mathematical core of SFF analysis. Every KTU problem reduces to plugging numbers into them, often preceded by a logic-diagram or fault-tree step to obtain λ_DU.

### 2.3 The Three Logical Steps of SFF Computation

The KTU board expects the following ordered reasoning whenever SFF appears in an examination:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the failure modes.** Read the problem statement and classify every named failure into one of {S, DD, DU} using the *operational* definition: *Would the failure cause the safety function to be lost? If yes → dangerous. Would the automatic diagnostic test catch it before the safety demand? If yes → detected, else → undetected.*
2. **Step 2 — Sum the rates.** Compute λ_S, λ_DD, λ_DU by summing the failure rates of every component in the channel that contributes to that category. *Watch out for series/parallel architectural adjustments — see Section 3.*
3. **Step 3 — Apply the formula.** Substitute into SFF = (λ_S + λ_DD) / λ_total. Express the result as a percentage. Verify that the answer lies in [0, 100] %.

### 2.4 Why SFF Is Always Paired with HFT

IEC 61508-2 (Table 2 for Type A, Table 3 for Type B elements) restricts permissible SFF/HFT combinations for each SIL. The hardware fault tolerance HFT = N means the system can tolerate N faults *without loss of the safety function* (e.g., a 1-out-of-2 voted system has HFT = 1). The SFF requirement is *tightest* when HFT is low:

- High SFF + low HFT ⇒ a single channel element can achieve high SIL.
- Low SFF + high HFT ⇒ acceptable for moderate SIL through redundancy.

**Real-world engineering utility:** SFF drives the *component selection* stage of any SIL-rated Safety Instrumented System (SIS) project. In a refinery burner management system, the flame scanner may have an SFF of 70% (acceptable for SIL 2 with HFT=1) but is *not* suitable as a single SIL 3 element; the engineer would need to vote 2-out-of-3 or upgrade the scanner. This SFF-vs-HFT trade-off is the **central architectural decision** in IEC 61508-compliant design.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Memorise the table below verbatim. Every KTU numerical in Module 3 of PESFT635 maps to exactly one of these formulas.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Output Range | Typical Use in KTU Papers |
|:-:|:-------------|:------------------------|:------------:|:--------------------------|
| F1 | Safe Failure Fraction | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | 0% to 100% | Core metric for all SFF problems |
| F2 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | 0% to 100% | Given when SFF is partly known |
| F3 | Total Failure Rate | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | fit (failures/hour) | Denominator of SFF |
| F4 | Dangerous Failure Rate | $\lambda_{D} = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = \lambda_{total}(1 - \text{SFF})$ | fit | Inverse relation with SFF |
| F5 | Subsystem SFF (1oo1) | $\text{SFF}_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\sum \lambda_{Si} + \sum \lambda_{DD,i}}{\sum \lambda_{total,i}}$ | 0% to 100% | When i components are in series |
| F6 | Inverse — λ_DU from SFF | $\lambda_{DU} = \lambda_{total}(1 - \text{SFF})(1 - DC)$ | fit | Reverse-engineering problems |
| F7 | Common-Cause Correction | $\lambda_{DU,sub} = \lambda_{DU,comp} + (1 - \beta)\lambda_{DU,comp}$ | fit | Redundant architectures |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Boundary condition check (high-yield for KTU):** A component with SFF = 100% means *every* failure is either safe or detected dangerous — it is the "perfect" safety element. A component with SFF = 0% means *every* failure is an undetected dangerous one — it is *prohibited* in any safety function (and is, in fact, the antithesis of functional safety).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SFF Equation from First Principles

The mathematical origin of SFF lies in the *partition-of-unity* idea: the universe of all possible failures of a subsystem is exhausted by the three mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) events {Safe, Detected Dangerous, Undetected Dangerous}. Therefore:

$$\Pr(\text{Safe}) + \Pr(\text{Detected Dangerous}) + \Pr(\text{Undetected Dangerous}) = 1$$

In reliability engineering, when components operate in their *useful life* (the flat portion of the bathtub curve), the **failure rate λ is a constant per unit time**, and over a small interval Δt, the probability of failure is approximately λ·Δt. Replacing Pr(·) with the corresponding rates:

$$\frac{\lambda_{S} \cdot \Delta t + \lambda_{DD} \cdot \Delta t + \lambda_{DU} \cdot \Delta t}{\lambda_{total} \cdot \Delta t} = 1$$

The Δt cancels identically, leaving:

$$\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = \lambda_{total}$$

By definition, SFF is the *complement* of the Undetected Dangerous share:

$$\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$$

Substituting the MECE identity for the denominator:

$$\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

Combining the two fractions over a common denominator yields the canonical KTU form:

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} - \lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

This is the **derivation KTU expects** in any "show that SFF = ..." part of a 14-mark question. Present it as above; do not skip the MECE step.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Single-Channel 1oo1 Architecture

**Problem (KTU University Exam – Jul 2023, adapted):** A pressure transmitter in a burner management system has the following failure rates as reported by its manufacturer (in failures per 10^6 hours, or fit):

- Safe failure rate: λ_S = 25 fit
- Detected dangerous failure rate (caught by the on-board self-diagnostic): λ_DD = 90 fit  
- Undetected dangerous failure rate: λ_DU = 35 fit

**Required:** (a) Compute the Safe Failure Fraction. (b) State whether the element can be used as a single channel (1oo1) in a Type B SIL 2 safety function per IEC 61508-2 Table 3. (c) If the diagnostic coverage can be improved such that λ_DU drops to 10 fit (with no change in λ_S, λ_total), what is the *new* SFF?

#### 3.2.1 Solution to Part (a) — Compute SFF

**Step 1 — List the three rates.**

$$\lambda_{S} = 25 \text{ fit}, \quad \lambda_{DD} = 90 \text{ fit}, \quad \lambda_{DU} = 35 \text{ fit}$$

**Step 2 — Sum to obtain λ_total.**

$$\lambda_{total} = 25 + 90 + 35 = 150 \text{ fit}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SFF formula.**

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{25 + 90}{25 + 90 + 35} = \frac{115}{150}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\text{SFF} = 0.7667 = 76.67\%$$

#### 3.2.2 Solution to Part (b) — SIL Feasibility Check

**Step 5 — Recall Type B element Table 3 (IEC 61508-2).** For a *Type B* element used in a 1oo1 (HFT = 0) configuration, the minimum SFF for SIL 2 is **60% ≤ SFF < 90%** (since SFF ≥ 90% would be required for SIL 3 with HFT = 0).

**Step 6 — Compare.** Computed SFF = 76.67%, which satisfies 60% ≤ 76.67% < 90%.

> **Conclusion:** The transmitter **can** be used as a single-channel element in a Type B SIL 2 safety function. It **cannot** be used in SIL 3 as a 1oo1 element (would need SFF ≥ 90% or a redundant 1oo2 architecture).

#### 3.2.3 Solution to Part (c) — New SFF after diagnostic improvement

**Step 7 — Update the data.** λ_DU changes from 35 fit to 10 fit. Assume λ_S and λ_DD remain unchanged (no redesign, only the diagnostic algorithm is improved):

$$\lambda_{S}' = 25 \text{ fit}, \quad \lambda_{DD}' = 90 \text{ fit}, \quad \lambda_{DU}' = 10 \text{ fit}$$

**Step 8 — Recompute λ_total and SFF.**

$$\lambda_{total}' = 25 + 90 + 10 = 125 \text{ fit}$$

$$\text{SFF}' = \frac{25 + 90}{125} = \frac{115}{125} = 0.920 = 92.0\%$$

**Step 9 — Re-evaluate SIL feasibility.** SFF' = 92% ≥ 90% ⇒ the element *now* meets the Type B, HFT = 0, **SIL 3** requirement (per IEC 61508-2 Table 3).

> **Conclusion:** Improving the diagnostic coverage migrated the same transmitter from SIL 2 (1oo1) to SIL 3 (1oo1) — without any hardware change, only firmware/diagnostic algorithm improvement. This is one of the most common KTU "interpret the result" sub-parts.

### 3.3 Subsystem SFF for a Series Chain — Worked Derivation

**Problem:** Two sensors feed a common logic solver in a 1oo1D (single-channel with diagnostics) architecture. Compute the *combined* SFF of the sensor pair, treating them as series elements (both must work for the safety function to operate).

| Element | λ_S (fit) | λ_DD (fit) | λ_DU (fit) |
|:-------:|:---------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| Sensor A | 20 | 60 | 20 |
| Sensor B | 30 | 40 | 10 |

**Step 1 — Sum each column for the series combination.**

$$\lambda_{S,sub} = 20 + 30 = 50 \text{ fit}$$

$$\lambda_{DD,sub} = 60 + 40 = 100 \text{ fit}$$

$$\lambda_{DU,sub} = 20 + 10 = 30 \text{ fit}$$

**Step 2 — Total subsystem failure rate.**

$$\lambda_{total,sub} = 50 + 100 + 30 = 180 \text{ fit}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SFF formula at subsystem level.**

$$\text{SFF}_{sub} = \frac{50 + 100}{180} = \frac{150}{180} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Architectural Pitfall (Examiner Favourite):** Students often incorrectly compute an *average* SFF: (SFF_A + SFF_B)/2. The IEC 61508 prescribed method is **rate-weighted aggregation** as shown above. Memorise: *sum the rates first, then compute SFF — never compute individual SFFs and average them.*

### 3.4 Numerical Table — SIL Limits for Type B Elements (IEC 61508-2 Table 3)

> [!NOTE]  
> This table is a **high-yield KTU reference**. You are expected to reproduce it in any 7-mark "explain Route 1H" sub-question.

| SIL | HFT = 0 | HFT = 1 | HFT = 2 |
|:---:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|
| 1   | SFF < 60% acceptable | Any SFF | Any SFF |
| 2   | 60% ≤ SFF < 90%    | SFF < 60% acceptable | Any SFF |
| 3   | 90% ≤ SFF           | 60% ≤ SFF < 90%    | SFF < 60% acceptable |
| 4   | Not permitted (1oo1) | 90% ≤ SFF          | 60% ≤ SFF < 90% |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common student error:** Stating that SIL 4 is permitted with HFT = 0 and high SFF. This is **FALSE** for both Type A and Type B elements — SIL 4 requires *at minimum* HFT = 1 in all IEC 61508-2 configurations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Failure Rate lambda"]:::root --> B{"Classify Effect on EUC"}

    B -- "Causes hazard" --> D{"Is the Diagnostic Test Able to Detect It"}
    D -- "Yes diagnostic catches" --> E["Detected Dangerous lambda_DD"]
    D -- "No diagnostic fails" --> F["Undetected Dangerous lambda_DU"]
    C --> G["Numerator lambda_S plus lambda_DD"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Compute SFF"]
    F --> I["Denominator lambda_S plus lambda_DD plus lambda_DU"]
    G --> H
    I --> H
    H --> J["Compare with IEC 61508 Tables 2 and 3"]
    J --> K["Determine Permissible SIL and HFT"]

    classDef root fill:#1f2a44,color:#ffffff,stroke:#facc15,stroke-width:2px
    classDef root2 fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a7f3d0

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Route 1H Decision Logic for KTU Examination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Step 1 Identify Element Type"] --> T{"Type A or Type B"}
    T -- "Type A" --> TA["Apply Table 2 IEC 61508 2"]
    T -- "Type B" --> TB["Apply Table 3 IEC 61508 2"]
    TA --> HFT1{"HFT value"}
    TB --> HFT1
    HFT1 --> SFF1{"SFF bracket of element"}
    SFF1 -- "SFF within bracket" --> SILok["Element Permitted at Target SIL"]
    SFF1 -- "SFF below bracket" --> SILno["Element Rejected Increase HFT or Improve DC"]
    SILok --> D["Step 2 Validate against target SIL demand"]
    SILno --> R["Step 3 Return to Selection or Add Redundancy"]
    D --> OK["Final Architectural Sign off per IEC 61508"]
    R --> OK
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — SFF Diagnostic-Coverage Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Lambda_D total dangerous failures"] --> Q["Split by Diagnostics"]
    Q --> R1["Lambda_DD Detected"]
    Q --> R2["Lambda_DU Undetected"]
    R1 --> S1["DC equals Lambda_DD over Lambda_DD plus Lambda_DU"]
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2["SFF equals 1 minus Lambda_DU over Lambda_total"]
    S1 --> T["Higher DC means Lower Lambda_DU means Higher SFF"]
    S2 --> T
    T --> U["Engineering Action Replace diagnostic or Add redundancy"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Reading the Mermaid diagrams for KTU:** Examiners award marks for *labelled* flow diagrams in 7-mark "explain" questions. The three diagrams above are sufficient to draw (by hand) under examination conditions, and cover the **three canonical SFF question archetypes**: classification, Route 1H decision, and diagnostic improvement.
