In the context of **Functional Safety** (governed by **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511** standards), **Analysis and Fault Detection – Basic Architecture** refers to the systematic engineering methodology of structuring safety-instrumented systems (SIS) using standardized redundancy topologies to detect, control, and mitigate dangerous failures. The **Basic Architecture** defines the voting logic (M out of N, or MooN) that determines how multiple channels of a safety function are interconnected to achieve a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IEC 61508 framework classifies basic architectures based on the relationship between the number of channels (N) and the minimum number required for safe operation (M). The most common configurations are **1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, and 2oo4**.

A **fault** is defined as the *abnormal condition* that may cause a reduction in, or loss of, the capability of a functional unit to perform a required function. **Fault detection** is the logical process of identifying the presence of a fault within a system, typically through **self-diagnostic mechanisms** embedded in the hardware/software.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a critical airplane landing system. Would you trust a **single sensor** telling the pilot the altitude? Probably not. So engineers use **two or three independent sensors** and combine their readings using a **voting system**.

- If the system is **1oo2 (1 out of 2)**: A single "safe" signal is enough to trigger the safety action. This is *fail-safe* but causes spurious trips.
- If the system is **2oo2 (2 out of 2)**: *Both* sensors must agree. This prevents false trips but may miss single faults.
- If the system is **2oo3 (2 out of 3)**: Two out of three sensors must agree. This is the **gold standard** used in nuclear reactors and aircraft — it tolerates one faulty sensor while maintaining safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world mapping:** In a car's **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**, multiple wheel-speed sensors use a 2oo3 or 1oo2D logic to prevent skidding — a single failed sensor must not disable braking (which is why ABS uses redundant voting).

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Mandated)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **MooN** | M out of N voting architecture (M channels must agree out of total N) |
| **λ_DU** | Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour) |
| **λ_DD** | Dangerous Detected failure rate (per hour) |
| **λ_S** | Safe failure rate (per hour) |
| **PFD** | Average Probability of Failure on Demand (for low-demand mode) |
| **PFH** | Average Probability of Failure per Hour (for high-demand/continuous mode) |
| **DC** | Diagnostic Coverage — fraction of failures detected by automatic diagnostics |
| **SFF** | Safe Failure Fraction |
| **HFT** | Hardware Fault Tolerance |
| **β-factor** | Fraction of failures common between redundant channels (CCF) |
| **T_proof** | Proof Test Interval (interval between manual safety checks) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Basic Architecture** is the *first design step* in any Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). The choice of architecture directly determines the achievable SIL, the spurious trip rate, and the cost of the system.

## 1.4 Why "Basic Architecture" Matters in Functional Safety

The architecture decides **how faults propagate**, **how diagnostics are applied**, and **what SIL is achievable** without additional measures. The IEC 61508 standard explicitly restricts the achievable SIL based on the type of subsystem (A or B) and its HFT.

- **Type A subsystems** (simple, well-defined, no complex components): Can achieve higher SIL with lower HFT.
- **Type B subsystems** (complex, failure modes not fully known): Require higher HFT for the same SIL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MooN Voting Logic — Conceptual Breakdown

The MooN architecture is governed by the voting equation:

$$
\text{Safety Output} = \text{TRUE} \iff \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Channel}_i \geq M
$$

**Operational logic step-by-step:**

1. **Channelization:** The safety function is replicated across N independent hardware/software channels.
2. **Voting:** A voter (hardware or software) tallies the outputs of all N channels.
3. **Decision:** The system transitions to (or remains in) the safe state if at least M channels demand it.
4. **Fault masking vs. fault tolerance:** Architectures like 1oo2D ("D" = diagnostic) actively *detect and shut down* a faulty channel, converting an undetected failure into a safe-detected failure.

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 **1oo1 (One out of One)**
- Single channel, no redundancy.
- Simplest and cheapest. HFT = 0.
- Failure of the single channel causes dangerous failure → **Low SIL achievable.**

### 2.2.2 **1oo2 (One out of Two)**
- Two parallel channels; one trip signal is enough.
- HFT = 1 (tolerates one dangerous failure).
- **High spurious trip rate** (any one channel's safe failure triggers shutdown).

### 2.2.3 **2oo2 (Two out of Two)**
- Two channels; *both* must agree.
- HFT = 0 (a single dangerous failure is *masked*).
- **Low spurious trip rate**, but **poor fault detection** without diagnostics.

### 2.2.4 **2oo3 (Two out of Three)**
- Three channels; two must agree.
- HFT = 1, with both fault tolerance AND fault detection capabilities.
- Industry standard for high-integrity applications.

### 2.2.5 **1oo2D (One out of Two with Diagnostics)**
- 1oo2 logic with continuous diagnostics that isolate a faulty channel.
- After isolation, the system degrades to 1oo1 with reduced SIL but remains operational.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, the vertical bar `|` inside mathematical expressions has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Architecture | PFD_avg (Low Demand) | PFH (High Demand) | HFT | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **1oo1** | $\lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$ | 0 | Simple, non-critical |
| 2 | **1oo2** | $\frac{(\lambda_{DU})^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3}$ (no CCF) | $2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}$ | 1 | High SIL, fire & gas |
| 3 | **2oo2** | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}$ | 0 | Burner management (BMS) |
| 4 | **2oo3** | $6(1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{3}$ (approx) | $6(1-\beta_D)^2 \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}$ | 1 | Nuclear, aerospace |
| 5 | **1oo2D** | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}$ (degraded) | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}$ | 1 (initially) | ESD systems |

> [!NOTE]
> $t_{CE}$ = Channel Equivalent Mean Downtime (typically $T_{proof}/2$).

### 2.3.1 Auxiliary Formulas (Diagnostic Coverage & SFF)

$$
DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}
$$

## 2.4 SIL Classification Boundaries (IEC 61508)

| SIL | PFD_avg Range (Low Demand) | PFH Range (High Demand, per hour) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6} \leq PFH < 10^{-5}$ | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7} \leq PFH < 10^{-6}$ | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$ | 1000 to 10000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9} \leq PFH < 10^{-8}$ | 10000 to 100000 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of basic architecture is a **trade-off engineering decision** driven by:

- **Cost of the safety function** (more channels = more money).
- **Cost of spurious trips** (lost production, plant shutdown).
- **Required SIL** (mandated by risk assessment — HAZOP, LOPA).
- **Failure mode of the actuator** (de-energize-to-trip vs. energize-to-trip).
- **Common Cause Failure (CCF) vulnerability** — mitigated by the β-factor model.

In process industries (oil & gas, chemical), 2oo3 is preferred for **high-consequence low-demand** scenarios (e.g., overpressure protection), while 1oo2D is preferred for **high-demand continuous** scenarios (e.g., turbine overspeed protection).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFD for 1oo1 Architecture

We start with a single channel subject to a constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$. Let the proof test interval be $T_{proof}$. The unavailability (probability that the system is in a dangerous failed state) as a function of time $t$ within an interval is:

$$
U(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\text{)}
$$

**Step 1: Express the average unavailability over one proof test interval.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_0^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_0^{T_{proof}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the bounds.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{(1oo1)} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}}
$$

This is the **canonical first-order approximation** used in IEC 61508 for a 1oo1 subsystem.

## 3.2 Derivation of PFD for 1oo2 Architecture (with CCF)

For 1oo2, *both* channels must fail dangerously (independent failures) for system failure, OR a common cause failure must occur.

**Step 1: Decompose the failure modes.**

Let $\beta$ be the fraction of failures that are common to both channels. Then:

$$
\lambda_{DU}^{(common)} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \quad \text{(per channel)}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU}^{(independent)} = (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \quad \text{(per channel)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the independent contribution.**

The independent part requires *both* channels to fail. For two independent channels, the joint probability is the product of their individual unavailabilities (Markov approximation):

$$
PFD_{indep} = \left[ (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2} \right]^2 = (1-\beta)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the channel mean downtime factor.**

For 1oo2, due to repair/maintenance, the equivalent mean downtime for the second channel is $T_{proof}/3$ (as the failing channel is repaired while the other is still operational). Thus:

$$
PFD_{indep} = (1-\beta)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{3}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the common cause contribution.**

A single common cause failure disables the entire 1oo2 system. It behaves like a 1oo1 channel with failure rate $\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU}$:

$$
PFD_{common} = \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the contributions.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{(1oo2)} = (1-\beta)^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The CCF term (β component) typically *dominates* the PFD. Reducing β (via diversity, separation, common-cause proof testing) is more effective than reducing $\lambda_{DU}$ itself.

## 3.3 Derivation of PFD for 2oo2 Architecture

For 2oo2, the system fails dangerously if **any one channel** fails in a dangerous undetected manner (since both must agree, a single undetected dangerous failure takes the system out of service safely, but the redundant check is lost — dangerous state can occur if a second fault coincides with repair).

**Step 1: System is equivalent to a 1oo1 with effective failure rate.**

The dangerous undetected failure rate for a 2oo2 system (with CCF considered) is approximately:

$$
\lambda_{DU}^{(2oo2)} \approx 2(1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}
$$

(factor 2 because either of the two channels can fail independently; multiplied by $(1-\beta)$ to exclude CCF).

**Step 2: Apply the 1oo1 formula with mean downtime $T_{proof}/2$.**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{(2oo2)} = 2(1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Add the common cause contribution (β-fraction).**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{(2oo2)} = 2(1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of PFD for 2oo3 Architecture (Simplified Approximation)

For 2oo3, the system fails if **two or more channels** fail dangerously. With independent failures, the dominant term comes from any pair of channels failing.

**Step 1: Number of ways to choose 2 failing channels out of 3.**

$$
\binom{3}{2} = 3 \text{ pairs}
$$

**Step 2: PFD for any single pair (treating pair as 1oo2).**

$$
PFD_{pair} = (1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3}
$$

**Step 3: Sum over all three pairs.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{(2oo3)} \approx 3(1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3} = (1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full IEC 61508-6 formulation includes the diagnostic-driven terms, but this leading-order form captures the essential quadratic behavior in $\lambda_{DU}$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 1oo2 safety instrumented system has the following parameters:

$$
\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}, \quad T_{proof} = 8760 \text{ hours (1 year)}, \quad \beta = 0.05
$$

Compute the PFD_avg and identify the SIL.

**Step 1: Compute independent failure contribution.**

$$
PFD_{indep} = (1-0.05)^2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot \frac{8760^2}{3}
$$

$$
= (0.9025) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-14}) \cdot \frac{76,737,600}{3}
$$

$$
= 0.9025 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 2.5579 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= 0.9025 \cdot 6.3948 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\approx 5.77 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2: Compute common cause contribution.**

$$
PFD_{common} = 0.05 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \frac{8760}{2} = 0.05 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 4380
$$

$$
= 0.05 \cdot 2.19 \times 10^{-3} = 1.095 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the contributions.**

$$
PFD_{total} = 5.77 \times 10^{-6} + 1.095 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.153 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4: Identify SIL from the table.**

Since $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$, this is **SIL 3**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Trade-off Summary

| Architecture | HFT | Diagnostic Demand | SIL Potential | Spurious Trip | Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1oo1 | 0 | Not required | SIL 1-2 (Type A) | Very Low | Lowest |
| 1oo2 | 1 | Optional | SIL 2-3 (Type A) | High | Medium-High |
| 2oo2 | 0 | **Mandatory** | SIL 1-2 | Very Low | Medium |
| 2oo3 | 1 | **Mandatory** | SIL 3-4 | Medium | High |
| 1oo2D | 1 → 0 (degraded) | **Mandatory** | SIL 2-3 | High (initially) | Medium-High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic MooN Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Process Variable e.g. Pressure] --> node2[Channel 1 Sensor]
    node1 --> node3[Channel 2 Sensor]
    node1 --> node4[Channel 3 Sensor]
    node2 --> node5[Logic Solver Voter]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6{M out of N Comparison}
    node6 --> node7[YES Trip to Safe State]
    node6 --> node8[NO Continue Normal Operation]
    node5 --> node9[Self-Diagnostic Engine]
    node9 --> node10[Alarm / Fault Log]
```

## 4.2 1oo1 Architecture (Single Channel — No Redundancy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1[Input Sensor] --> nodeA2[Logic Solver]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Final Element Actuator]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Safe State Shutdown]
    nodeA2 -.-> nodeA5[Self-Test]
    nodeA5 -.-> nodeA6[Fault Detection]
```

## 4.3 1oo2 Architecture (Parallel Redundancy — Fail-Safe Bias)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeB1[Input] --> nodeB2[Channel A]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB3[Channel B]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB4[OR Voter]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[Final Element]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB6[Safe Trip]
    nodeB7[Diagnostic Unit] --> nodeB2
    nodeB7 --> nodeB3
    nodeB7 --> nodeB8[Fault Report]
```

## 4.4 2oo2 Architecture (Coincidence Logic — Low False Trip)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeC1[Input] --> nodeC2[Channel X]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3[Channel Y]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC4[AND Voter]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5[Final Element]
    nodeC6[Diagnostic Voter Comparator] --> nodeC2
    nodeC6 --> nodeC3
    nodeC6 --> nodeC7[Disagreement Alarm]
```

## 4.5 2oo3 Architecture (Triple Modular Redundancy — TMR)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeD1[Input] --> nodeD2[Channel 1]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD3[Channel 2]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD4[Channel 3]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD5[Majority Voter 2oo3]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD5
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5
    nodeD5 --> nodeD6[Final Element]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD7[Disagreement Resolution Logic]
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8[Auto-Isolate Faulty Channel]
    nodeD5 --> nodeD9[Healthy Channel Status]
```

## 4.6 Fault Detection Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeE1[System Operation] --> nodeE2[Fault Occurs]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE3{Detected by Diagnostics}
    nodeE3 --> nodeE4[YES: Safe-Detected Failure]
    nodeE4 --> nodeE5[Alarm to Operator]
    nodeE5 --> nodeE6[Repair within Mean Time to Repair]
    nodeE3 --> nodeE7[NO: Undetected Failure]
    nodeE7 --> nodeE8[Proof Test Window]
    nodeE8 --> nodeE9[Manual Detection and Repair]
    nodeE6 --> nodeE10[System Restored]
    nodeE9 --> nodeE10
    nodeE10 --> nodeE1
```

## 4.7 Diagnostic Coverage Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeF1[Total Failures lambda_total] --> nodeF2[Safe Failures lambda_S]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF3[Dangerous Detected lambda_DD]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF4[Dangerous Undetected lambda_DU]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF5[DC: lambda_DD divided by lambda_DD + lambda_DU]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5
    nodeF2 --> nodeF6[SFF: lambda_S + lambda_DD divided by lambda_total]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF6
    nodeF4 --> nodeF6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a functional unit (sensor, logic solver, actuator, voter, or diagnostic engine). The arrows represent signal flow. Dotted arrows represent diagnostic/feedback signals (not part of the main safety path). The **voter** block is the heart of the MooN architecture — it implements the Boolean logic that defines the architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of MooN Architecture

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the MooN architecture as used in functional safety. Explain the meaning of "1oo2" and "2oo3" with one example of an application for each.*

**Model Answer:**

The **MooN architecture** is a redundancy configuration in safety-instrumented systems where M out of N parallel channels must agree (vote) to trigger the safe state, while N represents the total number of channels.

- **1oo2 (One out of Two):** Any single channel can trigger the safety action. HFT = 1. *Application:* Fire and gas detection systems in oil refineries, where a single sensor detection is sufficient to initiate fire suppression.

- **2oo3 (Two out of Three):** At least two of three channels must agree. HFT = 1 with fault detection. *Application:* Aircraft flight control computers and nuclear reactor trip systems, where tolerance to a single faulty sensor is essential.

**(End — 3 marks awarded for clear definition + one example each)**

---

### Question 2: Safe Failure Fraction

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**

*Define Safe Failure Fraction (SFF). A sensor has $\lambda_S = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_{DD} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$/h, and $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Compute the SFF.*

**Model Answer:**

**SFF** is the ratio of safe failures plus dangerous detected failures to the total failure rate:

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1: Sum the numerator.**

$$
\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} = 2 \times 10^{-6} + 3 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 2: Sum the denominator.**

$$
\lambda_{total} = 2 \times 10^{-6} + 3 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the SFF.**

$$
SFF = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100\% = 83.33\%
$$

**(End — 3 marks awarded for definition + correct computation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option for the Candidate

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of suitable block diagrams, explain the working of 1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3 architectures. Compare their hardware fault tolerance (HFT) and typical SIL capability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2oo3 safety system is designed with $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$/h per channel, $\beta_D = 0.02$, and $T_{proof} = 4380$ hours (6 months). Calculate the PFD_avg and identify the achievable SIL. State any assumptions made.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**1oo2 Architecture:**
Two channels operate in parallel. A single trip signal from either channel activates the safe state. The "OR" logic voter combines channel outputs. *HFT = 1* (tolerates one dangerous failure). The system is fail-safe biased — a single dangerous failure causes shutdown, so dangerous faults are *converted* to safe-detected faults. Disadvantage: very high spurious trip rate.

**2oo2 Architecture:**
Two channels operate in parallel with "AND" voting. Both must agree for a safety action. *HFT = 0* — a single dangerous undetected failure is *masked*. Spurious trip rate is very low, but dangerous fault detection requires explicit diagnostics. Used in burner management systems.

**2oo3 Architecture:**
Three channels with majority voting ("2 out of 3"). *HFT = 1* — tolerates any single channel failure while continuing safe operation. Disagreement between channels triggers diagnostics and isolation of the faulty channel. Highest integrity, used in nuclear, aerospace, and high-consequence process applications.

| Architecture | Voting Logic | HFT | Typical SIL | Spurious Trip |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1oo2 | OR | 1 | SIL 2-3 | High |
| 2oo2 | AND | 0 | SIL 1-2 | Low |
| 2oo3 | Majority | 1 | SIL 3-4 | Medium |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1: Identify the PFD formula for 2oo3.**

Using the leading-order approximation from Section 3.4:

$$
PFD_{avg}^{(2oo3)} \approx (1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
(1 - 0.02)^2 = (0.98)^2 = 0.9604
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU}^2 = (2 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
T_{proof}^2 = (4380)^2 = 1.9184 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply the three terms.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = 0.9604 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 1.9184 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= 0.9604 \cdot 7.6736 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\approx 7.37 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4: Identify SIL.**

Since $10^{-7} \leq PFD < 10^{-6}$, the system achieves **SIL 3** (high end) approaching **SIL 4**.

**Assumptions made:**

- All three channels are identical and independent (except for the $\beta_D$ CCF factor).
- Proof tests are perfect (100% coverage of dangerous undetected failures).
- Repair time is negligible compared to $T_{proof}$.
- Diagnostic coverage is 100% (no unmodelled dangerous failures).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Diagnostic Coverage (DC) in safety instrumented systems. A subsystem has the following failure rates: $\lambda_S = 4 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_{DD} = 6 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Compute the DC and SFF. Comment on the suitability of the subsystem for a SIL 2 application.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for PFD_avg of a 1oo1 architecture from first principles. Show all steps clearly. How does the addition of a second channel (1oo2) reduce the PFD, and what is the role of the β-factor in this reduction?

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** It is the fraction of dangerous failures that are detected by the automatic online diagnostics of the subsystem.

$$
DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1: Compute DC.**

$$
DC = \frac{6 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100\% = \frac{6}{7} \times 100\% \approx 85.71\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute SFF.**

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{4 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-6} + 6 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{11 \times 10^{-6}} \times 100\% \approx 90.91\%
$$

**Step 3: Comment on SIL 2 suitability.**

According to IEC 61508, a Type A subsystem requires **SFF ≥ 60% to 90%** depending on HFT for SIL 1-2. Our SFF of **90.91%** is at the high end and DC of **85.71%** is good. The subsystem is **suitable for SIL 2** with appropriate HFT (HFT = 0 acceptable for Type A with SFF ≥ 60%).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1: State the assumption.**

Assume a single channel with constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$, perfect proof tests that restore the channel to "as new" condition, and negligible repair time.

**Step 2: Write the instantaneous unavailability.**

The probability that the channel is in a dangerous failed state at time $t$ after a proof test:

$$
U(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the small-failure-probability approximation.**

Since $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ for typical industrial values:

$$
U(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t
$$

**Step 4: Compute the average unavailability over one proof test interval.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_0^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^2}{2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Discuss the effect of 1oo2 and the β-factor.**

Going from 1oo1 to 1oo2 changes the failure criterion from "single channel fails" to "both channels fail AND/OR a common cause failure occurs." This produces two changes:

- The independent term is **quadratic** in $\lambda_{DU}$ (product of two small probabilities), giving an enormous reduction in PFD (typically 100× to 10000×).
- However, the **β-factor** represents the fraction of failures common to both channels (e.g., design flaw, environmental stress, calibration error). This contributes a *linear* term in $\lambda_{DU}$ and *dominates* the PFD budget if not controlled. To benefit fully from 1oo2, β must be kept small (typically β < 0.10) through diversity, physical separation, and independent proof testing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the CCF term:** Many students write only the independent-failure part of the 1oo2 PFD equation. The **β-factor term is mandatory** and carries 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing HFT with redundancy level:** HFT = 1 means the system tolerates ONE dangerous failure; do not equate it with "number of redundant channels" without verification.
> 3. **Mismatching SIL bounds:** Always re-check the SIL classification table. A common error is to misplace the bounds (e.g., $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ is SIL 3, NOT SIL 2).
> 4. **Skipping units in computation:** KTU examiners deduct marks for omitting "per hour" in $\lambda$ values or "hours" in $T_{proof}$.
> 5. **Voter logic error:** Drawing an AND voter for 1oo2 (should be OR) or an OR voter for 2oo2 (should be AND). The voter symbol is the *most-checked* element in diagram questions.
> 6. **Not specifying assumptions:** In derivations, failing to state "perfect proof test," "negligible repair time," or "100% diagnostic coverage" results in a 1-mark deduction per missing assumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Analysis and Fault Detection — Basic Architecture**

- **MooN definition:** M out of N channels must demand the safe state; N is total channels, M is the minimum required.
- **Five canonical architectures:** 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D. Know the voter logic (OR vs. AND vs. majority) for each.
- **HFT values:** 1oo1 → 0; 1oo2 → 1; 2oo2 → 0 (masking!); 2oo3 → 1; 1oo2D → 1 initially, degrades to 0.
- **PFD formulas (must memorize):**
  - 1oo1: $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}/2$
  - 1oo2: $(1-\beta)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2/3 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}/2$
  - 2oo2: $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}/2 + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$
  - 2oo3: $\approx (1-\beta_D)^2 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2$
- **DC formula:** $\lambda_{DD} / (\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$
- **SFF formula:** $(\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}) / (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$
- **SIL-PFD mapping:** SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **CCF/β-factor:** Always present in redundant systems; must be subtracted from the redundancy benefit. Typical β ∈ [0.01, 0.10] for well-designed systems.
- **Diagnostics are mandatory** for 2oo2 and 2oo3 architectures to convert masked dangerous failures into detected ones.
- **Spurious trip rate** decreases in the order: 1oo2 > 2oo3 > 1oo1 > 2oo2 (1oo2 has the highest false-trip probability).
- **Type A vs. Type B subsystems:** Type A (simple) needs less HFT for a given SIL; Type B (complex) needs more.
- **Architecture selection drivers:** Required SIL, cost of spurious trips, cost of channels, common-cause vulnerability, and maintenance strategy.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Analysis and Fault Detection — Basic Architecture*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Choice Safety ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Choice Safety** refers to the systematic, risk-based decision-making methodology used by safety engineers to **select the most appropriate Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**, the corresponding **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**, and the **architectural configuration** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, etc.) for a process or machine hazard. It is the engineering bridge between *hazard identification* and *safety system realization*, governed by **IEC 61508**, **IEC 61511**, and **ISO 13849**. In other words, **Choice Safety** is *the answer to the question*: *"Given this hazard, what safety function should I implement, how reliable must it be, and what hardware structure achieves that reliability cost-effectively?"* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — Choosing a Home Lock:** > Imagine you are choosing a lock for your house. The choice is not arbitrary; it depends on **risk**: > 1. **What are you protecting?** (Consequence) — Jewels vs. newspapers. > 2. **How often is the house unoccupied?** (Exposure) — Always home vs. travelling daily. > 3. **Can an intruder escape unnoticed?** (Avoidance) — Guarded colony vs. isolated house. > 4. **How skilled is the typical intruder?** (Probability). > > Based on the answers, you *choose* a simple latch, a 3-tumbler lock, or a 7-tumbler biometric vault. **Choice Safety** is exactly this — but applied to industrial Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) - **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Discrete level (1, 2, 3, 4) corresponding to a range of **Probability of dangerous Failure on Demand (PFD)** or **Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH)**. - **Tolerable Risk (T):** The maximum acceptable frequency of a hazardous event, expressed as **events per year**. - **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** The ratio by which the safety function must reduce the *unmitigated* event frequency. - **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** The number of faults that can be tolerated without loss of the safety function (values: **0, 1, 2**). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Choice Safety Every safety choice rests on three structured decisions: **Pillar 1 — Choice of Safety Function (What must the system do?)** - Identify the *cause* of the hazard (e.g., over-pressure, over-temperature). - Define the *safe state* (e.g., shut valve, cut power). - Specify the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)**: *sensor → logic solver → final element*. **Pillar 2 — Choice of Safety Integrity Level (How reliable?)** - Determine the **tolerable hazard rate** for the process. - Determine the **demand rate** on the SIF. - Compute **Required Risk Reduction Factor (RRR)**. **Pillar 3 — Choice of Architecture (How to achieve reliability?)** - Select the redundancy structure (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, 2oo4). - Determine **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** and **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)**. - Account for **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** and **Proof Test Interval (T_proof)**. ### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters > [!NOTE] > - *Why a discrete SIL?* Because continuous reliability metrics are impractical to verify for certification; discrete bands allow **Type Approval** and **proven-in-use** arguments. > - *Why redundancy?* Because **single-channel (1oo1)** systems fail dangerously without warning; redundancy + diagnostics convert dangerous undetected failures into safe detected failures. > - *Why proof testing?* Because all components degrade with time; **T_proof** restores the calculated PFD budget. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula (Low-Demand Mode) | Formula (High-Demand Mode) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$ | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFH}}$ | dimensionless / per hour | | Required PFD | $\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{proc}}}{\text{Tolerable Rate}}$ | $\text{PFH}_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{proc}}}{\text{T}}$ | per demand / per hour | | Average PFD (1oo1) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | $\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_D$ | per demand | | Average PFD (1oo2) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2}{6}$ | $\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx 2 \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}$ | per demand | | Safe Failure Fraction | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ | — | fraction (0–1) | | SIL ↔ PFD Range (Low-Demand) | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ ; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ ; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ ; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | — | per demand | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering utility:** These equations are the *lingua franca* of every SIL calculation worksheet (exSILentia, RiskGraphizer, SAPHIRE) used by Functional Safety Engineers (FSE) in refineries, pharmaceutical plants, and autonomous-vehicle ECUs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Required PFD from Process Hazard Frequency **Step 1 — State the tolerable hazard rate.** The tolerable rate **T** (events/year) is set by the company's *risk policy* or regulatory limits (e.g., for worker fatality: typically **T = 10⁻⁵ per year**). **Step 2 — Identify the unprotected event frequency.** Let **f_proc** be the frequency (per year) of the initiating event *if no safety system were installed*. Example: a thermal runaway occurs roughly **once per 10 years**, so: $$f_{\text{proc}} = 0.1 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 3 — Define the risk-reduction equation.** For low-demand mode, the dangerous event frequency *with* the SIF installed must not exceed **T**: $$f_{\text{proc}} \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \leq T$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the required average PFD.** $$\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = \frac{T}{f_{\text{proc}}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical substitution** (using T = 10⁻⁵/yr, f_proc = 0.1/yr): $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{req}} &= \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-1}} \\ &= 10^{-4} \text{ per demand} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Map to SIL band.** From the formula sheet, PFD between **10⁻⁴ and 10⁻³** corresponds to **SIL 3** in low-demand mode. > Hence the *choice*: **SIL 3 SIF** is mandatory. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: PFD of a 1oo2 Architecture from First Principles A 1oo2 architecture fails dangerously only when **both channels fail dangerously**. Assuming constant failure rate λ_D and random failures within a proof-test interval T_proof: **Step 1 — Probability of a single channel failing within t:** $$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t \quad (\text{for } \lambda_D t \ll 1)$$ **Step 2 — Joint failure probability over the interval:** $$P_{\text{both fail}} \approx (\lambda_D t)^2$$ **Step 3 — Average over the interval [0, T_proof]:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} (\lambda_D \tau)^2 \, d\tau \\ &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \cdot \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^3}{3} \\ &= \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2}{3} \end{aligned}$$ The more precise form commonly cited in IEC 61508 is **λ_D²·T_proof²/3** (sometimes **/6** depending on the diagnostic model). The order of magnitude is decisive for SIL choice. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A pressure transmitter has λ_D = 5 × 10⁻⁶ per hour. Proof-test interval T_proof = 8760 hours (1 year). Compute the PFD_avg for a single channel and a 1oo2 architecture. Choose the architecture. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} &= \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} = \frac{5\times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} \\ &= 2.19 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$$ This falls in the **SIL 1** band (10⁻² to 10⁻¹). $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} &\approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2}{3} = \frac{(5\times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} \\ &= \frac{25\times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.677\times 10^{7}}{3} \\ &= 6.4 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ This falls in the **SIL 3** band (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³). > **Choice Safety verdict:** If the process hazard requires SIL 2 or higher, the **1oo2 architecture is mandatory** for this transmitter. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Choice Safety Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Identified
from HAZOP or FMEA] --> B[Estimate Unmitigated
Event Frequency f_proc] B --> C[Consult Risk Policy
for Tolerable Rate T] C --> D[Compute Required PFD_req
equals T divided by f_proc] D --> E{Map PFD_req to
SIL Band 1 to 4} E --> F[Determine Safe State
Define SIF Function] F --> G[Compute Achievable PFD
for Candidate Architectures] G --> H{Achievable PFD
less than or equal to PFD_req?} H -- No --> I[Add Redundancy
or Shorten Proof Interval] I --> G H -- Yes --> J[Validate SFF and HFT
per IEC 61508 Tables] J --> K[Document SIL Choice
in Safety Case] ``` ### 4.2 Architecture-Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR sens1[Sensor Channel A] --> logic1[Logic Solver] sens2[Sensor Channel B] --> logic1 logic1 --> act1[Final Element A] logic1 --> act2[Final Element B] logic1 --> diag[Diagnostics and Proof Test Module] diag -.-> maint[Maintenance Scheduler] maint -.-> sens1 maint -.-> sens2 ``` ### 4.3 SIL Selection Matrix (Reference Table) ```mermaid graph TD C1[Consequence: Minor] --> SIL1 C2[Consequence: Major] --> SIL2 C3[Consequence: Catastrophic] --> SIL3 F1[Exposure: Rare] --> SIL1 F2[Exposure: Frequent] --> SIL2 F3[Exposure: Continuous] --> SIL3 ``` > [!NOTE] > The block-level flows above represent the **decision logic** of a typical Risk Graph / Safety Layer Matrix tool. In KTU exam answers, draw these as labelled rectangular boxes connected with directional arrows and annotate each box with the **IEC 61508 clause reference**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]** **Q1. Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). State the PFD ranges corresponding to SIL 1, 2, 3, and 4 for a low-demand Safety Instrumented Function.** **Model Answer:** Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) that specifies the required *reliability* of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) in terms of its average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD_avg) or Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH). For low-demand mode (per IEC 61508-1): $$\begin{aligned} \text{SIL 1:}&\quad 10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-1} \\ \text{SIL 2:}&\quad 10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-2} \\ \text{SIL 3:}&\quad 10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-3} \\ \text{SIL 4:}&\quad 10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} < 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ Higher SIL → lower allowable probability of dangerous failure → stricter design, redundancy, and proof-test discipline. --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q2b]** **Q2. What is the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)? How is it related to the required PFD?** **Model Answer:** The Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) is the ratio between the *frequency* of the hazardous event *without* any protective layer and the *tolerable frequency* set by the company's risk policy. It quantifies *how much* the SIF must reduce risk. $$\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{tolerable}}}$$ For low-demand mode, since each demand on the SIF results in dangerous consequences only when the SIF itself fails: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = \frac{1}{\text{RRF}}$$ Hence, RRF = 100 → PFD_req = 10⁻² (SIL 1); RRF = 10000 → PFD_req = 10⁻⁴ (SIL 3). --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students routinely **forget to state the SIL band limits** when mapping a calculated PFD — losing 2 marks. > - Many omit the **assumption of constant failure rate** (λ = constant) and the **proof-test interval T_proof** — examiners deduct 2 marks. > - Architecture choice without justification via **PFD computation** is treated as an *unsubstantiated statement* — full 7 marks may not be awarded for the architecture sub-question. > - Always state the **mode of operation** (low-demand vs high-demand) at the very top of your answer. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(KTU Model Paper, Dec 2024)* **(a) [7 Marks]** A catalytic reactor has an over-pressure initiating event occurring on average **once every 5 years** (f_proc = 0.2/year). Company policy sets the tolerable hazardous-event rate at **T = 10⁻⁴ per year**. The proposed SIF uses a single pressure transmitter with λ_D = 3 × 10⁻⁶/hr and a proof-test interval of 1 year (8760 hours). Determine the required SIL and verify whether a 1oo1 architecture is sufficient. Show all calculations. **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to IEC 61508, explain the concept of **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**. Describe how these parameters jointly restrict the choice of architecture for SIL 2 and SIL 3 subsystems (use a Route 1H table). **Model Solution — Part (a):** Step 1 — Compute required PFD: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{req}} = \frac{T}{f_{\text{proc}}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{0.2} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$ Step 2 — Map to SIL band. Since **5 × 10⁻⁴** lies in the range [10⁻⁴, 10⁻³], the required SIL is **SIL 3**. Step 3 — Compute PFD for the 1oo1 candidate: $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} &= \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \\ &= \frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} \\ &= 1.314 \times 10^{-2} \end{aligned}$$ Step 4 — Compare. 1.314 × 10⁻² ≈ SIL 1 band, **far weaker** than the SIL 3 requirement (5 × 10⁻⁴). Therefore a 1oo1 architecture is **insufficient**. Step 5 — Recommend a 1oo2 architecture: $$\begin{aligned} \text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} &\approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2}{3} \\ &= \frac{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} \\ &= 2.3 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ This satisfies **PFD_req = 5 × 10⁻⁴**, so a **1oo2 architecture** is the appropriate Choice Safety recommendation. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** Defined in IEC 61508-2 as *the ability of a subsystem to continue to perform its required safety function in the presence of one or more dangerous faults*. A subsystem with HFT = 1 can tolerate **one** dangerous fault without losing the safety function; HFT = 2 tolerates two. **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** The ratio of the average failure rates of *safe* plus *dangerous-detected* failures to the *total* failure rate: $$\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$ **Route 1H Restriction Table (excerpt):** | SIL | Minimum HFT (Type A, SFF < 60%) | Minimum HFT (Type B, SFF ≥ 60%) | |---|---|---| | SIL 1 | 1 | 0 | | SIL 2 | 2 | 1 | | SIL 3 | (not allowed) | 2 | | SIL 4 | (not allowed) | (special requirements) | > Hence, to **choose** SIL 3 with a complex (Type B) sensor, the designer *must* configure at least a **1oo2 architecture (HFT = 1)** combined with diagnostics, often effectively becoming 2oo2. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Risk Graph method** for SIL determination. Identify the four input parameters and explain how each parameter influences the final SIL choice. Illustrate with a numerical example where the outcome is **SIL 2**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A high-demand Safety Instrumented Function operates continuously. The dangerous failure rate of its final element is λ_D = 1 × 10⁻⁷ per hour. Compute the PFH for (i) a 1oo1, (ii) a 1oo2, and (iii) a 2oo2 architecture. State the SIL each corresponds to in high-demand mode. **Model Solution — Part (a):** The Risk Graph method (IEC 61511-1, Clause 5.2) determines SIL using four ordinal parameters: 1. **C — Consequence:** Severity of harm (C1 = minor injury; C2 = serious injury; C3 = death of one person; C4 = multiple fatalities). 2. **F — Frequency of exposure to the hazard:** F1 = rarely to F4 = continuously. 3. **P — Possibility of avoidance:** P1 = avoidable under certain conditions; P2 = not avoidable. 4. **W — Probability of the unwanted event occurring without the SIF:** W1 = very slight to W3 = high. The Risk Graph is consulted as a **decision tree**: starting from C, the path through F, P, W leads to one of four terminal nodes, each labelled with a SIL (1, 2, 3, or 4). **Numerical Example (SIL 2 outcome):** A chemical dosing pump leak has parameters (C2, F2, P1, W2). Following the Risk Graph: C2 leads to a branch selecting between F2 → enters a sub-branch → W2 → combined with P1 → terminal node labelled **SIL 2**. The SIF must therefore achieve PFD_avg between 10⁻³ and 10⁻². **Model Solution — Part (b):** For high-demand (continuous) mode, the relevant metric is PFH. Using λ_D = 10⁻⁷/hr: (i) **1oo1 architecture:** $$\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$ Per IEC 61508: PFH in [10⁻⁶, 10⁻⁷) → **SIL 2**. (ii) **1oo2 architecture** (either channel can trip): $$\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx 2 \lambda_D^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}$$ Assuming T_proof = 8760 hr: $$\text{PFH}_{\text{1oo2}} = 2 \cdot (10^{-7})^2 \cdot 8760 = 1.75 \times 10^{-10} \text{ per hour}$$ PFH in [10⁻⁸, 10⁻⁷) → **SIL 3**. (iii) **2oo2 architecture** (both channels must trip — no single-channel fail-safe): $$\text{PFH}_{\text{2oo2}} \approx \lambda_D = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$ → **SIL 2** (same PFH as 1oo1, but with *higher availability* and *lower spurious trip rate*). > **Conclusion:** For high-demand SIL 3 applications, the **1oo2 architecture** is the appropriate *choice*. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Choice Safety** = methodology to *choose* SIF, SIL, and architecture. - Always begin by computing **PFD_req = T / f_proc** (low demand) or **PFH_req** (high demand). - Map the computed probability to a **SIL band** using IEC 61508-1 Table 2 or Table 3. - **Architecture formulas** to memorize: - 1oo1 PFD = λ_D · T_proof / 2 - 1oo2 PFD ≈ λ_D² · T_proof² / 3 - PFH ≈ λ_D for 1oo1 and 2oo2; ≈ 2 λ_D² T for 1oo2. - **Route 1H** governs HFT–SFF–SIL architectural restrictions for Type A and Type B subsystems. - Always state the **mode of operation** (low vs high demand) before any calculation. - **Proof-test interval (T_proof)** is a *design variable* — shortening it directly lowers PFD_avg. - **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** converts dangerous-undetected failures into dangerous-detected, reducing λ_DU and enabling higher SIL. - **Risk Graph parameters:** C (consequence), F (exposure), P (avoidance), W (probability of unwanted event). - For SIL 3 and above, **redundancy is generally mandatory** unless extremely high SFF and DC are proven. - Always validate architectural choice against **both Route 1H and Route 2H** per IEC 61508-2. - Document every choice in the **Safety Case** with explicit references to hazard, risk criterion, SIF specification, and verification evidence. - For KTU board answers: **always show units**, **always state assumptions**, and **always conclude with a clear architectural recommendation**. ---

topics.title: Choice Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Choice Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

**Choice Availability** in the domain of Functional Safety refers to the set of design, architectural, and procedural alternatives that a safety engineer can select from when implementing a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** to achieve a target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. According to **IEC 61508 (2010)** and **IEC 61511 (2016)** standards, multiple equally-valid compliance routes exist for reaching the same SIL, granting the designer a structured "freedom of choice" — provided each choice is rigorously justified and documented in the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (must memorize):**
> *"Choice Availability is the catalog of permissible routes, architectures, proof-test strategies, and diagnostic methods sanctioned by IEC 61508 to demonstrate compliance with a given SIL, while satisfying constraints on hardware fault tolerance, safe failure fraction, and systematic capability."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you must reach a city located on a mountain. There are several trails available:

- **Trail A** (steep & direct) — fewer stops, but riskier if you slip.
- **Trail B** (zigzag & long) — many checkpoints, slow but well-supported.
- **Trail C** (rope-bridge shortcut) — fastest, but depends on bridge integrity.

All three trails lead to the same destination (achieving SIL-2). However, the **cost, reliability, and risk** of each path differs. The engineer must choose which trail matches their process constraints, budget, and tolerable hazard rate. This is **Choice Availability** — the standard gives you multiple legitimate trails; *you must defend why you picked yours.*

## 1.3 The Three Core Categories of Choice

| Category | What the Engineer Chooses | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Route Choice** | Route 1H vs 1S vs 2H vs 2S | IEC 61508-2, Tables 2 & 3 |
| **Architectural Choice** | M-out-of-N voting (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3, etc.) | IEC 61508-6, Annex B |
| **Operational Choice** | Proof-test interval (T_I), diagnostic coverage (DC), operating mode | IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.5.16 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **Route 1H ↔ 1S** choice is fundamentally about whether you prove safety by **hardware metrics** (fault tolerance + SFF) or by **systematic capability** (proven-in-use + quality measures). Route 2H/2S are *enhanced* versions with stricter constraints.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Compliance Routes (IEC 61508 Architectural Constraints)

For any sensor, logic solver, or final element, the standard offers:

1. **Route 1H — Route 1 Hardware:**
   - Minimize hardware faults via Type A/B classification.
   - Constrained by **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**.
   - Applicable when systematic capability is not the limiting factor.

2. **Route 1S — Route 1 Systematic:**
   - Relies on **Systematic Capability (SC)** of the device (e.g., SC 1, 2, or 3).
   - Used for complex devices where proven-in-use evidence is available.

3. **Route 2H — Route 2 Enhanced Hardware:**
   - Higher SFF requirements (≥ 60% for SIL-1, ≥ 90% for SIL-2, etc.).
   - Stricter diagnostic coverage expectations.

4. **Route 2S — Route 2 Enhanced Systematic:**
   - Requires SC ≥ 2 and additional **field-proven record** of ≥ 10⁵ hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Examiners love asking: *"When would you choose Route 1S over Route 1H?"* The answer lies in the device's **Type classification**:
> - **Type A devices** (well-defined, simple failure modes) → Route 1H is permitted.
> - **Type B devices** (complex, undefined failure modes) → Route 1S or Route 2 must be used.

## 2.2 Why Choice Availability Matters

The standard does **not** mandate a single path because:

- Different industries have different tolerable risk profiles.
- Cost constraints differ between, say, a pharmaceutical plant and an oil refinery.
- Some devices are inherently simple (Type A); others are complex ASICs (Type B).
- Proof-test accessibility varies (some equipment can be tested monthly; others only during shutdowns every 5 years).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $PFD_{avg}(1oo1) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_I}{2}$ | Avg. Probability of Failure on Demand, 1oo1 architecture | $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (per hr); $T_I$ = proof test interval (hr) |
| $PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I)^2}{3}$ | PFDavg for 1oo2 voting | Both channels identical |
| $PFD_{avg}(2oo2) = 2 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_I}{2} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$ | PFDavg for 2oo2 voting | $\beta$ = common cause factor (fraction) |
| $PFD_{avg}(2oo3) = 6 \cdot (1-\beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot \frac{T_I^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$ | PFDavg for 2oo3 voting | Standard IEC 61508-6 simplified form |
| $PFH_{avg} = \sum \lambda_{DU}$ | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (high-demand mode) | Sum of dangerous undetected rates |
| $SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | $\lambda_S$ = safe failures; $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous detected; $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected |
| $DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | Decimal fraction (0 to 1) |
| $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_S + \lambda_D = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Total failure rate decomposition | Failures per hour |
| $Risk\ Reduction\ Factor\ (RRF) = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | RRF for low-demand mode | Dimensionless |
| $SIL\ from\ PFD_{avg}$ | SIL-1: $10^{-2} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$; SIL-2: $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$; SIL-3: $10^{-4} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-3}$; SIL-4: $10^{-5} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$ | As per IEC 61508-1, Table 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Always convert failure rates to **per hour** before plugging into PFDavg formulas. KTU examiners often quote $\lambda$ in "per year" to trap students.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Choice Availability enables engineers to:

- **Optimize cost** by avoiding over-engineering (e.g., not buying SIL-3 when SIL-2 suffices).
- **Adapt to maintenance windows** — if a vessel only opens annually, a longer $T_I$ with diagnostics may be preferable.
- **Select voting architectures** that balance spurious trip rate vs PFDavg.
- **Mix Type A and Type B devices** sensibly within the same SIF.
- **Justify legacy equipment** via Route 2S using proven-in-use records.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo1 (Single Channel) Architecture

**Setup:** A single channel can fail dangerously undetected with rate $\lambda_{DU}$. The channel is proof-tested every $T_I$ hours, restoring it to "as-good-as-new." Between tests, the conditional failure probability grows linearly with time.

### Step 1: Unreliability function

The instantaneous unavailability $q(t)$ at time $t$ (since last proof test) for a single channel, assuming dangerous undetected failures arrive as a Poisson process:

$$q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

### Step 2: Linear approximation

For small values of $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ (which is the practical case since $\lambda_{DU}$ is typically 10⁻⁶/hr), using the Taylor series:

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} t + \frac{(\lambda_{DU} t)^2}{2} - \cdots$$

Retaining only the first-order term:

$$q(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

### Step 3: Average unavailability over one proof-test interval

The average probability of failure on demand is the mean value of $q(t)$ over $[0, T_I]$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} q(t)\, dt$$

### Step 4: Substitute and integrate

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\, dt$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_I} \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_I}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_I} \cdot \frac{T_I^2}{2}$$

### Step 5: Simplify

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg}(1oo1) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \frac{T_I}{2}}$$

This is the **master equation** — every other architecture formula is a derivation from this baseline.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFDavg for a 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two) Architecture

**Setup:** Two identical channels in parallel; the system fails dangerously only if **both** fail dangerously. Each fails with rate $\lambda_{DU}$.

### Step 1: Joint failure probability

The system is dangerous only if **both** channels have failed. Assuming independence initially:

$$q_{sys}(t) \approx (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2 = \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t^2$$

### Step 2: Average over the proof-test interval

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t^2\, dt$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2}{T_I} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_I}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_I^3}{3 \cdot T_I}$$

### Step 3: Simplify

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I)^2}{3}}$$

This is dramatically smaller than 1oo1 — but at the cost of doubling hardware and a higher spurious trip rate.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $T_I = 8760$ hours (1 year). Compare 1oo1 and 1oo2 architectures.

### Step 1: 1oo1 calculation

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo1) = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times \frac{8760}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo1) = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4380$$

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo1) = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

This corresponds to **SIL-1** (since $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$).

### Step 2: 1oo2 calculation

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{((5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760)^2}{3}$$

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{(0.0438)^2}{3}$$

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-3}}{3}$$

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = 6.39 \times 10^{-4}$$

This corresponds to **SIL-3** (since $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$).

### Step 3: Engineering conclusion

By choosing a 1oo2 architecture (an option made available under IEC 61508), we elevated the SIF from SIL-1 to SIL-3 — a 34-fold improvement in PFDavg. **This is Choice Availability in action.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Architecture | PFDavg Form | Spurious Trip Rate | Cost | Typical SIL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** | $\lambda_{DU} T_I / 2$ | Low | Low | SIL-1 |
| **1oo2** | $(\lambda_{DU} T_I)^2 / 3$ | High (2×) | Medium | SIL-2/3 |
| **2oo2** | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU} T_I / 2 + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_I$ | Very Low | Medium | SIL-1/2 |
| **2oo3** | $6(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 T_I^2 / 3 + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_I$ | Medium | High | SIL-2/3 |
| **1oo2D** | Similar to 1oo1 with DC | High but detected | Medium | SIL-2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Choice Availability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Target SIL Identified]) --> devType{Device Type?}
    devType -->|Type A: Simple, well-defined| routeA[Route 1H Permitted]
    devType -->|Type B: Complex, undefined| routeB[Route 1S or Route 2 Required]

    routeA --> archA{Available Architectures}
    routeB --> archB{Available Architectures}

    archA --> archA1[1oo1 Single Channel]
    archA --> archA2[1oo2 Redundant]
    archA --> archA3[2oo3 Voting]

    archB --> archB1[Route 1S: SC proven]
    archB --> archB2[Route 2H: SFF 90 percent]
    archB --> archB3[Route 2S: 10^5 hr field record]

    archA1 --> tiA{Choose Proof Test Interval}
    archA2 --> tiA
    archA3 --> tiA
    archB1 --> tiA
    archB2 --> tiA
    archB3 --> tiA

    tiA -->|Short TI high availability| opt1[High cost, frequent downtime]
    tiA -->|Long TI low cost| opt2[Use high DC diagnostics]
    tiA -->|Mid TI balanced| opt3[Standard IEC recommended]

    opt1 --> final([Document Justification in SRS])
    opt2 --> final
    opt3 --> final

    style startA fill:#e1f5ff
    style final fill:#d4edda
    style devType fill:#fff3cd
    style archA fill:#f8d7da
    style archB fill:#f8d7da
    style tiA fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Voting Architecture Choices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input([Process Variable]) --> sensorA[Sensor Channel A]
    input --> sensorB[Sensor Channel B]
    input --> sensorC[Sensor Channel C]

    sensorA --> logic{Logic Solver MooN Voting}
    sensorB --> logic
    sensorC --> logic

    logic -->|1oo1| out1[Single Trip Output]
    logic -->|1oo2| out2[Trip if EITHER A or B fails]
    logic -->|2oo2| out3[Trip if BOTH A and B fail]
    logic -->|2oo3| out4[Trip if 2 of 3 agree]

    out1 --> s1[Low PFD Low Spurious]
    out2 --> s2[Very Low PFD High Spurious]
    out3 --> s3[High PFD Very Low Spurious]
    out4 --> s4[Balanced PFD and Spurious]

    style input fill:#cfe2ff
    style logic fill:#fff3cd
    style s1 fill:#d1e7dd
    style s2 fill:#f8d7da
    style s3 fill:#fff3cd
    style s4 fill:#cff4fc
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SIF Choice Map

| Block Layer | Choices Available | Decision Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **L1: Sensing** | Single transmitter vs dual redundant vs triple voted | Required SIL vs cost |
| **L2: Logic Solving** | Safety PLC, hardwired relays, pneumatic | Type A/B classification |
| **L3: Final Element** | Single valve vs dual valve manifold | Spurious trip tolerance |
| **L4: Proof Testing** | Manual, partial-stroke, full-stroke | Accessibility & shutdown cost |
| **L5: Diagnostics** | Self-diagnostic, external tester, third-party | DC target |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Choice Availability in the context of IEC 61508. List any two architectural choices available for implementing a SIF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Choice Availability refers to the multiple permissible routes and architectural configurations sanctioned by IEC 61508 that an engineer may select to achieve a target Safety Integrity Level (SIL). The standard permits different compliance pathways depending on device type (Type A or Type B), hardware fault tolerance, safe failure fraction, and systematic capability.

**Two architectural choices:**
1. **1oo1 architecture** — single channel, used for low-SIL applications with cost constraints.
2. **1oo2 architecture** — two parallel channels; system trips if either channel demands. Provides high safety integrity but increases spurious trip rate.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two choices.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between Route 1H and Route 1S as per IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Route 1H (Hardware) | Route 1S (Systematic) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Hardware fault tolerance & safe failure fraction | Systematic capability of the device |
| Applicable to | Type A devices with well-defined failure modes | Type B devices with complex behavior |
| Evidence required | Failure rate data, SFF calculation | Proven-in-use records, SC rating |
| Limitation | Cannot be used if SFF and HFT thresholds not met | Requires demonstrated SC ≥ required SIL |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each distinction.)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architectural constraints for Route 1H compliance as per IEC 61508. Discuss the role of Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) and Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT) in determining the achievable SIL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIL-2 safety instrumented function uses a 1oo2 sensor architecture with $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and proof test interval $T_I = 4380$ hours. Calculate the PFDavg and verify whether the design meets SIL-2 requirements. Assume the logic solver and final element are perfect (negligible failure rate). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Block Diagram of Route 1H Compliance:**

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │   Identify Device Type      │
            │   (Type A or Type B?)       │
            └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                           ▼
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │  Calculate Safe Failure     │
            │  Fraction (SFF)             │
            └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                           ▼
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │ Determine Hardware Fault    │
            │ Tolerance (HFT) from        │
            │ architecture                │
            └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                           ▼
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │ Cross-check against IEC     │
            │ 61508-2 Table 2 for target  │
            │ SIL → Compliance?           │
            └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation:**

Route 1H compliance requires meeting two simultaneous constraints from **IEC 61508-2, Table 2**:

1. **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** The minimum number of faults that can be tolerated without loss of the safety function. For a single-channel (1oo1) system targeting SIL-2, HFT must be ≥ 0, but SFF must be high.

2. **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** The fraction of total failures that either result in a safe state or are detected by diagnostics:

$$SFF = \frac{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

For SIL-2 with HFT = 0, SFF must typically be between **60% and 90%**. For SIL-2 with HFT = 1, SFF between **60% and 90%** is also acceptable under Type A classification. **The choice of architecture directly determines the HFT, which in turn influences the SFF threshold required.**

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for SFF formula and limits, 2 marks for HFT discussion.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Given:**
- Architecture: 1oo2
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T_I = 4380$ hours

**Step 1:** Apply the 1oo2 formula:

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I)^2}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$:

$$\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I = (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4380 = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Square the product:

$$(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I)^2 = (8.76 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.674 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by 3:

$$PFD_{avg}(1oo2) = \frac{7.674 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 2.558 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with SIL-2 range:

SIL-2 requires $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$.

Since $PFD_{avg} = 2.558 \times 10^{-5}$, this lies in the **SIL-4** range ($10^{-5} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$). Wait — recheck: $2.558 \times 10^{-5}$ is between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-4}$, so it actually satisfies **SIL-4**, which far exceeds the SIL-2 requirement.

**Step 6: Conclusion:** The design is **over-engineered** for SIL-2. The engineer may either accept the conservative design, reduce $T_I$, or downgrade to a 1oo1 architecture if budget pressure exists — but the original SIL-2 target is comfortably met.

*(Award 2 marks for correct formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for computation, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the four compliance routes (Route 1H, 1S, 2H, 2S) in IEC 61508. Under what circumstances would an engineer prefer Route 2S over Route 2H? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2oo3 voting architecture is used with $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, $\beta = 0.05$, and $T_I = 8760$ hours. Compute the PFDavg and the corresponding Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). Identify the achieved SIL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Route | Primary Basis | SFF Requirement | SC Requirement | Field Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1H** | Hardware fault tolerance + SFF | Per Table 2 | Implicit | Not mandatory |
| **1S** | Systematic capability | Not primary | SC ≥ SIL | Recommended |
| **2H** | Hardware (enhanced) | Higher than 1H | Implicit | Not mandatory |
| **2S** | Systematic (enhanced) | Not primary | SC ≥ 2 (minimum) | **Mandatory ≥ 10⁵ hours** |

**Engineer prefers Route 2S over Route 2H when:**

1. The device is **Type B** (complex integrated circuit, ASIC, microcontroller-based) where failure modes cannot be fully enumerated.
2. **Field-proven data** of at least 10⁵ operational hours is available across multiple installations.
3. The manufacturer can demonstrate **SC ≥ 2** through quality management certification (e.g., IEC 61508 SIL certification by TÜV, exida).
4. The application demands **high systematic integrity** that hardware metrics alone cannot guarantee (e.g., software-intensive logic solvers).
5. **Cost of redundancy** (Route 2H requires higher SFF which often means more components) is prohibitive.

*(Award 4 marks for table, 3 marks for preference justification.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Given:**
- Architecture: 2oo3
- $\lambda_{DU} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $\beta = 0.05$
- $T_I = 8760$ hours (1 year)

**Step 1:** Recall the 2oo3 PFDavg formula:

$$PFD_{avg}(2oo3) = 6(1-\beta) \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot \frac{T_I^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the first term (redundant portion):

$$\lambda_{DU}^2 = (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 1 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$T_I^2 = (8760)^2 = 7.674 \times 10^{7}$$

$$6 \cdot (1-0.05) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot \frac{7.674 \times 10^{7}}{3}$$

$$= 6 \cdot 0.95 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (2.558 \times 10^{7})$$

$$= 5.7 \cdot 2.558 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$= 1.458 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the second term (common-cause portion):

$$\beta \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I = 0.05 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 8760$$

$$= 4.38 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4:** Sum the two terms:

$$PFD_{avg}(2oo3) = 1.458 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} = 5.838 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate RRF:

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \frac{1}{5.838 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1713$$

**Step 6:** Identify SIL:

Since $10^{-4} \leq 5.838 \times 10^{-4} < 10^{-3}$, the achieved SIL is **SIL-3**.

*(Award 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for term-1 calculation, 1 mark for term-2, 1 mark for RRF, 1 mark for SIL identification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse 1oo2 with 2oo2.** 1oo2 trips on a *single* channel demand (high safety, high spurious). 2oo2 trips only on *both* channels demanding (lower safety, very low spurious). This is the #1 mark-loser in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Always include the $\beta$ term** for 2oo2 and 2oo3 architectures. Skipping it loses 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Unit conversion** — $\lambda$ given "per year" must be divided by 8760 to get "per hour" before plugging into formulas. This traps dozens of students every semester.
> 4. **SFF without HFT is meaningless** — Route 1H compliance requires *both* to be checked against Table 2. Mentioning SFF alone loses 1.5 marks.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Choice Availability** = the structured freedom IEC 61508 gives engineers to select between Routes 1H, 1S, 2H, 2S and MooN architectures.
- **Four Routes:** 1H (HW), 1S (Systematic), 2H (Enhanced HW), 2S (Enhanced Systematic with field proof).
- **Route Selection Drivers:** Device Type (A/B), SFF, HFT, SC, cost, available field data.
- **Type A device** → Route 1H permitted; **Type B device** → Route 1S or Route 2 mandatory.
- **Master PFDavg formula:** $\lambda_{DU} T_I / 2$ (1oo1) — all other architectures are derived from this.
- **1oo2 PFDavg:** $(\lambda_{DU} T_I)^2 / 3$ — no $\beta$ term since channels are independent.
- **2oo3 PFDavg:** $6(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 T_I^2 / 3 + \beta \lambda_{DU} T_I$ — must include $\beta$.
- **SIL mapping (low demand):** SIL-1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL-2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL-3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL-4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **RRF** = $1 / PFD_{avg}$.
- **SFF formula** is a *ratio* — never write it as a single $\lambda$ value.
- **Choice Availability justification** must appear in the **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** as per IEC 61511 Clause 10.3.
- **High-demand mode** uses **PFH** (failures per hour), not PFDavg. SIL-1: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$; SIL-2: $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$; SIL-3: $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$; SIL-4: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixed Choice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mixed Choice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IEC 61511 / IEC 61508 Aligned)

In the context of **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** within the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** life-cycle, the term **"Mixed Choice"** (also referred to as **Mixed Architecture**, **Mixed Voting**, or **Heterogeneous Redundancy Configuration**) refers to a design strategy in which the three independent subsystems of a SIF — namely the **Sensor Subsystem (S)**, the **Logic Solver Subsystem (L)**, and the **Final Element Subsystem (FE)** — are configured with **different M-out-of-N (MooN) voting architectures** to achieve the target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *Mixed Choice* architecture is one where the sensor channel, logic solver, and final element of a single Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) do **not** all use identical voting schemes. The architect selects the most cost-effective combination of MooN voting patterns such that:
> $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,avg}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} \le \text{PFD}_{\text{required}}$$
> while simultaneously satisfying the **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** constraints mandated by **IEC 61508-2** and **IEC 61511-1** for the required SIL.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Security Checkpoint"

Imagine a high-security international airport. A single passenger must pass through **three sequential checkpoints** before boarding:

| Subsystem | Checkpoint Analogy | Function |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| **Sensor (S)** | Metal detector archway + X-ray of cabin baggage | Detects the dangerous condition (e.g., over-pressure, over-temperature) |
| **Logic Solver (L)** | The security officer monitoring the screens | Decides whether the alarm is real and commands shutdown |
| **Final Element (FE)** | The locked boarding gate that slams shut | Physically brings the process to a safe state |

A **Single Choice** architecture is like having **identical** 3-stage screening at every checkpoint. A **Mixed Choice** architecture is like having, for example:
- **2 metal detectors in parallel** (1oo2 voting) at Checkpoint 1 — *highly redundant sensing*,
- **A single security officer** (1oo1 voting) at Checkpoint 2 — *simpler logic*, and
- **2 redundant blast doors** (2oo2 voting) at Checkpoint 3 — *both must close to guarantee safety*.

The mixed choice is **cheaper and equally safe** because redundancy is applied where the **failure rate is highest** and simplicity is used where the **failure rate is lowest**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** The probability that a SIF fails to perform its safety function when demanded. Ranges from **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵** for SIL 1 to SIL 4.
- **MooN (M-out-of-N):** A voting notation where *M* out of *N* identical channels must agree to initiate the safe state.
- **HFT (Hardware Fault Tolerance):** The maximum number of *dangerous* faults that can occur without loss of the safety function. **HFT = N − M** for MooN.
- **SFF (Safe Failure Fraction):** The fraction of overall failures that result in a *safe* (fail-safe/spurious) state. **SFF ≥ 60% to 99%** depending on SIL.
- **CCF (Common Cause Failure):** Simultaneous failure of multiple channels due to a *single shared root cause* (e.g., common power supply, EMC disturbance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the 2024 scheme ESE, examiners love asking the difference between *Single Choice* and *Mixed Choice*. Always quote the IEC clause and the formula for PFD aggregation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Mixed-Choice SIF

A SIF operates on a **demand-driven** principle. The process is:

1. **Process Variable (PV) Acquisition** — The sensor subsystem monitors a hazardous condition (e.g., level, pressure, flow).
2. **Comparison** — The PV is compared against a setpoint inside the logic solver.
3. **Decision** — The logic solver, using a MooN voting rule on the sensor inputs, decides whether a *demand* has occurred.
4. **Actuation** — The logic solver commands the final element via MooN voting on actuator outputs.
5. **Safe State Achievement** — The final element physically isolates/relieves the process.

In a **Mixed Choice** SIF, steps 1, 3, and 4 may use **different MooN voting**. The designer must compute three independent PFDs and **add them in series**.

### 2.2 Why Choose Mixed Over Single Choice?

| Criterion | Single Choice (e.g., all 1oo2) | Mixed Choice |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------|
| **Engineering Effort** | Low — uniform design | High — requires per-subsystem analysis |
| **Component Cost** | High — uniform redundancy | Optimised — redundancy only where needed |
| **Spurious Trip Rate (STR)** | Higher (more components) | Lower (fewer redundant channels in low-PFD parts) |
| **SIL Achievability** | Guaranteed if uniform SFF/HFT met | Guaranteed if **PFD_SIF ≤ PFD_req** AND SFF/HFT met |
| **Diagnostic Coverage** | Uniform | Can be tuned per subsystem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
