> **Engineering Rationale:** Sensors and final elements are typically *less reliable* (higher λ_DU) than modern logic solvers. Hence, designers often use **2oo3** for sensors, **1oo1** for the logic solver, and **1oo2** for the final element — a classic mixed architecture.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mermaid-Safe Notation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark this table — it is the single most tested content in Module 3.**

| # | Subsystem / Concept | Architecture | PFD Formula | HFT |
|---|---------------------|--------------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | Single Channel | **1oo1** | $\text{PFD} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | **0** |
| 2 | Two-Channel, Both Required | **1oo2** | $\text{PFD} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2}{3}$ | **1** |
| 3 | Two-Channel, Either Required | **2oo2** | $\text{PFD} = 2 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}/2}\right) - \text{CCF term}$ | **0** |
| 4 | Two-Channel, Diagnostic + Voted | **1oo2D** | $\text{PFD} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^2 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^2}{3} \cdot (1 - \beta)$ | **1** |
| 5 | Three-Channel, Two Voted | **2oo3** | $\text{PFD} = 6 \cdot (\lambda_{\text{DU}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}})^2 \cdot \text{diag. factor}$ | **1** |
| 6 | Three-Channel, All Voted | **1oo3** | $\text{PFD} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^3 \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^3}{4}$ | **2** |
| 7 | Series Aggregation (Mixed Choice) | **All** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}}$ | — |
| 8 | SFF (Safe Failure Fraction) | **All** | $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{\lambda_{\text{DU}} + \lambda_{\text{S}}}$ | — |
| 9 | Architectural Constraint (Type B) | **Mixed** | $\text{SFF} \ge 60\% \text{ (SIL 1)}, 90\% \text{ (SIL 2)}, 99\% \text{ (SIL 3)}$ | HFT ≥ 1 for SIL 3 |

Where:
- $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate (per hour)
- $\lambda_{\text{S}}$ = Safe (spurious) failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{\text{proof}}$ = Proof Test Interval in hours
- $\beta$ = Beta-factor for Common Cause Failure (typically **0.1 to 0.2**)
- $T_{\text{mission}}$ = Useful life / mission time of the device

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Mixed Choice architectures are the **industry default** in:

- **Oil & Gas** — Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves on offshore platforms.
- **Power Generation** — Turbine overspeed protection (triple-redundant sensors with 2oo3 logic, single PLC, dual solenoid dump valves).
- **Chemical Processing** — Reactor over-temperature trips with RTD sensors in 2oo3, DCS-based logic, and double-block-and-bleed valves in 1oo2.
- **Nuclear Reactor Protection** — Heterogeneous voting across diverse channels (different manufacturers) to defeat CCF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PFD of a 1oo2 Subsystem (Sensor Logic Block)

A **1oo2** channel pair triggers the safety action if **at least 1 of 2** channels detects the demand. The system *fails dangerously* only if **both** channels fail in the dangerous-un-detected mode.

Let $F_i(t)$ be the probability that channel $i$ has suffered a dangerous undetected failure by time $t$. For an exponential failure distribution with constant $\lambda_{\text{DU}}$ and proof testing at $T_{\text{proof}}$:

$$F_i(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} t}$$

The average (over one proof-test cycle) probability of dangerous failure for **one** channel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{F_i} &= \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \int_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} (1 - e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} t}) \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{proof}}} \left[ t + \dfrac{e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} t}}{\lambda_{\text{DU}}} \right]_{0}^{T_{\text{proof}}} \\[6pt]
&= 1 + \dfrac{e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}}} - 1}{\lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}}} \\[6pt]
&\approx \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \quad \text{(for } \lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}} \ll 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For the 1oo2 pair**, both channels must fail dangerously (independent assumption, ignoring CCF first):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} &= \overline{F_1} \times \overline{F_2} \\[6pt]
&= \left( \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}} T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \right)^2 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} T_{\text{proof}}^{2}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board universally accepts the simplified IEC 61508-6 form: $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} T_{\text{proof}}^{2}}{3}$ (the exact integral yields $1/3$ when the lower-order terms are kept — this is the **published IEC 61508-6 Table B.5** value and should be used in exams).

### 3.2 Worked Example: A Mixed-Choice SIF

**Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark):** A chemical reactor over-pressure protection SIF is designed as follows:
- **Sensor Subsystem:** 1oo2D (two pressure transmitters with diagnostics, $\beta = 0.1$)
- **Logic Solver:** 1oo1 (single certified Safety PLC)
- **Final Element:** 2oo2 (two solenoid-operated shut-off valves in series)

Given:
- $\lambda_{\text{DU,S}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ /h
- $\lambda_{\text{DU,L}} = 1 \times 10^{-8}$ /h
- $\lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /h
- $T_{\text{proof}} = 8760$ h (1 year)

**Calculate PFD_SIF and identify the achievable SIL.**

#### Step 1 — PFD of Sensor Subsystem (1oo2D)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{S}} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,S}}^{2} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}^{2}}{3} \cdot (1 - \beta) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-7})^{2} \times (8760)^{2}}{3} \times (1 - 0.1) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-14} \times 76{,}737{,}600}{3} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{3} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= 6.394 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= 5.755 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — PFD of Logic Solver (1oo1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{L}} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,L}} \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-8} \times 8760}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 4.380 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Critical Observation:** The single logic solver is *more* dangerous than the redundant sensors because of its much higher *occupancy* of the safety loop time. Designers counter this by demanding **Type-A** components (proven-in-use) with internal diagnostics.

#### Step 3 — PFD of Final Element (2oo2)

The 2oo2 system demands that **both** valves close. A dangerous failure occurs if **either** valve fails to close on demand. Using the IEC 61508-6 approximation for 2oo2 with $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} &= 2 \cdot \left( (1 - e^{-\lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} T_{\text{proof}}/2}) - \lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} T_{\text{proof}} \cdot \beta / 2 \right) \\[6pt]
&\approx 2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} T_{\text{proof}}}{2} - \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} T_{\text{proof}} \\[6pt]
&= \lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} T_{\text{proof}} \cdot (1 - \beta) \\[6pt]
&= 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 \times (1 - 0.1) \\[6pt]
&= 2.365 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget that for 2oo2, the dangerous failure is *not* the joint probability (both failing). The system is dangerous if *either one* fails. Hence the formula is *not* a product but a *sum adjusted for CCF*.

#### Step 4 — Series Aggregation (Mixed-Choice Rule)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,avg}} &= \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} \\[6pt]
&= 5.755 \times 10^{-6} + 4.380 \times 10^{-5} + 2.365 \times 10^{-2} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.370 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — SIL Classification

From **IEC 61511-1 Table 3** (Low-Demand Mode):

| SIL | PFD Range (Low Demand) |
|-----|------------------------|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

Our $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = 2.37 \times 10^{-2}$ falls in the **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$** band ⇒ **SIL 1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final Element Dominates.** In ~85% of real-world SIFs, the **final element** has the highest PFD contribution. This is why the *Mixed Choice* methodology often allocates redundancy budget primarily to the final element.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Mixed Choice Selection

| Sensor λ_DU | Logic λ_DU | FE λ_DU | Recommended Architecture | Reason |
|-------------|------------|---------|--------------------------|--------|
| **High** (> 10⁻⁶) | Low (< 10⁻⁸) | **High** (> 10⁻⁶) | **2oo3 (S) + 1oo1 (L) + 1oo2 (FE)** | Redundancy where unreliability is high |
| Medium (~ 10⁻⁷) | Medium | Medium | **1oo2 (S) + 1oo1 (L) + 1oo2 (FE)** | Standard cost-effective SIL 2 |
| **Low** (< 10⁻⁸) | **High** (> 10⁻⁶) | Low | **1oo1 (S) + 1oo2D (L) + 1oo1 (FE)** | Vote logic only |
| All Low | All Low | All Low | **1oo1 throughout** | Single choice — SIL 1 sufficient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Mixed-Choice SIF

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **signal and voting flow** of a generic mixed-choice SIF with heterogeneous MooN architectures. The architecture chosen is **2oo3 (S) → 1oo1 (L) → 1oo2D (FE)**, a common SIL 3 configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Under Protection] --> PT1[Pressure Tx 1]
    P --> PT2[Pressure Tx 2]
    P --> PT3[Pressure Tx 3]
    PT1 --> VOTES[Voting Logic 2 of 3]
    PT2 --> VOTES
    PT3 --> VOTES
    VOTES -->|Trip Signal| SOL[Logic Solver SIL 3 PLC]
    SOL -->|De-energise Coil A| V1[Solenoid Valve A 1oo2D]
    SOL -->|De-energise Coil B| V2[Solenoid Valve B 1oo2D]
    V1 --> PMV[Process Shut-off Valve]
    V2 --> PMV
    PMV --> SS[Safe State Achieved]

    PFDsub1[Compute PFD of Sensor Block] -.-> VOTES
    PFDsub2[Compute PFD of Logic Block] -.-> SOL
    PFDsub3[Compute PFD of Final Element] -.-> V1
    PFDsub4[Compute PFD of Final Element] -.-> V2
    PFDsub1 --> AGG[PFD SIF equals PFD S plus PFD L plus PFD FE]
    PFDsub2 --> AGG
    PFDsub3 --> AGG
    PFDsub4 --> AGG
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing a Mixed-Choice Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Target SIL and Process Risk] --> B{Required PFD known?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Allocate PFD Budget per Subsystem]
    B -- No --> C2[Use Default 35 percent S, 15 percent L, 50 percent FE]
    C --> D[Compute Sensor Lambda DU and Lambda S]
    C --> E[Compute Logic Solver Lambda DU]
    C --> F[Compute Final Element Lambda DU]
    D --> G{Sensor dominates?}
    E --> H{Logic dominates?}
    F --> I{FE dominates?}
    G -- Yes --> J[Choose 2oo3 or 1oo2D for Sensor]
    G -- No --> K[Choose 1oo1 for Sensor]
    H -- Yes --> L[Choose 1oo2D for Logic]
    H -- No --> M[Choose 1oo1 Logic]
    I -- Yes --> N[Choose 1oo2 or 2oo3 for Final Element]
    I -- No --> O[Choose 1oo1 Final Element]
    J --> P[Verify SFF and HFT per IEC 61508 Table 2]
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{All constraints satisfied?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Issue SIL Achievement Report]
    Q -- No --> S[Iterate Architecture or Shorten Tproof]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Reliability Model (Markov-Style Abstraction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OK[System OK State] -->|Lambda DU detected fail| S1[Safe Spurious Trip]
    OK -->|Lambda DU undetected fail| D1[Dangerous Undetected State 1]
    OK -->|Beta Lambda DU CCF| D2[Dangerous Common Cause State]
    D1 -->|Repair plus Proof Test| OK
    D2 -->|Repair plus Proof Test| OK
    S1 -->|Reset and Repair| OK
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **λ_DU β** branch represents **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** — the single greatest threat to redundant architectures. It is what differentiates *theoretical* redundancy from *real* redundancy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Mixed Choice" as applied to Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) architecture. How does it differ from "Single Choice"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A *Mixed Choice* SIF architecture is one in which the three subsystems — **Sensor**, **Logic Solver**, and **Final Element** — employ **different M-out-of-N voting schemes** (e.g., sensor 2oo3, logic 1oo1, final element 1oo2). A *Single Choice* architecture uses the **same MooN voting** across all three subsystems. The benefit of mixed choice is **optimised cost and reduced spurious trip rate** while still meeting the target SIL via the aggregated PFD equation $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the IEC 61511 series-averaging formula for the PFD of a complete SIF and explain the significance of the 1/3 factor in the 1oo2 PFD equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The complete SIF PFD is the **algebraic sum** of the three subsystem PFDs because they are in series from a reliability viewpoint:
> $$\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF,avg}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}}$$
> The factor $1/3$ in $\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2} = \lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} T^{2}/3$ arises from the **time-averaging of the joint failure probability** over one proof-test cycle. The exact integral yields $1/3$ (not $1/4$) because the joint failure density is *not* symmetric — earlier-in-cycle failures are weighted more heavily by the integral.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the rationale behind adopting a **Mixed Choice** architecture in safety-instrumented systems. Discuss the role of **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** and the **β-factor** model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIF is configured as **1oo2 (Sensor) + 1oo1 (Logic) + 2oo3 (Final Element)**. Given $\lambda_{\text{DU,S}} = 4 \times 10^{-7}$/h, $\lambda_{\text{DU,L}} = 2 \times 10^{-8}$/h, $\lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/h, and $T_{\text{proof}} = 4380$ h (6 months), with $\beta = 0.1$ for all subsystems, calculate the total PFD and state the achieved SIL. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks (detailed answer):**

1. **Cost vs. Safety Optimisation (2 marks):** Modern logic solvers (certified safety PLCs) are highly reliable ($\lambda_{\text{DU}} \sim 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$/h), while field devices (sensors, valves) have $\lambda_{\text{DU}} \sim 10^{-6}$/h. A *uniform* 1oo2 across all subsystems **wastes money** on a logic solver that doesn't need it. Mixed choice places redundancy where the failure rate is highest.

2. **Spurious Trip Minimisation (2 marks):** In 1oo2, *either* channel can trip the system. A 2oo3 final element provides *higher* availability because only 2 of 3 must agree. This reduces plant downtime and lost production — a key *non-safety* benefit of mixed choice.

3. **CCF and β-Factor Model (3 marks):** The β-factor model assumes that a fraction $\beta$ of component failures are **shared** across redundant channels due to a common root cause (common power supply, common calibration error, common EMC disturbance). For two redundant channels:
   $$\text{PFD}_{1\text{oo}2}^{\text{real}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}^{2} T^{2}}{3} \cdot (1 - \beta) + \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU}} T$$
   If $\beta = 0.1$, the **CCF term can dominate** at long proof-test intervals, making the redundant pair behave like a single channel. The IEC 61508-6 mandates the use of the **proven-in-use β-table** (e.g., IEC 61508-6 Annex D).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks (numerical solution):**

**Step 1 — Sensor (1oo2):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{S}} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,S}}^{2} \cdot T^{2}}{3} \cdot (1 - \beta) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(4 \times 10^{-7})^{2} \times (4380)^{2}}{3} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-13} \times 1.918 \times 10^{7}}{3} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= 9.21 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.9 \\[6pt]
&= 8.29 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Logic (1oo1):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{L}} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU,L}} \cdot T}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-8} \times 4380}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 4.38 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Element (2oo3):**
The 2oo3 system is dangerous when **all 3 valves** fail (product) or when a **2-out-of-3 CCF** occurs. Simplified IEC 61508-6 form (Annex B, Table B.5):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{FE}} &= 6 \cdot (\lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} \cdot T)^2 \cdot (1 - \beta) + \beta \cdot \lambda_{\text{DU,FE}} \cdot T \\[6pt]
&= 6 \times (1 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380)^2 \times 0.9 + 0.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 \\[6pt]
&= 6 \times 1.918 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.9 + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
&= 1.036 \times 10^{-4} + 4.38 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
&= 5.416 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} &= 8.29 \times 10^{-7} + 4.38 \times 10^{-5} + 5.42 \times 10^{-4} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5.86 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — SIL Identification:** $5.86 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$** ⇒ **SIL 2**.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, describe the **five standard MooN voting configurations** (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 1oo2D, 2oo3) used in sensor subsystems of a mixed-choice SIF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** and **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** constraints for a Type-B element, and explain why these two parameters — not just PFD — are the *primary* IEC 61508 gatekeepers for SIL achievement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
Provide block diagrams (5 × 1.4 marks each) showing the channels and the voting gate. Key points per architecture:
- **1oo1** — single channel, HFT = 0, lowest cost, lowest PFD reduction.
- **1oo2** — two channels OR-ed, HFT = 1, high availability, low PFD.
- **2oo2** — two channels AND-ed, HFT = 0, high spurious trip risk.
- **1oo2D** — 1oo2 with **automatic diagnostics** (e.g., stuck-valve detection); HFT = 1; PFD is the lowest of the 2-channel group.
- **2oo3** — three channels with 2-out-of-3 voting; HFT = 1; best **diagnostic coverage vs. availability** trade-off; used in high-SIL turbine protection.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
- HFT quantifies *how many dangerous faults* the hardware can sustain *without losing the safety function*. HFT is constrained by IEC 61508-2 Table 2.
- SFF is the *ratio of safe + dangerous-detected failures to total failures*:
  $$\text{SFF} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{S}} + \lambda_{\text{DD}}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{DU}}}{\lambda_{\text{DU}} + \lambda_{\text{S}}}$$
- A **Type B** element has *incomplete* failure coverage (e.g., complex ICs). The IEC 61508-2 Table 2 requires, e.g., for SIL 3 with Type B: **HFT ≥ 1** AND **SFF ≥ 99%**.
- Even if the PFD calculation yields a low number, the architecture is *disqualified* for the SIL if the SFF or HFT constraints are not met. This is the **architectural constraint** that operates *orthogonally* to the probabilistic PFD calculation.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Mixed-Choice Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong aggregation operator:** Students often *multiply* subsystem PFDs instead of *adding* them. The series subsystems use **additive** aggregation.
> 2. **Ignoring CCF β-factor:** A 1oo2 system with $\beta = 0.1$ is *not* simply $\lambda^2 T^2/3$. The CCF term must be added or the simplified IEC 61508-6 $(1-\beta)$-scaled form used.
> 3. **Wrong PFD formula for 2oo2:** Students use the 1oo2 product form. The 2oo2 is dangerous when *either* channel fails, so it uses a **sum** structure, not a product.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $\lambda$ in /h, $T$ in h, PFD is **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Mixing up HFT:** HFT = $N - M$ for MooN, *not* $M - 1$. A 2oo3 has HFT = 1, **not** 2.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mixed Choice** = different MooN voting across sensor, logic, and final element subsystems of a SIF.
- The core equation is **always**: $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{S}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{L}} + \text{PFD}_{\text{FE}}$.
- The **β-factor model** is the mathematical embodiment of Common Cause Failure. Use $\beta = 0.1$ as a default unless field data justifies otherwise.
- The **HFT** and **SFF** are the *architectural* constraints (IEC 61508-2 Table 2). A low PFD alone **does not** guarantee SIL achievement.
- The **final element** usually dominates the PFD budget. Allocate redundancy there first.
- **Proof Test Interval ($T_{\text{proof}}$)** is a free design variable — halving $T_{\text{proof}}$ approximately **quarters** the PFD of a 1oo1 subsystem and **quarters the square** for a 1oo2 subsystem.
- The KTU board accepts the simplified IEC 61508-6 Table B.5 values (1oo2 → 1/3, 2oo3 → 6) without derivation.
- **Single Choice** is a *special case* of mixed choice where all three subsystems happen to use the same MooN voting.
- Mixed choice provides **lower spurious trip rate** than uniform redundancy — a *non-safety* economic benefit.
- Always state the **Type** of the element (Type A or Type B) before applying the IEC 61508-2 Table 2 constraints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** V Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **V-Model (V-Cycle)** is a graphical representation of a system development lifecycle in which execution processes (decomposition and refinement on the left descending arm) are paired with corresponding validation and verification processes (integration and confirmation on the right ascending arm). In the context of Functional Safety (as per **ISO 26262** and **IEC 61508**), the V-Model defines the systematic relationship between safety requirement specification at each abstraction level and the corresponding confirmation measure at the same abstraction level. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The House Building Analogy":** > Imagine you are building a house. > - **Left side of the V (descending):** You start with the architect's vision (what kind of house do I need?), then the structural engineer designs the foundation, then the mason lays the bricks, then the electrician wires it. > - **Bottom of the V (the implementation point):** The actual physical house is built — bricks, wires, pipes exist. > - **Right side of the V (ascending):** You climb back up, but this time **verifying** that what was built matches the design — does the wiring carry the right current? (Unit test) Does the foundation hold the weight? (Integration test) Does the finished house withstand wind? (System test) Finally, does the house actually serve the family as intended? (Acceptance / validation). > > In functional safety, if a single wire is wrong in an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), the whole safety goal fails. The V-Model enforces that for **every design step on the left, there is a matching test step on the right** — so mistakes cannot slip silently down the chain. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Standard Value | Relevance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ASIL Levels** | **A, B, C, D** (per ISO 26262:2018) | Determines the rigor of methods required at each V-Cycle level. **ASIL D = Highest risk** | | **SIL Levels** | **1, 2, 3, 4** (per IEC 61508) | Safety Integrity Level for industrial systems | | **V-Model Phases** | **Left: 4 levels**, **Bottom: 1**, **Right: 4 levels** | Defines the minimum granularity for safety lifecycle | | **Confirmation Measures** | **Confirmation Reviews (CR)**, **Functional Safety Audit (FSA)**, **Functional Safety Assessment (FSA)** | Mandatory gate reviews at each phase transition | ### 1.4 Why the V-Model in Functional Safety? The V-Model is mandated by functional safety standards (ISO 26262, IEC 61508) because: 1. **Traceability** — every requirement (left) maps to a test (right). 2. **Defect Containment** — errors are caught at the same abstraction level where they originated. 3. **Deterministic Development** — required for safety cases submitted to certification bodies (TÜV, SGS, etc.). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Left Arm (Descending — Specification & Decomposition) The left arm represents the **refinement of safety goals** into concrete technical specifications. Each downward step adds implementation detail. > [!NOTE] > **Phase-by-Phase Logic (Why & How):** - **Step 1 — Item Definition / System Context:** - *Why:* You cannot analyze safety of something you have not bounded. - *How:* Define the function, boundaries, interfaces, and operational environment of the system (e.g., an Electric Power Steering system). - **Step 2 — Hazard Analysis & Risk Assessment (HARA):** - *Why:* Identify what could go wrong and how bad the harm would be. - *How:* Compute **Severity (S)**, **Exposure (E)**, and **Controllability (C)**. These determine the **ASIL**. - **Step 3 — Functional Safety Concept (FSC):** - *Why:* Translate hazards into high-level safety goals with **ASIL attributes** attached. - *How:* Derive **Safety Goals (SG)** → **Functional Safety Requirements (FSR)** assigned to architectural elements. - **Step 4 — Technical Safety Concept (TSC):** - *Why:* Hardware and software architects need measurable, verifiable specifications. - *How:* Decompose FSRs into **Technical Safety Requirements (TSR)** with diagnostics, fault reaction times (FTTI), and hardware metrics (SPFM, LFM, PMHF). - **Step 5 — System & Software Design:** - *Why:* Implementers need a blueprint. - *How:* Define HW/SW interfaces, modular structure, control algorithms, watchdog logic, and memory partitioning. ### 2.2 The Bottom of the V (Implementation) - *Why:* Specifications are abstract until realized. - *How:* Actual **coding (C/C++/Model-Based with Simulink/Stateflow)**, **circuit design (schematics, PCB layout)**, and **calibration**. ### 2.3 The Right Arm (Ascending — Verification & Validation) Each right-side phase **mirrors a left-side phase**. The pairing is the heart of the V-Model. - **Unit Testing** ↔ Implementation: Tests individual functions/modules in isolation. - **Integration Testing** ↔ HW/SW Design: Tests interaction between modules and hardware/software interfaces. - **System Testing** ↔ Technical Safety Concept: Tests the integrated item against TSRs (diagnostic coverage, fault injection). - **Vehicle/System Validation** ↔ Functional Safety Concept: Tests the built system against Safety Goals in a realistic environment (HIL rig, proving ground). - **Confirmation Measures** ↔ HARA / Item Definition: Independent review that the safety case is complete and consistent. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The V-Cycle itself is qualitative, but the artifacts it produces rely on these KTU-tested formulas: | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Risk (R)** | $R = S \times E \times C$ | Dimensionless product, classified into ASIL classes | | **ASIL Determination** | $ASIL = f(S, E, C)$ | Discrete output in $\{QM, A, B, C, D\}$ | | **FTTI (Fault Tolerant Time Interval)** | $t_{\text{FTTI}} = t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{react}}$ | Time (ms) — must be $< t_{\text{hazard}}$ | | **SPFM (Single Point Fault Metric)** | $\lambda_{\text{SPF,total}} / \lambda_{\text{total}}$ | $\geq 99\%$ for ASIL D | | **LFM (Latent Fault Metric)** | $\lambda_{\text{LF,total}} / (\lambda_{\text{LF,total}} + \lambda_{\text{DPF,detected}})$ | $\geq 90\%$ for ASIL D | | **PMHF (Probabilistic Metric for Hardware Failures)** | $M_{\text{PMHF}} = \sum \lambda_i$ | $< 10^{-8}$ h$^{-1}$ for ASIL D | | **Architectural Constraint** | Sum of violations $< $ ASIL budget | Discrete combinatorial check | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In automotive OEMs (Bosch, Continental, Tata Elxsi) and process industries (Honeywell, ABB, Siemens), the V-Model is the legal framework for submitting a **Safety Case** to certification bodies. Without V-Model artifacts, a product **cannot** be homologated or deployed in safety-critical markets. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of ASIL from Risk Triplets (HARA Step) The HARA classifies each hazardous event into a triplet $(S, E, C)$. The standard maps these triplets to an ASIL using a discrete lookup table. We will derive the **mathematical structure** of this mapping. **Given:** - $S \in \{S0, S1, S2, S3\}$ (no injury $\to$ life-threatening) - $E \in \{E0, E1, E2, E3, E4\}$ (incredibly low $\to$ high probability) - $C \in \{C0, C1, C2, C3\}$ (controllable $\to$ uncontrollable) **Step 1 — Risk R is formed as the product:** $$ R = S \oplus E \oplus C $$ where $\oplus$ denotes a **graded aggregation operator** (not ordinary multiplication, since the levels are ordinal). **Step 2 — Risk Classification:** Assign each triplet $(S, E, C)$ to one of the five ASIL classes: $$ \text{ASIL}(S, E, C) = \begin{cases} D & \text{if } S=S3 \text{ and } E \geq E4 \text{ and } C \geq C3 \\ C & \text{if } S=S3 \text{ and } E \geq E4 \text{ and } C=C2 \\ B & \text{if } S=S2 \text{ and } E \geq E4 \text{ and } C \geq C3 \\ A & \text{if } S=S1 \text{ and } E \geq E4 \text{ and } C \geq C3 \\ QM & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 — Example derivation:** Consider an **unintended acceleration** event in a highway-driving car: - Severity $S = S3$ (life-threatening crash possible) - Exposure $E = E4$ (driving on highway is very frequent) - Controllability $C = C3$ (average driver cannot brake-resist the unintended torque) Substituting into the ASIL equation: $$ \text{ASIL}(S3, E4, C3) = D $$ **Step 4 — Propagation Down the V:** This ASIL D propagates to the corresponding **Functional Safety Requirement (FSR)** at the FSC level, then to the **Technical Safety Requirement (TSR)** at the TSC level, then to the **Software Unit** at the implementation level. Each transition is **traceability-preserving**. $$ \text{ASIL}_{\text{goal}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASIL}_{\text{FSR}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASIL}_{\text{TSR}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASIL}_{\text{SW}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic Explanation:** Each step in the V-model adds **detail**, not **new safety class**. The ASIL is inherited downward (left arm) and verified upward (right arm). This is the central invariant of the V-model. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase-to-Phase Mapping | Left Arm (Specification) | Right Arm (Verification) | Artifact Linking | Failure If Mismatched | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Item Definition | Confirmation Review (CR) | Item Definition Report | Unbounded scope | | HARA | Confirmation Review + Functional Safety Audit | HARA Report, Safety Goals | Missing hazards | | Functional Safety Concept | Vehicle/System Validation Test | FSC + Validation Plan | Untested safety goals | | Technical Safety Concept | System-Level Integration Test | TSR + System Test Spec | Unverified diagnostics | | HW/SW Design | Integration Test | Design Spec + Integration Test Report | Interface faults undetected | | Implementation (Code) | Unit Test | Source Code + Unit Test Report | Hidden software defects | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Functional Safety V-Cycle ```mermaid graph TD A["ITEM DEFINITION
System boundaries and functions"] --> B["HARA
Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment"] B --> C["FUNCTIONAL SAFETY CONCEPT
Safety Goals and FSRs"] C --> D["TECHNICAL SAFETY CONCEPT
Technical Safety Requirements TSR"] D --> E["SYSTEM AND SW DESIGN
HW-SW Architecture and Interfaces"] E --> F["IMPLEMENTATION
Code, Circuits, Calibration"] F --> G["UNIT TESTING
Module and Function Tests"] G --> H["INTEGRATION TESTING
HW-SW Interface Tests"] H --> I["SYSTEM TESTING
TSR Verification and Fault Injection"] I --> J["VEHICLE VALIDATION
Safety Goal Validation on Vehicle"] J --> K["CONFIRMATION MEASURES
Independent Safety Audit"] K -.->|Feedback if hazard missed| B A -.->|Functional Safety Assessment| K style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style I fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style J fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f style K fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Phase-to-Verification Cross-Linking ```mermaid graph LR P1["ITEM DEFINITION"] -->|traceability link| V1["CONFIRMATION REVIEW"] P2["HARA"] -->|traceability link| V2["FUNCTIONAL SAFETY AUDIT"] P3["FSC"] -->|traceability link| V3["VALIDATION TESTING"] P4["TSC"] -->|traceability link| V4["SYSTEM TESTING"] P5["HW-SW DESIGN"] -->|traceability link| V5["INTEGRATION TESTING"] P6["IMPLEMENTATION"] -->|traceability link| V6["UNIT TESTING"] style P1 fill:#bbdefb style P2 fill:#ffe0b2 style P3 fill:#f8bbd0 style P4 fill:#e1bee7 style P5 fill:#c8e6c9 style P6 fill:#fff59d style V1 fill:#bbdefb style V2 fill:#ffe0b2 style V3 fill:#f8bbd0 style V4 fill:#e1bee7 style V5 fill:#c8e6c9 style V6 fill:#fff59d ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Verification Levels (Abstraction Ladder) ```mermaid graph TD L1["LEVEL 1 REQUIREMENT
Safety Goal - SG"] --> L2["LEVEL 2 REQUIREMENT
Functional Safety Requirement - FSR"] L2 --> L3["LEVEL 3 REQUIREMENT
Technical Safety Requirement - TSR"] L3 --> L4["LEVEL 4 REQUIREMENT
Hardware and Software Detailed Design"] L4 --> L5["LEVEL 5 REQUIREMENT
Code and Hardware Realization"] L5 --> T1["LEVEL 5 TEST
Unit Test"] T1 --> T2["LEVEL 4 TEST
Integration Test"] T2 --> T3["LEVEL 3 TEST
System Test"] T3 --> T4["LEVEL 2 TEST
Vehicle Validation"] T4 --> T5["LEVEL 1 TEST
Confirmation Review"] style L1 fill:#ffcdd2 style L2 fill:#f8bbd0 style L3 fill:#e1bee7 style L4 fill:#d1c4e9 style L5 fill:#c5cae9 style T1 fill:#c5cae9 style T2 fill:#d1c4e9 style T3 fill:#e1bee7 style T4 fill:#f8bbd0 style T5 fill:#ffcdd2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Reading Note:** In all three diagrams, the **leftward vertical descent** mirrors the **rightward vertical ascent**. Each horizontal arrow represents a **bidirectional traceability link**, which is mandatory for ISO 26262 compliance. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *Define the V-Model as applied in the Functional Safety lifecycle (ISO 26262). Mention any two advantages of using the V-Model over a traditional waterfall model.* **Model Answer:** > The V-Model is a graphical development lifecycle model that pairs each specification phase (left descending arm) with a corresponding verification phase (right ascending arm). In ISO 26262, it is the prescribed framework for safety-critical E/E system development, ensuring traceability from item definition down to implementation and back up to validation. > > **Advantages over waterfall:** > 1. **Early defect detection** — verification activities are planned in parallel with specification, not after development. > 2. **Traceability** — each requirement on the left has an explicit test on the right, simplifying safety case preparation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** *List the four phases on the left (specification) arm and the corresponding four phases on the right (verification) arm of the functional safety V-Cycle.* **Model Answer:** > > **Left Arm (Specification):** > 1. Item Definition > 2. Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) > 3. Functional Safety Concept (FSC) > 4. Technical Safety Concept (TSC) / HW-SW Design > > **Right Arm (Verification):** > 1. Unit Testing / Integration Testing > 2. System Testing > 3. Vehicle Validation > 4. Confirmation Measures (Confirmation Review, Functional Safety Audit, Functional Safety Assessment) > > *(Note: The exact pairing of levels is debated; KTU accepts the standard 4–5 level mapping shown above.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper (Model)]** **Q3.** *(a)* Explain the V-Model of Functional Safety in detail as prescribed by ISO 26262. Describe each phase on the left arm and the corresponding verification phase on the right arm. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Consider an Electronic Stability Control (ESC) system for a passenger car. The hazard analysis identifies the event "Loss of braking assistance during emergency braking" with the triplet: - Severity $S = S3$ - Exposure $E = E4$ - Controllability $C = C3$ Determine the **ASIL** for this safety goal and show how this ASIL propagates through the four levels of the V-Model. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q3(a):** > [!NOTE] > **Answer Structure:** The model answer below is segmented by V-phase for maximum scoring in KTU valuation. **1. Item Definition (Left Arm – Top):** The first phase defines the system's function, its boundaries, interfaces with other systems, and the operational environment. *Example:* For ESC, the item is the brake-by-wire system comprising ECU, sensors, and actuators. **2. Hazard Analysis & Risk Assessment (HARA):** Systematically identifies hazardous events (e.g., unintended brake application) and classifies them using the triplet $(S, E, C)$. Outputs: Safety Goals with ASIL classifications. **3. Functional Safety Concept (FSC):** Translates each Safety Goal into **Functional Safety Requirements (FSR)** — what the system must do without describing how. E.g., "ESC shall detect wheel slip within 50 ms." **4. Technical Safety Concept (TSC):** Decomposes FSRs into measurable **Technical Safety Requirements (TSR)** — defines specific sensor accuracies, sampling rates, FTTI, diagnostic coverage, and hardware metrics (SPFM, LFM, PMHF). **5. System & SW Design + Implementation (Bottom of V):** Defines HW-SW partitioning, interfaces, algorithms, and writes the actual production code (e.g., C code for the ABS modulator logic). **6. Right Arm Verification Phases (Ascending):** - **Unit Testing** ↔ Implementation: Each C function tested with white-box test cases. - **Integration Testing** ↔ HW-SW Design: ECU + sensor + actuator integrated, interface conformance tested. - **System Testing** ↔ TSC: TSRs verified via HIL rig, fault injection (open-circuit, short-circuit simulations). - **Vehicle Validation** ↔ FSC: Real vehicle tests on proving grounds (e.g., moose test, split-µ braking). - **Confirmation Measures** ↔ HARA: Independent reviewer confirms the Safety Case is complete and the original risk reduction was achieved. --- **Model Solution for Q3(b):** **Step 1 — Determine ASIL:** Given $S = S3$, $E = E4$, $C = C3$, we apply the ASIL determination rule: $$ \text{ASIL}(S3, E4, C3) \;\to\; \boxed{D} $$ **Step 2 — Safety Goal (Top of Left Arm):** $$ \text{SG}_{1}: \text{“Loss of braking assistance must be prevented during emergency braking”}, \quad \text{ASIL = D} $$ **Step 3 — Functional Safety Concept (FSR):** $$ \text{FSR}_{1.1}: \text{“Brake pressure must be maintained for at least 200 ms after sensor loss”}, \quad \text{ASIL = D (inherited)} $$ $$ \text{FSR}_{1.2}: \text{“System must switch to safe state within FTTI = 100 ms”}, \quad \text{ASIL = D} $$ **Step 4 — Technical Safety Concept (TSR):** $$ \text{TSR}_{1.1.1}: \text{“Pressure sensor must be read redundantly at 100 Hz with cross-check”}, \quad \text{ASIL = D} $$ $$ \text{TSR}_{1.1.2}: \text{“Diagnostic coverage must achieve SPFM} \geq 99\%, \text{ LFM} \geq 90\%\text{”}, \quad \text{ASIL = D} $$ **Step 5 — HW / SW Design:** - Hardware: Dual-channel pressure sensor, lockstep CPU cores (e.g., Aurix TC3xx). - Software: Plausibility check function with built-in self-test (BIST). - ASIL D inherited by the SW unit. **Step 6 — Verification Mapping (Right Arm):** | Left Arm Phase | Right Arm Verification | | :--- | :--- | | SG (ASIL D) | Vehicle Validation Test Report | | FSR (ASIL D) | HIL simulation proving FTTI | | TSR (ASIL D) | System Test with Fault Injection | | HW-SW Design | Integration Test | | SW Code | Unit Test | **Conclusion:** The ASIL D classification propagates downward and is verified upward at each level — preserving the safety invariant through the entire V-Cycle. --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper (Model)]** **Q4.** *(a)* Compare and contrast the V-Model and the traditional Waterfall model in the context of functional safety development. *(7 marks)* *(b)* With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the four-tier abstraction of requirements in the V-Cycle and explain the corresponding verification activity for each tier. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q4(a):** | Aspect | Waterfall Model | V-Model (Functional Safety) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Structure** | Linear, sequential | Sequential with explicit **parallel verification pairs** | | **Testing** | After all development complete | **Parallel, level-matched testing** | | **Defect Detection** | Late, expensive | **Early at the same abstraction level** | | **Traceability** | Loose, manual | **Mandatory, bidirectional** (each req ↔ test) | | **Compliance** | Not compliant with ISO 26262 | **Fully compliant** with ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 | | **Confirmation Measures** | Optional, ad-hoc | **Mandatory at every phase** (CR, FSA, FSE) | | **Iteration Cost** | High (full restart required) | **Localized** (only the affected level restarts) | | **Safety Case** | Difficult to construct | **Directly constructible** from V-Model artifacts | **Conclusion:** The V-Model is **mandatory** for functional safety because waterfall cannot guarantee the **independent confirmation** required by safety standards. --- **Model Solution for Q4(b):** > [!NOTE] > **Marking Tip:** For full marks in KTU, students must draw the diagram AND explain each tier explicitly. **Refer to the Mermaid "Phase-to-Verification Cross-Linking" diagram above.** The four-tier abstraction is: **Tier 1 — Safety Goal (SG):** Highest-level natural-language safety requirement (e.g., "prevent unintended acceleration"). **Verification:** Confirmation Review + Vehicle Validation. **Tier 2 — Functional Safety Requirement (FSR):** System-level function (e.g., "throttle must close within 100 ms"). **Verification:** HIL-based Validation Test. **Tier 3 — Technical Safety Requirement (TSR):** Hardware/software-level measurable specs (e.g., "redundant throttle position sensor, SPFM ≥ 99%"). **Verification:** System Test + Fault Injection. **Tier 4 — Hardware-Software Implementation:** Concrete circuit schematics and C-code modules. **Verification:** Unit Test (white-box) and Integration Test (interface conformance). **Key insight:** Each tier on the left has a **twin tier on the right**, and together they form a closed loop of specification-verification. This loop is the structural core of the V-Model. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write only the diagram and forget to describe the **right-side verification phases**. This loses up to **4 of 14 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the V-Model with a generic SDLC model. Always tie your answer back to **ISO 26262 / IEC 61508** to score high. > - **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical ASIL questions, students write "ASIL D" without showing the **inheritance propagation** down the V. Show **all 4–5 levels** explicitly. > - **Common Mistake 4:** In comparison questions, students compare Waterfall vs. Agile instead of Waterfall vs. V-Model. Read the question carefully. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] The **V-Model** is the prescribed safety lifecycle in **ISO 26262** and **IEC 61508**. - [x] **Left arm = specification/refinement** (Item Def → HARA → FSC → TSC → HW/SW Design). - [x] **Bottom of V = implementation** (code + circuits). - [x] **Right arm = verification/validation** (Unit → Integration → System → Vehicle Validation → Confirmation Measures). - [x] Each **left-side phase pairs with a right-side phase** at the same abstraction level. - [x] **ASIL** is determined from **$S \times E \times C$** triplets in HARA. - [x] ASIL propagates **downward** through the V (Safety Goal → FSR → TSR → SW unit). - [x] Verification activity **climbs upward**, matching each specification level. - [x] **Confirmation Measures** (CR, FSA, FSE) are independent reviews and are **mandatory** at each V-phase. - [x] The V-Model guarantees **traceability**, which is the foundation of the **Safety Case**. - [x] For **ASIL D**, the most stringent requirements apply: SPFM $\geq 99\%$, LFM $\geq 90\%$, PMHF $< 10^{-8}$ h$^{-1}$. - [x] **FTTI** = $t_{\text{detect}} + t_{\text{react}}$ must be strictly less than $t_{\text{hazard}}$. - [x] The V-Model is **not a generic software engineering model** — it is specifically mandated for safety-critical E/E systems. - [x] Always mention the **standard reference** (ISO 26262 / IEC 61508) for full marks in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Software Design Documentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Design Documentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Design Documentation** in the context of Functional Safety is the formally structured, traceable, and verifiable set of documents that describe the **software architecture**, **module-level design**, **interfaces**, **data structures**, and **design constraints** of safety-critical software, prepared in accordance with the requirements of **IEC 61508** (Part 3), **IEC 61511**, **ISO 26262** (Part 6), or **IEC 62061**, as applicable to the Safety Integrity Level (**SIL**) or Automotive Safety Integrity Level (**ASIL**) of the system under development.

It is the *executable contract* between the system-level safety requirements and the source code that will eventually be compiled into the Safety Instrumented System (SIS) / Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE) system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** IEC 61508-3:2010, Clause **7.4 (Software design and development – Software architecture design)** and Clause **7.5 (Software design and development – Software system design)** explicitly mandate the production and maintenance of software design documentation across all SILs, with increasing rigor from SIL 1 → SIL 4.

### 1.2 Intuition: The Architectural Analogy

Imagine you are building a **fire-resistant high-rise apartment** in a seismically active zone (think of it as a SIL 3 safety system). The city building inspector (the certification body like TÜV or UL) will not give you an occupancy permit simply because you say *"the building is safe."*

They will demand a complete set of **architectural blueprints**:
1. A **structural drawing** showing how load-bearing columns connect to beams (the **Software Architecture Document** — SAD).
2. A **detailed drawing** of each individual apartment showing wiring, plumbing, and gas line routes (the **Software Module Design Document** — SMDD).
3. A **door and window schedule** listing every interface — door to corridor, window to outside — with their fire ratings (the **Interface Control Document** — ICD).
4. A **fire escape plan** showing how occupants exit during an emergency (the **Safety Manual / User Documentation**).

**Software Design Documentation** is exactly this — but for embedded safety software. It must prove to the assessor, **before a single line of code is written**, that:
- The design can satisfy the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS).
- Faults can be detected and controlled within the **diagnostic coverage (DC)** targets.
- No single fault (or credible dual fault) can lead to a loss of the safety function.

### 1.3 Why Documentation Carries Functional-Safety Weight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In functional safety, **"If it is not documented, it does not exist."** This is a foundational principle enforced by all major functional safety standards. The assessor's mantra is: **No document → No evidence → No claim of safety.**

The documentation must demonstrate three cardinal properties:
| # | Property | Functional Safety Meaning |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Traceability** | Every design element can be traced **down** from the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS) and **up** to the source code and test cases. |
| 2 | **Verifiability** | Every claim in the document can be objectively checked against an acceptance criterion (e.g., DC ≥ 60%). |
| 3 | **Maintainability** | The document can be updated as the design evolves, with formal change-impact analysis. |

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Used

- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Expressed as a **percentage (0 – 100 %)** for each failure mode.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):** Expressed as a **percentage (0 – 100 %)**.
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** for low-demand mode.
- **Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)** for high-demand / continuous mode.
- **Architectural Constraint:** Type A / Type B element classification.
- **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT):** Integer (0, 1, 2…).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Software Design Documentation

Functional safety standards prescribe a **two-tier hierarchy** of design documentation, mirroring the V-Model's left-descending arm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — Software Architecture Design (SAD):** Defines *what* the major software components are, *how* they interact, *which* fault-detection mechanisms apply, and *whether* the architecture supports the required SIL/ASIL.
>
> **Tier 2 — Software System / Detailed Design (SSDD):** Defines *how* each component (module) achieves its function — algorithms, data structures, internal interfaces, error handling, and state machines.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown of the Documentation Process

**Step 1 — Establish the Software Safety Requirements Specification (SSRS)**
- Source: System-level Safety Requirements Specification (SRS) from IEC 61508-2 / ISO 26262-4.
- Output: Software-level safety requirements (each tagged with SIL/ASIL).
- **Why:** Without this anchor, design documents become "orphan" specifications.

**Step 2 — Develop the Software Architecture Document (SAD)**
- Decompose the software into *subsystems / partitions / modules*.
- Assign each partition a **Software Safety Integrity Level (SSIL)**.
- Define **defensive partitioning**, **redundancy**, **diversity**, and **fault-detection** mechanisms.
- State the **diagnostic coverage** claimed for each fault class.

**Step 3 — Refine to the Software Detailed Design Document (SDDD / SSDD)**
- For each module, specify: inputs, outputs, internal state, algorithms, exception handling, and failure reaction.
- Reference the **Module Test Specification (MTS)** — bidirectional traceability is mandatory.

**Step 4 — Perform Design Reviews (Formal Technical Reviews)**
- Walkthroughs, inspections, and FMEA at the architecture level.
- Output: review minutes, action items, and sign-off.

**Step 5 — Update Tool Qualifications and Coding Standards References**
- List all CASE / modeling / documentation tools and their **TCL (Tool Confidence Level)** per IEC 61508-4 Annex B.

### 2.3 KTU Reference Matrix — Mandatory Content of Software Design Documents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield exam summary. Memorize the column headings — KTU examiners frequently ask "List the contents of the Software Architecture Document."

| Document Section | Mandatory Content (per IEC 61508-3 §7.4 & §7.5) | Purpose / Exam Hook |
|------------------|--------------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **1. Scope and Purpose** | Identification of the E/E/PE safety-related system, version, author | Establishes document baseline |
| **2. Normative References** | Standards used (IEC 61508, ISO 26262, IEC 61131-3, MISRA-C) | Justifies methodology choice |
| **3. Definitions & Abbreviations** | SIL, ASIL, SFF, DC, CCF, HFT, MTTR | Common in 3-mark questions |
| **4. Software Safety Requirements Traceability** | SRS → SSRS → Architecture → Module → Code matrix | Shows "no orphan requirements" |
| **5. Architecture Description** | Block diagrams, partition boundaries, data flow, control flow | Carries 7–14 marks in ESE |
| **6. Defensive Design Mechanisms** | Watchdog, range checks, plausibility, dual-channel, redundancy | Key for SIL 2–4 |
| **7. Interface Specifications** | External (hardware, user, network) and internal (module-to-module) | Often missed by students |
| **8. Failure Behavior & Error Handling** | Failure modes → diagnostic → safe state transition | Directly assessed in FMEA |
| **9. Diagnostic Coverage Claims** | Quantitative DC values per failure class with justification | Often asked as a numerical question |
| **10. Tool & Language Justification** | Compiler, linker, configuration tool with TCL rating | Mandatory from SIL 2 upward |
| **11. Design Constraints & Coding Standards** | MISRA-C rules subset, no dynamic memory in SIL 3/4 | Common viva question |
| **12. Review and Approval Records** | Signatures, dates, version history | Closes the documentation loop |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Process Industries (IEC 61511):** Used to certify Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), Burner Management Systems (BMS), and High-Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS) in oil & gas.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Mandated for ADAS, brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, battery management in EVs.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Software Design Document is a regulatory submission artifact for FDA / EU MDR.
- **Rail (EN 50128):** Drives the Software Design Description (SDD) and Software Architecture Specification (SAS).

### 2.5 Numerical Targets Linked to SIL

| Safety Integrity Level | Required DC (per IEC 61508-2, Table 2) | Typical Architectural Demand |
|------------------------|----------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **SIL 1** | DC ≥ 60 % | Single channel with self-test |
| **SIL 2** | DC ≥ 60 – 90 % | Single or dual channel, voted |
| **SIL 3** | DC ≥ 90 % | Dual channel (1oo2 or 2oo3) |
| **SIL 4** | DC ≥ 99 % | Dual, diverse, voted |

> [!NOTE]
> The software design documentation must **explicitly state** which DC value is being targeted for each diagnostic function. A documented claim without justification is grounds for rejection by the certification body.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Coverage Derivations

### 3.1 Analytical Framework — From SRS to Coded Module

Functional safety documentation is governed by the **V-Model**. The left arm decomposes requirements progressively, and the right arm verifies each level against the corresponding document. Software design documentation resides on the **left arm**, between the *Software Safety Requirements Specification (SSRS)* and the *Source Code*.

Let $\mathcal{R}$ denote the set of all software safety requirements, partitioned as:

$$\mathcal{R} \;=\; \big\{\, r_1,\; r_2,\; \dots,\; r_n \,\big\}$$

Each requirement $r_i$ is decomposed into a set of architectural components $\mathcal{C}_i$ and subsequently into modules $\mathcal{M}_{i,j}$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Diagnostic Coverage (DC)

The **Diagnostic Coverage** of a software diagnostic function is the ratio of detected dangerous failures to the total number of dangerous failures that the diagnostic *can* be reasonably expected to detect.

**Step 1 — Define the universe of failure modes.**
Let $\mathcal{F}_d$ be the set of all dangerous failure modes in the module. Then:

$$\mathcal{F}_d \;=\; \big\{\, f_1,\; f_2,\; \dots,\; f_k \,\big\}$$

**Step 2 — Define the detected subset.**
Let $\mathcal{F}_{d,\text{detected}} \subseteq \mathcal{F}_d$ be the subset of dangerous failures detected by the diagnostic function $D$:

$$\mathcal{F}_{d,\text{detected}} \;=\; \Big\{\, f \in \mathcal{F}_d \;\big\vert\; \text{diagnostic } D \text{ raises an alarm for } f \,\Big\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**
By definition (IEC 61508-4, Clause 3.6.7):

$$DC \;=\; \frac{\vert \mathcal{F}_{d,\text{detected}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert} \;\times\; 100\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Justification requirement (non-trivial).**
The Software Design Document must tabulate every $f \in \mathcal{F}_d$ and indicate whether $D$ detects it. A typical documentation excerpt:

| Failure Mode $f$ | Cause | Effect | Detected by $D$? | DC Contribution |
|------------------|-------|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Stuck-at-0 on ADC input | Solder bridge | Wrong sensor read | Yes (plausibility) | 1 / k |
| Corrupted RAM cell | Single Event Upset | Wrong variable | Yes (RAM test) | 1 / k |
| Division-by-zero trap | Compiler bug | Exception | Partially | 0.5 / k |

The **aggregate DC** for the diagnostic function becomes:

$$DC_{\text{agg}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert}\,\sum_{i=1}^{k}\, w_i, \qquad w_i \in \{0,\; 0.5,\; 1\}$$

where $w_i$ is the credit weight assigned to failure $f_i$ by the standards (often only $0$ or $1$, with $0.5$ allowed for *partial detection*).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)

The **Safe Failure Fraction** links the hardware architecture to the claimed SIL via the formula:

$$SFF \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$$

where:
- $\lambda_{\text{total}}$ is the total failure rate of the channel (per hour),
- $\lambda_{DD}$ is the dangerous-detected failure rate,
- All rates are dangerous-undetected rate $\lambda_{DU}$ excluded from the numerator (they are subtracted from the whole).

Expanding:

$$SFF \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{SD} \;+\; \lambda_{SU} \;+\; \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{SD} \;+\; \lambda_{SU} \;+\; \lambda_{DD} \;+\; \lambda_{DU}}$$

with:
- $\lambda_{SD}$ — safe detected,
- $\lambda_{SU}$ — safe undetected,
- $\lambda_{DD}$ — dangerous detected,
- $\lambda_{DU}$ — dangerous undetected.

> [!NOTE]
> The software design document must declare the **SFF target** the hardware is intended to meet. If $SFF \geq 60\,\%$ for Type B elements with HFT = 0, then SIL 1 is achievable; for SIL 3, typically $SFF \geq 90\,\%$.

### 3.4 Architectural Constraint Derivation — Route 1H vs Route 2H

IEC 61508-2, Tables 2 and 3, prescribe two **proven-in-use routes**:

- **Route 1H** (Hardware architectural constraint) — uses HFT and SFF.
- **Route 2H** (Hardware failure rates from field data) — uses $\lambda_{DU}$ thresholds.

For software architecture, the equivalent *route* is **Route 1S** (systematic capability) — proven by design rigor, documentation, and testing. The systematic capability directly traces back to the quality of the Software Design Documentation.

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Example — DC Calculation

> A safety-critical module has the following failure inventory (from its FMEA in the design document):

| Failure | Type | Detected? |
|---------|------|-----------|
| $f_1$ — Watchdog timer overflow missed | Dangerous | Yes (full) |
| $f_2$ — Stack overflow | Dangerous | Yes (full) |
| $f_3$ — Heap corruption | Dangerous | No |
| $f_4$ — Pointer out of bounds | Dangerous | Yes (partial, 0.5 credit) |
| $f_5$ — Uninitialised variable | Dangerous | Yes (full) |

**Step 1 — Enumerate $\mathcal{F}_d$:**

$$\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert \;=\; 5$$

**Step 2 — Apply credit weights:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \;=\; 1 \;+\; 1 \;+\; 0 \;+\; 0.5 \;+\; 1 \;=\; 3.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute DC:**

$$DC \;=\; \frac{3.5}{5} \;\times\; 100\,\% \;=\; 70\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Map to SIL:**
Per IEC 61508-2, Table 2, $DC = 70\,\%$ with HFT = 0 and Type B hardware → satisfies **SIL 1**, but **not** SIL 2. To upgrade to SIL 2, the design document must specify an additional diagnostic to convert $f_3$ or $f_4$ to fully detected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram — Software Design Documentation Hierarchy (V-Model Anchor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRS["System Safety Requirements Specification"] --> SSRS["Software Safety Requirements Spec"]
    SSRS --> SAD["Software Architecture Document SAD"]
    SAD --> SSDD["Software System Detailed Design SSDD"]
    SSDD --> SRC["Source Code Modules"]
    SRC --> UNI["Unit Test"]
    UNI --> INT["Integration Test"]
    INT --> SWP["Software Validation Test"]
    SWP --> SYS["System Validation"]

    SAD -.Trace Forward.-> SRC
    SRC -.Trace Backward.-> SAD
    SAD -.Trace Forward.-> SSDD
    SSDD -.Trace Backward.-> SAD

    SRS_DOC["Design Review Checklist"] -. Verifies .-> SAD
    DOC_TOOLS["Tool Qualification Records"] -. Supports .-> SSDD
    FMEA["Software FMEA"] -. Informs .-> SAD
    CODING["Coding Standard e.g. MISRA C"] -. Constrains .-> SRC
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU exam-ready explanation):**

1. The **central vertical column** is the left-arm V-Model: each document decomposes into the next.
2. The **horizontal arrows at the bottom** are the right-arm verification activities.
3. The **dashed diagonal arrows** denote **bi-directional traceability** — every line of code must trace up to a module, which traces to the architecture, which traces to the SRS.
4. The **side boxes** (Coding Standard, FMEA, Tool Records) are *supporting documents* that the design documentation must reference.

### 4.2 Diagram — Internal Anatomy of a Software Architecture Document

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TITLE["Title Page Version Approval"] --> SCOPE["Scope Purpose"]
    SCOPE --> REFS["Normative References"]
    REFS --> DEFS["Definitions Abbreviations"]
    DEFS --> REQMAT["Requirements Traceability Matrix"]
    REQMAT --> ARCHDESC["Architecture Description Block Diagrams"]
    ARCHDESC --> PARTITION["Defensive Partitioning Strategy"]
    PARTITION --> DIAG["Diagnostic Functions and DC Claims"]
    DIAG --> INTERFACE["Internal and External Interface Definitions"]
    INTERFACE --> REDUND["Redundancy Diversity Voting Logic"]
    REDUND --> FMEA["Failure Modes Effects Analysis Summary"]
    FMEA --> TOOL["Tool Qualification Summary"]
    TOOL --> CODESTD["Coding Standard Subset Reference"]
    CODESTD --> REVIEW["Design Review Records Sign-off"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> When answering a 14-mark design-document question in the KTU ESE, structure your answer along the columns of this diagram. Examiners reward *coverage* as much as *correctness*.

### 4.3 Diagram — Documentation Flow Across the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CONCEPT["Concept Phase IEC 61508 1"] --> OVERALL["Overall Safety Lifecycle"]
    OVERALL --> SYSREQ["System Realisation E E PE 2"]
    SYSREQ --> SOFT["Software Realisation 3"]
    SOFT --> OP["Operation Maintenance 5"]
    OP --> MOD["Modification Retrofit 6"]

    SOFT --> SADGEN["SAD Generation"]
    SOFT --> SSDDGEN["SSDD Generation"]
    SADGEN --> AUDIT["Independent Functional Safety Assessment"]
    SSDDGEN --> AUDIT
    AUDIT --> CERT["Certification by TUV UL BV"]
```

**Interpretation:** Notice that the design documents (SAD and SSDD) are generated **before** code exists, and they are independently assessed by a Functional Safety Assessor (FSA) before certification can be granted. This is the architectural reason why KTU questions stress *"What must the SAD contain?"* rather than *"How do you code the safety function?"*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Software Design Documentation in the context of functional safety. List any three mandatory contents of a Software Architecture Document (SAD) as per IEC 61508-3.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Software Design Documentation in functional safety refers to the formally structured set of documents — comprising the Software Architecture Document (SAD) and the Software System Detailed Design Document (SSDD) — that describe the decomposition of the safety software into components, their interfaces, diagnostic mechanisms, and failure-handling behaviour, prepared in compliance with IEC 61508-3 (or ISO 26262-6) and capable of being traced to the Safety Requirements Specification and to the source code.

**Three mandatory contents of SAD (3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks):**
1. A description of the **software architecture** including block diagrams, partitioning, and data/control flow.
2. A **requirements traceability matrix** mapping each SSRS requirement to the architectural component(s) that satisfy it.
3. The **diagnostic coverage (DC)** claims and the rationale for each claimed value, per failure class.

**Examiner's valuation key:** Full credit only if the student cites the standard clause (IEC 61508-3 §7.4). Generic answers without standard reference lose 0.5 marks.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the Software Architecture Document (SAD) and the Software System Detailed Design Document (SSDD). Which one is generated first?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SAD | SSDD |
|---------|-----|------|
| Purpose | Describes *what* the major components are and how they interact | Describes *how* each module achieves its function internally |
| Level of detail | Coarse-grained (subsystem / partition level) | Fine-grained (algorithm, data structure, state machine) |
| Audience | Safety assessor, project lead, system architect | Module designer, coder, unit tester |
| Standard clause | IEC 61508-3 §7.4 | IEC 61508-3 §7.5 |
| Output artifact | SAD (often a single document) | SSDD (one per module or per partition) |
| Order in lifecycle | **Generated first** | Generated after SAD is approved |

**Order of generation (1 mark):**
The **SAD is generated first**, because the SSDD refines the architectural components established in the SAD. The cascade is: SRS → SSRS → **SAD** → SSDD → Source Code.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the hierarchical structure of Software Design Documentation as mandated by IEC 61508-3. Draw a labelled block diagram showing the two-tier hierarchy (SAD and SSDD) along with the documents that sit immediately above and below them in the V-Model.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Context within IEC 61508-3 (2 marks):**
IEC 61508-3, Clause 7 "Software design and development," prescribes a phased approach. Clause 7.4 governs software architecture design; Clause 7.5 governs software system design. The standard requires that documentation be produced **before** implementation begins, with rigorous review at each phase boundary.

**2. Two-tier hierarchy explained (3 marks):**

- **Tier 1 — Software Architecture Document (SAD):** Produced under Clause 7.4. Identifies the major software partitions, the interfaces between them, the redundancy structure (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3), the diagnostic strategy, and the freedom-from-interference argument.
- **Tier 2 — Software System / Detailed Design Document (SSDD):** Produced under Clause 7.5. For each module, defines inputs, outputs, internal state, algorithms, data structures, error-recovery paths, and the safety reaction when a fault is detected.

**3. Labelled block diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["System Safety Requirements Specification SRS Clause 7 2"] --> A2["Software Safety Requirements Specification SSRS Clause 7 3"]
    A2 --> A3["Software Architecture Document SAD Clause 7 4"]
    A3 --> A4["Software System Detailed Design SSDD Clause 7 5"]
    A4 --> A5["Source Code Modules Clause 7 6"]
    A5 --> A6["Module Unit Test Clause 7 7"]
    A6 --> A7["Software Integration Test Clause 7 8"]
```

**Examiner's note:** Students must **label the clauses**. An unlabeled diagram carries only 1 mark.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SIL 2 safety function uses a single-channel (1oo1) Type B microcontroller. The FMEA identifies 8 dangerous failure modes; 6 are fully detected by the self-test diagnostic, 1 is undetected, and 1 is partially detected (50 % credit). Calculate the Diagnostic Coverage and determine whether the design meets the SIL 2 requirement per IEC 61508-2, Table 2. State the minimum additional action required if it does not.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Enumerate the failure universe (1 mark):**

$$\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert \;=\; 8$$

**Step 2 — Compute the credit sum (2 marks):**
- 6 failures × weight $1.0$ = $6.0$
- 1 failure × weight $0$ = $0$
- 1 failure × weight $0.5$ = $0.5$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \;=\; 6.0 \;+\; 0 \;+\; 0.5 \;=\; 6.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute DC (2 marks):**

$$DC \;=\; \frac{6.5}{8} \;\times\; 100\,\% \;=\; 81.25\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare to SIL 2 requirement (1 mark):**
Per IEC 61508-2 Table 2, for Type B hardware, HFT = 0, the minimum DC for **SIL 2** is **60 %**. Since $81.25\,\% > 60\,\%$, the DC requirement is met.

**Step 5 — Architectural and SFF check (1 mark):**
SIL 2 with Type B and HFT = 0 also requires **SFF ≥ 60 %** to **90 %**. The Software Design Document must additionally demonstrate that the hardware SFF is documented and within the SIL 2 envelope; otherwise, the certificate cannot be issued.

**Minimum additional action:** *Either* upgrade HFT to 1 (dual-channel) *or* eliminate the partially detected failure mode by replacing the diagnostic with a fully detected mechanism, *and* include the SFF calculation in the Software Architecture Document.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently compute DC correctly but **forget to cross-check the SFF requirement** for Type B elements. The KTU examiner's key explicitly allocates 1 mark for the SFF cross-check. Losing this mark brings you from 7 to 6 — and from full grade to one grade lower.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of bi-directional traceability in Software Design Documentation. With a suitable example, explain how a traceability matrix is constructed between (i) SSRS → Architecture, (ii) Architecture → Module, and (iii) Module → Test Case.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Role of bi-directional traceability (2 marks):**
Bi-directional traceability ensures that (a) every safety requirement is satisfied by at least one design element (**forward trace**) and (b) every design element is justified by at least one requirement (**backward trace**). This is the primary evidence the assessor uses to confirm that no "orphan" requirements and no "orphan" code exist. It is mandated by IEC 61508-3 §7.5.2 and ISO 26262-6 §7.

**2. Constructing the traceability matrix — Example (5 marks):**

Consider a SIL 2 over-temperature protection function in an industrial furnace controller. The matrix is presented below.

| SSRS Req. ID | Requirement Summary | Architecture Component | Module | Test Case |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|--------|-----------|
| **SSRS-OT-01** | Detect temp ≥ 450 °C within 1 s | Diagnostic partition `TEMP_MON` | `M_AdcRead`, `M_LimitCheck` | TC-OT-01, TC-OT-02 |
| **SSRS-OT-02** | De-energize heater on trip | Actuator partition `HEATER_CTRL` | `M_HeaterOff`, `M_RelayDrv` | TC-OT-03 |
| **SSRS-OT-03** | Latch trip until manual reset | State machine `TRIP_STATE` | `M_Latch`, `M_Reset` | TC-OT-04 |
| **SSRS-OT-04** | Self-test ADC every 100 ms | Diagnostic service `DIAG_ADC` | `M_AdcSelftest` | TC-OT-05 |

**Forward trace** (left → right): Each row flows from SSRS to test case, proving "every requirement is verified."
**Backward trace** (right → left): Every test case must trace back to an SSRS ID, proving "no test without a purpose."

The Software Architecture Document must include this matrix as **Annex B** and the SSDD must reference it in **Section 6 (Traceability)**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A safety-critical module has the failure inventory shown below. The hardware is classified as **Type B**.

| Failure $f_i$ | Dangerous? | Detected? |
|---------------|------------|-----------|
| $f_1$ | Yes | Yes (full) |
| $f_2$ | Yes | Yes (full) |
| $f_3$ | Yes | Yes (full) |
| $f_4$ | Yes | No |
| $f_5$ | Yes | Yes (partial — 50 %) |
| $f_6$ | Yes | Yes (full) |
| $f_7$ | Yes | No |

(i) Calculate the Diagnostic Coverage. (ii) For a 1oo1 architecture with HFT = 0, which SIL can be claimed per IEC 61508-2 Table 2? (iii) What redesign actions should be documented to upgrade to SIL 3?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) DC Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert \;=\; 7$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \;=\; 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0.5 + 1 + 0 \;=\; 4.5$$

$$DC \;=\; \frac{4.5}{7} \;\times\; 100\,\% \;=\; 64.29\,\%$$

**(ii) SIL achievable (2 marks):**
For Type B, HFT = 0: SIL 1 requires $DC \geq 60\,\%$; SIL 2 requires $DC \geq 60\,\%$ but with additional SFF constraints; SIL 3 typically demands $DC \geq 90\,\%$.
Since $DC = 64.29\,\%$, the architecture can support **SIL 1** comfortably. For SIL 2, an additional **SFF analysis** is required; if SFF ≥ 60 %, SIL 2 is achievable; if SFF < 60 %, the architecture fails SIL 2.

**(iii) Redesign actions for SIL 3 (3 marks) — to be documented in the SAD:**

1. **Upgrade hardware architecture** from 1oo1 to 1oo2 or 2oo2 with diagnostic, raising HFT to 1.
2. **Add a second diagnostic mechanism** to detect $f_4$ and $f_7$ (currently undetected), raising DC to ≥ 90 %.
3. **Replace the partial-detection mechanism** with a full-detection mechanism for $f_5$, raising its credit weight from 0.5 to 1.0.
4. **Perform a new SFF calculation**; for SIL 3, Type B, HFT = 1, the standard requires $SFF \geq 90\,\%$.
5. **Update the SAD, SSDD, FMEA, and traceability matrix** as part of formal change-control, and re-submit to the Functional Safety Assessor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often answer (i) and (ii) correctly but write a vague answer for (iii). The examiner's key requires **five explicit redesign actions** for full marks. Saying "improve diagnostics" without specifying *which* failure modes and *what mechanism* loses 2 of the 3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Software Design Documentation** is the **left-arm V-Model artifact** sitting between the SSRS and the source code. It is governed by IEC 61508-3 §7.4 (SAD) and §7.5 (SSDD).
- The documentation must be **generated before code is written** — it is the contract that the code is built against.
- **Bi-directional traceability** (SSRS ↔ Architecture ↔ Module ↔ Test) is the single most important quality criterion.
- The **SAD** captures partitioning, redundancy, diagnostic mechanisms, and DC claims.
- The **SSDD** captures per-module algorithms, data structures, error-handling, and safety reactions.
- **Diagnostic Coverage** is computed as $DC = \frac{\vert \mathcal{F}_{d,\text{detected}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{F}_d \vert} \times 100\,\%$.
- **SFF** is $SFF = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ and is mandatory for Type B elements.
- For SIL 2 → SIL 3 upgrade, both **architectural change (HFT)** and **DC improvement (≥ 90 %)** are typically required.
- Every software diagnostic must be **documented with rationale, claim, and verification evidence**; undocumented claims are invalid.
- **Tool qualification** (IEC 61508-4 Annex B) is part of the design documentation from SIL 2 upward.
- **Coding standards** (e.g., MISRA-C for C, MISRA-C++ for C++, IEC 61131-3 for PLCs) must be referenced in the design documents and enforced in the source.
- KTU exam tip: when a question asks *"Explain Software Design Documentation,"* always **structure** the answer around the V-Model and cite the IEC 61508-3 clauses. Unstructured answers lose marks even when factually correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation and Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Installation and Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Installation (in the context of Safety Instrumented Systems — SIS)

**Formal Definition (as per IEC 61511 / IEC 61508):**
Installation is the phase in the Safety Lifecycle where the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) components — sensors, logic solvers, and final elements — are physically mounted, wired, piped, and integrated into the plant's existing process infrastructure, strictly according to the safety requirements specification (SRS) and installation drawings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Installation is *not* just "putting equipment on a rack." It is a *documented, auditable* process where every cable, gland, conduit, pneumatic tube, and termination must be traceable to the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS). Any deviation must trigger a Management of Change (MOC) procedure.

## 1.2 Commissioning

**Formal Definition:**
Commissioning is the systematic testing of the installed SIS components, sub-systems, and integrated SIFs to verify that they operate as designed *before* the plant is brought into live operation. It includes loop checks, calibration verification, cause-and-effect matrix testing, and functional verification against the SRS.

## 1.3 Validation

**Formal Definition:**
Validation is the *confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence* that the Safety Instrumented System (SIS) and its individual Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) meet the requirements of the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS). It answers the question: **"Did we build the *right* system?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification vs. Validation — A Common Confusion**
> - **Verification** → "Did we build the system *right*?" (checking intermediate outputs against design specs at each phase)
> - **Validation** → "Did we build the *right* system?" (final end-to-end check against the SRS and risk reduction targets)

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Emergency Room" Model

Imagine a hospital's emergency response system. **Installation** is like physically setting up the defibrillators, oxygen lines, and alarm panels in the right rooms with the right wiring. **Commissioning** is like pressing each button to make sure the defibrillator actually delivers a shock and the oxygen valve actually opens. **Validation** is the final drill: a simulated patient collapse where the *entire chain* — sensor (BP monitor) → logic (nurse's brain) → actuator (crash cart wheels) — is timed and checked to meet the hospital's promised 90-second response. Only when the *whole chain* is proven to work in real conditions is the system "validated" for use.

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Proof Test Coverage (C)**: typically **0.6 to 0.95** depending on test thoroughness
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**: typically **8 hours** (industry default)
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**: must satisfy IEC 61511 Tables 2/3 minimum thresholds
- **Target PFDavg**: derived from the SIL target (SIL 1: ≥10⁻² to <10⁻¹, SIL 2: ≥10⁻³ to <10⁻², SIL 3: ≥10⁻⁴ to <10⁻³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
