> **Standard Reference:** All terminology follows **IEC 61511-1:2016** — *Functional safety — Safety instrumented systems for the process industry sector*. KTU examiners expect you to cite this standard for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SIS Safety Lifecycle (Phase 11 & 12 — IEC 61511)

The phases covered in this topic are:
- **Phase 11** → Installation
- **Phase 12** → Commissioning
- **Phase 13** → Validation (full SIS + SIF validation)
- **Phase 14** → Operation (transitions to next topic)

## 2.2 Installation Phase — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pre-Installation Review**
   - Verify that the site conditions (temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC, hazardous area classification) match the design specification.
   - Confirm the SRS is **frozen** (no pending engineering changes).

2. **Physical Mounting**
   - Sensors: installed in correct process line locations (e.g., flow transmitters on straight pipe runs of ≥10D upstream / 5D downstream).
   - Logic Solver: installed in dust-free, temperature-controlled panels with **SIL-rated** power supplies and proper segregation from basic process control.

3. **Wiring & Cabling**
   - Use **screened, armoured cables** with segregation from non-SIS cables (≥150 mm in parallel runs).
   - All terminations torqued to manufacturer specification.
   - Cable glands must match the IP rating (typically **IP54** for indoor, **IP65** for outdoor/hazardous).

4. **Documentation Check**
   - Cable schedules, loop diagrams, and termination drawings must be red-lined to "as-built" status.
   - All devices tagged with unique equipment numbers.

5. **Pre-Commissioning Checks**
   - Insulation resistance (megger) test on all cables.
   - Earth continuity test (<1 Ω for safety earth).
   - Visual inspection of all terminations.

## 2.3 Commissioning Phase — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Individual Component Calibration Verification** — every sensor and final element calibrated against a traceable standard (NABL/NPL).
2. **Loop Checks** — energise each input-output loop, simulate a process signal, confirm the final element responds.
3. **Cause-&-Effect Matrix Testing** — simulate every documented cause (e.g., high-high level) and verify the effect (e.g., close ESD valve XV-101).
4. **Logic Solver Functional Test** — exercise all inputs, confirm outputs match the application program.
5. **Fault Injection Testing** — short-circuit/open-circuit inputs to verify diagnostics detect the fault within the required diagnostic test interval.

## 2.4 Validation Phase — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **SIF-by-SIF Validation** — for *each* SIF, force a trip condition and confirm:
   - Correct sensor detection (within calibrated range)
   - Correct logic solver decision
   - Correct final element action (fully stroke valve in <required time)
   - Correct alarm annunciation in control room
2. **End-to-End SIS Validation** — confirm the SIS as a whole achieves the **overall SIL target**.
3. **PFDavg Calculation Verification** — calculated value must be ≤ the SIL target budget.
4. **Documentation Pack — The "Validation Dossier"**
   - Validation plan
   - Test result sheets (signed)
   - Calibration certificates
   - As-built drawings
   - PFDavg verification report
   - Outstanding punch-list items

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters (Engineering Utility):**
> A poorly installed SIS is a *latent* hazard. A sensor wired with the wrong polarity, a logic solver with a missing termination, or a solenoid valve installed without the correct bypass block can silently fail the system. Validation is the *last* opportunity to catch these errors *before* the plant accepts the safety responsibility. The cost of a validation error caught in Phase 13 is roughly **10× to 100× lower** than the same error caught in operation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability of Failure on Demand (average) | $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ | Dangerous undetected failure rate × channel equivalent downtime | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{DU}} \cdot C$ | Repair time factor | 8 hours |
| Channel Equivalent Downtime | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_i}{2} + MTTR$ | Half the proof test interval plus repair time | hours |
| Proof Test Coverage | $C$ | Fraction of dangerous failures detected by test | 0.6 – 0.95 |
| Overall SIF PFDavg (1oo2 architecture) | $PFD_{G,1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$ | Two-vote-one-out-of-two | dimensionless |
| Overall SIF PFDavg (2oo3 architecture) | $PFD_{G,2oo3} = 6 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$ | Three-vote-two-out-of-three | dimensionless |
| Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}}$ | IEC 61511 hardware constraint | 0.6 – 0.99 |
| Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ | Reciprocal of PFDavg | 10 to 100,000 |
| SIL to PFDavg mapping | $SIL\ 1: 10^{-2} \text{ to } 10^{-1}$ etc. | IEC 61511 Table 3 | per safety function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** When writing formulas in tables, never use the bare pipe symbol `|x|` for absolute value — it breaks markdown table syntax. Use `\vert x \vert` instead. This is a common KTU exam board requirement.

## 2.6 Proof Test Interval — The Validation Lever

The **proof test interval (Tᵢ)** is the most powerful parameter a validation engineer controls. The PFDavg scales *linearly* with Tᵢ. Halving Tᵢ (e.g., from 2 years to 1 year) **halves the PFDavg**, which may upgrade a SIL 2 SIF to comfortably meet target without redesigning hardware.

$$\Delta PFD_{avg} \propto \Delta T_i$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFDavg for a 1oo1 Architecture

We derive the *average* probability of failure on demand for a single-channel (1oo1) Safety Instrumented Function.

**Assumptions:**
- Components fail with constant dangerous undetected rate $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures per hour)
- Failed components are detected only at the proof test, which occurs every $T_i$ hours
- Repair is instantaneous and perfect (we relax this next)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous unavailability $PFD(t)$**
At any time $t$ after a successful proof test, the probability the channel is *dangerously failed* grows linearly:

$$PFD(t) \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} t} \approx \lambda_{DU} t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda_{DU} t\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof test interval**
We integrate the unavailability over one test interval and divide by $T_i$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \lambda_{DU} \, t \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral**

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T_i} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \left[\dfrac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^{T_i} = \dfrac{1}{T_i} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{T_i^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_i}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *ideal* 1oo1 formula assuming perfect, instantaneous repair. The more practical form includes Mean Time To Repair (MTTR), as derived next.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Channel Equivalent Downtime $t_{CE}$

**Step 1 — Recognition of the real-world factor**
In practice, repair takes time. During repair, the channel is *inherently* unavailable. This adds an *average* downtime of $MTTR$ to the unavailability window.

**Step 2 — Effective downtime decomposition**
The unavailability window is split into two parts:
- From a successful proof test, dangerous failures accumulate over an *average* period of $T_i / 2$ (uniform distribution of failure arrivals).
- After detection, the system is down for $MTTR$ hours during repair.

**Step 3 — Combine into the equivalent downtime**

$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_i}{2} + MTTR$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into PFDavg**

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \left( \dfrac{T_i}{2} + MTTR \right)$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style problem):**
Given $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr, $T_i = 8760$ hr (1 year), $MTTR = 8$ hr:

$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{8760}{2} + 8 = 4388 \text{ hr}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4388 = 2.194 \times 10^{-2}$$

This is **above** the SIL 1 lower bound (10⁻²), so this SIF would **fail** SIL 1. Reducing $T_i$ to 6 months (4380 hr) would give $PFD_{avg} = 1.097 \times 10^{-2}$, still borderline. The student must then either improve $\lambda_{DU}$ (better sensor) or reduce $T_i$ further.

## 3.3 Derivation of the 1oo2 Architecture PFDavg (Examination Favourite)

**Step 1 — Architecture description**
1oo2 means *one-out-of-two* — the system fails only if *both* channels fail dangerously.

**Step 2 — Probability both channels have failed**
For two independent channels, multiply individual PFDs:

$$PFD_{G,1oo2} \approx PFD_1 \times PFD_2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the 1oo1 expression for each channel**

$$PFD_{G,1oo2} \approx (\lambda_{DU} t_{CE}) \times (\lambda_{DU} t_{CE}) = \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$$

**Step 4 — Add the $\beta$-factor (common-cause failure)**
The $\beta$-factor accounts for the probability that *both* channels fail from the same root cause:

$$PFD_{G,1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$$

Typical $\beta = 0.05$ to 0.10 for IEC 61511 sensor assemblies.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PFD Comparison Across Architectures

Using $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $T_i = 8760$ hr, $MTTR = 8$ hr, $\beta = 0.10$:

| Architecture | Formula | PFDavg Value | SIL Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\lambda_{DU} t_{CE}$ | 2.19 × 10⁻² | Fails SIL 1 |
| 1oo2 | $2(1-\beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2$ | 2.16 × 10⁻⁴ | **SIL 3** ✓ |
| 2oo3 | $6 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2$ | 6.48 × 10⁻⁴ | SIL 2 |
| 2oo4 | $12 \lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2$ | 1.30 × 10⁻³ | Fails SIL 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Redundancy reduces PFDavg *by orders of magnitude*, but the trade-off is increased spurious trip probability. The validation engineer must justify the architecture choice to the KTU board examiner using *both* PFDavg *and* spurious trip rate.

## 3.5 Proof Test Coverage — Validation Effectiveness

If the proof test does not exercise **100%** of dangerous failure modes, the *effective* dangerous failure rate is:

$$\lambda_{DU,eff} = (1 - C) \cdot \lambda_{DU} + C \cdot 0 = (1 - C) \cdot \lambda_{DU}$$

For $C = 0.9$ (90% coverage), $\lambda_{DU,eff} = 0.1 \cdot \lambda_{DU}$, drastically reducing PFDavg. Validation must therefore **prove** the coverage figure is achievable — typically by witnessing a test that detects at least 9 out of 10 injected faults.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Installation-to-Validation Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SRS Frozen] --> B[Pre-Installation Review]
    B --> C[Physical Mounting]
    C --> D[Wiring and Cabling]
    D --> E[Pre-Commissioning Megger Test]
    E --> F[Commissioning Loop Check]
    F --> G[Cause Effect Matrix Test]
    G --> H[Fault Injection Test]
    H --> I{Pass All Tests?}
    I -->|No| J[Punch List MOC]
    J --> F
    I -->|Yes| K[SIF by SIF Validation]
    K --> L[End to End SIS Validation]
    L --> M[PFDavg Verification]
    M --> N{Meets SIL Target?}
    N -->|No| O[Revisit T_i or Architecture]
    O --> K
    N -->|Yes| P[Validation Dossier Issued]
    P --> Q[Plant Handover to Operations]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Validated SIF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor 1] --> LS[SIL Logic Solver]
    S2[Sensor 2] --> LS
    LS --> F1[Final Element 1 Valve]
    LS --> F2[Final Element 2 Valve]
    LS --> ANN[Control Room Alarm HMI]
    LS --> TRIP[Hardwired Shutdown Trip]
    TRIP --> F1
    TRIP --> F2
```

## 4.3 Validation Dossier Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Validation Dossier]
    ROOT --> A1[Validation Plan]
    ROOT --> A2[Calibration Certificates]
    ROOT --> A3[Loop Test Records]
    ROOT --> A4[Cause Effect Verification]
    ROOT --> A5[Fault Injection Results]
    ROOT --> A6[PFDavg Calculation Sheet]
    ROOT --> A7[As Built Drawings]
    ROOT --> A8[Punch List and Closeout]
    A8 --> A9[All Sign Offs by PE and HSE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exam:** When asked to "draw the SIS validation flow," examiners expect a *chronological* arrow diagram showing **phases**, **decision gates** (diamonds), and **documentation outputs** (parallelograms). The Mermaid diagrams above follow this convention.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the context of a Safety Instrumented System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Verification (1 mark):** The process of evaluating the SIS at each stage of the safety lifecycle to confirm that the output of that phase meets the requirements set at its start. It is an *internal*, *phase-by-phase* check. Example: checking that a sensor's measured range matches the SRS specification.
- **Validation (1 mark):** The process of confirming by examination and objective evidence that the installed and commissioned SIS meets the *overall* safety requirements specification, particularly the SIL target. It is a *holistic*, *end-to-end* check performed after installation and commissioning.
- **Mnemonic (1 mark):** Verification = *"Are we building the product right?"* Validation = *"Are we building the right product?"*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is meant by "Proof Test Coverage" in SIS validation? State its typical numerical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Proof Test Coverage (C) is the fraction of *dangerous undetected* failures of a SIF channel that can be *detected and corrected* by a given proof test procedure. A test with $C = 0.9$ means 9 out of 10 dangerous failure modes are caught.
- **Typical Range (1 mark):** $C$ ranges from approximately **0.6** (basic visual check) to **0.95** (comprehensive end-to-end forced trip with full stroke of valve and calibration verification).
- **Significance (0.5 mark):** Higher $C$ lowers the *effective* dangerous failure rate $\lambda_{DU,eff} = (1-C)\lambda_{DU}$, directly reducing $PFD_{avg}$ and improving the achieved SIL.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse "validation" with "commissioning" and vice versa. The board examiner will *deduct 2 marks* if the terms are interchanged.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When asked to compute $PFD_{avg}$, students frequently omit the MTTR term. Always use the *full* formula $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU}(T_i/2 + MTTR)$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to cite **IEC 61511** as the governing standard. A bare answer without standard reference loses at least 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing the validation workflow as a *linear* arrow diagram without a *decision gate* (diamond) — deduct 1 mark for missing the "Pass/Fail" branching.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the various activities involved in the **Installation** and **Commissioning** of a Safety Instrumented System as per IEC 61511. List the key documents that form the validation dossier.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 1oo1 SIF has a dangerous undetected failure rate of $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, a proof test interval of $T_i = 4380$ hours (6 months), and a Mean Time To Repair of $MTTR = 8$ hours. Compute the $PFD_{avg}$ and determine the achieved SIL. If the target is SIL 2, suggest two engineering actions to achieve compliance.

#### Model Solution to A(a) [7 marks]

**Installation Activities (3.5 marks):**
1. Pre-installation design review (1 mark)
2. Physical mounting per layout drawings, ensuring proper segregation of SIS from BPCS (0.5 mark)
3. Cable laying with segregation, glanding, IP rating compliance (0.5 mark)
4. Termination and earthing (0.5 mark)
5. Pre-commissioning checks — megger test, earth continuity, visual inspection (1 mark)

**Commissioning Activities (2.5 marks):**
1. Calibration verification of all sensors against traceable standards (0.5 mark)
2. Loop testing — sensor → logic solver → final element (0.5 mark)
3. Cause-&-Effect matrix verification (0.5 mark)
4. Fault injection testing to validate diagnostics (0.5 mark)
5. Logic solver application program verification (0.5 mark)

**Validation Dossier Documents (1 mark):**
Validation plan, calibration certificates, loop test records, as-built drawings, PFDavg calculation report, fault injection results, cause-&-effect verification matrix, punch-list closeout, and final sign-off by competent persons (process engineer, HSE head).

#### Model Solution to A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute channel equivalent downtime (1 mark)**
$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_i}{2} + MTTR = \dfrac{4380}{2} + 8 = 2198 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute PFDavg (1.5 marks)**
$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 2198 = 8.79 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Identify SIL achieved (1.5 marks)**
Comparing with IEC 61511 Table 3:
- SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ → $8.79 \times 10^{-3}$ is **below** $10^{-2}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ → **$8.79 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in this band** → **SIL 2 is achieved** ✓

**Step 4 — Engineering actions to maintain SIL 2 over the lifecycle (3 marks):**
1. **Reduce proof test interval** to e.g. 4 months (2920 hr), giving $t_{CE} = 1468$ hr and $PFD_{avg} = 5.87 \times 10^{-3}$ — a **safety margin improvement**.
2. **Improve proof test coverage** to $C = 0.95$, giving $\lambda_{DU,eff} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}$ and $PFD_{avg} = 4.4 \times 10^{-4}$ — a substantial *order-of-magnitude* improvement.
3. *(Alternative valid action)* Upgrade to a **1oo2 redundant architecture** with the same sensor, giving $PFD_{G,1oo2} \approx 8.7 \times 10^{-5}$ (SIL 3 territory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Full marks require the student to (a) show the formula explicitly, (b) compare the result with the SIL table values, and (c) state the *engineering justification* for each action.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a labelled flow diagram, describe the **Validation** activities carried out in the Safety Lifecycle (Phase 13 of IEC 61511). What are the acceptance criteria for issuing the "Validation Dossier"?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the $PFD_{avg}$ values for **1oo1, 1oo2, and 2oo3** architectures using $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/hr, $T_i = 8760$ hr, $MTTR = 8$ hr, $\beta = 0.07$. State the achieved SIL for each.

#### Model Solution to B(a) [7 marks]

**Validation Activities with Flow Diagram (4 marks):**
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. The student should describe:
1. Validation *Plan* prepared and approved (0.5 mark)
2. SIF-by-SIF validation — each SIF forced to trip, response time measured (1 mark)
3. End-to-end SIS validation — integrated test of all SIFs in concert (1 mark)
4. PFDavg verification against SIL target (0.5 mark)
5. Validation report / dossier compiled (0.5 mark)
6. Independent third-party review (where required) (0.5 mark)

**Acceptance Criteria for Dossier Issue (3 marks):**
1. *All* SIFs pass functional trip test (0.5 mark)
2. *Calculated* $PFD_{avg} \leq$ target SIL budget for *each* SIF (0.5 mark)
3. *All* punch-list items closed (0.5 mark)
4. *All* as-built drawings match installation (0.5 mark)
5. *Independent* peer review by a competent functional safety engineer (0.5 mark)
6. *Final* sign-off by Plant Manager, Process Engineer, HSE Manager (0.5 mark)

#### Model Solution to B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute common $t_{CE}$ (1 mark)**
$$t_{CE} = \dfrac{8760}{2} + 8 = 4388 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — 1oo1 Architecture (1.5 marks)**
$$PFD_{G,1oo1} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4388 = 8.776 \times 10^{-3}$$
→ Achieves **SIL 2** ✓ (lies in band $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$)

**Step 3 — 1oo2 Architecture (2 marks)**
$$PFD_{G,1oo2} = 2(1 - \beta)\lambda_{DU}^2 t_{CE}^2$$
$$= 2 \times (1 - 0.07) \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (4388)^2$$
$$= 2 \times 0.93 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.9255 \times 10^{7}$$
$$= 1.4325 \times 10^{-4}$$
→ Achieves **SIL 3** ✓ (lies in band $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$)

**Step 4 — 2oo3 Architecture (2 marks)**
$$PFD_{G,2oo3} = 6 \cdot \lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot t_{CE}^2$$
$$= 6 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.9255 \times 10^{7}$$
$$= 4.621 \times 10^{-4}$$
→ Achieves **SIL 3** ✓ (lies in band $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$)

**Step 5 — Comparative Tabulation (0.5 mark)**

| Architecture | PFDavg | SIL Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | 8.78 × 10⁻³ | SIL 2 |
| 1oo2 | 1.43 × 10⁻⁴ | SIL 3 |
| 2oo3 | 4.62 × 10⁻⁴ | SIL 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for the Answer:** 1oo2 gives a *better* PFDavg than 2oo3 in this calculation, but 1oo2 has a *higher* spurious trip rate. The KTU examiner expects a comment on this trade-off for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Installation ≠ Commissioning ≠ Validation — they are **three distinct phases** (IEC 61511 Phases 11, 12, 13).
- ✅ **Validation answers "Did we build the right system?"** and must be performed *before* plant handover.
- ✅ The **Validation Dossier** is the legal/audit document; without it, the SIS cannot be put into service.
- ✅ **PFDavg formula (1oo1):** $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \left( \dfrac{T_i}{2} + MTTR \right)$ — remember the MTTR term.
- ✅ **PFDavg scales linearly with Tᵢ** — halving test interval halves the PFDavg.
- ✅ **Proof Test Coverage C** appears as $(1-C)$ in the *effective* dangerous failure rate.
- ✅ **SIL 1**: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | **SIL 2**: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | **SIL 3**: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- ✅ **1oo2 architecture** = safer but more spurious trips; **2oo3** = best balance for high-integrity applications.
- ✅ **Common-cause failure (β)** must always be considered in redundant architectures.
- ✅ Cite **IEC 61511-1:2016** as the governing standard in all KTU answers.
- ✅ *Redundancy* reduces PFDavg by *orders of magnitude*; *better proof testing* reduces PFDavg *linearly*; *better hardware* (lower $\lambda_{DU}$) reduces it *linearly*.
- ✅ Validation must be performed by *competent persons independent of the design team* — this is a KTU favourite 3-mark question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** When answering KTU Part B numericals, always present the answer in the *exact* format: (i) given data, (ii) formula, (iii) substitution, (iv) final numerical answer with units, (v) SIL interpretation, (vi) engineering recommendation. Skipping any one of these six steps will cost at least 1 mark per step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nominal Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nominal Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nominal Tests** in the context of functional safety (as defined under IEC 61508 and IEC 61511) refer to a comprehensive suite of **systematic verification and validation activities** performed during the design, development, manufacturing, installation, and commissioning phases of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) to confirm that the Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) operate in accordance with their design specifications under **nominal (expected, within-specification) operating conditions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT635 - Module 4):**
> Nominal Tests establish the **baseline performance characteristics** of a SIS, including the quantification of failure rates ($\lambda_{DU}$, $\lambda_{DD}$, $\lambda_{SU}$, $\lambda_{SD}$), verification of Safe Failure Fraction (SFF), confirmation of Diagnostic Coverage (DC), and proof of compliance with the target Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1, 2, or 3).

These tests are fundamentally distinct from **Proof Tests** (periodic in-service tests) in their **timing, scope, and intent**. Nominal tests are performed **once** (or per design change) at controlled conditions, while proof tests are recurring activities throughout the operational life of the plant.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a commercial aircraft manufacturer like Boeing designing a new aircraft. Before any passenger ever boards the plane, the manufacturer performs:

- **Wind tunnel tests** (type tests) on a prototype
- **Engine stress tests** under controlled conditions
- **Avionics system tests** in a simulated environment
- **Pre-delivery test flights** (factory/site acceptance tests)
- **Verification of all safety systems** before commercial release

These are all **"nominal tests"** — performed under **controlled, specified, expected conditions** to verify the system works *as designed*. Once the aircraft is in service, the airline performs **periodic maintenance checks (proof tests)** to catch any degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Nominal Tests** = "Before service" tests → Verify design conformance
> - **Proof Tests** = "During service" tests → Detect hidden failures in operation

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are quantified through nominal testing:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ per hour |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $DC$ | **0% to 100%** |
| Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF$ | **60% to 99%+** |
| Proof Test Interval | $TI$ | **1 to 10 years** (typical) |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR$ | **8 hours** (standard assumption) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The default **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) = 8 hours** is the **IEC 61508 standard assumption** used when actual repair time data is unavailable. This is a high-yield fact for KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Nominal Tests

Nominal tests are organized into a **phased hierarchy**, each phase having specific objectives, test conditions, and acceptance criteria:

### 2.1.1 Phase 1: Design Verification Tests (Type Tests)
- Performed on **prototype or representative samples**
- Verify the design conforms to specification
- Include **environmental tests**: temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC
- Results feed into **manufacturer's failure rate database**

### 2.1.2 Phase 2: Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT)
- Conducted at **manufacturer's facility**
- Performed on **every production unit** (not just samples)
- Verifies functional behavior under **simulated process conditions**
- Customer witnesses the tests; results documented with sign-off

### 2.1.3 Phase 3: Site Acceptance Tests (SAT)
- Performed at **customer's site** after delivery
- Verifies no damage occurred during **transit**
- Confirms the unit operates correctly under **real site conditions** (power supply, grounding, ambient temperature)

### 2.1.4 Phase 4: Installation Tests
- Verifies **proper mechanical and electrical installation**
- Checks wiring, cabling, termination, earthing
- Confirms compliance with installation drawings and standards

### 2.1.5 Phase 5: Commissioning Tests
- **Final tests** before the SIS is put into operational service
- Performed with the process in a **safe state** (often with plant shut down or bypassed)
- Verifies **end-to-end SIF loop**: Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element
- This is the **last nominal test** before operation begins

## 2.2 Critical Failure Rate Parameters Determined by Nominal Tests

Nominal tests are the **source** of all failure rate data used in SIL calculations:

$$\lambda_{D} = \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD}$$

$$\lambda_{S} = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD}$$

$$\lambda_{Total} = \lambda_{D} + \lambda_{S}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = Dangerous Detected (by diagnostics) failure rate
- $\lambda_{SU}$ = Safe Undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{SD}$ = Safe Detected failure rate

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{TI}{2}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1 architecture) | Dimensionless | Low-demand mode, single channel |
| $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot TI^2}{3}$ | 1oo2 architecture PFD | Dimensionless | Redundant low-demand SIF |
| $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot TI}{2} + \dfrac{(\lambda_{DD} \cdot MTTR)^2}{3}$ | 1oo1 with diagnostics | Dimensionless | When DC $\gt$ 0% |
| $PFH = \lambda_{DU}$ | Probability of Failure per Hour (1oo1) | per hour | High-demand / continuous mode |
| $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D}}$ | Diagnostic Coverage | Fraction (0 to 1) | Quantifying self-test effectiveness |
| $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{D}}$ | Safe Failure Fraction | Fraction (0 to 1) | Hardware fault tolerance classification |
| $PTC = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU, undetected\,after\,test}}{\lambda_{D}}$ | Proof Test Coverage | Fraction (0 to 1) | Nominal test effectiveness |
| $\lambda_{DU, effective} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - PTC)$ | Effective undetected failure rate | per hour | After applying test coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **$(TI/2)$** factor in the PFD formula assumes a **uniform failure distribution** over the test interval with **perfect proof test coverage (PTC = 100%)**. If PTC < 100%, you **must** use the **effective $\lambda_{DU}$** form shown above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Nominal tests are the **foundation of the entire safety lifecycle** as defined in IEC 61511:

1. **Regulatory Compliance**: Industries like oil & gas, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and nuclear require documented nominal test records for **legal and audit** purposes.
2. **Insurance and Liability**: Failure to perform nominal tests can void equipment warranties and create **legal liability** in case of incidents.
3. **Baseline for Future Proof Tests**: The "as-good-as-new" assumption after a proof test is **only valid** if the proof test replicates the nominal test conditions.
4. **SIL Verification**: Without nominal test data, **no PFD/PFH calculation can be performed**, making SIL verification impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average PFD for 1oo1 Architecture with Periodic Proof Tests

This is the **most important derivation** in functional safety and a frequent KTU exam topic.

### Step 1: Define the Failure Density Function

Assume dangerous undetected failures occur at a **constant rate** $\lambda_{DU}$ (failures per hour) over the proof test interval $TI$.

The probability density of a failure occurring at time $t$ (where $0 \le t \le TI$) is:

$$f(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Low-Demand Simplification

In low-demand mode, $\lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \ll 1$, so the exponential term approximates to **1**:

$$f(t) \approx \lambda_{DU}$$

This is the **uniform distribution assumption** — failures are equally likely at any moment in the interval.

### Step 3: Compute the Conditional PFD

If a demand occurs at time $t$ after the last proof test, and a failure has occurred at some time $t_f$ where $0 \le t_f \le t$, then the system fails to respond. The **conditional probability of failure on demand** is:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Average Over the Test Interval

The **average PFD** is obtained by integrating the conditional PFD over the interval $[0, TI]$ and dividing by $TI$:

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{TI} \int_{0}^{TI} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Integral

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{TI} \cdot \left[\dfrac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{TI}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{TI} \cdot \dfrac{TI^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{TI}{2}}$$

### Step 6: Verify with Numerical Example

Let $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and $TI = 8760$ hours (1 year):

$$PFD_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{8760}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 2.19 \times 10^{-2}$$

Since $2.19 \times 10^{-2}$ lies between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$, this corresponds to **SIL 2** per IEC 61508. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective PFD with Non-Perfect Proof Test Coverage

### Step 1: Decompose the Undetected Failure Rate

When a proof test has coverage $PTC < 100\%$, a fraction $(1 - PTC)$ of dangerous failures remain undetected:

$$\lambda_{DU, undetected} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - PTC)$$

### Step 2: Identify the Re-Set Boundary Conditions

After the proof test:
- The fraction $PTC$ of dangerous failures is **detected and repaired** (system restored)
- The fraction $(1 - PTC)$ remains **hidden** and continues to accumulate

### Step 3: Compute the Average PFD Over the Interval

The **average PFD** now has two contributing terms:

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot PTC \cdot \dfrac{TI}{2} + \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - PTC) \cdot TI$$

The first term represents detected-and-repaired failures (linear growth then reset), and the second term represents **continuously accumulating** undetected failures.

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot \left[\dfrac{PTC}{2} + (1 - PTC)\right]$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{PTC}{2}\right]}$$

### Step 5: Verify Boundary Conditions

- If $PTC = 1$ (perfect test): $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot 0.5 = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI/2$ ✓
- If $PTC = 0$ (useless test): $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI$ ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SIL Classification vs PFD Range

| SIL Level | PFD Range (Low Demand) | PFH Range (High Demand) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ per hour | 10 to 100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ per hour | 100 to 1000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ per hour | 1000 to 10000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ per hour | 10000 to 100000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nominal Test Lifecycle Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Design Verification] --> B[Phase 2: Factory Acceptance Test FAT]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Site Acceptance Test SAT]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Installation Tests]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Commissioning Tests]
    E --> F{SIL Verification Pass}
    F -- Yes --> G[SIS Released for Operation]
    F -- No --> H[Design Modification Loop]
    H --> A
    G --> I[Periodic Proof Tests Begin]
    I --> J{Next Proof Test Due}
    J -- Yes --> K[Proof Test Execution]
    K --> L{Test Pass}
    L -- Yes --> J
    L -- No --> M[Failure Analysis and Repair]
    M --> J
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: End-to-End SIF Loop Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensor Element] --> LGC[Logic Solver]
    LGC --> FIN[Final Element]
    FIN --> PROC[Process]
    PROC --> SENS
    NT1[Nominal Test 1: Sensor Calibration] -.-> SENS
    NT2[Nominal Test 2: Logic Solver Logic Verification] -.-> LGC
    NT3[Nominal Test 3: Final Element Response Time] -.-> FIN
    NT4[Nominal Test 4: End to End Loop Check] -.-> PROC
    SILV[SIL Verification Calculation] --> SENS
    SILV --> LGC
    SILV --> FIN
```

## 4.3 Nominal Test Coverage vs Failure Detection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FLT[Failure Occurs] --> CLS{Type of Failure}
    CLS -- Safe --> S1[Lambda SU Undetected]
    CLS -- Safe --> S2[Lambda SD Detected by Diagnostics]
    CLS -- Dangerous --> D1[Lambda DU Undetected]
    CLS -- Dangerous --> D2[Lambda DD Detected by Diagnostics]
    NT[Nominal Test Activity] --> D1
    NT --> S1
    DC[Diagnostic Coverage Lambda DD by Lambda D] --> D2
    SFF[Safe Failure Fraction Lambda S plus Lambda DD divided by Lambda Total] --> S1
    SFF --> S2
    SFF --> D1
    SFF --> D2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Nominal Tests in the context of functional safety. List any three types of nominal tests.**

**Model Answer:**

Nominal Tests are a set of systematic verification and validation activities performed during the design, development, manufacturing, installation, and commissioning phases of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) to verify that the safety functions operate as designed under nominal (specified, expected) operating conditions.

**Three types of nominal tests:**

1. **Type Tests** — Performed on representative samples to verify design conformance to specification, including environmental tests (temperature, humidity, vibration, EMC).
2. **Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT)** — Performed on every production unit at the manufacturer's facility, witnessed by the customer, with documented sign-off.
3. **Commissioning Tests** — The final end-to-end test performed at the customer site before the SIS is put into operational service, verifying the complete sensor-to-final-element loop.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Nominal Tests and Proof Tests.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Nominal Tests | Proof Tests |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Performed once before service | Performed periodically during service |
| **Purpose** | Verify design conformance | Detect hidden/dormant failures |
| **Conditions** | Controlled, specified | Often on-line / in-service |
| **Frequency** | Once (or per design change) | Recurring at intervals $TI$ |
| **Scope** | All SIFs and components | Specific SIF or subsystem |
| **Output** | Failure rate data, SFF, DC | Confirmation of operational integrity |

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a)** — *7 Marks*: A 1oo1 SIF has $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and the nominal test verified that the proof test coverage $PTC = 80\%$. If the proof test interval $TI = 4380$ hours (6 months), calculate the average PFD and identify the achieved SIL level.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $PTC = 0.80$
- $TI = 4380$ hours

**Step 1:** Apply the effective PFD formula with non-perfect proof test coverage:

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{PTC}{2}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 \times \left[1 - \dfrac{0.80}{2}\right]$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 \times 0.60$$

**Step 3:** Compute the product:

$$PFD_{avg} = 3 \times 4380 \times 0.60 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 7884 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = 7.884 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4:** SIL Classification:

The achieved PFD $= 7.884 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, which corresponds to **SIL 2** per IEC 61508. ✓

**Part (b)** — *7 Marks*: Explain the role of nominal tests in determining Diagnostic Coverage (DC) and Safe Failure Fraction (SFF). How do these parameters affect hardware fault tolerance classification?

**Model Solution:**

**Diagnostic Coverage (DC) Determination via Nominal Tests:**

Nominal tests (particularly type tests and FAT) systematically inject simulated failures of all known types into the device under test. The diagnostic system's ability to detect each failure is recorded:

$$DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

For example, if a transmitter is tested with 100 different dangerous failure modes, and the internal diagnostics detect 60 of them, then $DC = 60\%$.

**Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) Determination:**

SFF is computed from the complete failure rate distribution established by nominal testing:

$$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{D}}$$

**Effect on Hardware Fault Tolerance Classification:**

Per IEC 61508 (Table 2/3), the achieved SIL for a given hardware fault tolerance depends on the SFF:

| SFF | Type A Device (1oo1) | Type A Device (1oo2) | Type B Device (1oo1) | Type B Device (1oo2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 60% | Not allowed | SIL 1 | Not allowed | SIL 1 |
| 60% to < 90% | SIL 1 | SIL 2 | SIL 1 | SIL 2 |
| 60% to < 99% | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| ≥ 90% | SIL 2 | SIL 3 | SIL 2 | SIL 3 |
| ≥ 99% | SIL 3 | SIL 4 | SIL 3 | SIL 4 |

**Conclusion:** Without nominal test data, neither DC nor SFF can be quantified, and consequently, the SIL capability of the hardware cannot be determined.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*

**Part (a)** — *7 Marks*: With a neat block diagram, describe the five phases of nominal testing in the SIS safety lifecycle as per IEC 61511. State the primary objective of each phase.

**Model Solution:**

The five phases of nominal testing are:

**1. Design Verification (Type Tests)**
- **Objective:** Verify the design conforms to specifications under controlled environmental conditions
- **Activities:** Temperature cycling, humidity, vibration, EMC, and performance tests on prototype units

**2. Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)**
- **Objective:** Verify every production unit functions correctly before shipment
- **Activities:** Functional tests witnessed by customer, documentation review, calibration verification

**3. Site Acceptance Test (SAT)**
- **Objective:** Confirm no transit damage and verify operation under actual site conditions
- **Activities:** Visual inspection, power-up tests, environmental verification, interface checks

**4. Installation Test**
- **Objective:** Verify correct mechanical and electrical installation
- **Activities:** Wiring checks, earthing verification, loop checks, cable integrity tests

**5. Commissioning Test**
- **Objective:** Final end-to-end verification of the complete SIF before operational service
- **Activities:** Full sensor-to-final-element loop test, response time measurement, alarm/shutdown verification, SIL calculation verification

**Part (b)** — *7 Marks*: A safety valve has $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour, $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour, $\lambda_{SU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour, and $\lambda_{SD} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is $TI = 17520$ hours (2 years) with perfect coverage. Calculate: (i) Diagnostic Coverage, (ii) Safe Failure Fraction, (iii) Average PFD, and (iv) State the achieved SIL.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $\lambda_{SU} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $\lambda_{SD} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $TI = 17520$ hours
- $PTC = 1.0$ (perfect)

**Step 1: Diagnostic Coverage (DC)**

$$\lambda_{D} = \lambda_{DU} + \lambda_{DD} = 2 \times 10^{-7} + 8 \times 10^{-7} = 10 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}$$

$$DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-7}}{10 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{DC = 0.80 = 80\%}$$

**Step 2: Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)**

$$\lambda_{S} = \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{SD} = 5 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\lambda_{Total} = \lambda_{D} + \lambda_{S} = 10 \times 10^{-7} + 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$

$$SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{Total}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-6} + 8 \times 10^{-7}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$SFF = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-6} + 0.8 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2.3 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{SFF = 0.92 = 92\%}$$

**Step 3: Average PFD (1oo1, perfect PTC)**

$$PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{TI}{2} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{17520}{2}$$

$$PFD_{avg} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 1.752 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} \approx 1.75 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4: SIL Classification**

Since $1.75 \times 10^{-3}$ lies in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, the achieved SIL is **SIL 2**. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve TI** — Students often write $PFD = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI$ instead of $\lambda_{DU} \cdot (TI/2)$. This results in **50% mark loss**.
> 2. **Mixing up $\lambda_D$ and $\lambda_{Total}$** — In SFF calculation, the denominator is $\lambda_{Total} = \lambda_D + \lambda_S$, NOT just $\lambda_D$.
> 3. **Unit confusion in $TI$** — Always express $TI$ in **hours** to match $\lambda$ units (per hour). Converting $TI$ to years requires multiplication by **8760**.
> 4. **PTC effect ignored** — When $PTC < 100\%$, the standard $PFD = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI/2$ formula is **invalid**. Use the modified formula $PFD = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot (1 - PTC/2)$.
> 5. **DC and SFF unit confusion** — Both are **dimensionless fractions** (0 to 1), NOT percentages in the formula. Convert to decimal before substituting.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nominal Tests** are pre-service verification activities; **Proof Tests** are in-service periodic tests.
- The **five phases** are: Type Test → FAT → SAT → Installation Test → Commissioning Test.
- **PFD formula for 1oo1** (perfect PTC): $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI/2$
- **PFD with imperfect PTC**: $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot TI \cdot (1 - PTC/2)$
- **Diagnostic Coverage**: $DC = \lambda_{DD} / \lambda_{D}$
- **Safe Failure Fraction**: $SFF = (\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}) / \lambda_{Total}$
- **MTTR standard value** = **8 hours** (IEC 61508 default)
- **SIL ranges**: SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
- **Conversion factor**: 1 year = **8760 hours**
- Nominal tests provide the **failure rate data** that is the **input** to all SIL calculations.
- SFF and DC together determine the **maximum achievable SIL** for a given hardware fault tolerance.
- For high-demand mode, use **PFH = $\lambda_{DU}$** (for 1oo1) instead of PFD.
- Always state **units** explicitly in KTU exam answers — failure to do so loses 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outside Limit Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Outside Limit Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Outside Limit Test (OLT)** is a diagnostic verification procedure performed on a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) to confirm that the SIF will respond correctly when the monitored process variable (PV) exceeds its specified **Normal Operating Range (NOR)** but remains within the **Safe Operating Envelope (SOE)**. Unlike proof tests that exercise the full safety path under simulated dangerous conditions, an Outside Limit Test validates the *boundary detection* capability of the sensor, logic solver, and final element without forcing the plant into a real dangerous state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**  
> An Outside Limit Test is a continuous or periodic on-line diagnostic that verifies a safety instrumented loop by automatically detecting when the process variable has strayed outside the configured normal operating band, thereby providing an opportunity to verify the trip path and the **Dangerous Undetected (DU)** failure detection capability of the subsystem.

In the architecture of **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**, the safety instrumented system (SIS) is composed of three subsystems:
- **Sensor Subsystem (1ooN or 2ooN voted)**
- **Logic Solver Subsystem (PLC / Safety PLC)**
- **Final Element Subsystem (Valve, Circuit Breaker, Drive)**

The OLT exercises all three subsystems together as a closed loop, but the stimulus is *real process noise / real process drift*, not a synthetic injected trip signal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the **speedometer and brake system of a car**:

- The **Normal Operating Range** is driving at 0–80 km/h.
- The **Outside Limit** condition begins when speed exceeds 80 km/h.
- The car manufacturer programs a *silent self-check*: every time the speed goes above 80 km/h, the ABS sensor, the speedometer needle driver, and the brake-pressure modulator all run a self-test.
- The driver does NOT have to slam the brakes to verify them; the very act of driving slightly fast triggers the test.
- If any sensor or actuator fails this silent test, the dashboard lights up the warning lamp so the car can be serviced *before* a real emergency braking is required.

> **The car analogy directly maps to industrial OLT:**
> The process plant has many transient excursions (start-up ramps, load swings, batch transitions) that naturally push the PV outside the *normal* band. OLT uses these *real-life transients* to verify the safety path.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics and Physical Standards (Bolded)

- **Normal Operating Range (NOR):** Manufacturer-specified PV window in which the sensor is designed to operate with guaranteed accuracy.
- **Safe Operating Envelope (SOE):** Maximum PV span the sensor can survive without physical damage.
- **Trip Set-Point (SP):** The PV value that initiates the safety action.
- **Outside Limit Window (OLW):** The narrow band between NOR and SP — the operating zone where OLT is most useful.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** The fraction of dangerous failures that an OLT can reveal.
- **Test Interval (TI):** Time between two consecutive OLT executions, typically in **hours** or **days**.
- **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR):** Time to repair after a detected failure, typically **8 hours** per KTU valuation key.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):**  
> Outside Limit Tests are studied in conjunction with *Proof Testing*, *Partial Stroke Testing (PST)*, and *Diagnostic Coverage (DC)*. They are a key part of the **Operational Phase** of the Safety Lifecycle as defined in **IEC 61511 Clause 16**.

## 1.4 Where OLT Fits in the KTU 2024 Safety Lifecycle

The safety lifecycle per IEC 61511 has 16 phases. Outside Limit Tests belong to **Phase 13 — Operation, Maintenance, and Modification**, specifically the *Proof Testing* sub-activity. They are an *additive* strategy — they do NOT replace the full proof test, but they *augment* it by providing continuous in-service verification of a portion of the dangerous failure space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How an Outside Limit Test Works

The Outside Limit Test follows a strict five-step operational logic:

- **Step 1 — Process Variable Monitoring:** The smart transmitter samples the PV continuously using its on-board microprocessor (e.g., Rosemount 3051S with HART or FOUNDATION Fieldbus).
- **Step 2 — Limit Comparison:** The PV is compared in real-time to two pre-configured thresholds:
  - $PV_{low}$: Lower edge of the Normal Operating Range
  - $PV_{high}$: Upper edge of the Normal Operating Range
- **Step 3 — Boundary Detection Trigger:** When $PV < PV_{low}$ OR $PV > PV_{high}$, the transmitter marks this as an *Outside-Limit Event* and increments an internal counter $N_{OL}$.
- **Step 4 — Implicit Path Verification:** Because the analog signal sent to the Safety PLC has just left the calibrated band, the PLC must continue to read *accurate* values. Any ADC drift, stuck-at, or range error will become a *visible discrepancy*. The same applies for the final element positioner if the loop is closed.
- **Step 5 — Diagnostic Logging:** The event is logged with timestamp, peak PV, response time, and any deviation from the expected dynamic model. A diagnostic coverage credit is given if the OLT detects the failure before the next manual proof test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OLT works without disturbing the process:**  
> Plant processes naturally cycle through their operating range. A reactor temperature may swing from 145 °C to 158 °C in a normal batch cycle. The OLT does not *force* this swing — it *observes* it. The *probability* of observing a swing in any given day is high; therefore, the effective test interval for the OLT-detected failures is much shorter than the manual proof-test interval.

## 2.2 Failure Modes Detected vs. Missed by Outside Limit Tests

| Failure Mode (Sensor) | Detected by OLT? | Detected by Full Proof Test? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Stuck-at-zero output | Yes (PV cannot reach SP if it cannot leave NOR) | Yes |
| Span drift > 2% | Yes (deviation flagged) | Yes |
| Calibration memory corruption | Partial | Yes |
| HART communication failure | Yes (diagnostic flag) | Yes |
| Random bit-error in ADC (rare) | No | Yes |
| Set-point comparator stuck | No (logic solver only) | Yes |
| Final element packing seizure (no movement) | No | Yes |
| Loss of power supply | Yes (loop current < 3.6 mA) | Yes |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

All formulas are presented in standard markdown tables. Every `|` in mathematics is replaced with `\vert` to protect table syntax.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Failure Rate Sum | $\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | $\lambda$ in **FIT** (failures / $10^{9}$ h) | Failure classification |
| 2 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | DC is dimensionless (0 to 1) | Quantifies OLT effectiveness |
| 3 | Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF = \dfrac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | SFF dimensionless | SIL hardware constraints |
| 4 | PFDavg (single channel, OLT-augmented) | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{residual} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Dimensionless; $T_{proof}$ in **hours** | Average probability of failure on demand |
| 5 | Residual DU Rate after OLT | $\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - DC_{OLT})$ | FIT | What OLT leaves undetected |
| 6 | OLT Test Interval | $T_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{f_{excursion}}$ | $f$ in excursions per hour | Average time between two natural PV excursions |
| 7 | Coverage Reduction Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{PFD_{OLT}}{PFD_{no-test}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Performance gain from OLT |
| 8 | Achieved SIL (sensor) | $PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{residual} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Per IEC 61508 Tables 2 & 3 | Maps PFD to SIL band |
| 9 | MooN Voting PFD (1oo2) | $PFD_{1oo2} = 2 \cdot (1 - R) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot \dfrac{TI}{2}$ | R = proof test coverage | Voting architecture effect |
| 10 | Trip Set-Point Margin | $SP_{margin} = \vert PV_{high} - SP \vert$ | Engineering units (°C, bar, m³/h) | Defines Outside Limit Window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized SIL Bands (IEC 61508 Table 2):**  
> SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$  
> SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$  
> SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-3}$  
> SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

- **Refineries:** Hydrocracker reactor differential pressure transmitters use OLT to detect impulse line blockages between scheduled turnarounds.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** Temperature OLT detects sensor drift during SIP (Steam-In-Place) cycles when the temperature naturally exceeds the normal 25 °C–80 °C band.
- **Power Plant Boiler Drum Level:** OLT verifies the differential pressure transmitter during start-up when the drum is being filled from empty.
- **Offshore ESD Valves:** Hydraulic pressure sensor OLT detects pump degradation every time the wellhead is shut in for routine operations.
- **Chemical Storage Tanks:** Level transmitter OLT is exercised during every tanker-filling event, eliminating the need for a separate proof test of the level loop.

> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** A complete answer must *quantify* how OLT reduces $PFD_{avg}$, not just describe it qualitatively. Examiners award full marks only when the student demonstrates that OLT reduces the effective $\lambda_{DU}$ entering the SIL calculation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Diagnostic Coverage of an Outside Limit Test

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the effective Diagnostic Coverage $DC_{OLT}$ of an Outside Limit Test on a smart transmitter.

**Given Data (from a worked example used in KTU valuation keys):**
- Dangerous Detected failure rate: $\lambda_{DD} = 3.5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- Dangerous Undetected failure rate: $\lambda_{DU} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- OLT trigger threshold: $PV_{high} = 105\%$ of calibrated range
- Expected PV excursions per year: $f_{exc} = 365$ (i.e., 1 per day)
- Proof test interval: $T_{proof} = 8760$ h (1 year)

**Step 1:** Write the generic Diagnostic Coverage formula.

$$DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values (note: keep $10^{-7}$ common factor for clarity).

$$DC = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^{-7}}{(3.5 \times 10^{-7}) + (1.2 \times 10^{-7})}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $10^{-7}$ from numerator and denominator.

$$DC = \dfrac{3.5}{3.5 + 1.2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$3.5 + 1.2 = 4.7$$

**Step 5:** Compute the ratio.

$$DC = \dfrac{3.5}{4.7} = 0.7447$$

**Step 6:** Express as a percentage.

$$DC = 74.47\%$$

**Step 7:** Apply the OLT coverage discount factor (typical engineering practice — OLT detects only the *excursion-related* portion of the failure space, typically 0.85 of the DC).

$$DC_{OLT} = 0.85 \times DC = 0.85 \times 0.7447 = 0.6330$$

**Step 8:** Final answer.

$$DC_{OLT} = 63.30\%$$

This means **63.3% of dangerous failures are detected by the OLT** before the next annual proof test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFD Reduction Due to Outside Limit Test

**Statement:** Compare the $PFD_{avg}$ of a SIF sensor with and without OLT, and compute the Coverage Reduction Factor (CRF).

**Given Data (continued):**
- $T_{proof} = 8760$ h
- $\lambda_{DU} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour
- $DC_{OLT} = 0.6330$ from previous derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the residual DU failure rate after OLT detection.

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - DC_{OLT})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (1 - 0.6330)$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(1 - 0.6330)$.

$$1 - 0.6330 = 0.3670$$

**Step 4:** Multiply.

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.3670 = 4.404 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{per hour}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the PFD with OLT using the simplified 1oo1 formula.

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{residual} \cdot T_{proof}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute.

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4.404 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 8760$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the product.

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3.8579 \times 10^{-4} = 1.929 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8:** Compute PFD without OLT for comparison.

$$PFD_{no-test} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 8760 = 5.256 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 9:** Compute the Coverage Reduction Factor.

$$CRF = \dfrac{PFD_{OLT}}{PFD_{no-test}} = \dfrac{1.929 \times 10^{-4}}{5.256 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 10:** Evaluate the ratio.

$$CRF = 0.367$$

**Step 11:** Equivalent percentage reduction in PFD.

$$\Delta PFD\% = (1 - 0.367) \cdot 100 = 63.3\%$$

This matches $DC_{OLT}$ exactly — a fundamental identity that KTU examiners love to test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identity Worth Remembering:** $CRF = 1 - DC_{OLT}$. The percentage reduction in $PFD_{avg}$ equals the Diagnostic Coverage of the test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SIL Classification With and Without OLT

**Step 1:** Look up the PFD band for $PFD_{no-test} = 5.256 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$5.256 \times 10^{-4} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 3}$$

**Step 2:** Look up the PFD band for $PFD_{OLT} = 1.929 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$1.929 \times 10^{-4} \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-3}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIL 3 (maintained)}$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion. The SIF is SIL 3 capable in *both* cases for this sensor alone; however, OLT provides a **safety margin** of approximately a factor of 2.7, which is critical when the sensor is in series with other subsystems (logic solver + valve) whose $PFD_{avg}$ also consumes the SIL 3 budget.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Test Strategies

| Test Strategy | Coverage Achieved | Process Disturbance | Interval | KTU Exam Frequency |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Manual Full Proof Test** | 100% (ideal) | **High** (process must be shut down) | 1–5 years | Very High |
| **Partial Stroke Test (PST)** | 60–80% | **Low** (valve moves 10–30%) | Days to months | Very High |
| **Outside Limit Test (OLT)** | 50–90% (PV-dependent) | **None** (uses real transients) | Hours to days | High |
| **Smart Valve Diagnostics** | 70–95% | None | Continuous | Medium |
| **No Test** | 0% | None | — | (reference only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Outside Limit Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Process Plant — Physical PV]) --> B[Smart Transmitter Sample]
    B --> C{PV in Normal Operating Range}
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue Normal Operation]
    C -- No --> E[OLT Triggered: PV outside NOR]
    E --> F[Compare PV with Trip Set-Point SP]
    F --> G{PV reached SP}
    G -- No --> H[Implicit Loop Verification]
    H --> I[Check ADC / Sensor / HART / PLC integrity]
    I --> J{All Subsystems OK}
    J -- Yes --> K[Log OLT Pass — Increment N_OL counter]
    J -- No --> L[Annunciate Diagnostic Alarm to Operator]
    L --> M[Schedule Corrective Maintenance within MTTR]
    G -- Yes --> N[Initiate Real Safety Trip Action]
    N --> O[Process Shutdown and Safe State]
    K --> P[(OLT Database with Timestamp)]
    L --> P
    P --> Q[Update PFDavg for Next Proof Test Interval]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — SIS Subsystems Showing OLT Application Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Sensor Subsystem 1oo2]
      T1[Transmitter A — with OLT Logic]
      T2[Transmitter B — with OLT Logic]
    end
    subgraph S2[Logic Solver Subsystem]
      LS[Safety PLC — Voting and Trip Logic]
    end
    subgraph S3[Final Element Subsystem 1oo2]
      V1[Shutdown Valve A]
      V2[Shutdown Valve B]
    end
    T1 -- 4-20 mA / HART --> LS
    T2 -- 4-20 mA / HART --> LS
    LS -- Solenoid Drive --> V1
    LS -- Solenoid Drive --> V2
    T1 -. OLT Boundary Detection .-> OLDB[(OLT Diagnostic Database)]
    T2 -. OLT Boundary Detection .-> OLDB
    V1 -. Smart Positioner PST .-> OLDB
    V2 -. Smart Positioner PST .-> OLDB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Time-Line Diagram — Test Coverage Over Proof-Test Interval

```mermaid
gantt
    title Outside Limit Test Coverage Timeline
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD
    section Proof Test Cycle
    Manual Full Proof Test    :milestone, m1, 2025-01-01, 0d
    Manual Full Proof Test    :milestone, m2, 2026-01-01, 0d
    section OLT Events
    OLT Trigger 1 (PV High)   :olt1, 2025-02-15, 1d
    OLT Trigger 2 (PV Low)    :olt2, 2025-04-22, 1d
    OLT Trigger 3 (PV High)   :olt3, 2025-07-10, 1d
    OLT Trigger 4 (PV High)   :olt4, 2025-10-05, 1d
    OLT Trigger 5 (PV Low)    :olt5, 2025-12-12, 1d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Gantt Chart:** Between two annual full proof tests, the OLT fires roughly 5 times in this example, each one providing an *implicit* verification event. The Dangerous Undetected failure window is therefore sliced into 6 smaller intervals, dramatically reducing the average probability of failure on demand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Outside Limit Test (OLT) as applied to a Safety Instrumented System. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** An Outside Limit Test is an on-line diagnostic verification procedure in which a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is implicitly tested whenever the monitored process variable (PV) moves outside the configured normal operating range of the sensor, but remains below the trip set-point.
- **Advantage 1 (0.5 mark):** It provides continuous in-service verification of the sensor path without disturbing the process.
- **Advantage 2 (0.5 mark):** It reduces the effective Dangerous Undetected (DU) failure rate, thereby lowering the average Probability of Failure on Demand ($PFD_{avg}$) and helping to maintain the target Safety Integrity Level (SIL).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a Partial Stroke Test (PST) and an Outside Limit Test (OLT) in any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Stimulus Source (1 mark):** PST uses a *synthetic command pulse* from the logic solver to move the final element by 10–30%. OLT uses *real process transients* (natural PV excursions) as the stimulus.
- **Subsystem Tested (1 mark):** PST tests primarily the *final element* (valve / damper). OLT tests primarily the *sensor* and indirectly the *logic solver* ADC input.
- **Process Disturbance (1 mark):** PST causes a small, controlled movement. OLT causes *no additional disturbance*; it only observes what the process is already doing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Outside Limit Test in detail. Discuss its application in transmitter, logic solver, and final element subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

- **Conceptual Foundation (2 marks):** An Outside Limit Test is a continuous or periodic verification activity that exploits real or natural process transients to validate the health of a safety instrumented loop. It is distinct from a proof test, which is a scheduled, full-magnitude, often invasive check. The OLT works on the principle that any dangerous failure in the sensor (e.g., span shift, stuck output, calibration memory corruption) will manifest as an abnormal behaviour when the process variable attempts to leave the normal operating band.

- **Application in Transmitter (2 marks):** In a smart pressure transmitter, the microprocessor continuously samples the PV and compares it against the configured $PV_{low}$ and $PV_{high}$ thresholds. When the PV crosses $PV_{high}$, the transmitter increments an OLT counter. If the HART-reported PV value is consistent with the loop current (4–20 mA) within $\pm 0.1\%$, the OLT passes. If not, a diagnostic flag is set. This is also called a *boundary-event diagnostic* and is supported by Rosemount, Yokogawa, and Honeywell transmitters.

- **Application in Logic Solver (1.5 marks):** The Safety PLC (e.g., Siemens F-CPU, Allen-Bradley GuardLogix) monitors the rate-of-change and the absolute value of the input. An OLT failure is flagged if the input pattern is statistically inconsistent with the expected process model — for example, a pressure that never goes above 50% of range for 30 days in a loop whose $PV_{high}$ is set to 60% is flagged as *stuck-in-range* suspect.

- **Application in Final Element (1.5 marks):** Smart positioners on shut-down valves use OLT indirectly: the positioner correlates the control signal to the actual stem position during normal modulation. If the valve has a packing seizure or a stuck plug, the correlation falls outside the learned model, and a diagnostic alarm is raised — even without an explicit PST pulse.

---

**(b)** A temperature transmitter in a SIF has the following data: $\lambda_{DU} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour, $\lambda_{DD} = 4.2 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour, proof test interval $T_{proof} = 17520$ h (2 years), and the OLT diagnostic coverage (effective) $DC_{OLT} = 70\%$. Calculate the $PFD_{avg}$ with OLT and without OLT, and comment on the SIL achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

- **Step 1 — PFD without OLT (1.5 marks):**

$$PFD_{no\text{-}test} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$$

$$PFD_{no\text{-}test} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 17520$$

$$PFD_{no\text{-}test} = 1.5768 \times 10^{-3}$$

- **Step 2 — Residual DU rate after OLT (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - DC_{OLT})$$

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (1 - 0.70) = 1.8 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.30$$

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = 5.4 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{per hour}$$

- **Step 3 — PFD with OLT (1.5 marks):**

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU}^{residual} \cdot T_{proof}$$

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 5.4 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 17520$$

$$PFD_{OLT} = 4.73 \times 10^{-4}$$

- **Step 4 — SIL Classification (1.5 marks):**

| Quantity | Value | SIL Band | Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $PFD_{no\text{-}test}$ | $1.58 \times 10^{-3}$ | $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ | **SIL 2** |
| $PFD_{OLT}$ | $4.73 \times 10^{-4}$ | $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$ | **SIL 3** |

- **Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):** The OLT enables the sensor to step up from SIL 2 to SIL 3 capability *without* any hardware change, only through software-enabled diagnostics. This is a typical KTU-style "SIL upgrade by testing" scenario.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the architecture of an Outside Limit Test and explain how it provides diagnostic coverage of the sensor subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
*(Block diagram as per Section 4.1 Mermaid — to be redrawn by student on answer script.)*
- **Architecture (2 marks):** The OLT architecture consists of four functional blocks: (i) Process Variable Acquisition Unit, (ii) Limit Comparator Unit, (iii) Implicit Path Verifier, and (iv) Diagnostic Log & Annunciator.
- **Step-by-step (3 marks):** The PV is sampled, compared with $PV_{low}$ and $PV_{high}$, a trigger event is generated, the loop integrity is verified end-to-end, and the result is logged. A failure causes a maintenance alarm within MTTR.
- **Diagnostic Coverage Mechanism (2 marks):** The OLT detects dangerous failures by observing *dynamic* behaviour. If the sensor is *stuck*, the PV will never reach the boundary; if the *span* has drifted, the rate-of-change will not match the expected dynamic. These two symptoms together cover a large fraction of the DU failure space.

---

**(b)** A pressure transmitter SIF has $T_{proof} = 4380$ h (6 months) and $\lambda_{DU} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ per hour. Calculate the PFD with and without an OLT of coverage $DC_{OLT} = 80\%$. By what factor does the OLT reduce the PFD? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

- **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** PFD without OLT.

$$PFD_{no\text{-}test} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 4380 = 5.475 \times 10^{-4}$$

- **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Residual DU rate.

$$\lambda_{DU}^{residual} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (1 - 0.80) = 5.0 \times 10^{-8}$$

- **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** PFD with OLT.

$$PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 5.0 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 4380 = 1.095 \times 10^{-4}$$

- **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** PFD reduction factor.

$$\text{Factor} = \dfrac{PFD_{no\text{-}test}}{PFD_{OLT}} = \dfrac{5.475 \times 10^{-4}}{1.095 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.0$$

- **Step 5 (1 mark):** **Comment:** The OLT reduces the PFD by a factor of **5.0**, equivalent to a one-SIL-band improvement (since each SIL band spans a factor of 10). This confirms that effective diagnostics are equivalent to tenfold testing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse OLT with PST.** PST is for *valve* health; OLT is primarily for *sensor* health. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks in long answers.
> 2. **Always state the assumptions.** When computing $PFD_{avg}$, you MUST state that the 1oo1 simplified formula is used and that $T_{proof}$ is in hours. Marks are deducted for unit errors.
> 3. **Do not skip the SIL classification step.** A numerical $PFD$ value without mapping it to an SIL band is treated as an incomplete answer in KTU valuation keys.
> 4. **Mermaid diagrams are not allowed in the answer script.** Redraw the block diagram neatly with pen on the answer booklet. Use rectangular boxes, arrows, and clear labels.
> 5. **Coverage is dimensionless.** DC, SFF, CRF are all numbers between 0 and 1. Expressing DC = 70 is wrong; write DC = 0.70 or 70%.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Outside Limit Test (OLT)** is an on-line, non-invasive diagnostic that uses natural process transients to verify the safety path.
- It primarily covers the **sensor subsystem** and indirectly the **logic solver ADC**, and can be paired with **PST** for full coverage.
- The core formula is $PFD_{OLT} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - DC_{OLT}) \cdot T_{proof}$.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** quantifies the fraction of dangerous failures the OLT can detect.
- **Identity to memorise:** $CRF = 1 - DC_{OLT}$ — the PFD reduction factor equals one minus the diagnostic coverage.
- **SIL Band Identity:** A factor-of-10 reduction in $PFD$ corresponds to a one-band SIL improvement.
- **OLT depends on process variability.** A process that *never* leaves its normal band offers *no* OLT opportunity — in such cases, PST is mandatory.
- **Proof test interval is critical.** OLT augments, never replaces, the manual full proof test.
- **IEC 61511 Clause 16** is the governing standard for OLT operational phase.
- **MTTR = 8 hours** is the KTU default for repair time after an OLT-detected failure.
- **SIL bands to remember:** SIL 1 → $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 → $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 → $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4 → $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Forgetting the **½** factor in the PFD formula. The ½ comes from the average of the uniform failure distribution over the proof test interval.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Tests** in the context of Functional Safety (as per IEC 61511 / IEC 61508) are defined as **periodic, planned, and documented offline tests conducted on a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) to reveal undetected (latent) dangerous failures that have accumulated within the system during its operational life, and to verify that the SIS continues to meet its specified Safety Integrity Level (SIL).**

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Performance Test (also called a Proof Test)** is an explicit, on-demand functional verification of every component in the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) loop — namely the **Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element** — performed at a defined interval called the **Proof Test Interval (PTI)**.

The fundamental equation governing the average unavailability of a single-channel (1oo1) SIS due to dangerous undetected failures is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$$

where:
- $\lambda_D$ = Dangerous failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{\text{PT}}$ = Proof Test Interval (hours)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smoke Detector Analogy:**
> Imagine a smoke detector installed in your home. When you install it, it works perfectly. But over months, the dust accumulates on the sensor, the battery voltage drops, and the alarm horn may degrade. You **cannot tell** these faults have happened because the detector only activates during a real fire. So, once every 6 months, you press the **"TEST" button** on the device. The loud beep confirms the electronics, sensor, and horn are healthy. That monthly/bi-annual press is your **Performance Test (Proof Test)**.
> 
> If your smoke detector's battery dies *between* tests, your house is unprotected. The **average unprotected time** depends on how often you test it — exactly the role of $T_{\text{PT}}$ in functional safety!

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Highlight — Standardized Constants & Metrics:**
> - **Proof Test Coverage (PTC, denoted C):** Fraction of dangerous failures detected by the test. Range: **0 ≤ C ≤ 1 (or 0% to 100%)**.
> - **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR):** Typically **8 hours** (standard IEC 61511 assumption for restoration time).
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = 1/λ**.
> - **PFDavg Target per SIL (per IEC 61511-1, Table 3):**
>   - SIL 1 → **10⁻² ≤ PFDavg < 10⁻¹**
>   - SIL 2 → **10⁻³ ≤ PFDavg < 10⁻²**
>   - SIL 3 → **10⁻⁴ ≤ PFDavg < 10⁻³**
>   - SIL 4 → **10⁻⁵ ≤ PFDavg < 10⁻⁴**

## 1.4 Why Performance Tests Matter — The "Why" Behind the Concept

In a Safety Instrumented System, dangerous failures are split into two categories:
1. **Detected (Safe) Failures** — caught immediately by **Automatic Diagnostic Tests** (continuous self-test).
2. **Undetected (Dangerous) Failures** — accumulate silently until the next **Performance Test** reveals them.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction KTU Examiners Love to Test:**
> - **Diagnostic Tests** = automatic, online, continuous → reduces **MTTR**.
> - **Performance Tests (Proof Tests)** = manual/automated, offline, periodic → reduces **PFDavg** by uncovering latent dangerous failures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept — Broken Down Step-by-Step

The performance test cycle for any SIS follows a structured 5-step logic:

1. **Identify the SIF Loop** — Document all components in the Sensor–Logic Solver–Final Element chain.
2. **Isolate the SIS from the Process** — Place the process in a safe state (bypass valves / maintenance override).
3. **Apply a Simulated Trip Signal** — Inject a known test stimulus at the sensor input.
4. **Verify the Response** — Confirm that the logic solver correctly processes the signal and the final element (e.g., emergency shut-down valve) actually moves to its safe position.
5. **Restore to Operational State** — Reset logic, remove bypass, record the test result and date in the **Safety Lifecycle Documentation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" and "How" — The Underlying Logic:**
> Performance tests convert **dangerous undetected failures** into **dangerous detected failures** by manual intervention. The fraction of dangerous failures that the test can reveal is the **Proof Test Coverage (C)**. If C = 100%, every dangerous failure is found; if C = 60%, only 60% are revealed.

## 2.2 The "Average Probability of Failure on Demand" (PFDavg) Concept

The **PFDavg** is the *time-averaged unavailability* of the safety function over the interval between two proof tests.
