For a 1oo1 (one out of one) architecture with a single non-repairable failure mode, the failure rate is essentially constant, and the unavailability rises linearly from zero to a peak value $\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}$ at the end of the interval.

**Mathematical Justification:** If a component fails at a uniform random time within the interval $[0, T_{\text{PT}}]$, the expected value of the time-to-failure is $T_{\text{PT}}/2$. Therefore, the average unavailability over one proof test cycle is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_D \cdot \frac{T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$$

## 2.3 Proof Test Coverage (PTC) Refinement

When the proof test cannot detect *all* dangerous failure modes, only a fraction **C** is revealed. The remaining fraction (1 − C) remains latent. The effective failure rate becomes:

$$\lambda_{DU,\text{effective}} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - C)$$

And the refined PFDavg becomes:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU Examinable Formulas — Print This Table for Your Exam Day**

| # | Parameter / Architecture | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PFDavg (1oo1)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | $\lambda_D$: failures/hr; $T_{\text{PT}}$: hours |
| 2 | **PFDavg with Proof Test Coverage** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{(1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | $C$: PTC fraction (0 to 1) |
| 3 | **PFDavg (1oo2 — voted)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_D)^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}$ | Neglecting common cause |
| 4 | **PFDavg (2oo2 — voted)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \lambda_D \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | Both must fail (rare) |
| 5 | **PFDavg (2oo3 — voted)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{(\lambda_D)^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{4}$ | Best compromise architecture |
| 6 | **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** | $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_D + \lambda_{SU}}$ | Used in hardware fault tolerance rules |
| 7 | **Average Unavailability per Channel** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \displaystyle\int_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} (1 - e^{-\lambda_D t})\,dt$ | General integral form |
| 8 | **Mean Unavailability** | $\overline{u} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | For small $\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}$ |
| 9 | **Probability of Failure on Demand (single channel with MTTR)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_D \cdot \text{MTTR}$ | When MTTR $\ll$ PTI |
| 10 | **Maximum Allowable PTI** | $T_{\text{PT,max}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{target}}}{\lambda_D \cdot (1-C)}$ | Inverted for design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute value and fraction notations are written using `\vert` or `\dfrac` — **never** the vertical pipe `|` — to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **Oil & Gas:** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves on offshore platforms are proof-tested every 6 or 12 months.
> - **Chemical Plants:** High Integrity Pressure Protection Systems (HIPPS) are proof-tested at intervals derived from this exact math.
> - **Power Plants:** Reactor Trip Systems in nuclear facilities use SIL-3 architectures with quarterly proof tests.
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Batch reactor over-temperature protection systems.

The entire **SIL Verification Calculation** (SILCalc / exSILentia software tools) is built on these formulas — students mastering this topic can directly work as **Functional Safety Engineers** in process industries.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: PFDavg for a 1oo1 Channel from First Principles

### Step 1 — Setup the Hazard Function
For a single component with constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ (assumed small, so failures are rare), the probability that the component has **NOT failed** by time $t$ is the reliability function:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

### Step 2 — Define Unavailability
The instantaneous unavailability $u(t)$ — the probability the component is failed and dangerous at time $t$ — is:

$$u(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

### Step 3 — Average Over the Proof Test Interval
The average unavailability over the interval $[0, T_{\text{PT}}]$ is the time-average of $u(t)$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\right) dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral
Apply the standard integral rule $\int (1 - e^{-at}) dt = t + \dfrac{e^{-at}}{a} + \text{constant}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ t + \frac{e^{-\lambda_D t}}{\lambda_D} \right]_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ \left(T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}}{\lambda_D}\right) - \left(0 + \frac{1}{\lambda_D}\right) \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}} - 1}{\lambda_D} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Small-Failure-Rate Approximation
For typical industrial values, $\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}} \ll 1$ (e.g., $10^{-6}/\text{hr} \times 8760 \text{ hr} \approx 0.009$). Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + x^2/2 - \dots$, we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}} &\approx 1 - \lambda_D T_{\text{PT}} \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}} - 1 &\approx -\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}} \\[6pt]
\text{Substituting back:} \quad \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ T_{\text{PT}} - \frac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}{\lambda_D} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ T_{\text{PT}} - T_{\text{PT}} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — both terms cancel! This is because the leading-order Taylor series exactly cancels. We need to expand to **second order**:

$$e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}} \approx 1 - \lambda_D T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}} - 1 &\approx -\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{1}{\lambda_D}\left(-\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{2}\right) \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \left[ T_{\text{PT}} - T_{\text{PT}} + \frac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}^2}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}^2}{2 T_{\text{PT}}} = \frac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Final Result (1oo1 Architecture):**

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Including Proof Test Coverage (C)

The fraction (1 − C) of dangerous failures remains undetected. These failures accumulate just as before, but only over the undetected portion of the failure rate. Replacing $\lambda_D$ with $\lambda_{DU,\text{eff}} = \lambda_D \cdot (1 - C)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU,\text{eff}} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2} \\[6pt]
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{(1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: PFDavg for 1oo2 Architecture

In a 1oo2 system, the safety function is tripped if **either** channel trips. The dangerous failure occurs when **both** channels have failed dangerously — a much rarer event because both channels must fail within the interval.

For small $\lambda_D$, the joint probability that **both** channels fail by time $t$ is approximately:

$$u_{1oo2}(t) \approx \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\right)^2 \approx (\lambda_D t)^2$$

The average is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\lambda_D)^2}{T_{\text{PT}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} t^2 \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\lambda_D)^2}{T_{\text{PT}}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{PT}}^3}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}})^2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Including the standard simplification (which replaces the factor 3 with 6 because only one of the two channels being failed undetected matters for the system trip), the IEC 61511 simplified formula is:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Architectures

| Architecture | PFDavg Formula | Typical Magnitude | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** | $\dfrac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Low SIL, single sensor |
| **1oo2** | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}$ | $\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | High SIL, voted trip |
| **2oo2** | $\dfrac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ | $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Fire & gas, fire-doors |
| **2oo3** | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{4}$ | $\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Turbine overspeed trip |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a 1oo1 SIF has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The Proof Test Interval is $T_{\text{PT}} = 8760$ hours (1 year). The Proof Test Coverage is C = 90%.

**Find:** (a) PFDavg, (b) Verify SIL level achieved.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Part (a) — PFDavg calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{(1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(1 - 0.9) \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.1 \times 0.0438}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.00438}{2} = 0.00219
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — SIL Verification:**

PFDavg = $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$, which falls in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ → **SIL 2 achieved**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIS Loop Architecture with Proof Test Insertion Points

The following Mermaid diagram shows a complete Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) loop and indicates **exactly where Performance Tests are inserted** to validate each segment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC[Process Variable\ne.g. Pressure, Temp] --> S1[Sensor 1\nTransmitter]
    S1 --> S2[Sensor 2\nRedundant]
    S2 --> LS[Logic Solver\nPLC / Safety PLC]
    LS --> FE1[Final Element 1\nShut-Down Valve A]
    LS --> FE2[Final Element 2\nShut-Down Valve B]
    FE1 --> OUT[Safe State\nProcess Isolated]

    PT_S[Performance Test\nPoint - Sensor] -.-> S1
    PT_S -.-> S2
    PT_L[Performance Test\nPoint - Logic] -.-> LS
    PT_FE[Performance Test\nPoint - Final Element] -.-> FE1
    PT_FE -.-> FE2

    DIAG[Automatic\nDiagnostics] -.-> LS

    classDef sensor fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#e1eaff,stroke:#33c,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#3c3,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc3,color:#000
    class S1,S2 sensor
    class LS logic
    class FE1,FE2 final
    class PT_S,PT_L,PT_FE,DIAG test
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = normal signal/process flow during operation.
- **Dashed arrows** = Performance Test injection points where a simulated trip signal is injected to verify each block works.
- **Yellow block** `DIAG` represents *automatic* diagnostics (continuous), distinct from *manual* performance tests.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Proof Test Lifecycle

This diagram captures the **state transitions** of a single SIS channel as it moves through one complete proof test cycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Operational
    Operational --> Operational : Normal Operation\n(monitor process)
    Operational --> TestInProgress : Scheduled\nPTI elapsed
    TestInProgress --> Operational : Test Passed\nDocument result
    TestInProgress --> Maintenance : Test Failed
    Maintenance --> Operational : Repair complete\nMTTR ~ 8 hours
    Operational --> Operational : Dangerous\nUndetected Failure\naccumulates silently
    Operational --> Operational : Dangerous\nDetected Failure\ncaught by Diag
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The loop `Operational → Operational` (with annotation "Dangerous Undetected Failure") is the **latent risk window** — this is exactly what PFDavg quantifies.
- The transition `TestInProgress → Maintenance` reveals that a **failed performance test** triggers repair (MTTR).
- The shorter the loop between **Operational → TestInProgress → Operational**, the lower the PFDavg.

---

## 4.3 Architecture Voting Logic (1oo1 / 1oo2 / 2oo3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Channel A] --> V1{1oo1\nVoter}
    V1 --> Trip1[Trip Output]

    A2[Channel A] --> V2{1oo2\nOR Voter}
    B2[Channel B] --> V2
    V2 --> Trip2[Trip Output]

    A3[Channel A] --> V3{2oo3\nMajority Voter}
    B3[Channel B] --> V3
    C3[Channel C] --> V3
    V3 --> Trip3[Trip Output]

    classDef oneooone fill:#ffd5d5,stroke:#900
    classDef oneootwo fill:#d5e8ff,stroke:#009
    classDef twooothree fill:#d5ffe8,stroke:#090
    classDef voter fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa0
    class A1,V1,Trip1 oneooone
    class A2,B2,V2,Trip2 oneootwo
    class A3,B3,C3,V3,Trip3 twooothree
```

**Key Insight for KTU Exam:**
- In **1oo2** (OR voter): one failed channel → system trips → *high availability*, but **dangerous failure** requires both to fail → very low PFDavg.
- In **2oo3** (Majority voter): one failure does NOT trip, two must agree → *high safety against spurious trips*, but PFDavg is intermediate.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Performance Test in the context of IEC 61511. Differentiate it from Automatic Diagnostic Testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Performance Test** is a periodic, manual or automated offline test performed on a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) at specified intervals (the Proof Test Interval, $T_{\text{PT}}$) to detect accumulated dangerous undetected failures and to verify that the safety function continues to meet its specified SIL.

**Differentiation:**
| Aspect | Performance Test | Automatic Diagnostic Test |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Periodic (every $T_{\text{PT}}$) | Continuous (real-time) |
| Mode | Offline / partial shutdown | Online (no shutdown) |
| Detects | Latent dangerous failures | Detected dangerous failures |
| Effect on metric | Reduces PFDavg | Reduces MTTR |
| Coverage | Proof Test Coverage (C) | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) |

*(Award full 3 marks for clear definition + any 2 differentiation points.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is Proof Test Coverage (PTC)? A pressure transmitter has a PTC of 85%. Explain the practical implication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Proof Test Coverage (PTC or C)** is the fraction of dangerous failures that are detected by the performance test. It ranges from 0% to 100%.

If **C = 85%**, then **15%** of dangerous failures remain undetected even after the test. The effective dangerous undetected failure rate is:

$$\lambda_{DU,\text{eff}} = (1 - 0.85) \cdot \lambda_D = 0.15 \, \lambda_D$$

**Practical implication:** The PFDavg is multiplied by 0.15 (instead of being reduced to zero). Therefore, to compensate, either $T_{\text{PT}}$ must be reduced (more frequent testing) or a redundant (voted) architecture must be chosen to achieve the target SIL.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q: A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) consists of a single pressure transmitter, a Safety PLC, and a shut-down valve arranged in a 1oo1 architecture.**

**Given:**
- Dangerous failure rate of sensor: $\lambda_{D,\text{sensor}} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Dangerous failure rate of logic solver: $\lambda_{D,\text{logic}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Dangerous failure rate of final element: $\lambda_{D,\text{FE}} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Proof Test Interval: $T_{\text{PT}} = 4380$ hours (6 months)
- Proof Test Coverage: C = 100% for sensor, 90% for logic solver, 80% for final element
- Target SIL: 2

**Tasks:**
**(a)** Calculate the **PFDavg for each component** and the **total PFDavg** for the SIF. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Verify whether the SIF meets **SIL 2**. Comment on the contribution of each component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — PFDavg Calculations (7 marks)

**General formula (1oo1):**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg},i} = \frac{(1 - C_i) \cdot \lambda_{D,i} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$$

**Sensor (C = 100% = 1.0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,sensor}} &= \frac{(1 - 1.0) \times (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4380}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0 \times 0.01314}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Solver (C = 90% = 0.9):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,logic}} &= \frac{(1 - 0.9) \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4380}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.1 \times 0.00438}{2} = \frac{4.38 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 2.19 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Element (C = 80% = 0.8):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,FE}} &= \frac{(1 - 0.8) \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4380}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.2 \times 0.00876}{2} = \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total PFDavg (sum of independent channels):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,total}} &= 0 + 2.19 \times 10^{-4} + 8.76 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 10.95 \times 10^{-4} = 1.095 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — SIL Verification & Component Contribution (7 marks)

**SIL Check:** 

PFDavg = $1.095 \times 10^{-3}$. 

This falls in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$ → **SIL 2 is achieved** ✅.

**Component Contribution (as percentage of total):**

- Sensor: $\dfrac{0}{1.095 \times 10^{-3}} = 0\%$
- Logic Solver: $\dfrac{2.19 \times 10^{-4}}{1.095 \times 10^{-3}} = 20\%$
- Final Element: $\dfrac{8.76 \times 10^{-4}}{1.095 \times 10^{-3}} = 80\%$

**Conclusion / Recommendation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Final Element (shut-down valve) contributes 80%** of the total PFDavg. To improve SIL margin:
> - Increase PTC for the valve (better partial-stroke testing).
> - Reduce $T_{\text{PT}}$ (e.g., quarterly instead of semi-annually).
> - Consider a 1oo2 sensor architecture (cost-effective since sensor currently contributes nothing but redundancy adds protection against detected failures).

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the concept of Proof Test Coverage in detail. For a SIF with the following components, determine the maximum allowable Proof Test Interval to achieve SIL 3.**

| Component | $\lambda_D$ (per hr) | PTC (C) | Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | $2 \times 10^{-6}$ | 95% | 1oo2 |
| Logic Solver | $5 \times 10^{-7}$ | 99% | 1oo1 |
| Final Element | $3 \times 10^{-6}$ | 90% | 1oo2 |

**Tasks:**
**(a)** Derive the **PFDavg formulas for 1oo2 architecture** and explain each term. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the **maximum allowable PTI** such that total PFDavg ≤ $10^{-3}$ (SIL 3 boundary). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — PFDavg Derivation for 1oo2 (7 marks)

In a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) architecture**, the SIF trips when *any one* of two parallel channels demands a trip. The dangerous failure of the SIF requires **both** channels to have failed dangerously. For independent channels with identical failure rates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}\right)^2 \approx (\lambda_D t)^2 \quad \text{for small } \lambda_D t \\[6pt]
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{PT}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{PT}}} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\lambda_D)^2}{T_{\text{PT}}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{PT}}^3}{3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_{\text{PT}})^2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

After applying the standard simplification (only one channel failing dangerously undetected suffices to cause SIF failure in 1oo2 when considering the OR logic with $\beta$-factor):

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \frac{(\lambda_D)^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}}$$

**Each term explained:**
- $(\lambda_D)^2$: joint failure probability (both must fail).
- $(T_{\text{PT}})^2$: quadratic dependence on the interval — much less sensitive than 1oo1.
- Division by 6: integration of $t^2$ over the interval, halved by symmetry.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Maximum Allowable PTI Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate each component's PFDavg as a function of $T_{\text{PT}}$.**

**Sensor (1oo2):**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,sensor}} = \frac{[(1 - 0.95) \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6}]^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}
$$

**Logic Solver (1oo1):**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,logic}} = \frac{(1 - 0.99) \cdot 5 \times 10^{-7} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2}
$$

**Final Element (1oo2):**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,FE}} = \frac{[(1 - 0.90) \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6}]^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot (T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Total PFDavg expression:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,total}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-14} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}^2}{6} + \frac{5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}}{2} + \frac{9 \times 10^{-14} \cdot T_{\text{PT}}^2}{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Set total ≤ $10^{-3}$ (SIL 3 boundary):**

For an iterative solution, assume the logic solver (linear term) dominates for reasonable intervals. Testing $T_{\text{PT}} = 40,000$ hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logic term} &= 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 40000 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
\text{Sensor term} &= 1.67 \times 10^{-15} \times (40000)^2 \approx 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
\text{FE term} &= 1.5 \times 10^{-14} \times (40000)^2 \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
\text{Total} &\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 0.0267 \times 10^{-3} + 0.024 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.29 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies SIL 3. Trying $T_{\text{PT}} = 1 \times 10^5$ hours (~11.4 years) — but this is impractical.

**Practical recommendation:** $T_{\text{PT,max}} \approx 1 \times 10^5$ hours (~11 years) theoretically, but in practice cap at **1 year (8760 hours)** to allow margin and meet real-world maintenance schedules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer (boxed):** Maximum allowable $T_{\text{PT}} \approx 1 \times 10^5$ hours from PFDavg calculation, but practically constrained to **8760 hours (annual proof testing)** for operational manageability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor (1 − C):** Many students write $\lambda_D$ instead of $(1 - C) \cdot \lambda_D$ when PTC is less than 100%. This is a **2-mark penalty** per occurrence.
> 2. **Using 1oo1 formula for 1oo2 architecture:** The $(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2 / 6$ formula is fundamentally different. Mistaking it loses **3-4 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the summation principle:** PFDavg of the SIF = sum of PFDavg of each independent subsystem. Do **not** multiply them.
> 4. **No units in the final answer:** Always write the PFDavg with proper scientific notation and unit "per demand" (dimensionless probability).
> 5. **Confusing MTTR with PTI:** MTTR is typically 8 hours; PTI is typically thousands of hours. They are *not* interchangeable in the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before the Exam:**
> - **Performance Test = Proof Test** = periodic offline test revealing latent dangerous failures.
> - **Proof Test Interval (PTI)** = time between consecutive proof tests.
> - **PFDavg (1oo1)** = $\dfrac{\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}}{2}$ — linear dependence on interval.
> - **PFDavg (1oo2)** = $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}})^2}{6}$ — quadratic dependence (much better!).
> - **PTC (C)** = fraction of dangerous failures detected by the test; $0 \le C \le 1$.
> - **Effective dangerous failure rate with PTC:** $\lambda_{DU,\text{eff}} = (1 - C) \lambda_D$.
> - **SIL target ranges:** SIL 1: $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$; SIL 2: $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$; SIL 3: $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3})$; SIL 4: $[10^{-5}, 10^{-4})$.
> - **SIF PFDavg** = sum of PFDavgs of Sensor + Logic Solver + Final Element (independent channels).
> - **MTTR (8 hours)** ≠ PTI. MTTR is repair time after a test failure; PTI is the cycle period.
> - **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** detects failures *online* — complements, does NOT replace, proof tests.
> - **Rule of Thumb:** Halving the PTI halves the PFDavg in 1oo1 architecture.
> - **Best Architecture for high SIL:** 1oo2 voted sensors + 1oo2 voted final elements + redundant logic solver.
> - **Standards to cite in answers:** IEC 61511-1 (process industry), IEC 61508 (generic), ISA 84.00.01.
> - **Always state assumptions:** small $\lambda_D T_{\text{PT}}$, independent channels, no common cause failures (unless $\beta$ given).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interface Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Interface Tests** in the context of Functional Safety are a category of systematic verification activities performed to confirm that all *physical, electrical, communication, and logical boundaries* between the Safety Instrumented System (SIS) and the external world (field devices, operators, BPCS, other systems, and power supplies) operate as specified in the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS).

As per **IEC 61508** (Part 1, Clause 7) and **IEC 61511** (Clause 11.5), interface tests are mandatory activities during the SIS **integration** and **validation** lifecycle phases. They confirm the *compatibility, data integrity, deterministic behavior, and fault tolerance* of all interconnections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Interface tests are the bridge between *component-level validation* and *system-level validation*. Without these tests, individual safety components may be perfect, but the *integrated safety function* could fail catastrophically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Control Tower" Model

Imagine an **airport control tower** (the Safety Logic Solver) communicating with **pilots in cockpits** (field devices) through **radios** (interface channels). An interface test is equivalent to:

- **Checking that the radio frequency is correctly tuned** (signal integrity)
- **Verifying that voice commands are understood without misinterpretation** (data integrity)
- **Confirming that if a radio fails, a backup channel activates** (redundancy / fault tolerance)
- **Ensuring the tower can *command* an emergency landing** (control direction)
- **Ensuring the pilot's *response* (e.g., transponder code) is received** (feedback direction)

If any of these "interfaces" fail silently, the entire safety chain collapses — even if both the tower and the aircraft are functioning correctly individually.

### 1.3 Categories of Interfaces in Functional Safety

| Interface Class | Example | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Field-to-Logic** | Transmitter → Safety PLC | Input (analog/digital) |
| **Logic-to-Field** | Safety PLC → Solenoid Valve | Output (switching) |
| **Operator Interface** | HMI/SCADA → Safety System | Bidirectional |
| **System-to-System** | SIS ↔ BPCS (DCS) | Bidirectional |
| **Power Interface** | UPS → SIS | Unidirectional (supply) |
| **Communication** | Safety Bus (Profisafe, CIP Safety) | Bidirectional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Standards:** All interface tests must reference the *Safety Requirements Specification* (SRS) and trace back to the *Cause & Effect Matrix* (C&E) and *P&ID*. Any deviation constitutes a **non-conformance** requiring a Management of Change (MOC) action.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

Interface testing follows a structured 6-step methodology aligned with the **V-Model** of functional safety:

- **Step 1 — Interface Identification:** Enumerate every physical, logical, and communication port from the SRS. Output: *Interface List Document*.
- **Step 2 — Test Specification:** Define *test cases*, *acceptance criteria*, *signal ranges*, and *fault injection points*. Output: *Interface Test Plan*.
- **Step 3 — Test Environment Setup:** Configure calibration equipment (e.g., HART communicator, multimeter, signal generator) and isolate the system to prevent process upset.
- **Step 4 — Stimulus Application:** Inject predefined *normal* and *abnormal* signals (e.g., 4–20 mA with HART overlay) into the interface boundary.
- **Step 5 — Response Verification:** Compare the system's response against expected behavior. *Time-stamped logs* and *trend recordings* are mandatory evidence.
- **Step 6 — Documentation & Traceability:** Record results in the *Validation Summary Report*. Each test must have a *Traceability Matrix* entry linking it to a specific SRS clause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** The V-Model ensures that *every abstraction level* (sensor → logic → actuator) is tested as a *whole*, not just as parts. Interface tests cover the *seams* between these abstraction levels.

### 2.2 Types of Interface Tests

- **2.2.1 Galvanic Isolation Test** — Verifies that interface circuits maintain proper isolation voltage (typically **500 V AC** or higher per IEC 61131-2) to prevent ground loops.
- **2.2.2 Signal Integrity Test** — Confirms analog signals (4–20 mA) maintain linearity, **HART** communication, and noise rejection across the interface cable.
- **2.2.3 Communication Protocol Test** — Validates deterministic behavior of safety protocols (**Profisafe, CIP Safety, PROFIBUS-PA**). Checks *watchdog time*, *consecutive number*, and *CRC* checks.
- **2.2.4 Operator Interface Test** — Confirms that the *Human-Machine Interface* (HMI) can display alarms, acknowledge events, and bypass commands without corrupting SIS state.
- **2.2.5 Power Interface Test** — Validates *ride-through* capability (typically **20 ms**) during supply interruptions, and proper switchover to redundant power.
- **2.2.6 Bypass / Override Interface Test** — Confirms that bypass commands are *logged, time-limited*, and require *permission* (engineering or maintenance level access).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Standard / Formula | Typical Value | Unit / Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Analog Signal Range (Current Loop) | $I_{min} = 4\ \text{mA}$, $I_{max} = 20\ \text{mA}$ | 4 – 20 | mA |
| Fault Current Detection (NAMUR NE 43) | $I \leq 3.6$ or $I \geq 21.0$ | 3.6 / 21.0 | mA (fault threshold) |
| Galvanic Isolation Voltage | $V_{iso} \geq 500$ | 500 | V AC (per IEC 61131-2) |
| Power Ride-Through | $t_{rt} \geq 20$ | 20 | ms |
| Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{repair,i}}{N}$ | Variable | hours |
| Diagnostic Coverage (DC) | $\text{DC} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}} \times 100$ | 60 – 99 | % |
| Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | 60 – 99 | % |
| Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg) | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{t_{CE}\cdot\lambda_{DU}}{2}$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ | dimensionless (per IEC 61508) |
| Proof Test Interval | $T_{proof}$ | 1 – 10 | years |
| SIL Availability Check | $\text{PFD}_{avg} \leq 10^{-(SIL+1)}$ | — | See IEC 61508 Table 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **NAMUR NE 43** is a critical industry standard that defines *failure currents* for 4–20 mA loops. Any current outside the [3.8, 20.5] mA "valid" range is interpreted as a *fault* by the safety logic solver.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interface tests are deployed across virtually every safety-critical industry:

- **Oil & Gas:** Verifying ESD (Emergency Shutdown) valves respond to fire & gas detector signals.
- **Nuclear Power:** Validating reactor trip system interfaces with the plant protection system.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Testing CAN, LIN, and FlexRay bus interfaces for ADAS functions.
- **Process Industries (IEC 61511):** Confirming SIS-BPCS separation to avoid common-cause failures.
- **Railway (EN 50128/50129):** Validating ATP (Automatic Train Protection) interfaces with trackside equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) from Failure Rate Data

The **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** is one of the most important interface-test metrics because it directly determines whether a *Type B* subsystem can achieve a target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

**Starting Definition (per IEC 61508):**

$$
\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{D}}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{S}$ = Failure rate of *safe* failures (per hour)
- $\lambda_{DD}$ = Failure rate of *dangerous detected* failures (per hour)
- $\lambda_{D}$ = Total failure rate of *dangerous* failures (per hour) = $\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\lambda_{D} = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ into the denominator:

$$
\text{SFF} = \frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by 1 to normalize. Define total failure rate:

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}
$$

**Step 3:** Express SFF in terms of total:

$$
\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The SFF depends *only* on the *undetected dangerous failure rate*. **Interface tests must specifically target $\lambda_{DU}$** by exercising *diagnostic* paths and verifying that *detected* failures are actually *detected*.

### 3.2 Analytical Example — Interface Test for a 4–20 mA Pressure Transmitter

**Scenario:** A safety pressure transmitter must trigger an SIS shutdown at **15 bar**. The HART protocol overlays a digital signal on the 4–20 mA loop. The interface test must verify *both* the analog pressure signal *and* the HART digital communication.

**Step 1:** Apply a calibrated pressure of **5.0 bar** using a dead-weight tester.

**Step 2:** Measure the loop current using a calibrated multimeter (resolution ± 0.01 mA).

**Step 3:** Calculate the expected current using the linear transfer function:

$$
I = 4\ \text{mA} + \left(\frac{20\ \text{mA} - 4\ \text{mA}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}\right) \cdot P_{measured}
$$

Assuming $P_{min} = 0$ bar and $P_{max} = 25$ bar:

$$
I = 4 + \frac{16}{25} \cdot 5.0 = 4 + 3.2 = 7.20\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify NAMUR NE 43 validity window:

$$
3.8\ \text{mA} \leq I_{measured} \leq 20.5\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 5:** Send HART command `Read PV` and verify the digital value matches the analog value within **± 0.5%** of span.

**Step 6:** Gradually increase pressure to the **trip setpoint (15 bar)** and verify:
- The safety logic solver receives the trip signal.
- The output to the final element (e.g., shutdown valve) is energized.
- The HMI displays the alarm with correct *time-stamping*.
- The event is logged in the *Sequence of Events* (SOE) recorder with **± 1 ms** resolution.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Interface Test Pass/Fail

| Test Aspect | Acceptance Criterion | Failure Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Analog signal accuracy | $\vert I_{measured} - I_{expected} \vert \leq 0.5\% \cdot \text{span}$ | Minor (rectify + re-test) |
| HART digital data integrity | CRC = valid; PV matches analog | Major (replace device) |
| Response time | $t_{response} \leq t_{SRS}$ | Major |
| Galvanic isolation | $V_{iso} \geq 500$ V AC for 60 s | Critical (re-design) |
| Power ride-through | No system reset for $t_{supply\ loss} \leq 20$ ms | Critical |
| Operator alarm acknowledgement | HMI event logged with operator ID + timestamp | Minor |
| Bypass / Override | Engineering password required; bypass auto-clears | Major (procedural) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Students often confuse *acceptance criteria* with *design criteria*. **Acceptance criteria** are derived from the SRS *after* design; they are the *measuring stick* during interface tests. Never use a *design* value as an *acceptance* value without independent verification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interface Test Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    fieldA[Field Devices: Sensors and Transmitters] --> intfA[Input Interface: Analog Digital HART]
    intfA --> sls[Safety Logic Solver: SIS PLC]
    sls --> intfB[Output Interface: Relay Profisafe Solenoid Driver]
    intfB --> fieldB[Final Elements: Valves Motors Heaters]
    sls --> intfC[Operator Interface: HMI SCADA]
    sls --> intfD[BPCS Interface: Modbus Ethernet]
    sls --> intfE[Power Interface: UPS Redundant Supply]
    sls --> intfF[SOE Interface: Sequence of Events Recorder]
    intfC --> op1[Operator Console]
    intfD --> bp1[Basic Process Control System]
    intfE --> pwr1[Main Power Source]
    intfF --> soe1[Event Logger]
    test1[Interface Test Stimulus Generator] -.-> intfA
    test2[Interface Test Response Validator] -.-> intfB
    test3[HMI Simulator] -.-> intfC
    test4[Power Dip Simulator] -.-> intfE
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Interface Test Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ENGR as Test Engineer
    participant TC as Test Coordinator
    participant SIS as Safety Logic Solver
    participant FLD as Field Device
    participant HMI as Operator Interface
    participant LOG as SOE Logger
    ENGR ->> TC: Submit Test Permit and Isolation Request
    TC ->> SIS: Apply Maintenance Override
    ENGR ->> FLD: Inject Calibrated Stimulus
    FLD ->> SIS: Transmit Signal
    SIS ->> LOG: Log Event with Timestamp
    SIS ->> HMI: Display Alarm
    ENGR ->> HMI: Verify Display and Acknowledgement
    ENGR ->> SIS: Remove Override and Restore
    SIS ->> LOG: Log Restoration Event
    ENGR ->> TC: Submit Test Report
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Interface Verification States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Setup: Test Initiated
    Setup --> StimulusApply: Environment Ready
    StimulusApply --> ResponseCheck: Signal Injected
    ResponseCheck --> Pass: Meets Acceptance Criteria
    ResponseCheck --> Fail: Deviation Detected
    Fail --> Rectify: Issue NCR Non Conformance Report
    Rectify --> StimulusApply: Retest
    Pass --> Documented: Evidence Captured
    Documented --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *Interface Test* as applied to a Safety Instrumented System. List any **four** categories of interfaces that must be tested.

**Model Answer:**
An *Interface Test* in a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is a verification activity that confirms the *correct exchange of signals, data, and power* between the SIS and external systems across physical, electrical, communication, and logical boundaries. It validates that the *integrated safety function* operates as specified in the Safety Requirements Specification (SRS).

The four major categories of interfaces are:
- **Field-to-Logic Interface** (transmitter to safety PLC)
- **Logic-to-Field Interface** (safety PLC to final element)
- **Operator Interface** (HMI/SCADA to SIS)
- **Communication Interface** (SIS to BPCS or other networks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State the **NAMUR NE 43** standard. What is the significance of the *3.8 mA* and *20.5 mA* thresholds?

**Model Answer:**
**NAMUR NE 43** is an international recommendation that standardizes the *failure information* for 4–20 mA analog signal transmitters. The standard defines three regions:

- **Valid measurement region:** 3.8 mA $\leq I \leq$ 20.5 mA → represents the *normal* process variable.
- **Under-range fault region:** $I \leq 3.6$ mA → indicates a *sensor failure* (e.g., broken wire, open circuit).
- **Over-range fault region:** $I \geq 21.0$ mA → indicates a *sensor failure* (e.g., short circuit, out-of-calibration).

The **3.8 mA** and **20.5 mA** thresholds are the *validity limits* within which the signal is interpreted as a true process measurement. Interface tests must verify that the safety logic solver correctly distinguishes between *valid process values* and *fault conditions*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain with a suitable block diagram, the **six major categories of interfaces** that must be tested in a Safety Instrumented System per IEC 61511. For each interface, state the *direction* of signal flow and give one *real-world example*.

**Model Solution:**

The six major interface categories tested in an SIS per IEC 61511 are:

**1. Field-to-Logic Input Interface (Input Direction):**
- Carries sensor signals (analog or digital) from the field to the safety logic solver.
- *Example:* 4–20 mA pressure transmitter signal entering a Safety PLC analog input module.
- *Test objective:* Verify linearity, accuracy, and HART communication.

**2. Logic-to-Field Output Interface (Output Direction):**
- Carries actuation commands from the safety logic solver to the final element.
- *Example:* Safety PLC output energizing a solenoid coil of an ESD valve.
- *Test objective:* Verify proper switching, voltage levels, and load driving capability.

**3. Operator Interface (Bidirectional):**
- Allows operator interaction with the SIS for alarm acknowledgement, bypass, and trending.
- *Example:* HMI/SCADA display of SIS alarms and operator override commands.
- *Test objective:* Verify display correctness, acknowledgement logging, and access control.

**4. System-to-System Communication Interface (Bidirectional):**
- Exchanges data between SIS and BPCS (or between multiple SIS subsystems).
- *Example:* Profisafe link between safety PLC and distributed I/O.
- *Test objective:* Verify protocol conformance, watchdog timing, and CRC integrity.

**5. Power Interface (Unidirectional Supply):**
- Supplies conditioned power from UPS / battery to the SIS.
- *Example:* 24 V DC power feed from redundant UPS to safety I/O cards.
- *Test objective:* Verify ride-through, redundancy switchover, and voltage regulation.

**6. Sequence of Events (SOE) Interface (Output Direction):**
- Records time-stamped events from the SIS for post-incident analysis.
- *Example:* SOE recorder capturing valve closure timestamp with ±1 ms resolution.
- *Test objective:* Verify timestamp accuracy and event sequence integrity.

*(Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

---

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A safety pressure transmitter has a range of **0 to 30 bar** and outputs a 4–20 mA signal. During an interface test, a dead-weight tester applies a pressure of **12 bar**. Calculate the *expected loop current* and verify whether the signal falls within the *NAMUR NE 43 validity window*. State the *interface test acceptance criterion* in mA.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the linear transfer function:**

$$
I_{expected} = 4\ \text{mA} + \left(\frac{20 - 4}{30 - 0}\right) \cdot 12
$$

$$
I_{expected} = 4 + \left(\frac{16}{30}\right) \cdot 12
$$

$$
I_{expected} = 4 + 6.4 = 10.40\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify NAMUR NE 43 validity window:**

The valid measurement region per NAMUR NE 43 is:

$$
3.8\ \text{mA} \leq I \leq 20.5\ \text{mA}
$$

Since $3.8 \leq 10.40 \leq 20.5$, the signal is **VALID**.

**Step 3 — State the acceptance criterion:**

The interface test acceptance criterion is that the measured current must be within **± 0.5% of span** of the expected value.

Span = 16 mA, so tolerance = $0.005 \times 16 = 0.08$ mA.

Acceptable range: $10.40 \pm 0.08$ mA → **[10.32, 10.48] mA**.

If the measured current falls within this range, the test **PASSES**; otherwise, a *Non-Conformance Report* (NCR) is raised.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the **role of interface tests in the V-Model lifecycle** of functional safety. Explain how interface tests bridge *component-level validation* and *system-level validation*.

**Model Solution:**

**The V-Model Context:**
The V-Model of functional safety (per IEC 61508) maps *design phases* on the left arm to *validation phases* on the right arm. Interface testing sits at the **bottom vertex** of the V, where *integration testing* occurs.

**Role of Interface Tests:**

1. **Bridge between Subsystem and System:** Individual components (sensors, logic solvers, valves) are tested at the *unit* level. Interface tests verify that when these components are *integrated*, the *seams* between them do not introduce latent failures.

2. **Verification of Design Intent:** The design specifies how components must interact (e.g., HART communication on a 4–20 mA loop). Interface tests confirm that the *integrated system* realizes this design intent.

3. **Fault Coverage Validation:** Interface tests inject *faults* (e.g., broken wire, short circuit, communication loss) at boundaries to verify *diagnostic coverage* and *fail-safe* behavior.

4. **Configuration Verification:** Many interface failures arise from *configuration mismatches* (e.g., mismatched baud rate, incorrect addressing). Interface tests catch these *before commissioning*.

5. **Documentation of Evidence:** Interface test reports provide *objective evidence* for the *Safety Case* and *Validation Summary Report* (VSR) required by regulatory bodies.

6. **Risk Reduction in Commissioning:** By performing interface tests in a *controlled* environment, the *risk of process upset* during live commissioning is minimized.

7. **Baseline for Future Proof Tests:** The results of the initial interface test establish a *baseline* against which *future proof tests* (typically every 1–10 years) are compared.

---

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A Safety PLC has the following failure rate data (per IEC 61508):

- $\lambda_{S} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour (safe failures)
- $\lambda_{DD} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour (dangerous detected)
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour (dangerous undetected)

Calculate the **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** and the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** assuming a proof test interval $T_{proof} = 2$ years (17,520 hours).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Failure Rate:**

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}
$$

$$
\lambda_{total} = 5 \times 10^{-6} + 8 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} = 15 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{per hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Safe Failure Fraction (SFF):**

$$
\text{SFF} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}} = 1 - \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\text{SFF} = 1 - 0.1333 = 0.8667 \approx 86.67\%
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate PFDavg:**

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{T_{proof} \cdot \lambda_{DU}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg} = \frac{17520 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{PFD}_{avg} = 17520 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01752
$$

**Step 4 — Check SIL Compliance:**

For **SIL 1:** $\text{PFD}_{avg} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$ → 0.01752 is within range ✓
For **SIL 2:** $\text{PFD}_{avg} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$ → 0.01752 is **above** the SIL 2 upper limit ✗

**Conclusion:** The system meets **SIL 1** but not **SIL 2**. To improve, the proof test interval must be reduced, or the $\lambda_{DU}$ must be lowered through additional diagnostics.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Interface Test Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PFDavg with PFH:** PFDavg is for *low-demand* mode; PFH is for *high-demand/continuous* mode. Using the wrong formula costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Proof test interval $T_{proof}$ must be in *hours* when failure rates are in *per hour*. Mixing years and hours is a frequent error.
> 3. **Omitting acceptance criteria:** When describing an interface test, students often list *what* to test but forget *how* to decide *pass/fail*. Always state the acceptance criterion with a numerical threshold.
> 4. **Ignoring NAMUR NE 43:** Any 4–20 mA interface test answer that does not mention the [3.8, 20.5] mA validity window is considered *incomplete* by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Missing traceability:** Interface tests must be *traceable* to the SRS clause and the C&E matrix entry. Omitting this loses marks for "documentation rigor."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interface Test =** Verification of all physical, electrical, communication, and logical *boundaries* of the SIS.
- **Six Categories:** Field-Input, Field-Output, Operator, System-to-System, Power, SOE.
- **NAMUR NE 43 validity window:** 3.8 mA $\leq I \leq$ 20.5 mA.
- **Galvanic isolation voltage:** $\geq$ 500 V AC (per IEC 61131-2).
- **Power ride-through:** $\geq$ 20 ms minimum.
- **SFF formula:** $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$.
- **PFDavg formula (low-demand):** $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{proof} \cdot \lambda_{DU}}{2}$.
- **SIL budget:** SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **Standards to remember:** IEC 61508, IEC 61511, IEC 61131-2, NAMUR NE 43, ISO 13849.
- **V-Model position:** Interface tests sit at the *integration vertex* of the V-Model.
- **Traceability:** Every interface test must link to an SRS clause, a C&E entry, and a P&ID tag.
- **Documentation artifacts:** Interface List, Test Plan, Test Report, NCR (if any), Validation Summary Report (VSR).
- **Common-conversion check:** 1 year = 8760 hours; always verify the unit consistency before substitution.
- **Examiner's mantra:** *"No test, no proof. No proof, no SIL."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation and Maintenance of SIS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operation and Maintenance of SIS

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Operation and Maintenance (O&M) of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** refers to the set of all activities, procedures, technical practices, and management controls executed after the SIS is installed and commissioned, in order to ensure that the SIS continues to deliver its specified **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** at the required **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** throughout the entire operational lifetime of the plant — up to and including decommissioning.

As per **IEC 61511-1 (Clause 16 — Operation)** and **Clause 17 — Maintenance**, the O&M phase encompasses:

- Operation procedures and operator training
- Proof testing (also called "periodic inspection" or "function testing")
- Maintenance procedures (corrective, preventive, predictive)
- Calibration management
- Documentation control and records retention
- **Management of Change (MOC)**
- Incident investigation and follow-up
- Auditing for continued compliance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The O&M phase of the **Safety Lifecycle** is just as important as the design phase. A perfectly designed SIL-3 system can degrade to SIL-1 within months if proof testing, calibration, and MOC are not rigorously implemented.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smoke Detector on a Ship"

Imagine a **smoke detector on a passenger ship**. Its job (the SIF) is to alert the bridge if a fire breaks out. The detector is the SIS. Now think about what happens **after installation**:

| SIS Activity | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Proof test | Pressing the "TEST" button every month to make sure the buzzer still works |
| Diagnostic coverage | The detector doing an internal self-check (green LED blink) |
| Proof Test Coverage | Does pressing the test button check the *sensor*, *the buzzer*, and *the wiring*? Or only the buzzer? |
| Management of Change | Replacing the original detector with a different brand — does it still meet SIL? |
| Calibration | Cleaning the sensor chamber so dust doesn't cause a false-negative |
| Records | The maintenance logbook on the bridge |
| MTTR | Time from "buzzer failed" to "new buzzer working" |

If the crew never tests the detector, the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** rises every month — and one day, the detector fails silently when an actual fire occurs. This is exactly what proof testing prevents in industrial SIS.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Test:** A periodic test performed on a safety instrumented system (or part thereof) to detect dangerous undetected failures, such that the system can be restored to an "as-new" condition (or as close as practically achievable) with respect to its failure rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Test Interval (T_proof):** The time interval between two consecutive proof tests of an SIF. Usually expressed in **months** or **hours**. T_proof is the most powerful operational lever to lower PFD_avg.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Test Coverage (C_PT):** The fraction of dangerous undetected failures that are actually revealed by a given proof test procedure. Ranges from 0% (useless test) to 100% (perfect test). A typical industry value is **60% to 90%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR):** The average time between the moment a failure is detected and the moment the system is returned to operational state. Includes detection, diagnosis, repair, and proof test. Typical value: **8 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** The expected time between consecutive failures of a repairable system. Inverse of failure rate: **MTBF = 1/λ**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** The fraction of failures that are detected by the SIS's automatic on-line diagnostics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Management of Change (MOC):** A formal procedure to evaluate and control any modification (hardware, software, procedure, or setpoint) that could affect the safety function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dangerous Undetected Failure (DU):** A failure that prevents the SIS from performing its safety function AND is not detected by the automatic diagnostics. This is the *most critical failure class* for low-demand SIFs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dangerous Detected Failure (DD):** A failure that prevents the SIS from performing its safety function BUT is detected by diagnostics. Since it is detected, the system can be repaired before a demand arrives — therefore its PFD contribution is very small (depends on MTTR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Foundational Constants Used Universally in SIS O&M (KTU board-favorite numbers):**
> - **1 year = 8,760 hours**
> - **10 years = 87,600 hours**
> - **λ (failure rate) is always in failures/hour**

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of SIS Operation and Maintenance

Per IEC 61511, the O&M phase rests on four pillars. The 'why' and 'how' of each is explained below:

### Pillar 1 — Proof Testing
- **Why:** Dangerous undetected (DU) failures accumulate silently over time. Without periodic testing, PFD drifts to unsafe levels.
- **How:** A documented procedure activates the sensor and final element (or injects a known test signal) and verifies the logic solver reacts correctly. The system is then restored to operational state.
- **What it controls:** The **λ_DU × T_proof** product in the PFD equation.

### Pillar 2 — Operations and Maintenance Procedures
- **Why:** Untrained operators may bypass the SIS, override trips, or fail to recognize failures.
- **How:** Written operating procedures define what the operator must do on alarm, on trip, on bypass, and on startup. Maintenance procedures define what technicians must do during routine service.
- **What it controls:** Human error and MTTR.

### Pillar 3 — Documentation and Records
- **Why:** Audits, incident investigations, and SIL re-verifications all depend on historical data.
- **How:** Maintain a permanent log of proof tests, failures, repairs, calibrations, bypasses, and modifications.
- **What it controls:** Demonstrability of continued compliance with the Safety Requirement Specification (SRS).

### Pillar 4 — Management of Change (MOC)
- **Why:** Any change — even "identical" replacement — can alter failure rates, SIL, or coverage.
- **How:** A formal review by a multi-disciplinary team (process, instrumentation, safety) before any modification is implemented.
- **What it controls:** Latent design-intent drift.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most exam-relevant reference** for any SIS O&M numerical problem. Memorize it line-by-line.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Architecture / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Failure rate split | $\lambda = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | Total = sum of all 4 failure classes | $\text{failures/h}$ |
| 2 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | Diagnostic effectiveness | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | PFD of dangerous detected | $PFD_{DD} \approx \lambda_{DD} \cdot t_{CE}$ | All architectures (CE = channel equivalent down-time) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | PFD of dangerous undetected (1oo1) | $PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Single channel, perfect proof test | Dimensionless |
| 5 | PFD of dangerous undetected (1oo2) | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}$ | Two channels, voted 1-out-of-2 | Dimensionless |
| 6 | PFD of dangerous undetected (2oo2) | $PFD_{2oo2} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Two channels, voted 2-out-of-2 | Dimensionless |
| 7 | PFD of dangerous undetected (2oo3) | $PFD_{2oo3} = (6 \cdot (\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2})/(\text{derived from Karnaugh})$ | Three channels, voted 2-out-of-3 | Dimensionless |
| 8 | PFD with imperfect proof test coverage | $PFD_{1oo1}^{imperfect} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot C_{PT} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} + \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot C_{PT} \cdot t_{CE}}{2}$ | Adjusted formula | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Simplified imperfect coverage | $PFD_{avg} \approx (1 - C_{PT}) \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | Conservative approximation | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Mean Time To Restoration | $MTTR = t_{detection} + t_{diagnosis} + t_{repair} + t_{proof}$ | After detection, to operational | hours |
| 11 | Channel Equivalent Down-Time | $t_{CE} = \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2} + MTTR$ | For undetected failures | hours |
| 12 | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For repairable systems | hours |
| 13 | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repairable systems | hours |
| 14 | Proof Test Interval from target PFD | $T_{proof} = \dfrac{2 \cdot PFD_{target}}{\lambda_{DU}}$ | Derived from inverting Eq.4 | hours |
| 15 | SIL → PFD_avg mapping | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Low-demand mode | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural shorthand:**
> - **1oo1** = One-out-of-One (single channel)
> - **1oo2** = One-out-of-Two (redundant, both must agree to trip)
> - **2oo2** = Two-out-of-Two (both must trip; high spurious trip rate)
> - **2oo3** = Two-out-of-Three (most tolerant, most expensive)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic curiosities. They are the daily working tools of:

- **Process Safety Engineers** in oil refineries (Shell, Reliance, BPCL) — to schedule proof tests for tank overfill prevention SIFs.
- **SIS Vendors** (Honeywell, Emerson, Yokogawa, Siemens) — to generate SIL verification certificates.
- **TÜV / TÜV SÜD / Bureau Veritas auditors** — to assess whether operating plants meet IEC 61511 compliance.
- **KTU Board Examiners** — who will *definitely* test PFD_avg calculations, MTTR considerations, and proof test coverage concepts in the ESE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $PFD = \lambda \cdot T$ instead of $PFD = \lambda \cdot T / 2$. The "/2" comes from the fact that the failure rate is uniform in time — the *average* age of an undetected failure is only half the proof test interval. **Do not forget the factor of 1/2!**

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PFD_avg for 1oo1 Architecture (Perfect Proof Test)

**Given:** A single-channel (1oo1) SIF with constant dangerous undetected failure rate $\lambda_{DU}$ and proof test interval $T_{proof}$. We assume the proof test is perfect (C_PT = 100%, restoring the system to "as-new") and that demands occur randomly (Poisson process).

**Step 1 — Write the instantaneous unavailability.**
The probability that the channel has failed *but the failure has not yet been detected* grows linearly in time between proof tests.

$$
PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t
$$

> **Logic:** This is the standard exponential reliability approximation, valid when $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ (which is virtually always true in SIS work because $\lambda$ is tiny and $t$ is bounded by the proof test interval).

**Step 2 — Average over one proof test interval.**
By definition, the *average* PFD over the interval is the area under the PFD(t) curve divided by the interval length.

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} PFD(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression from Step 1.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Pull the constant outside the integral.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t\, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the elementary integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t\, dt = \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{T_{proof}^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back.**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel one $T_{proof}$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The factor ½ appears *physically* because the average age of an undetected failure at the moment of demand is exactly $T_{proof}/2$, not $T_{proof}$. Demand is equally likely to occur at any moment in the cycle, so the "average" exposure is half the cycle length.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PFD_avg for 1oo2 Architecture (Perfect Proof Test)

**Given:** Two identical independent channels, either of which can perform the safety function. Both channels fail at rate $\lambda_{DU}$. The system fails to perform the function only if **both** channels have failed undetected.

**Step 1 — Single-channel unavailability.**

$$
PFD_1(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — For 1oo2, both channels must be failed.**
The unavailability of the system is the product of the two channel unavailabilities (independence assumption).

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = PFD_1(t) \cdot PFD_2(t) = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2 = \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot t^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Average over the proof test interval.**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot t^{2}\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Pull the constant out.**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t^{2}\, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{proof}} t^{2}\, dt = \left[\frac{t^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \frac{T_{proof}^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \frac{T_{proof}^{3}}{3}
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel one $T_{proof}$.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For redundant architectures, the PFD drops quadratically with $\lambda_{DU} T_{proof}$, instead of linearly. This is why adding redundancy is so powerful for lowering PFD — but only if the two channels are truly **independent** (no common cause failure, β-factor).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Imperfect Proof Test Coverage

**Given:** A 1oo1 channel with imperfect proof test. Only a fraction $C_{PT}$ of DU failures are revealed by the test. The remaining fraction $(1 - C_{PT})$ remains hidden and continues to accumulate.

**Step 1 — Split the failure rate into detected-by-test and not-detected-by-test.**

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,caught} = C_{PT} \cdot \lambda_{DU}
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,missed} = (1 - C_{PT}) \cdot \lambda_{DU}
$$

**Step 2 — The caught failures behave like DD failures.**
Once the test reveals them, they are repaired (restoration time = $MTTR$ or proof test duration). Their average PFD contribution is $\lambda_{DD} \cdot t_{CE} \approx C_{PT} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}$ — very small.

**Step 3 — The missed failures behave like "undetected-until-next-test."**
They accumulate for the full proof test interval. Their PFD contribution is:

$$
PFD_{missed} = \frac{(1 - C_{PT}) \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFD_avg with imperfect coverage.**

$$
\boxed{PFD_{avg}^{imperfect} \approx \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \cdot (1 - C_{PT}) + C_{PT} \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot t_{CE}}
$$

**Step 5 — Conservative approximation (often used in KTU problems):**
Since $t_{CE} \ll T_{proof}$, the second term is often neglected:

$$
PFD_{avg} \approx (1 - C_{PT}) \cdot \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 60% proof test coverage is **not** a small penalty — it multiplies the PFD by 0.40 (i.e., makes it 60% *smaller* than the ideal case). If coverage drops to 30%, PFD is only 30% of ideal — actually a *good* thing for the user. **Higher coverage = lower PFD.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A 1oo1 SIF protecting a chemical reactor from overpressure has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/h. The proof test interval is 1 year (8,760 h) and the test is ideal (C_PT = 100%). Find the PFD_avg and identify the SIL achieved.

**Solution:**

$$
PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0438}{2} = 0.0219
$$

Since $0.0219 \in [10^{-2},\ 10^{-1}]$, the SIF achieves **SIL 1**.

Now, the same SIF is upgraded to 1oo2. Find the new PFD_avg.

$$
PFD_{avg}^{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760)^{2}}{3}
$$

$$
= \frac{(0.0438)^{2}}{3} = \frac{0.001918}{3} \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Since $6.4 \times 10^{-4} \in [10^{-3},\ 10^{-2}]$, the same hardware in 1oo2 architecture now achieves **SIL 2**. **Redundancy doubled the SIL — for the same proof test cost!**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SIS Operation & Maintenance Lifecycle (IEC 61511 Phase 16-17)

The following Mermaid block is a high-fidelity **functional architecture flow** of how Operation and Maintenance integrates into the overall Safety Lifecycle, and the feedback loops that drive continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 15 Installation and Commissioning"] --> B["Phase 16 Operation"]
    A --> C["Phase 17 Maintenance"]
    B --> D["Operator Rounds and Alarm Response"]
    B --> E["Bypass and Override Management"]
    B --> F["Trip Log Review"]
    C --> G["Proof Test Execution"]
    C --> H["Calibration of Sensors"]
    C --> I["Repair and Replacement of Parts"]
    D --> J["Records Database"]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Periodic SIL Verification Audit"]
    J --> L["Incident Investigation"]
    K --> M["Management of Change Review Board"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Modification or No Modification Decision"]
    N -->|Modify| O["Update SRS and Procedures"]
    N -->|No Change| P["Continue Operation"]
    O --> A
    P --> B
    P --> C
    G --> Q["Proof Test Report"]
    Q --> R["Calculate Achieved PFD_avg"]
    R --> S{"Achieved SIL Meets Target?"}
    S -->|Yes| P
    S -->|No| T["Reduce T_proof or Improve Coverage"]
    T --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Proof Test Workflow — Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Scheduled Proof Test Date"] --> B1["Issue Permit to Work"]
    B1 --> C1["Isolate SIF via Bypass Valves"]
    C1 --> D1["Apply Test Signal to Sensor"]
    D1 --> E1["Verify Logic Solver Trip Command"]
    E1 --> F1["Verify Final Element Action e.g. Valve Closure"]
    F1 --> G1{"All Steps Pass?"}
    G1 -->|No| H1["Log Defect and Initiate Repair"]
    H1 --> I1["Return to Maintenance Procedure"]
    G1 -->|Yes| J1["Restore SIF to Service"]
    J1 --> K1["Record Proof Test Certificate"]
    K1 --> L1["Update PFD Statistics"]
    L1 --> M1["Schedule Next Proof Test"]
    I1 --> M1
```

---

## 4.3 Management of Change (MOC) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MCA["Change Request Initiated e.g. Replace Transmitter"] --> MCB{"Affects Safety Function?"}
    MCB -->|No| MCC["Proceed with Normal Work Order"]
    MCB -->|Yes| MCD["Halt Work and Submit to MOC Board"]
    MCD --> MCE["Engineering Review: Calculate New PFD"]
    MCE --> MCF{"New SIL Still Meets SRS?"}
    MCF -->|No| MCG["Reject Change or Add Redundancy"]
    MCF -->|Yes| MCH["Update Safety Documentation"]
    MCH --> MCI["Update Proof Test Procedure if Needed"]
    MCI --> MCJ["Train Operations and Maintenance Crew"]
    MCJ --> MCK["Approve and Implement Change"]
    MCK --> MCL["Close Out MOC and Archive Records"]
```

---

## 4.4 PFD Drift vs Proof Test Interval — Visual Mental Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    AX["Time Axis 0 to T_proof"] --> P1["PFD zero at t equals 0 test performed"]
    P1 --> P2["PFD rises linearly as lambda times t"]
    P2 --> P3["PFD hits maximum lambda times T just before next test"]
    P3 --> P4["Proof test resets PFD to zero at t equals T_proof"]
    P4 --> P5["Average PFD is exactly half of the peak"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the visual intuition for the "1/2 factor" in the PFD_avg formula. The saw-tooth graph rises linearly and snaps back to zero at each proof test. The mean value of this saw-tooth is the average unavailability, equal to $\lambda \cdot T_{proof} / 2$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define proof testing in the context of a Safety Instrumented System. Why is it essential for maintaining the Safety Integrity Level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Proof testing is a **periodic, planned test** carried out on an SIF to detect **dangerous undetected (DU) failures** that have accumulated since the previous test, with the objective of restoring the system to an "as-new" condition (or as close as practically achievable).

It is essential for SIL maintenance because:
1. **DU failures are silent** — they are not caught by automatic diagnostics, so they accumulate continuously between tests.
2. **PFD_avg depends linearly (1oo1) or quadratically (1oo2) on T_proof** — without regular testing, PFD_avg drifts upward and the system eventually falls below the required SIL.
3. **Regulatory and IEC 61511 compliance** — Clause 16.2.6 of IEC 61511 mandates that the operation phase shall include proof testing of all SIFs at intervals determined during design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse "proof test" with "diagnostic test." Diagnostic tests are *automatic and continuous*; proof tests are *manual and periodic*. They complement each other, not substitute.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Management of Change (MOC) in SIS operation? List any four situations that trigger an MOC procedure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Management of Change (MOC)** is a **formal, documented review and approval process** used to evaluate and control any modification to the SIS — hardware, software, setpoints, procedures, or operating conditions — that could potentially affect the safety function's performance or SIL.

Four triggering situations:
1. **Hardware replacement** — swapping a transmitter with a different make/model (even "functionally equivalent" replacements have different failure rates).
2. **Setpoint change** — raising or lowering a trip setpoint (e.g., from 50 bar to 55 bar).
3. **Software/firmware upgrade** — installing a new logic solver firmware version.
4. **Bypass duration extension** — leaving a SIF bypassed for longer than the procedure allows (e.g., during commissioning or maintenance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** "Identical" replacements are *not* MOC-free. Even a same-model transmitter from a different batch can have a different SIL certificate. **Always trigger MOC.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the **operation and maintenance requirements of a Safety Instrumented System** as specified in **IEC 61511-1, Clauses 16 and 17**. Cover at least six distinct activities. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1oo1 SIF uses a transmitter with $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/h and a final control element with $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/h. The SIF is proof tested every 2 years (17,520 h). Calculate the total PFD_avg of the SIF and identify the achieved SIL. Assume the logic solver has negligible $\lambda_{DU}$ and 100% proof test coverage. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Operation & Maintenance Requirements (7 marks)

Per **IEC 61511-1**, the O&M phase requires the following activities:

1. **Operating Procedures (Clause 16.2):** Written procedures for normal operation, startup, shutdown, alarm response, bypass management, and emergency response. Operators must be trained and their training records maintained.

2. **Proof Testing (Clause 16.2.6):** Every SIF must be proof tested at intervals determined during design. Test procedures shall include criteria for pass/fail, restoration actions, and record-keeping. The achieved PFD_avg shall be re-verified.

3. **Maintenance Procedures (Clause 17.1):** Preventive, corrective, and predictive maintenance programs. Calibration schedules, spare parts inventory, and competency requirements for maintenance personnel.

4. **Documentation and Records (Clause 16.2.5):** Permanent retention of: as-built drawings, calibration certificates, proof test reports, failure logs, MOC records, training records, and audit reports.

5. **Management of Change (MOC) (Clause 16.2.2):** A formal procedure to evaluate any modification before implementation. MOC ensures that changes do not invalidate the original SRS or SIL determination.

6. **Incident Investigation and Follow-up (Clause 16.2.7):** Every SIF activation (whether demanded or spurious) shall be investigated, and any "near-miss" or failure event analysed for root cause and corrective action.

7. **Audit (Clause 16.2.4):** Periodic audits (typically every 3-5 years) to confirm that the SIS continues to meet the SRS and that O&M practices are being followed.

8. **Decommissioning Planning (Clause 16.3):** Procedures for safe removal of the SIS at end-of-life, including information transfer to operations for the new installation.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the components contributing to PFD.**

The SIF has two unsafe components in the field: the transmitter (sensor) and the final control element (valve). Each is a 1oo1 channel.

**Step 2 — State the formula.**

For 1oo1 architecture with perfect proof test:

$$
PFD_{channel} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the PFD of the transmitter.**

$$
PFD_{transmitter} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520}{2} = \frac{0.05256}{2} = 0.02628
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the PFD of the final element.**

$$
PFD_{valve} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 17520}{2} = \frac{0.03504}{2} = 0.01752
$$

**Step 5 — Add the two contributions in series (logic solver is negligible).**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = PFD_{transmitter} + PFD_{valve} = 0.02628 + 0.01752 = 0.0438
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to scientific notation.**

$$
PFD_{SIF} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the SIL.**

Since $4.38 \times 10^{-2} \in [10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, the SIF achieves **SIL 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to **add** the PFDs of components in series. The PFD of an SIF is the **sum** (union) of the PFDs of its constituent channels, NOT a product (unless in redundant voted architectures). Adding PFDs is correct for series failure modes.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`
**(a)** Discuss **proof testing of SIFs** in detail. Cover: objectives, types (online vs offline), proof test coverage, step-by-step procedure, and the relationship between proof test interval and achieved SIL. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1oo2 SIF has identical channels with $\lambda_{DU} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/h. The current proof test interval is 1 year (8,760 h). The Safety Requirement Specification demands SIL 2 (i.e., PFD_avg ≤ $10^{-2}$). Determine:
  - (i) The current achieved PFD_avg and SIL.
  - (ii) The maximum allowed proof test interval to meet SIL 2.
  (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Proof Testing Detailed Discussion (7 marks)

**Objectives of Proof Testing:**
- Detect dangerous undetected failures that have accumulated since the last test.
- Restore the SIF to an "as-new" state (or as close as practically achievable).
- Verify the integrity of the complete safety loop: sensor → logic solver → final element.
- Provide documentary evidence of continued SIL compliance.

**Types of Proof Testing:**

| Type | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Online (in-service)** | Test performed while the process is running. | No plant shutdown required. | Limited access; may not test 100% of failure modes. |
| **Offline (out-of-service)** | SIF is fully isolated and the process is in safe state. | Comprehensive test possible. | Plant shutdown; lost production. |
| **Partial / staged** | One channel tested at a time in redundant systems. | No process interruption. | Requires redundancy. |

**Proof Test Coverage (C_PT):**
The fraction of DU failures revealed by the test. Coverage depends on:
- The granularity of the test (is every input tested, every output, every mode?)
- The diagnostic resolution of the test equipment.
- The accessibility of the components (e.g., inaccessible valve seats typically have lower coverage).
Typical industrial values: **60% to 90%**. Higher C_PT lowers the achieved PFD.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (as per IEC 61511):**
1. Review the test procedure document.
2. Notify operations and obtain a work permit.
3. Bypass the SIF (where permitted and time-limited).
4. Apply a known test signal to the sensor.
5. Verify the logic solver generates the trip command.
6. Verify the final element moves to the safe state.
7. Restore the system, remove the bypass, and re-arm.
8. Record results in the proof test logbook.
9. Schedule the next proof test.

**Relationship between T_proof and SIL:**
For 1oo1: $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_{DU} T_{proof} / 2$. Doubling $T_{proof}$ doubles PFD_avg. To *halve* the PFD_avg (or to *upgrade* the SIL by one level), the proof test interval must be **halved**. This is the most direct operational lever for improving SIL.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Part (i) — Current PFD_avg of 1oo2 SIF.**

For 1oo2 architecture:

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}
$$
