| BC | 87% | ≥ 100% | **FAIL** |
| MC/DC | 70% | ≥ 100% | **FAIL** |

**Verdict:** The module **FAILS** the SIL 3 verification gate. All four coverage criteria are below the recommended 100% threshold.

**Corrective Actions:**

1. **Add requirements-based test cases** to lift Functional Coverage from 82% to 100% — specifically, identify the 9 untested requirements and write at least one test case for each, including negative tests for fault injection.
2. **Add structural test cases targeting uncovered statements, branches, and conditions** to lift SC to 100%, BC to 100%, and MC/DC to 100% — for MC/DC, ensure every Boolean sub-expression in safety-critical decisions is varied *independently*.
3. Re-run the instrumented build, re-measure coverage, and submit a **delta-coverage report** to the Functional Safety Assessor (FSA) for approval.

---

**Q A(b)** **[7 Marks]**: With the aid of a suitable diagram, explain how the **V-Model of functional safety verification** incorporates both *test coverage* and *test depth* at each verification level. Indicate which levels contribute primarily to **coverage** and which contribute primarily to **depth**. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**The V-Model Structure (left = specification, right = verification, bottom = integration):**

The diagram from Section 4.1 above illustrates the standard V-Model. The left descending arm contains the *decomposition* of safety requirements (User Requirements → System SRS → Hardware/Safety Requirements → Software Safety Requirements → Module Design), and the right ascending arm contains the corresponding *verification* activities (Module Test → Integration Test → System Validation). The horizontal bars across the V indicate the *traceability* between requirement and verification.

**Coverage and Depth Mapping:**

| Verification Level | Primary Contribution | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware Module Test | **Depth** | Verifies internal circuitry, register behavior, timing margins, fault-injection effects. |
| Software Module Test | **Depth** | Verifies internal logic, branches, conditions; provides SC, BC, MC/DC. |
| Software Integration Test | **Coverage + Depth** | Verifies inter-module interfaces (coverage) and data-flow (depth). |
| Hardware Integration Test | **Coverage** | Verifies all hardware channels operate per spec. |
| System Validation Test | **Coverage** | Verifies end-to-end safety function under all operating modes. |

**Why both are required at SIL 3/4:**

A pure coverage-based test plan at SIL 3/4 may report 100% functional coverage but have exercised each function only at nominal conditions — failing to reveal boundary-condition faults, environmental stress faults, or rare-mode faults. A pure depth-based test plan may exhaustively analyze a single module but leave 30% of the system untested. The V-Model forces *systematic breadth (coverage)* at the system level and *systematic rigor (depth)* at the module level, satisfying the IEC 61508 requirement that *no safety function be both untested and un-traced.*

**Diagrammatic Summary:**

```
Specification Side (Decomposition)          Verification Side (Integration)
[User Requirements]                         [Acceptance Test]
       |                                          ^
[SRS]                                      [System Validation Test]
       |                                          ^
[HW/SW Safety Requirements]                 [HW/SW Integration Test]
       |                                          ^
[Module Design]                            [Module Test]
       |_________________________________________|
                  Module Test Bridge
        (Depth: structural & fault-coverage)
        (Coverage: requirements & functional)
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Track

**Q B(a)** **[7 Marks]**: A single-channel (1oo1) sensor subsystem is characterized by the following failure rates:
- $\lambda_{SD}$ (safe-detected) = $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{SU}$ (safe-undetected) = $0.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{DD}$ (dangerous-detected) = $2.4 \times 10^{-6}$/h
- $\lambda_{DU}$ (dangerous-undetected) = $0.1 \times 10^{-6}$/h

**(i)** Compute the Diagnostic Coverage (DC) and Safe Failure Fraction (SFF). **(ii)** Using IEC 61508-2 Tables 3 and 4, determine the **maximum SIL** for which a 1oo1 architecture of a **Type B** element is permissible. **(iii)** Justify the answer with reference to the SIL-vs-architecture-vs-coverage constraints. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Computation of DC and SFF:**

Step 1 — Sum the dangerous failure rate:

$$
\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = (2.4 + 0.1) \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

Step 2 — Compute Diagnostic Coverage:

$$
DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.4}{2.5} \times 100\% = 96.0\%
$$

Step 3 — Sum the total failure rate (safe + dangerous):

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU} = (1.0 + 0.5 + 2.4 + 0.1) \times 10^{-6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}
$$

Step 4 — Compute Safe Failure Fraction:

$$
SFF = \frac{\lambda_{SD} + \lambda_{SU} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.0 + 0.5 + 2.4}{4.0} \times 100\% = \frac{3.9}{4.0} \times 100\% = 97.5\%
$$

**(ii) Maximum SIL Determination:**

Per IEC 61508-2 Table 4, for a **1oo1 architecture with a Type B element**, the permissible combinations are:

| SIL | Required DC | Required SFF |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | ≥ 60% (Low) | ≥ 60% |
| SIL 2 | ≥ 90% (Medium) | ≥ 90% |
| SIL 3 | **NOT PERMITTED** | — |

Our values: $DC = 96\%$, $SFF = 97.5\%$. This satisfies the **SIL 2** thresholds but **does not qualify for SIL 3** in a 1oo1 Type B architecture.

**Maximum SIL = SIL 2** (in 1oo1, Type B).

**(iii) Justification:**

The result of (ii) is correct because the architectural constraint of 1oo1 (single channel with no redundancy and no diagnostic-voted redundancy) places a hard ceiling on the achievable SIL once the element complexity is non-trivial (Type B, defined as having non-deterministic behavior or complex diagnostics). To achieve SIL 3, the subsystem must be re-architected to **1oo2D** (one-out-of-two with diagnostic) or a voted group such as **2oo3**, while maintaining the same coverage levels.

---

**Q B(b)** **[7 Marks]**: Explain **MC/DC (Modified Condition / Decision Coverage)**. Why is it not equivalent to *Branch Coverage + Condition Coverage* combined? Illustrate with a decision containing at least **three** Boolean conditions. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of MC/DC:**

MC/DC (Modified Condition / Decision Coverage) is a structural coverage criterion defined by **Chilton & Oxburg** that requires:
1. **Every** decision outcome (true and false) must be exercised at least once.
2. **Every** Boolean sub-condition must be exercised to both true and false.
3. **Each** condition must be shown to *independently* affect the decision outcome — i.e., there must exist a pair of test cases that differ *only* in that one condition, with all others held constant, and the decision outcome must differ.

**Why MC/DC ≠ Branch Coverage + Condition Coverage:**

- *Branch coverage* verifies that both outcomes of a decision are taken.
- *Condition coverage* verifies that each Boolean sub-expression evaluates to both T and F.
- The **union** of these two does *not* guarantee that the *coupling logic* between conditions has been probed. A subtle bug may exist where the operator precedence, short-circuit behavior, or coupling logic produces an incorrect decision that is hidden because two correlated conditions are toggled *together* rather than *independently*.

**Worked Example with Three Conditions:**

Let the decision be $D = (A \land B) \lor C$ with three Boolean conditions $A, B, C$.

Test pair construction for independence:

| Test # | A | B | C | D | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T | T | F | T | For A and B independence |
| 2 | F | T | F | F | For A independence (differs only in A) |
| 3 | T | F | F | F | For B independence (differs only in B) |
| 4 | T | T | T | T | For C independence |
| 5 | F | F | T | T | For C independence (differs only in C) |

- **A independence:** Rows 1 vs 2 — A changes T→F, B and C held constant (T and F); D changes T→F. ✓
- **B independence:** Rows 1 vs 3 — B changes T→F, A and C held constant; D changes T→F. ✓
- **C independence:** Rows 4 vs 5 — C changes T→F, A and B held constant (T and T); D changes T→F. ✓

**Independence of each condition is proven.** $MC/DC = 100\%$.

**The critical contrast:** A test suite that achieved *Branch + Condition* coverage by simply toggling $(A, B)$ together would *pass* branch and condition criteria but would *fail* MC/DC because the *independence* condition is not met. Hence MC/DC is strictly stronger than the union of BC and CC, and is mandated at SIL 3/4 to expose such coupled-condition faults.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. *Coverage* and *Depth* are **not interchangeable** — students often write one definition and assume the other is implied. Always state BOTH definitions explicitly for full marks.
> 2. In MC/DC problems, students frequently claim that "varying each condition to both T and F" constitutes MC/DC. This is **Condition Coverage (CC)**, **NOT** MC/DC. The *independence* (pair-wise variation) requirement is the *defining* characteristic of MC/DC and the most-tested concept.
> 3. For DC/SFF problems, always show the *intermediate* sums ($\lambda_D$, $\lambda_{total}$) — losing one mark for a missing intermediate step is the most common KTU penalty.
> 4. The V-Model question is the easiest to over-write; the examiner awards marks for the **mapping of coverage/depth to verification levels**, not for re-drawing the V-model itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Coverage** = *breadth* (how many items tested). **Depth** = *rigor* (how thoroughly each item tested). Both are required.
- **Test coverage** is measured along three axes: **Functional, Structural, Fault** coverage.
- **Structural coverage** has a hierarchy: **SC** (weakest) < **BC** < **CC** < **MC/DC** (strongest, mandated for SIL 3/4).
- **MC/DC requires independence** — every condition must be varied in a *pair of test cases* that differ only in that condition, with all others held constant, and the decision outcome must differ.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** = $\frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%$ — high DC means most dangerous faults are detected online.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** = $\frac{\lambda_{S} + \lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}} \times 100\%$ — high SFF supports higher SIL in lower-redundancy architectures.
- IEC 61508-2 quantifies DC into bands: **< 60% None, 60%–90% Low, 90%–99% Medium, ≥ 99% High**.
- **Type A elements** (well-defined, deterministic) tolerate lower coverage than **Type B** (complex) for the same SIL.
- **1oo1 architecture + Type B** has a hard ceiling of **SIL 2** regardless of coverage.
- **1oo2D** is the typical SIL 3 architecture with DC ≥ 90% and SFF ≥ 90% (Type B).
- **2oo3** is the typical SIL 3 architecture for high-availability process control (e.g., turbine overspeed, ESD).
- The **V-Model** pairs specification with verification; the **Module Test level** is where *test depth* dominates; the **System Validation level** is where *test coverage* dominates.
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **FMEDA (Failure Mode, Effects and Diagnostic Analysis)** are the input documents that drive the *fault coverage* calculations.
- For KTU, always show the *units* in final answers (FIT, per hour, per demand, %), as the unit itself is a marking checkpoint.
- The **Combined Verification Confidence Index** $V_{CI} = w_f \cdot FC + w_s \cdot SC + w_b \cdot BC + w_m \cdot MC/DC$ is a useful analytical composite for short numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Test Depth in PFD Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importance of Test Depth in PFD Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Functional Safety** under **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**, **Test Depth (TD)** is defined as the *proportion (or fraction) of the total dangerous failure rate of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) or Safety Instrumented System (SIS) that is actually revealed, exercised, or detected by the periodic proof test.* It is a quantitative measure of how thoroughly the proof test exercises the failure modes of the Safety-Related Parts of a Control System (SRP/CS).

Formally, it is expressed as:

$$TD = \frac{\lambda_{DU,detected}}{\lambda_{DU,total}}$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{DU,total}$ is the total dangerous undetected failure rate
- $\lambda_{DU,detected}$ is the portion of the dangerous undetected failure rate that is actually revealed by the test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Depth vs Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Diagnostic Coverage (DC) is a *design property* representing the fraction of failures that the **built-in automatic diagnostics** can detect during operation. Test Depth, on the other hand, is a *proof-test property* representing the fraction of failures revealed by the **manual/periodic offline test**. They are related but distinct concepts; together they determine the effective $\lambda_{DU}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Health Checkup"

Imagine your car is fitted with a **Safety Airbag System**. The airbag will deploy only if the crash sensor, the control unit, and the inflator **all** work together on demand.

- **Without testing (Test Depth = 0%):** You buy the car and never inspect it. Even if the inflator's chemical charge has degraded, you have **no way of knowing**. The probability that the airbag fails when needed (**PFD**) is very high.
- **With a 30% test depth:** You only visually check that the dashboard warning lamp works. Many hidden failures (sensor drift, capacitor aging, squib degradation) remain undetected. PFD is reduced, but not dramatically.
- **With a 95% test depth:** A certified engineer applies a calibrated shock pulse to the sensor, simulates a crash, electrically fires the squib through a dummy load, and verifies the entire chain end-to-end. Now almost **all** dangerous failure modes are revealed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The deeper the test, the more dangerous failures you "find and fix" before they can cause a hazardous event, and the **lower the PFDavg of the safety function**.

## 1.3 Why Test Depth Matters in PFD Calculation

The **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** is the primary metric for low-demand mode operation in IEC 61508. The PFD is inversely proportional to how effectively the system is tested. A shallow test leaves dangerous undetected failures ($\lambda_{DU}$) "alive" in the system, which accumulate over the proof test interval **T**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In board examinations, questions frequently connect Test Depth to the **$\lambda_{DU}$ term** in the simplified PFDavg equation. A higher Test Depth directly reduces the effective $\lambda_{DU}$ and therefore reduces the PFDavg.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Chain — How Test Depth Enters PFD

The flow of failure rates through a Safety Instrumented Function is as follows:

1. **Total Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** Every component fails at some base rate.
2. **Partition into Safe ($\lambda_S$) and Dangerous ($\lambda_D$):** Dangerous failures prevent the SIF from performing its function on demand.
3. **Sub-partition Dangerous into Detected ($\lambda_{DD}$) and Undetected ($\lambda_{DU}$):** Diagnostic tests catch some; the rest are silent killers.
4. **Test Depth acts on $\lambda_{DU}$:** During a proof test, only a fraction of the $\lambda_{DU}$ failures are actually triggered and revealed. The rest remain hidden.

## 2.2 The Core Math — Test Depth in PFD Equations

For a **1oo1 (one-out-of-one) low-demand architecture**, the average PFD is:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

When Test Depth is applied, the effective dangerous undetected failure rate becomes:

$$\lambda_{DU,eff} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD)$$

Substituting into the PFD equation:

$$PFD_{avg,TD} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-relevant equation** for KTU 2024 Scheme questions on Test Depth.

For a **1oo2 architecture**, the simplification becomes:

$$PFD_{1oo2,TD} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD))^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3}$$

## 2.3 Test Depth Categories (Per IEC 61508)

| Category | Test Depth Range | PFD Reduction Impact |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Low Test Depth** | TD < 60% | Minor PFD reduction; cannot compensate for short proof test interval |
| **Medium Test Depth** | 60% ≤ TD ≤ 90% | Moderate PFD reduction; commonly used in SIL 1 / SIL 2 systems |
| **High Test Depth** | TD > 90% | Significant PFD reduction; mandatory for SIL 3 architectures without redundancy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) Connection:** Test Depth, along with DC, contributes to the overall SFF. A high Test Depth raises SFF, which is a key parameter in the architectural constraints for hardware fault tolerance in IEC 61508 Tables 2 and 3.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas (IEC 61511):** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves are often tested with partial stroke testing (PST), achieving 60–80% test depth without shutting down production.
- **Nuclear (IEC 61513):** Reactor protection systems demand > 90% test depth with full surveillance testing during outages.
- **Process Industry:** SIL 3 achieved by combining medium test depth (70%) with high redundancy (1oo2D), rather than relying on a 100% test alone (which is practically impossible).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $TD = \lambda_{DU,detected} / \lambda_{DU,total}$ | Definition of Test Depth | Dimensionless (%) |
| $\lambda_{DU,eff} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD)$ | Effective undetected failure rate after test | failures/hour |
| $PFD_{1oo1} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}) / 2$ | PFD for 1oo1 with Test Depth | Dimensionless |
| $PFD_{1oo2} = ((\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD))^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2) / 3$ | PFD for 1oo2 with Test Depth | Dimensionless |
| $DC = \lambda_{DD} / (\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU})$ | Diagnostic Coverage (related concept) | Dimensionless |
| $SFF = (\lambda_S + \lambda_{DD}) / (\lambda_S + \lambda_D)$ | Safe Failure Fraction | Dimensionless |
| $PFH_{1oo1,TD} = \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD)$ | Probability of Failure per Hour (high demand) | failures/hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students confuse $TD$ with $DC$. Remember — **DC is automatic and continuous**; **TD is manual and periodic**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effect of Test Depth on PFDavg for 1oo1 Architecture

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (1 year)
- $TD = 0.85$ (85%)

**Step 1:** Compute the effective undetected failure rate.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU,eff} &= \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - 0.85) \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.15 \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the PFDavg with Test Depth applied.

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,TD} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU,eff} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \\
&= \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 8760}{2} \\
&= \frac{3.285 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= 1.6425 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** For comparison, compute PFDavg *without* Test Depth.

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,noTD} &= \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} \\
&= \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}{2} \\
&= 1.095 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the reduction ratio (a key analytical metric).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction Factor} &= \frac{PFD_{avg,noTD}}{PFD_{avg,TD}} \\
&= \frac{1.095 \times 10^{-2}}{1.6425 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 6.67
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An 85% test depth reduces the PFD by a factor of ~6.67, allowing the SIF to meet a tighter SIL target for the same proof test interval.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Test Depth for Target PFD

**Problem:** A 1oo1 transmitter has $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and is tested every 2 years ($T_{proof} = 17520$ h). What is the **minimum Test Depth** required to achieve SIL 2 (PFDavg in range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$)?

**Step 1:** Set up the inequality for SIL 2 upper bound.

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,TD} &\leq 10^{-2} \\
\frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}}{2} &\leq 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot 17520}{2} &\leq 10^{-2} \\
(5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 17520 / 2) \cdot (1 - TD) &\leq 10^{-2} \\
(4.38 \times 10^{-2}) \cdot (1 - TD) &\leq 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for TD.

$$\begin{aligned}
(1 - TD) &\leq \frac{10^{-2}}{4.38 \times 10^{-2}} \\
(1 - TD) &\leq 0.2283 \\
TD &\geq 0.7717 \\
TD &\geq 77.17\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A minimum Test Depth of approximately **78%** is required. A practical design would use TD = 80% to provide margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | $\lambda_{DU}$ (per hr) | $T_{proof}$ (hr) | TD | $PFD_{avg}$ | SIL Achievable |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------|----|-------------|-----------------|
| A | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 | 0% (no test) | $2.19 \times 10^{-2}$ | SIL 1 (borderline) |
| B | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 | 50% | $1.095 \times 10^{-2}$ | SIL 2 |
| C | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 | 90% | $2.19 \times 10^{-3}$ | SIL 2 |
| D | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | 8760 | 99% | $2.19 \times 10^{-4}$ | SIL 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for Board Exams:** As TD approaches 100%, PFDavg approaches zero *for a given proof test interval*. However, 100% TD is practically impossible — there is always a small residual "perfectly hidden" failure rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Test Depth Influence Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how Test Depth filters the dangerous failure rate, ultimately shaping the final PFDavg of the Safety Instrumented Function.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Component Failure Rate Lambda] --> B[Lambda Safe plus Lambda Dangerous]
    B --> C[Lambda Dangerous Detected Lambda DD]
    B --> D[Lambda Dangerous Undetected Lambda DU]
    C --> E[Handled by Automatic Diagnostics DC]
    D --> F[Periodic Proof Test Execution]
    F --> G{Test Depth TD factor}
    G --> H[Lambda DU detected fraction TD times Lambda DU]
    G --> I[Lambda DU residual fraction 1 minus TD times Lambda DU]
    I --> J[PFDavg calculation integration over Tproof]
    H --> K[Failure repaired during proof test]
    J --> L[Final SIL rating achieved]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block — Low vs High Test Depth Impact

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW[Low Test Depth Scenario]
        L1[Lambda DU full] --> L2[Untested failures accumulate]
        L2 --> L3[High PFDavg]
        L3 --> L4[Lower SIL achievable]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High Test Depth Scenario]
        H1[Lambda DU full] --> H2[Test reveals most failures]
        H2 --> H3[Low PFDavg]
        H3 --> H4[Higher SIL achievable]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sequential Proof Test Cycle — Test Depth Effect

```mermaid
graph TD
    T0[Start of Proof Test Interval] --> T1[SIF commissioned]
    T1 --> T2[Failures begin to accumulate in SIF]
    T2 --> T3{Proof Test Performed}
    T3 --> T4[Detected fraction TD repaired]
    T3 --> T5[Undetected fraction 1 minus TD remains]
    T5 --> T6[Continue to next proof test interval]
    T4 --> T6
    T6 --> T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The "Undetected fraction 1 minus TD" arrow is the key. The smaller this is, the fewer dangerous undetected failures carry over to the next interval, and the lower the integrated PFDavg.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define Test Depth in the context of functional safety. How does it differ from Diagnostic Coverage (DC)?*

**Model Answer:**
Test Depth (TD) is defined as the fraction of the total dangerous undetected failure rate of a Safety Instrumented Function that is actually revealed during a periodic proof test. It is expressed as:

$$TD = \frac{\lambda_{DU,detected}}{\lambda_{DU,total}}$$

**Difference from DC:** Diagnostic Coverage is an *automatic, continuous, online* capability built into the device firmware/hardware to detect failures during normal operation. Test Depth, by contrast, is a *manual, periodic, offline* property determined by the comprehensiveness of the proof test procedure. DC is a design attribute, while TD is a maintenance/testing attribute.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the formula and skip the **distinction between DC (automatic) and TD (manual)** — losing 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the simplified PFDavg equation for a 1oo1 low-demand architecture incorporating Test Depth. Explain the effect of increasing Test Depth on PFDavg.*

**Model Answer:**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

**Effect of increasing Test Depth:** As TD increases, the factor $(1 - TD)$ decreases proportionally, which linearly reduces the PFDavg. For example, increasing TD from 50% to 90% reduces PFDavg by a factor of 5. This allows the same architecture and proof test interval to achieve a higher SIL rating.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for PFDavg of a 1oo1 low-demand Safety Instrumented Function with Test Depth factor TD included. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo1 pressure transmitter in a chemical reactor Safety Instrumented Function has the following parameters:
- $\lambda_{DU} = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- Proof test interval $T_{proof} = 4380$ hours (6 months)
- Test Depth $TD = 0.80$

Compute the PFDavg and identify the SIL level achieved (use standard IEC 61508 ranges: SIL 1: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 2: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 3: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three categories of Test Depth (Low, Medium, High) as per IEC 61508, and discuss their respective impacts on PFDavg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1oo2 voting group has identical transmitters with $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour each, proof test interval $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours, and Test Depth $TD = 0.90$. Calculate the PFDavg and determine the SIL level. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Low-demand mode of operation (demand rate ≤ 1 per year)
2. Single channel (1oo1) architecture
3. Failures are independent and follow exponential distribution
4. All detected failures are repaired to "as-new" condition
5. The proof test is performed instantaneously at interval $T_{proof}$
6. The Test Depth factor TD applies uniformly to all dangerous undetected failures

**Derivation:**

The probability of failure on demand, PFD(t), for a 1oo1 system, ignoring Test Depth, integrates the failure rate over the proof test interval:

$$PFD(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

For small values of $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ (typical for safety systems), the exponential is approximated as $1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$:

$$PFD(t) \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

The **average PFD** over the interval $T_{proof}$ is obtained by integrating the PFD(t) and dividing by the interval:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

Introducing Test Depth, the effective failure rate is $\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD)$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}}{2}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the effective undetected failure rate.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU,eff} &= \lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \\
&= 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - 0.80) \\
&= 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.20 \\
&= 6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate PFDavg.

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 4380}{2} \\
&= \frac{2.628 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= 1.314 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Identify SIL level.

Since $10^{-3} \leq 1.314 \times 10^{-3} \leq 10^{-2}$, the system achieves **SIL 2**.

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Detailed Explanation:**

**1. Low Test Depth (TD < 60%):**
A low test depth covers less than 60% of the dangerous failure modes. Typical examples include basic visual inspection or single-point alarm checks. The PFDavg is reduced only slightly compared to an untested system. Such depth is generally **insufficient for SIL 3** without architectural redundancy.

**2. Medium Test Depth (60% ≤ TD ≤ 90%):**
Medium test depth covers most — but not all — failure modes. Examples include partial stroke testing of valves, end-to-end simulation with limited calibration, and loop checks. This is the **most common industrial range** and is suitable for SIL 1 and SIL 2 in single-channel architectures and SIL 3 in redundant (1oo2, 2oo3) architectures.

**3. High Test Depth (TD > 90%):**
A high test depth covers nearly all dangerous failure modes, requiring comprehensive offline testing with calibrated test equipment. Typical of nuclear and aerospace safety systems. PFDavg is reduced by a factor of 10 or more compared to no testing.

**Impact on PFDavg:** As Test Depth increases from Low → Medium → High, the factor $(1 - TD)$ in the PFD equation drops from 0.4+ → 0.1–0.4 → <0.1, producing a **proportional linear reduction** in PFDavg. This allows the same hardware to satisfy progressively stricter SIL targets.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

For a **1oo2 voting group**, the simplified PFDavg equation is:

$$PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD))^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2}{3}$$

**Step 1:** Calculate the effective undetected failure rate.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{DU,eff} &= 2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1 - 0.90) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the 1oo2 PFD equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,1oo2} &= \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 7.674 \times 10^{7}}{3} \\
&= \frac{3.0696 \times 10^{-6}}{3} \\
&= 1.0232 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Identify SIL level.

Since $10^{-6} \leq 1.0232 \times 10^{-6} \leq 10^{-5}$, the system achieves **SIL 3** (borderline SIL 4 in some interpretations, but conservatively **SIL 3** per IEC 61508 standard ranges).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Using the **1oo1 equation** for a 1oo2 system — losing up to 5 marks.
> 2. Forgetting the **squared term** $(\lambda_{DU})^2$ in 1oo2 PFD calculations.
> 3. Confusing **TD** with **DC** and substituting incorrectly.
> 4. Not stating **assumptions** in the derivation (assumptions carry 1–2 marks).
> 5. Forgetting the **factor of 3** (denominator) in the 1oo2 equation — a very common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Test Depth in PFD Calculation**

- **Definition:** Test Depth (TD) is the fraction of dangerous undetected failures revealed by the periodic proof test. It is a *maintenance/procedural* attribute, not a design attribute.
- **Formula:** $TD = \lambda_{DU,detected} / \lambda_{DU,total}$
- **Key Equation (1oo1):** $PFD_{avg} = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD) \cdot T_{proof}) / 2$
- **Key Equation (1oo2):** $PFD_{avg} = ((\lambda_{DU} \cdot (1 - TD))^2 \cdot T_{proof}^2) / 3$
- **Distinction:**
  - **DC (Diagnostic Coverage)** → Automatic, online, continuous
  - **TD (Test Depth)** → Manual, offline, periodic
- **Categories:**
  - Low TD: < 60%
  - Medium TD: 60–90%
  - High TD: > 90%
- **Effect:** Higher TD → lower PFDavg → potentially higher SIL achievable without changing hardware.
- **Connection to SFF:** Test Depth contributes (along with DC) to the Safe Failure Fraction, which determines the architectural constraints in IEC 61508.
- **Practical Reality:** 100% Test Depth is *theoretically desirable* but *practically unachievable* — there is always a residual hidden failure rate.
- **Trade-off:** Deep testing is expensive and may require process shutdown; therefore, industries often combine *medium TD* with *redundancy* to achieve SIL 3.
- **Board Exam Favourite:** Always substitute the value of $(1 - TD)$ first before plugging into the PFD equation — saves calculation time and errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bypass Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bypass Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bypass** is the deliberate, temporary, and authorized defeat or deactivation of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF), a Safety Instrumented System (SIS), or any part thereof, performed to permit a process operation, maintenance activity, or proof test that would otherwise cause the safety function to trip or shut down the process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (IEC 61511/61508 Aligned):**
> A bypass is *not* a failure — it is a **planned, risk-assessed, time-limited override** of a safety function. The act of bypassing **transfers risk** from the SIF to the operational procedures, and therefore demands explicit authorization, compensating measures, and a rigorous Management of Change (MOC) workflow.

The associated discipline, **Bypass Management**, is the set of policies, procedures, technical controls, and administrative practices that govern the entire lifecycle of a bypass — from initiation, authorization, and risk assessment to active monitoring, alarm management, and the verified restoration of the bypassed function.

**Related Terminology to Master:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Bypass** | A switch/jumper/key-operated override that disables a SIF or its sensor/logic/actuator chain. |
| **Inhibit (Soft Bypass)** | A software-driven suppression of an alarm or trip action without physically altering the field wiring. |
| **Override** | A forcing signal applied to a controller or logic solver to maintain a process state. |
| **Force** | The continuous application of a digital signal (1 or 0) in the logic solver, independent of actual field conditions. |
| **Maintenance Bypass** | Bypass applied during scheduled maintenance or proof testing. |
| **Operational Bypass** | Bypass applied during normal operation due to a known faulty device awaiting repair. |
| **Compensating Measure** | An alternative risk reduction mechanism applied while the SIF is bypassed. |
| **MOC (Management of Change)** | The formal procedure used to authorize any temporary or permanent change to the safety system. |
| **Tolerable Bypass Duration** | The maximum time a SIF may remain bypassed before the unmitigated risk becomes unacceptable. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car's Airbag System**
> Imagine driving a car whose airbag system has a maintenance fault light glowing on the dashboard. The garage technician places the system in **service mode** (bypass) so he can safely work on the wiring. While the airbag is bypassed:
> - You must be **notified** (instrumented alarm/operator awareness).
> - A **compensating measure** is taken — you drive at a lower speed, avoid traffic, wear a seatbelt tightly.
> - The bypass is **time-limited** — the technician must restore the system before the day ends.
> - The key to re-enable the airbag is **physically locked** with the maintenance supervisor.
> - A **logbook entry** records who, when, why, and how long the airbag was bypassed.
>
> Bypass management in a process plant (refinery, power plant, chemical facility) is **structurally identical** — only the consequences of failure are amplified from "personal injury" to "potential mass casualty, asset loss, and environmental catastrophe."

### 1.3 Why Bypass Management Matters

> [!WARNING]
> A significant percentage of major process industry incidents involve a **bypassed, overridden, or inhibited safety function at the time of the accident** (Bhopal, Texas City, BP Whiting Refinery — post-event findings). The SIF is not the protection during bypass; **operations and procedures** are. Hence bypass management is the *bridge* between engineered protection and procedural protection.

### 1.4 The Three Categories of Bypass

1. **Engineering Bypass (Hard Bypass):** Physical jumpers, removed wires, blocked valves, or key-locked switch positions. Highest integrity, hardest to defeat.
2. **Software/Logic Solver Bypass (Soft Bypass):** A flag or bit set in the Safety Logic Solver (e.g., in a Safety PLC like HIMA, Siemens S7 F-systems, ABB 800xA HI) that disables a specific tag or function block.
3. **Operational Override (Force/Inhibit):** A higher-level operational action, usually a forced output, applied through the operator HMI/DCS to maintain process continuity during a known fault.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Bypass** = removes the SIF from active protection (the SIF will not respond to a process demand).
> - **Inhibit** = suppresses only the alarm or operator notification; the SIF may still trip if it receives a real demand.
> - **Override** = forces an output state (e.g., close a valve) regardless of the SIF logic decision.
> These three are *frequently confused* in exam answers — examiners specifically test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bypass Lifecycle (Six Phases)

A correctly managed bypass moves through six discrete phases, each of which is auditable and documented:

**Phase 1 — Identification of Need**
- Trigger: a planned maintenance activity, proof test, commissioning, a known device failure, or a process constraint.
- Output: a *Bypass Request* with justification, scope (full/partial), and intended duration.

**Phase 2 — Risk Assessment (Compensating Measures)**
- Evaluation of the residual risk while the SIF is bypassed.
- Determination of additional risk reduction (operator monitoring, reduced throughput, alternative SIFs, gas testing, restricted access, additional PPE, etc.).
- Quantification against the **Tolerable Hazard Rate / Tolerable Risk**.

**Phase 3 — Authorization (MOC)**
- Approval by the responsible authority — typically the *Process Safety Manager* + *Operations Supervisor* + *Maintenance Supervisor*. Some sites require plant manager sign-off.
- Time-boxed: bypasses have a **maximum allowable duration** (commonly 8 hours, 24 hours, or shift-based).

**Phase 4 — Implementation**
- Application of the physical key, software flag, or force command.
- **Independent verification** by a second competent person (the "two-person rule").
- Update of the **Bypass Log/Register**.
- Communication to all affected operators (shift handover, logbook, alarm).

**Phase 5 — Active Monitoring**
- Continuous awareness of the bypassed state — typically a **dedicated, high-priority, un-acknowledgeable alarm** on the operator HMI.
- Time-to-expiry countdown visible.
- Periodic verification that compensating measures are still in place.

**Phase 6 — Restoration and Verification**
- Removal of the bypass in the *reverse order* of application.
- Functional test of the restored SIF (often a partial proof test).
- Closure of the bypass entry in the logbook.
- **Communication to all shifts** that protection is restored.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> The Examiner often asks: *"What is the first action when a bypass must be applied during an emergency?"* The correct order is **NOT** to bypass first — it is to **implement the compensating measure, then authorize, then bypass**. The SIF must be the *last* thing removed, not the first.

### 2.2 Risk Quantification During Bypass

While a SIF is bypassed, its risk reduction contribution is removed. The process risk temporarily rises from its normal operating level.

**KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $PFD_{SIF}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand of the SIF | dimensionless (per year of demand) |
| $RRF_{SIF}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of the SIF, $= 1 / PFD_{SIF}$ | dimensionless |
| $f_{demand}$ | Demand rate on the SIF (events per year) | yr$^{-1}$ |
| $T_{bypass}$ | Duration the SIF remains bypassed | hours |
| $T_{annual}$ | Total operating hours per year (typically $8760$ h) | h/yr |
| $MTTF$ | Mean Time To Failure of the bypassed channel | h |
| $\lambda_{DU}$ | Dangerous Undetected failure rate | h$^{-1}$ |
| $R_{normal}$ | Risk level with SIF active | events/yr |
| $R_{bypass}$ | Risk level during bypass (SIF unavailable) | events/yr |
| $\Delta R$ | Incremental risk from bypass $= R_{bypass} - R_{normal}$ | events/yr |
| $t_{max}$ | Maximum tolerable bypass duration | h |

### 2.3 Core Equations

**(a) Average Probability of Failure on Demand (1oo1 architecture, simplified)**

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$

where $T_{proof}$ is the proof test interval in hours.

**(b) Incremental Risk During Bypass**

$$\Delta R = f_{demand} \cdot \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}}$$

This represents the additional hazard frequency introduced by the unavailability of the SIF during the bypass window.

**(c) Maximum Tolerable Bypass Duration**

$$t_{max} = \frac{Tolerable\;Hazard\;Rate \;-\; f_{demand} \cdot PFD_{SIF}}{f_{demand}} \cdot T_{annual}$$

> Derivation and use explained in Section 3.

**(d) PFD with Bypass Considered (Modified IEC 61511 formulation)**

$$PFD_{SYS} = PFD_{SIF} + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \cdot (1 - PFD_{SIF})$$

This treats the bypass window as a "100% unavailability" period on top of the normal SIF unavailability.

### 2.4 The Bypass Register (Mandatory Record)

Every operational site maintains a **Bypass Register / Logbook** with the following mandatory fields:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Bypass ID | Unique identifier (e.g., BP-2024-0312-001) |
| Date/Time Applied | Timestamp (with timezone) |
| SIF/SIS Tag | Equipment identifier (e.g., SIF-101, LSH-2030) |
| Reason | Maintenance / Test / Fault / Commissioning |
| Authorized By | Name and role of approver(s) |
| Compensating Measure | Description of the alternative risk control |
| Expiry Time | Date/time when bypass must be removed |
| Applied By | Name of person who physically applied the bypass |
| Verified By | Name of the independent verifier |
| Removal Date/Time | Actual restoration timestamp |
| Verified Restored By | Name of restoration verifier |
| Functional Test Result | Pass/Fail of post-removal SIF test |
| Status | Open / Closed / Extended |

### 2.5 Bypass Position Indicators and Alarms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Concept — Bypass Indication**
> Per IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.3, when a SIF is bypassed, the BPCS / operator HMI must generate a **dedicated, distinct, high-priority alarm** that:
> - Cannot be silenced or shelved without operator acknowledgment.
> - Is visible on every relevant operator console.
> - Is repeated at every shift handover.
> - Includes a **countdown timer** for time-bounded bypasses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pattern to Avoid in Exam Answers:**
> Stating *"put a sign on the valve"* is not a sufficient compensating measure. The compensating measure must be **active, verifiable, and at least equivalent in risk reduction** to the SIF being bypassed.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bypass management is the operational interface of Functional Safety. It is the *only* mechanism by which a SIF can be made temporarily inactive in a controlled manner. Without it, *every* maintenance activity or proof test would be impossible. With it, plants can:
- Execute SIL-rated proof tests safely.
- Replace transmitters without unscheduled shutdowns.
- Operate during known component failures while replacement parts are in transit.
- Manage brownfield commissioning where SIFs are not yet operational.

The cost of *poor* bypass management is documented in countless incident reports (e.g., the BP Texas City Refinery explosion, 2005 — 15 fatalities, where multiple safety instruments were bypassed at the time of the incident).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Tolerable Bypass Duration

The objective is to keep the **total process risk below the Tolerable Hazard Rate** even while the SIF is unavailable.

**Starting condition** (normal operation, SIF active):
$$R_{normal} = f_{demand} \cdot PFD_{SIF}$$

**During bypass** (SIF unavailable for fraction $T_{bypass} / T_{annual}$ of the year):
$$R_{bypass} = f_{demand} \cdot \left( PFD_{SIF} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}}\right) + 1 \cdot \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \right)$$

The risk during the bypass window is dominated by the $1 \cdot (T_{bypass}/T_{annual})$ term, since the SIF being bypassed is treated as having $PFD = 1$ (i.e., it cannot respond).

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{bypass} &= f_{demand} \cdot \left[ PFD_{SIF} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}}\right) + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \right] \\
&= f_{demand} \cdot \left[ PFD_{SIF} - PFD_{SIF} \cdot \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \right] \\
&= f_{demand} \cdot \left[ PFD_{SIF} + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \cdot (1 - PFD_{SIF}) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Constraint (safety requirement):**
$$R_{bypass} \leq R_{tolerable}$$

Solving for $T_{bypass}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{demand} \cdot \left[ PFD_{SIF} + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \cdot (1 - PFD_{SIF}) \right] &\leq R_{tolerable} \\
PFD_{SIF} + \frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \cdot (1 - PFD_{SIF}) &\leq \frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} \\
\frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} \cdot (1 - PFD_{SIF}) &\leq \frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF} \\
\frac{T_{bypass}}{T_{annual}} &\leq \frac{\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF}}{1 - PFD_{SIF}} \\
T_{bypass} &\leq T_{annual} \cdot \frac{\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF}}{1 - PFD_{SIF}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} = T_{annual} \cdot \frac{\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF}}{1 - PFD_{SIF}}\,}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The maximum tolerable bypass duration shrinks as $f_{demand}$ grows (higher-demand SIFs are more sensitive) and grows as the tolerable risk $R_{tolerable}$ grows (more lenient site).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Evaluation

**Given:**
- $f_{demand} = 1$ demand per year (a low-demand SIF).
- $PFD_{SIF} = 10^{-2}$ (SIL 1–2 boundary SIF).
- $R_{tolerable} = 10^{-4}$ events/year.
- $T_{annual} = 8760$ hours.

**Step 1 — Compute the bracketed numerator term:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} &= \frac{10^{-4}}{1} = 10^{-4} \\
\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF} &= 10^{-4} - 10^{-2} = -9.9 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result:**

Because $R_{tolerable} / f_{demand} < PFD_{SIF}$, the inequality has **no positive solution** for $T_{bypass}$. This means the SIF is *already* at the boundary of acceptable risk during normal operation — the site must **not** permit any bypass without additional compensating measures that *reduce $f_{demand}$* (e.g., reduce throughput, isolate the process).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:**
> A bypass is only administratively feasible if the SIF's residual headroom (the gap between $PFD_{SIF}$ and $R_{tolerable}/f_{demand}$) is positive. If the SIF is already at the edge of acceptance, **bypassing it is mathematically equivalent to operating outside the safety envelope.**

### 3.3 Numerical Example Where a Bypass is Permissible

**Given:**
- $f_{demand} = 0.1$ demand/year.
- $PFD_{SIF} = 10^{-3}$ (SIL 2 SIF).
- $R_{tolerable} = 10^{-5}$ events/year.
- $T_{annual} = 8760$ h.

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} &= \frac{10^{-5}}{0.1} = 10^{-4} \\
10^{-4} - 10^{-3} &= 9 \times 10^{-4} \\
1 - 10^{-3} &\approx 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{max}$:**

$$T_{max} = 8760 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} = 7.88 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** The SIF may be bypassed for at most **≈ 7.9 hours** under the stated risk envelope, assuming no other compensating measures are applied. With additional compensating measures that effectively reduce $f_{demand}$ by a factor $k$ (e.g., reduced throughput lowers the demand probability), $T_{max}$ scales linearly with $1/k$.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Permit a Bypass

| Plant State | Demand Rate | $PFD_{SIF}$ | Compensation Feasible? | Bypass Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Normal, low demand | $f_d \leq 0.1$/yr | $\leq 10^{-3}$ | Yes | Permitted, time-limited, MOC required |
| Normal, high demand | $f_d \geq 1$/yr | $\leq 10^{-3}$ | Yes | Permitted only with strong compensation |
| Already near tolerable risk | any | $\geq R_{tol}/f_d$ | No | **REJECT bypass** — shutdown for repair |
| Emergency / unknown fault | unknown | unknown | limited | **REJECT bypass** — apply isolation / safe shutdown |
| Commissioning (no SIF yet) | any | n/a | mandatory | Permitted only under commissioning procedure |

### 3.5 Comparative Matrix — Bypass vs. Inhibit vs. Override vs. Force

| Property | Bypass | Inhibit | Override | Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Disables the SIF response? | **Yes** | No (suppresses alarm only) | No (forces output) | No (forces output) |
| Removes operator alarm? | Yes (replaced with bypass alarm) | Yes (suppresses) | No | No |
| Permits operation with SIF unable to act? | Yes | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| Most common use | Maintenance, proof test | Alarm rationalization | Process control | Logic solver testing |
| Requires MOC? | **Yes** | Yes (typically) | Case-by-case | Yes |
| Risk severity if misused | Highest | Medium | Medium | Medium-High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bypass Management Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Operational Need Identified]) --> stepB[Bypass Request Drafted]
    stepB --> stepC{Risk Assessment}
    stepC -- Risk Acceptable --> stepD[Define Compensating Measures]
    stepC -- Risk Unacceptable --> stopZ([Reject Bypass Initiate Safe Shutdown])
    stepD --> stepE[MOC Authorization Two Person Rule]
    stepE --> stepF[Apply Bypass Physical or Software]
    stepF --> stepG[Update Bypass Register]
    stepG --> stepH[Notify All Shifts Continuous Bypass Alarm]
    stepH --> stepI{Maximum Duration Reached}
    stepI -- No --> stepJ[Active Monitoring Periodic Verification]
    stepJ --> stepI
    stepI -- Yes or Work Complete --> stepK[Remove Bypass in Reverse Order]
    stepK --> stepL[Functional Test of Restored SIF]
    stepL --> stepM{Test Passed}
    stepM -- Yes --> stepN[Close Bypass Entry Communicate Restoration]
    stepM -- No --> stopZ
    stepN --> endO([Process Resumes Normal SIF Operation])
```

### 4.2 Bypass Authorization Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reqOriginator[Request Originator Maintenance or Operations] --> supLevel1[Shift Supervisor]
    supLevel1 --> supLevel2[Process Safety Manager]
    supLevel2 --> supLevel3[Plant Manager if High Risk Bypass]
    supLevel3 --> authFinal[Authorized Bypass Issued]
    authFinal --> applyEng[Engineering Implementation with Key Lock]
    applyEng --> verifyEng[Independent Verifier Signs Off]
    verifyEng --> logUpdate[Bypass Register Updated]
    logUpdate --> opsNotify[Operator HMI Alarm Activated]
    opsNotify --> monitorLoop[Continuous Monitoring with Countdown]
    monitorLoop --> restoreStep[Removal Verification and Functional Test]
    restoreStep --> closeOut[Log Closure and Shift Handover]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Where a Bypass Sits in the SIF Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Field Sensor e.g. Pressure Transmitter] --> logicA[Safety Logic Solver e.g. Safety PLC]
    logicA --> actuatorA[Final Element e.g. Shut Down Valve]
    sensorA -. Bypass Point 1 .-> bypassNode1[Bypass Switch or Software Flag]
    logicA -. Bypass Point 2 .-> bypassNode2[Logic Solver Inhibit Flag]
    actuatorA -. Bypass Point 3 .-> bypassNode3[Valve Bypass Lock]
    bypassNode1 --> monitorPanel[Operator HMI with Dedicated Bypass Alarm and Countdown]
    bypassNode2 --> monitorPanel
    bypassNode3 --> monitorPanel
    monitorPanel --> logBook[Bypass Logbook and Register]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A bypass can be inserted at *any* of the three points of a SIF — sensor, logic, or final element. Each point has its own access controls and audit requirements. The dedicated operator HMI alarm and the central logbook are the *single source of truth* for the bypass state.

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Bypass Feasibility Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{Bypass Requested}
    qStart -- Yes --> qA[Calculate PFD of SIF]
    qA --> qB[Determine Demand Rate]
    qB --> qC[Compute Tmax using formula]
    qC --> qD{Is Tmax greater than 0}
    qD -- No --> rejectNode[Reject Bypass Force Safe Shutdown]
    qD -- Yes --> qE[Identify Compensating Measures]
    qE --> qF{Compensating Measures Effective}
    qF -- No --> rejectNode
    qF -- Yes --> qG[Set Bypass Duration to min Needed Tmax]
    qG --> qH[Issue MOC Authorization]
    qH --> qI[Implement with Two Person Verification]
    qI --> qJ[Monitor with HMI Alarm and Register]
    qJ --> qK{Restoration Time}
    qK -- On Schedule --> removeNode[Remove Bypass and Verify SIF]
    qK -- Overdue --> escalateNode[Escalate to Plant Manager Immediate Action]
    removeNode --> doneNode[Close Bypass Entry Process Resumes]
    escalateNode --> removeNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
Define the term **Bypass** in the context of Safety Instrumented Systems. How does it differ from an **Inhibit**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- *Bypass (1 mark):* A Bypass is the deliberate, temporary, authorized deactivation of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) or part of it (sensor, logic, or final element), so that maintenance, proof testing, or commissioning activities can be carried out without spurious process trips.
- *Inhibit (1 mark):* An Inhibit is a software-level suppression of an alarm or operator notification only; the underlying safety function remains operational and will still respond to a genuine process demand.
- *Key difference (1 mark):* Bypass removes the SIF's ability to act on a process demand (the function is "blind"), whereas an Inhibit only silences the alarm and the SIF continues to be capable of performing its protective role.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**
List the **mandatory compensating measures** that must be in place before a SIL 2 SIF is bypassed for a planned proof test.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. A **documented risk assessment** demonstrating that the residual process risk during bypass is within the tolerable limit. (1 mark)
2. **Continuous enhanced operator monitoring** of the protected process variable with a clear escalation path. (1 mark)
3. A **dedicated, high-priority, un-acknowledgeable alarm** on the operator HMI indicating the SIF is bypassed, with a visible **countdown timer** for the maximum allowed bypass duration. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Internal Choice A]**
**(a)** Explain the complete **Bypass Management Lifecycle** in a process plant, clearly distinguishing the role of the **MOC (Management of Change)** procedure, the **Bypass Register**, and **compensating measures** in ensuring functional safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SIL 2 SIF in a refinery has the following parameters: demand rate $f_{demand} = 0.5$/year, $PFD_{SIF} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$, tolerable hazard rate $R_{tolerable} = 10^{-5}$ events/year, $T_{annual} = 8760$ h. Compute the **maximum allowable bypass duration** and state the operational implication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) — Bypass Management Lifecycle (7 marks)**

The bypass management lifecycle consists of **six sequential phases**:

1. *Identification of Need (1 mark):* A bypass becomes necessary due to planned maintenance, proof test, commissioning, or a known equipment fault. The origin of the request must be documented (requestor, equipment ID, reason, scope of bypass — full/partial, intended duration).

2. *Risk Assessment and Compensating Measures (1.5 marks):* The residual risk with the SIF unavailable is calculated. If it exceeds the tolerable limit, the bypass is rejected. If acceptable, **compensating measures** are defined — these may include enhanced operator monitoring, reduced throughput, additional gas testing, isolation, restricted access, or an alternative SIF. The measures must be **active, verifiable, and at least equivalent** in risk reduction to the SIF being bypassed.

3. *MOC Authorization (1.5 marks):* The **Management of Change (MOC)** procedure ensures that no safety function is removed without formal multi-level approval — typically the Process Safety Manager, Operations Supervisor, and Maintenance Supervisor. MOC is the *gatekeeper* that prevents unauthorized or under-assessed bypasses.

4. *Implementation and Independent Verification (1 mark):* The bypass is physically applied (jumper, key, or software flag) and immediately verified by a second competent person under the **two-person rule**. The **Bypass Register** is updated with a unique bypass ID, timestamps, signatures, and expiry time. All shifts are informed.

5. *Active Monitoring (1 mark):* A dedicated, un-acknowledgeable, high-priority HMI alarm with a countdown timer continuously displays the bypass state. Compensating measures are periodically verified. Any anomaly triggers escalation.

6. *Restoration and Functional Verification (1 mark):* The bypass is removed in the **reverse order** of application, followed by a **partial or full functional test** of the restored SIF. The Bypass Register entry is closed, and all shifts are notified that protection is restored.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Identify the given values (1 mark):*
- $f_{demand} = 0.5$ /year
- $PFD_{SIF} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$
- $R_{tolerable} = 10^{-5}$ events/year
- $T_{annual} = 8760$ h

*Step 2 — Apply the maximum bypass duration formula (2 marks):*

$$T_{max} = T_{annual} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} - PFD_{SIF}}{1 - PFD_{SIF}}$$

*Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator term (1.5 marks):*

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{tolerable}}{f_{demand}} &= \frac{10^{-5}}{0.5} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \\
2 \times 10^{-5} - 2 \times 10^{-3} &= -1.98 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 4 — Evaluate the full expression (1.5 marks):*

$$T_{max} = 8760 \cdot \frac{-1.98 \times 10^{-3}}{1 - 2 \times 10^{-3}} = 8760 \cdot \frac{-1.98 \times 10^{-3}}{0.998} \approx -17.4 \text{ h}$$

*Step 5 — Interpretation and operational implication (1 mark):*

The result is **negative**, which is physically impossible. This means the SIF's existing risk reduction is **already at or below the tolerable limit** — the system is operating at the edge of its safety envelope. **No bypass is permitted.** The plant must either:
- Perform the work without bypassing (using online measurement alternatives),
- Shut down the affected process section, or
- Apply a **strong compensating measure** that effectively reduces the demand rate $f_{demand}$ until $T_{max}$ becomes positive.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**
**(a)** Compare and contrast **Bypass, Inhibit, Override, and Force** as used in safety logic solvers. Why is *Bypass* considered the most safety-critical of the four? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **technical and administrative controls** required during the active phase of a bypass, including the role of operator HMI alarms, the two-person rule, the Bypass Register, and shift handover procedures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (14 Marks):**

**Part (a) — Comparison of Bypass, Inhibit, Override, Force (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Bypass | Inhibit | Override | Force |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Disables the protective action? | **Yes (the SIF cannot act)** | No, only alarm is suppressed | No, output is commanded | No, output is commanded |
| Removes operator awareness? | Yes (replaced by a dedicated bypass alarm) | Yes (alarm silenced) | No | No |
| Typical use | Maintenance, proof test | Alarm rationalization | Process control | Logic testing |
| Risk if misused | **Highest** — accident may occur with no SIF response | Medium — alarm missing | Medium | Medium |

*Why Bypass is the most safety-critical (2 marks within the 7):* A Bypass is the only one of the four that **physically disconnects** the SIF from its protective duty. While bypassed, the SIF is *guaranteed* to fail to respond to a demand — the probability of dangerous failure is effectively 1.0. In contrast, an Inhibit only silences an alarm, an Override commands an output (which can still include a trip if logic demands it), and a Force freezes a single output for testing without affecting the entire function. The KTU 2024 syllabus therefore places the Bypass at the top of the safety hierarchy of overrides and demands the strictest administrative envelope.

**Part (b) — Technical and Administrative Controls During Active Bypass (7 marks)**

1. *Dedicated High-Priority HMI Alarm (1.5 marks):* Per IEC 61511 Clause 16.2.3, every bypass must be reflected on the operator HMI as a **distinct, high-priority, non-silenceable alarm** that includes the **SIF tag, reason, expiry time, and countdown timer**. The alarm must be visible on all operator consoles and cannot be shelved or muted.

2. *Two-Person Rule / Independent Verification (1.5 marks):* No single individual can apply a bypass alone. The bypass must be applied by one competent person and **independently verified** by a second. Both sign the Bypass Register. This prevents accidental or malicious bypasses and ensures that the operator community has redundant awareness of the safety gap.

3. *Bypass Register (Logbook) Maintenance (1.5 marks):* A central, site-wide, real-time accessible **Bypass Register** records every active and historical bypass. Mandatory fields include Bypass ID, SIF tag, reason, authorizing signatures, applied-by, verified-by, expiry time, compensating measures, and removal verification. The Register is the audit backbone of bypass management.

4. *Shift Handover Communication (1 mark):* At every shift change, the outgoing operator must explicitly communicate the **list of active bypasses** to the incoming operator, and this communication must be **co-signed** in the shift logbook. Bypasses that are about to expire must be flagged for the incoming shift.

5. *Periodic Verification of Compensating Measures (1 mark):* At a defined frequency (e.g., every 2 hours), the operator or shift supervisor must confirm that the compensating measures are still in place. Any lapse is treated as a safety incident and triggers escalation.

6. *Escalation on Expiry (0.5 marks):* If a bypass cannot be removed by its expiry time, an automatic escalation to the **Plant Manager** and **Process Safety Manager** is triggered, and the affected process may be required to move to a safe state.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> Examiners specifically **deduct 1–2 marks** for any of the following common errors:
> 1. Confusing **Bypass** with **Inhibit** in definitions — the SIF's response capability is the discriminator.
> 2. Stating that a **sign on the valve** is a sufficient compensating measure — it must be an *active, risk-equivalent* measure.
> 3. Omitting the **countdown timer** when describing HMI alarms — it is mandatory under IEC 61511.
> 4. Saying the **same person** can apply and verify the bypass — violates the two-person rule.
> 5. Using a **bypass to hide a known SIF failure** without raising an MOC — this is an unauthorized defeat and a regulatory non-conformance.
> 6. Forgetting to mention **functional testing** after bypass removal — restoration without verification is incomplete.
> 7. Using $PFD = 0$ in calculations for a bypassed SIF — the correct value is $PFD = 1$ (absolute unavailability).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bypass = authorized, time-limited, risk-assessed defeat of a SIF**; it is *not* a failure but a *transfer* of risk from engineered protection to procedural protection.
- **Bypass ≠ Inhibit ≠ Override ≠ Force.** Bypass disables the protective action; Inhibit silences the alarm; Override/Force command outputs.
- A bypass can be applied at **three points** in a SIF: sensor, logic solver, or final element. Each has distinct access controls.
- The **Bypass Register** is the single source of truth and audit artifact for every bypass — it must be real-time, complete, and signed.
- **MOC (Management of Change)** is the mandatory authorization gate; no bypass may be applied without it (except in narrowly defined emergencies, with immediate post-hoc MOC).
- The **two-person rule** (apply + independently verify) is non-negotiable in IEC 61511 compliant sites.
- **Compensating measures** must be active, verifiable, and risk-equivalent — passive signs and labels do not qualify.
- The **maximum tolerable bypass duration** is computed from the residual risk inequality: $T_{max} = T_{annual} \cdot \dfrac{R_{tolerable}/f_{demand} - PFD_{SIF}}{1 - PFD_{SIF}}$.
- A **negative $T_{max}$** means the SIF is at the edge of acceptable risk — bypass is **not permitted**; the process must be shut down or compensation must reduce $f_{demand}$.
- During bypass, a **dedicated, high-priority, un-acknowledgeable HMI alarm with a countdown timer** must be active.
- **Shift handover** must explicitly communicate every active bypass, with co-signature.
- **Removal** must be in the **reverse order of application**, followed by a **functional test** of the restored SIF, and formal closure of the Register entry.
- A bypassed SIF has **$PFD = 1$** for the duration of the bypass; this is the key analytical assumption.
- **Documentation** is the legal and operational backbone: every bypass must be traceable to a person, a reason, an authorization, a duration, and a verification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Demand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT635 - FUNCTIONAL SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Demand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU/IEC 61508/61511 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Process Demand (Functional Safety Definition)**
> A **Process Demand** is an *occurrence of a hazardous event* (or a condition/state of the process) that requires the **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** or **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** to perform its safety action in order to bring the process to a safe state. In quantitative terms, it is an *event* characterized by its **demand rate (λ)** — expressed as the number of dangerous demands per year placed on the safety function.

Formally, in **IEC 61511-1:2016 (Clause 3.2.16)** terminology:

> "A *demand* is an event that requires the SIF to take action to prevent a hazard from occurring or to mitigate its consequences."

In **IEC 61508-4:2010 (Clause 3.5.5)**:

> "A *dangerous failure* of an element, subsystem, or system, which has the potential to cause a *demand* on the safety-related system."

---

## 1.2 Types (Modes) of Demand

The IEC standards distinguish **three principal demand modes** that fundamentally change how the Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is verified:

| Demand Mode | Demand Rate (λ) | Verification Metric | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Demand Mode** | λ ≤ **10⁻² demands/year** (i.e., less than once in 100 years) | **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFDavg)** | Emergency Shutdown (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G), High-Integrity Pressure Protection |
| **High Demand Mode** | λ > **10⁻² demands/year** (more than once in 100 years) | **Probability of a dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)** | Burner Management Systems (BMS), Turbine Control, Compressor Anti-Surge |
| **Continuous Mode** | Safety function *continuously* maintains process in safe state | **Probability of a dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)** | Reactor protection, overspeed trips, critical valve control |

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction for KTU Exam**
> - **PFDavg** is dimensionless (probability per demand) → used for **Low Demand**
> - **PFH** has units of **per hour** (failures/hour) → used for **High Demand / Continuous**
> This boundary condition of **λ ≤ 10⁻² /year** is a frequently asked KTU conceptual question.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Airbag in a Car" Analogy**
> Think of your car as a process, and an *accident* as a **hazardous demand**.
> - Your car's **seatbelt** is like a **Low-Demand SIF** — you expect to wear it every day but hope the *airbag* (the actual demand-driven SIF) never fires. We measure its reliability as: *"On average, if an accident happens, what is the probability the airbag fails to deploy?"* → That is **PFDavg**.
> - The car's **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** is like a **High-Demand/Continuous SIF** — it must work every single braking event. We measure its reliability as: *"How many dangerous failures occur per hour of operation?"* → That is **PFH**.
>
> **Process Demand = the accident happening.** The SIF is the airbag; its job is to act when the demand arrives.

---

## 1.4 Core Physical/Reliability Constants

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — average time between consecutive failures (typically in years or hours).
- **Mean Time To Restoration (MTTR)** — average time required to restore the system after a dangerous failure is detected.
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** — fraction of dangerous failures detected by automatic diagnostic tests.
- **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** — ratio of safe failures plus detected dangerous failures to total failures.
- **Proof Test Interval (TProof)** — the periodic interval at which the SIF is manually tested to reveal undetected dangerous failures.
- **Dangerous Undetected Failure Rate (λDU)** — failures that occur *without* being caught by diagnostics; these directly degrade PFD.

> [!IMPORTANT] All of the above are typically expressed as **fractions, per year (yr⁻¹), or hours (h⁻¹)**. KTU board questions often demand consistency in units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Demand Rate Governs the SIL Verification Method

The **demand rate (λ)** is the *gateway variable* that determines which reliability metric the engineer must compute:

- If **λ is small** (low demand): the system *rests* in a dormant state most of its life. Failures accumulate silently. The only meaningful metric is *"if a demand arrives, will the SIF work?"* → **PFDavg**.
- If **λ is large** (high/continuous demand): the system is *active* nearly continuously. Failures occur *during operation*, so we measure the *frequency* of dangerous failures during operation → **PFH**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Decision Rule (KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked):**
> $$\text{If } \lambda \leq 10^{-2} \, \text{demands/year} \Rightarrow \text{use PFDavg (Low Demand)}$$
> $$\text{If } \lambda > 10^{-2} \, \text{demands/year} \Rightarrow \text{use PFH (High Demand)}$$
> $$\text{If the safety function is continuous} \Rightarrow \text{use PFH (Continuous)}$$

---

## 2.2 Failure Rate Decomposition (λ-Framework)

The total failure rate **λ** of any safety component is partitioned by IEC 61508:

$$\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_D$$

where:

- **λ_S** = Safe failure rate (failures that *safely* trip the process or are detectable)
- **λ_D** = Dangerous failure rate (failures that *prevent* the SIF from acting on demand)

The dangerous failures are further decomposed by diagnostic coverage:

$$\lambda_D = \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$$

- **λ_DD** = Dangerous *Detected* failure rate (caught by self-diagnostics → system fails *safely*)
- **λ_DU** = Dangerous *Undetected* failure rate (hidden → only revealed by *proof testing*)

> [!NOTE] **PFD is overwhelmingly driven by λ_DU**, not by λ_DD. This is why proof testing is the most powerful operational tool to reduce PFDavg.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Demand rate definition | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{demands}}}{T_{\text{observation}}}$ | demands/year |
| 2 | PFDavg — single channel (low demand) | $PFD_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | PFDavg — 1oo2 redundant channel (low demand) | $PFD_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}^2}{6}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | PFH — single channel (high/continuous) | $PFH \approx \lambda_{DU}$ | per hour (h⁻¹) |
| 5 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD_{\text{avg}}}$ | dimensionless, must be ≥ 1 |
| 6 | Achievable Risk Reduction | $R_{\text{reduced}} = \dfrac{R}{RRF}$ | risk units |
| 7 | Failure rate partition | $\lambda = \lambda_S + \lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}$ | per hour or per year |
| 8 | Probability of Safe Failure | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 9 | Mean Unavailability (Low Demand) | $\bar{U} = PFD_{\text{avg}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Equivalent Continuous Availability | $PFH \approx \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$ | per hour |

> [!IMPORTANT] **SIL ↔ PFDavg ↔ PFH Mapping Table (IEC 61511 / 61508)**
>
> | SIL | PFDavg (Low Demand) | PFH (High/Continuous Demand) | RRF (Low Demand) |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6} \leq PFH < 10^{-5}$ | $10 \leq RRF < 100$ |
> | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7} \leq PFH < 10^{-6}$ | $100 \leq RRF < 1000$ |
> | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$ | $1000 \leq RRF < 10000$ |
> | **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq PFD_{avg} < 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9} \leq PFH < 10^{-8}$ | $10000 \leq RRF < 100000$ |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** PFDavg is *NOT* a percentage — it is a **probability between 0 and 1** (e.g., 0.01 = 1%). Conflating the two is the #1 valuation error.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Process Demand concept drives every phase of the **Safety Lifecycle**:

1. **Hazard & Risk Assessment (HRA)** — establishes *how often* the process demands protection (λ).
2. **SIL Determination** — pairs λ with the tolerable risk to set the required PFDavg target.
3. **SIF Design** — selects sensors, logic solvers, and final elements whose combined PFDavg ≤ target.
4. **Operation & Maintenance** — sets the **Proof Test Interval (TProof)** to keep PFDavg in target throughout the SIF's life.
5. **Modifications / MOC (Management of Change)** — any change in λ (e.g., new feedstock) triggers a re-determination.

> In **oil & gas**, **chemical**, **petrochemical**, **nuclear**, and **rail signalling** industries, Process Demand quantification is the *entry point* of every Safety Integrity Level (SIL) study.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PFDavg for a Single, Unrepaired Component (Low Demand)

### Context
Consider a single-channel SIF with only **dangerous undetected failures (λ_DU)**. The component is *not* repaired after failure; only an *annual* proof test reveals the failure. We want the **average unavailability** over one proof test interval.

### Step 1 — Define the unavailability Q(t)

Let the probability that the component has failed at time *t* (but failure is undetected) be:

$$Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the small-failure-rate approximation

Since $\lambda_{DU} \cdot t \ll 1$ for SIF components (typically λ_DU ≈ 10⁻⁶ /h), we Taylor-expand:

$$e^{-\lambda_{DU} \cdot t} \approx 1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

Substituting:

$$Q(t) \approx 1 - (1 - \lambda_{DU} \cdot t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Average the unavailability over the proof test interval

The *average* unavailability (mean value theorem):

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{Proof}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{Proof}}} Q(t) \, dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{Proof}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{Proof}}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt$$

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{\text{Proof}}} \left[\dfrac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^{T_{\text{Proof}}}$$

### Step 5 — Final closed form

$$PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The unavailability grows *linearly* between two proof tests and is then reset to zero (or a small residual) when the failure is detected and repaired. The *average* is half the peak — hence the **factor of ½**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PFDavg for a 1oo2 Redundant System

### Context
A 1oo2 architecture has *two* identical channels in parallel. The system fails only when **both** channels have dangerous undetected failures simultaneously.

### Step 1 — Joint failure probability

For independent channels:

$$Q_{1oo2}(t) = Q_A(t) \cdot Q_B(t) = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2$$

(assuming identical λ_DU for both channels).

### Step 2 — Average over the proof interval

$$PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{Proof}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{Proof}}} (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^2 \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2}{T_{\text{Proof}}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{Proof}}^3}{3}$$

$$PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^2 \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}^2}{6}$$

### Step 4 — Compare with single-channel

$$\dfrac{PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo2}}}{PFD_{\text{avg, 1oo1}}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{\text{Proof}}}{3}$$

> [!NOTE] **Trade-off:** Redundancy *drastically reduces PFDavg* (by a factor of λ_DU·T/3), but **introduces common-cause failure** (β-factor) and roughly *doubles* the safe failure rate (more nuisance trips). KTU board questions frequently ask this trade-off.

---
